title: `extract_atoms` slug (`slice(0,60)`) can end in a hyphen; import's `slugifyPath` strips it → duplicate atom on export→sync round-trip

body:
**Type:** enhancement · **Area:** cycle / budget-meter

> **Author's correction (for honesty):** an earlier internal note claimed a
> single phase blew past its cap ("$8+ in propose_takes past a $5 cap"). That
> was a measurement error — the `dream-budget-YYYY-Www.jsonl` ledger is
> **weekly** and `cumulative_cost_usd` **resets per run**; summing
> `estimated_cost_usd` across a whole week's rows conflated ~7 separate runs.
> Re-checked: **max per-run cumulative was $1.20, well under the $5 cap.** The
> per-phase gate works. The real, narrower observation is below.

### Summary
Each cycle phase enforces its own budget cap via `BaseCyclePhase` +
`src/core/cycle/budget-meter.ts` (`budgetUsdKey`/`budgetUsdDefault`, e.g.
`cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd=5`, `cycle.extract_atoms.budget_usd=0.3`,
`cycle.calibration_profile.budget_usd=0.5`, …). These **work**: the meter
refuses the next submit once a phase's cumulative estimate exceeds that phase's
cap.

What's missing is a **cycle-level aggregate cap**. A full `dream` tick runs ~19
phases; several are LLM-backed, each with its own independent budget. The tick's
total spend is therefore the **sum** of all per-phase caps (order of $10–20+
depending on config), with no single knob bounding a whole tick. The only
effective backstop for total tick spend is the workspace/provider credit limit.

### Suggestion
Add an optional `cycle.budget_usd` (whole-tick) cap that the budget meter
threads across phases and that halts the remaining phases once the tick total is
reached — analogous to the per-phase caps but at the cycle level. Even a
`--max-cost` flag on `gbrain dream` would let operators bound an unattended tick.

### Why it matters
For unattended/cron `dream` on metered API credits, operators reason about
"what can one nightly tick cost me" — today that answer requires summing every
phase's cap by hand, and nothing enforces the sum.
title: No tick-level (whole-cycle) budget cap; a full `dream` tick's cost is the unbounded sum of ~19 per-phase budgets

body:
**Type:** bug · **Area:** cycle / lens packs (extract_atoms + synthesize_concepts)

### Summary
On a brain running the creator/everything lens (or any pack that declares the
`extract_atoms` + `synthesize_concepts` phases), `extract_atoms` produces good
atoms, but `synthesize_concepts` **always yields 0 concepts** — regardless of
corpus size. Root cause: the two phases disagree on the join key.

- `synthesize_concepts` groups atoms **exclusively** by each atom's
  `frontmatter.concepts` array (`src/core/cycle/synthesize-concepts.ts`, the
  `SELECT … WHERE frontmatter->>'concepts' …` filter + the group-by over
  `atom.concept_refs`). If no atom has that field it returns early:
  `status:'skipped', summary:'synthesize_concepts: no atoms with concept refs'`.
- `extract_atoms`' `EXTRACT_PROMPT` (`src/core/cycle/extract-atoms.ts`) asks the
  model for `{title, atom_type, body, source_quote, lesson, virality_score,
  emotional_register}` — **there is no `concepts` (or `tags`) field in the
  schema**, so the written atom pages never carry `frontmatter.concepts`.

There is no fallback clustering (no tag-based, no embedding/topic clustering),
so the concept tier (T1–T4) can never populate from a fresh extraction.

### Reproduce
1. Activate a pack that declares both phases.
2. Feed a corpus (transcripts via `dream.synthesize.session_corpus_dir`, or
   `meeting`-typed pages) and run `gbrain dream --phase extract_atoms`.
3. Confirm atoms exist but none has a `concepts` field:
   `SELECT count(*) FILTER (WHERE frontmatter ? 'concepts') FROM pages WHERE type='atom';`
   → **0** (observed: 0 of 26 atoms).
4. Run `gbrain dream --phase synthesize_concepts` →
   `synthesize_concepts: no atoms with concept refs` in ~0.1s, **0 concepts**.

### Expected
Either (a) `extract_atoms` emits a `concepts:` array (topic/theme tags) per
atom, or (b) `synthesize_concepts` clusters by an available signal (tags,
embeddings, or title/lesson similarity) when `concepts:` is absent. Today the
producer and consumer are wired to different fields, so the concept half of the
lens is a guaranteed no-op.

### Note: the intended writer appears to have been dropped
`synthesize-concepts.ts` (top-of-file comment) states the field is populated
"when the Haiku 3-check from extract_atoms decides 'this atom is about concept
X', it stamps the field" — but no such step exists in the current
`extract-atoms.ts` (the prompt and the whitelist parser have no `concepts`
field). So the producer was assumed to live inside `extract_atoms` but was never
implemented (or removed). Meanwhile the ingest-side skills (signal-detector,
idea-ingest, voice-note-ingest) write concept **pages** directly, bypassing
atoms — so there are effectively two disjoint concept paths, and the
atom-frontmatter one has no writer.

### Impact
`dream` becomes an atom/Take layer only; the advertised "atoms → tier-promoted
concepts" pipeline never completes without manual `concepts:` tagging.
title: `synthesize_concepts` produces 0 concepts because `extract_atoms` never writes the `concepts:` field it clusters on (structural)

body:
`postgres-engine.ts:990` uses `COALESCE(${chunkerVersion}::smallint, 1)` in the INSERT. When callers don't supply `chunker_version` (which no MCP/subagent caller does — it's internal metadata), new pages land at version 1.

The `importFromFile` path correctly stamps `MARKDOWN_CHUNKER_VERSION` (currently 3), but dream subagents write through `putPage` directly — their pages get v1. Doctor then flags them as "older chunker_version" in the `contextual_retrieval_coverage` check forever.

The pages are actually chunked and embedded correctly at write time (embedding coverage stays 100%). Only the metadata column is wrong.

**Impact:** On an active brain with dream running twice daily, doctor fires `contextual_retrieval_coverage: warn` continuously. Running `reindex --markdown` clears it, but it recurs the next cycle.

**Fix (one line):**

```diff
- COALESCE(${chunkerVersion}::smallint, 1)
+ COALESCE(${chunkerVersion}::smallint, ${MARKDOWN_CHUNKER_VERSION})
```

Import `MARKDOWN_CHUNKER_VERSION` from `../core/chunkers/recursive.ts` (already imported elsewhere in the codebase).

The COALESCE-preserve on UPDATE is correct (don't regress pages already at a higher version). Only the INSERT default needs the bump.
title: putPage defaults chunker_version to 1 on INSERT, causing perpetual doctor warning

body:
## Problem

Subagent jobs using `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` provider (`deepseek:deepseek-v4-flash`) fail with `Invalid prompt: The messages do not match the ModelMessage[] schema` at ~85-92% rate.

## Root Cause

AI SDK v6.0.174's `standardizePrompt()` caches an internal Zod v4 `modelMessageSchema` (a `discriminatedUnion('role', ...)`). This schema has a race condition during lazy initialization on the first `generateText()` call — ~50% of calls reach an inconsistent discriminator-key state, rejecting structurally valid messages.

## Evidence

- 16 test jobs: 2 completed, 14 dead (87.5%)
- All dead: identical InvalidPrompt error
- Zod cause chain: discriminator resolves to wrong branch, `text` claimed as `undefined` when actual value is valid string
- Retry with 2s backoff recovers ~50%, proving non-deterministic (not a data issue)
- Error persists across all 3 retry attempts for jobs that die

## Affected

- `ai` v6.0.174, `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` v2.0.45, Zod v4
- `MissingToolResultsError` is fully resolved (PR #2802) — this is the sole remaining failure

## Workarounds Applied (Partial)

- Retry loop with `freshTools` rebuild — ~50% recovery
- `toModelMessages` user text→string conversion (for retry-loaded messages)
- `toModelMessages` null-text block filtering

## Related

- PR #2802 (MissingToolResultsError fix + InvalidPrompt workarounds)
- Issues #2273, #2609 (MissingToolResultsError — resolved)
title: subagent: InvalidPrompt (ModelMessage[] schema) from AI SDK v6 Zod discriminator race — 87% failure

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

Two documentation inconsistencies a fresh operator cannot resolve without reading source (both bitten here during a from-scratch deployment-design exercise):

**1. Worker concurrency — three different answers, none of them the default.**
- Prose guides recommend `--concurrency 4`: `docs/guides/minions-deployment.md:33,69,72` and `docs/guides/queue-operations-runbook.md:101`.
- All three copy-paste deployment snippets use `--concurrency 2`: `docs/guides/minions-deployment-snippets/systemd.service:21`, `fly.toml.partial:13`, `Procfile:13`.
- The actual code defaults are **neither**: `jobs work` defaults to **1** (`src/commands/jobs.ts:83`) and `jobs supervisor` defaults to **2** (`jobs.ts:1179`).

This matters more than a typical docs nit because concurrency is load-bearing for the cycle-subagent starvation class (#2782): at the real `jobs work` default of 1, an autopilot cycle's own patterns subagent can never be claimed. An operator following any of the three documented values gets three different behaviors, and one following none of them (the actual default) gets the silent no-op.

**2. Doctor cadence — weekly vs daily.**
- `docs/guides/cron-schedule.md:26,44-45`: doctor is a **weekly** task (Mondays 6 AM cron).
- `docs/guides/operational-disciplines.md:54-56,109`: "DISCIPLINE 4: Daily Heartbeat Check" runs `gbrain doctor` **daily** ("the daily heartbeat catches these before they compound").

**What did you expect?**

State the actual defaults in exactly one place (per command — work vs supervisor differ), link every guide and snippet to it, reconcile the recommended values with a scale note (when 1 is fine, when 2/4 are needed and what they cost in DB connections), and pick one doctor cadence story (or state explicitly that heartbeat=daily, deep-doctor=weekly, if that's the intent).

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (docs verified at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)

---

Related: #2782 (why the concurrency default deserves accurate documentation).
title: docs: worker-concurrency guidance is three-way inconsistent (guides say 4, snippets say 2, real defaults are 1/2); doctor cadence weekly vs daily across guides

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

The top-level help advertises a sync daemon installer:

```
sync --install-cron                Install persistent sync daemon
```
(`src/cli.ts:2231`)

No handler exists. A whole-tree grep finds `install-cron` nowhere else; `runSync` (`src/commands/sync.ts:3281`, flag parsing `:3351-3372`) never references it, the `sync --help` usage block doesn't list it, and unknown flags aren't validated — so `gbrain sync --install-cron` **silently performs an ordinary one-off incremental sync**. An operator now believes scheduled syncing is installed; nothing is.

That's worse than dead surface area: it manufactures false confidence in the exact durability layer (freshness syncs) operators reach for it to secure.

**What did you expect?**

Either implement it or delete the help line. Observation from production in favor of deletion: autopilot already owns freshness syncs (44 sync jobs/24h observed on this deployment) — a separate sync cron may be a competing scheduler rather than a missing feature. If deleted, a `--help` pointer at autopilot's role closes the loop; if implemented, unknown-flag validation in `runSync` would have caught this class from the start.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `gbrain --help` → note the `sync --install-cron` line.
2. `gbrain sync --install-cron` → observe a normal sync run; no cron/launchd/systemd artifact created anywhere.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant (CLI help/handler mismatch).
title: bug(cli): sync --install-cron exists only in help text — no handler; it silently runs an ordinary sync instead of installing anything

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

`autopilot --install` never persists `--interval` — in fact it never reads it:

- `installDaemon` (`src/commands/autopilot.ts:1144-1157`) parses only `--repo` from its args.
- `writeWrapperScript` (`:1118-1142`) emits a fixed exec line — `exec '<gbrain>' autopilot --repo '<path>'` (`:1138`) — with no interval, so the installed daemon always runs the 300s default (`:335`).
- The wrapper is unconditionally rewritten on every `--install`, so a hand-edited interval is silently reverted by any reinstall.

Net: `autopilot --install --interval 1800` silently installs a 300s daemon **on first install**, and any operator tuning is lost on every regenerate. At scale the interval is a load-bearing knob, not a preference — via the interval→timeout stamp (#2781) the default budget dead-letters every substantive cycle on a ~7k-page brain — so a reinstall that drops it is a delayed outage with no error message.

**What did you expect?**

`--install` to persist tuning flags: either record `--interval` (and future flags) into config and have the wrapper read it, or bake the flags into the generated exec line and preserve them on regenerate. Failing that, at least error on flags the installer ignores.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `gbrain autopilot --install --interval 1800`.
2. Inspect the generated run script: exec line carries only `--repo`; daemon runs at interval 300.
3. Hand-edit the wrapper to add `--interval 1800`; run `--install` again → edit silently overwritten.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant (installer defect).

---

Related: #2781 (why the interval is load-bearing at scale), #2608 / #1868 (other `--install` wrapper defects).

Local workaround: hand-edited run script with in-file comments + dated backups documenting the edit and its undo — exactly the artifact this fix deletes.
title: bug(autopilot): --install never reads or persists --interval — tuning silently dropped at first install and overwritten on every regenerate

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

`autopilot --install` generates a supervised service unit (`src/commands/autopilot.ts:321`; launchd plist writer ~`:1213`, systemd user unit ~`:1233`). MCP serving — an equally always-on core surface — has no equivalent: `src/commands/serve.ts` parses `--http/--port/--token-ttl/--enable-dcr/--public-url/--bind/...` but no `--install`, and generates nothing. (Careful readers of `cli.ts`: the `[--install]` at `:2340` belongs to the `connect` command on the line above, not to `serve`.)

Every deployment that serves MCP hand-writes a service unit. Ours did (launchd, with the PATH-export line bun needs); an independent from-scratch design exercise against the same version hand-wrote the same thing. Asymmetric operator experience for a surface the docs position as a first-class always-on component.

**What does the solution look like?**

`gbrain serve --install` mirroring the autopilot installer: generate the launchd/systemd unit with the invocation's serve flags baked in (port, --http, tunnel-friendly bind), same wrapper-script conventions (shell-profile sourcing) as the autopilot wrapper, same uninstall story.

**Alternatives considered**

Hand-written unit (deployed, works) — but it silently misses every improvement the autopilot installer gets, and new operators rediscover the PATH/wrapper footguns one at a time.

---

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(serve): --install (launchd/systemd unit generation) mirroring autopilot --install — MCP serving currently requires a hand-written service unit

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

`gbrain sources archive <id>` failed twice in a row with no reason printed (just `Failed to archive source "<id>".`) on an isolated code source; `sources remove --confirm-destructive` then worked. Reproduced 2026-07-08 on v0.42.57.

From source at HEAD (a25209bb), the silent path — and a correction to our own first diagnosis (it is NOT a code-source type check; archive is fully type-agnostic):

- `runArchive` (`src/commands/sources.ts:484-510`): after the existence check passes, the only non-throwing failure is `softDeleteSource` returning null, which `runArchive` collapses into the generic reason-free error at `:505`.
- `softDeleteSource` (`src/core/destructive-guard.ts:180-198`) is a guarded UPDATE — `WHERE id = $1 AND archived = false` (`:194`) — returning null on 0 rows (`:198`). So the null means precisely **"the source is already archived"**, and the error message discards that: it doesn't say "already archived", doesn't distinguish it from anything else, and exits 4. Repeated attempts produce the identical opaque failure (archive is not idempotent), which is exactly what pushes an operator to the destructive `remove` instead — trading a soft archive for a hard cascade delete just to get an operation that reports what it did.

**What did you expect?**

`archive` on an already-archived source should say so — and arguably exit 0 (idempotent: the desired state holds). Any other 0-row outcome should be distinguished from it. The one-line shape: have `softDeleteSource` (or `runArchive`) check `archived` state when the guarded UPDATE hits 0 rows and report it.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `gbrain sources archive <id>` on any source → succeeds.
2. Run the same command again → `Failed to archive source "<id>".`, exit 4, no reason. (Matches the field observation where the first archive's outcome wasn't visible to the operator.)

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant (CLI diagnostics defect).

---

Related: #1175 (sources archive undocumented in --help — the surface is under-specified at both ends).
title: bug(sources): archive collapses already-archived into a reasonless "Failed to archive" — not idempotent, no diagnostic, pushes operators toward destructive remove

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

gbrain ships audio-transcription machinery, but none of it is reachable — a fresh operator designing from gbrain's own surfaces has NO audio ingestion path. Verified at HEAD (a25209bb):

- `src/core/transcription.ts` (Whisper via Groq/OpenAI, ffmpeg chunking) is imported by **tests only** — no CLI, daemon, or MCP path reaches it. The gateway's own `transcribe()` is a `NotMigratedYet('transcription')` stub (`src/core/ai/gateway.ts:3438`).
- `ingest_capture` rejects audio: `isText` allows only text/markdown/html/json (`src/core/minions/handlers/ingest-capture.ts:90-108`) and throws pointing at processor skillpacks ("e.g. gbrain-audio-transcribe") that don't exist. (The handler itself IS wired — `jobs.ts:1841`, `serve-http.ts:1925` — what's missing is any audio processor upstream of it.)
- `IngestionDaemon` (`src/core/ingestion/daemon.ts:164`) is constructed only in tests; its own header describes wiring that exists nowhere in production code.

Evidence this gap forces duplicate work: our deployment AND an independent from-scratch design exercise against v0.42.57's native surfaces both had to hand-build shape-identical audio lanes — drop-folder → external diarizing STT → transcript → the existing text ingest chain. When two independent designs converge on the same custom scaffolding, the recipe is writing itself.

**What does the solution look like?**

Wire the shipped transcription module into a reachable ingestion path — either `ingest_capture` accepts audio via a transcription processor, or a packaged audio recipe (drop-folder collector → transcription.ts → text ingest). The IngestionDaemon header's own design (MinionQueue → ingest_capture) is most of the plan.

**Alternatives considered**

Full custom audio sense deployed here (collector, diarizing STT call, fenced judgment subagent, deterministic apply leg, containment tiers). Happy to share the shape as a seed for the recipe — it has been in daily production since 2026-07-08.

---

Related: #1522 (ingest_capture provenance).

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(ingestion): ship the audio path — transcription.ts is unimported, gateway transcribe() is a NotMigratedYet stub, ingest_capture rejects audio, IngestionDaemon is test-only

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

The calendar-to-brain recipe's headline promise is meeting **prep**: "Your agent knows who you're meeting tomorrow, what you discussed last time, and what context matters" (`recipes/calendar-to-brain.md:37-39`), "When you have a meeting tomorrow, the agent pulls attendee dossiers automatically" (`:48`).

As shipped, that promise is structurally unfulfillable — every window the recipe prescribes ends at *today*:

- Backfill: `--start 2020-01-01 --end $(date +%Y-%m-%d)` (`:224`)
- Weekly cron: `--start $(date -v-7d +%Y-%m-%d) --end $(date +%Y-%m-%d)` (`:266-267`)
- No lookahead flag exists anywhere in the recipe or its prescribed collector design (repo-wide: no `--days-ahead`/lookahead in any calendar context).

Tomorrow's events are never fetched, so they can never be ingested — and the read side that WOULD surface them ("Upcoming within lookahead window", `src/core/context-engine.ts:436`) has nothing to read. The recipe's weekly-sync + daily-briefing cron pairing (`docs/guides/cron-schedule.md`) compounds it: even day-of events are up to a week stale.

**What does the solution look like?**

Add a lookahead parameter to the recipe spec (e.g. `--days-ahead 7`, `timeMax = today + N`), so day files cover upcoming events; note in the cron guidance that the lookahead is what feeds the daily briefing. One parameter closes the gap between the recipe's promise and its mechanics.

**Alternatives considered**

Local collector fetches a lookahead window (deployed — the daily briefing cites tomorrow's meetings from it). Every operator who reads the recipe's promise will have to rediscover and rebuild this.

---

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(recipes): calendar-to-brain lookahead window — recipe promises "knows who you're meeting tomorrow" but every prescribed window ends at today

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

Two credential-resolution defects in `integrations`, same family:

**1. `show`/`status` resolve secrets from `process.env` only.** `checkSecrets` (`src/commands/integrations.ts:460-471`) tests `process.env[s.name]` with no config-plane folding, and drives both `cmdShow` (`:611`) and `cmdStatus` (`:636`). The runtime reaches keys through `buildGatewayConfig`'s config.json→env folding — so credentials stored in `~/.gbrain/config.json` power a perfectly healthy sense while `integrations show/status` reports them `[missing]`. A dashboard that false-negatives on healthy senses trains operators to ignore it. (Same class as #2728 on the `providers` command; this is the `integrations` face.)

**2. The X secret name is pinned to a key the runtime never reads.** The recipe (`recipes/x-to-brain.md:9,:16` and its curl examples) and the features registry (`src/commands/features.ts:45`) pin `X_BEARER_TOKEN`. The actual consumer — the built-in `x_handle_to_tweet` resolver — reads config `x_api_bearer_token` or env `X_API_BEARER_TOKEN` with **no fallback** to the recipe's name (`src/core/resolvers/builtin/x-api/handle-to-tweet.ts:347-354`; `X_API_BEARER_TOKEN` is also the name in `docs/designs/KNOWLEDGE_RUNTIME.md:172` and the resolver tests). Follow the recipe → the resolver can't see your token. Set the name the code reads → `integrations show/status` flags a healthy sense `[missing]`. There is no name both sides accept.

**What did you expect?**

1. `integrations show/status` resolve secrets the way the runtime does — apply the same config.json→env folding `buildGatewayConfig` performs before declaring anything missing.
2. Recipe + `features.ts:45` renamed to the resolver's `X_API_BEARER_TOKEN` (or a fallback added in the resolver) so one name works end-to-end.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Store a working credential in `~/.gbrain/config.json` only → sense works, `integrations show` prints `[missing]`.
2. Set `X_BEARER_TOKEN` per the recipe → `x_handle_to_tweet` returns null token. Set `X_API_BEARER_TOKEN` instead → resolver works, integrations dashboard shows `[missing]`.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant (integrations-layer defect).

---

Related: #2728 (providers command bypasses gateway config resolution — same divergence, different command).
title: bug(integrations): show/status resolve secrets from process.env only (config-stored creds report [missing]); X recipe + features registry pin X_BEARER_TOKEN while the resolver reads only X_API_BEARER_TOKEN

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

When an operator declares a collector's output directory in `storage.db_only`, the ingestion path for that collector silently dies — with everything reporting green:

- `manageGitignore()` runs on every successful sync and auto-appends each db_only dir to `.gitignore` (`src/commands/sync.ts:4513`, `:4598-4610`).
- Git-walking sync diffs via `git diff --name-status` (`sync.ts:875,893,1810`), so gitignored files never appear in the diff and never reach the DB.
- At HEAD, `gbrain import` ALSO honors `.gitignore` inside a work tree (`import.ts:582-592`, `git ls-files --exclude-standard`) — so even a manual re-import skips the files. The trap has no floor.

The calendar-to-brain recipe prescribes writing day FILES to exactly such a directory (`recipes/calendar-to-brain.md:50-66` → `daily/calendar/{YYYY}/{date}.md`) and relies on `gbrain sync` to ingest them (Step 7, `:262-273`). On this deployment, `daily/calendar/` had been declared db_only in a storage cleanup — and **no calendar page reached the DB for seven weeks** (2026-05-19 → 2026-07-08) while the collector ran green the whole time.

No warning exists anywhere for this combination: the only db_only diagnostics at HEAD is the tier-overlap warning (`storage-config.ts:262`), and `integrations.ts` has zero awareness of storage tiers.

**What does the solution look like?**

A doctor check or sync-time warning when a configured recipe/collector's output path falls inside a db_only prefix — "collector X writes to `daily/calendar/` which is db_only: files there will never be ingested by sync or import." Recipes could declare their output dir in frontmatter so the check has ground truth.

**Alternatives considered**

Local fix (deployed): the calendar handler imports day files via `importFromFile` (hash-skip idempotent) on every run, bypassing the git walk. Works, and stays useful — but the silent-death class needs the native warning.

---

Related: #2784 (declared-db_only auditing — same storage-tier blind-spot family).

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(sync/doctor): warn when a collector's output dir is declared db_only — auto-gitignore makes sync AND import silently skip its files (7-week green-collector outage)

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

Recipe `health_checks` support only point-in-time types — `http`, `env_exists`, `command`, `any_of` (`src/commands/integrations.ts:108`, dispatch switch `:173-292`), plus a deprecated embedded-recipe string type. None is staleness-aware, and `gbrain integrations doctor` (`cmdDoctor`, `integrations.ts:687-708`) runs ONLY health_checks — it never reads an integration's heartbeat file (heartbeat age is read by `getStatus`/`cmdStatus`/`cmdList`/`cmdStats`, but never inside a health-check type and never in doctor).

Live consequence: a calendar sense that had been **16 days stale passed `integrations doctor` cleanly**. Its only health check (`recipes/calendar-to-brain.md:21-30`) is an `any_of` of `http GET <gateway>/health` OR `env_exists GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` — both can stay green forever while zero data flows. This is the sense silent-death mode: the collector runs green, the checks pass, and the brain quietly stops learning. It took this deployment's senses down for weeks in June before local tooling existed.

(`cmdStatus` does print a hardcoded "no events in N days" note at `integrations.ts:676`, but it is not a declarable check type and doctor never surfaces it — the ask is specifically a *declarable staleness type* that `doctor` enforces.)

**What does the solution look like?**

A `heartbeat_max_age` health-check type: the recipe declares its expected cadence (e.g. `max_age: 48h`), and `integrations doctor` reads the integration's heartbeat file age and fails past the threshold. Recipes that install crons already know their cadence — the declaration is one line.

**Alternatives considered**

A local sense-health script enforcing "silence > 2× cadence = BREACH" plus a daily alert cron (deployed here; it caught every subsequent stall). It works, but every operator rebuilding this same shim is exactly what a native check type should prevent.

---

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(integrations): heartbeat_max_age health-check type — doctor passes senses that have been dead for weeks (all shipped check types are point-in-time)

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

During cleanup of a legacy artifacts source that carried same-slug mirror copies of default-source pages, we found **19 `life/events/*` pages derived from meeting pages (whose canonical home is `default`) sitting under the LEGACY source** — and those wrong-source rows were the ONLY copies: db-only, no file backing, one `sources remove` away from silent data loss. Observed 2026-07-08 on v0.42.57.

Root cause, verified at HEAD (a25209bb) — and correcting our own initial diagnosis, so a maintainer doesn't have to: there is **no cross-source stamping bug inside the extractor.** `runChronicleExtract` (`src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:99-164`) reads the source page and writes every derived event strictly under `opts.sourceId` — the scope of the enqueue (`chronicle_backfill`: `operations.ts:5303`; put_page backstop: `chronicle/backstop.ts:33`), and page selection is source-scoped (`sourceScopeOpts`, `operations.ts:424`). The failure needs a precondition: **same-slug copies existing under two sources.** Then a backfill scoped to the mirror source derives a full, legitimate-looking set of `life/events/*` under that source — db-only, indistinguishable from canonical derivations, with no warning that the slugs it processed are mirrors of another source's canonical pages.

So the defect class is: **chronicle derivation has no awareness of the mirrored-slug condition that `multi_source_drift` already knows how to detect**, and its output placement (db-only, under whatever source was scoped) makes the resulting fork invisible until someone audits or deletes a source.

**What did you expect?**

Any of:
1. Derived pages carry provenance of the origin page's source (not just `event.depth` path), so a wrong-source fork is detectable and repairable;
2. `chronicle_backfill` warns (or skips) when the scoped source's page is a same-slug mirror of another source's page — the `multi_source_drift` detection logic already identifies exactly this condition;
3. At minimum, docs state that chronicle output is enqueue-source-scoped and that mirrored slugs will fork derived history under whichever source is backfilled.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Same slug under source A (canonical, file-backed) and source B (mirror copy).
2. `chronicle_backfill` scoped to B.
3. `life/events/*` derived under B, db-only — sole copies if A was never backfilled; removing B destroys them.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
`multi_source_drift` flags the mirror condition itself (that's how we found the mirrors) but nothing flags the derived-page fork.

---

Local remediation (one-time): chronicle-backfill re-derived the events at `default` from the canonical meeting pages, then the legacy source was removed.
title: bug(chronicle): backfill scoped to a source holding same-slug mirrors silently derives sole-copy db-only life/events pages under that source

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

`GBRAIN_PLUGIN_PATH` plugins can ship **subagent defs only**. Verified at HEAD (a25209bb): the plugin manifest has no handler field (`src/core/minions/plugin-loader.ts:39-45` — `name, version, plugin_version, subagents?, description?`), `loadSinglePlugin` reads only `subagents/*.md` (lines 155-229), and the trust-policy docstring is explicit (lines 21-26): plugins "cannot declare new tools." The job-handler registry is an in-process `Map` populated exclusively by hardcoded `worker.register(...)` calls (`worker.ts:153,230-236`; `src/commands/jobs.ts:1323-2114`).

Consequence: any deployment with deterministic collector/apply job handlers is forced into a **two-worker topology** — a custom worker process for site-local handlers, plus the autopilot's embedded child for builtins (including `subagent`). That means two supervised processes, two concurrency stories, ~2× the DB connections, and it is the exact seam where the subagent-capacity starvation lived (#2782 — the patterns silent no-op). An independent from-scratch design exercise against v0.42.57's native surfaces converged on the same forced shape: the one-worker design is foreclosed purely by this gap, not by anything architectural.

**What does the solution look like?**

Let plugins register custom job handlers that `gbrain jobs work` loads — the same conformance/trust pattern as subagent defs (manifest-declared, path-loaded, name-spaced). One worker then runs builtins + site-local handlers, with one concurrency budget and one supervision story.

**Alternatives considered**

A standing custom worker process (launchd, KeepAlive) registering collector/apply handlers, with its own test harness — deployed here and working, but it is a whole second supervised process that exists only because handlers can't ride the native worker. Consolidates to one worker the day handler plugins ship.

---

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(jobs): plugin-registered job handlers loadable by jobs work — plugins ship subagent defs only, forcing two-worker topologies

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

A full DB-vs-file sweep of 6,913 pages on this brain found **1,805 DB-only pages outside any declared db_only path** — all of it engine derive-phase output: `life/events/` 1,323, `atoms/` 433, `extracts/` 33, `dream-cycle-summaries/` 10. These are re-derivable machine output by upstream design (we ratified keeping them DB-only), but they sit **undeclared**: not git-backed, not in `storage.db_only` (which has no built-in defaults — `storage-config.ts:157,221-226` reads only what `gbrain.yml` declares, defaulting to `[]`), and therefore invisible to any file-lane backup/recovery reasoning. An operator auditing "what would survive a DB loss" gets a silently wrong answer.

The same sweep also caught 4 genuinely-missing pages (a local worker defect on our side) — a doctor check would have surfaced those in a day instead of via a user-facing miss weeks later.

Verified at HEAD (a25209bb): no doctor check compares DB rows against backing files — the check registry (`doctor-categories.ts`) has nothing in this class, and doctor's checks are deliberately "Strictly SQL — no filesystem/git access" (`doctor.ts:3090`), so this needs a new check that is allowed to stat the repo.

**What does the solution look like?**

1. **Doctor check:** a DB page outside every declared db_only path with no backing file in the source repo → warn (with a count + sample slugs, like existing checks).
2. **Ship the engine's own derive-phase output prefixes as declared-db_only defaults** (`life/events/`, `atoms/`, `extracts/`, `dream-cycle-summaries/`) so the declared list matches what the engine actually does, and the check above stays quiet on healthy brains.

Together these make the file-lane story auditable: everything is either file-backed, declared db_only, or flagged.

**Alternatives considered**

A hand-run read-only DB-vs-file sweep script (deployed locally; found the numbers above). Works, but it's exactly the kind of operator-side scaffolding a doctor check should replace.

---

Related: #2426 (db-only durability), #344 / #1856 (DB-only page/timeline → file drift family).

Environment: gbrain 0.42.57.0 (verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb) · macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode).
title: feat(doctor): warn on DB pages with no backing file outside declared db_only paths; ship derive-phase output prefixes (life/events, atoms, extracts, dream-cycle-summaries) as db_only defaults

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

`gbrain jobs retry` re-queues a dead job but never resets its wall-clock anchor, so any retry issued after the original window is claimed and instantly dead-lettered:

- `retryJob` (`src/core/minions/queue.ts:474-485`) resets `status/error_text/locks/delay/finished_at` — but **not** `started_at`, `attempts_made`, or `attempts_started`.
- On re-claim, `claim()` preserves the original first-claim time: `started_at = COALESCE(started_at, now())` (`queue.ts:620`) — the retry re-stamps `timeout_at` but inherits the old `started_at`.
- The wall-clock sweep `handleWallClockTimeouts()` (`queue.ts:729-749`) anchors on `started_at`: a retry claimed more than `timeout_ms * 2` after the ORIGINAL first claim immediately trips the check and is dead-lettered with `wall-clock timeout exceeded`, with `attempts_made` incrementing past max.

Observed 2026-07-11 on jobs #23954 / #24020 / #24042 / #24085 / #23990 — jobs killed by an API-credit outage, retried after the outage cleared: every retry died in **under 1 second**, displaying `attempt 4/3`. Retry is therefore useless for exactly the case it exists for: recovering work after an outage that lasted longer than the job's timeout.

**What did you expect?**

On explicit `jobs retry`: clear `started_at` (so re-claim re-stamps it) and reset `attempts_made`/`attempts_started`. An operator explicitly retrying a dead job is asserting "run this fresh."

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Create a job with `timeout_ms` T; let it die (e.g. worker down / provider outage).
2. Wait > 2×T.
3. `gbrain jobs retry <id>` → job is claimed, then dead-lettered `wall-clock timeout exceeded` in <1s with the attempt counter past max.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant (jobs-engine defect; job rows available on request).

---

Related: #1737 (wall-clock attempt accounting — this is the retry-side gap in the same machinery), #1014 (wall-clock cap tuning).

Operational note for anyone hitting this today: for slot-keyed collector work, do NOT hand-retry — the next slot's sweep with unburned idempotency keys re-covers the missed window naturally (verified here: missed digests self-covered on later slots).
title: bug(jobs): retry never resets started_at/attempts — the wall-clock sweep dead-letters any retry issued past the original window in under a second

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

In the stock topology, the autopilot's embedded child worker is the only subagent-capable worker and runs at **concurrency 1**: `jobs work` defaults to `'1'` (`src/commands/jobs.ts:83` — `parseFlag(args, '--concurrency') ?? env.GBRAIN_WORKER_CONCURRENCY ?? '1'`), and autopilot spawns it with no `--concurrency` flag (`autopilot.ts:396`). A running autopilot-cycle therefore occupies the sole slot — **the cycle's own patterns subagent can never be claimed.**

The phase then hides the failure. In `src/core/cycle/patterns.ts`, a child `TimeoutError` sets `outcome = 'timeout'` without throwing (lines 99-102), and the phase returns `ok(...)` — which hardcodes `status: 'ok'` (patterns.ts:324-325) — with `child_outcome: 'timeout'`, `patterns_written: 0`. Every "completed" cycle's patterns phase reported ok while producing nothing, for days. Verified on jobs #23745/#23747; live-reproduced: subagent job #24262 sat WAITING through the entirety of cycle #24261.

Doctor cannot see it either: `computeAutopilotFanoutConcurrencyCheck` (`doctor.ts:1604-1637`) compares the autopilot per-source cycle fan-out against worker concurrency, reserving one slot (`effectiveSlots = max(1, concurrency - 1)`), but does not model that each RUNNING cycle additionally needs a free slot for its patterns subagent. At concurrency 1 with fan-out 1: `1 > max(1, 0)` is false → reports ok — the exact starvation case. (And when no supervisor audit event exists, the check skips entirely.)

**What did you expect?**

1. A subagent-awaiting phase should **fail loudly or run inline** when no free subagent-capable worker slot exists — never report `status: ok` on a timed-out child that wrote nothing. Silent no-op phases are the worst outage class: everything looks green while a whole judgment layer is dead.
2. The doctor fan-out check should account for **cycle-spawned subagent capacity** — including the autopilot's own embedded worker concurrency — not just cycle fan-out with a single reserved slot.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Default install (embedded worker at concurrency 1) on a brain where cycles run for minutes.
2. Let autopilot dispatch a cycle; watch the patterns child job sit WAITING while its parent occupies the only slot.
3. Observe the cycle complete with `status: ok`, `child_outcome: timeout`, `patterns_written: 0`, and `gbrain doctor` fan-out check green.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Fan-out check reports ok in the failing topology (that's defect 2); excerpts on request.

---

Related: #1737 (long-job starvation family), #2278 (patterns silent skip), #2194 (fan-out clamp + doctor check, v0.42.52), #2781 (cycle budget — the two interact: short budgets kill cycles that DO get a slot; slot starvation silently no-ops the ones that survive).

Local workaround: `GBRAIN_WORKER_CONCURRENCY=2` exported in the autopilot run script so the cycle's judgment child can run — an interim value pending guidance on weighing the native supervisor default against the DB connection budget.
title: bug(cycle): patterns phase reports status:ok with child_outcome:timeout and writes nothing when no subagent-capable worker slot is free — silent no-op for days; doctor's fan-out check can't see cycle-spawned subagent capacity

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

Autopilot stamps `timeout_ms = Math.max(baseInterval * 2 * 1000, 300_000)` on every autopilot-cycle job (`src/commands/autopilot.ts:641`, threaded through `autopilot-fanout.ts:399`/`:452`), and an explicit stamp always beats the handler default (`queue.ts:285` — `opts?.timeout_ms ?? defaultTimeoutMsFor(jobName)`; `handler-timeouts.ts:18-19` even documents "An explicit `opts.timeout_ms` always wins"). Consequence: the engine's own `THIRTY_MIN_MS` anchor for `autopilot-cycle` (`handler-timeouts.ts:26,37` — the #1737 fix, shipped v0.42.29.0) **never applies**. At the default 300s interval, cycles get a 600s budget.

On a ~7k-page brain this dead-letters every substantive cycle. Observed 2026-07-08: **38 dead cycles/24h**, cycle_freshness FAIL at 40h, extraction backlogs ballooning — the exact thrash #1737 was written to prevent.

Escalation, and the deeper shape of the bug: even 1800s budgets were insufficient — 30 more dead cycles, **every one aborted mid-`patterns`** — because the patterns phase submits its subagent with a hardcoded 30-min job timeout and waits up to 35 min (`src/core/cycle/patterns.ts:86,95`). One phase's worst case exceeds ANY interval-derived budget ≤ 35 min, so the six tail phases (consolidate → schema-suggest) starved for days. Real full cycles at a corrected budget measured 2502s (job #23745) and 2355s (#24261) — i.e. the native default, and even a "reasonable" 900s interval, are lethal at this scale.

**What did you expect?**

- Floor the stamped cycle timeout at the handler default (min 30 min), or add an `autopilot.cycle_timeout_ms` config knob decoupled from `--interval`.
- Sharper fix: budget `patterns` (and any phase that awaits a subagent) from **remaining cycle time**, not a hardcoded constant — a fixed 35-min phase worst-case inside an interval-derived budget can never be safe at every interval.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Brain large enough that a full cycle takes >10 min (ours: ~7k pages). Autopilot at default `--interval 300`.
2. Observe autopilot-cycle jobs created with `timeout_ms: 600000` (stamp at autopilot.ts:641) instead of the 30-min handler anchor.
3. Observe cycles dead-letter mid-patterns; consolidate → schema-suggest never run; cycle_freshness degrades while autopilot logs look busy.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
`cycle_freshness` FAIL at 40h during the incident window; happy to paste the excerpt on request.

---

Related: #1737 (this stamp defeats its shipped anchor), #1594 (hardcoded-subagent-timeout family — patterns.ts:86 is another member).

Local workaround: `--interval 1800` hand-edited into the generated run script (→ 3600s stamped budgets). Note the installer does not persist this (filed separately).
title: bug(autopilot): interval-derived cycle timeout stamp overrides the #1737 handler anchor — 600s budgets at default interval dead-letter every substantive cycle at scale; patterns' 35-min worst case exceeds any interval-derived budget

body:
**What happened?**

Deployment context for this batch of reports: see #2778 (same production operation, environment, and receipts discipline).

chronicle_backfill / chronicle_extract swept a page under the operator's `GBRAIN_SEARCH_EXCLUDE` prefix (sensitive third-party HR content under a zero-propagation hold) and wrote **8 derived `life/events/*` pages OUTSIDE the excluded prefix** — searchable once embedded, defeating the exclusion. Observed 2026-07-08, job #23157, `events_written=8`. Each derived page carries `event.depth: <excluded-page-path>` frontmatter (`extract-events.ts:150-153`), which is what proves the leak path.

Verified at HEAD (a25209bb): `GBRAIN_SEARCH_EXCLUDE` is consumed exclusively in the search-ranking SQL (`source-boost.ts` `resolveHardExcludes` → `postgres-engine.ts:1568/1717/1838`). None of the derive phases apply ANY exclusion when selecting source pages:

- **chronicle** — `operations.ts:5293-5294`, `listPages` by type, no prefix filter
- **facts** — `extract-facts.ts:176-185`, `getAllSlugs()` = every page in the brain
- **atoms** — `extract-atoms.ts:166-195`, raw SELECT with a `dream_generated` guard but no prefix exclusion
- **takes** — `extract-takes.ts:197-199`, `listAllPageRefs()` = all live pages

Derived output lands under `life/events/` and `atoms/` — outside any excluded prefix, embedded and searchable.

To pre-empt a fair objection: `GBRAIN_SEARCH_EXCLUDE` is documented as a search-ranking knob (source-boost.ts:12-13), so the derive phases are not violating its stated contract. The defect is that **no containment mechanism exists that derivation honors**: content an operator has excluded from search gets re-materialized by the engine as new, searchable pages outside the excluded prefix. Cleanup is not a fix — nothing prevents recurrence on the next derive pass, so this is an ongoing privacy-risk class.

**What did you expect?**

Derive phases (chronicle, facts, atoms, takes) to honor a containment prefix. Either:
- skip pages under excluded prefixes by default (extend `GBRAIN_SEARCH_EXCLUDE` to gate derivation), or
- file derived output under the source page's prefix (so exclusion follows the content), or
- add a dedicated containment/privacy prefix that both search AND derivation respect.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `GBRAIN_SEARCH_EXCLUDE=private/`; create a meeting-type page under `private/`.
2. Run `chronicle_backfill` (or trigger the put_page backstop on that page).
3. Observe `life/events/*` pages derived from the private page, outside `private/`, carrying `event.depth: private/…` — searchable once embedded.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 · Bun 1.3.13 · Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant (no doctor check covers this; that absence is part of the point).

---

Local mitigation: derived pages enumerated via the `event.depth` marker and queued for operator-approved deletion after each pass — a treadmill, not a fix.
title: bug(privacy): derive phases (chronicle/facts/atoms/takes) re-materialize excluded-prefix pages as searchable pages outside the prefix — no containment mechanism gates derivation

body:
**What happened?**

> Deployment context (applies to a batch of reports filed today, all from the same production operation): ~1 week of intensive operation (2026-07-06 → 2026-07-12) of a ~7,000-page brain (6,907 indexed pages / 8,945 chunks / 100% embedded, single source) on a Mac mini against Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer transaction mode). Full native surface plus a thin custom layer: `gbrain autopilot` (launchd, KeepAlive), Minions with five sense collectors dispatching idempotency-keyed jobs, fenced triage/apply subagents from plugin defs, remote MCP over a cloudflared tunnel, hourly git backup lane. Every report was hit in real operation, has receipts (job numbers, log lines, source line numbers), and most have a local workaround deployed whose retirement is the point of filing. All source references verified against v0.42.58.0 (a25209bb).

Three interacting defects in the fenced subagent write path make it impossible for a fenced subagent to update any page beyond ~12KB:

1. **The allowlist omits gbrain's own canonical timeline op.** `BRAIN_TOOL_ALLOWLIST` (`src/core/minions/tools/brain-allowlist.ts:48-71`) includes `put_page` but not `add_timeline_entry` — with no justifying comment (the only write-exclusion comment in the file, lines 57-59, argues for excluding LINK writes specifically). Yet the compiled-truth guide (`docs/guides/compiled-truth.md:71-88`) prescribes `add_timeline_entry` for ALL timeline appends ("timeline is NOT passed" to put — put is for compiled-truth rewrites only), and `engine.addTimelineEntry` is an O(entry) idempotent insert — `ON CONFLICT (page_id, date, summary, source) DO NOTHING` (`postgres-engine.ts:3387-3392`) — that works at any page size. The allowlist blocks the canonical surface and leaves full-page-echo `put_page` as the only write path.

2. **The default subagent loop caps every turn at `max_tokens: 4096`** (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts:483`, the default/legacy path — the gateway loop is opt-in per lines 186-187).

3. **Truncated tool_use is not recovered.** The loop never reads `stop_reason`; tool_use blocks from a turn cut off at max_tokens are dispatched anyway (`toolDef.execute(use.input, ...)`, subagent.ts:648), so tools receive undefined/partial args silently. The file header acknowledges this: `NOT in v0.15: refusal detection, stop_reason=max_tokens partial` (subagent.ts:20).

Consequence, with receipts: job #22761 burned **26 turns / 2.8M input tokens** on 15 `put_page` calls that ALL arrived with `content` undefined against 18–21KB pages (body-append + full-page echo cannot fit a >12KB page inside a 4096-token turn). Second face of the same cap: a newer model's thinking blocks overflow the turn and return EMPTY verdicts (job #23357), forcing an older-model pin on the fenced lane. The "~12KB page cap" operators may report is not a page limit — it is the emergent artifact of these three defects.

**What did you expect?**

1. `add_timeline_entry` allowlisted for fenced subagents — it is the doctrine-prescribed append op and strictly safer than the full-echo `put_page` that IS allowlisted (idempotent, O(entry), no page-size exposure).
2. The per-turn `max_tokens` raised or made configurable.
3. Truncated tool_use to fail loudly instead of silently passing undefined args to tools.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Run any fenced subagent def against a page >12KB and instruct it to record a dated update.
2. Observe it cannot use `add_timeline_entry` (not allowlisted) and falls back to `put_page`, echoing the full page.
3. Observe the 4096-token turn truncate mid-tool-call and the tool execute anyway with `content` undefined — no error raised, turn burned; the loop retries into the same wall.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.57.0 (all source refs re-verified unchanged at 0.42.58.0 / a25209bb)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 (Mac mini)
- Bun version: 1.3.13
- Database: Supabase Postgres (PgBouncer, transaction mode)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
Not relevant to this defect (verified in source + job transcripts); excerpts of the burned jobs available on request.

---

Related: #1974 (subagent tool registry too narrow), #1594 (hardcoded subagent timeout silently drops output), #2606 (output truncated at maxTokens silently recorded as empty — same fail-loud class as defect 3).

Local workarounds deployed, all of which retire on a fix: LARGE-PAGE LIMIT rules in plugin subagent defs (report blocked writes instead of retrying against physics), a daily sweep surfacing blocked writes for hand-application, a truncation-retry lane, and the older-model pin.
title: bug(subagent): fenced allowlist omits add_timeline_entry (the canonical timeline op); 4096-token turn cap + unrecovered tool_use truncation make pages >~12KB unwritable

body:
**Version:** v0.42.58.0 (also affects every 0.42.x that includes migration v121 / Life Chronicle #2390)
**Engine:** PGLite
**Impact:** Any existing PGLite brain whose `config.version` (schema) is **< 121** cannot be upgraded via the documented path. `gbrain init --migrate-only` and `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` both fail to advance the schema, so the brain is stuck on the old version and every normal command that calls `initSchema` errors out.

## Symptom

```
$ gbrain init --migrate-only
column "event_page_id" does not exist
# exit 1
```

`gbrain doctor` reports e.g. `schema_version: Version 97, latest is 122. Fix: gbrain apply-migrations --yes`, but `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` prints `All migrations up to date.` and does nothing.

## Root cause — schema-blob-before-migration ordering

In `src/core/pglite-engine.ts`, `initSchema()` (≈ line 380) runs in this order:

```
async initSchema() {
  ...
  await this.applyForwardReferenceBootstrap();     // line ~390
  ...
  await this.db.exec(getPGLiteSchema(dims, model)); // line ~406  <-- fails here
  const { applied } = await runMigrations(this);    // line ~408
}
```

- The embedded schema blob `getPGLiteSchema()` (`src/core/pglite-schema.ts` line 387) contains:
  ```sql
  CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page
    ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
  ```
- `timeline_entries.event_page_id` is a **v121** column. The migration that adds it, `migrate.ts` `version: 121` (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`, ≈ line 5443), runs in `runMigrations()` — i.e. **after** the blob.
- On a **fresh** install the blob's `CREATE TABLE ... timeline_entries` includes `event_page_id`, so the index build works. On an **existing** brain the `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` is a no-op (table already exists without the column), so the `CREATE INDEX ... (event_page_id)` dies before `runMigrations` can add the column.

`applyForwardReferenceBootstrap()` (`pglite-engine.ts` lines 438–898) exists precisely to pre-create columns the blob forward-references on older brains — it probes and `ALTER ... ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` for ~35 columns (`source_id`, `deleted_at`, `link_source`, `origin_page_id`, `search_vector`, `embedding_image`, `effective_date`, `links_extracted_at`, …). **`event_page_id` is missing from that list** (grep confirms: zero occurrences of `event_page_id` in lines 438–898). That's the gap.

## Suggested fix (either works)

**Option A (matches existing pattern, minimal):** add `event_page_id` to `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` — a probe in the big EXISTS query plus:
```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
```
(The FK + partial indexes can stay in migration v121, which is already idempotent.)

**Option B (structural):** run `runMigrations()` *before* `this.db.exec(getPGLiteSchema())` in `initSchema()`, so incremental ALTERs land before the blob re-asserts indexes. (Riskier — the blob also creates base tables that some migrations assume exist, so ordering would need care. Option A is safer.)

## Workaround (for anyone stuck now)

Call the numbered migrations directly, bypassing the blob-first path. They apply in version order, so v121 adds the column at the right time:

```js
import { createEngine } from './src/core/engine-factory.ts'
import { loadConfig, toEngineConfig } from './src/core/config.ts'
import { runMigrations } from './src/core/migrate.ts'
const cfg = loadConfig();
const engine = await createEngine(toEngineConfig(cfg));
await engine.connect(toEngineConfig(cfg));
console.log(await runMigrations(engine));  // applies v98..v122 in order
await engine.disconnect();
```
After this the schema is at head; subsequent normal opens (which run the blob) succeed because `event_page_id` now exists.

## Repro

1. Any PGLite brain created before v121 (schema `config.version` < 121, e.g. 97).
2. Update gbrain to ≥ 0.42.x that contains migration v121.
3. `gbrain init --migrate-only` → `column "event_page_id" does not exist`.

## Environment
- gbrain 0.42.58.0 (bun/TypeScript, symlinked source install)
- PGLite (`@electric-sql/pglite`), bun 1.3.14, Linux
- Brain: ~6,900 pages, was at schema 97
title: gbrain init --migrate-only fails on pre-v121 PGLite brains: column "event_page_id" does not exist

body:
## Reproduction

On macOS with the current upstream HEAD (`a25209bbb2bacf1b88e06fd5282b27f1bf4a3e7a`, `gbrain 0.42.58.0`):

```bash
gbrain --version
# gbrain 0.42.58.0

gbrain book-mirror --help
# Usage: gbrain book-mirror
#
# gbrain book-mirror - run gbrain --help for the full command list.
```

The same result occurs when invoking the installed entrypoint directly with `bun run src/cli.ts book-mirror --help`.

## Expected

The Book Mirror help text from `src/commands/book-mirror.ts`, then normal execution via:

```bash
gbrain book-mirror --chapters-dir <path> --context-file <path> --slug <slug>
```

This matches the v0.25.1 changelog and the bundled `skills/book-mirror/SKILL.md`.

## Diagnosis

The implementation and downstream switch both exist:

- `src/commands/book-mirror.ts` exports the command implementation.
- `src/cli.ts` has `case 'book-mirror'` at ~1654.

But top-level dispatch checks `CLI_ONLY`, then `cliOps`/`cliAliases`, before reaching that later switch. `book-mirror` is not registered in `CLI_ONLY`, `cliOps`, or `cliAliases`, so it exits through the generic unknown-command path and the handler is unreachable.

`gbrain skillpack scaffold book-mirror --dry-run` copies the skill and shared conventions only (`pairedSourcesWritten: 0`), so scaffolding does not provide an alternate runnable command.

## Requested fix

Route `book-mirror` through the engine-backed command dispatcher before the shared-operation lookup, add a regression test for `gbrain book-mirror --help`, and release a tagged fix.

I am deliberately avoiding a local edit to the global install so that upgrades remain reproducible.
title: bug(cli): book-mirror dispatcher is unreachable in v0.42.58.0

body:
**Version:** gbrain 0.42.53.0 (mechanism dates to the v0.31 facts layer / v0.32.2 fence migration; still present on current)

## Summary

On a brain whose active source has **no `local_path`** (thin-client / DB-only install), every fact written through the real-time backstop (`mcp:put_page`, `mcp:extract_facts`, etc.) that resolves to an `entity_slug` is inserted with **`row_num = NULL`** and **`source_markdown_slug = NULL`**.

The `extract_facts` cycle phase has an empty-fence guard that refuses to run whenever any row matches `row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL`, on the assumption that such rows are v0.31 legacy facts pending the `v0_32_2` fence backfill. But on a `local_path`-less source these rows are **not legacy and can never be backfilled** — so the guard trips **permanently**, and the count **grows with every `put_page`**. `extract_facts` is effectively disabled for the life of the brain.

The recommended remedy (`gbrain apply-migrations --yes`, emitted in the guard's own hint) does nothing: `v0_32_2` is already ledgered as applied, and even a forced re-run skips these rows.

## Root cause (three cooperating spots)

1. **Write path** — `src/core/facts/backstop.ts`, `runPipelineWithBody` Phase 4 (the `localPath === null` branch). Parented facts fall through to single-row `engine.insertFact`, which inserts **neither `row_num` nor `source_markdown_slug`** (verified in `postgres-engine.ts` `insertFact` and `pglite-engine.ts` `insertFact` — both omit those columns → they default `NULL`).

2. **Guard** — `src/core/cycle/extract-facts.ts`:
   ```sql
   SELECT COUNT(*) FROM facts WHERE row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL
   ```
   `> 0` → returns early with `guardTriggered`, warning: *"N legacy v0.31 fact rows pending fence backfill. Run `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` …"*. The predicate cannot distinguish genuine v0.31 legacy rows from live DB-only-source writes.

3. **Migration** — `src/commands/migrations/v0_32_2.ts`, Phase B:
   ```ts
   const localPath = localPathById.get(row.source_id);
   if (!localPath) { outcome.skipped_no_local_path += 1; continue; }
   ```
   Rows on a `local_path`-less source are counted as `skipped_no_local_path` and the phase still returns **`status: 'complete'`**. So the migration is ledgered as successfully applied while leaving guard-tripping rows behind, and `apply-migrations` reports "up to date." The guard and the migration disagree on what "done" means.

## Minimal repro

```
# 1. Source with no local_path (DB-only / thin-client)
gbrain sources add db-only-source          # no --local-path

# 2. Write a page/fact via the put_page backstop that resolves an entity
#    (any people/* or companies/* fact), targeting that source
gbrain call put_page '{"slug":"people/jane-doe","type":"person","body":"Jane Doe founded Acme."}'

# 3. The fact lands with row_num NULL:
psql "$DB" -c "SELECT row_num, source_markdown_slug, entity_slug, source
               FROM facts WHERE entity_slug='people/jane-doe';"
#  row_num | source_markdown_slug | entity_slug      | source
#  --------+----------------------+------------------+--------------
#  (null)  | (null)               | people/jane-doe  | mcp:put_page

# 4. extract_facts refuses to run:
gbrain dream --phase extract_facts
#  -> skipped/warn: "1 legacy v0.31 fact rows pending fence backfill.
#     Run `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` ..."

# 5. The recommended fix is a no-op:
gbrain apply-migrations --yes
#  -> all migrations up to date

# Guard never clears; count grows with each subsequent put_page.
```

## Impact

- `extract_facts` (fence → DB reconcile) never runs on affected brains — silently disabled.
- The operator hint is a dead-end loop (`apply-migrations` can't fix it).
- Observed live: count climbed 1126 → 1301 → 1395 over ~9 days of normal `put_page` activity on one DB-only source.

## Remediation options

**Option A — fix the write path (recommended).** In `backstop.ts` Phase 4, when `localPath === null` and the fact has a resolved `entity_slug`, assign a `row_num` so it never enters the guard set — while **leaving `source_markdown_slug` NULL**. The NULL slug is load-bearing: `deleteFactsForPage` deletes `WHERE source_markdown_slug = slug`, so NULL-slug rows stay invisible to the fence reconcile and are never deleted. One-file change; no engine-signature or `NewFact` changes required (uses `engine.executeRaw` for a per-source `row_num` stamp). Full diff available.

**Option B — narrow the guard predicate.** Scope the guard in `extract-facts.ts` to genuine legacy rows only, e.g. add `AND source NOT LIKE 'mcp:%' AND source NOT LIKE 'cli:%'`, or exclude rows whose `source_id` has no `local_path`. Smaller, but leaves the underlying `row_num IS NULL` rows in place.

Either way, `v0_32_2` Phase B arguably should not report `complete` when it leaves `skipped_no_local_path > 0` rows that the guard will then treat as "migration pending" — the migration/guard contract should agree on the DB-only case.

## Workaround (no code change)

Idempotent nightly pre-step before `gbrain dream`, scoped exactly to the guard set, leaving `source_markdown_slug` NULL (reconcile-safe):

```sql
UPDATE facts f SET row_num = n.rn
FROM (
  SELECT id, (row_number() OVER (ORDER BY id)) - 1 AS rn
  FROM facts WHERE row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL
) n
WHERE f.id = n.id;
```
This clears the guard each night; autopilot cycles between resets still show the (cosmetic) skip warning.
title: extract_facts cycle phase permanently blocked on sources without `local_path` (put_page facts land `row_num IS NULL`, and `apply-migrations` reports up-to-date)

body:
### Problem

`gbrain sources list` shows each source's id, name, page count, and federation flag, but nothing about **what the source is for**. Once a brain has several isolated (non-default) sources, an operator or an agent deciding which source to query has no metadata to go on. The source id/name alone is often ambiguous, e.g. telling a synthesized-knowledge layer apart from raw conversation/turn history, or one domain's synthesis from another's.

Isolated sources have to be targeted explicitly by name (a plain unscoped search skips them), but there is no place to record what a source contains, when it should be queried, and how. Agents therefore either query the wrong source or fall back to the default and miss everything isolated.

### Proposal

An optional per-source `description` string:

- Stored in the existing per-source **config JSONB** — no schema migration required.
- Set via a new subcommand, e.g. `gbrain sources describe <id> "<what it contains / when to query it>"`.
- Surfaced in `gbrain sources list` (and `sources list --json`) alongside each source, so the choice of source becomes self-documenting.

This keeps the routing decision data-driven (each source describes its own purpose) instead of relying on out-of-band tribal knowledge about what each source id means.

### Implementation note

I implemented this locally against **v0.42.56.0** as a small change to `src/commands/sources.ts`: a `runDescribe` handler, a `description` field on the list entry, dispatch wiring, and list/`--json` rendering. Existing `sources` tests pass. Happy to open a PR if you'd take it.
title: Feature: per-source description field, surfaced in `sources list`
putPage leaves a soft-deleted page hidden while reporting a successful write

body:
## Summary

At commit `a25209bbb2bacf1b88e06fd5282b27f1bf4a3e7a` (currently `upstream/HEAD`), `skills/manifest.json` declares `"resolver": "RESOLVER.md"`, but `gbrain skillpack scaffold --all` does not export `skills/RESOLVER.md` to the target workspace. The bundle also has no documented canonical export for that resolver.

## Minimal reproduction

1. Start with an empty workspace.
2. Run `gbrain skillpack scaffold --all --workspace <empty-workspace> --trust`.
3. Check for `<empty-workspace>/skills/RESOLVER.md`. It is absent.
4. Compare with the source manifest, which declares `RESOLVER.md` as the resolver.

## Expected

A scaffolded stock skill system should have a supported, portable routing surface. The command should either:

- export `skills/RESOLVER.md` (and any required manifest) as managed scaffold output; or
- provide a documented, machine-readable canonical resolver export/lookup mechanism that downstream hosts can use without copying source files manually.

## Actual

The skill files are scaffolded, but the stock dispatcher that defines their trigger routing is absent. Consumers must invent a host-local fallback or reference a separate checkout, neither of which is a portable bundle contract.

## Impact

Host integrations cannot reproduce stock routing from the canonical scaffold alone, and local workarounds can drift from the pinned bundle.
title: skillpack scaffold does not export the stock resolver declared by skills/manifest.json

body:
## Summary

At commit `a25209bbb2bacf1b88e06fd5282b27f1bf4a3e7a` (currently `upstream/HEAD`), the stock resolver routes users to `frontmatter-guard` and `smoke-test`, but `openclaw.plugin.json` does not include either directory in its `skills` bundle list. A consumer that scaffolds the bundle can therefore receive resolver routes to skill files that were not installed.

## Evidence

- `skills/RESOLVER.md` routes frontmatter validation to `skills/frontmatter-guard/SKILL.md` and restart health checks to `skills/smoke-test/SKILL.md`.
- `skills/manifest.json` declares both skills.
- `openclaw.plugin.json#skills` includes neither `skills/frontmatter-guard` nor `skills/smoke-test`.

## Minimal reproduction

1. Start with an empty workspace.
2. Run `gbrain skillpack scaffold --all --workspace <empty-workspace> --trust`.
3. Confirm that `<empty-workspace>/skills/frontmatter-guard/SKILL.md` and `<empty-workspace>/skills/smoke-test/SKILL.md` are absent.
4. Read the stock `skills/RESOLVER.md` and route a matching request. Both target paths are missing from the scaffolded workspace.

## Expected

Every skill referenced by the stock resolver should either be included by the canonical `--all` scaffold bundle, or the resolver/scaffold command should emit an explicit supported exception with a canonical retrieval path.

## Actual

The resolver references two absent skill directories without an installation-time warning or supported workspace-local fallback.

## Suggested fix

Add the two directories to the bundled skill list, or make the bundle/resolver contract explicit and machine-checkable (including a warning or canonical external reference for intentionally excluded skills).

## Impact

Downstream routing can select a missing implementation, making an otherwise valid stock intent fail at runtime.
title: skillpack scaffold omits skills referenced by the stock resolver

body:
## Summary `extract-conversation-facts` can never run on iMessage exports, even though gbrain's own imessage-sync integration produces them. Three independent gaps stack up: 1. **Type allowlist omits iMessage.** `ALLOWED_TYPES` in `src/commands/extract-conversation-facts.ts` is `['conversation', 'meeting', 'slack', 'email']` — the `imessage` / `imessage-daily` types the integration writes are excluded, so extraction never considers those pages at all. 2. **No pattern for the time-only 12-hour message shape.** iMessage exports produce `**Speaker** (9:04 AM): text` — bold speaker, paren-wrapped 12h wall-clock time **without a date**, colon outside the bold. This falls in the gap between the `imessage-slack` builtin (requires a full `YYYY-MM-DD` inside the parens) and `bold-paren-time` (24h only, rejects an AM/PM suffix). Result: `no_match` on every line. 3. **Multi-day exports anchor every message to one fallback date.** iMessage pages commonly carry a frontmatter `date_range` plus per-day `## YYYY-MM-DD` section headers. The parser ignores markdown date headers, so every message in a multi-day page gets the single frontmatter fallback date — and facts inherit extraction-time `valid_from` instead of the conversation's real time. ## Why it matters iMessage is a first-party source (imessage-sync ships with gbrain), and conversation-facts is the feature that makes conversational sources queryable. Together these gaps mean the entire iMessage corpus silently contributes zero facts — no error, no warning, just `no_match`/skip. ## Tested fix (running in production since 2026-06-01) We've been running the three patches below on a ~43K-page Postgres brain since June 1 (currently v0.42.58.0), reapplied across every upgrade. Conversation-facts extraction on iMessage pages works end-to-end with them; the new pattern's test_negative fixtures pin the non-shadowing guarantees (24h shapes still fall through to `bold-paren-time`, full-date shapes to `imessage-slack`). (Test fixtures below are sanitized — phone numbers and message text are placeholders.)
Patch 1 — builtins.ts: bold-paren-time-12h PatternEntry ```diff --- a/src/core/conversation-parser/builtins.ts +++ b/src/core/conversation-parser/builtins.ts @@ -176,6 +176,54 @@ ], source_doc: 'OpenClaw meeting-ingestion pipeline reformat of Circleback transcripts (see your OpenClaw skills/meeting-ingestion/SKILL.md)', + }, + + { + // time-only 12-hour AM/PM variant of bold-paren-time. Matches the shape + // imessage-sync exports produce: `**Me** (9:04 AM): text` — bold speaker, + // paren-wrapped 12-hour wall-clock time WITHOUT a date, colon outside the + // bold. Falls in the gap between imessage-slack (requires a full + // `YYYY-MM-DD` date inside the parens) and bold-paren-time (24h only, + // rejects an AM/PM suffix before the closing paren). + // + // NON-SHADOW GUARANTEE: the (AM|PM) group is REQUIRED, so 24h shapes + // `(00:00)` still fall through to bold-paren-time, and imessage-slack's + // full-date shape never matches here (the leading date digits break the + // `(\d{1,2}):` anchor right after the open paren). + id: 'bold-paren-time-12h', + origin: 'builtin', + regex: /^\*\*(.+?)\*\*\s*\((\d{1,2}):(\d{2})\s*(AM|PM|am|pm)\)\s*:\s*(.*)$/, + captures: { + speaker_group: 1, + hour_group: 2, + minute_group: 3, + ampm_group: 4, + text_group: 5, + }, + date_source: 'frontmatter', + time_format: '12h_ampm', + timezone_policy: 'utc_assumed_with_warn', + multi_line: false, + quick_reject: /^\*\*/, + test_positive: [ + '**Me** (9:04 AM): sounds good, see you then', + '**+15555550135** (9:39 AM): Will do', + '**Alice Example** (12:00 PM): noon message', + '**Bob Example** (5:38 pm): lowercase ampm', + ], + test_negative: [ + // bold-paren-time 24h shape MUST fall through to bold-paren-time: + '**Alice** (00:00): 24h shape', + // imessage-slack full-date shape MUST fall through to imessage-slack: + '**Alice Example** (2024-03-15 9:00 AM): full-date iMessage shape', + // telegram-bracket shape: + '**[18:37] G T:** telegram bracket', + // No bold markers: + 'Alice (9:00 AM): missing the bold', + ], + source_doc: + 'Time-only 12h AM/PM iMessage export shape: `**Speaker** (H:MM AM): text` (no date in parens; date from frontmatter)', }, { ```
Patch 2 — parse.ts: ## YYYY-MM-DD section headers update the running date anchor ```diff --- a/src/core/conversation-parser/parse.ts +++ b/src/core/conversation-parser/parse.ts @@ -321,10 +321,26 @@ if (!body) return []; const out: MatchedMessage[] = []; const lines = body.split(/\r?\n/); + // markdown date headers (`## 2020-03-25`) update the running date anchor so + // multi-day exports (e.g. iMessage pages with a frontmatter date_range and + // per-day `## YYYY-MM-DD` sections) get per-day timestamps instead of + // anchoring every message to one fallback date. The running context is a + // local copy so candidate-scoring passes never mutate the caller's ctx. + const runningCtx: DateContext = { ...dateCtx }; + const DATE_HEADER_RE = /^#{1,4}\s+(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})\s*$/; for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) { const rawLine = lines[i]; const line = rawLine.trim(); if (!line) continue; + + // date-header lines update the running anchor and are + // consumed (never treated as message continuations). + const dateHeader = DATE_HEADER_RE.exec(line); + if (dateHeader) { + runningCtx.fallbackDate = dateHeader[1]; + continue; + } // Quick-reject fast path. if (entry.quick_reject && !entry.quick_reject.test(line)) { @@ -339,7 +355,7 @@ const m = entry.regex.exec(line); if (m) { - const iso = buildIso(m, entry, dateCtx); + const iso = buildIso(m, entry, runningCtx); if (iso === null) continue; // reconstruction failed; skip line const rawSpeaker = m[entry.captures.speaker_group] ?? ''; const speaker = cleanSpeaker(rawSpeaker, entry.speaker_clean); ```
