Patch 3 — extract-conversation-facts.ts: allow imessage types + anchor valid_from to segment time ```diff --- a/src/commands/extract-conversation-facts.ts +++ b/src/commands/extract-conversation-facts.ts @@ -140,7 +140,11 @@ * `--types` flag is an explicit per-run override; cycle config is * the single source of truth. */ -export const ALLOWED_TYPES = ['conversation', 'meeting', 'slack', 'email'] as const; +// upstream's allowlist omits the 'imessage' / 'imessage-daily' types that +// gbrain's own imessage-sync integration produces, so conversation-facts +// extraction can never run on iMessage pages without this. +export const ALLOWED_TYPES = ['conversation', 'meeting', 'slack', 'email', 'imessage', 'imessage-daily'] as const; export type AllowedType = (typeof ALLOWED_TYPES)[number]; /** @@ -765,6 +769,14 @@ source_markdown_slug: page.slug, source: PER_SEGMENT_SOURCE_PREFIX, source_session: sessionId, + // anchor the fact's valid_from to the segment's real conversation time + // instead of letting insertFacts default to extraction-time (now()). + // Skipped for epoch-anchored segments (parser had no real date) so those + // keep the pre-patch fallback behavior. + ...(seg.startIso && !seg.startIso.startsWith('1970-') + ? { valid_from: new Date(seg.startIso) } + : {}), context: fact.context ?? `from ${page.slug} segment ${seg.startIso}..${seg.endIso}`, })); ```
Happy to turn these into a PR if the approach looks right — the three changes are independent and could land separately (the `ALLOWED_TYPES` one-liner is the minimum to stop the silent skip).
title: conversation-facts cannot run on iMessage exports: type allowlist omits imessage, 12h time-only pattern missing, multi-day date headers not anchored

body:
## Summary

Upgrading a managed-Postgres brain from v0.42.54.0 to either v0.42.57.0 or v0.42.58.0 causes a reproducible base-query retrieval regression. The canonical exact-match page drops out of the top 10 and scores collapse from ~1.67 to <0.90.

This was tested with zero-traffic Cloud Run canaries against the same production database and OAuth client, while v0.42.54.0 remained the primary revision. The release was automatically rolled back by a top-1 retrieval smoke gate.

## Environment

- Engine: Postgres
- Primary: v0.42.54.0
- Canary A: v0.42.58.0 (`a25209bb`) plus the Postgres half of upstream bootstrap fix PR #2735
- Canary B: v0.42.57.0 (`058f448b`) plus the same bootstrap fix
- Schema: v122 (v120-v122 additive migrations completed successfully)
- Embedding model pinned: `openai:text-embedding-3-small`
- Embedding dimensions pinned: `1536`
- Same database, same stored embeddings, same remote MCP query, same caller credentials
- Reproduces with cache, expansion, and autocut disabled
- Wiring the existing `ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY` into the v0.42.58 service did not change results

## Reproduction

MCP tool call:

```json
{
  "name": "query",
  "arguments": {
    "query": "remote brain operating protocol",
    "use_cache": false,
    "expand": false,
    "autocut": false,
    "limit": 20
  }
}
```

### v0.42.54.0 primary

1. `concepts/shared/remote_brain_operating_protocol` — 1.6727255273
2. `concepts/reference/current-repo-map` — 1.3454763257
3. `concepts/engineering/gbrain-fleet-onboarding` — 1.3358453887
4. `concepts/shared/unified_org_vault_spec` — 1.2941799779
5. `concepts/shared/canonical_sources` — 1.2188558074

### v0.42.57.0 and v0.42.58.0 canaries (identical)

1. `concepts/engineering/gbrain-fleet-onboarding` — 0.8974818576
2. `concepts/shared/unified_org_vault_spec` — 0.8376932840
3. `readme` — 0.7621238869
4. `concepts/engineering/gbrain-architecture` — 0.5547434656
5. `concepts/wiki/raava-org-structure` — 0.5416850823

The exact canonical page is absent from the top 10 in both newer versions.

## Expected

The v0.42.57/v0.42.58 query path should preserve v0.42.54 retrieval quality against unchanged 1536-dimension OpenAI embeddings. At minimum, the exact canonical page should remain top-1 for its title phrase.

## Notes

- The v119→v122 schema replay wedge was separately resolved using the approach in PR #2735; migrations then completed and the admin migrate-only check passed.
- The regression persists after migrations are complete, so this is not the missing-`event_page_id` failure.
- It persists when the ZE key is present and when query cache/expansion/autocut are disabled.
- Production remained/was restored to v0.42.54.0; newer revisions were deployed only as 0%-traffic tagged canaries after automatic rollback.
title: retrieval regression on managed Postgres brain after v0.42.54

body:
## Problem

`gbrain doctor --full` recommends:

```
gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true
```

But the command rejects the key as unknown. With `--force`, it writes the value while warning that nothing in gbrain reads it. That warning is false: `src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts` and the doctor both read the DB key.

The adjacent `gbrain agent` help is also stale: it describes `--model` as Anthropic-only and says the worker requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, even though the gateway loop supports provider-neutral models.

## Reproduction

1. Configure a non-Anthropic subagent model.
2. Run `gbrain doctor --full --json` and follow its gateway-loop fix.
3. Observe `Unknown config key "agent.use_gateway_loop"`.
4. Re-run with `--force`; observe `Nothing in gbrain reads this` even though the subagent handler reads it.

## Expected

- add `agent.use_gateway_loop` to the canonical config registry
- make the config command and doctor fix agree without `--force`
- update `gbrain agent` help to describe provider-neutral model IDs and gateway-loop requirements
- add a regression test that the doctor-proposed command is accepted by the config validator
title: fix(config): register agent.use_gateway_loop and make agent help provider-neutral

body:
## Problem

An extract-atoms-drain job configured with window=120 ran for 282.5 seconds and had to be cancelled. Doctor still reports a 60-page backlog and a 50% atoms halt rate. The window currently does not bound lock acquisition, database reads, LLM work, writes, bookkeeping, and lock release as one operation.

## Root cause

Cancellation is not propagated through the full drain lifetime. Several database and bookkeeping paths can outlive the caller deadline, and partial progress is not durably recorded before returning.

## Acceptance

- One combined AbortSignal starts before lock acquisition and covers acquisition, discovery, chat, atomic writes, bookkeeping, and release.
- Postgres direct queries are cancellable; PGLite refuses deadline mode when it cannot guarantee cancellation.
- Lock cleanup is attempt-unique and cannot release another attempt.
- Multi-atom output writes in one atomic statement.
- Partial completed work writes receipt and rollup under a bounded grace period.
- External abort rejects after releasing the lock; internal window expiry returns a truthful partial result.
- Collision/idempotency and JSONB sanitation tests cover same-title origins, NUL, and lone surrogates.
- A live window=120 canary reaches a terminal state within window plus documented grace and leaves no active lock.
title: fix(cycle): extract-atoms drain window does not bound lock/query/write lifetime

body:
## Environment
- gbrain 0.42.38.0
- 
- Host: Linux x64 VPS (Debian-based), not macOS
- 
- Engine: postgres (migrated from pglite via `gbrain migrate --to supabase --url ...`)
- 
- Runtime: reproduced identically under both Bun 1.3.14 and Node 20.20.2
- 
- Install method: git clone + bun install (documented/blessed path, not `bun install -g github:...`)
- 

## Summary
After migrating from PGLite to Postgres, running `gbrain autopilot` triggers the managed-worker dispatch path, since `useMinionsDispatch` requires `engineType === 'postgres'`. This path calls `resolveGbrainCliPath()` (src/commands/autopilot.ts), which throws:

```
Could not resolve the gbrain CLI path. Install gbrain so it is on $PATH (e.g. /usr/local/bin/gbrain), or run autopilot from the compiled binary directly.
```

...even though `gbrain` is confirmed on $PATH at the time of the crash. Manually reproducing the exact same call (`execSync('which gbrain')`) in the same shell/process context, with the same inherited env, succeeds and returns a valid path (`/usr/local/bin/gbrain` symlink -> `~/.bun/bin/gbrain` -> `src/cli.ts`). So `which gbrain` works standalone but fails specifically inside the autopilot worker-spawn code path.

## Impact
Since this only fires when `spawnManagedWorker` is true (postgres engine + minion_mode enabled), autopilot's dispatch loop still submits jobs, but no worker process ever picks them up — including embed jobs. This silently accumulates stale/missing embeddings over time with no error surfaced to the operator; `gbrain doctor` just shows a growing 'N stale chunks' warning that looks like a normal backlog, not a broken worker.

## Workaround
Running with `gbrain autopilot --no-worker` avoids the crash (dispatch-loop-only mode), but this also means embed jobs never get worked automatically — they queue but nothing processes them. Not a real fix, just avoids the loud failure.

## Suspected cause
`resolveGbrainCliPath()` (src/commands/autopilot.ts) resolution order is:
1. `execSync('which gbrain')`
2. 
2. `process.execPath` ends with `/gbrain`
3. 
3. `process.argv[1]` ends with `/gbrain`
4. 
4. throw
5. 

Worth checking whether the managed-worker spawn path runs this resolution in a context with a different/sanitized `env` (e.g. via `ChildWorkerSupervisor` passing an explicit `env` object that doesn't fully match the parent's inherited PATH at the exact moment of the `execSync` call), since a direct, identical `execSync('which gbrain')` call outside that path succeeds every time in the same shell, same PATH.

## Reproduction
1. `gbrain init --url <postgres-connection-string>` (or `gbrain migrate --to supabase --url ...` from an existing pglite brain)
2. 
2. Confirm `gbrain` resolves via `which gbrain` standalone
3. 
3. `gbrain autopilot --repo <path>` (no `--no-worker`)
4. 
4. Observe immediate throw on first cycle needing a managed worker
5. 

Also worth noting separately: the generic 'PGlite failed to initialize its WASM runtime... Most common cause: the macOS 26.3 WASM bug' wrapper message (from #223) fires on any PGLite abort regardless of host OS — it fired identically on this Linux x64 box during a genuine WAL-corruption crash unrelated to macOS. Might be worth softening that message to not assume macOS when the host is detectably something else.
title: resolveGbrainCliPath() fails inside managed-worker spawn when engine=postgres, silently blocking embed jobs

body:
## Summary

Add a deterministic reconciliation pass that materializes folder-to-page containment edges from gbrain's indexed slug tree. Managed edges use `link_source = structural-containment` and `link_type = contains`. This addresses pages that are unreachable only because folder membership is not represented as a graph edge, without pretending the edge was hand-authored.

The pass must be source-scoped, stable across repeated syncs, and derived from indexed pages only. It must not depend on a private corpus, external checkout conventions, or README files that normal sync did not ingest.

## Current behavior

`findOrphanPages` treats any inbound edge as reachability. A multi-page folder can therefore contain a linked hub and several unlinked siblings; the siblings remain orphans even though their structural relationship is deterministic from indexed slugs. External backfills can approximate the tree, but they must duplicate gbrain's normalization and cannot safely reason about metafiles that were skipped at ingest.

## Proposed reconciliation contract

Run a managed containment reconciliation after the page set and ordinary link reconciliation are current.

### Stable candidate set

For each target page, determine non-structural reachability using inbound links from any source while explicitly excluding `link_source = structural-containment`. Existing structural edges therefore never remove their own targets from the desired set; repeated syncs converge instead of alternating between add and delete.

A page is a containment candidate only when it has no inbound non-structural edge and its slug is not excluded by the existing orphan pseudo-page policy. The containment pass does not change the meaning of ordinary authored or cross-source reachability.

### Source ownership

Partition candidate and hub selection by target `source_id`.

- Parse folder ancestry from normalized indexed slugs within one `source_id`.
- Select hubs only from live indexed pages with the same `source_id` as the target. Never resolve a same-named slug from another source.
- Write only same-source `hub --contains--> target` edges.
- Reconcile stale managed edges by `source_id`; a partial sync for source A must not add, retain, or delete source B's desired set.
- Cross-source authored inbound links still count as non-structural reachability and can keep an A page out of A's structural candidate set, matching current orphan semantics.

### Deterministic hub selection

For a candidate at `dir/leaf`, consider live pages located directly in `dir` within the same source.

1. If an indexed folder-eponymous page exists, designate it as the one structural sibling hub. Otherwise designate one explicit `overview`, `*-foundation`, or `master-plan` page, using that priority and then shortest/lexical tie-break. Candidate siblings attach to that designation whether or not the designation already has an authored inbound edge; the designated page itself must walk upward or remain residual and can never select a sibling.
2. If no designation exists but one or more direct pages already have inbound non-structural links, choose only from that reachable set, tie-breaking by inbound non-structural degree, shortest slug, then lexical slug.
3. If neither a designation nor a reachable direct page exists, no orphan sibling may become a hub; walk to the parent folder for every candidate.
4. If no eligible hub exists at a parent, keep walking until the source root boundary.
5. Do not synthesize or assume `README`/`index` hubs. They are eligible only if they actually exist in the indexed live-page set and meet the reachable/designation rule above.
6. If the only possible hub is a source root or no eligible hub exists, leave the page residual rather than creating a low-information root edge.

Self-links and directed cycles are forbidden. Tests must assert that a two- or three-page folder produces either one designated-star topology plus the designation's upward edge/residual state, one reachable-hub star when no designation exists, or only upward edges/residuals—never sibling mutual links.

Build the desired edge set in deterministic target order: designated hubs before their siblings, then folder depth (shallow to deep), then lexical slug. Before accepting `hub --contains--> target`, test reachability from `target` to `hub` across the complete current non-structural graph plus already accepted desired containment edges. If the folder's one designated hub would close a cycle, do not promote another sibling—the target skips directly to the parent walk. Only when the folder has no designation and selection is from the ordered reachable-hub set may reconciliation try the next reachable direct hub before walking upward. If every permitted option cycles, leave the target residual. This check is source-aware for node identity but includes permitted cross-source authored paths, matching reachability semantics. Re-running with the same graph must make the same choices.

### Managed-source ownership and rollback

Reserve `structural-containment` in the engine's managed link-source set so user-facing `add_link` rejects it. Reconciliation owns creation and deletion transactionally; the public `remove_link` operation is not the bulk rollback mechanism because it requires endpoints.

Disabling the feature (or an engine maintenance command scoped by source) must delete the managed rows directly by `link_source` plus `source_id`, leaving manual and other managed provenances untouched. Re-enabling and syncing recreates the same desired set.

### Orphan reporting

Default orphan reachability may continue counting structural edges. Add an optional `ignoreSources`/CLI equivalent so a caller can also ask for human-authored-link health while ignoring `structural-containment`. The filter applies only to the inbound anti-join; source scoping and pseudo-page exclusions retain their current behavior.

## Synthetic regression fixture

Create an upstream-only fixture with two sources and no private slugs:

- source A: `guides/guides`, `guides/install`, `guides/troubleshoot`, `lonely/note`;
- source B: the same `guides/install` slug plus `external/reference`;
- an authored B→A link to `guides/troubleshoot`;
- no indexed README page.

Expected behavior:

1. A's `guides/guides --contains--> guides/install` is materialized.
2. A's `guides/troubleshoot` gets no structural edge because the cross-source authored inbound link already makes it reachable.
3. B's same-named slug is never selected as A's hub or target.
4. `lonely/note` stays residual because no non-root hub exists.
5. A second identical reconciliation produces zero changes.
6. Removing all managed edges and reconciling reproduces the identical set.
7. Syncing only source A does not modify source B's managed rows.
8. Deleting or moving a page removes only the now-stale A managed edge on the next A reconciliation.
9. Default orphan reporting excludes the structurally reached page; `ignoreSources=["structural-containment"]` includes it.
10. User `add_link(..., link_source="structural-containment")` is rejected as reconciliation-managed.
11. Adding an authored `guides/install -> guides/guides` path prevents the reverse containment edge; reconciliation chooses the next non-cycling parent hub or leaves `guides/install` residual.

## Acceptance criteria

- Postgres and PGLite produce the same desired set and stale-edge deletion behavior.
- Reconciliation is idempotent and non-oscillating across at least three identical syncs.
- Candidate, hub, write, and deletion paths are explicitly source-scoped.
- Cross-source non-structural inbound reachability retains current semantics.
- Only indexed live pages can become hubs; skipped metafiles are never assumed.
- The managed source appears in `list_link_sources` and link/backlink results.
- Public mutation rejects the managed source; engine-owned disable/maintenance removal is source-scoped and provenance-safe.
- Unit coverage includes self-link refusal, direct and transitive cycle refusal with deterministic fallback, parent walk, all-candidate folder designation, no-root residual, duplicate slug across sources, partial-source sync, move/delete cleanup, and the optional orphan ignore-source filter.

## Why upstream

gbrain owns the normalized source-local slug tree and the reconciliation lifecycle. Implementing containment there avoids duplicating normalization in each corpus, gives every source the same deterministic behavior, and keeps structural provenance distinguishable from authored intent.
title: Feature: materialize source-scoped structural-containment links during sync

body:
## Class

Pre-\`[[wiki-link]]\`-era meeting pages reference entities as prose (\"Jack Risenhoover\", \"Scharf\", \"Meridian\") without wiki-link syntax. The \`auto_link\` post-hook can only reconcile explicit \`[[wiki-links]]\` — prose mentions of known canonical entities remain invisible to the graph. Fanout is silently thin on the historical corpus.

## Concrete evidence surfaced 2026-07-10

During a meeting-ingest quality backfill covering May-July 2026 pages, we found the May 2026 backlog (12 canonical calls) had rich synthesis bodies but consistently thin `frontmatter.links[]`. Root cause: those pages predate the team's wiki-link adoption. An ephemeral static-map matcher covering ~60 canonical people/companies/concepts/deals meaningfully upgraded fanout on **8 of 12** May pages. Grade uplift on the touched set: A/B/C 10/16/7 → 24/11/4.

## Proposed fix

Add a prose-name → slug resolution pass to `gbrain extract` (or a signal-detector variant that fires during ingest and on demand). Contract:

- **Input:** a page body + the current canonical entity map (aliases per slug).
- **Resolution:** case-insensitive whole-word match, longest-alias-wins, mask `[[wiki-links]]` and code fences to avoid double-firing / false positives from code samples.
- **Output:** the set of matched slugs not already in `frontmatter.links[]`.
- **Non-destructive:** append-only to `links[]`; never overwrite existing entries; never modify body text.
- **Idempotent:** running the resolver twice must produce the same result.
- **Scope:** all `meetings/*` pages by default; extensible to `people/*` and `companies/*` bodies for symmetric fanout.

## Where the entity map comes from

Two options, complementary:

1. **Derived** — walk the graph nightly; for each page in `people/`, `companies/`, `concepts/`, `deals/`, extract aliases from the title + any explicit `aliases:` frontmatter field. This is the durable answer.
2. **Static supplement** — allow a user-maintainable JSON at a known path for names that don't appear as explicit graph nodes (nickname / initial variants).

## Workspace-side inoculation (already landed)

We shipped a workspace-side sweep (\`skills/prose-name-fanout\`) with a codified static entity map + non-destructive append + smoke tests. See michaeladair44/zeus-openclaw-workspace PR #74. That covers the historical local corpus but new pages coming through ingest still miss the fanout unless the resolver fires upstream.

## Test cases upstream should carry

- empty body → no matches
- single prose match → correct slug returned
- multi-class match (person + company + concept) → all returned in first-seen order
- `[[wiki-link]]` already present → not re-fired
- fenced code block → skipped
- longest alias wins (`\"Velocity Health\"` shadows `\"Velocity\"`)
- append is dedup + preserve-order over existing `links[]`
- case-insensitive match → canonical-cased slug

## Not a PR

Filing as an ISSUE per the workspace-side review contract; PR authorship on `garrytan/gbrain` is gated on Mike.

## Related

- Meeting-ingest backfill incident report: `status/2026-07-10-meeting-backfill-complete` substrate finding #3 (in the reporter's brain repo).
- Workspace-side inoculation: michaeladair44/zeus-openclaw-workspace#74.
- Sibling substrate issue from the same backfill: #2743 (multi-frontmatter corruption class).
title: gbrain extract should run prose-name → slug fanout over meetings/* bodies

body:
## Class

`gbrain put` (both the CLI form and `mcp__gbrain__put_page`) accepts input containing more than one leading `---...---` YAML frontmatter block and stores it as-is. At read time the second block silently shadows the first, corrupting page state (missing `type`, wrong `title`, dropped `links[]`).

## Concrete failure surfaced 2026-07-10

An enrich script emitted `links:\n` for the empty-list case. The next round's regex saw `links: null` and inserted a second `---...---` frontmatter block on top of the file. The put succeeded. The page now had two frontmatter blocks stacked. Downstream reads silently used the second block. Fanout audit was blind to it until forensic YAML parsing.

Root cause is that `gbrain put` does no YAML shape validation on the leading region.

## Proposed fix

At `gbrain put` ingest time:

1. Count leading `---...---` blocks in the input (walk from position 0 skipping blank lines; stop at first non-frontmatter character).
2. If `> 1`:
   - **Strict mode (default):** reject the put with a structured error identifying \"YAML double-put corruption\" and pointing to this class doc.
   - **Auto-canonicalize mode (opt-in `--merge-frontmatter`):** merge the blocks with last-wins semantics for scalar keys and array-append for `links[]`, `tags[]`, then proceed. Log the merge to the ingest log so it is auditable.

The count is well-defined and cheap: single-pass, no YAML parser needed for detection. Merging is more work but the code path already knows how to serialize `gbrain page frontmatter` so the reverse (parse + merge two YAML blocks) is a small addition.

## Scope

- Applies to both the CLI `gbrain put` path and the MCP `put_page` tool.
- Does NOT apply to `---` horizontal rules inside the body — the detector must be leading-only (stop at first non-frontmatter character).
- Body-side edits should be untouched.

## Workspace-side inoculation (already landed)

We shipped a workspace-side PreToolUse hook (\`multi-frontmatter-guard\`) that catches this class before it reaches `gbrain put` from the paths we control (MCP put_page + `gbrain put <slug> < file` Bash redirects). See michaeladair44/zeus-openclaw-workspace PR #73. The hook is a stopgap; the canonical fix belongs here in `gbrain put`.

## Test cases upstream should carry

- empty input → allow (0 blocks)
- body-only, no frontmatter → allow (0 blocks)
- single well-formed frontmatter → allow (1 block)
- stacked double-put (the 2026-07-10 class) → reject in strict, merge in opt-in
- pathological triple stacked → same
- single frontmatter + body `---` horizontal rule → allow (rules are not frontmatter)
- BOM-prefixed frontmatter → allow

## Not a PR

Filing as an ISSUE per the workspace-side review contract; PR authorship on `garrytan/gbrain` is gated on Mike. Happy to sketch the upstream code shape in comments if useful.

## Related

- Meeting-ingest backfill incident report: `status/2026-07-10-meeting-backfill-complete` substrate finding #2 (in the reporter's brain repo).
- Workspace-side inoculation: michaeladair44/zeus-openclaw-workspace#73.
title: put_page silently accepts multi-frontmatter content (YAML double-put corruption class)

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

The native Anthropic subagent loop currently places explicit 5-minute cache breakpoints on the last tool definition and the system prompt (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts`). Those static prefixes cache correctly, but the growing `messages` history is not marked for caching.

On every multi-turn tool loop, prior user messages, assistant responses, tool-use blocks, and tool results are therefore sent again as ordinary input tokens. This leaves the largest growing prefix uncached even when every previous block is byte-identical.

A content-free audit of one recent production week found approximately:

- 2.26M uncached input tokens
- 179K cache-read tokens
- 33K cache-write tokens
- 60K output tokens
- cache-hit share of total input: about 7.2%

The existing system/tool caching works, but these figures suggest that conversation history dominates the remaining input cost in tool-heavy jobs.

This is separate from #2490 / #2442. Those address correct placement of static system and tool cache markers in `gateway.chat()`. They do not add a moving conversation-history breakpoint.

**What does the solution look like?**

For Anthropic subagent tool loops, cache the growing conversation prefix as well as the existing static prefixes. Two possible implementations:

1. Add Anthropic automatic caching at the request level while retaining explicit breakpoints for tools and system; or
2. Add an explicit ephemeral breakpoint to the final eligible message content block on each turn, allowing Anthropic's lookback to hit the previous turn's write.

The implementation should preserve these constraints:

- Keep tool definitions and the deterministic system prompt independently cacheable.
- Use the default 5-minute TTL initially. A 1-hour TTL should be a separate, evidence-led decision because cache writes cost more.
- Stay within Anthropic's four-breakpoint limit when static and moving breakpoints are combined.
- Apply only to providers/routes that genuinely support the relevant cache-control shape.
- Persist and expose `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens` per turn and per job.
- Add request-shape tests plus a live/manual verification that turn 2 produces non-zero cache reads for turn 1's message history.
- Preserve crash replay and tool-result ordering exactly.

An acceptance test could run a two- or three-turn Anthropic tool loop with a cacheable message history and assert:

- turn 1 writes the eligible conversation prefix;
- turn 2 reads the unchanged prior history from cache;
- only the newly appended turn is processed as fresh input/cache creation;
- output and tool behaviour are unchanged.

**Alternatives considered**

- Keep caching only tools and system prompts. This leaves accumulated conversation and tool-result history at full input price.
- Use a 1-hour TTL for the current static breakpoints. This may improve reuse between separate jobs, but it does not solve uncached history within a multi-turn job and can cost more when prefixes are not reused enough.
- Implement this as a downstream patch. This belongs upstream because it is generic Anthropic tool-loop cost control and touches an active request-construction seam.
title: Cache growing conversation history in Anthropic subagent tool loops

body:
## Summary

The Supabase direct-pool kill-switch is read **only** from `process.env`:

```ts
// src/core/connection-manager.ts:175-176
return process.env.GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL === '1' ||
  process.env.GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL === 'true';
```

There is no `config.json` field for it. So any process that does not inherit the
env var (a `launchd`/`systemd` daemon, a cron job, a headless `claude -p`
subprocess) silently falls back to the direct connection, which then fails with
`ENOTFOUND`/`ECONNREFUSED` on Supabase Session-Pooler brains (the
`deriveDirectUrl` hostname-swap bug already tracked in #1641 / #1006).

The failure is **silent**: the daemon just never writes, and an interactive
shell "works" because the flag happens to be exported there, which masks the
root cause.

## Repro (real-world)

1. Brain on Supabase Postgres via the **session pooler** (host
   `aws-*.pooler.supabase.com`, port 6543).
2. Install `gbrain autopilot` as a `launchd` job (LaunchAgent plist, no `env`
   block; the wrapper sources no profile that exports the flag).
3. Every cycle fails: `deriveDirectUrl` synthesizes `db.<ref>.supabase.co:5432`,
   which does not resolve on an IPv4-only / no-IPv6 network, so `getaddrinfo
   ENOTFOUND`. Nothing is written; no actionable error surfaces to the operator.
4. Interactive `gbrain <cmd>` in a normal shell works fine (the flag is exported
   in the shell), so the bug looks environment-specific and is hard to spot.
5. Fix that unblocked it: manually add `export GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1` to
   `~/.zshenv` so the non-interactive daemon subprocess inherits it.

## Why it matters

Env-only flags are invisible to exactly the processes that most need the
kill-switch: long-running background writers (autopilot, sync, cron ingest) and
headless agent jobs. Requiring every wrapper/plist to plumb the env var by hand
is fragile and fails silently when missed.

This is the same "no working config path" shape already reported for API keys in
#2119 / #2120: the pooler flag has the identical file-plane vs env gap.

## Proposed fix

Let the kill-switch also be read from `config.json` (file plane), for example
`disable_direct_pool: true`, with precedence env > config > default (mirrors the
existing `GBRAIN_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL` override). Then:

```
gbrain config set disable_direct_pool true   # file plane; persists for ALL processes
```

would make daemons and headless jobs pick it up with no shell-profile plumbing.
(Per #2119, ensure `config set` for this key writes the plane the runtime
actually reads.)

## Related

- #1641, #1006: the direct-pool hostname bug this flag works around
- #2119, #2120: config-plane inconsistency for keys (same root shape)

## Environment

- gbrain 0.42.58.0
- macOS (Darwin 25), IPv4-only network
- Supabase Postgres, session pooler (port 6543)
title: GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL is env-only (no config.json plane): daemons/headless jobs silently fall back to the failing direct pool

body:
## Summary

`bunx tsc --noEmit` can fail on a clean v0.42.58.0 checkout depending on an undeclared `openclaw` package found in a parent `node_modules` directory.

The real plugin-load E2E test dynamically imports `openclaw/plugin-sdk`, but TypeScript still resolves and type-checks that bare module. The `@ts-ignore` is attached to the import line; the actual error occurs on the following property-access line.

## Affected version

- `v0.42.58.0`
- commit `a25209bbb2bacf1b88e06fd5282b27f1bf4a3e7a`

## Reproduction

On a clean checkout where a parent directory contains an OpenClaw SDK version without a typed `registerContextEngine` export:

```bash
bun install --frozen-lockfile
bunx tsc --noEmit
```

Observed:

```text
test/e2e/openclaw-plugin-load-real.test.ts(281,37): error TS2339:
Property 'registerContextEngine' does not exist on type
'typeof import(".../node_modules/openclaw/dist/plugin-sdk/index")'.
```

`package.json` declares OpenClaw compatibility metadata but does not declare `openclaw` as a dependency or devDependency. Bun/TypeScript therefore walks upward and compiles against ambient parent state.

Relevant code:

```ts
// @ts-ignore — bare specifier resolution depends on node_modules.
const sdk = await import('openclaw/plugin-sdk');
registerContextEngine = sdk.registerContextEngine;
```

The ignore does not cover the failing property access.

## Expected behavior

A clean checkout's typecheck should not depend on whatever OpenClaw SDK happens to be installed in a parent directory. The runtime compatibility test may still dynamically probe the installed CLI/SDK, but compile-time verification should remain hermetic.

## Possible fix

Treat the dynamically imported SDK as an unknown runtime object and validate the optional property structurally, for example with an explicit `unknown` cast and a local interface/type guard. This keeps the runtime test's intent without binding repository typecheck to an undeclared ambient package version.

## Baseline proof

The failure was reproduced after stashing all local changes and running `bunx tsc --noEmit` against untouched v0.42.58.0. Restoring the unrelated provider patch produced the identical single failure.
title: Typecheck depends on ambient parent openclaw SDK types

body:
## Summary

`gbrain providers` builds gateway configuration separately from the rest of the CLI instead of using `buildGatewayConfig()`. On v0.42.58.0 this means provider diagnostics can ignore file-plane API keys and can behave differently from the real configured gateway path. The explicit `providers test --model` override also drops configured provider base URLs.

This is especially visible when a launcher injects an empty provider environment variable: `buildGatewayConfig()` correctly filters the empty value and preserves the configured key, while the providers command does not use that ownership seam.

## Affected version

- `v0.42.58.0`
- commit `a25209bbb2bacf1b88e06fd5282b27f1bf4a3e7a`

## Reproduction

1. Put a valid Anthropic key and native base URL in the GBrain config plane.
2. Run from an environment where `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is unset or injected as an empty string.
3. Run:

```bash
gbrain providers test --touchpoint chat --model anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6
```

`src/commands/providers.ts` currently constructs gateway config manually:

- `configureFromEnv()` copies model/base URL fields plus raw `process.env`, but does not map file-plane API keys.
- The `--model` test override passes only the model/dimensions plus raw `process.env`, dropping the configured key and configured base URL.

The result can disagree with the real CLI path that uses `buildGatewayConfig()`.

## Expected behavior

Provider listing/testing should use the same gateway configuration ownership seam as normal CLI execution. `providers test --model` should override only the requested model and embedding dimensions; credentials, base URLs, fallback config, and empty-env handling should continue to come from `buildGatewayConfig()`.

## Proposed fix

- Route `configureFromEnv()` through `buildGatewayConfig(loadConfig() ?? { engine: 'pglite' })` so the command still works before `gbrain init`.
- Add a pure `buildProviderTestGatewayConfig()` helper that starts from the canonical config and changes only the tested model/dimensions.
- Add a regression test using the repository's `withEnv()` helper to prove an empty `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` does not clobber the configured key and that configured base URLs survive the override.

## Verification

On a clean branch based directly on v0.42.58.0:

```text
bun test test/providers.test.ts test/ai/build-gateway-config.test.ts
21 pass, 0 fail
```

The repository verification suite is 30/31 on the untouched baseline and on the patch. The one pre-existing failure is unrelated: `test/e2e/openclaw-plugin-load-real.test.ts:281` resolves an undeclared parent `openclaw/plugin-sdk` type that lacks `registerContextEngine`.

An apply-ready two-file patch/commit is available.
title: providers command bypasses canonical gateway config

body:
## The gap

Remote MCP callers with `read` scope have no safe way to contribute content. `put_page` needs `write` scope, so trust is all or nothing: either an agent can write straight into the brain, or it can't offer anything at all. In practice a read-only chat or coding session often produces a page worth keeping, and today it just gets lost.

## Proposal: human-gated artifact staging

A `propose_artifact` operation available at `read` scope:

- Stages markdown (slug, title, content, source label) OUTSIDE the brain: a pending dir of JSON files with a TTL (default 72h). Nothing touches pages, chunks, or embeddings.
- Unaccepted proposals evaporate at TTL. No cleanup job needed.
- Accept and reject live ONLY on the admin HTTP surface (requireAdmin). Deliberately no MCP accept tool, so an agent can never approve its own proposal.
- Accept runs the normal `put_page` path with a distinct `source_kind`, so provenance shows the human approval.
- Re-proposing the same slug supersedes the earlier pending proposal (old one marked rejected with a superseded_by pointer). Iterating agents leave one latest proposal instead of a pile of near-duplicates.
- Proposal content gets force-stamped `provenance: derived` at create time, so agent-authored pages can't masquerade as human-authored ones downstream.

## Why this shape

It keeps the write boundary exactly where it is (only humans and write-scope callers mutate the brain) while giving every read-only agent a knock-on-the-door channel. The pattern generalizes: any gbrain user exposing a public or semi-trusted MCP endpoint wants agents to be able to offer without being able to insert.

## Status

I have this implemented and running in production on my own brain: staging module, MCP wiring, admin accept/reject endpoints, supersede semantics, provenance stamping, tests. No changes to existing behavior; the feature is fully additive. If the direction sounds right I'll PR it with `gbrain eval replay` numbers and a full `bun run verify` / `test:full` pass per repo conventions.
title: Human-gated artifact staging: let read-only agents propose pages for admin approval

body:
## Summary

`writeFactsToFence` derives its target file as `join(target.localPath, `${target.slug}.md`)` (fence-write.ts:169). `FenceTarget` carries only `{ sourceId, localPath, slug }` — the page's real `pages.source_path` never reaches the write path. For any page whose file basename differs from its slug (the common case for human-titled pages: `10-people/Victor Braca.md` owns slug `10-people/victor-braca`), the fence write targets a file that doesn't exist.

Observed on v0.42.53.0.

## Mechanism (source-confirmed)

1. `existsSync(filePath)` misses the canonical spaced-name file, so the write **stub-creates a new file at the hyphen path** (the stub-creation guard at ~line 183 only refuses slugs with no directory prefix — a fully-qualified `10-people/…` slug passes).
2. `upsertFactRow` computes the next `row_num` from the **empty new fence** → `rowNum=1`.
3. The DB already holds rows for `(source_id, source_markdown_slug, row_num=1…N)` from the canonical page's history → the insert collides with `idx_facts_fence_key` and fails.
4. But the file rename (step 5, fence-write.ts:249) lands **before** the DB stamp (step 6) — so every failed attempt leaves a resurrected orphan file behind, containing only the new facts.

## Consequences

- **Fact writes to these pages fail permanently** — every `extract_facts` targeting such an entity errors on the fence key, forever.
- **Dup-slug dedupes silently undo themselves**: deleting a hyphen-slug stub file leaves the DB page alive; the next fact write re-creates the stub at the hyphen path. We watched 3 of 11 deduped stubs resurrect within days, each carrying a divergent fence.
- The canonical page's fence and the DB drift apart with no error surfaced to the user (the failure is only visible in write-failure logs).

## Repro

1. Create `10-people/Jane Doe.md` (spaced filename), sync — page gets slug `10-people/jane-doe`.
2. Run `extract_facts` on any transcript mentioning Jane so a fact resolves to her.
3. Result: a new `10-people/jane-doe.md` stub appears; if the canonical page already had fence rows, the insert dies on `idx_facts_fence_key`, and the stub remains.

## Ask

Resolve the write path from `pages.source_path` when the page row exists (thread it through `FenceTarget`, or look it up inside `writeFactsToFence`); fall back to the slug-derived path only for genuinely new pages. Secondarily, consider deferring the file rename until after the DB insert succeeds (or unlinking the stub on insert failure) so a failed write can't strand an orphan file.

Related: the legacy `localPath === null` fallback can also mint DB-only rows with `row_num = NULL`, which the dream-phase fence reconcile then can't match — same family of fence/DB identity drift.
title: fence-write derives the file path from slug, never pages.source_path — fact writes to spaced-filename pages fail on idx_facts_fence_key and resurrect deleted dup-slug stubs

body:
## Bug

`gbrain jobs prune --dry-run` executes the prune and deletes rows while the operator believes they are previewing. Observed on v0.42.53.0 (Postgres engine): the command printed `Pruned 8732 jobs older than 30 days.` and the rows were really gone (row count verified against a pre-command `pg_dump`).

## Root cause

`src/commands/jobs.ts` `case 'prune'` parses only `--older-than`:

```ts
case 'prune': {
  const olderThanStr = parseFlag(args, '--older-than') ?? '30d';
  ...
  const count = await queue.prune({ olderThan: new Date(Date.now() - days * 86400000) });
  console.log(`Pruned ${count} jobs older than ${days} days.`);
}
```

Unknown flags are silently dropped, so `--dry-run` (which `jobs submit` DOES support, teaching users to expect it) falls through and the destructive default runs.

## Expected

Either honor `--dry-run` (print the would-be count, delete nothing) or reject unknown flags on destructive subcommands with a hard error. Silent-ignore of a safety flag on a delete path is the worst of both.

## Repro

1. Any brain with job history older than 30 days.
2. `gbrain jobs prune --dry-run`
3. Rows are deleted; output claims a completed prune.
title: jobs prune silently ignores --dry-run and deletes rows

body:
## Bug (two related UX gaps around the git-repo requirement)

**(a) `sources add` accepts a non-git directory that can never sync.** Registering a plain directory succeeds; every subsequent sync then fails with:

```
Not a git repository: <local_path>. GBrain sync requires a git-initialized repo.
```

Nothing at registration time validates or hints at the requirement, so in an automated pipeline the source just goes silently stale (`last_sync_at` frozen) until an operator reads the sync logs. In our case an email-archive source sat unsyncable for 3 days while its markdown looked healthy from the earlier import.

**(b) After `git init` + commit in that directory, sync dies on a stale anchor with a raw git fatal.** `sources.last_commit` still held a SHA from the pre-git era, and `gbrain sync --source <id> --full` failed with:

```
fatal: git cat-file: could not get object info
```

No message names the stale anchor, and there is no CLI to reset it — we recovered by NULL-ing the `sources.last_commit` column in the database by hand, after which the full sync ran perfectly.

Worth noting: #1205 shows autopilot HAS a graceful path for this exact condition ("Sync anchor commit <sha> missing (force push?). Running full reimport."). CLI `sync --full` in this shape did not take that path — it surfaced the raw fatal instead.

**(c) Docs gap:** `git init` + an empty commit is NOT sufficient — the walker reads files through git objects, so untracked working-tree files are invisible. Files must actually be committed. This is discoverable only by experiment.

## Why this matters

The git requirement is reasonable, but its failure modes are all silent-or-cryptic: a source that never syncs, a fatal with no remediation hint, and a recovery that requires editing the database. Each one costs an operator a debugging session.

## Expected fix

- Validate (or at least warn) at `sources add` when `local_path` is not a git repo.
- When the anchor commit is missing from the repo, treat it as a first sync / full reimport (reusing the recovery that autopilot already has per #1205) instead of surfacing `git cat-file` fatals.
- Provide `gbrain sources reset-anchor <id>` (or document the supported way to clear `last_commit`).
- Document the "files must be committed, not just present" requirement.

Related: #1205 (anchor invalidation under autopilot), #753 (git-root discovery for subdir sources).

## Workaround

`git init && git add -A && git commit` in the source dir, then manually `UPDATE sources SET last_commit=NULL WHERE id='<id>'` and re-run `gbrain sync --source <id> --full`.
title: sources add accepts non-git dirs that can never sync; stale last_commit after re-init dies on raw 'git cat-file' fatal with no self-heal or reset CLI

body:
## Bug

Image pages are created with `source_id='default'` regardless of which registered source's sync imported them. Sibling markdown pages from the same sync get the correct `source_id`; only image-asset pages misroute.

## Evidence from v0.42.56.0 (macOS, Postgres 17)

After `gbrain sync --source <feed-id> --full --parallel 1` across ~10 registered feed sources containing ~35k images total:

```sql
SELECT source_id, count(*) FROM pages
WHERE slug ~ '\.(png|jpg|jpeg|gif|webp)$'
GROUP BY source_id;
-- every row lands under 'default'; zero under the syncing source ids
```

Markdown pages from the same syncs carry the right `source_id`.

## Why this matters

- **Per-source fidelity accounting is impossible**: you cannot compare images-on-disk vs images-in-brain per source (we had to fall back to a single global disk-vs-DB count).
- **Search scoping**: `search --source <feed-id>` excludes that source's own images.
- **`sources status` page counts** exclude the images a source actually contributed.

Related to the broader source-isolation efforts in #929 and #753 — this is the image-import corner of the same problem.

## Expected fix

`importImageFile` (and whatever creates the image page row) should inherit the `source_id` of the sync run that imported it, same as markdown imports.

## Workaround

Global (cross-source) disk-vs-DB image accounting only; per-source scoped searches must be repeated against `default` to pick up that source's images.
title: Image pages are created with source_id='default' regardless of the syncing source

body:
## Bug

`embedMultimodal` sends original image bytes to Voyage. Images whose dimensions fall outside voyage-multimodal-3's permitted resolution range get a 400 and land in the sync-failures ledger — permanently, since the same original bytes are retried on every sync and rejected every time.

## Evidence from v0.42.56.0 (macOS, Postgres 17)

Ledger entries (several, across two email-feed sources):

```
"error":"embedMultimodal failed for <dir>/img_01.png: Voyage multimodal returned 400:
{\"detail\":\"Request to model 'voyage-multimodal-3' failed. The image resolution is not
within the permitted range: [base64 image at input 0, index 0] - For more information on
input limitations, please see the documentation:
https://docs.voyageai.com/reference/multimodal-embeddings-api\"}"
```

The same files were handled fine by the OCR-side vision model during the same import — only the Voyage embed step rejects them, so the page ends up half-ingested and ledgered.

## Why this matters

Real-world email/document archives contain occasional extreme-dimension images (tall receipts, wide banners, tiny icons). Each becomes a permanent ledger entry that re-fails forever, adding noise that drowns actionable failures, with no path to resolution short of deleting the file from the source.

## Expected fix

Before calling Voyage, check dimensions against the model's documented limits and downscale/resize (aspect-preserving) into range. Only ledger the file if it still fails after normalization. Bonus: log the normalization in the import output so operators can see it happened.

## Workaround

None clean — the entries sit in the ledger. Pre-resizing at the collection layer is possible but duplicates knowledge of Voyage's limits outside gbrain.
title: embedMultimodal: Voyage 400 'image resolution is not within the permitted range' — downscale before embedding instead of permanently ledgering the file

body:
## Bug

Keyword indexing builds one `tsvector` over the whole page, and Postgres hard-caps a tsvector at 1,048,575 bytes. A single markdown file over that size fails with `string is too long for tsvector` — and instead of failing just that file, the **whole source sync reports `Sync BLOCKED`** and the checkpoint cannot advance until the file is fixed or skipped.

## Evidence from v0.42.56.0 (macOS, Postgres 17)

Sync-failures ledger entries for a mailing-list feed source containing large exported Google-Docs markdown (1.1–7.5 MB):

```
"error":"string is too long for tsvector (1888898 bytes, max 1048575 bytes)"
"error":"string is too long for tsvector (3547984 bytes, max 1048575 bytes)"
```

Sync output:

```
Sync BLOCKED at 0e1313a1: 5 file(s) failed to parse.
  See ~/.gbrain/sync-failures.jsonl for details, or run 'gbrain doctor'.
```

- Other files in the same run DID import, but the checkpoint stays pinned.
- With `--retry-failed` the same files re-fail every run.
- The 3-consecutive-failure auto-skip eventually moves past them, but for a scheduled collector that means hours of blocked cycles per oversized file, per source.

Notably, **embeddings are already chunk-level** — only the keyword tsvector is whole-page, so this limit looks like an implementation artifact rather than inherent.

## Why this matters

Sources fed by automated exporters (Drive docs, email archives, large tables) routinely produce multi-MB markdown. One such file silently degrades an entire source's freshness, and the failure mode ("Sync BLOCKED") reads like a sync-infrastructure problem rather than "one file is too big for the keyword index".

## Expected fix

Any of:

- Build `search_vector` per chunk (matching the embedding granularity), or
- Truncate/cap the tsvector input for oversized pages (index the first ~1MB, warn, keep full text in the page body), or
- At minimum: treat tsvector overflow as a per-file ledger entry that does NOT block the source checkpoint.

## Workaround

Cap markdown exports at the collection layer (we truncate to ≤900KB with a marker before gbrain ever sees the file).
title: One md file >1MB overflows page-level tsvector and blocks the entire source sync at checkpoint

body:
## Summary

On a Postgres brain at schema `version=119`, running `gbrain init --migrate-only` (or any path that calls `PostgresEngine.initSchema`) throws:

```
column "event_page_id" does not exist
```

with exit 1, and the migration array never runs. Schema stays wedged at 119.

## Environment

- gbrain `0.42.57.0` (latest master, `058f448b`)
- Postgres brain (local dev pool)
- Starting schema version: **119** (op_checkpoints CHECK constraint)
- Target schema version: **122**

## Repro

Fresh brain at v119. Any of these paths triggers the failure:

```bash
gbrain init --migrate-only      # exit 1
gbrain apply-migrations --yes   # silently succeeds ("All migrations up to date") but schema stays at 119
gbrain doctor                   # reports Schema probe failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist
```

## Root cause

`PostgresEngine.initSchema` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:277-363`) runs, in order:

1. `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap(conn)` — probes for forward-referenced columns and pre-creates them (`pages.deleted_at`, `links.link_source`, `content_chunks.language`, etc.).
2. `conn.unsafe(sqlText)` — replays the full embedded schema.
3. `runMigrations(this)` — applies pending migrations.

The embedded schema (`src/core/schema-embedded.ts:561-562`) contains:

```sql
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
```

On a pre-121 brain, `timeline_entries` exists but `event_page_id` does not. `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS` protects against "index already exists" — not against "column does not exist." Step 2 throws → step 3 never runs → the migration that would have created the column (`migrations/[121] timeline_entries_event_page_id`, `src/core/migrate.ts:5442-5477`) never fires. Wedge is permanent until manually resolved.

This is the same forward-reference class that `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` was built to handle. `event_page_id` is just missing from the probe list.

## Verification the migration SQL itself is fine

Ran migration 121's SQL in isolation on a v119 brain via psql (with `BEGIN; ... ROLLBACK`): clean. Same for migrations 120 and 122. All three succeed when driven directly.

## Proposed fix

Extend `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:389-...`) with an `event_page_id_exists` probe + guarded add, mirroring the existing pattern for `pages.deleted_at` etc.

Sketch (~10 lines):

```typescript
// Add to the probe SELECT (single round-trip):
event_page_id_exists: boolean;  // add to the type
// SELECT clause:
EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM information_schema.columns
   WHERE table_schema = current_schema()
     AND table_name = 'timeline_entries'
     AND column_name = 'event_page_id'
) AS event_page_id_exists

// Then in the bootstrap step, after the existing probes:
if (probe.pages_exists && !probe.event_page_id_exists) {
  await conn`ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER`;
}
```

The migration 121 body remains as-is; on a bootstrapped v119 brain, `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` in the migration is now a no-op but the FK-constraint DO block and the two indexes still land through the migration path (the migration's own DDL is idempotent by design).

Alternative sketch: wrap the two `CREATE INDEX` statements in `schema-embedded.ts:561-562` in a `DO $$ IF column_exists ... $$` block. Slightly less invasive but doesn't add the column so the migration must still land — same net effect, just a different placement.

## Workaround for users hitting this now

Manual DDL (matches migration 121's exact shape), then re-run `gbrain init --migrate-only`:

```sql
BEGIN;
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
DO $$
BEGIN
  IF NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM pg_constraint
     WHERE conname = 'timeline_entries_event_page_id_fkey'
       AND conrelid = 'timeline_entries'::regclass
  ) THEN
    ALTER TABLE timeline_entries
      ADD CONSTRAINT timeline_entries_event_page_id_fkey
      FOREIGN KEY (event_page_id) REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
  END IF;
END $$;
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
COMMIT;
```

Then:

```bash
gbrain init --migrate-only
# ✓ Schema version 119 → 122 (3 migration(s) pending)
# ✓ [120] schema_lint_hardening_search_path_security_invoker
# ✓ [121] timeline_entries_event_page_id
# ✓ [122] facts_ontology_dimension
```

## Impact

Any brain at v119 attempting to reach v122 via the standard `gbrain init --migrate-only` or auto-migrate paths. `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` silently reports "up to date" without applying — that's a separate warning-quality issue (should surface the failed schema replay rather than reporting success).

## Not filing a PR

Filing the issue for triage — happy to leave the fix implementation to maintainers to keep the forward-reference bootstrap pattern consistent.
title: Schema replay throws on v0.42.x upgrade: CREATE INDEX on `timeline_entries(event_page_id)` runs before migration 121 adds the column

body:
## Environment
gbrain 0.42.53 (`github:garrytan/gbrain#814258d`), PGLite, macOS 26.5.1, `anthropic_api_key` set in `~/.gbrain/config.json` (verified working).

## Repro
1. `gbrain config set models.think anthropic:claude-sonnet-5` — accepted, no warning. The model id resolves fine at config time.
2. `gbrain think "anything"` — synthesis comes back as "no LLM available" (and per #1698 the empty result is saved), with no error mentioning the model id.
3. `gbrain config set models.think anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6` — everything works with the identical key/config.

So the registry/config layer and the chat-client build disagree about which Anthropic models exist: the id passes validation where it's cheap to reject, then fails where the failure is silent. Current Anthropic model ids that fail this way include `claude-sonnet-5` (and presumably the other Claude 5 family ids); `claude-sonnet-4-6` and other 4.x ids work.

## Asks
1. Refresh the Anthropic allowlist for the Claude 5 family — or better, stop allowlisting chat models for providers whose APIs already return a clean "unknown model" error, and surface that error instead (same stale-allowlist class as #2613/#2507/#1607 for Google).
2. When the chat client can't be built for the configured model, fail loudly at `config set` time or at `think` time with the model id in the message — the current path (accept at config, degrade to "no LLM available" at runtime) cost us a debugging session that ended in bisecting model ids. #1698 covers the saving-empty-synthesis half of this.
title: Anthropic chat allowlist predates Claude 5: claude-sonnet-5 accepted by config but chat client build fails silently → 'no LLM available' empty synthesis

body:
## Environment
gbrain 0.42.53 (`github:garrytan/gbrain#814258d`), PGLite, macOS 26.5.1, bun 1.3.14.

## Problem
When the source directory is a git repo, `gbrain import` (and `sync`) enumerate files via `git ls-files`, so anything matched by `.gitignore` is silently excluded — and there is no flag or config key to opt out.

This bites the (I suspect common) layout where a knowledge vault is a git repo that intentionally gitignores its machine-ingested content: in our case `.gitignore` excludes `Sources/Slack|GoogleDrive|GitHub|Slite` and `Meetings/*` because they're reproducible ingester output — but they are exactly the ~12k pages we want in the brain. Tracked-in-git vs wanted-in-brain are different sets, and right now git's view wins unconditionally.

## Workaround
Prepend a stub `git` to PATH so repo detection fails and the FS-walk fallback runs:

```sh
mkdir -p /tmp/stubgit && printf '#!/bin/sh\nexit 1\n' > /tmp/stubgit/git && chmod +x /tmp/stubgit/git
PATH="/tmp/stubgit:$PATH" gbrain import ~/Vault/
```

This works but is fragile and non-obvious, and has to be repeated for every periodic re-import since `sync` has the same limitation.

## Suggestion
A CLI flag (`--no-gitignore` / `--walk fs`) and/or a per-source config key deciding the enumeration strategy. Related issues showing the current behavior is also inconsistent across code paths, so an explicit knob would resolve those too: #2082 (full reimport walker *ignores* .gitignore while incremental sync respects it), #1073 (opt-in gitignore for code strategy), #1169, #2607.
title: import/sync: no way to include gitignored files — git ls-files fast path has no CLI override (stub-git shim is the only workaround)

body:
## Environment
- gbrain 0.42.53 (`github:garrytan/gbrain#814258d`), PGLite store
- bun 1.3.14, macOS 26.5.1 (arm64)
- Embeddings: `openai:text-embedding-3-large` @ 1280 dims, ~60k chunks total

## Symptom
Any single `gbrain embed` process that runs longer than roughly 20–40 minutes stops making progress: CPU pins at 100%, no further output, no further rows embedded, and it never recovers (left one overnight — 8+ hours, zero progress). Killing and restarting the same command resumes fine and runs at full speed — until the fresh process hits the same wall ~20–40 min later.

The pattern (fast fresh process, degradation as a function of process age rather than data position, unbounded CPU with no I/O) looks like GC pressure / heap fragmentation in the bun+PGLite process rather than anything about the specific pages being embedded — the same pages embed instantly after a restart.

Separately, `embed --pace` never gets going at all on this corpus: it spins at 100% CPU before embedding anything. It looked like the token estimator hangs on some pages, so we stopped using `--pace` entirely.

## Workaround that completed a ~60k-chunk backfill
Loop `gbrain embed --stale --batch-size 1500` in *fresh processes*, with a per-batch watchdog that kills any batch exceeding 22 minutes:

```sh
while :; do
  timeout 1320 gbrain embed --stale --batch-size 1500 || true
  # exit loop when nothing left; `gbrain embed --stale` exits quickly when clean
done
```

This never stalled: every batch either finished or was killed and resumed cleanly.

## Suggestion
Either an internal restart/fork per N pages (worker process recycling), or documenting a supported `--max-batch-runtime`/auto-recycle, would make bulk embeds hands-off. Happy to run instrumented builds against this corpus if that helps.
title: Long-running embed degrades into a 100%-CPU, zero-output stall after ~20–40 min (bun + PGLite); --pace hangs outright

body:
**Version:** 0.42.52.0 (SHA `bb2e88c`), Postgres engine, multi-source brain.

## Summary
Every CLI take-write (`takes add`, `takes update`, `takes supersede`) resolves its target page via
`getPageId` (`src/commands/takes.ts:86`):

```ts
const rows = await engine.executeRaw<{ id: number }>(
  `SELECT id FROM pages WHERE slug = $1 LIMIT 1`, [slug]);
```

No `source_id` filter, no `ORDER BY`. When the **same slug exists in more than one source**, the
pick is arbitrary — neither `GBRAIN_SOURCE` nor `--source` participates (verified: `--source` on
`takes add`/`takes supersede` lands in the take's provenance `source` column; it does not scope the
page resolution).

## Repro (two sources, same slug)
1. `put_page` the same slug into source A and source B (e.g. a dept source and a shared/canon source).
2. Under `GBRAIN_SOURCE=<source A>`, run
   `gbrain takes supersede <slug> --row 1 --claim "x" --who someone --source <source A> --dir /tmp`.
3. Observed: the supersede resolved the **source B** page — struck source B's take row 1 and
   appended the replacement take onto source B's page. Source A's take was untouched. (In our case
   this silently struck a `world`-held take in the shared source while the command was scoped to a
   dept source.)

`takes add` has the same exposure via the same helper (call sites ~198/226/271/350): a scoped add
can attach a take to another source's page that happens to share the slug.

## Expected
Page resolution for take-writes should be scoped to the resolved source (`GBRAIN_SOURCE` / ctx
source), like `delete_page` already is — `softDeletePage(slug, { sourceId })` threads the source
and behaves correctly in the same setup. The asymmetry between the takes commands and the page ops
is the trap.

## Workarounds we use meanwhile
- Never let a slug exist in two sources (namespace shared-source copies, e.g. `canon/<slug>`).
- A post-condition guard around supersede: re-list the page's active takes under the scope and
  verify the requested take id actually flipped `active=false`; treat "still active" as a hard
  failure.

Related: the `takes supersede` active-take lookup bug (listTakes({active:false}) at takes.ts:274,
which we patch locally) — same file; a `getPageId` fix could land together.
title: CLI take-writes resolve pages by slug only (getPageId has no source filter) - cross-source writes when a slug exists in multiple sources

body:
## Summary

On v0.42.56.0, incremental sync can strand image files while advancing the source anchor/checkpoint. The default incremental strategy appears to drop image files instead of routing them through `importImageFile`; using `--strategy auto` admits image paths but routes PNG bytes into the text importer, causing UTF-8 errors. A full sync/walk is the only path that successfully imports image files.

## Observed

Environment: macOS, GBrain v0.42.56.0, Postgres engine, multimodal/image OCR enabled.

- Source repos contained many markdown email pages plus referenced/downloaded image files.
- Incremental/delta sync with the default markdown strategy imported markdown but did not import images.
- The source anchor/checkpoint advanced anyway, so skipped images became permanently stranded from future incremental runs.
- `--strategy auto` did pick up image files, but treated PNG bytes as text and failed with an error like `invalid byte sequence for encoding "UTF8": 0x00`.
- A full sync/walk dispatches image paths correctly through the image import path and can repair the gap, but only if the operator knows to run it.

## Why this is bad

This creates a silent fidelity gap: source markdown looks synced, `last_sync_at` moves forward, but images/OCR never land in the brain. Once the anchor advances, normal incremental sync will not revisit those image files.

## Expected

Incremental sync should handle image assets the same way full sync does:

- If multimodal/image ingestion is enabled, changed image files should dispatch to `importImageFile` / image importer.
- If images are intentionally unsupported for a chosen strategy, the anchor should not silently advance past them; emit a warning/error and/or ledger entry.
- `--strategy auto` should route binary image files to image import, not the text importer.

## Suggested fix direction

Unify full-walk and incremental routing for syncable files. For any changed file whose extension/MIME indicates an image, route to the same image importer used by full sync. Also add a regression test where an incremental commit adds both a markdown file and a PNG/JPEG; after sync, the page/chunk count should include the image asset and the source anchor should advance only after that succeeds or is explicitly ledgered.
title: Incremental sync strands image files while advancing anchors; full sync is required to import images

body:
## Summary

When image OCR fails during import (observed with Anthropic API returning insufficient-credit errors), GBrain imports the image as a filename/stub chunk, reports `errors=0`, and advances normally. The brain looks healthy at the sync/import layer even though OCR text is absent for those image assets.

## Observed

Environment: macOS, GBrain v0.42.56.0, Postgres engine, multimodal/image OCR enabled.

- Anthropic OCR call failed with an API error.
- Image import still completed with `errors=0`.
- `content_chunks.chunk_source='image_asset'` rows were created, but `chunk_text` was only a filename/short stub.
- Passive counters showed the damage only after digging: many OCR attempts failed while importer success/error reporting stayed green.
- The nightly fidelity check had to add a custom heuristic: recent image chunks with very short `chunk_text` indicate OCR failure.

## Why this is bad

This is a silent data-quality failure. Operators see a successful import/sync, but the searchable text layer is missing. Because sync has already advanced, there is no obvious built-in re-OCR/retry path for the damaged image chunks.

## Expected

Any OCR failure should be surfaced as one or more of:

1. Per-file import error/non-zero import `errors` count.
2. A durable failure ledger row with enough metadata to retry OCR later.
3. `gbrain doctor` warning when image chunks have filename/stub text despite OCR being enabled.
4. A repair command such as `gbrain ocr --stale` / `gbrain images repair-ocr` that re-runs OCR for image chunks whose text is missing or suspiciously short.

## Suggested detection

A cheap doctor check could flag:

```sql
SELECT count(*) FILTER (WHERE length(chunk_text) < 120), count(*)
FROM content_chunks
WHERE chunk_source = 'image_asset'
  AND created_at > now() - interval '24 hours';
```

If OCR is enabled and most recent image chunks are short stubs, the import path is not healthy even if sync reports zero errors.
title: Image OCR import fail-opens: OCR API errors create filename stubs with errors=0 and no repair path

body:
**Observed on** 0.42.53.0; **confirmed still present at HEAD** (`master`, checked 2026-07-09).

## Symptom

Importing content whose slug contains any uppercase character fails with:

```
addTag failed: page "session/<sid>/generateText-shape-confirmed" (source=default) not found
```

and because `putPage` and `addTag` run inside one transaction in `importFromContent`, the **entire import rolls back** — pages earlier in the same import are lost too (clean rollback, no partial state, but the whole import fails).

## Root cause

- `validateSlug` (`src/core/utils.ts:32-52`) is the shared chokepoint for `putPage`/`updateSlug` and **returns the slug lowercased** (`return slug.toLowerCase();` at line 52), so the row is inserted under the lowercased slug.
- `importFromContent` (`src/core/import-file.ts`) then calls `tx.addTag(slug, tag, txOpts)` at line 812 with the **original, un-lowercased** `slug` variable.
- `addTag` (`src/core/pglite-engine.ts:3264-3272`) pre-checks existence with a case-sensitive `SELECT id FROM pages WHERE slug = $1` and never normalises, so it cannot find the row `putPage` just wrote, and throws — rolling back the transaction.

The code-tagging path has the same mismatch: `tx.addTag(slug, 'code', ...)` / `tx.addTag(slug, lang, ...)` at `import-file.ts:1171-1172` after `tx.putPage(slug, ...)` at 1161. `addLink` likely too, if it shares the case-sensitive lookup.

## Repro

Any import where the slug (e.g. derived from an item id) is camelCase — `generateText-shape-confirmed` in our case (LLM-generated ids frequently mirror code identifiers). All-lowercase slugs import fine; the first mixed-case one kills the transaction.

## Suggested fix

Normalise once at the top of `importFromContent` (or have `addTag`/`addLink` run their lookups through the same `validateSlug` normalisation) so every statement in the transaction sees the same slug.

## Workaround we're using

Lowercasing slugs on the caller side before import.

Happy to provide more detail. Thanks for gbrain!
title: importFromContent: mixed-case slug rolls back whole import — putPage lowercases via validateSlug, addTag matches case-sensitively

body:
**Version:** 0.42.57.0, PGLite engine, Linux ARM64, bun 1.3.14. Companion to #2675 — hit immediately after working around it.

**Repro:**

`~/.gbrain/config.json`:
```json
{
  "embedding_model": "llama-server:bge-m3",
  "embedding_dimensions": 1024,
  "provider_base_urls": { "llama-server": "http://127.0.0.1:8889/v1" }
}
```

```
$ gbrain import .
Provider "llama-server" requires a specific model name to be configured.

  Set one: gbrain config set embedding_model llama-server:<model-name>
  Or run with --no-embed to import-only and embed later.
```

The config string plainly carries a model (`bge-m3`), and `gbrain config get embedding_model` echoes it back — but every embed path refuses with the "unset" error, telling you to set the thing that is already set.

**Cause:** the preflight in src/core/ai/gateway.ts checks the recipe's *static* touchpoint model list, which is empty **by design** for user-provided recipes, without ever consulting the parsed model id from the config string:

```ts
if (
  Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
  tp.models.length === 0 &&
  (recipe.id === 'litellm' || isUserProvided)
) {
  return { ok: false, reason: 'user_provided_model_unset', ... };
}
```

So `user_provided_model_unset` fires for every valid config, and (with #2675) the llama-server/litellm embedding recipes can never be used.

**Suggested fix:** only reject when the parsed config string itself lacks a model id:

```ts
  (recipe.id === 'litellm' || isUserProvided) &&
  !(parsed.modelId && String(parsed.modelId).trim())
```

With both this and #2675 patched locally, llama-server-recipe embeddings work end-to-end (85-page import, 166 chunks, hybrid query) against a local OpenAI-compatible embedding server.
title: embed preflight rejects user_provided_models recipes even when the config string names a model ("requires a specific model name to be configured")

body:
**Version:** 0.42.57.0, PGLite engine, Linux ARM64, bun 1.3.14

**Repro:**

Point the llama-server recipe at any OpenAI-compatible local embedding server (mine serves BAAI/bge-m3, native 1024-dim, at `http://127.0.0.1:8889/v1` — `/v1/models` and `/v1/embeddings` both implemented):

```
$ gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model llama-server:bge-m3
Provider llama-server requires --embedding-dimensions <N> when using --embedding-model llama-server:bge-m3.

$ gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model llama-server:bge-m3 --embedding-dimensions 1024
Refusing to init: Provider "llama-server" model "bge-m3" does not support custom dimensions 1024 (this model only emits its default vector size). Either drop --embedding-dimensions or pick a Matryoshka-aware model.
```

Every path is rejected, so the recipe is unusable for embeddings as shipped.

**Cause:** `validateDimAgainstTouchpoint` (src/core/embedding-dim-check.ts) treats any `requestedDims !== defaultDims` as a Matryoshka truncation request and walks the custom-dim allow-lists. For user-provided-model recipes the touchpoint declares `default_dims: 0` ("forces explicit --embedding-dimensions" per the recipe comment), so the explicit value can never equal the default and always falls through to the tier-3 rejection.

**Suggested fix:** when `default_dims === 0` (user-provided-model recipes), treat the explicit dims as the declared native size and skip the custom-dim validation:

```ts
if (requestedDims !== undefined && requestedDims !== defaultDims && defaultDims !== 0) {
  // ... existing precedence walk
}
```

Locally patched this way and the recipe works end-to-end (init → import → query) against a llama-server-wire embedding endpoint.
title: user_provided_models recipes (llama-server, litellm): init is a catch-22 — requires --embedding-dimensions, then rejects every value

body:
## Summary
When a PGLite brain store is damaged (heap corruption from a crash-mid-write), every new process aborts during PGLite init with `Aborted(). Build with -sASSERTIONS for more info.` — and gbrain prints the canned macOS 26.3 WASM bug hint (#223) for it. The hint sent us chasing a phantom OS bug for a corrupted-store problem.

## Evidence it was not the OS/WASM bug (macOS 27.0 beta, Bun 1.4.x, gbrain 0.42.44.0, PGLite 0.4.3)
- Fresh standalone PGLite 0.4.3 **with vector + pg_trgm** created and queried fine on the same machine while gbrain commands aborted.
- `gbrain doctor` (filesystem checks) reported 95 unresolved sync failures with `dead heap-only tuple (0, 136) is not linked to from any HOT` — classic Postgres heap corruption, likely from a crash during concurrent access to the single-writer store.
- Quarantining the store and running `gbrain init --pglite` fresh: all commands work immediately; full re-sync + re-embed restored parity.

## Ask
1. Distinguish init-abort causes where possible (corrupted-store read vs runtime WASM incompatibility), or soften the banner to "most common causes include ... OR a damaged store — try `gbrain init` into a scratch GBRAIN_HOME to isolate".
2. Consider a `gbrain doctor` probe that attempts a scratch-store init to separate "environment can't run PGLite" from "this store is damaged".

Happy to provide more detail from our forensics.
title: Corrupted-store PGLite init abort is mislabeled as the macOS 26.3 WASM bug — banner masks the real cause

body:
## Summary

On an existing Postgres brain, `initSchema()` (and therefore `gbrain init --migrate-only`, `apply-migrations`, and any command that brings up the schema) crashes with:

```
column "event_page_id" does not exist
```

The schema stays stuck at v119 and migrations v120–v122 never apply. `gbrain sources status` also prints `Schema probe failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist` on every run.

## Root cause

`initSchema()` runs `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap(conn)` and then replays the embedded schema (`SCHEMA_SQL`) *before* running incremental migrations:

- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:327` → `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap(conn)`
- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:329` → `conn.unsafe(sqlText)` (SCHEMA_SQL replay)
- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:332` → `runMigrations(this)`

The embedded schema now references `timeline_entries.event_page_id` (added by migration **v121** `timeline_entries_event_page_id`):

- `src/core/schema-embedded.ts:549` → `event_page_id INTEGER REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE`
- `src/core/schema-embedded.ts:561-562` → `CREATE INDEX ... ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) ...`

On an existing brain `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS timeline_entries` is a no-op, so the column is **not** added, and the subsequent `CREATE INDEX ... (event_page_id)` in the SCHEMA_SQL replay throws. Because this happens before `runMigrations`, v121 (which would add the column) never gets a chance to run — the transaction rolls back and the version stays at 119.

`applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` exists precisely to add columns the embedded schema forward-references but old brains lack. Every other such reference has a bootstrap entry — e.g. v79 `pages.last_retrieved_at` at `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:802` (`ALTER TABLE pages ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS last_retrieved_at ...`). The `event_page_id` case is missing its equivalent.

## Fix

Add an `event_page_id` bootstrap entry to `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` (both the Postgres and PGLite mirrors), matching the v79 pattern:

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
```

(guarded by a `timeline_entries` existence probe, like the others) so the SCHEMA_SQL index creation succeeds and `runMigrations` can then apply v121's FK + indexes idempotently.

## Repro

1. A Postgres brain stamped at schema v119 (pre-v121).
2. Upgrade to >= the version that introduced v121 / the embedded `event_page_id` index.
3. `gbrain init --migrate-only` → prints `column "event_page_id" does not exist`, schema stays 119.

## Workaround

Manually apply v121's DDL (`ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;` plus its FK/indexes), then re-run `gbrain init --migrate-only` — migrations then advance 120→122 cleanly.

Seen on v0.42.57.0 (commit 058f448), Postgres backend.
title: v119 to v122 migration failure: column "event_page_id" does not exist

body:
