### Summary

Upgrading an old brain (created around v0.17/v0.18, PGLite engine) from v0.18.2 straight to v0.42.57.0 fails on the very first pending migration: `v0.11.0 (GBrain Minions)` aborts with `column "event_page_id" does not exist`.

### Root cause (as far as I can tell)

Migration `v0.11.0` "Phase A (schema)" replays the current embedded schema, which includes the Life Chronicle indexes introduced much later:

```
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
```

But the column `event_page_id` itself is added by a v0.42.x migration (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`, src/core/migrate.ts) that runs AFTER v0.11.0 in the ledger order. On a brain whose ledger still has v0.11.0 pending (mine was "MINIONS HALF-INSTALLED (partial migration: 0.11.0)" per doctor), the old migration references a column created by a newer one: chicken-and-egg, and `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` aborts.

### Workaround that unblocked me

Manually adding the column first (same definition as the upstream migration, additive and idempotent):

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
```

then re-running `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` (multiple passes until the PARTIAL backfills drained). Everything completed cleanly afterwards: doctor reports 0 failed checks on a 14 GB / ~14.8k pages PGLite brain.

### Suggested fix

Either make v0.11.0 Phase A tolerant of forward-referenced columns (skip index creation when the column is missing: the later migration recreates it), or have the schema replay apply the guarded column additions before index creation.

### Environment

- gbrain v0.42.57.0 (git 058f448b), upgraded from v0.18.2 (git 08b3698e)
- PGLite engine, brain ~14 GB, ~14 800 pages
- bun on Linux x64

Thanks for #2348 by the way: we hit the live-lock steal corruption in production the very same day, and the new never-steal behavior plus the recovery hints are exactly right.
title: Migration wedge: v0.11.0 (Minions) Phase A references event_page_id, a column created by a later v0.42.x migration (old-brain upgrade 0.18.2 -> 0.42.57)

body:
## Summary

On the real PostgreSQL engine, `gbrain init --migrate-only` deterministically fails to apply the v0.42.56.0 (Life Chronicle) schema migrations **v121** (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`) and **v122** (`facts_ontology_dimension`) with:

```
column "event_page_id" does not exist
```

The migration transaction rolls back cleanly (no partial state), so the brain stays at the pre-migration version. **PGLite is unaffected** (it tolerates the pattern), which is likely why the chronicle test-suite (PGLite-based) didn't catch it — same class as the v0.42.55.0 `#1385` fix where "PGLite tolerated it" but real PG rejected it.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.42.57.0 (introduced in v0.42.56.0 with migrations v121/v122)
- Engine: PostgreSQL + pgvector, direct connection (port 5432)
- Repro: `gbrain init --migrate-only` on a brain sitting below schema v121

## Root cause

Each migration's whole multi-statement `sql` block is sent to the engine in one call: `runMigrationSQL` (`src/core/migrate.ts`) → `tx.runMigration(version, sql)` / `conn.executeRaw(sql)` → `PostgresEngine.runUnsafe` → **`conn.unsafe(sql)`** (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts`, postgres.js).

On real PostgreSQL that path **parse-analyzes the entire multi-statement batch before executing any statement**. v121's block is:

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
DO $$ BEGIN IF NOT EXISTS (...) THEN ALTER TABLE timeline_entries
  ADD CONSTRAINT timeline_entries_event_page_id_fkey
  FOREIGN KEY (event_page_id) REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE; END IF; END $$;
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
```

So `CREATE INDEX ... (event_page_id)` is validated **before** the preceding `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN event_page_id` has executed → "column does not exist". v122 has the same shape (`ALTER TABLE facts ADD COLUMN dimension/value/value_hash/dim_status`, then `CREATE INDEX ... (dimension, ...)`).

The **exact same SQL applies cleanly via `psql`** (the simple query protocol executes statement-by-statement), verified in a `BEGIN; … ROLLBACK;` transaction against a live brain.

## Notes

- `GBRAIN_PREPARE=false` does **not** fix it — that toggles prepared-statement caching, not simple-vs-extended protocol. `runUnsafe` calls `conn.unsafe()` (not `.simple()`), so it still batch-parses. Confirmed: re-running with `GBRAIN_PREPARE=false` ("Prepared statements disabled" logged) fails identically.
- The daemon's `initSchema` path (on `gbrain serve` startup) **does** apply these migrations successfully on the same real-Postgres brain — so the CLI `--migrate-only` path and the daemon path diverge in how they execute migration SQL. Worth reconciling the two.

## Suggested fix

Execute each migration's `sql` **statement-by-statement** (a `DO $$…$$`-aware splitter, or postgres.js `.simple()` for the batch) so an `ADD COLUMN` commits its catalog change before a later `CREATE INDEX` in the same migration is parse-analyzed — matching PGLite, `psql`, and the daemon's initSchema behavior. (Alternatively split each add-column-then-index migration into two ordered entries, but the executor fix protects every future migration of this shape.)

## Workaround

Pre-create the columns via `psql`, then re-run `gbrain init --migrate-only` (the `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` becomes a no-op and the `CREATE INDEX` then parse-validates fine):

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
ALTER TABLE facts ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS dimension  TEXT;
ALTER TABLE facts ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS value      TEXT;
ALTER TABLE facts ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS value_hash TEXT;
ALTER TABLE facts ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS dim_status TEXT;
```
title: init --migrate-only fails on real Postgres for v121/v122 chronicle migrations: "column event_page_id does not exist"

body:
## Symptom

On a multi-source brain, dream phase outputs (patterns / synthesize product files) sometimes land in the work tree of a *different* registered source instead of the host/default source.

Observed on 0.42.56.0 (three sources: default wiki + a gstack checkout + a read-only KB clone):
- After a patterns run, 3 stray output files appeared inside the gstack source work tree and 1 inside the KB clone work tree — none belong to those sources.
- This recurs on every patterns run (verified twice on consecutive runs after clean-up).
- Earlier in June the same mechanism left ~74 stray pages that later synced into the wrong source; because one of those work trees prunes untracked dirs, strays can also trigger un-syncable page deletions downstream.

## Impact

Wrong-source pages pollute cross-source search and require manual surgery (we've now cleaned up twice, with backups). Combined with source-side pruning, it can escalate from cosmetic to data-loss-adjacent.

## Suggested fix

Dream writers should resolve output paths against the host/default source root explicitly, rather than whatever work tree the phase's cwd/traversal happens to touch.
title: dream: patterns/synthesize write output files into other registered sources' work trees (multi-source brain)

body:
## Symptom

The `synthesize` dream phase respects a cooldown (`cooldown_hours`), but the `patterns` phase has none. With autopilot micro-cycles at a short interval (ours: 300s), patterns re-runs immediately after completing — every run at full LLM price.

Observed on 0.42.56.0 (macOS, multi-source brain, patterns on claude-haiku):
- Two complete patterns runs within ~5 minutes of each other, both full work (no "recently ran, skipping").
- When an external scheduled run overlaps an autopilot window, we get a guaranteed daily double-run. Single run measured at ~920K input / ~10K output tokens, so the duplicate roughly doubles daily dream spend.

## Workaround we run today

`dream.patterns.enabled=false` + an external launchd job that opens a once-daily window. Works, but re-implements what a phase cooldown would do natively.

## Suggested fix

Give patterns the same cooldown treatment as synthesize (`cooldown_hours`-style config, or a shared dream-phase cooldown framework), so autopilot can keep the phase enabled without duplicate spend.
title: dream: patterns phase has no cooldown — back-to-back full-price re-runs under autopilot micro-cycle

body:
## Symptom

`gbrain schema stats` (and the MCP `schema_stats` op) reports a declared prefix as dead even when live pages exist under it.

Repro on our brain (gbrain 0.42.56.0): custom pack declares `ops` with `path_prefixes: [ops/]`. We have 5 non-deleted pages with slugs `ops/tasks`, `ops/_task-inbox`, etc. `dead_prefixes` still lists `{type: ops, prefix: ops/}`.

## Root cause

`src/core/schema-pack/stats.ts` → `detectDeadPrefixes()` counts by **source_path**:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pages WHERE deleted_at IS NULL AND source_path LIKE 'prefix%'
```

Pages created via `put_page` / MCP (rather than file sync) have `source_path = NULL` — on our brain that is 4394 of 5950 pages. Any prefix populated mostly/only by put_page pages reads as dead, even though the slugs match the prefix.

## Suggested fix

Match on `slug` (the taxonomy identity the prefix hints describe), or `COALESCE(source_path, slug)`:

```sql
WHERE deleted_at IS NULL AND slug LIKE 'prefix%'
```

Cosmetic only (the hint list misleads schema audits), no behavior impact.
title: schema stats: dead_prefixes false-positives — detectDeadPrefixes matches source_path, so put_page-created pages (NULL source_path) never count

body:
## Summary

`gbrain takes supersede <slug> --row N` always fails with **"Row #N not found"** when the target take is **active** — i.e. the normal case. `cmdSupersede` looks up the target with `listTakes({ active: false, ... })`, which on the Postgres engine renders as `WHERE t.active = false`, so it can only ever match an already-*inactive* take, never the active one you want to supersede. The command `process.exit(1)`s before `engine.supersedeTake` runs, so nothing is superseded.

Observed on **gbrain 0.42.52.0** (Postgres engine). The offending line is unchanged on `master` as of this writing.

## Repro (Postgres-backed brain)

```sh
gbrain put demo <<'MD'
---
type: note
title: demo
---
# demo
MD
gbrain takes add demo --claim "Original claim." --kind take --who people/alice --dir /tmp
# take is added at row 1, active=true
gbrain takes supersede demo --row 1 --claim "New claim." --dir /tmp
# -> "Row #1 not found on demo."  (exit 1); the take is NOT superseded
```

## Root cause

`src/commands/takes.ts`, `cmdSupersede`:

```ts
const existing = await engine.listTakes({ page_id: pageId, active: false, limit: 500 });
const target = existing.find(t => t.row_num === rowNum);
if (!target) { console.error(`Row #${rowNum} not found on ${slug}.`); process.exit(1); }
```

`listTakes` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts`) does `const active = opts.active ?? true;` then
`AND (${active}::boolean IS NULL OR t.active = ${active}::boolean)`, so `active: false`
filters to **inactive** takes. The row being superseded is active, so it is excluded and
never found.

## Fix

Look up the **active** take being superseded:

```ts
const existing = await engine.listTakes({ page_id: pageId, active: true, limit: 500 });
```

With `active: true`, the lookup finds the row and `engine.supersedeTake` runs correctly
(verified against Postgres: the old row flips `active=false` + `superseded_by` is set, and
the winning row is appended).

## Minor related note

On a Postgres-backed / no-repo brain, `takes add` and `takes supersede` also hard-exit with
"No brain directory configured" unless `--dir <existing path>` is passed (`resolveBrainDir`).
This may be intended, but it's surprising for headless/Postgres deployments and worth
documenting.
title: takes supersede cannot supersede an active take (cmdSupersede queries listTakes({active:false}))

body:
## Bug

`~/.gbrain/config.json` can contain `voyage_api_key`, but the AI gateway does not fold it into `VOYAGE_API_KEY`. As a result, multimodal/image embedding with Voyage fails unless the caller manually exports `VOYAGE_API_KEY` in the process environment.

## Evidence from v0.42.56.0

- `src/core/ai/build-gateway-config.ts` folds only:
  - `openai_api_key` → `OPENAI_API_KEY`
  - `anthropic_api_key` → `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
  - `zeroentropy_api_key` → `ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY`
- It does **not** fold:
  - `voyage_api_key` → `VOYAGE_API_KEY`
- `src/core/brain-score-recommendations.ts` explicitly excludes `VOYAGE_API_KEY` from `HOSTED_EMBED_KEY_CONFIG`, with a comment saying this is because `buildGatewayConfig` does not thread it today.
- `gbrain providers list` reports Voyage missing when `voyage_api_key` is present only in config; with `VOYAGE_API_KEY` exported from the same config value, Voyage reports ready.

## Why this matters

Image-bearing sources need multimodal embeddings/OCR during sync. For launchd/daemon/MCP contexts, config-file keys are the durable mechanism; requiring an extra process-env export makes Voyage behave differently from OpenAI/Anthropic/ZeroEntropy and leads to silent or confusing image-pipeline failures.

## Expected fix

Map config-plane `voyage_api_key` into the gateway env just like ZeroEntropy:

```ts
if (c.voyage_api_key) envFromConfig.VOYAGE_API_KEY = c.voyage_api_key;
```

Then update the recommendation/doctor key map and tests so config-keyed Voyage is treated as usable by the same runtime that executes embeds.

## Workaround

Every wrapper that can import/sync image-bearing sources must currently do something like:

```bash
export VOYAGE_API_KEY="$(python3 -c 'import json,os;print(json.load(open(os.path.expanduser("~/.gbrain/config.json"))).get("voyage_api_key",""))')"
```
title: voyage_api_key in config.json is not folded into gateway env; multimodal requires manual VOYAGE_API_KEY export

body:
## Bug / safety issue

`gbrain dream --phase lint` appears to run the lint core in fix mode and can write back to the source tree. For agent-managed workspaces this is a surprising side effect: most dream phases are DB-only/read-only, but `lint` can mutate markdown files.

## Expected behavior

One of:

- `dream --phase lint` defaults to read-only and reports proposed fixes, or
- expose an explicit `--fix` / `--no-fix` flag, with non-mutating behavior available for scheduled maintenance, or
- document prominently that `lint` is filesystem-mutating and should be excluded from read-only dream cycles.

## Why this matters

Users may schedule dream/maintenance cycles expecting brain/index updates, not source rewrites. Agents also need a safe mode they can run without risking unreviewed edits in a synced/git-managed workspace.

## Current workaround

Exclude `lint` from scheduled dream loops and run other phases only.
title: dream lint phase mutates source tree with no no-fix/dry-run mode

body:
## Problem

`INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS.md` is read by autonomous coding agents during setup. In its current shape it can push an agent toward a much larger, more privileged install than the user actually asked for.

Concrete hazards from an agent-operator perspective:

- It encourages broad scaffolding/install behavior instead of a minimal, user-scoped setup.
- It tells agents to save durable/persistent memories, which crosses the boundary between project docs and an operator's agent memory policy.
- It makes it easy for an agent to enable long-running automation/autopilot behavior before the user has explicitly chosen that operating mode.

## Expected behavior

Agent-facing install docs should be constrained and consent-aware:

- Default to the smallest useful setup.
- Separate optional power-user setup behind explicit user choices.
- Avoid telling the agent to write to its own persistent memory; instead ask it to document local project state in normal project files.
- Call out long-running daemons/autopilot as opt-in.

## Why this matters

Agent install docs are not normal human docs. Agents may treat them as operating instructions and execute broad steps literally. The safer pattern is "mechanics only by default; escalation requires explicit user approval."
title: INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS encourages over-broad agent setup and persistent-memory writes

body:
`src/mcp/server.ts` hardcodes the takes visibility allow-list for the stdio transport:

```ts
return dispatchToolCall(engine, name, params, {
  remote: true,
  takesHoldersAllowList: ['world'],
  ...
```

There is no env var, config key, or CLI flag to widen it. Any take whose `holder` is not exactly `world` (e.g. `self`, or a named holder) is invisible to **every** MCP caller on stdio — `takes_list`, `takes_search`, and takes surfaced via `query`. The code comment suggests `gbrain call <op>` as the operator escape hatch, which works, but agents connected over stdio MCP (Claude Code/Desktop, the primary integration) can never see their operator's own private takes even though the pipe is local and single-user.

### Repro (v0.42.8.0, Postgres engine)

1. A takes row with `holder='self'`, `active=true`, on a live page (written via `mcp:put_page` fence).
2. `gbrain call takes_list '{}'` → returns the row (local trusted path, allow-list unset). ✅
3. Same brain over stdio MCP (`gbrain serve`): `takes_list {}` → `[]`; `takes_list {"holder":"self"}` → `[]`; `takes_search` → `[]`. ❌
4. Verified the row passes every SQL filter in `listTakes` (active, live page join); the only excluding predicate is `holder = ANY(['world'])`.

### Why it matters

For a single-user brain, `holder='self'` is the natural holder for the operator's own claims — the v0.28 default-deny (sensible for multi-tenant HTTP tokens) makes them permanently unreachable on the transport that local agents actually use. Downstream effect: an agent pipeline that checks "do I have a prior take on X?" via `takes_search` always gets an empty set and concludes NO PRIOR, which is worse than an error because it reads as a confident negative.

### Suggested fix

Any of:
- `GBRAIN_MCP_TAKES_HOLDERS=self,world` env override read by `src/mcp/server.ts` (stdio only, keep the `['world']` default);
- honor `permissions.takes_holders` from a config key for stdio like the http path does;
- a `serve --takes-holders` flag.

Related: #2529 covers the http-transport flavor (legacy bearer tokens ignore `permissions.takes_holders`); this issue is the stdio transport, where there is no token to carry permissions at all. The CLI-verb siblings (`gbrain takes list` printing "No takes on list" while `gbrain call takes_list` returns rows, and `gbrain call takes_search` dying on `JSON.stringify cannot serialize BigInt`) look like #2450.
title: stdio MCP: takesHoldersAllowList hardcoded to ['world'] — no operator override, so non-world takes are unreachable via MCP (v0.42.8, still on master)

body:
Running gbrain 0.42.25.0 (PGLite engine) as the intended store for a discovery-style workload (an enterprise-architecture graph: ~31,000 nodes, ~128,000 typed edges). Three gaps forced the benchmark to run on PGLite directly instead of through gbrain — filing them with the measured evidence.

## 1 · Bulk import of nodes + typed edges (the blocker)

`gbrain import <dir>` bulk-imports pages, but edges only exist via `gbrain link <from> <to>` — one CLI invocation (~1s engine boot) per edge. 128k edges ≈ 35+ hours of process spawns. Body wikilinks (`[[slug]]`) are **not** parsed into link records (verified: `backlinks` returns `[]` for a page whose body carries wikilinks).

**Ask** — any of: `gbrain import --edges edges.jsonl` (`{src, dst, type}` per line) · opt-in wikilink→link extraction on import · a documented JSONL/CSV bulk format for pages + links in one pass.

Any discovery-style ingestion (codebase graphs, lineage, org models) produces edges in bulk; per-edge CLI calls make the graph features unusable at scale.

## 2 · Brain isolation (`--db` / env var / profiles)

One global brain per user (`~/.gbrain/config.json` → `database_path`). A benchmark, test, or per-project estate has no safe home — loading 31k synthetic nodes into a personal brain isn't acceptable, so there's currently no way to test against gbrain at all.

**Ask:** `gbrain --db <path>` or `GBRAIN_DB=` or named profiles.

## 3 · Design note: materialize partition-scoped edge sets

Measured on the engine underneath (same schema shape): partition-scoped traversal via query-time join-filtering was **slower** (3.7s) than whole-graph traversal (p95 2.8s) despite a 3× smaller result — the per-hop join costs more than the frontier reduction saves. If gbrain grows scoped traversal (per tag/type), materialized partition edge sets (partial index / edge-table partition) beat query-time filters decisively at 100k+ edges.

## Benchmark numbers (for context)

PGLite handled the scale respectably: full load 9–15s, incremental 1% upsert <110ms, depth-5 traversal p95 ~4.5s (realistic node-text width). Server Postgres 16, same schema and code: **p95 860ms**. Happy to share the harness.

Filed by a daily gbrain user — these two features would let this workload run through gbrain proper (including its hybrid retrieval, which is the part we'd most like to benchmark).
title: Bulk graph import (nodes + typed edges) + brain isolation (--db) — with benchmark evidence at 31k nodes / 128k edges

body:
`dream.drift.enabled` is documented as the switch for drift detection — the dream cycle re-checking existing takes against newer evidence. Setting it does nothing, at three levels:

1. **Never wired.** `runPhaseDrift` (src/core/cycle/drift.ts) has zero call sites. The dream cycle's phase chain (`lint → backlinks → sync → synthesize → extract → patterns → embed → orphans`, per the dream command's own docs) never included a drift phase. Same shipped-but-unwired class as the conversation-parser probe before #2630.

2. **Scaffold-only even if wired.** The v0.28 body resolves a model and builds a `BudgetMeter`, then discards both (`void modelId; void meter;`) and returns only a candidate count. No report, no judgments, nothing an operator can read.

3. **The judge never shipped.** The in-code comments defer LLM-driven weight adjustment to a "v0.29 follow-up" that never landed. `dream.drift.auto_update` gates code that does not exist. The module arrived in v0.28.6 (#563) and has only been touched since by the v0.31.12 model-id sweep (#844) — fourteen-plus releases of a config surface with no feature behind it.

## The surfacing half works, and it aged well

I ran `findDriftCandidates` directly against a live brain (~10.7k takes from the bootstrap, timeline populated by the v0.42.56.0 chronicle backfill): it surfaced **76 candidates** — soft-band takes (weight 0.3–0.85, active, unresolved) on pages with fresh `timeline_entries` evidence from the last 30 days. The Life Chronicle gives this query exactly the evidence signal the original design wanted; the two features compose neatly. The candidate set looked genuinely worth re-examining, so the missing judge is the only thing between this scaffold and a useful feature.

## Offer

Happy to implement the full phase as a PR — wiring into the dream chain, the LLM judge with real `BudgetMeter` enforcement, a report surface, and `auto_update` semantics. Holding off because the design calls feel like yours to make first:

- judge model tier (the scaffold resolves `models.drift` → reasoning tier → sonnet fallback);
- where drift reports land (a page? doctor check? dream summary line?);
- what `auto_update=true` is allowed to change (weight only? `resolved_at`? supersede?).

Point me at your preferred shape and I'll build it.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01FQgByq4aqQq2PP8UHCdnfk
title: dream.drift.enabled gates an unwired scaffold — drift detection never actually ships
rerank: very long docs can stall local llama-server rerankers — per-doc truncation?

body:
## Bug Description

A Bun global GitHub install can miss an upstream update and then fail the obvious force-update path with Bun's `DependencyLoop` error.

Observed on a global install where the package was already installed from GitHub:

- Installed package before update: `gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#6461790`
- Installed CLI version before update: `gbrain 0.42.56.0`
- Remote GitHub `HEAD`: `058f448b9a4ba3d522e2c2a7a4615bccdd00ae76`
- Remote package version at that commit: `0.42.57.0`

`gbrain self-upgrade --check-only --json` reported no update even though GitHub HEAD was ahead. Running `gbrain self-upgrade` also reported the install was up to date.

Trying to force the GitHub update directly with Bun failed while replacing one GitHub commit with another:

```console
$ bun add -g github:garrytan/gbrain
bun add v1.3.14 (0d9b296a)
Resolving dependencies
Resolved, downloaded and extracted [36]
error: Package "gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#058f448" has a dependency loop
  Resolution: "gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#6461790"
  Dependency: "gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain"
error: An internal error occurred (DependencyLoop)
```

The workaround was to back up the installed package, remove it globally, then install GitHub HEAD cleanly:

```bash
cp -a ~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/gbrain ~/.hermes/backups/gbrain-force-update-20260706-212121/gbrain
bun remove -g gbrain
bun add -g github:garrytan/gbrain
gbrain apply-migrations --yes --non-interactive
```

After that:

```console
$ gbrain --version
gbrain 0.42.57.0

$ bun pm ls -g | grep -i gbrain
└── gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#058f448

$ gbrain self-upgrade --check-only --json
{
  "current_version": "0.42.57.0",
  "latest_version": "",
  "update_available": false,
  "install_method": "bun",
  "release_url": "",
  "changelog_diff": ""
}
```

Note: Bun also blocked the package `postinstall`, so migrations needed to be run manually. That part is adjacent to #218, but the `DependencyLoop` during commit-to-commit global GitHub update appears separate.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Install gbrain globally from GitHub with Bun at one commit:
   ```bash
   bun add -g github:garrytan/gbrain
   ```
2. Wait for `garrytan/gbrain` `master`/`HEAD` to advance.
3. Check the package and remote:
   ```bash
   gbrain --version
   bun pm ls -g | grep -i gbrain
   git ls-remote https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain.git HEAD
   ```
4. Run the self-upgrade check:
   ```bash
   gbrain self-upgrade --check-only --json
   gbrain self-upgrade
   ```
5. Try the direct Bun GitHub reinstall:
   ```bash
   bun add -g github:garrytan/gbrain
   ```

## Expected Behavior

Either:

1. `gbrain self-upgrade --check-only --json` detects that the installed GitHub commit is behind remote HEAD and reports `update_available: true`, then `gbrain self-upgrade` updates it; or
2. `gbrain self-upgrade` recognizes that this Bun global GitHub install cannot be updated in-place and performs a safe remove/reinstall flow itself; or
3. at minimum, the tool reports a paste-ready recovery command that works for Bun global GitHub installs.

## Actual Behavior

- `gbrain self-upgrade --check-only --json` returns `update_available: false` with empty `latest_version`.
- `gbrain self-upgrade` reports the current version is up to date.
- Direct `bun add -g github:garrytan/gbrain` fails with Bun `DependencyLoop`.
- Manual remove/reinstall is required.

## Related Issues / PRs

- #302: similar update-detection blind spot, but for source-linked installs.
- #486: `check-update --json` reports no releases / empty latest version.
- #1029 / #1030: Bun global upgrade cwd issue.
- #218: Bun global install can block `postinstall`, hiding migrations.

This issue is specifically the Bun global GitHub package path where the installed package is already `gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#<old_commit>` and replacing it with `#<new_commit>` via `bun add -g github:garrytan/gbrain` trips `DependencyLoop`.

## Environment

- OS: macOS 26.5.2
- Bun: `1.3.14 (0d9b296a)`
- gbrain before: `0.42.56.0`, `gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#6461790`
- gbrain after manual workaround: `0.42.57.0`, `gbrain@github:garrytan/gbrain#058f448`
- Install location: `~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/gbrain`
- Binary symlink: `~/.bun/bin/gbrain -> ../install/global/node_modules/gbrain/src/cli.ts`
title: Bun global GitHub install: self-upgrade misses HEAD and direct reinstall fails with DependencyLoop

body:
## Summary

The `extract_facts` dream phase is permanently blocked by its legacy-row guard when pre-v0.32 fact rows exist, even after `apply-migrations` reports up-to-date — and `forget_fact` cannot clear it because the guard's SQL does not exclude expired rows.

## Environment

- gbrain 0.42.56.0, postgres engine (self-hosted), macOS (Apple Silicon)
- v0.32.2 migration: **applied** (per `gbrain apply-migrations --list`)

## Repro / observed behavior

1. Brain had 17 fact rows with `row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL` (written 2026-06-22 ~ 06-29 by a pre-fence-era writer, i.e. *after* v0.32.2 was applied — so the migration backfill never saw them).
2. Every `gbrain dream --phase extract_facts` run exits with:
   `extract_facts skipped: 17 legacy v0.31 facts pending fence backfill. Run gbrain apply-migrations --yes to complete v0_32_2 ...`
3. `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` → `All migrations up to date.` (no-op; guard count unchanged)
4. `forget_fact` on one of the rows → `{"expired": true, "path": "legacy_db"}` — but the guard count **stays at 17**, because the guard query is:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM facts WHERE row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL
```

(`src/core/cycle/extract-facts.ts` ~L120) — no `expired_at IS NULL` filter. So the officially sanctioned removal path (`forget_fact`) can never unblock the phase.

## Impact

`extract_facts` (fence → DB reconcile) is silently dead for any brain in this state; downstream consolidate/takes see stale facts. We only noticed via a manual dream-log audit.

## Suggested fixes (either)

1. Guard excludes soft-removed rows: `AND expired_at IS NULL` (lets `forget_fact` drain the backlog), and/or
2. `apply-migrations` re-runs the v0_32_2 backfill when matching rows exist even if the migration is marked applied (rows can postdate the migration if an old writer is still around).

## Workaround we used

Backed up the 17 rows to JSON, deleted them directly in SQL — phase immediately recovered (`507 fact(s) reconciled across 4455 page(s)`).
title: extract_facts guard counts expired legacy rows — forget_fact can't unblock the phase, apply-migrations no-ops

body:
Environment: macOS, Postgres engine (Postgres.app + pgvector), upgrading 0.42.37.0 → 0.42.57.0 via bun.
What happens: After gbrain upgrade, schema sits at 115 (latest 122). Both gbrain apply-migrations --yes and gbrain init --migrate-only fail identically:
=== Applying migration v0.11.0: GBrain Minions — durable background agents ===
Phase A (schema) failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist. Aborting; re-run after fixing.
Root cause: The v0.11.0 feature migration (or its schema probe) queries timeline_entries.event_page_id, but that column is created by schema migration v121 (timeline_entries_event_page_id, migrate.ts ~5444), which hasn't run yet at schema 115. The probe path in advisor/collect-chronicle.ts guards for this ("may be absent pre-migration; ignore") but the migration path does not. Deterministic; retries fail identically.
Workaround (verified): Manually apply v121's own idempotent SQL via psql (ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS, guarded FK, both partial indexes), then gbrain init --migrate-only and gbrain apply-migrations --yes complete normally: schema 115 → 122, all 16 feature migrations pass, data intact (1,241 pages).
Suggested fix: Run pending schema migrations (v116-122) before feature migrations, or guard the v0.11.0 probe the same way collect-chronicle.ts does.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: (`gbrain version`)
- OS:
- Bun version: (`bun --version`)
- Database: Supabase / self-hosted Postgres

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**
```json
(paste output here)
```
title: Migration ordering bug: v0.11.0 Minions probe queries timeline_entries.event_page_id before schema migration v121 creates it

body:
## Problem

`query` and `search` hits return `slug, title, type, chunk_text, score, effective_date`, but not the page's frontmatter. The frontmatter is already in `pages.frontmatter` (JSONB, GIN-indexed) on the row being projected, and `get_page` already returns it in full.

So any consumer that wants to act on metadata (filter hits by a frontmatter key, route by document class, respect a custom tagging scheme) has to make one `get_page` call per hit: a query with `limit: 24` becomes 25 round-trips. Over the MCP transport or the HTTP connector that's the difference between one call and a chatty loop, and it pushes consumers toward re-deriving metadata from slug paths instead, which is fragile.

Every mainstream retrieval store (Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, pgvector wrappers) returns document metadata alongside matches for exactly this reason. Feels like a projection gap rather than a missing feature: the data's there, the query result shape just doesn't carry it.

## Use cases

- **Class-filtered retrieval.** Vaults tag pages with a taxonomy key in frontmatter (document type, visibility, status, an epistemic class like authored-vs-imported). A consumer that should only surface one class, say "only my own notes, never imported articles," currently can't tell hits apart without fetching each page. This is the one I hit: I filter hits by a frontmatter key before passing them to a local model, and today that's a file read per hit.
- **Richer result rendering.** Anything showing query results to a human (a picker, a dashboard, a brief) wants author, date, source URL, or tags on the result card. All of it is in frontmatter; none of it is on the hit.
- **Downstream routing.** Agent pipelines that branch on metadata (send meeting notes to one prompt, reference docs to another) need the key at hit time, not after a second fetch, or the routing cost scales with `limit`.

## Proposed shape

An opt-in param on the query/search operations, default off so existing consumers see zero change in payload size or behavior:

```
query { query: "...", limit: 24, include_frontmatter: true }
```

When set, each hit gains a `frontmatter` field (the same object `get_page` returns). Contract-first as usual: param added in `src/core/operations.ts`, so CLI, MCP, and tools-json pick it up from the one definition and the parity tests keep them in sync.

## What it doesn't touch

Ranking, fusion, and SQL ranking paths are untouched, it's a projection-only change. I'd include `gbrain eval replay` results in the PR (expecting Jaccard@k 1.0, top-1 stability 100%, latency delta ~0) plus unit and E2E coverage per the R1-R4 isolation rules.

Happy to PR this if the shape works for you. Also fine if you'd rather it ride the existing audit-column direction (`source_kind`, `ingested_via`) instead, the need is "hits carry metadata," not this exact param.
title: feat(query): opt-in frontmatter on query/search hits (avoid N+1 get_page for metadata-aware consumers)

body:
## Summary

The default Dream cycle can successfully process changed pages in its incremental extract phase while leaving `pages.links_extracted_at` unchanged. Those pages remain permanently visible to `gbrain extract --stale` and `links_extraction_lag` until an operator runs a separate stale sweep.

This is a gap in the #1696 / #1755 maintenance-loop fix.

## Reproduction

Run Dream with both `sync` and `extract` enabled against a non-default source after changing pages.

Observed production example:

- Dream sync: `+6 added, ~6 modified`
- Dream extract: `0 link(s), 0 timeline entries (incremental: 12 slugs)`
- `pages_processed: 12`
- all 12 pages retained stale or NULL `links_extracted_at`

Across the affected brain, doctor reported 84 stale pages: 63 had NULL watermarks and 21 had `updated_at > links_extracted_at`. Forty-two of those pages already had outgoing links, confirming that extraction work and freshness accounting had diverged.

## Root cause

`runCycle` disables sync inline extraction when the separate extract phase is selected. It then calls:

`runPhaseExtract -> runExtractCore({ slugs }) -> extractForSlugs`

The incremental `extractForSlugs` path flushes link and timeline batches but never calls `stampExtracted` or `markPagesExtractedBatch`. It also does not receive the cycle source id, so source-correct stamping is impossible there.

When the next sync is up to date, Dream passes `slugs: []`, returns immediately, and never drains the accumulated stale backlog.

## Expected behavior

After both link and timeline batches successfully flush, incremental all-mode extraction should stamp every processed page using the cycle source id. Dry runs and links-only/timeline-only runs must not stamp the combined watermark.

## Workaround

`gbrain extract --stale --source-id <source>` clears the accumulated backlog. In the production example it converged from 84 stale pages to 0 and added 7 links plus 11 timeline entries.

## Proposed fix

- Thread `cycleSourceId` through `runPhaseExtract` and `runExtractCore`.
- Source-qualify incremental link/timeline batch rows.
- After both batches flush, stamp processed pages in non-dry-run `mode: all`.
- Add a non-default-source regression test asserting `countStalePagesForExtraction(...) === 0` after incremental extraction.
title: Dream incremental extract processes pages but never stamps links_extracted_at

body:
## Observed

Running skillopt with --no-mutate (v0.42.56.0-era): training completes,
the promote gate accepts an edit, best.md is written with the winning
skill content — but proposed.md is never created.

## Expected

In --no-mutate mode, proposed.md is the contract artifact: the produced
proposal for human review, with the live skill left untouched. Automation
watching for proposed.md (our human-gate pipeline does exactly this) sees
nothing and can't distinguish "run produced no proposal" from "run
produced a proposal that only landed in best.md."

## Repro

1. skillopt run on any skill with --no-mutate, benchmark where the
   optimizer finds an accepted edit
2. Run completes clean (exit 0)
3. best.md exists with the edited skill; proposed.md absent

## Suspected location

The gate/emit path in bundled-skill-gate.ts — the no-mutate branch
appears to write the best-candidate file but skip the proposal-emit step.

Happy to provide full run logs if useful. (Related: we just opened a PR
on the gateway max_tokens default from the same production debugging arc.)
title: skillopt --no-mutate: best.md is written but proposed.md is never emitted

body:
**Version:** gbrain 0.42.56.0 (run from source via bun)
**Goal:** run the agentic dream phases (`patterns`, `synthesize`) on a GLM Coding Plan subscription (z.ai) instead of metered Anthropic. The subscription exposes **two** endpoints — an Anthropic-style one (`/api/anthropic`) and an OpenAI-compatible one (`/api/coding/paas/v4`). I tried both. Every subagent path fails; the 12 single-call phases work fine.

This issue documents the **full path** so it's clear which routes are already ruled out.

## What I tried (and why each failed)

1. **Native Anthropic path** — model on the `anthropic` recipe with `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://api.z.ai/api/anthropic`. The subscription is Anthropic-style natively, so this was the obvious route. **Failed:** the subagent loop dies with `Tool results are missing for tool calls toolu_…` — the raw Anthropic loop can't pair GLM's tool-use blocks back. (dead job, `toolu_`-style ids.)

2. **`agent.use_gateway_loop=true` on the Anthropic path** — the in-process gateway loop instead of the raw Anthropic loop. **Still failed** the same pairing way.

3. **OpenAI-compatible path** — since the Anthropic endpoint was a dead end, I routed GLM through z.ai's OpenAI-compatible coding endpoint (`/api/coding/paas/v4`). gbrain has no working native GLM/OpenAI recipe (the `zhipu` recipe has no chat touchpoint → the capability classifier throws), so I **repurposed the `deepseek` recipe as a generic OpenAI-compatible transport**: `deepseek:glm-4.6` = the deepseek recipe (`implementation: openai-compatible`, accepts any base_url + model id) + `provider_base_urls.deepseek` → the z.ai coding endpoint. **Note: this is GLM, not actual DeepSeek** — deepseek is only the transport vehicle. This got past auth + the capability gate and ran a healthy multi-turn tool loop… then hit the two bugs below.

**Net: no subagent path works on GLM/z.ai in 0.42.56.** Anthropic-compat = pairing bug; OpenAI-compat = the two gateway-loop bugs below.

## Bug 1 — `toModelMessages` yields a ModelMessage[] the AI SDK v6 rejects (kills the first attempt)

On a **fresh** run the in-memory `messages` array is correctly interleaved (`user, assistant, user, assistant, …`), but after a few turns `gateway.chat()` throws:

```
Invalid prompt: The messages do not match the ModelMessage[] schema.
```

Content-dependent (observed at turn 3 on one run, turn 6 on another — depends on what the model returns that turn). The wire body actually sent to the provider is well-formed and returns **HTTP 200** when replayed by hand, so the rejection is **client-side** in `generateText`'s prompt standardization — i.e. `toModelMessages()` (`src/core/ai/gateway.ts`) is emitting a block shape v6's zod schema refuses (candidates: empty-text parts, or a `{type:'json', value:null}` tool-result output). Single-call phases are unaffected because they never build a multi-turn tool history.

## Bug 2 — retry/replay reconstruction drops tool-result messages (kills every retry)

When a subagent job retries, `toolLoop` seeds `messages` from `replayState.priorMessages`, built in `runGatewaySubagentLoop` (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts` ~L783) from `loadPriorMessages()` — i.e. **only** `subagent_messages` rows.

But the gateway loop (`src/core/ai/gateway.ts` ~L3162) pushes the synthesized `{role:'user', content: toolResultBlocks}` turn **without persisting it** — only assistant turns are persisted (`onAssistantTurn`); there is no `onUserTurn`. Tool results live solely in `subagent_tool_executions`.

Result: on replay, `priorMessages` = `[user(task), assistant(tool-calls), assistant(tool-calls), …]` with **no tool results between them**, so the very next `chat()` throws:

```
Tool results are missing for tool calls call_… , call_… , …
```

(all tool-call ids, oldest first). All `max_attempts` (default 3) burn instantly → job dead. So even a transient failure on attempt 1 becomes a permanent death.

## Reproduce

1. `gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true`
2. Point a subagent phase at an OpenAI-compatible non-Anthropic model (`gbrain config set models.dream.patterns deepseek:glm-4.6` + `provider_base_urls.deepseek` → a GLM OpenAI-compatible endpoint in `config.json`).
3. `gbrain dream --phase patterns --repo <a source with enough reflections>` with a worker running.
4. Job runs a healthy multi-turn tool loop, then dies with the schema error (Bug 1); all retries die with "Tool results are missing" (Bug 2).

## Evidence the endpoint / serialization are NOT at fault

- Hand-replaying the exact deepest request body (29 messages / 21 balanced tool-calls) to the endpoint → **HTTP 200** with a valid next tool-call.
- Every request body actually sent is balanced (each `tool_call` has a matching `tool` result).
- A faithful reconstruction of the conversation passes the AI SDK validator; the failing production array (captured in-memory at throw time) is unbalanced only because of the two bugs above.

## Suggested fixes

- **Bug 2:** in `runGatewaySubagentLoop`, reconstruct the tool-result `user` turns from `subagent_tool_executions` when building `priorChatMessages` (the data is already loaded as `priorTools`), interleaving one `{role:'user', content:[tool-result…]}` after each persisted assistant turn — mind the `messageIdx` accounting (the live loop increments `messageIdx` for both the assistant and the user-results turn, so persisted assistant rows sit at non-contiguous indices). Alternatively add an `onUserTurn` persist hook to `toolLoop`.
- **Bug 1:** harden `toModelMessages` against the block shapes GLM/OpenAI-compat models emit at depth (drop empty-text parts; ensure tool-result `output` is always a schema-valid part).
- **Anthropic-compat pairing** (route 1/2 above): the raw Anthropic subagent loop can't pair GLM's tool-use ids — worth a look if GLM-on-Anthropic-endpoint is meant to be supported.

Happy to provide the captured in-memory message dumps and the request-body replay for any of the three.
title: Subagent tool loop unusable with GLM (z.ai) on every path: Anthropic-compat pairing bug + gateway-loop schema/replay bugs

body:
## Summary

On an **existing** Postgres brain upgrading across schema version **121** (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`, the Life Chronicle feature #2390), `initSchema()` deadlocks and every command that connects fails.

`schema.sql` unconditionally runs, on **every** `initSchema`:

```sql
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
```

But on a pre-121 DB the `event_page_id` **column** doesn't exist yet — it's added by migration 121. Because `initSchema` runs `schema.sql` (the baseline) **before** `runMigrations`, the index DDL throws `column "event_page_id" does not exist`, the connection aborts, and migrations 116–122 never get to run. The column that would fix it is gated behind the very step that keeps crashing. `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS` still evaluates the column reference, so `IF NOT EXISTS` doesn't save it.

## Environment

- gbrain **v0.42.56.0** (master `64617904`)
- engine: `postgres` (remote)
- DB was at schema version **115**, target **122** (i.e. an upgrade that crosses the 121 boundary)

## Symptoms

- `gbrain init --migrate-only` → `column "event_page_id" does not exist`
- `gbrain health` / `gbrain embed --stale` / `gbrain dream` → `Schema probe failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist` (embed-stale even prints `Re-run: gbrain apply-migrations --yes`, which does not help — see below)
- `gbrain dream` **synthesize** and **propose_takes** phases fail with `write CONNECTION_CLOSED …:5432` (the pooled connection drops when `initSchema` aborts mid-phase). This silently half-breaks the nightly `dream` job — orphan/synthesis work stops while embed/sync still appear to run.

## Root cause (file references on master `64617904`)

- `src/schema.sql` **L557–558** — the two `event_page_id` indexes, created on every `initSchema`.
- `src/core/pglite-schema.ts` **L387–388** — same, for the pglite path.
- `src/schema.sql` **L545** — `event_page_id` is declared inside `CREATE TABLE timeline_entries (…)`, which is a **no-op on an existing table** (`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`), so the column is *not* backfilled onto existing DBs by the baseline.
- `src/core/migrate.ts` **L5443+** (migration `version: 121`, `timeline_entries_event_page_id`) — the only place the column is actually added to existing DBs (`ALTER TABLE … ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;` at L5457, FK at L5467, indexes at L5473–5475).

Ordering: `initSchema` → `schema.sql` (eager index, **crashes here**) → `runMigrations` (never reached).

## Why fresh installs are unaffected (and CI stays green)

A fresh install's `CREATE TABLE timeline_entries (… event_page_id …)` creates the column up front, so the subsequent eager index DDL succeeds. Only DBs that already have a `timeline_entries` table and cross the 121 boundary hit the deadlock — a path a fresh-install test matrix won't exercise.

## `apply-migrations` is a red herring

The embed-stale hint suggests `gbrain apply-migrations --yes`, but that path runs named backfills (`v0.13.1`, `v0.29.1`, …) and does **not** fix the connect-time `schema.sql` index; it also surfaces a misleading `Applying migration v0.29.1 … status=failed` which is really the same missing column hit via `initSchema`.

## Workaround (what unblocked us — non-destructive)

Run once against the DB, then let the normal migrator finish:

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
```
```
gbrain init --migrate-only
```

With the column present, `schema.sql`'s index DDL becomes a no-op, `initSchema` stops aborting, and migrations 116–122 apply normally — migration 121 idempotently adds the FK + indexes and stamps the ledger. Schema reached 122; `health`/`dream` are clean.

## Suggested fix (approach is yours to choose)

The `event_page_id` index creation shouldn't live in the always-on `schema.sql` baseline **ahead of** the column that a later migration adds. Options:

1. **Move** the two `event_page_id` index statements out of `schema.sql` / `pglite-schema.ts` and rely solely on migration 121 (it already creates them `IF NOT EXISTS`) — assuming fresh installs run migrations after the baseline.
2. **Guard** them in `schema.sql` with a `DO $$ … IF column_exists … END $$;` block.
3. **Reorder** `initSchema` so `runMigrations` executes before the eager index DDL.

This class of bug will recur on any future migration that adds a column *and* eagerly indexes it in `schema.sql`, so a guard/convention (or a CI job that runs `init` against a snapshot of an older schema, not just a fresh install) would prevent the next one.

Happy to open a PR for whichever approach you prefer.
title: Upgrade deadlock at schema v121: schema.sql eager-indexes timeline_entries.event_page_id before migration 121 adds the column

body:
## Summary

`gbrain serve --http` prints the auto-generated **admin token** in plaintext to stdout in the startup banner:

```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  Admin Token (paste into /admin login):              ║
║  ab1ab1…c5e36cf8d8239f                                ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

In any containerized deploy, stdout is shipped to centralized log storage (Splunk, CloudWatch, Loki, etc.). That turns a privileged admin credential into a **standing secret in log storage** — anyone with log read access can recover a valid `/admin` token and reach the admin API (`/admin/api/*`) if the port is reachable.

## Impact

- The token grants admin scope (the admin console + `/admin/api/*`).
- It is generated once per process and lives for the pod's lifetime (not the `--token-ttl`, which governs issued access tokens), so the logged value stays valid until restart.
- Deployments that expose the HTTP port (e.g. behind an ingress routing `/`) expose `/admin` alongside `/mcp`.

## Request

Provide a way to avoid emitting the secret to stdout. Any of:

1. **Redact by default** — print `Admin Token: <hidden — set GBRAIN_ADMIN_TOKEN or see …>` instead of the value; only print the raw value when an explicit `--print-admin-token` / interactive TTY is set.
2. **Accept an operator-supplied token** — read `GBRAIN_ADMIN_TOKEN` from env so operators can inject a known secret from their secret manager and never have it generated+printed.
3. **`--no-banner` / `--quiet`** flag to suppress the banner entirely for headless/server deploys.

Option 2 is the most operator-friendly (lets us source the token from a k8s Secret / Whisper and rotate it), but any of the three closes the log-leak.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.42.56.0, `serve --http`, containerized (Bun on debian), OAuth 2.1 enabled.
- Workaround in place on our side: a higher-precedence ingress rule edge-redirects `/admin` off the external VIP so the logged token can't be replayed externally. That's defense-in-depth; the token is still in our logs, which is why the root fix belongs here.
title: serve --http prints admin token to stdout (leaks into container log storage)

## Summary
The Google recipe's chat touchpoint allowlist (`src/core/ai/recipes/google.ts`) only accepts `gemini-2.0-flash-exp`, `gemini-2.0-flash`, and `gemini-1.5-pro`. As of July 2026, Google's live API no longer serves `gemini-1.5-pro`, and none of the current-generation chat models (`gemini-3.5-flash`, `gemini-2.5-flash`, the `gemini-3.x` line) nor the auto-tracking aliases (`gemini-flash-latest`, `gemini-pro-latest`) pass `probeChatModel`.

Practical effect: on a Google-only setup (no Anthropic/OpenAI keys), `gbrain think` degrades to the "no LLM available" gather-only stub, and `--model google:gemini-flash-latest` hard-errors with `unknown_model`.

## Repro (v0.42.56.0)
gbrain think "test" --model google:gemini-flash-latest
# unknown_model — Known models: gemini-2.0-flash-exp, gemini-2.0-flash, gemini-1.5-pro
(gemini-1.5-pro in that list 404s on the live API.)

## Suggestion
- Add `gemini-flash-latest` / `gemini-pro-latest` (retirement-proof aliases) + current GA models; drop `gemini-1.5-pro`.
- Longer term: a doctor check probing the allowlist against ListModels would catch both directions of drift (the embedding path already went through this exact churn).

Happy to PR the one-line refresh. Locally patched and confirmed working with `gemini-flash-latest` end-to-end.
title: Google recipe chat allowlist is stale — gemini-1.5-pro is retired on the live API, no current models accepted

body:
## Summary

`extract-conversation-facts` can mark pages as complete by writing `cli:extract-conversation-facts:terminal` audit rows even when facts extraction failed for every segment due to model configuration/runtime errors. This can make the doctor backlog disappear while no semantic facts were extracted.

This is distinct from parser coverage: after the parser returns valid segments, the extraction path can still silently degrade to `[]` per segment.

## Observed

Version: `gbrain 0.42.55.0`

A facts model config resolved through `facts.extraction_model` / reasoning tier to an Anthropic model id not accepted by the active gateway recipe:

```text
anthropic:claude-sonnet-5 ERR AIConfigError Model "claude-sonnet-5" is not listed for Anthropic chat.
```

In the batch command, the same class of failure was swallowed by `extractFactsFromTurn()` and treated as “no facts”:

```text
[extract-conversation-facts] <slug>: 0 facts inserted across <N> segments
...
Done: extracted 0 facts (0 inserted) across <N> segments from <M>/<M> pages in 1 source(s). Spent ~$0.0000.
```

The command still wrote terminal audit rows:

```sql
select source, count(*)
from facts
where source_markdown_slug in (select slug from pages where type='<conversation-like type>')
group by source;

cli:extract-conversation-facts:terminal | <M>
```

After pinning a valid model explicitly:

```bash
gbrain config set facts.extraction_model anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6
```

a dry run of the same page/segment produced real extracted facts:

```text
Done: (dry run) would extract <nonzero> facts (0 inserted) across 1 segments from 1/1 pages ...
```

## Expected

If every segment fails because the extractor cannot call the configured model/provider, the command should not mark the page complete. It should surface a hard or at least loud failure and leave the page in the backlog for retry.

Suggested invariants:

- `AIConfigError` / invalid model / provider unavailable should not be collapsed to `[]` in bulk extraction.
- `extract-conversation-facts` should write the terminal audit row only when the page was successfully attempted with a working extractor, or should record a distinct failed-terminal status that doctor does not treat as complete.
- Doctor should flag pages that have only `cli:extract-conversation-facts:terminal` rows and zero per-segment facts when the run spent `$0.0000` / hit extractor errors.
- `models doctor` should validate configured per-task models such as `facts.extraction_model` against the same gateway path used by `extractFactsFromTurn()`.

## Relationship to existing issues

Related but not identical:

- #1466 — harden fact extraction and fallback error handling.
- #2113 — facts extraction silently returns zero when model output is truncated.
- #2228 — provider-agnostic model config hardening.
- #1897 / #1898 — parser coverage for plain transcript lines. Parser coverage can be fixed while this terminal-audit failure still occurs.

This issue is specifically about **false completion / backlog corruption** when extractor/model failures are swallowed as zero facts.

## Minimal repro shape

1. Configure `facts.extraction_model` or `models.tier.reasoning` to a model id that resolves but is rejected by the gateway recipe.
2. Run `gbrain extract-conversation-facts --slug <page-with-valid-segments> --force --types <conversation-like type> --yes`.
3. Observe `0 facts inserted`, `$0.0000` spend, and terminal audit row written.
4. Fix the model id and dry-run the same slug/segment; facts are extracted, proving content/parser were not the issue.
title: extract-conversation-facts writes terminal audit rows after swallowed model/config failures

body:
## Symptom

Dream-cycle synthesize subagents (`agent.use_gateway_loop=true`, provider `deepseek` via `provider_base_urls.deepseek` custom base URL) die on any transcript where the model uses tools:

```
[chat(deepseek:deepseek-v4-pro)] Tool results are missing for tool calls call_3f2f11a684ea42a692eab6b4, call_2307aad6f31f465cbe7e0bcc.
```

## Key evidence: the tools DID run

`subagent_tool_executions` shows both calls completed, with tool_use_ids exactly matching the ones the error claims are missing:

```
 job_id |          tool_use_id          |   tool_name    |  status
--------+-------------------------------+----------------+----------
  12512 | call_3f2f11a684ea42a692eab6b4 | brain_search   | complete
  12512 | call_2307aad6f31f465cbe7e0bcc | brain_get_page | complete
```

So execution + logging succeed, but the loop's history builder never appends the tool-result messages before the next model turn, and its own validator then rejects the history. Reproduced with both `deepseek-v4-flash` and `deepseek-v4-pro`, with 6 parallel calls and with 2. Injecting `parallel_tool_calls: false` at a proxy doesn't help (and a tool-less synthesize run on the same setup completes fine — job 12507).

Looks like the result-threading path only handles the Anthropic-loop message shape; OpenAI-compatible tool_call ids never get matched back. Sibling of the non-Anthropic blind spots reported in #2577.

Env: gbrain 0.42.56.0, Postgres engine, macOS (mini), model via OpenAI-compatible aggregator behind custom base URL.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: gateway-native loop: tool results executed but never threaded back for OpenAI-compatible providers — subagent dies with 'Tool results are missing'

**Version:** 0.42.56.0 (also current master `6461790`) · **Engine:** Postgres 16 + pgvector · **Runtime:** Bun · **Host:** remote Linux VM, systemd user unit · **Chat:** corporate OpenAI-compatible endpoint (key in a dedicated env file)

## What happens

`gbrain autopilot --install` generates `~/.gbrain/autopilot-run.sh` whose only mechanism for loading API keys is sourcing shell rc files (`writeWrapperScript`, `src/commands/autopilot.ts:1118-1141`):

```bash
[ -f ~/.zshenv ] && source ~/.zshenv 2>/dev/null
source ~/.zshrc 2>/dev/null || source ~/.bashrc 2>/dev/null || true
exec '<gbrain>' autopilot --repo '<repo>'
```

If `~/.zshrc` exists, the `||` chain stops there — `~/.bashrc` is never tried, and there is no fallback to any gbrain-owned env file. On a host where the LLM provider env (`OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL`, `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`, …) lives in a dedicated env file rather than in zsh rc files, the autopilot worker starts with **no provider environment at all**.

The failure is then completely silent. Every LLM-dependent job no-ops instead of erroring:

- the chronicle judge checks `isAvailable('chat')` and returns `{ events: [] }` when false (`src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:172-173`), which becomes an ordinary `status: 'no_events'` result;
- dream/enrich phases likewise produce nothing.

Observed symptom: autopilot runs green for days — cycles complete, sync/embed work (they don't need chat) — while chronicle and dream extract zero events and spend zero tokens. Nothing in `autopilot.err`, job output, or doctor points at "the worker has no chat credentials".

## What should happen

Either or both:

1. **Source the env the daemon actually needs**, not the user's interactive shell config: a gbrain-owned env file (e.g. `~/.gbrain/keys.env` or a configured `env_file`) sourced unconditionally after the rc-file attempts. #1868 suggests the same for the Linux `PATH`/bun variant of this problem — the rc-file-sourcing approach is the shared root cause.
2. **Fail loudly when chat is unavailable in a daemon context**: if `isAvailable('chat')` is false during an autopilot cycle that includes LLM phases, log a prominent warning (and/or surface it in `doctor`/`status`) instead of letting every judge return empty results indefinitely.

## Where (master `6461790`)

- `src/commands/autopilot.ts:1118-1141` (`writeWrapperScript` — rc-file sourcing, no env-file fallback)
- `src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:172-173` (`isAvailable('chat')` → silent `{ events: [] }`)

## Repro

1. Put provider keys in a dedicated env file only (none in `~/.zshrc`); ensure `~/.zshrc` exists.
2. `gbrain autopilot --install` on a systemd host; start the unit.
3. Watch chronicle/dream phases: all pages return `no_events`, token spend 0, no errors anywhere.

## Workaround

Hand-patch `autopilot-run.sh` to `source ~/.gbrain/<env-file>` — but `autopilot --install` regenerates the wrapper, so the patch must be reapplied after every reinstall/upgrade.

## Related papercut

After an unclean stop, a stale `~/.gbrain/autopilot.lock` makes the systemd unit flap ("Another autopilot instance is running… Exiting" in a restart loop) until the lock is removed manually. #2503 covers the adjacent stale-PID-reuse case; a daemon-managed lock probably shouldn't require manual cleanup after any crash.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: autopilot --install wrapper sources ~/.zshrc for API keys — worker env silently lost, every LLM job no-ops with no warning

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0 (also current master `6461790`) · **Engine:** Postgres 16 + pgvector · **Runtime:** Bun

## What happens

Incremental sync and full sync disagree about what belongs in the corpus, because the full-sync file enumerator bypasses the canonical exclusion gate.

**Incremental** sync filters every manifest entry through `isSyncable` (`src/commands/sync.ts:445-447` and `1849-1855`), whose `classifySync` (`src/core/sync.ts:341-360`) excludes:

- any path with a segment blocked by `pruneDir` — dot-dirs, `node_modules`, `vendor`, `dist`, `build`, `venv`, `.raw`, and `ops/` at any depth (`PRUNE_DIR_NAMES`, `src/core/sync.ts:242-259`);
- metafile basenames `schema.md` / `index.md` / `log.md` / `README.md` (`SYNC_SKIP_FILES`, `src/core/sync.ts:330`).

**Full sync** (`performFullSync` → `runImport` → `collectSyncableFiles`, `src/commands/sync.ts:2952`) never applies `isSyncable`. When the source is a git work tree it takes the `git ls-files` fast path, `gitListSyncableFiles` (`src/commands/import.ts:530-560`), which filters **only** by strategy/extension (`isCollectibleForWalker`) plus `.gitignore` — no `pruneDir`, no metafile exclusion. The non-git FS-walk fallback does call `pruneDir` at descent time (`import.ts:614`) but still imports the metafile basenames. And `runImport` itself applies no `isSyncable` check downstream.

Net effect on a git-backed source (the common case): `sync --full` imports — and *re-imports/resurrects previously soft-deleted* — everything incremental sync is designed to exclude: `ops/**`, `index.md`, `README.md`, `log.md`, `schema.md`, etc. A brain that was clean under months of incremental syncs silently regains excluded pages after any event that forces a full pass (first sync, `--full`, chunker-version bump, lost sync anchor).

## Observed

On a personal markdown vault synced as a source: pages under `ops/` that incremental sync had correctly excluded (and that had been soft-deleted) came back after a full re-import, with the incremental path then treating them as deletable again — the two modes cycle content in and out depending on which one ran last.

## What should happen

The full path should route candidate files through the same single source of truth: filter `gitListSyncableFiles` output (and `runImport`'s inputs generally) through `isSyncable(relPath, { strategy })`, exactly like the incremental manifest path. `classifySync` already exists precisely so the two "cannot drift" — the git fast path just doesn't call it.

## Where (master `6461790`)

- `src/commands/import.ts:530-560` (`gitListSyncableFiles` — extension filter only)
- `src/commands/import.ts:578-599` (`collectSyncableFiles` — git fast path returns early, before any prune logic)
- `src/core/sync.ts:242-259, 330, 341-360` (`PRUNE_DIR_NAMES`, `SYNC_SKIP_FILES`, `classifySync` — the gate the full path skips)
- `src/commands/sync.ts:445-447, 1849-1855` (incremental path applying `isSyncable`)

## Related

- #2082 — the mirror-image divergence (gitignored files: invisible to incremental, imported by full FS-walk). Same root disease: the two enumeration paths don't share a filter.
- #2404 — questions `ops/` being in `PRUNE_DIR_NAMES` at all; this issue is orthogonal (whatever the exclusion set is, full and incremental should agree on it).
- #2426 — `sync --full` delete-reconcile; another case of `--full` behaving differently from steady state.

## Workaround

Avoid `--full` on sources that rely on the built-in exclusions; after an unavoidable full pass, re-delete the resurrected pages by slug prefix.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: sync --full git fast path bypasses pruneDir/isSyncable — re-imports ops/ and metafile pages that incremental sync excludes

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0 (also current master `6461790`) · **Engine:** Postgres 16 + pgvector · **Runtime:** Bun · **Chat:** OpenAI-compatible endpoint

## What happens

The chronicle judge caps its completion at `maxTokens: 1500` (`src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:186`) and then treats any unparseable output as "no events":

- `parseJudgeJson` returns `[]` on any parse failure — missing `]`, truncated JSON, anything (`extract-events.ts:200-214`);
- `runChronicleExtract` maps an empty proposals array to `status: 'no_events'` (`extract-events.ts:129-130`);
- the only `stopReason` values handled are `refusal` / `content_filter` (`extract-events.ts:188`) — a `max_tokens` / length stop is not detected.

So when a page is event-dense enough that the JSON array exceeds ~1500 output tokens, the response is cut off mid-array, `lastIndexOf(']')`/`JSON.parse` fails, and the page is recorded as legitimately having no events. Nothing in logs, job output, or the result distinguishes this from a genuinely empty page.

## Evidence

Running chronicle extraction across ~61 daily/meeting-shaped pages: 49 extracted fine, but 11 pages persistently returned `no_events` across retries — and they were consistently the larger ones (3-4K chars of dense bullet lists, i.e. many events), while smaller pages extracted normally. That pattern matches the JSON array being truncated at the token cap and silently swallowed, rather than the model finding nothing.

## What should happen

Any of (ideally all):

1. Detect truncation: when `stopReason` indicates the token cap was hit, do not feed the text to `parseJudgeJson` as if complete — retry with a higher cap, or fail the page explicitly.
2. Distinguish `parse_failed` from `no_events` in `ChronicleExtractResult` (there is already a `reason` field and a `malformed_proposal` skip path — a parse failure after non-empty model text deserves the same visibility).
3. Make the judge `maxTokens` configurable (or scale it with input size), so event-dense pages have headroom.

## Where

- `src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:186` (`maxTokens: 1500`)
- `src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:188` (only refusal/content_filter checked)
- `src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:200-214` (`parseJudgeJson` → `[]` on failure)
- `src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:129-130` (`[]` → `no_events`)

## Workaround

None clean. Splitting large pages or hand-summarizing them before extraction works but defeats the point; the affected days were covered by deterministic timeline rows instead.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: Chronicle judge: output truncated at maxTokens=1500 is silently recorded as no_events (parse failure indistinguishable from empty page)

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0 (also current master `6461790`) · **Engine:** Postgres 16 + pgvector · **Runtime:** Bun

## What happens

Chronicle extraction eligibility is a hard-coded whitelist with no configuration surface:

```ts
// src/core/chronicle/eligibility.ts:10-13
const ELIGIBLE_TYPES: PageType[] = ['meeting', 'conversation', 'calendar-event'];
const RESCUE_SLUG_PREFIXES = ['meetings/', 'conversations/', 'cal/', 'calendar/'] as const;
```

`src/core/chronicle/config.ts` exposes only `auto_chronicle` and `chronicle.tz` — there is no way to extend the eligible set.

Users whose temporal ground truth lives in daily work-log pages (`daily/YYYY-MM-DD.md` — bullet lists of what happened that day, not private diaries; the explicit `diary` exclusion is fine and should stay) can never get auto-chronicle or `chronicle-backfill` coverage for them. The Chronicle day/week views then miss exactly the pages that describe what happened on a given day.

## Why this looks safe to open up

Eligibility is enforced at enqueue time only — `runChronicleExtract` (`src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:99`) performs no eligibility re-check. Manually submitting jobs per slug works today:

```bash
gbrain jobs submit chronicle_extract --params '{"slug":"daily/2026-06-26","sourceId":"default"}' --follow
```

Doing this across 61 daily pages extracted ~215 well-typed event atoms (work/decision/conflict/meeting/…) with sensible dates — i.e. the extractor already handles these pages well; only the whitelist stands in the way. (The enqueue-time-only check also means the whitelist is advisory rather than a real boundary, which is worth knowing regardless.)

## What should happen

A config knob for extending eligibility without forking, e.g.:

- `chronicle.extra_types` / `chronicle.extra_prefixes` config keys merged into `ELIGIBLE_TYPES` / `RESCUE_SLUG_PREFIXES`; and/or
- a schema-pack-level declaration ("this page type is chronicle-eligible"), since packs already define custom types.

The `diary` / `life/diary/` / `life/events/` exclusions should remain non-overridable.

## Where

- `src/core/chronicle/eligibility.ts:10-13` (hard-coded whitelist)
- `src/core/chronicle/config.ts` (no extension knob)
- `src/core/chronicle/extract-events.ts:99` (`runChronicleExtract` — no eligibility check, which is what makes the per-slug workaround possible)

## Workaround

Per-slug `gbrain jobs submit chronicle_extract` as above (needs re-running for every new page; auto-chronicle on write never fires for these types).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: Chronicle eligibility whitelist is hard-coded — no config to make daily/journal work-log types chronicle-eligible

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0 (also current master `6461790`) · **Engine:** Postgres 16 + pgvector · **Runtime:** Bun

## What happens

The `get_timeline` operation exposes only a `slug` parameter. Any date-window arguments a caller passes (`since`/`until` — the names agents naturally reach for, since the sibling `query` op uses them) are silently accepted and dropped: there is no unknown-param rejection, so the caller gets the full unfiltered timeline back with no hint that the window was ignored.

Meanwhile the engine underneath already implements date filtering: `TimelineOpts` declares `after`/`before` (`src/core/types.ts:1271`) and `PostgresEngine.getTimeline` applies them (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:3439-3440`, `afterCond`/`beforeCond`). The op handler just never passes them:

```ts
// src/core/operations.ts:2165-2178
const get_timeline: Operation = {
  name: 'get_timeline',
  description: 'Get timeline entries for a page',
  params: {
    slug: { type: 'string', required: true },
  },
  handler: async (ctx, p) => {
    return ctx.engine.getTimeline(p.slug as string, sourceScopeOpts(ctx));
  },
  ...
```

So date-window timeline reads are unreachable over MCP and the CLI even though the SQL support exists.

## What should happen

Either of:

- Expose `after`/`before` (and ideally `limit`, which the engine also supports) as op params and thread them through to `TimelineOpts`; or
- at minimum, reject or warn on unrecognized params so an agent that passes `since`/`until` learns the filter did not apply, instead of silently receiving unfiltered results.

## Where

- `src/core/operations.ts:2165-2178` (`get_timeline` op — slug only)
- `src/core/types.ts:1271-1288` (`TimelineOpts.after/before/limit` — implemented but unexposed)
- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:3422-3445` (working `afterCond`/`beforeCond` SQL)

## Evidence

Hit while building date-scoped recall for a personal markdown vault synced as a source: MCP callers passing `since`/`until` to `get_timeline` always got the complete timeline, indistinguishable from a correctly filtered empty/partial window. Related: #1520 covers the sibling bug where `query`'s `since`/`until` bind to `updated_at` instead of `effective_date` — together these left no working date-window read surface pre-Chronicle.

## Workaround

Fetch the full timeline per page and filter client-side; or use the 0.42.56 Life Chronicle commands (`gbrain day` / `since`), which read `timeline_entries.date` correctly.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: get_timeline: engine implements after/before date filtering but the op exposes only slug — window args silently ignored

body:
## Summary

`gbrain embed --stale` progress output can show counts above the apparent denominator, for example values shaped like `862/51`, while the embedding job itself continues and completes.

## Observed

During a local stale-embedding backfill, `gbrain embed --stale --batch-size 64` made forward progress and eventually reached zero stale chunks, but intermediate progress reporting showed the processed count exceeding the displayed total.

## Expected

The displayed denominator should represent the same unit as the numerator, or the CLI should label the units separately so progress cannot exceed 100% unless explicitly reporting a moving estimate.

## Why it matters

The underlying embed path worked, but progress output above 100% looks like cursor/accounting corruption and makes automated audit logs harder to interpret.

## Evidence boundary

Observed with local `gbrain 0.42.56.0` during a DStack/DBrain no-key embedding closeout. This report is about progress accounting only; final stale count and embedding coverage were healthy.
title: embed --stale progress denominator can exceed 100%

## Summary

`gbrain providers list` appears to render user-provided chat recipes as not chat-capable when the recipe intentionally has `touchpoints.chat.user_provided_models = true` and an empty `models` array.

## Observed

In a local DStack/DBrain integration, a LiteLLM-compatible recipe with a user-provided chat model can route chat successfully through the gateway, but `providers list` still displays the chat column as unavailable (`CHAT=-`).

## Expected

Provider capability display should treat `touchpoints.chat.user_provided_models === true` as chat-capable, even when `touchpoints.chat.models.length === 0`.

## Why it matters

The current display makes a working provider route look unsupported, which complicates post-upgrade/runtime audits and can send operators chasing a false provider configuration problem.

## Evidence boundary

This is from local runtime proof against `gbrain 0.42.56.0` at observed head `646179047a8e4ad9c462d83ce9a67a50ba076de8`. No secrets or provider payloads are included here.
title: providers list should show user-provided chat recipes as chat-capable

body:
## Summary

Upgrading a **legacy brain** (existing `timeline_entries` table) to **v0.42.56.0** (`6461790`, Life Chronicle #2390) wedges schema migrations in a chicken-and-egg deadlock: the embedded schema forward-references `timeline_entries.event_page_id`, but that column is only added by **schema migration 121** — which runs *after* the schema replay that crashes on the missing column. All Chronicle ops then fail with:

```
column te.event_page_id does not exist
```

Fresh installs are unaffected (they get the full `CREATE TABLE` with the column), which is likely why CI didn't catch it.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.42.56.0 (`6461790`), upgraded from v0.42.53.0 (`814258d`)
- Postgres 17 engine (`GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL`), macOS, bun
- Brain schema_version was 119 before upgrade; `timeline_entries` existed (pre-Chronicle shape)

## Symptom

- `chronicle_since` / `chronicle_day` (MCP + CLI) → `internal_error: column te.event_page_id does not exist`
- serve startup log:
  ```
  Schema probe failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist
  Re-run: `gbrain apply-migrations --yes`
  ```
- Re-running `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` does **not** help — it reports `All migrations up to date.` (that command tracks the orchestrator/file migrations, not the `migrate.ts` schema migrations), so the suggested remediation is a no-op for this failure.
- `config.version` stays pinned at 119; migrations 120/121/122 never apply, no matter how many restarts.

## Root cause (as of `6461790`)

`PostgresEngine.initSchema()` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts`) executes in this order:

1. `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap(conn)` — adds columns that the embedded schema forward-references but older brains lack. **Its probe list does not include `timeline_entries.event_page_id`** (it covers `pages.source_id`, `links.link_source`, `content_chunks.*`, `oauth_clients.*`, `sources.*`, etc.).
2. `conn.unsafe(sqlText)` — replays the embedded schema. On a legacy brain, `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS timeline_entries` is a no-op (table exists without the new column), then:
   ```sql
   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
   ```
   throws `column "event_page_id" does not exist`, aborting `initSchema`.
3. `runMigrations(this)` — **never reached**, so migration 121 (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`, the one that adds the column) can never run. Deadlock.

Note the post-migration `verifySchema()` self-heal (which exists precisely to fix missing columns) also sits after the failure point, so it can't save the situation either.

The PGLite engine appears to have the same gap by inspection: `src/core/pglite-schema.ts` ships the same two `CREATE INDEX ... (event_page_id)` statements, and `PGLiteEngine#applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` doesn't cover the column. (Only verified live on Postgres.)

Migration 122 (`facts_ontology_dimension`) is *not* affected — `facts` is migration-created and absent from the static schema, exactly the pattern that avoids this class of bug.

## Repro

1. Take any brain at schema_version ≤ 120 (i.e. `timeline_entries` exists without `event_page_id`) on Postgres.
2. Upgrade the repo to `6461790`, restart serve (or run any CLI command that triggers `connectEngine`).
