3. Observe `Schema probe failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist` on every start; `chronicle day/since` ops fail; schema migrations 120–122 never apply.

## Suggested fix

Add `timeline_entries.event_page_id` to `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` in both engines (same guarded `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` pattern as the existing entries, mirroring the doc-comment list), so the schema replay finds the column and `runMigrations` can then record 121 normally (it's already idempotent).

Longer-term, a CI guard that diffs "columns referenced by embedded-schema indexes" against "columns guaranteed by CREATE TABLE ∪ bootstrap list" would catch this whole class — this is at least the second time a new feature's index forward-referenced a migration-added column.

## Workaround (for anyone else hitting this)

Manually apply migration 121's own SQL (additive + idempotent), then restart serve:

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
DO $$
BEGIN
  IF NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM pg_constraint
     WHERE conname = 'timeline_entries_event_page_id_fkey'
       AND conrelid = 'timeline_entries'::regclass
  ) THEN
    ALTER TABLE timeline_entries
      ADD CONSTRAINT timeline_entries_event_page_id_fkey
      FOREIGN KEY (event_page_id) REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
  END IF;
END $$;
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup
  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
```

After restart, `initSchema` completes, migrations 120/121/122 apply and are recorded (121 no-ops cleanly), `config.version` reaches 122, and Chronicle ops work. Verified on the affected brain.
title: v0.42.56.0: embedded schema forward-references timeline_entries.event_page_id (added by migration 121) — initSchema deadlocks on legacy brains, Chronicle unusable

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0, Postgres engine. `chat_model = openai:gpt-4o-mini` (confirmed via `gbrain config get chat_model`), `OPENAI_API_KEY` exported in the shell.

**Observed:**
- `gbrain enrich --thin --types person --limit 3 --max-usd 0.25` → exits with `Chat gateway unavailable. Configure a chat model…`
- `gbrain enrich … --dry-run` → works (14 candidates enumerated).
- `gbrain enrich … --background` + `gbrain jobs work` → **all jobs complete successfully** with the same env/config.
- Passing `--model openai:gpt-4o-mini` explicitly does not change the direct-path failure.

**Cause (from reading the source):** the gate at src/commands/enrich.ts (`if (!parsed.dryRun && !isAvailable('chat'))`) runs before the engine/gateway is configured. `isAvailable` (src/core/ai/gateway.ts) returns `false` whenever `_config` is null — `configureGateway()` hasn't been called yet at that point in the direct CLI path — so the check reports "unavailable" regardless of actual config/keys. The jobs worker configures the engine before executing the handler, which is why `--background` succeeds.

**Suggestion:** move the availability gate after engine/gateway initialization (or call `configureGateway` from resolved config + process.env before the gate). The current error message sends users chasing model/key config that is actually fine.
title: enrich: 'Chat gateway unavailable' despite valid chat model + key — availability gate runs before gateway configuration (--background path unaffected)

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0, Postgres engine, multi-source brain (6 federated sources).

**Observed:** pages in source A containing bare wikilinks that resolve (via `link_resolution.global_basename=true`) to pages in source B produce **no edges and no diagnostics**. In a brain with a `comms-imessage` source of ~1,100 transcript pages wikilinking people whose pages live in two vault sources, extraction produced 0 edges for those targets, while identical links to pages that happened to also exist in the `default` source produced edges.

**Cause:** `resolveCandidateSources` (src/commands/extract.ts) returns `null` unless `targetSources.includes(fromSourceId)` or `targetSources.includes('default')`. `resolveSlugAll` correctly returns the cross-source matches (verified by unit-driving it directly); they're then dropped at candidate-source resolution.

**Why this seems unintended:** the v0.37.7.0 comment in the same file says resolver maps are built from `listAllPageRefs` "so cross-source wikilinks resolve correctly". The basename fallback (#972) also advertises one-edge-per-match with no silent winner — but cross-source matches become silent zero-edge drops, indistinguishable from unresolved links (`dead_links` stays 0).

**Suggestion:** allow edges between federated sources (or gate behind a config like `link_resolution.cross_source=true`); at minimum, record the drop (dead_links or a counter) so multi-source graph sparsity is observable.

**Workaround used:** materialize entity hub pages in the `default` source so every person has a linkable home — works well, but took code-reading to discover.
title: Cross-source wikilink edges silently dropped unless target exists in same source or 'default'

body:
Found while running gbrain v0.42.53.0 multi-tenant (per-client sources, client_credentials grants).

**1. `list_pages` ignores `source_id`.** Calling `list_pages {source_id: "u-1"}` and `list_pages {source_id: "shared"}` returns byte-identical results — every page the calling client can read. Returned page objects also carry no `source_id` field, so a caller can't even filter client-side. This makes per-source listings (e.g. "show this user's own pages") impossible without app-side slug-prefix conventions.

**2. `query` with no term returns an empty set.** There's no way to ask for "recent pages" — the natural recent-items listing — through the MCP surface.

**Asks:**
- (a) honor `source_id` on `list_pages`
- (b) include `source_id` on returned page objects
- (c) make a no-term `query` return recent pages (or add a `recent` op)

Happy to provide reproduction transcripts if useful.
title: list_pages ignores source_id; query without a term returns nothing

body:
## Summary

`schema_apply_mutations` (MCP) documents "ATOMIC: all mutations succeed or all roll back", but when a batch fails validation partway, the mutations that ran *before* the failing one remain applied to the pack file. The error response itself shows this: `partial_results` lists mutation 0 with a changed sha.

## Environment

0.42.56.0 (`64617904`), PGLite, HTTP serve, mutating a user-installed pack at `~/.gbrain/schema-packs/<name>/pack.yaml`.

## Repro

Submit a batch of 7 `add_type` mutations where a later one triggers a validation failure (here: two types declaring the same `path_prefix`):

```json
{
  "error": "mutation_failed",
  "code": "INVALID_RESULT",
  "message": "mutation would produce invalid pack: prefix_collision: path_prefix 'fitness/workouts/' is declared by 2 types: workout-log, workout; type inference is undefined",
  "batch_id": "batch-...",
  "partial_results": [
    { "index": 0, "op": "add_type", "prev_sha8": "647b3b36", "new_sha8": "a79a7c87", ... }
  ]
}
```

Then inspect the pack file: the index-0 type **is present on disk**. Re-submitting a corrected batch fails immediately:

```json
{ "error": "mutation_failed", "code": "TYPE_EXISTS", "message": "type 'workout-log' already exists in pack '...'", "partial_results": [] }
```

## Expected

Per the tool description, a failed batch should leave the pack file byte-identical to its pre-batch state (`prev_sha8` of index 0).

## Workaround

Restore the pack file from a backup (or re-copy the fork source) before retrying the corrected batch.

🤖 Filed with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) from a live debugging session.
title: schema_apply_mutations: failed batch is not atomic — earlier mutations stay applied to the pack file

body:
## Summary

`gbrain schema fork` and `gbrain schema list` cannot see `gbrain-base-v2` — the current *install default* pack — because they use a stale bundled-pack list. Users who want to customize the default taxonomy (the documented path for mutating a bundled pack is "fork it") hit a dead end.

## Environment

0.42.56.0 (`64617904`), macOS, clone + `bun link` install, PGLite brain with `gbrain-base-v2` active.

## Repro

```
$ gbrain schema fork gbrain-base-v2 engram-base
Source pack `gbrain-base-v2` not found.

$ gbrain schema list
Bundled packs:
  gbrain-base
  gbrain-recommended
```

Meanwhile the pack is bundled and active: `src/core/schema-pack/base/gbrain-base-v2.yaml` exists, and `loadActivePack` resolves it fine (its `BUNDLED` array in `src/core/schema-pack/load-active.ts` lists all seven packs, including gbrain-base-v2, creator, investor, engineer, everything).

## Root cause

Two bundled-pack lists have drifted: `load-active.ts` knows all 7; the fork/list code path only knows the original 2 (`gbrain-base`, `gbrain-recommended`).

## Workaround (verified)

Manual fork: `mkdir -p ~/.gbrain/schema-packs/<name>/ && cp src/core/schema-pack/base/gbrain-base-v2.yaml ~/.gbrain/schema-packs/<name>/pack.yaml`, edit the `name:` field, then mutate normally (`schema_apply_mutations` / CLI authoring commands work against the user copy; `schema validate <name>` passes).

## Suggested fix

Point fork/list at the same `BUNDLED` source of truth as `load-active.ts`.

🤖 Filed with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) from a live debugging session.
title: schema fork/list use a stale bundled-pack list — cannot fork gbrain-base-v2 (the install default)

body:
## Summary

Upgrading an existing PGLite brain from 0.42.44.0 to 0.42.56.0 (`64617904`) fails during `apply-migrations`: the baseline schema blob now references `timeline_entries(event_page_id)` (partial indexes added by the Life Chronicle release, #2390), but `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap()` in `src/core/pglite-engine.ts` was not extended to pre-add the column. Fresh installs are unaffected (the embedded schema includes the column), which is likely why it shipped.

## Environment

- macOS (Apple Silicon), install via `git clone + bun link`
- Engine: PGLite, existing brain at schema version 117 (created on 0.42.44.0)
- Upgrade: `git pull` 090bb532 → 64617904, `bun install`

## Repro

1. Have a PGLite brain created on ≤0.42.44.0 (schema version 117).
2. Upgrade the checkout to 0.42.56.0 and run `gbrain apply-migrations --yes`.

```
=== Applying migration v0.11.0: GBrain Minions — durable background agents ===
Phase A (schema) failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist. Aborting; re-run after fixing.
Migration v0.11.0 reported status=failed.
```

`gbrain doctor` shows the same and its suggested fix (`gbrain apply-migrations --yes`) loops on the identical failure.

## Root cause

`PGLiteEngine.initSchema()` runs in this order:

1. `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap()` — pre-adds columns the schema blob forward-references on older brains
2. `this.db.exec(getPGLiteSchema(...))` — the baseline blob, which now contains (`src/core/pglite-schema.ts:387-388`):
   ```sql
   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
   CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
   ```
3. `runMigrations()` — where migration **121** (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`) adds the column

On an existing brain, step 2 executes before step 3 and fails: `IF NOT EXISTS` guards the index name, not the referenced column. The bootstrap's own doc comment states the maintenance contract ("when a future migration adds a column-with-index … referenced by PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL, extend this method AND `test/schema-bootstrap-coverage.test.ts`") — migration 121's column was not added to it, and the coverage test evidently didn't catch the drift.

## Workaround (verified)

Pre-add the column using migration 121's own SQL via the engine, then re-run migrations:

```ts
await engine.db.exec(`ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;`);
```

After that, `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` completes cleanly (migration 121 lays down the FK + indexes idempotently) and a second run is a clean no-op.

## Suggested fix

Extend `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap()` (and `REQUIRED_BOOTSTRAP_COVERAGE`) with `timeline_entries.event_page_id`; possibly also investigate why the coverage test missed it.

🤖 Filed with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) from a live debugging session.
title: PGLite upgrade to 0.42.56 fails: baseline schema references event_page_id before migration 121 adds it (bootstrap drift)

body:
## Summary

`ChatOpts` has no way to pass provider-specific request-body params (e.g. DeepSeek's `thinking: {"type": "disabled"}`), even though the underlying `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` layer already forwards unknown `providerOptions[<providerName>]` keys into the request body verbatim. For hybrid-reasoning models (DeepSeek V4 family) this makes strict-output ops silently unusable: thinking mode is the API default, the reasoning channel starves `content`, and e.g. `extract-conversation-facts` returns 0 facts across every segment with no error surfaced.

## Repro / evidence

Setup: Postgres brain, `chat_model = deepseek:deepseek-v4-flash` (openai-compatible recipe, `provider_base_urls.deepseek` pointing at an aggregator that serves DeepSeek V4).

1. DeepSeek V4 (`deepseek-v4-flash` / `deepseek-v4-pro`) defaults to **thinking mode ON** (verified against both api.deepseek.com and a Tencent-MaaS aggregator). `POST /chat/completions` with `"thinking": {"type": "disabled"}` cleanly disables it on both.
2. In thinking mode, strict-JSON extraction prompts return their tokens in `reasoning_content`; `content` is empty/truncated → `extractFactsFromTurn` parses nothing → `0 facts inserted across N segments` for every page. Same prompt with thinking disabled: 9 good facts on our test window.
3. gbrain cannot send that field today:
   - `ChatOpts` (src/core/ai/gateway.ts ~L2394) only carries `model / system / messages / tools / maxTokens / abortSignal / cacheSystem`.
   - Inside `chat()` the `providerOptions` object passed to `generateText` is local and only ever populated with `providerOptions.anthropic` for `cacheSystem`.
   - No config key feeds per-provider chat params.
4. The plumbing below is already done: `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible`'s `getArgs` spreads unknown keys from `providerOptions[this.providerOptionsName]` into the request body (only schema-known keys are filtered). So a passthrough would reach the wire with no SDK work.

## Suggested fix (small)

Either:
- add `providerOptions?: Record<string, Record<string, unknown>>` to `ChatOpts` and merge it into the local `providerOptions` in `chat()`, or
- a config surface like `models.chat_provider_options.<recipeId> = {...}` merged at the same spot (lets `facts.extraction_model = deepseek:deepseek-v4-flash` run non-thinking without any caller changes).

Workaround we run today: a tiny local proxy in front of the provider that injects `thinking: {"type":"disabled"}` for `deepseek-v4*` chat calls. Works, but it's a daemon nobody should need.

## Two sibling blind spots we hit on the same non-Anthropic path (can split into separate issues if preferred)

1. **BudgetTracker TX2 prices only via `ANTHROPIC_PRICING`** (src/core/budget/budget-tracker.ts `lookupPricing`): with `maxCostUsd` set (CLI default, minion jobs, cycle phases), any non-Claude chat model → `no_pricing` hard-throw → absorbed by callers like extract.ts → silent 0-output runs. DeepSeek/GLM/Kimi users can't run budget-capped ops at all. Suggestion: price chat via recipe `cost_per_1m_*` touchpoints (like embeddings already do) before falling back to hard-fail.
2. **`createAnthropic({ apiKey })` ignores `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`** (src/core/ai/gateway.ts native-anthropic branches): users on Anthropic-compatible proxies (common where api.anthropic.com is unreachable) get opaque `Invalid JSON response` on every native-anthropic call. One-liner: pass `baseURL` from env/config when present.

Happy to provide more repro detail or test a branch. Thanks for gbrain — the rest of the stack has been remarkably solid to align with.
title: chat: no providerOptions passthrough for openai-compatible providers — hybrid-reasoning models (DeepSeek V4) silently break strict-output ops

body:
## Summary

`gbrain extract --stale` silently disables basename resolution by swapping in a `nullResolver` (no `resolveBasenameMatches`), diverging from the working `extractLinksFromDB` path. Additionally, `DIR_PATTERN` omits several directory prefixes (e.g. `ops/`), causing path-style qualified wikilinks like `[[ops/services/pointer-agent]]` to fall through to broken basename lookup instead of being recognized as qualified refs.

## Bug 1: `--stale` path uses `nullResolver` instead of real resolver

**File:** `src/commands/extract.ts`, `extractStaleFromDB` (~line 1618)

The stale extraction path sets:
```ts
const resolver = makeResolver(engine, { mode: 'batch' });
const nullResolver = { resolve: async () => null as string | null };
const activeResolver = includeFrontmatter ? resolver : nullResolver;
```

When `--include-frontmatter` is not passed (the default), `nullResolver` is used. It has no `resolveBasenameMatches` method, so the gate at `link-extraction.ts:485` (`!opts.globalBasename || typeof resolver.resolveBasenameMatches !== 'function'`) skips basename resolution entirely — regardless of whether `globalBasename` is enabled.

The non-stale path (`extractLinksFromDB`) always uses the real `resolver` and passes `{ skipFrontmatter: !includeFrontmatter, globalBasename }` to `extractPageLinks`. Frontmatter suppression is handled by the `skipFrontmatter` opt, not by swapping in a null resolver.

**Fix:** Pass the real `resolver` always; thread `{ skipFrontmatter, globalBasename }` opts (mirroring `extractLinksFromDB` ~line 1396).

## Bug 2: `DIR_PATTERN` omits `ops/` (and potentially other domain prefixes)

**File:** `src/core/link-extraction.ts` (~line 86)

```ts
const DIR_PATTERN = '(?:people|companies|meetings|concepts|deal|civic|project|projects|source|media|yc|tech|finance|personal|openclaw|entities)';
```

Brains with `ops/` directories (change records, runbooks, service notes) have their `[[ops/services/pointer-agent]]` wikilinks silently dropped during extraction because `ops` isn't in the whitelist. The refs never enter the qualified resolver's ancestor-walk + pg_trgm fuzzy path.

**Fix:** Add `ops` to `DIR_PATTERN`. Also: `normalizeBasename` strips slashes (`[^a-z0-9\s-]`), so path-style inputs like `ops/changes/2026-05-01-pointer-agent` normalize to `opschanges2026-05-01-pointer-…` — never matching the tail-keyed basename index. Extract the path tail before normalizing.

## Bug 3: `LINK_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS` needs periodic bumping

After running `extract --stale` once, all pages get stamped `links_extracted_at = now`. A subsequent `extract --stale` (after a fix to the extraction logic) finds 0 stale pages and no-ops. The version timestamp is designed to force re-extraction when the extractor logic changes, but it hasn't been bumped since `2026-05-31`.

## Reference implementation

We've validated a bundled fix for all three issues. Patch available here: https://github.com/drizzyenterprises/asset-007-hermes-runtime/pull/27

## Environment

- gbrain 0.42.53.0
- Postgres + pgvector
- 340 pages, 317 with un-extracted edges after initial bulk sync
title: extract --stale silently disables basename resolution (nullResolver) + DIR_PATTERN gaps for non-standard dirs

body:
Hi @garrytan 👋

Heads up on a real deployment blocker, + workaround, in case it helps other macOS 26.3 users.

**Bug**: On macOS 26.3, gbrain's default PGLite (WASM) crashes at startup — WASM runtime abort, process won't come up. The zero-config (PGLite) path is broken on macOS 26.3; gbrain is unusable until you switch engines. Repro: `gbrain status` with default config → WASM abort.

**Workaround**: Switch to the postgres engine (already supported). `brew postgresql@17 + pgvector`, set `engine: postgres`. Confirmed in engine-factory.ts that PGLite WASM is never loaded for postgres users (dynamic imports), so the crash is fully sidestepped. Trade-off: loses zero-config (you host postgres), but the brain becomes multi-connection — several agents share one brain simultaneously, which PGLite's single-process write-lock doesn't allow. Useful for users whose systems have dependencies that make upgrading macOS inconvenient right now.

**Side tool (separate, just sharing)**: The admin dashboard shows ops stats but not memory content, so I built a small `memory-browser` web UI (read/edit/delete/bulk, Bun + postgres). Open-sourced: https://github.com/yang1996202-cpu/gbrain-memory-browser

Thanks for gbrain! 🙏
title: PGLite WASM crashes on macOS 26.3 (breaks zero-config) — postgres engine works as a workaround

body:
## Summary

`buildGatewayConfig()` in `src/core/ai/build-gateway-config.ts` maps `openai_api_key`, `anthropic_api_key`, and `zeroentropy_api_key` into the gateway environment dict, but **does not map `openrouter_api_key`**. Users who configure their OpenRouter API key in `~/.gbrain/config.json` (file-plane) find it silently dropped.

## Root Cause

Three missing seams:

1. **`GBrainConfig` interface** (`src/core/config.ts`) — no `openrouter_api_key` field to accept the key from file-plane parse
2. **`loadConfig` env overlay** (`src/core/config.ts`) — no `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` to `openrouter_api_key` merge path
3. **`buildGatewayConfig` mapping** (`src/core/ai/build-gateway-config.ts`) — missing the `c.openrouter_api_key` to `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` line

The OpenRouter recipe declares `auth_env.required: ['OPENROUTER_API_KEY']`. When the gateway's `defaultResolveAuth` reads `cfg.env[required[0]]`, it finds `undefined` even though the key exists in `config.json`. The gateway then reports `missing_env` and skips the OpenRouter recipe entirely.

## Symptoms

| Operation | Outcome |
|-----------|---------|
| `gbrain takes extract --from-pages --yes` | Fails with "chat gateway unavailable (no API key configured)" |
| `gbrain dream` propose_takes phase | Runs but produces 0 proposals via chat gateway |
| Embedding via OpenAI recipe | Works (uses OPENAI_API_KEY, which IS mapped) |
| MCP server sessions | Work (inherits real OS env vars) |

## Workaround

Set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` as a real OS environment variable (in `~/.bashrc`, `~/.zshrc`, or the process launcher). This bypasses the file-plane gap because `process.env` is spread into the gateway env dict last.

## Fix

PR: https://github.com/psam-717/gbrain/pull/new/fix/openrouter-api-key-gateway

Adds `openrouter_api_key` to all three seams following the same pattern as `zeroentropy_api_key` (v0.37 fix wave).
title: buildGatewayConfig does not map openrouter_api_key into gateway env — chat-based operations fail for file-plane key users

body:
## What problem does this solve?

Dream/synthesis can write pages autonomously, but generated pages currently enter the normal brain corpus without a native review/adoption boundary. Unlike proposed takes — which have a review-first path before promotion into canonical knowledge — generated pages have no first-class way to distinguish:

- generated but pending
- adopted
- dismissed
- superseded

This creates two failure modes:

1. **Inert generated knowledge.** Useful generated pages exist, but remain linked only from dream summaries or sibling generated pages and are never operationally adopted.
2. **Over-trusted generated knowledge.** Generated pages can be served by retrieval/agents with the same apparent authority as curated human-authored pages.

This depends on durable generated-page provenance first: see #2569. Once GBrain can reliably enumerate generated pages, it can expose their review/adoption state without turning synthesis into manual admin homework.

## Evidence from one production corpus

Measured under `wiki/originals/ideas/`:

- 156 generated/synthesis idea pages inspected
- 32 pages (`21%`) have zero inbound links
- 40 pages (`26%`) are linked only from a dream-cycle summary
- 12 pages (`8%`) are linked only from sibling generated pages in the generated cluster
- 72 pages (`46%`) have some non-generated external inbound link

So **84 / 156 pages (`54%`) have no meaningful adoption signal**.

Existing orphan detection can only see the first group. The summary-only and generated-cluster-only pages are technically linked, but those links are provenance artifacts, not adoption. Connectivity is not the same thing as review/adoption state.

## Related upstream context

- #2418 — review-first take proposals pattern
- #2269 — MCP accept/reject for take proposals
- #160 — trust-gate discussion for untrusted enrichment writes
- #2163 — generated/synthesized pages unreachable by retrieval
- #2415 — generated-page filing/path concerns
- #2283 — dream synthesize backfill/frontmatter/operator-visibility cluster
- #1511 — prior no-write dream review lane was withdrawn/fork-carried, not rejected

This proposal is the pages half of the same review-first doctrine already emerging for takes: generated/proposed knowledge should not silently become stronger canonical knowledge without an explicit adoption boundary.

## Proposed v1: read-only report

Add a read-only command, for example:

```bash
gbrain generated list --since 30d --source-id default --json
```

or similar naming preferred by the maintainer.

v1 should do **zero writes**. It should be a query/report surface only.

### Inputs/signals

Deterministic signals only:

- durable generated-page provenance from #2569
- created/updated window (`--since`)
- source scoping (`--source-id`)
- inbound-link classification
- page type/tags passthrough

No LLM importance judgment in v1.

### Link classification

Classify generated pages into buckets such as:

```text
none
summary_only
generated_cluster_only
externally_linked
```

Where:

- `none` = no inbound links
- `summary_only` = only inbound links are dream-cycle summaries
- `generated_cluster_only` = only inbound links are other generated pages / same generated cluster
- `externally_linked` = at least one inbound link from outside generated/summary provenance surfaces

### Output shape

JSON should include counts for all buckets and a capped candidate list, e.g. top 5–10. Reason codes are enough; no fake precision/confidence floats.

Example shape:

```json
{
  "summary": {
    "generated_pages_scanned": 156,
    "none": 32,
    "summary_only": 40,
    "generated_cluster_only": 12,
    "externally_linked": 72
  },
  "candidates": [
    {
      "slug": "wiki/originals/ideas/example",
      "title": "Example generated page",
      "bucket": "summary_only",
      "reasons": ["only_dream_summary_backlink"]
    }
  ]
}
```

Optional: add a `doctor` check (`unreviewed_generated_pages`) that warns with counts and points to the command.

## Possible v2: explicit state, after demand is proven

If v1 proves useful, add explicit operator-driven state:

```text
pending (implicit: generated + no decision marker)
→ adopted | dismissed | superseded
```

This mirrors `take_proposals` semantics better than a vague `reviewed` state. Review is the act; adoption/dismissal/supersession is the decision.

Possible storage:

- namespaced frontmatter marker, following the quarantine marker-contract precedent; or
- a table similar to `take_proposals`, if preferred for auditability.

Write verbs should remain explicit:

```bash
gbrain generated adopt <slug>
gbrain generated dismiss <slug> --reason "not useful"
gbrain generated supersede <slug> --by <new-slug>
```

No automatic promotion, no automatic task creation, no notification loop, no dashboard/inbox in v1.

## Non-goals

- auto-promoting generated pages to doctrine/canonical pages
- auto-creating tasks
- auto hub-linking
- LLM judging importance
- mandatory manual approval for every generated page
- recurring nag notifications
- dashboard/productivity-inbox surface
- path-based `_pending/` moves that collide with frontmatter/DB-status direction

## Alternatives considered

- **Use existing orphan detection.** Insufficient: many unadopted generated pages are technically linked via dream summaries or generated siblings.
- **Doctor check only.** Useful, but insufficient for agents/MCP unless there is a stable JSON report contract.
- **Agent recipe/skill only.** A recipe cannot fix provenance durability and every core consumer needs the same generated-page membership predicate.
- **LLM scoring.** Excluded from v1; deterministic topology/state signals are enough.
- **Full review queue/dashboard.** Too much surface too early and risks turning dream/synthesis into admin homework.

## Acceptance criteria

- Report mode performs zero writes; repeated runs are idempotent.
- JSON output is stable and versionable.
- Counts are always included, with candidates capped.
- Source scoping is respected; no cross-source leakage.
- A generated page linked only from dream summaries and/or generated siblings is flagged as unadopted/pending.
- A generated page linked from an external non-generated page is classified separately.
- If a doctor check is added, it reports counts and a remediation pointer rather than creating tasks.
- If v2 state is added later, `adopted`, `dismissed`, and `superseded` pages do not reappear as pending unless content changes after the decision.
title: Add review-first lifecycle for dream/synthesis-generated pages

body:
## What problem does this solve?

`dream_generated: true` is the identity surface for dream/synthesis output. It is used by the self-consumption guard (`isDreamOutput`, `src/core/cycle/transcript-discovery.ts`) and by generated-output demotion conventions in search/source handling. But on current `master` (`646179047a8e4ad9c462d83ce9a67a50ba076de8`), the stamp is not durable for generated child pages:

1. **DB/file split.** `renderPageToMarkdown()` in `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts` stamps generated child pages via `frontmatterOverrides` only (`dream_generated: true`, `dream_cycle_date`). `serializePageToMarkdown()` then merges `{...page.frontmatter, ...frontmatterOverrides}` at render time (`src/core/markdown.ts`). That gets the marker into the rendered markdown file, but it does not persist the marker into the `pages.frontmatter` JSONB row for those pages. DB-level consumers therefore cannot reliably enumerate generated child pages via `frontmatter->>'dream_generated'`.

2. **Stamp decay on later write-through.** `put_page` write-through in `src/core/operations.ts` re-renders from the saved DB row with only ingestion/source overrides. If the DB row never had `dream_generated`, a later `put_page` write can silently erase the marker from the file.

3. **Reverse-write failures/skips are hard to observe as provenance loss.** The summary page persists its own generated marker into DB, but generated children rely on reverse-write rendering. If a child page is listed in a dream-cycle summary but the reverse-write marker is missing or later erased, downstream systems have no durable membership predicate.

4. **Agent/MCP-driven synthesis has no generated-page identity.** Pages produced by synthesis-driving agents through `put_page` can look like ordinary curated writes (`ingested_via: mcp:put_page`) rather than generated output, even when operationally they are part of a synthesis run.

Measured on one production brain corpus that motivated this report, under `wiki/originals/ideas/`:

- 156 generated/synthesis idea pages inspected
- 68 stamped / **88 unstamped** (`56%` unstamped)
- 67 pages are listed from dream-cycle summaries; **8 of those are unstamped**
- 80 unstamped pages were not listed from the summaries and look like ordinary `put_page` writes

That means generated pages can be impossible to enumerate reliably, and can be treated by retrieval/agents with the same authority as human-authored/curated pages.

## Code references checked against current master

Current `origin/master`: `646179047a8e4ad9c462d83ce9a67a50ba076de8`.

- `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts:1113-1125` — `renderPageToMarkdown()` stamps `dream_generated` / `dream_cycle_date` as `frontmatterOverrides`.
- `src/core/markdown.ts:667-670` — `serializePageToMarkdown()` merges `page.frontmatter` with render-time overrides; this is a render funnel, not a DB write.
- `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts:1157-1165` — the dream summary is different: its marker is passed into `serializeMarkdown()` before parsing/persisting, so the summary marker survives in DB.
- `src/core/operations.ts:909-917` — `put_page` write-through only passes ingestion/source overrides when re-rendering.
- `src/core/cycle/transcript-discovery.ts:57-73, 114-120, 238-240` — the self-consumption guard depends on the frontmatter marker.
- `src/core/search/source-boost.ts:48-53` — generated receipts/search conventions rely on `dream_generated`-style demotion semantics.

## Expected behavior

Generated pages should have durable provenance. A page generated by dream/synthesis should remain identifiable as generated across:

- DB queries
- file render/write-through
- later `put_page` updates
- doctor/MCP/agent consumers
- backfills and summary-link reconciliation

## Possible fix shape

- Persist `dream_generated: true` / `dream_cycle_date` into the DB row frontmatter at generated-page write time, not only during markdown rendering.
- Preserve the stamp on later `put_page` write-throughs.
- Surface reverse-write provenance mismatches in cycle result details and/or a doctor check.
- Add an idempotent backfill path for pages already linked from `dream-cycle-summaries/*` and pages whose markdown files already carry the stamp.

## Alternatives considered

- **Query markdown files instead of DB.** This leaves MCP/doctor/engine consumers blind and does not solve stamp decay on rewrite.
- **Use dream summary links as the only provenance.** Summary links are useful evidence, but they are not a durable identity contract and miss out-of-band/agent-driven synthesis.
- **Do nothing.** The generated-page guard/demotion/review story silently degrades as generated corpora age and pages are rewritten.

Happy to contribute a focused fix and regression tests if this approach sounds right.

## Acceptance criteria

- Generated child pages persist `dream_generated: true` in DB frontmatter as well as rendered markdown.
- `put_page` update of a stamped generated page preserves the stamp in DB and file.
- DB-level generated-page count for a fresh run approximately matches summary-listed generated children, modulo explicit write failures/skips surfaced in the result.
- Backfill is idempotent on both PGLite and Postgres.
- Tests cover the render/write-through decay path.
title: dream/synthesize: dream_generated provenance is render-time only — generated pages are unqueryable in DB, can leak during runs, and put_page writes erase it

body:
every new gbrain starts empty - and the densest personal corpus most people have is years of AI chat history locked in per-vendor export formats. we run gbrain in production and hit this on day one: the cold-start problem isn't building the brain, it's filling it.

we built an open MIT standard for the export side (memvelope - one portable JSON envelope any chat export converts into; gbrain was the biggest influence on how we think about portable memory, credited in the spec) and a reference importer that maps an envelope into gbrain's markdown-brain-repo model: one page per conversation, provenance frontmatter, message-id citations, `type: conversation`.

live-verified against upstream v0.42.56.0: a real 662MB Claude export -> 353 conversations -> 353 distinct pages, keyword-searchable, provenance and citations intact. deterministic, zero deps, no network.

PR follows with the importer adapted to this repo's conventions plus a self-contained test. no behavior change to any existing path. happy to maintain it.
title: import: seeding a brain from AI chat exports (ChatGPT/Claude) via memvelope envelopes

body:
## Summary

A source registered with `--federated` (`config.federated: true`) never appears in unqualified local CLI `gbrain query` / `gbrain search` results, contradicting `docs/guides/multi-source-brains.md`:

> | `true` | Source participates in unqualified `gbrain search "X"` results. |

Only the resolved source (for me: `default`) is ever searched.

## Environment

- gbrain 0.42.56.0 (local build at 21b137f0), Postgres engine
- Brain: `default` (federated, ~24k pages) + several isolated sources + the new test source below

## Repro

```bash
mkdir -p ~/gbrain-sources/recipes && cd ~/gbrain-sources/recipes
git init && echo "# Recipe: knowledge-base cleanup (dedup + thin-content quarantine) ..." > recipe.md
git add -A && git commit -m seed

gbrain sources add recipes --path ~/gbrain-sources/recipes --federated
# → "federated: true — appears in cross-source default search"
gbrain sync --source recipes    # 1 file imported, 1 chunk, 1 page embedded

cd $HOME
gbrain search "thin-content quarantine" --source recipes --limit 3
# → [1.1224] recipe ...  ✓ found, top score

gbrain search "thin-content quarantine" --limit 5
# → only default-source pages, best [0.8791]; the 1.1224 recipes hit is ABSENT
gbrain query "recipe knowledge base cleanup duplicates quarantine" --limit 5 --expand false
# → same: recipes page absent despite outscoring every returned hit within its source
```

Page row verified clean: `frontmatter = {"date": "2026-07-03"}` (no quarantine key), `sources.config->>'federated' = 'true'`, synced + embedded.

## Where I think it lives (code read, not bisected)

- `src/core/source-resolver.ts` resolves to exactly ONE `source_id` (flag → env → dotfile → cwd → brain default → `'default'`). From `$HOME` that's `default`.
- `OperationContext.sourceId` is required and scalar; `sourceScopeOpts()` emits `{sourceId}` for local CLI, and reads filter `WHERE source_id = $X`.
- The only code that produces a multi-source `sourceIds[]` scope is the OAuth `federated_read` grant path (#876/#2200). I could not find any path where local CLI unqualified search expands the resolved source with `sources.config.federated = true` rows.

So `config.federated` appears to affect doctor/sync reporting but not local CLI search scope — i.e. the documented "joins unqualified search" behavior seems unimplemented or regressed by the v0.34 source-scoping work (D4/D9).

Related but distinct: #1913 (config corruption flips federated→isolated — not my case, flag verified true), #2200 (OAuth federated grant scope, not local CLI).

## Expected

Unqualified local `gbrain search` / `gbrain query` = resolved source ∪ all `config.federated=true` sources, per the guide.

## Workaround

Import the content into the `default` source directly (`gbrain import <dir>`) — surfaces fine (top-1, 0.9047).
title: sources.config.federated ignored by unqualified local CLI search/query — federated source invisible unless --source named (docs say it joins cross-source search)

body:
On a standard `serve --http` deployment (docker, Postgres), `sync` enqueues `embed-backfill` jobs but **nothing ever dequeues them** — our oldest job has been `waiting` since the box was born:

```
id=1 name=embed-backfill queue=default status=waiting attempts=0 created=2026-07-02
```

There is no worker process unless the operator explicitly runs one (`jobs work`? autopilot?), and nothing in the standard setup says so. All real embedding on our box has come from the CLI path; the "async backfill" implied by the enqueue never existed.

If serve is not supposed to run a worker by default: the docs should state that a worker must be started explicitly, and `doctor`/`advisor` could flag jobs stuck in `waiting` beyond N hours (advisor already has a "stalled jobs" collector — a never-started worker seems to evade it). If serve IS supposed to process the queue, this is a bug. Related pain: #1737 (long-running Minion jobs thrash when a worker does run).
title: Default deployment has no job-queue worker: embed-backfill jobs stay 'waiting' forever

body:
`gbrain think "…" --anchor note/politica-sconti --take` (local CLI, v0.42.52.0 @ bb2e88c, chat model openai:gemma4:e4b) completes and prints the synthesis; the footer itself says `Takes: 0`, and `SELECT count(*) FROM takes` stays 0 afterwards. No error, no hint. The MCP variant with `take: true` behaves the same (when it doesn't hit client timeouts first).

Expected: a take row appended to the anchor page, or an explicit message saying what's missing.

Possibly the same holder-identity fragmentation family as #2464 / #2465 (take written under a holder that never matches?), or related to #2471 (takes never embedded — suggests the takes write path has low traffic). If takes require extra config (holder identity, calibration profile), `--take` should say so instead of silently writing nothing.
title: think --take silently writes no take row (CLI and MCP)

body:
Legacy bearer token federated over `["generale","default","libri","prova"]` with floor source `generale` (permissions.source_id array), v0.42.52.0 @ bb2e88c, remote MCP:

- `get_page("clienti/acme")` (page lives in source `prova`) → **works** (federated read)
- `search` → returns that page's chunk fine
- `get_chunks("clienti/acme")` → **`[]`** (empty array, no error)

Either get_chunks doesn't apply the federated-read grant (scoping bug), or cross-source chunk reads are intentionally floor-only — in which case an explicit `permission_denied` would beat an empty array, which reads as "this page has no chunks".

Same trust-boundary family as #2529 (legacy bearer scoping for takes_holders).
title: MCP get_chunks returns [] for pages readable via federated grant (outside the token's floor source)

body:
The CLI backfill (`embed --stale`) commits **per page** (single upsert when the page completes), and each invocation carries a 30-minute wall-clock budget (`GBRAIN_EMBED_TIME_BUDGET_MS`, embed.ts:872 area @ bb2e88c).

A page whose chunks need more than 30 minutes of embedding (big book, CPU-only Ollama) therefore **always dies before its first commit and restarts from zero** on the next invocation — a cron/loop of `embed --stale` never converges (Sisyphus). Only `--catch-up` (which removes the budget) breaks the loop; we found it by reading source, not docs.

Repro: CPU-only ollama/bge-m3, one page with ~2,400 chunks, run `embed --stale` in a loop → the same page re-embeds forever.

Suggestion: document the interaction prominently, or auto-lift the budget when a single page alone exceeds it (the budget exists to bound a run, not to make a page unembeddable). Related: #1946.
title: embed --stale: 30-min time budget + page-level commit makes >30min pages unembeddable without --catch-up (Sisyphus loop)

body:
With `models.default = openai:gemma4:e4b` (Ollama masquerade via OPENAI_BASE_URL, CPU-only), `gbrain eval suspected-contradictions --query "..."` produced on a **planted contradiction** (policy "discounts max 10%, no exceptions" vs plan "25% discount for everyone"):

```
Judge errors: 10 (parse_fail=9 timeout=1)
Total findings flagged: 0
```

PROMPT_VERSION 2, v0.42.52.0 @ bb2e88c. So the probe pipeline (budget, cache, tiers) works, but the judge's rigid output format is not followed by small local models → 100% parse failures → every real contradiction sails through undetected.

Notably the SAME model identifies the same contradiction correctly in free prose via `think` — the capability is there, the judge output contract isn't met. #1145 reported a similar all-judges-fail mode (closed); this is the small-local-model flavor.

Suggestion: a tolerant parse / one-shot repair pass for non-frontier judges, or a documented "judge model must be X-class" requirement with a pre-flight check.
title: eval suspected-contradictions: judge output unparseable with small local models (gemma-class) — 10/10 parse_fail on a planted contradiction
Cloud-tuned embedding defaults (concurrency 20, no_batch_cap, char-based batch estimate) silently wedge CPU-only Ollama boxes
serve --http: /admin/events SSE never opens behind a buffering reverse proxy (no initial flush/heartbeat)

body:
## Summary

`recordCompleted()` in `src/core/op-checkpoint.ts` writes `completed_keys` as a **JSONB scalar string** instead of a JSONB array, on Postgres (not PGLite). This breaks checkpoint resume for every op that uses `recordCompleted` (observed: `sync-target` pin write in `sync.ts`, and `extract-conversation-facts`), forcing full-corpus replays on every run instead of incremental resume — which in turn re-overwrites hand-edited/curated pages with stale synthesized content on every sync cycle.

## Root cause

```ts
// src/core/op-checkpoint.ts, recordCompleted()
const sorted = [...keys].sort();
return durableWrite(engine, key, 'write', () =>
  engine.executeRawDirect(
    `INSERT INTO op_checkpoints (op, fingerprint, completed_keys, updated_at)
     VALUES ($1, $2, $3::jsonb, now())
     ON CONFLICT (op, fingerprint) DO UPDATE
       SET completed_keys = EXCLUDED.completed_keys,
           updated_at     = now()`,
    [key.op, key.fingerprint, JSON.stringify(sorted)],  // <-- bug
  ));
```

Binding `JSON.stringify(sorted)` (a JS **string**) to a `$N::jsonb` parameter through `postgres.js`'s `.unsafe(sql, params)` double-encodes: the driver serializes the already-JSON-text string again before Postgres applies the `::jsonb` cast, so the column ends up holding a jsonb **scalar string** containing the JSON text, not a jsonb array.

This is exactly the double-encode class the project already guards against in `sqlQueryForEngine`/`executeRawJsonb` and `scripts/check-jsonb-pattern.sh` — but the CI guard only pattern-matches the tagged-template-literal form (`` `${JSON.stringify(...)}::jsonb` ``), so it doesn't catch this parameterized-bind form, and this call site slipped through.

Downstream, `loadOpCheckpoint()`'s `jsonb_array_elements_text(completed_keys)` then throws on the scalar value:

```
[op-checkpoint] load failed (sync-target, <fingerprint>): cannot extract elements from a scalar
```

`loadOpCheckpoint` swallows this (returns `[]`, logs to stderr), so the op silently believes nothing is done yet and re-walks everything. For `sync`'s ~15-18k file repos, that means every single run does a full replay instead of an incremental resume — and since sync re-imports/re-synthesizes pages from source material, it clobbers any manually-curated edits to those pages made between runs.

## Reproduction (isolated, against real Postgres — not PGLite)

```js
import postgres from 'postgres';
const sql = postgres(DATABASE_URL, { max: 1 });

// Pattern A — current gbrain code
await sql.unsafe(
  `INSERT INTO op_checkpoints (op, fingerprint, completed_keys, updated_at)
   VALUES ($1, $2, $3::jsonb, now())
   ON CONFLICT (op, fingerprint) DO UPDATE SET completed_keys = EXCLUDED.completed_keys, updated_at = now()`,
  ['repro-op', 'a', JSON.stringify(['hash1', 'hash2'])]
);

// Pattern B — fix: bind the raw array, no JSON.stringify
await sql.unsafe(
  `INSERT INTO op_checkpoints (op, fingerprint, completed_keys, updated_at)
   VALUES ($1, $2, $3::jsonb, now())
   ON CONFLICT (op, fingerprint) DO UPDATE SET completed_keys = EXCLUDED.completed_keys, updated_at = now()`,
  ['repro-op', 'b', ['hash1', 'hash2']]
);

const rows = await sql.unsafe(
  `SELECT fingerprint, jsonb_typeof(completed_keys) AS typ, completed_keys::text AS val
   FROM op_checkpoints WHERE op = 'repro-op' ORDER BY fingerprint`
);
console.log(rows);
```

Output:

```json
[
  { "fingerprint": "a", "typ": "string", "val": "\"[\\\"hash1\\\",\\\"hash2\\\"]\"" },
  { "fingerprint": "b", "typ": "array",  "val": "[\"hash1\", \"hash2\"]" }
]
```

`postgres@3.4.x`, confirmed on gbrain v0.42.37.0 (latest at time of filing).

## Fix

One-line change — bind the raw array, don't pre-stringify:

```diff
- [key.op, key.fingerprint, JSON.stringify(sorted)],
+ [key.op, key.fingerprint, sorted],
```

Verified this produces `jsonb_typeof = 'array'` and that `gbrain sync` correctly resumes from checkpoint afterward (`resuming checkpoint: N file(s) already done; draining ...` instead of a full replay).

## Suggested follow-ups

1. Apply the same one-line fix to `recordCompleted` (affects every caller: `extract.ts`, `sync.ts`'s target-pin write, `enrich.ts`, `extract-conversation-facts.ts`).
2. Extend `scripts/check-jsonb-pattern.sh` to also catch the parameterized form (`JSON.stringify(...)` appearing as a bound parameter value where the corresponding `$N` position in the SQL string is cast `::jsonb`), not just the tagged-template interpolation form. The current regex (`\$\{JSON\.stringify\([^)]*\)\}::jsonb`) can't see this shape at all since there's no `${...}` interpolation in the query string itself.
3. Consider a schema-level backstop (e.g. a `BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE` trigger or a `CHECK (jsonb_typeof(completed_keys) = 'array')` constraint on `op_checkpoints`) so a future regression of this class fails loudly at write time instead of silently degrading resume behavior.

Happy to open a PR with the one-line fix + a regression test if useful.
title: recordCompleted() double-encodes completed_keys as jsonb string on Postgres, breaking checkpoint resume (sync-target, extract-conversation-facts)

body:
## Summary

Running gbrain against a hosted Postgres (Supabase) accumulated ~20 GB egress in a month, tracked to two query patterns in the Postgres path. Both are read-side byte-over-wire issues, not correctness bugs.

## 1. `getChunks` fetches embedding columns it discards

`PostgresEngine.getChunks()` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts`, ~L2187) uses `SELECT cc.*`. `content_chunks` has three vector columns — `embedding` (1536-d), `embedding_image` (1024-d), `embedding_multimodal` (1024-d), ~18 KB/row combined — that this path never uses: `rowToChunk` is called without `includeEmbedding`, so they're parsed to `null`/dropped. `getChunksWithEmbeddings()` is the separate path that actually needs them.

Fix: select the explicit non-embedding column list. Same applies to the PGLite sibling `PgliteEngine.getChunks()` (`src/core/pglite-engine.ts`, ~L2102) for parity, though PGLite is in-process so it's memory, not egress.

Patch (Postgres):
```sql
SELECT cc.id, cc.page_id, cc.chunk_index, cc.chunk_text, cc.chunk_source,
       cc.model, cc.token_count, cc.embedded_at, cc.language, cc.symbol_name,
       cc.symbol_type, cc.start_line, cc.end_line, cc.parent_symbol_path,
       cc.doc_comment, cc.symbol_name_qualified
FROM content_chunks cc ...
```

## 2. `getAllSlugs` full-table scan once per put_page

`SlugResolver.ensureBasenameIndex()` (`src/core/link-extraction.ts`, ~L905) calls `engine.getAllSlugs()` — `SELECT slug FROM pages` — once per extract run / put_page to build the basename→slug fuzzy-match index. Over a bulk ingest this is N_pages × N_ingested rows. In our brain: ~584 slugs × ~1,972 runs ≈ 1.18 M rows, the single largest egress line in `pg_stat_statements`.

The index is cached within a resolver instance but the resolver is rebuilt per put_page. Options: cache the slug snapshot across an ingest batch, build the basename index once per batch run, or scope the fetch to slugs referenced by the page's links. (This one has wikilink-resolution correctness implications, so it needs more care than #1.)

## Environment
- gbrain v0.42.38.0, Postgres engine (Supabase, `postgres` role via pooler)
title: Postgres egress: getChunks selects cc.* (unused embeddings) + per-put_page getAllSlugs full scan

body:
**Thesis:** for visually-rich PDFs, "PDF → text → chunks" is the wrong abstraction. A text extractor — #173's suggested pluggable `PDFExtractor`, or any of pdftotext / PyMuPDF / marker / markitdown — linearizes the page and discards the two things that carry meaning in papers and reports: **table structure** and **figures**. This isn't an extractor-quality problem you fix with a better tool; it's inherent to making *text* the representation.

**Evidence** (RAG paper, arXiv:2005.11401): plain-text extraction flattened a `55.2 / 66.1` table cell to `55.266.1`, and a whole-document single-vector embedding silently dropped ~32% of pages (including the last). A vision-read of *detected semantic units* preserves both. Rationale + method: [research.md](https://github.com/muhammedhunaid/gbrain/blob/feat/visual-doc-retrieval/docs/proposals/visual-doc-retrieval/research.md).

**Proposed primitive:** a `units` table — table/figure/section regions with a **multimodal image embedding** + provenance — retrieved by multimodal recall + `rerank-2.5`, answered by a vision-read of the top unit. A parallel high-fidelity path, not a variant of the text pipeline.

**Why gbrain, why now:** it's **Bun-pure** (`pdftoppm` + the gateway — no Python, unlike marker/PyMuPDF/markitdown), and it *extends the Voyage multimodal + image ingestion already shipped in v0.27.1 (#664)* from single images to structured documents. One additive `units` table (parity-pinned across both engines), all AI through the gateway, source-isolated, budget-bounded, model/version pinned in provenance.

**Status:** working, tested implementation as 6 small stacked PRs on [my fork](https://github.com/muhammedhunaid/gbrain/pulls), each TDD'd and reviewed; supersedes the docs-only proposal in #2322. Happy to open against upstream in reviewable slices and wire into gbrain-evals.

**The decision I'm asking for:** is the visual-unit primitive worth a dedicated path in core, or does gbrain intentionally stay text-only and treat this as an external tool? Either answer is useful — but I don't think the research-paper use case in #173 can be met text-first.
title: proposal: visual semantic units as the PDF primitive — text extraction can't ingest research papers

body:
---

**Version:** 0.42.56.0 (bun install from master, 2026-07-02)
**Area:** embedding-dim validation for local/user-provided models

Three connected facets, all in the same corner (`src/core/embedding-dim-check.ts` +
recipes). Net effect: a fully-local embedder that isn't one of the three registered Ollama
models cannot be brought up through any documented path.

## Facet 1 — llama-server: mandatory explicit dims are always rejected

`recipes/llama-server.ts` declares `default_dims: 0 // forces explicit
--embedding-dimensions`, but declares no `dims_options`. In
`validateDimAgainstTouchpoint`, any `requestedDims !== defaultDims` (i.e. anything ≠ 0)
routes into `isCustomDimValidForProvider`, which has no Tier-1 `dims_options` for this
recipe and no Tier-2 allow-list, so it falls to the Tier-3 rejection:

```
Refusing to init: Provider "llama-server" model "<any>" does not support custom
dimensions <any> (this model only emits its default vector size).
```

The recipe's own required flag can never validate. Repro:
`gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model llama-server:anything --embedding-dimensions 1024`

Suggested fix: give llama-server (and other `user_provided_models` recipes) either a
permissive `dims_options` or a `user_provided_models`-aware bypass in
`isCustomDimValidForProvider` — the operator asserting dims is the recipe's design intent.

## Facet 2 — Ollama: any model outside the 3-model list is unusable

`recipes/ollama.ts` registers `['nomic-embed-text','mxbai-embed-large','all-minilm']` at
`default_dims: 768`. For any other Ollama model (e.g. `qwen3-embedding:0.6b`, native
1024d, in the Ollama library), explicit `--embedding-dimensions 1024` is rejected by the
same Tier-3 fallthrough — even though the requested dims are the model's NATIVE size.
Note `mxbai-embed-large` is natively 1024d, so the single 768 default is also wrong for
one of the three registered models.

## Facet 3 — `providers test --model` self-defeats for these recipes

`src/commands/providers.ts` (isolation-test path) configures the gateway with
`recipe.touchpoints.embedding.default_dims ?? 1536` — for llama-server that's `0`, so the
probe always reports "Embedding provider not configured or not ready". Separately,
`diagnoseEmbedding` returns `user_provided_model_unset` whenever the recipe's static
`models[]` is empty, even when `config.embedding_model` carries a fully-qualified
`llama-server:<model>` — so availability can never pass for that provider.

## Facet 4 — wikilink extraction ignores the active schema pack's path prefixes

`src/core/link-extraction.ts` gates `[[dir/...]]` wikilink extraction on a hardcoded
`DIR_PATTERN` whitelist (people, companies, meetings, …). Custom schema packs declare
their own `path_prefixes`, but the extractor never consults the active pack — so wikilinks
between pages of a custom pack are silently dropped and the knowledge graph (the
advertised +31 P@5 lift) never forms. Empirically on a 232-page custom-pack brain:
13 links extracted before patching the whitelist, 174 after. Suggested fix: build
DIR_PATTERN from the active pack's declared path_prefixes (plus the canonical defaults).

## Workaround we're running (happy to PR any of this)

Local recipe patches: add the model to `ollama.ts` `models[]` + `dims_options: [768,1024]`;
add `dims_options` to `llama-server.ts`. With that, `ollama:qwen3-embedding:0.6b` at 1024d
inits, embeds, and retrieves correctly (verified against Ollama's OpenAI-compatible
`/v1/embeddings`).
title: Local custom embedders can't pass dim validation; wikilink extractor ignores schema-pack path prefixes (4 related bugs, v0.42.56.0)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain doctor`'s `cycle_freshness` check FAILs with "never completed a full cycle" **permanently** on installs where (a) the active schema pack doesn't declare optional phases like `extract_atoms` / `synthesize_concepts`, and/or (b) the dream runs via the in-process `gbrain dream --dir` path. Every successful (rc=0) nightly dream is classified "partial", and `last_full_cycle_at` is never stamped — so a healthy install shows a permanent doctor FAIL.

## Repro (v0.42.53.0)

1. Run an active pack that omits `extract_atoms`/`synthesize_concepts` (or disable those phases).
2. Run nightly dreams via `gbrain dream --dir <vault>`; observe rc=0 completions on all phases the pack declares.
3. `gbrain doctor` → `cycle_freshness: FAIL — never completed a full cycle`; `last_full_cycle_at` stays NULL on every source indefinitely.

## Why it matters

- "Full cycle" is defined against the universe of all possible phases, not the phases the active pack/config actually enables — so the check can never pass on a legitimately configured install.
- A permanent FAIL erodes doctor's signal. On our install it also masked a real problem for weeks (stale federated clone sources) because doctor showing red was "normal".

## Suggested fix

Define completeness relative to the enabled phase set (pack-declared ∩ config-enabled): if every enabled phase completed, stamp `last_full_cycle_at` — including on the `--dir` in-process path. Optionally emit an INFO listing permanently-disabled phases instead of failing.
title: doctor cycle_freshness FAILs permanently when the pack omits optional phases / dream runs via --dir

body:
## Summary

`gbrain onboard --check` reports chunks with NULL embeddings as `[critical] N chunks invisible to vector search` and recommends an embed run — but the count includes chunks belonging to pages stamped `embed_skip: true`, whose embeddings are **intentionally** NULL (the documented privacy mechanism). On an install using `embed_skip` for sensitive subtrees, the check cries critical forever, and the number never goes down.

## Repro (v0.42.53.0)

1. Stamp `embed_skip: true` on some pages; full-reimport so the guard applies (chunks stored, embeddings NULL).
2. `gbrain onboard --check` → `[critical] (auto) embed — N chunks invisible to vector search`, where N includes all the embed_skip chunks.
3. Verify via SQL: join `content_chunks` (embedding IS NULL) to `pages` — the rows are on `embed_skip` pages.

## Why it matters

- A permanent `[critical]` finding trains users to ignore onboard output — and it masks *real* embed backlogs (e.g. post-upgrade re-chunk backlogs) behind a number that never reaches zero.
- The remediation is marked `(auto)` — an auto-apply path would attempt to embed content the user explicitly opted out of embedding (egress to the embedding provider).

## Suggested fix

Exclude chunks whose page frontmatter has `embed_skip` from the invisible-chunk count (same key-existence test the embed path uses), or report them as a separate informational line ("N chunks embed-skipped by policy"). The auto-remediation must never embed `embed_skip` pages.
title: onboard 'chunks invisible to vector search' counts intentional embed_skip NULLs as critical backlog

body:
## Summary

`checkPackUpgradeAvailable` (onboard) resolves the active schema pack with `cfg: null`, which makes a file-plane `schema_pack` set in `~/.gbrain/config.json` invisible to the check. With no DB-side `schema_pack` config row, resolution falls through to the default (`gbrain-base`), so the check recommends the `unify-types` migration to installs that are **already on a custom or v2-based pack**. The same blind spot makes `gbrain doctor`'s pack checks report the wrong active pack identity.

## Repro (v0.42.53.0)

1. Activate a custom pack the file-plane way: `gbrain schema use my-pack` → `~/.gbrain/config.json` gets `"schema_pack": "my-pack"`; the DB `config` table has no `schema_pack` row.
2. `gbrain schema active` → correctly reports `my-pack` (it reads the file config).
3. `gbrain onboard --check` → emits `unify-types — Pack upgrade gbrain-base → gbrain-base-v2`, even though the install is not on `gbrain-base`.

## Root cause

`src/core/onboard/checks.ts` (`checkPackUpgradeAvailable`):

```ts
const active = await loadActivePack({ cfg: null, remote: false, dbConfig })
```

The comment says resolution "falls through to file-plane/env/default resolution when unset" — but it can't: `buildResolutionInput` (`src/core/schema-pack/load-active.ts:281-295`) reads the file-plane tier only from `input.cfg?.schema_pack`, so `cfg: null` guarantees `homeConfig` is undefined. With `dbConfig` also unset, resolution lands on the default `gbrain-base`, and `findPackSuccessors('gbrain-base', …)` finds `gbrain-base-v2` via its `migration_from`.

## Why it matters

- The emitted remediation is the `unify-types` Minion job. If a user on a custom fork follows it with `target_pack: gbrain-base-v2`, the run applies v2's `mapping_rules` **including the `*unknown*` catch-all**, flattening every custom page type to `note`, and then flips the active pack to `gbrain-base-v2` — silently deactivating their fork.
- Doctor's `pack_upgrade_available` / `type_proliferation` checks show the same wrong pack identity, which erodes trust in doctor output (`gbrain schema active` and doctor disagree permanently).

## Suggested fix

Load the file-plane config and pass it through (e.g. `loadActivePack({ cfg: loadConfigFileOnly(), … })`), or extend `buildResolutionInput` to fall back to `loadConfigFileOnly()` when `cfg` is null. A regression test: file-plane-activated pack + no DB config row → `pack_upgrade_available` reports "current".
title: onboard pack_upgrade_available resolves active pack with cfg:null — file-plane schema_pack invisible, false unify-types recommendation

body:
## Symptoms

Upgrading a pre-v121 brain (schema `config.version < 121`) to v0.42.x wedges with `column "event_page_id" does not exist` before any migration runs. Same class as #695, #755, #682, #741, #1115 — `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` is missing a probe for v121's `timeline_entries.event_page_id`, so `SCHEMA_SQL` / `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL` replay aborts on the index that forward-references the column.

Actual failure output from `gbrain upgrade` (0.32.0 → 0.42.56.0, bun-link install, Supabase Postgres backend):

```
⚠️  Schema version 50 is behind latest 122.
   Schema migrations run automatically on next connectEngine() / initSchema().
   To run them now: gbrain init --migrate-only

=== Applying migration v0.11.0: GBrain Minions — durable background agents ===
  Pre-v0.21 brain detected, applying forward-reference bootstrap
Phase A (schema) failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist. Aborting; re-run after fixing.
Migration v0.11.0 reported status=failed.
```

Once aborted, `runMigrations()` never gets to v121 (which adds `event_page_id` via `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS`), so every subsequent `gbrain` invocation hits the same failure. `gbrain doctor` post-upgrade drops to `0/100` and exits 1 with the same `column "event_page_id" does not exist` error.

## Affected versions

Any brain with `schema_version < 121` upgrading to a binary at v0.42.x+ (v121 shipped in the Life Chronicle feature, #2390, v0.42.56.0). Both engines are affected — the same forward-referencing index appears in both `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL` and `SCHEMA_SQL`.

In my case: 2026-05-11-vintage Supabase brain, 326 pages, `config.version=50`, upgraded from gbrain 0.32.0 → 0.42.56.0 via `git pull --ff-only && bun install` (bun-link source clone install).

## Root cause

At `origin/master` @ 6461790:

1. **Migration v121** (`src/core/migrate.ts:5443`, `timeline_entries_event_page_id`) is idempotent and guarded — it correctly adds the column via `ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER`, plus the FK and two indexes. That part is fine.

2. **`SCHEMA_SQL`** (`src/core/schema-embedded.ts:561-562`) and **`PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL`** (`src/core/pglite-schema.ts:387-388`) also declare the two forward-referencing indexes on `timeline_entries(event_page_id)`. On brains where the `timeline_entries` table already exists (which is every brain with `schema_version >= 12` or so), the `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS timeline_entries (…)` is a no-op — the new `event_page_id INTEGER REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE` column is NOT minted — and then `CREATE INDEX … ON timeline_entries(event_page_id)` aborts.

3. **`applyForwardReferenceBootstrap`** in `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:389` and `src/core/pglite-engine.ts:418` runs before schema replay for exactly this scenario. But its probe list (`postgres-engine.ts:~580-607`) does not include `timeline_entries.event_page_id`, so on a pre-v121 brain, all its `needs*` flags are false, the short-circuit at line 607 returns, and the schema replay crashes.

Verified against my Supabase brain:

```sql
SELECT column_name FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='timeline_entries';
-- id, page_id, date, source, summary, detail, created_at   (7 rows, no event_page_id)

SELECT key, value FROM config WHERE key='version';
-- version=50
```

## Repro

```bash
# On any brain with config.version < 121 (Postgres or PGLite):
gbrain init --migrate-only
# → column "event_page_id" does not exist
gbrain apply-migrations --yes
# → same failure looped through v0.11.0 orchestrator's Phase A
```

## Suggested fix

Mirror the pattern established by #695, #755, #682, #741. Three edits per engine (Postgres and PGLite), following the existing bootstrap conventions:

1. **Probe.** Extend the round-trip EXISTS check with `timeline_entries_event_page_id_exists` in `probeCr`.

2. **Bootstrap injection.** Add a `needsTimelineEventPageId` branch to the `if (…)` chain and to the short-circuit guard, injecting:
   ```sql
   ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER
     REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
   ```
   Only the column itself is strictly required by the SCHEMA_SQL indexes on lines 561-562. Migration v121 remains idempotent and completes the FK constraint + indexes properly in its own phase.

3. **Pin in CI.** Extend `REQUIRED_BOOTSTRAP_COVERAGE` in `test/schema-bootstrap-coverage.test.ts`:
   ```ts
   // v0.42.x (v121) — forward-referenced by
   //   CREATE INDEX idx_timeline_event_page ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) …
   //   CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_timeline_event_dedup ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) …
   { kind: 'column', table: 'timeline_entries', column: 'event_page_id' },
   ```
   Strip-block extended accordingly so the test exercises the new branch.

## Downstream note (separate issue, mentioning for context)

Immediately after the bootstrap gap surfaces, `gbrain doctor` also flags a `facts.embedding` dimension mismatch — the column is `halfvec(1536)` on a pre-v0.37 brain but the v0.42.x default embedding model is `zeroentropyai:zembed-1` (1280 dims). Doctor's remediation SQL is helpful but requires a maintenance window (nulls all fact embeddings + rebuilds the HNSW index). Flagging in case the two problems co-occur enough to warrant a combined upgrade note in `docs/embedding-migrations.md`.

## Workaround for affected operators

Back up the brain (Supabase: pg_dump; PGLite: `cp -r ~/.gbrain/brain.pglite ~/.gbrain/brain.pglite.bak-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)`), then pre-create the column manually:

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER
  REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
```

Then re-run `gbrain apply-migrations --yes`. Migration v121's `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` is a no-op, its FK add and index create idempotently succeed, and the chain proceeds.

Note that this workaround only unblocks this specific column; anything else v121-v122 adds that's forward-referenced will need the same treatment until the bootstrap gets extended.
title: Forward-reference bootstrap missing `timeline_entries.event_page_id` — pre-v121 Postgres/PGLite brains wedge on upgrade to v0.42.x

body:
**Version:** 0.42.56.0 · **Engine:** postgres (Postgres 16) · **OS:** Windows host

Two related defects hit while upgrading a long-lived Postgres brain (created around the v0.3x era) to 0.42.56.0. Together they made recovery needlessly hard: the first wedges migrations, the second hides why.

## 1. Schema bootstrap deadlock: `initSchema` references `timeline_entries.event_page_id` before migration 121 can add it

On a brain whose `timeline_entries` predates Life Chronicle, connect-time `initSchema` fails with:

```
column "event_page_id" does not exist
```

Mechanism: `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS timeline_entries (...)` no-ops on the legacy table, then `schema.sql`'s partial indexes (`idx_timeline_event_page`, `idx_timeline_event_dedup`) reference `event_page_id` and fail — before migration 121 (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`), whose first statement would add the column, ever runs. `gbrain init --migrate-only` and `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` both die on the same error, so there is no in-band recovery path.

Workaround that unblocked us (migration 121's own first statement, run manually):

```sql
ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER;
```

After that, `init --migrate-only` applied 116→122 cleanly.

Suggested fix: per your own KEY_FILES rule ("forward-referenced columns/indexes go in the bootstrap probe set"), `event_page_id` and its two partial indexes look like they should be in the probe set / guarded so initSchema tolerates the pre-121 shape. `test/schema-bootstrap-coverage.test.ts` presumably should have caught this for the postgres engine.

## 2. Wedged orchestrator migrations point at diagnostics that are never written

A separate orchestrator migration (v0.32.2 in our case) failed repeatedly and wedged. The runner prints only:

```
Migration v0.32.2 reported status=failed.
...
0.32.2 is WEDGED (3+ consecutive partials with no completion). Check ~/.gbrain/upgrade-errors.jsonl for the last failure reasons
```

But `upgrade-errors.jsonl` does not exist — neither under the brain home's `.gbrain/` nor under the user profile — and the per-phase `detail` strings the orchestrator returns (which contain the actual failure reason) are never printed, even with `--json`. So the operator is told to consult a file that was never created, and the phase-level diagnosis is unreachable.

Suggested fix: either write the promised `upgrade-errors.jsonl`, or (simpler) have the runner print `result.phases` (name/status/detail) on failure. Both would turn a dead-end into a one-look diagnosis.

Happy to provide more environment details if useful.
title: apply-migrations: bootstrap deadlock on legacy Postgres brains (timeline_entries.event_page_id referenced before migration 121) + wedged-migration diagnostics never written

body:
## Context

[REAL: A Reasoning-Enhanced Graph Framework for Long-Term Memory Management of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10694) (arXiv:2606.10694, Peking University + Kuaishou, June 2026) proposes storing long-term memory as a temporal, confidence-aware directed property graph of atomic facts, with non-destructive interval-based updates and evaluator-guided graph retrieval. Claims +22.72% avg over Mem0/Mem0-g/MemGPT/A-MEM across LoCoMo, LongMemEval, PersonaMem, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue.

Read against the current codebase (v0.43-era), gbrain is already ahead of the paper's baselines in places — the `facts` table (migration v40) has `valid_from`/`valid_until`/`superseded_by`, per-kind confidence with half-life decay (`src/core/facts/decay.ts`), and relational recall (v0.43) already does alias-aware entity anchoring + typed-edge fanout as a 4th RRF arm. REAL has no confidence decay at all.

The gaps are on the `links` graph and retrieval behavior: links carry no temporal interval or confidence; traversal is flat score-free BFS; retrieval is passive when results are weak.

## Proposed work, ranked

### 1. Surface fact-evolution intervals in answer context (S)
REAL serializes intervals + confidence into the evidence the generator sees, enabling "used to X, now Y" reasoning. gbrain already stores all the raw material (`valid_from`/`valid_until`/`superseded_by`, timeline_entries) but retrieval mostly serves current state. Include the validity interval and superseded-chain inline when facts/timeline rows are blended into search results. Formatting change only — no schema work. **Do this first.**

### 2. Confidence + intent filtering inside `relationalFanout` (S–M)
REAL's cascading neighbor filters (temporal / confidence / intent-compatibility) are cheap SQL predicates. Extend `RelationalFanoutOpts` with `minConfidence` and an intent→link_type compatibility map so e.g. causal queries prefer causal-ish typed edges over `mentions` noise. `relational-intent.ts` already classifies the query side. Low-confidence edges excluded from normal traversal unless the query contains uncertainty markers (one more regex class in `query-intent.ts`).

### 3. Temporal validity + confidence on `links` edges, close-don't-delete (M)
REAL's biggest dialogue-benchmark ablation for storage (−3.03) is valid-time intervals. Add `valid_from`/`valid_until`/`confidence` to `links` (schema.sql, migration), populate from link extraction (extractor already sees `effective_date` context), and implement cardinality-aware interval-closing for single-valued relation types (e.g. `current_role_at`, `lives_in`): a conflicting new edge closes the old edge's interval rather than deleting it. Then `relationalFanout` can filter by reference time — "who was X working with in 2023" traverses only edges valid then, and stale relationship edges stop ranking equal to current ones.

### 4. Evaluator-scored stop-or-expand traversal for the relational arm (M–L)
REAL's biggest overall ablation (−4.44) is the semantic path evaluator. `relationalFanout` currently returns everything within depth-2 ordered by `hop ASC, edge_count DESC` — no notion of whether a path answers the query. Port: after fanout, score candidate paths with one batched cheap-model call (relevance + sufficiency), use scores to rank the RRF arm and decide whether to expand depth 2→3. Gate behind the relational-intent classifier so the LLM call only fires on relational queries; fail-open; reuse query cache.

### 5. Counterfactual repair as a below-floor fallback (M)
REAL ablation −2.22. When fused results all land below `computeFloorThreshold`, run one budgeted repair pass: inverse/symmetric relation traversal, hyponymy via tags, co-occurrence via `mentions` edges (the principled place to opt them in). Budgeted, fail-open, audit-logged like `graph-signals.ts`. Converts "no results" into "here's what implicitly connects."

## Explicitly not doing

- **Stored per-fact intent enums** (FACT/CAUSAL/TEMPORAL/…): weakest ablation vs cost; query-side intent + typed link vocabulary covers it. Steal the filtering idea (#2), skip the storage taxonomy.
- **LLM-in-the-loop beam search at depth 3–5:** wrong latency budget for interactive search. Beam-lite (#4) captures most of the value.
- **Triple-store replatform:** the page-centric compiled_truth + chunks + facts-sidecar design is deliberate; enrich the sidecars, don't replace them.

## Files touched (estimate)

`src/schema.sql`, `src/core/migrate.ts`, `src/core/link-extraction.ts`, `src/core/postgres-engine.ts` (`relationalFanout`), `src/core/search/relational-recall.ts`, `src/core/search/relational-intent.ts`, `src/core/search/query-intent.ts`, `src/core/types.ts`, facts-recall evidence serialization.

Suggested order: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 (quick win, then filtering, then schema, then the LLM-scored pieces).
title: Retrieval + links-graph upgrades from REAL (arXiv:2606.10694): temporal edges, confidence-filtered fanout, path scoring, counterfactual repair

body:
### Summary

`gbrain auth permissions <token> set-takes-holders ...` has no effect when the brain is served via `gbrain serve --http`. Remote MCP callers authenticated with a legacy bearer token always get the fail-closed default allow-list `['world']`, regardless of what `access_tokens.permissions.takes_holders` says. Since takes written by the owner/agent typically carry `holder=brain` or `holder=people/<slug>`, every takes surface over HTTP MCP (`takes_list`, `takes_search`, `takes_scorecard`, `takes_calibration`) returns empty/zero for them, while the same ops via local CLI (`gbrain call takes_list ...`) return the rows.

Version: 0.42.52.0, PGLite engine, macOS (arm64), Bun 1.3.11.

### Repro

```bash
gbrain auth create my-token                      # default takes_holders=["world"]
gbrain auth permissions my-token set-takes-holders world,brain
gbrain takes add notes/decision-ledger --claim "test claim" --kind take --who brain --weight 0.75
gbrain serve --http --port 3131 &

curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3131/mcp \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"takes_list","arguments":{}}}'
# → "[]"   (expected: the holder=brain row)

gbrain call takes_list '{}'   # local CLI, serve stopped
# → returns the row
```

I verified the permissions row itself is stored correctly (proper jsonb object, `{"takes_holders":["world","brain"]}`), and that `listTakes` returns the rows when handed that allow-list directly — the drop happens in auth threading.

### Root cause

`GBrainOAuthProvider.verifyAccessToken` (`src/core/oauth-provider.ts`), legacy `access_tokens` branch: it parses `permissions` and reads `permissions.source_id` for the source grant, but never reads `permissions.takes_holders`, and the returned AuthInfo carries no `takesHoldersAllowList` field.

The consumer in `src/commands/serve-http.ts` (POST /mcp handler) then does:

```ts
const tokenAllowList = (authInfo as AuthInfo & { takesHoldersAllowList?: string[] }).takesHoldersAllowList
  ?? ['world'];
```

so the fallback `['world']` always wins for legacy bearer tokens. (The OAuth-client branch of `verifyAccessToken` doesn't thread it either.)

Note the older transport (`src/mcp/http-transport.ts`) does this correctly — its `verifyAccessToken` returns `takesHoldersAllowList` read from `permissions.takes_holders` and threads it into `dispatchToolCall`.
