1. Set up a brain with correctly path-prefixed pages via `gbrain import /path/to/brain-root`. Pages get slugs like `people/brian-swem`, `projects/my-project`.
2. Accidentally run `gbrain import /path/to/brain-root/brain` (one level too deep; the `brain/` prefix is dropped from all slugs).
3. Observe: the database now contains both `people/brian-swem` and `brian-swem` with identical content. Same for all other page types.
4. Run `dream --phase purge`. Bare-slug duplicates are **not** removed.
5. Run `gbrain doctor`. No warning is surfaced.

## Expected behavior

`gbrain doctor` detects and reports content-hash duplicate pairs where one slug is bare and the other is path-prefixed:

```
⚠  2 content-hash duplicate pairs detected (same content, differing slug forms):
   brian-swem  ←→  people/brian-swem
   my-project  ←→  projects/my-project
   To resolve: gbrain pages delete <bare-slug> for each, then gbrain pages purge-deleted --older-than 0
```

## Actual behavior

`gbrain doctor` completes without any duplicate warning. Bare-slug pages persist indefinitely, producing:

1. Two results for the same entity in semantic queries
2. Inflated page counts in doctor health metrics
3. Wikilink ambiguity when both slug forms exist in the database

## Proposed fix / approach

A SQL health check in `gbrain doctor` that groups pages by `content_hash` and flags groups where the same hash appears under both a bare slug (no `/` separator) and a path-prefixed slug (contains `/`). Report as a structured warning with the resolution command.

Optionally, a `gbrain dedup --dry-run` / `--apply` subcommand for operational cleanup would pair well with this, but the doctor check alone addresses the visibility gap.

## Context

1. gbrain version: 0.42.36.0
2. Node: v24.14.1, Linux / WSL2
3. The wrong-root import mistake is easy to make when the `brain/` subdirectory and its parent have similar names in a shell prompt. The correct import command targets the parent of `brain/`.
4. `dream --phase purge` scope is intentionally limited to file-backed orphans (CHANGELOG v0.42.35.0). This is not a request to expand purge, just a request to make `gbrain doctor` visible about the duplicate condition.
5. Manual resolution once duplicates exist: `gbrain pages delete <bare-slug>` for each, then `gbrain pages purge-deleted --older-than 0`.
title: Feature: gbrain doctor should detect content-hash duplicate pages (bare-slug vs path-prefixed from wrong import root)

body:
## Summary

The conversation-parser **LLM fallback** is implemented and unit-tested but **never wired into the parse path** — yet `gbrain doctor` actively tells users to enable it via a config key that nothing reads. The advice is a no-op.

Verified on `master` @ **v0.42.47.0**.

## Evidence

- `src/core/conversation-parser/llm-fallback.ts` exports `runLlmFallback(...)`, but it has **zero production callers** — the only references in the repo are its definition and `test/conversation-parser/llm-fallback.test.ts`:
  ```
  $ git grep -n runLlmFallback -- 'src/**'
  src/core/conversation-parser/llm-fallback.ts:68:export async function runLlmFallback(
  ```
  No module imports `llm-fallback` except the test.
- `src/core/conversation-parser/parse.ts:430` confirms it: `// LLM fallback (T4; not yet wired).` The `noFallback` option in `parseConversation` gates the **regex** full-body re-score fallback, not an LLM path.
- `src/commands/doctor.ts:4768` (the `conversation_format_coverage` check) recommends:
  > Enable LLM fallback (opt-in): `gbrain config set conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled true`
- But `conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled` is **read by nothing** — the only references are doc comments (`llm-fallback.ts:6`, `llm-base.ts:11`). `gbrain config set` rejects it as unknown and, with `--force`, warns: *"Nothing in gbrain reads this."*

## Impact

A user whose `conversation_format_coverage` is flagged follows the doctor's advice, sets the flag, and nothing changes — there's no wired code path that consumes it. (Separately, the metric measures regex coverage with `noFallback:true`, so an LLM fallback wouldn't move it even if wired — but that's a second-order point.)

## Repro

1. `gbrain doctor` on a brain with >10% unmatched conversation pages → warns, recommends the flag.
2. `gbrain config set conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled true` → `[config] ... Unknown config key`; with `--force` → `WARN: ... Nothing in gbrain reads this.`
3. `git grep -n runLlmFallback -- 'src/**'` → only the definition.

## Suggested fix (one of)

- **Wire it:** call `runLlmFallback` from `parseConversation`'s no-match path, gated by `conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled`, and register the config key in the schema.
- **Or stop recommending it:** until T4 lands, drop the "enable LLM fallback" hint from the `conversation_format_coverage` doctor message so it doesn't point users at a no-op flag.
title: conversation-parser LLM fallback is unwired: `runLlmFallback` has no callers, but `doctor` recommends enabling a no-op `conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled` flag

body:
## Summary

On a high-core-count host (32 cores), a large `gbrain sync` saturates **one** core while the other 31 sit idle. Two separate ceilings cause this, and neither scales with the box:

1. **The autopilot-driven sync path runs fully sequential** (no worker pool at all).
2. **`defaultWorkers()` hard-caps parallelism at 8** via `byPool`, independent of core count.

## Evidence

- Host: 32 cores, ~126GB RAM.
- During a ~6,700-file backlog sync, the worker process sat in `R` state pegged at ~88–106% (a single core); all V8 HeapHelper threads idle.
- Confirmed CPU-bound (not DB-bound): the time is spent in `importFile()` markdown chunking, not waiting on the Postgres pooler.

## Root cause 1 — sequential rename/import loop (autopilot path)

`src/commands/sync.ts` processes renames + imports in a plain sequential `for ... await` loop (`await updateSlug` then `await importFile` per file). When a sync is enqueued via the autopilot cycle (rather than an explicit `gbrain sync --workers N` invocation), it never engages the parallel import worker pool — so it defaults to one file at a time on one thread. A multi-thousand-file commit then takes hours on a box that could do it in minutes.

## Root cause 2 — `defaultWorkers()` capped at 8 regardless of cores

`src/commands/import.ts`:

```ts
function defaultWorkers(): number {
  const cpuCount = cpus().length;
  const memGB = totalmem() / (1024 ** 3);
  const byPool = 8;                       // <-- hard ceiling
  const byCpu = Math.max(2, cpuCount);
  const byMem = Math.floor(memGB * 2);
  return Math.min(byPool, byCpu, byMem);
}
```

`byPool = 8` means a 32-core / 128GB host gets the same default (8) as an 8-core laptop. The DB-pool justification is real, but it should be derived from the configured pool size, not a magic 8, and it shouldn't silently cap the CPU-bound chunking phase that doesn't even need a DB connection per worker.

## Requested changes

1. **Make the autopilot/queue-driven sync engage the parallel import pool** with a core-aware default, instead of falling back to the sequential loop. Large syncs from the daemon should parallelize the same as an explicit `--workers N`.
2. **Lift the `byPool = 8` ceiling to be configuration-derived.** Suggest: `byPool = max(8, dbPoolSize - reserved)` where `dbPoolSize` comes from the actual connection-pool config, so operators with a larger pool get more workers. CPU-bound chunking workers that don't hold a DB connection per file shouldn't be gated by the pool at all.
3. **Optionally** expose `sync.default_workers` (or honor an env override) so a high-core host can opt into `min(cpuCount, configuredCap)` without passing `--workers` on every invocation.

## Workaround in use

`gbrain sync --source <id> --workers 12 --no-embed` engages the pool and uses 12 cores — turns a multi-hour backlog drain into ~10–15 min. But this requires a manual invocation; the daemon-scheduled path still crawls on one core.

## Environment

- gbrain 0.42.45.0
- 32 cores, ~126GB RAM
- Remote Postgres via transaction-mode pgbouncer (port 6543)
title: sync: parallelize core-aware — single-core saturation on high-core hosts (sequential autopilot path + defaultWorkers byPool=8 cap)

body:
## Summary

On a multi-source Postgres brain (v0.42.37.0), the **`conversation_facts_backfill`** cycle phase throws `No database connection: connect() has not been called` for certain sources during the autopilot cycle. After repeated worker crashes the autopilot hits `max_crashes` and **exits with status 0**, so a systemd `Restart=on-failure` unit does **not** restart it — the daemon stays silently dead. In our case it went unnoticed for ~5 days (no syncs, no cycles, embed backlog re-accumulated).

Two distinct issues, the second is the more impactful:

### 1. `connect() has not been called` in `conversation_facts_backfill`

Same error class as the dream-cycle `connect()` bug fixed in **v0.42.21.0** (#1404/#1471/#1619), but it recurs in this phase's path. The phase is opt-in (`cycle.conversation_facts_backfill.enabled`, default false); when enabled it crashes the worker for some sources.

- Phase: `runPhaseConversationFactsBackfill` → `src/core/cycle/conversation-facts-backfill.ts` → `runExtractConversationFactsCore(engine, …)` (~line 214). The `engine` reaching the extract/write path appears not to be connected in the worker re-entry context — the same shape as the `--dir`-scoped extract phases the v0.42.21.0 module-singleton-ownership fix addressed.

### 2. Autopilot clean-exits after `max_crashes`, defeating `Restart=on-failure`

When a phase crash-loops to `max_crashes`, the autopilot daemon gives up and **exits 0**. systemd treats exit 0 as success, so `Restart=on-failure` never fires and the daemon stays down indefinitely with no signal. 

**Suggestion:** exit **non-zero** on the give-up path so `Restart=on-failure` recovers it (and/or document that operators should use `Restart=always`). A daemon that has decided it can't make progress should signal failure, not success.

## Environment

- gbrain 0.42.37.0, Postgres (Supabase pooler: transaction port 6543 for the read pool + session pool for the direct/DDL pool)
- Multi-source brain, `gbrain autopilot` under systemd `--user`

## Workarounds in place

- `Restart=always` on the autopilot unit (recovers regardless of exit code)
- Disabled the opt-in `conversation_facts_backfill` phase (returns to default)

Happy to provide more detail / test a fix.
title: autopilot: conversation_facts_backfill 'connect() has not been called' crash-loops, then clean-exits past Restart=on-failure

body:
## Summary

`createVersion failed: page "<slug>" (source=<source>) not found` still fires on **v0.42.37.0**, after #1434's fix in #1456 (v0.41.13.0). #1456 only solved the single-vault `gbrain sync` symptom via source auto-routing; it did not touch (a) **multi-source brains**, (b) the `gbrain import` path, or (c) the underlying logic mismatch between the existence check and the version write. This is the same error string and same `(source_id, slug)` root cause as #1434, but a strictly larger scope, so filing fresh rather than reopening a thread #1456 marked "Closes."

## Environment

- gbrain `0.42.37.0`
- Engine: Postgres (Neon), pooler `session` mode
- Multi-source brain: 4 sources — `default` (55 pages), `gstack-artifacts-nicholasblasgen` (201), `gstack-code-364e0db3-c3ae05` (438), `gstack-code-7b906e97-4d07b8` (184)
- Trigger: gstack `gstack-memory-ingest` → `gbrain import <stagingDir> --no-embed --json` (no `--source`)

## Reproduction

1. A slug exists under a **non-default** source — e.g. two transcripts ingested under `gstack-artifacts-nicholasblasgen` during a historical bulk import (confirmed via `psql`: rows present in `pages`, `deleted_at IS NULL`, 128/97 chunks each).
2. A later flag-less `gbrain import` of the same slug runs against a brain with 2+ non-default sources, so it resolves to `'default'` / unscoped.
3. Result: `imported:0, skipped:N, errors:2` and per-file `Warning: skipped <path>: createVersion failed: page "<slug>" (source=default) not found`.

`gbrain delete "<slug>"` from a pinned cwd reports `page_not_found` (it scopes to the pinned source, not the source that actually holds the row), and `get_page`/`get_versions`/`restore_page` all return not-found for `(slug, default)` — the page genuinely isn't in `default`, it's in `gstack-artifacts-nicholasblasgen`.

## Root cause (code-level)

The two "no `sourceId`" fallbacks disagree:

- **Existence check is global.** `import-file.ts:561`:
  ```ts
  const existing = await engine.getPage(slug, sourceId ? { sourceId } : undefined);
  ```
  In `postgres-engine.ts` `getPage`, when both `sourceId` and `sourceIds` are undefined the source filter is an **empty fragment**, so the query is `WHERE slug = $1 AND deleted_at IS NULL LIMIT 1` — it matches the row under *any* source (here, the `gstack-artifacts-nicholasblasgen` copy) → `existing` is truthy.
- **Version write defaults to literal `'default'`.** `import-file.ts:733` then does `if (existing) await tx.createVersion(slug, txOpts)`, and `postgres-engine.ts:4538` (`pglite-engine.ts:4466`) resolves `const sourceId = opts?.sourceId ?? 'default'` → `WHERE slug = $1 AND source_id = 'default'` → 0 rows → throws at `postgres-engine.ts:4547` (`pglite-engine.ts:4475`).

So an unscoped import of a slug owned by another source sees `existing = true` (global) but tries to version it under `default` (literal) and throws — instead of **inserting** a new `(default, slug)` row, which `pages_source_slug_key UNIQUE (source_id, slug)` explicitly permits.

## Why #1456 doesn't cover this

#1456 added tier 5.5 (`source-resolver.ts:130-142`, `pickSoleNonDefaultSource`), which only auto-routes when **exactly one** non-default source exists. Its own comment is explicit:

> `source-resolver.ts:136` — *"Conservative: fires only when there's literally one option — multi-source brains still require explicit --source or sources.default."*

With 2+ non-default sources, `pickSoleNonDefaultSource` returns `null`, resolution falls to the terminal `'default'` (`source-resolver.ts:146`), and the `getPage`/`createVersion` mismatch above is reached unchanged.

## Suggested fix

Make the existence check resolve the same default the write uses. At `import-file.ts:561`:

```ts
const existing = await engine.getPage(slug, { sourceId: sourceId ?? 'default' });
```

Then an unscoped import of a slug owned by another source resolves `existing = null` and **inserts** into `default` cleanly, instead of colliding. (Alternatively, make `getPage`'s no-source fallback match `createVersion`'s, or have `createVersion` throw a typed error the importer can catch and fall back to insert.) The same divergence exists on PGLite (`createVersion` at `pglite-engine.ts:4466`, throw at `:4475`).

## Secondary, orthogonal

`gbrain import` returns a **non-zero exit whenever `errors > 0`**, even with `status:"success"` and `imported:0`. A batch importer that can't gate per-file state (gstack `gstack-memory-ingest`) then books the **whole batch** as failed and never advances its state — so it re-stages and re-fails the full window every run (e.g. `failed: 54` when only 2 pages are actually stuck). Consider an exit code (or `--skip-failed`-style summary) that distinguishes "all writes succeeded, N pre-existing/cross-source skips" from "writes failed."

## Related

- #1434 — original report (single-vault `gbrain sync` symptom); still open despite #1456's "Closes."
- #1456 — the merged partial fix (v0.41.13.0).
- I left a code-level comment on #1434 earlier with the same `getPage`/`createVersion` trace: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/1434#issuecomment-4723923649
title: createVersion source-scoping bug persists after #1456 — multi-source brains + `import` path 404 on cross-source slugs (v0.42.37.0)

body:
## Summary

I am running a personal GBrain backfill/dream workflow and want to use the best available model/provider for each phase, not only the default provider path. Today it is possible to configure many model keys, but the operator experience is still confusing enough that I am not fully confident which provider/model path is supported for expensive one-time runs.

Concrete workflow:

- Large historical conversation facts backfill
- Nightly dream/synthesis pass
- Subagent/tool loop work
- Retrieval with ZeroEntropy embeddings/reranking
- Possible provider choices: direct Anthropic, OpenRouter, OpenAI-compatible gateways, or local/user-hosted endpoints

## What I recommend

Add an explicit provider-agnostic model configuration layer for all LLM-backed phases, with first-class support for custom base URLs and provider env vars.

Something like:

```json
{
  "models": {
    "facts_extraction": {
      "provider": "anthropic",
      "model": "claude-opus-4-7",
      "api_key_env": "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",
      "base_url": null,
      "max_output_tokens": 8192,
      "prompt_cache": true
    },
    "dream_synthesize": {
      "provider": "anthropic",
      "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "api_key_env": "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",
      "base_url": null,
      "prompt_cache": true
    },
    "subagent": {
      "provider": "anthropic",
      "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
      "api_key_env": "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",
      "base_url": null,
      "prompt_cache": true
    }
  }
}
```

or, if keeping the existing key style, document and validate the exact supported shape for:

- `facts.extraction_model`
- `models.dream.synthesize`
- `models.dream.patterns`
- `models.subagent`
- `models.chat`
- `models.tier.*`
- `provider_base_urls.*`
- provider-specific API key env vars

The important part is: make arbitrary providers/gateways unambiguous. If a model string is `openrouter:anthropic/claude-opus-4.1`, `litellm:anthropic/claude-opus-4.1`, or `anthropic:claude-opus-4-7`, GBrain should clearly tell the user:

- whether the provider is supported for that phase
- which env var is required
- whether pricing/budget tracking is known or estimated
- whether prompt caching is active
- whether the model id is valid for the provider
- what command would test a one-call health check before a full backfill

## Why this matters

The current backfill/dream path can cost real money and run for hours. The existing config system is powerful, but it is easy to end up with a high-level model setting that resolves to a provider path that is technically accepted but operationally suboptimal.

Example concerns:

- Direct Anthropic likely has better prompt-cache behavior for long memory/dream loops than a generic OpenRouter route.
- OpenRouter or OpenAI-compatible gateways are useful fallback paths, but nested model ids can confuse pricing, budget caps, and capability detection.
- Facts extraction quality matters enough that I may want Opus for one-time historical backfill and Sonnet for ongoing nightly work.
- A dry-run/doctor command should validate the actual phase model, provider credentials, pricing, and prompt-cache posture before the user starts a full run.

## Suggested operator UX

Add a command like:

```bash
gbrain models doctor --phase facts-backfill
gbrain models doctor --phase dream
gbrain models doctor --phase subagent
gbrain models test --phase facts-backfill --sample
gbrain config recommend --quality=max --budget=high
```

Expected output should include the resolved model, provider, key source, base URL, pricing source, prompt-cache status, and warnings like "OpenRouter route works, but Anthropic direct is recommended for cached long-context loops."

## My current intended max-quality setup

For reference, the setup I am trying to run is:

- Embeddings: `zeroentropyai:zembed-1`, 1280 dimensions
- Reranker: `zeroentropyai:zerank-2`
- Search mode: `tokenmax`
- Historical facts backfill: direct Anthropic Opus
- Nightly dream/synthesis: direct Anthropic Sonnet
- Subagent/tool loop: direct Anthropic Sonnet
- High backfill caps because this is a one-time historical ingest

## Question

What model/provider/settings do you recommend for best-quality personal-brain backfill and dream in production?

Specifically:

1. Best model for historical conversation facts extraction?
2. Best model for nightly dream/synthesis?
3. Best model for subagent/tool loops?
4. Should operators prefer direct Anthropic over OpenRouter for long-context cached workflows?
5. Are there recommended budget caps, worker counts, and walltime settings for a full historical backfill on Postgres?
6. Is ZeroEntropy `zembed-1` plus `zerank-2` still the recommended retrieval spine for this setup?
title: Clarify and harden provider-agnostic model config for facts backfill and dream

body:
# Bug: connector `put_page` silently collapses a page's timeline; `add_timeline_entry` writes to a store that isn't rendered

**Affected version:** 0.42.29.0 (postgres engine; logic is engine-agnostic)
**Severity:** silent data corruption — a "successful" write makes a page's timeline disappear from every timeline-rendering surface, with no error.

## Summary

Two independent defects combine so that an external MCP connector (any client that does
`get_page` → edit → `put_page`, or calls `add_timeline_entry`) reports a **successful, verifiable
write** while the change is **invisible** on the page as rendered from `pages.timeline`. Neither
throws; both return success. The client can re-read its own write (so it "confirms" it), but the
user looking at the rendered page sees nothing.

## Bug 1 — `put_page` collapses the timeline when the `<!-- timeline -->` sentinel is absent

### Root cause

- `get_page` (`src/core/operations.ts`, the `get_page` op) returns the page object with
  `compiled_truth` and `timeline` as **separate fields**. It does **not** return a single
  reassembled markdown body, and the `<!-- timeline -->` sentinel is **not** present in either field.
- `put_page` accepts a single `content` markdown blob and parses it with `parseMarkdown`
  (`src/core/markdown.ts:73`), which calls `splitBody` (`markdown.ts:106`).
- `splitBody` / `findTimelineSplitIndex` (`markdown.ts:307-345`) only recognize a timeline
  boundary at: (1) `<!-- timeline -->`, (2) `--- timeline ---`, or (3) a bare `---` rule
  **immediately followed by** `## Timeline`/`## History`. **A bare `## Timeline` heading is not a
  sentinel.** If no sentinel is found, the **entire body — including the timeline — becomes
  `compiled_truth`, and `timeline` is set to `''`.**

So the `get → edit → put` round-trip is **lossy**: the client is handed `compiled_truth` and
`timeline` separately, with no sentinel, and has no way to know it must re-insert an internal
`<!-- timeline -->` marker between them when it reassembles a `content` blob. Any connector that
reconstructs the page (LLM clients routinely regenerate the whole body) drops the sentinel →
`splitBody` finds nothing → `pages.timeline` goes to **0 bytes** and the timeline text is buried
in `compiled_truth`.

`serializeMarkdown` (`markdown.ts:351-374`) only re-emits `<!-- timeline -->` when `timeline` is
non-empty — so once collapsed, the round-trip can't self-recover either.

### Reproduction

1. Take any page that has a timeline, e.g. `people/alice-example`, whose stored form is
   `compiled_truth` + `<!-- timeline -->` + `## Timeline\n- 2026-01-01 — ...`.
2. `get_page people/alice-example` → returns `{ compiled_truth: "...", timeline: "## Timeline\n- ..." }`
   (two fields, **no sentinel**).
3. Reassemble naively and write it back (what an MCP connector does):
   `put_page { slug: "people/alice-example", content: compiled_truth + "\n\n" + timeline }`
   — i.e. the body now contains `## Timeline` but **no** `<!-- timeline -->`.
4. Observe: `pages.timeline` is now `''` (0 bytes); the timeline text is inside `compiled_truth`.
   Any UI or export that renders `pages.timeline` shows an empty timeline. No error was raised.

### Suggested fixes (any one closes it; 1+2 together is best)

1. **Make the round-trip lossless.** Have `get_page` also return a canonical `content` field
   produced by `serializeMarkdown(...)` (frontmatter + compiled_truth + `<!-- timeline -->` +
   timeline), and document that connectors should edit/return *that*. The sentinel then survives.
2. **Defensive normalization on import.** In `splitBody` (or in `put_page` before persisting), if
   the body contains a `## Timeline`/`## History` heading but no recognized sentinel, treat the
   heading as the split point (or re-insert the sentinel) instead of collapsing everything into
   `compiled_truth`. The docstring at `markdown.ts:298-302` already implies the heading is meant to
   be recognized; the code only honors it when preceded by `---`.
3. **Guardrail:** refuse/flag a `put_page` that would take a page's `timeline` from non-empty → empty
   while the incoming body still contains a `## Timeline` heading (very likely a dropped sentinel).

## Bug 2 — `add_timeline_entry` writes to `timeline_entries`, which isn't reflected in `pages.timeline`

### Root cause

- `add_timeline_entry` (`src/core/operations.ts`, the op) → `engine.addTimelineEntry`
  (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:3166-3190`, mirror in `pglite-engine.ts`) `INSERT`s into the
  **`timeline_entries` table** (`ON CONFLICT (page_id,date,summary,source) DO NOTHING`).
- `get_timeline` reads back from `timeline_entries` — so a connector that writes then calls
  `get_timeline` **sees its own row** and "confirms" the write.
- But the page body / `pages.timeline` column (what markdown export and timeline-rendering UIs
  display) is **not** updated from `timeline_entries`. The two stores are disconnected for any
  page whose timeline lives in the markdown body.

Net effect: `add_timeline_entry` is a **write-to-nowhere** for the rendered page. It returns
`{status:'ok'}` quickly, the row exists, `get_timeline` shows it, but the user never sees it on the
page. (Note: this is distinct from gbrain's own auto-timeline extraction, which also populates
`timeline_entries` — so the rows aren't "wrong", they're just not surfaced where body-timeline pages
are read.)

### Suggested fixes

1. Reconcile the stores: either render/merge `timeline_entries` rows when returning/serializing a
   page (so `get_page`/export reflect them), or have `add_timeline_entry` also splice the entry into
   the page body's timeline section (keeping markdown the source of truth).
2. At minimum, document clearly that `add_timeline_entry` targets the structured `timeline_entries`
   store and is **not** reflected in `pages.timeline` / markdown for body-timeline pages — today a
   connector reasonably assumes "timeline add" updates the visible timeline.

## Why this matters

LLM connectors (ChatGPT, claude.ai, etc.) are exactly the clients that (a) regenerate a whole page
body on edit and (b) trust a `get_timeline`/`get_page` re-read as verification. Both defects make
them confidently report success on a write the user can't see — the worst failure shape for a memory
system. A fix should land in **both** `postgres-engine.ts` and `pglite-engine.ts` (engine parity)
with a round-trip test: `get_page` → reassemble without sentinel → `put_page` → assert
`pages.timeline` is preserved.

## Notes for the maintainer

- The collapse is recoverable by re-inserting `<!-- timeline -->` before the `## Timeline` heading
  and re-importing — but that's a manual/cron repair, not prevention.
- Redacted MCP request logging (`mcp_request_log.params` = key-shape only) means these phantom-looking
  writes can't be diagnosed from logs alone; the page/table state is the only source of truth. A
  per-op "rows affected / timeline bytes delta" field in the log would make this class observable.
title: put_page silently collapses a page's timeline when the <!-- timeline --> sentinel is dropped; add_timeline_entry writes to an unrendered store

body:
## Issue

The Release workflow (`.github/workflows/release.yml`) runs `bun test` before `bun build` but skips the `verify` job (which includes `typecheck`, `check:privacy`, `check:admin-build`, and 24 other static checks). A TypeScript compile error, privacy leak, or broken admin build step can ship to users as a published binary.

## Evidence

**release.yml build step — no verify step:**
```yaml
- run: bun install
- run: bun test
- run: bun build --compile --target=${{ matrix.target }} --outfile bin/${{ matrix.artifact }} src/cli.ts
```

**test.yml has a separate "verify" job that gates merges but NOT releases:**
```yaml
# test.yml
verify:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - run: bun run verify   # runs scripts/run-verify-parallel.sh (27 checks)
```

**What verify covers that release is missing:**
- `typecheck` — TS errors caught before publish
- `check:privacy` — no real names / PII in agent voice
- `check:admin-build` — admin bundle must compile
- `check:jsonb` — no jsonb injection patterns
- `check:source-config-leak` — no API keys in source config
- `check:cli-exec` — CLI is executable after build
- `check:worker-pool-atomicity` — concurrent worker correctness
- `check:gateway-routed` — no direct Anthropic SDK calls
- 20 more checks in `scripts/run-verify-parallel.sh`

## Expected Impact

A TS error introduced between releases would block the release immediately instead of producing a broken binary that gets published. The same applies to any privacy or build-break regressions introduced in release-branch commits that never touched master.

## Suggested Fix

In `.github/workflows/release.yml`, add `bun run verify` between `bun test` and `bun build`:

```yaml
- run: bun install
- run: bun test
- run: bun run verify   # add this line
- run: bun build --compile --target=${{ matrix.target }} --outfile bin/${{ matrix.artifact }} src/cli.ts
```

## Commands Inspected

```bash
# Confirmed: release.yml has no verify step
grep -n "verify" .github/workflows/release.yml

# Confirmed: test.yml has a verify job
grep -n "verify:" .github/workflows/test.yml

# Confirmed: verify runs 27 checks via run-verify-parallel.sh
head -60 scripts/run-verify-parallel.sh
```

## Duplicate Check

- Searched 200 open issues for "verify in release", "typecheck in release", "release verify", "verify before build" — no matches.
- Related but distinct: #2142 (artifact attestations) and #2182 (OSV scanner), both already open.
title: release.yml: run verify before bun build

body:
## Suggestion

Improve the facts extractor as one cohesive quality pass, rather than treating this as separate changes for metrics vs. local models.

The main opportunity is to make extractor output more schema-consistent and robust across both frontier and local models, especially for:

1. Typed metric claims that are already emitted with `metric/value/unit/period` fields.
2. Multi-claim user turns that should split into multiple atomic facts.
3. Low-value/routine messages that should be skipped even when they contain numbers.
4. Local-model pipelines, where a strict second-pass judge/normalizer can help but should not lower frontier-model quality.

## Why this came up

I ran a no-insert benchmark against the current facts extractor behavior using 70 human-curated, Telegram/GBrain-style fixture messages. The fixtures were actual-style/anonymized test cases, not private conversation exports.

Environment under test:

```text
GBrain package version: 0.42.38.0
Repo commit tested:     03ffc6eb
Default facts model:    anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6
Local extractor model:  Gemma 26B via an OpenAI-compatible local endpoint
Local judge model:      atla/selene-mini via Ollama
```

The current extractor prompt already asks models to emit typed metric fields:

```text
metric, value, unit, period
```

But when models correctly emit those typed fields, they sometimes still classify achieved/current metric claims as `event` instead of `fact`.

## Benchmark summary

```text
Pipeline                            Score      Notes
----------------------------------  ---------  -----------------------------------------------
Sonnet raw                          59/70      Current default facts model behavior
Sonnet + metric normalizer          63/70      Metric positives fixed: 6/10 -> 10/10
Local Gemma raw                     56/70      Local extractor, no judge
Local Gemma + metric normalizer     59/70      Metric positives fixed: 7/10 -> 10/10
Gemma + Selene judge                59/70      Fixed commitments, but regressed one static fact
Gemma + Selene + metric normalizer  62/70      Best local pipeline in this run
```

The most reliable improvement was a narrow deterministic post-processing rule:

```text
If the extractor emits a typed metric claim for an achieved/current/historical metric,
normalize kind to `fact` unless the source/fact text is a question, goal, target,
forecast, hope, or other future/uncertain statement.
```

This improved both the frontier baseline and local-model results.

## Examples

### 1. Achieved/current metrics should normalize to `fact`

These are objective metric facts, not events, if the model already emitted typed metric fields:

```text
"The company reached $50K MRR in January 2026."
Expected: kind=fact, metric=mrr, value=50000, unit=USD

"Churn dropped to 5% in Q1."
Expected: kind=fact, metric=churn_rate, value=0.05, unit=pct

"The fundraise closed at $15M."
Expected: kind=fact, metric=fundraise, value=15000000, unit=USD

"The newsletter reached 100,000 subscribers in May."
Expected: kind=fact, metric=subscribers/users/newsletter_subscribers
```

### 2. Future/target metric statements should not become facts

Metric-looking text still needs modality checks:

```text
"Can we get to $50K MRR by January?"
Expected: skip, or at least not kind=fact

"We should target $2M ARR next year."
Expected: commitment, not fact

"Revenue might be $500K next quarter."
Expected: belief/forecast, not fact
```

One easy pitfall: do not treat bare case-insensitive `may` as future language, because it collides with the month name in examples such as:

```text
"The newsletter reached 100,000 subscribers in May."
```

### 3. Multi-claim turns still need better splitting

The benchmark still had multi-claim misses after metric normalization:

```text
"I think the local pipeline is close, but we should compare it to Sonnet before trusting it."
Expected: belief + commitment
Common failure: one collapsed belief

"Yesterday I moved the wiki hub, and the active path is now the local wiki directory."
Expected: event + fact
Common failure: one combined event

"I prefer concise answers; if a task needs tools, run them instead of explaining."
Expected: preference + commitment/policy
Common failure: preference only
```

### 4. Low-value numeric facts should still be skipped

Numerical text should not automatically become a metric/fact:

```text
"Dinner is at 6."
Expected: skip

"There were 12 people on the Zoom today."
Expected: skip

"The steak costs $50 at that restaurant."
Expected: skip
```

## Suggested implementation direction

I would suggest treating this as a single extractor-quality improvement:

1. Add a deterministic post-parse normalizer for typed metric claims.
   - Only operate when `claim_metric`/`metric` and a finite `value` are present.
   - Normalize achieved/current/historical metric assertions to `kind=fact`.
   - Block normalization for questions, goals, targets, forecasts, hypotheticals, hopes, and future plans.
   - Keep the blocker narrow enough not to misclassify month names such as `May`.

2. Strengthen the extractor prompt/tests around atomic splitting.
   - One fact per atomic claim is already in the prompt, but the benchmark shows models still collapse multi-claim turns.
   - Add tests for belief+commitment, event+fact, preference+policy, and metric+commitment turns.

3. Add tests for low-value numerical/routine skips.
   - Make sure routine logistics do not enter memory just because they contain a number or dollar amount.

4. Consider an optional source-aware second-pass judge/normalizer for local models.
   - In the benchmark, a local Selene judge improved local commitment classification.
   - But it also introduced at least one regression by changing a static infrastructure fact into an event.
   - So I would not suggest making a local judge default without more no-over-derivation tests.

## Acceptance criteria

A useful first version could include:

- Unit/eval fixtures covering achieved/current metrics vs. metric goals/questions/forecasts.
- Unit/eval fixtures covering multi-claim splitting.
- Unit/eval fixtures covering routine numeric skips.
- A deterministic metric normalizer that improves both default Sonnet and local model outputs without requiring provider-specific logic.
- No change that treats arbitrary numbers/currency as facts unless typed metric extraction has already happened.

## Why this helps

This should improve GBrain for both hosted/frontier and local-model users:

- Hosted models already emit useful typed metric fields, but sometimes choose the wrong high-level `kind`.
- Local models benefit even more from deterministic schema guards because they are more likely to be inconsistent on kind/modality.
- The change can be narrow and schema-aligned rather than a broad prompt-only tweak.
title: Improve facts extractor reliability for typed metrics, multi-claim turns, and local models

body:
## Symptom

`gbrain orphans` reports thousands of "orphan" pages on brains organized by anything other than gbrain's own internal conventions. The same overcount drives `orphan_ratio` in `gbrain doctor`'s brain-score, so the user's brain-score is dominated by reference material that is orphan-by-design.

Two distinct overcount sources contribute, each living at a different filter layer:

1. **Slug-prefix layer**: PARA brains have `resources/`, `archives/`, `inbox/`; Obsidian brains have `clips/`, `attachments/`; code corpora have `transcripts/` (gstack-memory-ingest landing dir); agent runtimes have `agents/<name>/state`. The shipped `DENY_PREFIXES` doesn't cover any of these.
2. **Page-type layer**: code-symbol extractions (`pages.type = 'code'`), system artifacts (`extract_receipt`), gstack-memory-ingest commit-summary pages (`feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `chore`). These have no inbound-link semantics regardless of slug.

## Concrete numbers

A 2K-page Obsidian vault organized PARA-style plus 11 federated code-stage source repos (gstack-memory-ingest) plus a `transcripts/` corpus:

```text
$ gbrain orphans --count
2906       # raw (shipped DENY_PREFIXES only)

# Adding PARA convention dirs to the prefix knob:
$ gbrain config set orphans.exclude_prefixes 'resources/,transcripts/,agents/,clips/'
$ gbrain orphans --count
1418       # dropped 1488 PARA convention pages

# Adding default page-type filter (post-fix):
$ gbrain orphans --count
928        # dropped 490 code + system + commit-summary pages
```

928 actual signal vs 2906 raw. The 928 remaining are dominated by `concept` (414), `project` (291), and `note` (204) types: pages the user actually wrote and might want to graph.

## Diagnosis

`src/commands/orphans.ts:47-56` carries the shipped exclusion lists:

```ts
const DENY_PREFIXES = ['output/', 'dashboards/', 'scripts/', 'templates/', 'openclaw/config/'];
const FIRST_SEGMENT_EXCLUSIONS = new Set(['scratch', 'thoughts', 'catalog', 'entities']);
```

These cover gbrain-internal convention dirs but miss the convention layer AND the page-type layer entirely. There's no mechanism for a user to extend the deny list or filter by page type short of patching source and rebuilding.

## Proposed fix

Two complementary per-brain config knobs, layered at the surface where the noise actually lives:

```text
# Slug-prefix layer (opt-in; additive to shipped DENY_PREFIXES):
gbrain config set orphans.exclude_prefixes 'resources/,transcripts/,agents/'

# Page-type layer (shipped defaults apply automatically; additive via config):
gbrain config set orphans.exclude_types 'guide,reference'
```

Shipped `DEFAULT_EXCLUDE_TYPES` covers types whose pages are orphan-by-construction on every brain shape:

- `code`: code-symbol extractions. Code files don't wikilink to code files.
- `extract_receipt`: system artifact from extract phases.
- `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `chore`: gstack-memory-ingest commit-summary types.

Same one-directional contract as `DENY_PREFIXES`: shipped entries always apply; user config extends but cannot remove.

The fix touches `src/commands/orphans.ts` (helpers + filter wiring), `src/core/engine.ts` + both engines (`findOrphanPages` gains optional `excludeTypes`), `src/core/config.ts` (registry), plus tests.

## Why this fixes more than just `gbrain orphans --count`

`getOrphansData` is the canonical "what counts as an orphan in this brain" pure-data function shared between `gbrain orphans` and doctor's `orphan_ratio` check (per the v0.42.0.0 D1 unification). Both knobs therefore correct the brain-score input. A brain federating code-stage sources stops being penalized for code-symbol extractions, and a PARA-organized brain stops being penalized for web clips, the moment the config lands.

## Behavior change to call out

The `DEFAULT_EXCLUDE_TYPES` list shifts orphan counts downward for every brain that has any of those six types. The orphan-ratio doctor check stops counting code-symbol and commit-summary pages. This is a one-time correction, not a regression. A code-symbol page never had inbound-link semantics; the count was just lying about it.

## Future surface (out of scope here, worth noting)

- **Frontmatter-level `orphan_expected: true` per-page opt-out.** Complementary to dir-level prefixes + type-level filters; useful for one-off pages where neither rule fits. Worth a follow-up.
- **Glob support** for `orphans.exclude_prefixes`. The existing semantics are `startsWith`; adding globs would need a wider rework. Prefix-only is sufficient for the patterns this PR targets.

Adjacent issue from the same operational-health theme: `orphan_ratio` recommends `extract links --by-mention` as the remediation, but backlink counting excludes mention-only links so the recommendation is structurally unactionable. Out of scope here; flagging for theme awareness.
title: gbrain orphans / orphan_ratio overcount on PARA / Obsidian / agent-organized brains — no user-extensible deny list

body:
## Summary

`gbrain dream --phase synthesize` can reverse-write generated pages into a nested `wiki/` directory when the active source id is already `wiki` and the source root is the wiki checkout.

In a source-aware setup, dream synthesis currently prompts/allows child jobs to write slugs shaped like:

```text
wiki/personal/reflections/...
wiki/originals/ideas/...
```

If source `wiki` maps to `/path/to/wiki`, reverse-rendering those slugs to disk produces:

```text
/path/to/wiki/wiki/personal/reflections/...
/path/to/wiki/wiki/originals/ideas/...
```

That conflates the **source id** (`wiki`) with the **slug/path inside the source** (`personal/reflections/...`).

## Why this seems wrong

The documented brains/sources model says:

- a source is the named repo inside a brain (`source_id = wiki`)
- slugs are scoped per source
- slug uniqueness is `(source_id, slug)`, not global slug strings

So for a wiki source rooted at `/path/to/wiki`, the source-aware representation should be one of:

```text
source_id = wiki
slug = personal/reflections/...
```

not:

```text
source_id = default or wiki
slug = wiki/personal/reflections/...
```

## Reproduction shape

1. Configure/add a source named `wiki` whose local path is the wiki repo root, e.g. `/Users/example/wiki`.
2. Run a non-dry dream synthesize pass that writes reflection/original pages.
3. Observe generated markdown under a nested `wiki/` directory inside the repo root.

Observed locally after a controlled smoke run:

```text
/Users/.../wiki/wiki/personal/reflections/...
/Users/.../wiki/wiki/originals/ideas/...
```

## Expected behavior

Dream synthesis should keep source id and in-source slug separate, so generated files land under:

```text
/path/to/wiki/personal/reflections/...
/path/to/wiki/originals/ideas/...
```

## Possible fix direction

Avoid a global string hack. The safe fix should be source-aware:

- either make synthesize prompts/allow-lists use in-source slugs while passing the resolved `sourceId` to subagent tools / reverse writes, or
- normalize only at the source boundary: when reverse-writing to a source root, treat a leading `<sourceId>/` prefix as a virtual prefix and strip it before joining to the source root, with regression tests.

Regression coverage should include:

- `source_id = wiki`, `slug = wiki/personal/reflections/foo` does not write `/wiki/wiki/...`
- existing/default single-source behavior still writes the same paths
- allow-list enforcement remains fail-closed for disallowed paths
- `(source_id, slug)` isolation is preserved
title: Dream synthesize can reverse-write nested wiki/ paths for source-aware wiki repos
Consolidate operational documentation entrypoints

body:
**What happened?**

The `litellm` recipe ships only an `embedding` touchpoint. When `models.tier.subagent` (or `models.default`, or `GBRAIN_MODEL`) resolves to a `litellm:*` model, the resolver and capability gate disagree silently:

1. `gbrain models` warns `[models] tier.subagent resolved to "litellm:gpt-5.4" via "models.tier.subagent", which is an unrecognized provider` and falls back to `TIER_DEFAULTS.subagent` (`anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`).
2. On a brain that routes paid traffic through a LiteLLM-shaped proxy and has no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env, every subagent loop dispatch then throws `AIConfigError: Anthropic ... requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.

The warn text says "unrecognized provider," but the recipe IS registered. The gate is gating on missing touchpoint, not on missing recipe. Specifically: `getProviderCapabilities()` in `src/core/ai/capabilities.ts:74-104` throws when `recipe.touchpoints.chat` is missing; `classifyCapabilities()` catches and returns `'unknown'`; `enforceSubagentCapable()` in `src/core/model-config.ts:231-281` treats `'unknown'` as fail-closed and replaces the user's choice. Net effect: explicit `models.tier.subagent = litellm:*` is overridden without the user's knowledge, and the loop dies on missing Anthropic auth.

**What did you expect?**

`models.tier.subagent = litellm:gpt-5.4` should dispatch through the litellm route (which forwards to whatever the proxy is configured to back). The subagent capability gate should classify a litellm chat as capable, not unknown.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Fresh brain with no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env.
2. `gbrain config set models.tier.subagent litellm:gpt-5.4` (or any `litellm:*` string).
3. `gbrain models` → warns "unrecognized provider" and falls back to `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`.
4. Submit any subagent job → `AIConfigError: Anthropic ... requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.

**Environment**

- gbrain version: 0.42.42.0+ (reproduces against current `master`).
- OS: Linux + macOS reproduce.
- Bun: 1.3.14.
- Database: Postgres 16 + PGLite both reproduce.

**Proposed fix**

Declare `chat` and `expansion` touchpoints on the litellm recipe, mirroring the openai recipe's shape:

- `models: []` (litellm proxies arbitrary backends; `assertTouchpoint` already skips allowlist checks for `tier: 'openai-compat'`)
- `supports_tools: true`
- `supports_subagent_loop: true`
- `supports_prompt_cache: false` (OpenAI-compat backends don't honor Anthropic-style `cache_control`; loop emits a one-time `degraded:no_caching` warn; accepted trade for the proxy path)
- `max_context_tokens: 200_000` (conservative GPT-5-family default; per-deployment override needed for smaller-context backends — see "Open design questions" below)
- costs `undefined` (varies by proxied provider)

After this change, `classifyCapabilities('litellm:gpt-5.4', recipe)` returns `degraded:no_caching` (chat-capable, no Anthropic-style prompt cache). `enforceSubagentCapable` no longer steals the model choice.

**Open design questions** (worth settling in review):

1. Should `ChatTouchpoint` grow a `user_provided_models?: true` field (parallel to the existing `EmbeddingTouchpoint.user_provided_models`) so the design intent is explicit, rather than relying on `tier === 'openai-compat'` to skip the allowlist check in `assertTouchpoint`? Cleaner long-term shape.
2. Should `max_context_tokens` be `200_000` (correct for GPT-5 family but wrong for many openai-compat backends; Llama 3 = 8K–128K depending on quant, DeepSeek = 128K, Mistral = 32K–128K) or `undefined` (let `getProviderCapabilities` fall through to its own 128K default at line 102)? Probably `undefined` so per-deployment reality wins, with a doctor warn when a route's actual context exceeds the recipe's declared cap.
3. Should the same patch land on the `llama-server` recipe (currently embedding-only too)? And is `ollama`'s `supports_subagent_loop: false` too conservative for modern tool-capable local models (Qwen2.5, Llama 3.1+, Mistral Small 3.1+ all support function calling natively via Ollama)?

**Adjacent observation worth surfacing in the same PR:** the warn message "unrecognized provider" is misleading. The recipe IS in the registry; the classifier reports `'unknown'` because the `chat` touchpoint is missing. A clearer message would be `provider does not declare a chat touchpoint` (or similar): same severity, less misdirection. Drop-in change at `src/core/model-config.ts:258`.

PR ready for review: #2208 .
title: litellm: subagent loop falls back to anthropic and dies without ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (chat/expansion touchpoints missing)

body:
## Problem

When using a non-default embedding provider with `user_provided_models: true` (such as a local LM Studio / llama-server embedding endpoint), `diagnoseEmbedding()` in `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` unconditionally returns `user_provided_model_unset` if the recipe has an empty `models: []` array, even when a valid model name is supplied via the config string.

For example, a config like:
```
embedding_model: "llama-server:text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b"
```
correctly parses `modelId = "text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b"`, but the preflight guard ignores it and rejects the call.

This makes custom embedding providers (LM Studio, Ollama, llama-server, vLLM) entirely non-functional when configured through `user_provided_models`.

## Reproduction

1. Set up gbrain with a custom embedding provider (e.g. LM Studio running on localhost):
   ```
   embedding_model: "llama-server:text-embedding-model-name"
   ```
   with provider config:
   ```json
   {
     "providers": {
       "llama-server": {
         "base_url": "http://localhost:1234/v1",
         "user_provided_models": true,
         "models": []
       }
     }
   }
   ```
2. Run any command that triggers embedding: `gbrain search`, `gbrain embed`
3. Observe: embedding fails with `user_provided_model_unset`

## Root Cause

In `src/core/ai/gateway.ts`, the `diagnoseEmbedding()` function checks:
```typescript
if (
  Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
  tp.models.length === 0 &&
  (recipe.id === "litellm" || isUserProvided)
) {
  return { ok: false, reason: "user_provided_model_unset", ... };
}
```

The guard rejects based solely on `models` array being empty and `isUserProvided` being true, without checking whether `parsed.modelId` was successfully extracted from the config string. If the user has specified a model name in the embedding_model config, the guard should accept it.

## Proposed Fix

Add `!parsed.modelId` to the condition:
```typescript
if (
  Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
  tp.models.length === 0 &&
  (recipe.id === "litellm" || isUserProvided) &&
  !parsed.modelId
) {
  return { ok: false, reason: "user_provided_model_unset", ... };
}
```

When a valid modelId has been parsed from the config string, the guard no longer rejects.

## Impact

- Fixes local LM Studio / llama-server / Ollama / vLLM embedding for all gbrain users
- Minimal change: one extra conjunct in an existing condition
- No behavioral change for other embedding configurations

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: Duplicate of another issue or PR

title: diagnoseEmbedding() falsely returns user_provided_model_unset when modelId is parsed from config string

body:
## Problem

Several OpenAI-compatible providers, notably **DeepSeek v4-flash**, default to thinking/reasoning mode. When gbrain uses these models for non-reasoning calls (embeddings, search expansion, linting, etc.), every response carries unnecessary thinking tokens, inflating token costs by roughly 50%.

DeepSeek v4-flash is a popular provider for gbrain because of its competitive pricing and good performance, but the default thinking mode silently increases billable tokens for every chat call that gbrain makes — including short, fact-based queries that gain nothing from reasoning.

## Reproduction

1. Configure gbrain with a DeepSeek provider (or any openai-compatible provider that supports thinking)
2. Run any gbrain command that triggers a chat call: `gbrain query`, `gbrain search`, `gbrain embed`, etc.
3. Observe that the response includes reasoning/thinking tokens for every call

## Root Cause

The `chat()` function in `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` does not explicitly disable thinking for openai-compatible providers. Unlike Anthropic Claude (which uses a different parameter `thinking` under `providerOptions.anthropic`) and OpenAI o-series (which uses `reasoning_effort`), openai-compatible providers pass their thinking parameter through `providerOptions[recipe.id]`, which gets spread into the request body. If the provider defaults to thinking enabled, gbrain has no way to opt out.

## Proposed Fix

Explicitly set `thinking: { type: "disabled" }` for all openai-compatible providers (`recipe.implementation === "openai-compatible"`) in the `chat()` function. The `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` provider spreads `providerOptions[recipe.id]` into the request body; providers that do not support thinking silently ignore the parameter — so the fix is safe for ALL openai-compatible providers, not just DeepSeek.

This does not affect native providers (OpenAI o-series, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini), which use different parameter names for reasoning control.

## Impact

- Token waste: ~50% reduction in unnecessary output tokens for DeepSeek users
- Backward compatible: no change in behavior for providers that don't support thinking
- Future-proof: covers providers (Ollama, OpenRouter, Groq, Together, llama-server) that may add thinking support later

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: Duplicate of another issue or PR

title: OpenAI-compatible providers with default thinking (e.g. DeepSeek) waste ~50% tokens on non-reasoning calls

body:
## Summary
On a Postgres-engine brain, migration **v0.32.2** ("Facts join the system-of-record — your hot memory now lives in markdown, indexed by the DB") **fails silently** and becomes WEDGED, which permanently blocks the `extract_facts` cycle phase — so `recall` / hot-facts never populate and `cycle_freshness` stays FAIL.

Critically, it **still fails with 0 rows in the `facts` table**, so it is not data-dependent — the migration itself appears broken.

## Environment
- gbrain **0.42.44.0** (commit `090bb53`), installed via `git clone master` + `bun install` + `bun link` (the `bun install -g github:garrytan/gbrain` path aborts with a self-dependency loop — separate minor issue).
- Engine: **Postgres 16.14** + pgvector; upgraded from 0.41.14.0.
- Schema auto-migrated 97 → 117 cleanly on first `gbrain doctor`; only **v0.32.2** fails.

## What happens
```
$ gbrain apply-migrations --yes
=== Applying migration v0.32.2: Facts join the system-of-record — your hot memory now lives in markdown, indexed by the DB ===
=== v0.32.2 — facts join the system-of-record invariant ===
Migration v0.32.2 reported status=failed.
```
- **No error detail is printed.**
- `~/.gbrain/upgrade-errors.jsonl` **does not exist** (the wedged-migration message points there, but nothing is ever written).
- `gbrain doctor` → `[FAIL] minions_migration: WEDGED MIGRATION(s): 0.32.2`
- `--force-retry 0.32.2` writes a marker, but the next `apply-migrations --yes` fails the same silent way.
- Deleting the only 2 legacy v0.31 facts (`facts` table = **0 rows**) does **not** help.

## Impact
- `extract_facts` skipped → `recall` / hot-facts never populate; `cycle_freshness` permanently FAIL.
- All other cycle phases (sync, embed, purge, synthesize, takes) run fine on 0.42.44.

## Asks
1. **Surface the real error** for v0.32.2 (zero diagnostics; `upgrade-errors.jsonl` stays empty).
2. Make v0.32.2 succeed (or no-op) on Postgres when `facts` is empty.
title: gbrain dream: migration v0.32.2 silently fails / wedges (even with 0 facts), blocking extract_facts

body:
# PGLite WASM runtime fails on Windows 11 x64 (AMD64) — same `Aborted()` as #223 / #939

## Summary

`gbrain init --pglite` and all subsequent DB-touching commands fail with `PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime` on Windows 11 **x64 (AMD64)** — not ARM64. The error message points to the macOS 26.3 bug (#223), but this is a separate platform.

## Environment

- **OS:** Microsoft Windows 11 Pro, Build 26200 (10.0.26200), **x64 (AMD64)**
- **Bun:** 1.3.14 (installed via `powershell -c "irm bun.sh/install.ps1 | iex"`)
- **gbrain:** 0.42.40.0 (installed via `git clone + bun install + bun link`)
- **@electric-sql/pglite:** version bundled with gbrain 0.42.40.0
- **Node.js:** v24.11.1 (available on same machine)

## Steps to Reproduce

```powershell
git clone https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain.git E:\OpenClaw-GBrain\app\gbrain
cd E:\OpenClaw-GBrain\app\gbrain
bun install
bun link
gbrain init --pglite --no-embedding --brain-path "E:\OpenClaw-GBrain\data\brain.pglite"
```

## Actual Result

```
Setting up local brain with PGLite (no server needed)...
  --no-embedding: deferred setup — configure with `gbrain config set embedding_model <id>` before import
PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime.
  Most common cause: the macOS 26.3 WASM bug
  (https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/223).
  Run `gbrain doctor` for a full diagnosis.
  Original error: [object Object]
```

`gbrain doctor` reports:
- `connection: Could not connect to configured DB (URL from config-file-path); filesystem checks only`
- Overall health score: **65/100**

## Additional Diagnostics

- **Bun `WebAssembly` object is complete** — Bun has WASM support
- **PGLite loads fine under Node.js** — `node -e "const { PGlite } = require('@electric-sql/pglite'); console.log('ok')"` outputs `ok` from the gbrain directory
- The issue is specific to **Bun + PGLite WASM on Windows x64**, not a general PGLite failure

## Related Issues

- **#939** — Same `Aborted()` on Windows 11 + Bun 1.3.13 (no comments, no fix)
- **#1502** — Same on Windows 11 ARM64 (confirms arm64 is not the only affected arch)
- **#1549** — Windows 10 x64: init succeeds but pgvector extension missing from WASM binary (different symptom, same root cause: PGLite WASM on Windows is broken)
- **#223** — macOS 26.3 ARM64 (the error message currently points here, misleading Windows users)
- **#2024** — Draft PR to fix diagnostic routing (not yet merged)

## Notes

- All related Windows issues (#939, #1502, #1549) have **zero comments** from maintainers
- The error message currently tells Windows users to look at a **macOS-specific** bug (#223), which is misleading
- There is **no documented workaround** for Windows users other than installing a full PostgreSQL server

## Request

1. Acknowledge Windows x64 as a known-affected platform (not just ARM64)
2. Provide a documented workaround or fix for PGLite WASM on Windows
3. Update the error message to not point Windows users to a macOS-specific issue
title: PGLite WASM runtime fails on Windows 11 x64 (AMD64) — same Aborted() as #223 / #939

**What problem does this solve?**
Recently few initiatives came to public with the effort to have a standard knowledge document format.

And the Doclang initiative from Docling (IBM), but more companies support the initiative
The AI-native document format.
https://doclang.ai/

I would like to open a discussion for memory pages
title: Open standard knowledge document format

body:
When configuring GBrain with a LiteLLM Proxy embedding model using a user-defined model name, GBrain does not recognize the litellm provider as embedding-capable.

The LiteLLM provider is documented/hinted as supporting usage like:

gbrain init \
  --url "$GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL" \
  --embedding-model litellm:gbrain-embedding \
  --embedding-dimensions 4096

However, after initialization, gbrain providers list shows litellm with EMBED —, and embedding commands fail with:

Provider "litellm" requires a specific model name to be configured.
  Set one: gbrain config set embedding_model litellm:<model-name>
  Or run with --no-embed to import-only and embed later.

This happens even though embedding_model is already configured as litellm:gbrain-embedding.

Environment

* GBrain version: 0.42.42.0
* Engine: PostgreSQL
* pgvector: enabled
* LiteLLM Proxy: running locally on http://localhost:4000/v1
* Embedding backend: vLLM OpenAI-compatible endpoint
* Embedding model: qwen3-embedding-8b-nvfp4
* Embedding dimensions: 4096
* Chat model: Qwen model behind LiteLLM, exposed as gpt-4o-mini

LiteLLM model list:

model_list:
  - model_name: gpt-4o-mini
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/qwen3.6-35b
      api_base: http://192.168.1.12:30000/v1
      api_key: EMPTY
  - model_name: gbrain-embedding
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/qwen3-embedding-8b-nvfp4
      api_base: http://192.168.1.12:30001/v1
      api_key: EMPTY
      mode: embedding
      extra_body:
        encoding_format: float

The LiteLLM endpoint works correctly:

curl -s http://localhost:4000/v1/embeddings \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer EMPTY" \
  -d '{
    "model": "gbrain-embedding",
    "input": "test"
  }' | jq '.data[0].embedding | length'

Returns:

4096

Steps to reproduce

1. Run LiteLLM Proxy with a user-defined embedding model, for example:

model_list:
  - model_name: gbrain-embedding
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/qwen3-embedding-8b-nvfp4
      api_base: http://192.168.1.12:30001/v1
      api_key: EMPTY
      mode: embedding
      extra_body:
        encoding_format: float

2. Export environment variables:

export GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL=postgresql://gbrain:password@localhost:5432/gbrain
export LITELLM_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4000/v1
export LITELLM_API_KEY=EMPTY
export OPENAI_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4000/v1
export OPENAI_API_KEY=EMPTY

3. Initialize GBrain:

gbrain init \
  --url "$GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL" \
  --embedding-model litellm:gbrain-embedding \
  --embedding-dimensions 4096

4. Check providers:

gbrain providers list

Actual behavior

litellm appears as ready, but not embedding-capable:

PROVIDER              TIER              EMBED   EXPAND  CHAT    STATUS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
litellm               openai-compat     —       —       —       ✓ ready

Then embedding fails:

gbrain embed --all

Output:

Provider "litellm" requires a specific model name to be configured.
  Set one: gbrain config set embedding_model litellm:<model-name>
  Or run with --no-embed to import-only and embed later.

But config already contains:

{
  "embedding_model": "litellm:gbrain-embedding",
  "embedding_dimensions": 4096
}

Expected behavior

If embedding_model is configured as:

litellm:gbrain-embedding

and embedding_dimensions is explicitly provided, GBrain should treat the LiteLLM provider as embedding-capable and allow:

gbrain providers test --model litellm:gbrain-embedding
gbrain embed --all

Expected providers list:

litellm               openai-compat     yes     —       —       ✓ ready

Expected provider test:

Probing embedding provider...
  ✓ 99ms, 4096 dims
All probes green.

Temporary local workaround

I patched src/core/ai/recipes/litellm-proxy.ts to expose an embedding touchpoint:

touchpoints: {
  embedding: {
    models: ['gbrain-embedding'],
    user_provided_models: true,
    default_dims: 4096,
    cost_per_1m_tokens_usd: 0,
    price_last_verified: '2026-06-14',
    no_batch_cap: true,
    supports_multimodal: true,
  },
}

After this change:

gbrain providers list

correctly shows:

litellm               openai-compat     yes     —       —       ✓ ready

and:

gbrain providers test --model litellm:gbrain-embedding

returns:

Probing embedding provider...
  ✓ 99ms, 4096 dims
All probes green.

Suggested fix

The LiteLLM recipe should advertise embedding support when user-provided models are allowed.

Instead of requiring hardcoded models, the provider could support:

embedding: {
  models: [],
  user_provided_models: true,
  default_dims: 0,
  no_batch_cap: true,
  supports_multimodal: true,
}

and the embedding pipeline should treat litellm:<model-name> as a valid embedding model when --embedding-dimensions is provided.

Why this matters

LiteLLM is commonly used as a proxy in front of OpenAI-compatible providers, including vLLM. Modern local/self-hosted embedding models are often exposed this way, and may use custom model names.

In this case, the backend was:

* vLLM
* Qwen3-Embedding-8B-NVFP4
* 4096-dimensional embeddings
* LiteLLM Proxy as the routing layer

This setup works at the API level, but GBrain does not currently recognize the LiteLLM provider as embedding-capable without patching the recipe.
title: LiteLLM user-defined embedding models are not recognized as embedding-capable

body:
`resolveTodayTasks()` (the OpenClaw live-context surfacer) and `daily-task-manager` (the documented task writer) disagree on both *what heading* and *what store* the task list uses, so tasks added the documented way silently never appear. Verified against v0.42.43.0.

## The chain, traced

1. `skills/daily-task-manager/SKILL.md` saves the list via `gbrain put ops/tasks` (`put_page`) and emits headings `## P0 — Urgent` / `## P1 — Today` / `## P2` / `## P3`. `put_page` writes to the DB `pages` table only — there is no DB→disk writeback.
2. `src/core/sync.ts` (`PRUNE_DIR_NAMES`, ~L258) prunes `ops/` at any depth, so the disk file is never ingested into the DB and the DB page is never reconciled back to disk.
3. `src/core/context-engine.ts` `resolveTodayTasks()` reads the **disk file** `ops/tasks.md` via `readFileSync` and matches only a section headed literally `## Today`, lines shaped `- [ ] **bold**`.
4. `skills/briefing/SKILL.md` never reads the task file at all — action items come from `gbrain query` + timeline.

## Two independent breaks

- **Heading:** the writer emits `## P1 — Today`; the reader requires the substring `## Today`, which never occurs in `## P1 — Today`. Match fails → `[]`.
- **Storage:** even with matching headings, the writer persists to the DB while the reader reads disk, and `ops/` is pruned from sync — so the two stores never reconcile.

Net: a task added via the documented skill reaches neither the file the reader reads nor (for briefing) any path that's consulted. The only thing matching the reader's format is the test fixtures, which fabricate `## Today` input inline — so the suite passes while the real write path produces nothing the reader can see.

## Repro

1. Add a task via daily-task-manager → lands as a DB page under `## P1 — Today`.
2. `gbrain get ops/tasks` → `page_not_found` (ops/ pruned from sync).
3. `resolveTodayTasks()` → `[]` (no `## Today` on disk).
4. Briefing shows no action items.

## Suggested direction — converge on one contract

Reading the disk file by path looks correct given the deliberate `ops/` sync exclusion, so:

- Make `daily-task-manager` read/write `ops/tasks.md` by path instead of `gbrain get/put` (fixes the storage split).
- Align the heading: either `resolveTodayTasks()` also matches the documented `## P0` / `## P1 — Today` headings, or the writer emits a `## Today` section. (Heading alone is not sufficient — the storage split must be fixed too, or a rename leaves it still broken.)
- Give `briefing` a path-based read of `ops/tasks.md` for its action-items section.

Environment: gbrain 0.42.43.0.
title: Task list has conflicting write/read contracts — tasks added via daily-task-manager never surface in live context

body:
## Summary

`parseFlag` resolves flags with a naive `args.indexOf(flag)`, which returns `-1` cleanly for any flag the function doesn't know about. Unknown flags are therefore silently accepted and ignored — no warning, no error, non-zero exit. This is a foot-gun for safety-critical flags that a caller assumes will prevent side effects.

## Concrete incident

```
gbrain init --migrate-only --dry-run
```

This applied real migrations. `--dry-run` is not a parsed flag on `init`, so `parseFlag`/the flag check silently no-op'd it, and `--migrate-only` ran for real. A command that the operator believed was a no-op preview mutated the schema (in our case 92 → 115, 23 migrations). The command returned success, which told the operator nothing about whether `--dry-run` had any effect.

## Root cause

`src/commands/agent.ts:25` (and the same pattern elsewhere, e.g. `src/commands/book-mirror.ts:81`):

```ts
function parseFlag(args: string[], flag: string): string | undefined {
  // indexOf-based lookup; unknown flags are simply never found and never rejected
}
```

There is no strict-flag layer that rejects or warns on unrecognized flags.

## Suggested resolution

Add a strict-flag validation pass per subcommand that errors (or at least warns loudly) on unrecognized flags, especially for commands with destructive/irreversible side effects. At minimum, `--dry-run` should be a recognized no-op-or-error everywhere it's plausibly expected, rather than silently ignored.

Observed on v0.42.36.0.
title: parseFlag silently accepts unknown flags (e.g. --dry-run on init --migrate-only no-ops and applies real migrations)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain-base-v2` omits `meeting` and `conversation` from `page_types`, but the engine treats both as first-class, fact-extractable conversation types. A brain that migrates `gbrain-base@1.x → gbrain-base-v2` therefore silently loses meeting/conversation fact extraction: the pages hit the `*unknown*` catch-all retype rule and become `note` + `legacy_type`, which no longer matches the hardcoded fact-extraction allow-list.

These two surfaces are out of sync — the question is which one is canonical.

## Evidence

Engine treats `meeting` + `conversation` as first-class:
- `src/core/extract-conversation-facts.ts:143` — `ALLOWED_TYPES = ['conversation', 'meeting', 'slack', 'email']`
- `src/commands/extract.ts:451` — `topDir === 'meetings' ? 'meeting'` (directory → type mapping)
- `src/commands/doctor.ts:2851` and `:4564`, `src/commands/sources.ts:998`, `src/commands/jobs.ts:1494` — all carry the same `['conversation','meeting','slack','email']` allow-list

But `src/core/schema-pack/base/gbrain-base-v2.yaml` declares neither `meeting` nor `conversation` as a page_type, subtype, or alias, and there is no explicit `mapping_rule` for either. (`slack` and `email` *are* declared in v2.) So both fall through the D12 catch-all (`from_type: '*unknown*' → note`, lines 537-541).

## Impact

After `unify-types` to v2, `meetings/*` pages become `type: note` (`legacy_type: meeting`). Subsequent `extract-conversation-facts` / `sources backfill` runs skip them because `note ∉ ALLOWED_TYPES`. Fact extraction over meetings silently stops.

## Suggested resolution

Either (a) add `meeting` and `conversation` as canonical page_types (or subtypes of a conversation root) in `gbrain-base-v2.yaml`, or (b) if they're intended to live under `note`, update the five `ALLOWED_TYPES` sites to match on `legacy_type`. Option (a) seems right given the engine's existing first-class treatment.

Observed on v0.42.36.0.
title: gbrain-base-v2 drops meeting/conversation but engine treats them as first-class fact-extraction types

body:
## Problem

GBrain preserves source boundaries with `source_id`, but cross-source ranking does not currently understand user-defined source authority.

In practice, some sources are canonical while others are staging, provisional research, archives, or working synthesis. A staging/research source can outrank a canonical source in a cross-source query when it has stronger lexical/vector relevance.

That is useful for recall, but not enough for authority-sensitive answering.

## Requested Feature

Add optional source-aware authority weighting for search/ranking.

Example config shape:

```yaml
authority_profiles:
  operating_context:
    canonical-ops: 1.5
    orchestration-docs: 1.2
    research-staging: 0.6
    archives: 0.3
```

Then search could support something like:

```bash
gbrain search "AI operating systems" --authority-profile operating_context
```

or the equivalent API/MCP parameter.

## Desired Behavior

- Keep retrieving across sources.
- Do not hide lower-authority sources.
- Re-rank or boost/demote based on the active authority profile.
- Preserve `source_id` on every result.
- Expose the applied authority profile/weight in result metadata.
- Allow query-time override, not only global config.
- Keep existing relevance-only search available.

## Use Case

For a user with multiple source wikis/repos:

- a canonical ops/wiki source may contain approved doctrine or durable operating context;
- a research/staging source may contain provisional synthesis;
- archives may be useful for recall but lower-authority for current answers.

For authority-sensitive questions, the canonical source should generally outrank staging/research material when both are relevant, while lower-authority sources should remain discoverable.

## Non-goal

This is not asking GBrain to determine truth automatically.

It is asking GBrain to support explicit, user-defined source authority policy as one ranking signal.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Users can define per-source authority weights.
- Search can invoke a named authority profile.
- Results show which profile/weight affected ranking.
- Existing relevance-only search remains available.
- Lower-authority sources remain discoverable but are not treated as equally canonical when an authority profile is active.
title: Add source-aware authority weighting / authority profiles for cross-source search

body:
## Improvement opportunity

Add an OSV-Scanner GitHub Actions workflow for dependency vulnerability scanning.

## Evidence inspected

Repo-local evidence:

- `package.json:98-137` declares a non-trivial runtime/dev dependency surface (`@ai-sdk/*`, `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, `@aws-sdk/client-s3`, `express`, `cookie-parser`, `js-yaml`, `openai`, `postgres`, `typescript`, etc.).
- Existing workflows are strong on secret scanning and Actions hardening, but not dependency vulnerability drift:
  - `.github/workflows/test.yml:71-82` runs Gitleaks in PR CI.
  - `.github/workflows/test.yml:14-15`, `.github/workflows/e2e.yml:12-13`, and `.github/workflows/heavy-tests.yml:22-23` use least-privilege `contents: read`.
  - `.github/workflows/release.yml:9-12` now has provenance permissions for release attestations.
- Repo/workflow grep found no `osv-scanner`, `OSV-Scanner`, `dependency-review`, `Semgrep`, `CodeQL`, or `OpenSSF Scorecard` workflow in `.github/workflows/`.
- `CONTRIBUTING.md:148-161` documents the local CI gate as `gitleaks + unit + E2E`, with no dependency vulnerability scan step.

Current external baseline:

- OSV-Scanner's GitHub Action docs describe two reusable workflows: PR scanning for newly introduced vulnerabilities and scheduled/full scans for dependency trees. The scheduled workflow fails on known vulnerabilities by default and can upload SARIF to GitHub code scanning.
- Reference inspected: https://google.github.io/osv-scanner/github-action/

## Expected impact

Maintainers get an automated signal when an existing dependency becomes vulnerable after merge, not just when a secret is introduced. This is especially useful for a CLI/agent project with direct runtime dependencies and frequent release tags: the project can catch dependency risk before cutting binaries or before a contributor bumps a package in a PR.

## Suggested fix

Keep this scoped and low-noise:

1. Add `.github/workflows/osv-scanner.yml` using the official reusable workflow.
2. Trigger it on a staggered weekly schedule plus `workflow_dispatch` first. Optionally add `pull_request` later if maintainers want dependency changes blocked in PRs.
3. Use least-privilege permissions required by OSV SARIF upload:
   - `contents: read`
   - `actions: read`
   - `security-events: write`
4. Use `scan-args` scoped to the repository root / lockfiles, and start with the scheduled full scan so false positives can be triaged before making it a required PR check.
5. If the repo wants PR blocking instead, use the OSV PR reusable workflow on `pull_request` to fail only on newly introduced vulnerabilities.

## Duplicate check

I inspected the required open items with `gh` before filing:

- `gh issue list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state open --limit 200 --json number,title,body,url,labels,updatedAt`
  - 200 open issues inspected.
- `gh pr list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state open --limit 200 --json number,title,body,url,labels,updatedAt,headRefName,author`
  - 200 open PRs inspected.

Keyword scans over those 400 open titles/bodies found no covering OSV/dependency-vulnerability issue or PR. The only broad supply-chain hit was #2142 (`Add artifact attestations for release binaries`), which is release provenance, not dependency vulnerability scanning.

Exact GitHub search results:

- `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "OSV-Scanner"` → 0
- `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "osv-scanner"` → 0
- `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "vulnerability scan"` → 0
- `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "vulnerability scanning"` → 0
- `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "dependency vulnerability"` → 0
- `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "dependency review"` → only #2142 via its duplicate-search command list, not as its requested fix

## Commands/files inspected

- `README.md`
- `CONTRIBUTING.md`
- `docs/guides/skill-development.md`
- `examples/skillpack-reference/README.md`
- `SECURITY.md`
- `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/tier5-queries.md`
- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature_request.md`
- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md`
- `.github/workflows/test.yml`
- `.github/workflows/e2e.yml`
- `.github/workflows/heavy-tests.yml`
- `.github/workflows/release.yml`
- `package.json`
- `gh pr list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state merged --limit 30 --json number,title,body,url,mergedAt,files`
- `git grep -n -i -E 'dependency-review|osv|semgrep|codeql|scorecard|openssf|gitleaks|attest|permissions:|uses:' -- .github package.json SECURITY.md CONTRIBUTING.md README.md`
- `gh api -X GET search/issues -f q='repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open ...'`
title: Add scheduled OSV-Scanner dependency vulnerability scan

body:
## Context

I run a downstream side-car ([apliteni/brain-access](https://github.com/apliteni/brain-access)) that wraps `gbrain` and adds per-company SSO, RBAC, and a `Register Agent` UI for MCP clients (Claude Code, Codex, etc).

Today the side-car has no way to influence the OAuth bearer TTL that gbrain mints for a freshly registered agent — the bearer always inherits whatever `--token-ttl` (or the gbrain default ~24h) is configured server-side. Per-agent control would require an upstream surface.

## Ask

Allow the DCR ([RFC 7591](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7591)) `/register` endpoint to accept a `token_ttl_seconds` field in the request body. When present and within an admin-configured `[min_ttl, max_ttl]` range, gbrain would mint subsequent access tokens for that `client_id` with the requested TTL. Out-of-range requests should be clamped (not rejected) and surfaced in the registration response so the caller can show the effective value to the user.
