Optional second knob: a per-client `default_ttl` returned in the DCR response so side-cars can display "this agent's tokens last N hours" without round-tripping.

## Why DCR is the right place

- Side-cars can pre-decide TTL per agent without touching gbrain config or restarting.
- It matches the OAuth 2.1 model where the client (not the server admin) chooses lifetime within policy bounds.
- It is backward compatible: clients that omit `token_ttl_seconds` inherit the current default.

## Acceptance (rough)

- `POST /register` accepts `token_ttl_seconds` (integer, seconds).
- Server enforces `min_ttl` / `max_ttl` flags (defaults: 300s and 7d would cover the realistic span).
- `POST /token` for that `client_id` returns `expires_in = clamp(requested, min, max)`.
- `GET /register/<client_id>` response includes the effective TTL.
- Brief mention in README / CHANGELOG.

## Downstream tracking

This unblocks [`apliteni/brain-access#175`](https://github.com/apliteni/brain-access/issues/175) — once shipped, the side-car can build a real "Token lifetime" picker on its agent-registration form.

Happy to PR if you confirm the shape is acceptable.
title: feat(dcr): expose token_ttl_seconds hint at Dynamic Client Registration time

body:
## Summary

`hybridSearch` returns **0 results for a federated source scope** (`{ sourceIds: ["source-a"], sourceId: undefined }`) while `engine.searchKeyword` returns matching pages for the **same** `sourceIds`. This silently blinds the `think` and `query` ops (both route through `hybridSearch` / `hybridSearchCached`) for any agent scoped to sources via a grant — i.e. the multi-source **company-brain** path. The `search` op is unaffected because it bypasses hybrid and calls `engine.searchKeyword` directly.

Observed on **gbrain 0.42.42.0**.

## Repro

A source (`source-a`) holds embedded pages. The caller's token grants `allowedSources = ["source-a"]`, so the op handler's `sourceScopeOpts(ctx)` yields `{ sourceIds: ["source-a"] }` — scalar `sourceId` is **undefined** (the federated path).

```
# WORKS — search op → engine.searchKeyword(q, { sourceIds:["source-a"] })
search "<query>" --source-id source-a
#   → page  score 0.917  (full content)

# FAILS — query op → hybridSearchCached(...) → hybridSearch(...)
query "<query>" --source-id source-a
#   → []   (silent empty, no error thrown)

# FAILS — think → runGather → hybridSearch(...)
think "<question>" --source-id source-a
#   → pagesGathered: 0  ("the brain contains no data"; the LLM runs but is handed 0 pages)
```

Same pages, same source, same token, same query text. Keyword finds them; hybrid does not. **No exception** — the hybrid stream returns an empty array (no `[think.gather] hybrid stream failed` log line).

## Already ruled out (verified)

1. **Engine SQL filtering is correct.** Both `engine.searchKeyword` (`postgres-engine.ts:1614-1620`) and `engine.searchVector` (`:1882-1887`) handle both forms:
   ```ts
   if (opts?.sourceIds?.length) clause = `AND p.source_id = ANY($n::text[])`;
   else if (opts?.sourceId)     clause = `AND p.source_id = $n`;
   ```
   With `sourceIds:["source-a"]` this filters correctly — proven by the working `search` op.
2. **`hybridSearch` forwards scope to its main sub-search opts.** `hybrid.ts:876-877` rebuilds the inner `searchOpts` with `sourceId: opts?.sourceId, sourceIds: opts?.sourceIds` (the #861 leak-seal), and `engine.searchKeyword(query, searchOpts)` runs at `:933`.
3. **Not cache-only.** Forcing the **uncached** `hybridSearch` (not `hybridSearchCached`) also returns 0, so a stale cache entry isn't the sole cause.

So: scope reaches `hybridSearch`, it forwards to the engine sub-searches, and those filter correctly in isolation — yet the **final** hybrid result is empty. The defect is **between the sub-searches and the returned result**, specific to the **federated (`sourceIds` set, `sourceId` undefined)** case.

## Suspected locus

`hybrid.ts` carries explicit scars here: `cacheScopeKey` (`:1810`) and its comment (`:1800-1807`) describe a prior bug where *"a federated query fell through to scalar"* and *"federated (`sourceIds`) reads a different graph than … scalar `sourceId`."* This strongly suggests a post-retrieval step still branches on `opts.sourceId` (undefined here) — e.g. `if (opts.sourceId)` or `opts.sourceId ?? 'default'` — and so skips or mis-scopes when only `sourceIds` is set.

A concrete latent instance of the same omission: the expansion path `expandAnchors(engine, anchorSet, { walkDepth, nearSymbol, sourceId: opts?.sourceId })` (`hybrid.ts:1369`) passes **`sourceId` only, no `sourceIds`** (off in this repro since `expansion:false`, but it will bite the `walkDepth>0` path).

## Suggested bisection (resolves retrieval-vs-fusion in one run)

Log the per-stream counts inside `hybridSearch` before/after each post step:

```ts
process.stderr.write(`[hybrid.dbg] kw=${keywordResults.length} vec=${vectorResults.length} ` +
  `fused=${fused.length} afterRerank=${reranked?.length ?? '-'} afterAutocut=${cut.length} ` +
  `scope=${JSON.stringify({ sourceId: opts?.sourceId, sourceIds: opts?.sourceIds })}\n`);
```

- `kw=0` with `sourceIds` set → a sub-search branch isn't applying the federated filter in the hybrid path (there are several `searchOpts` builders — `:876`, `:993`, `:1027`, `:1259`, `:1432`, and the scalar-only `:1369`; confirm the live branch includes `sourceIds`).
- `kw>0` but `fused`/`afterRerank`/`afterAutocut` collapses to 0 → fusion/rerank/autocut drops federated rows.

## Suggested fix

1. From the bisection, fix the single step that zeroes the federated case — make `sourceIds` authoritative when `sourceId` is undefined everywhere downstream (mirror the engine's `sourceIds ?? sourceId` precedence in post-processing, not just the SQL `WHERE`).
2. Patch the latent `expandAnchors` call (`hybrid.ts:1369`) to forward `sourceIds: opts?.sourceIds`.
3. **Regression test** — parity between the bare engine path and hybrid under federation:
   ```ts
   const kw = await engine.searchKeyword(q, { sourceIds: [S] });
   const hy = await hybridSearch(engine, q, { sourceIds: [S] }); // sourceId undefined
   expect(hy.map(r => r.slug)).toEqual(expect.arrayContaining(kw.map(r => r.slug)));
   ```
   Run once with `sourceId: S` (scalar) and once with `sourceIds: [S]` (federated) — only the federated variant fails today.

## Impact

This blinds the **synthesis layer** (`think`/`query`) for every multi-source / company-brain deployment that scopes agents via `allowedSources` — the exact shape gbrain markets. `search` still works, so it presents as "retrieval is fine but the brain won't synthesize." Single-source scalar deployments don't hit it, which is likely why it survived.
title: hybridSearch returns 0 for federated sourceIds while searchKeyword returns matches (think/query blind under multi-source scope)

body:
## Summary

Configuring an embedding model through the **`litellm`** proxy recipe is impossible: gbrain requires `--embedding-dimensions` for it, but then rejects any value you pass. The two errors are mutually exclusive, so there is no valid invocation. The **`llama-server`** recipe has the identical defect (both declare `default_dims: 0`).

Verified by source inspection of **v0.42.42.0** (installed via `bun install -g github:garrytan/gbrain`), macOS, PGLite engine.

## The catch-22

**Omit `--embedding-dimensions`:**
```
Embedding dimensions must be a positive integer; got 0.
```

**Pass `--embedding-dimensions 768` (or any N > 0):**
```
Provider "litellm" model "<model>" does not support custom dimensions 768
(this model only emits its default vector size).
Either drop --embedding-dimensions or pick a Matryoshka-aware model.
```

So: the first error says *add* `--embedding-dimensions`; the second says *drop* it. No setting satisfies both. Reproduced against every backend tried through the proxy (openai `text-embedding-3-small`/`-3-large`, `cohere/embed-v4.0`, fireworks `nomic`).

## Root cause (source-pinpointed)

1. `src/core/ai/recipes/litellm-proxy.ts:27` sets `default_dims: 0` with the comment *"user must declare --embedding-dimensions explicitly"*, and `setup_hint` (line 44) instructs the user to `pass --embedding-model litellm:<model> and --embedding-dimensions <N>`.

2. In `src/core/embedding-dim-check.ts`, `validateDimAgainstTouchpoint()`:
   - Line 369: `const dim = requestedDims ?? defaultDims;` → with no `--embedding-dimensions`, `dim = 0`.
   - Lines 371-374: `dim <= 0` → **"Embedding dimensions must be a positive integer; got 0."** (the *omit* half).
   - Line 386: `if (requestedDims !== undefined && requestedDims !== defaultDims)` → since `defaultDims = 0`, **any** passed `N > 0` enters custom-dim validation.

3. `isCustomDimValidForProvider()` (lines 408-461) only trusts user-declared dims for a hardcoded set of `recipe.id` values — `voyage` (426), `zeroentropyai` (435), `openai` (444). `litellm` matches none, so it falls through to **Tier 3 (lines 454-461)**: *"...does not support custom dimensions ... Either drop --embedding-dimensions."* (the *pass* half).

The `litellm` recipe is a **passthrough proxy** with `user_provided_models: true` (litellm-proxy.ts:26) — gbrain cannot introspect the arbitrary backend behind LiteLLM, which is exactly why `default_dims` is 0 and the user is told to declare dims. The recipe's own comment (litellm-proxy.ts:39-40) states the design intent: *"we trust the model id and surface the provider's rejection (D12 dim-validation catches mismatched-dim responses pre-storage)"* — i.e. trust the user's declared dims and validate the **actual returned vector size** after the first embedding call, not pre-reject via the Matryoshka allow-list.

The pre-flight allow-list in `isCustomDimValidForProvider` contradicts that intent for any `user_provided_models` / `default_dims: 0` recipe.

## Affected recipes

- `litellm` (`src/core/ai/recipes/litellm-proxy.ts`, `default_dims: 0`)
- `llama-server` (`src/core/ai/recipes/llama-server.ts:34`, `default_dims: 0`, *"forces explicit --embedding-dimensions"*)

Native `openai:` and `ollama:` recipes are unaffected (the former is in the allow-list; the latter declares a non-zero `default_dims`).

## Reproduction

```bash
# either form fails:
gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model litellm:openai/text-embedding-3-small --embedding-dimensions 1536
gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model litellm:openai/text-embedding-3-small
```
(Requires `LITELLM_BASE_URL` set per the recipe's setup_hint; the failure is in client-side dim validation, before any call to the proxy.)

## Proposed fix

In `isCustomDimValidForProvider` (or its caller at `embedding-dim-check.ts:386`), short-circuit for passthrough recipes: when the touchpoint has `user_provided_models === true` **and** `default_dims === 0`, accept the user-declared `requestedDims` as authoritative and defer to the post-call D12 dim-validation (which already catches a mismatch between declared and actually-returned dims before storage). This restores the documented "trust the user, validate the response" behavior for `litellm` and `llama-server` without weakening the Matryoshka checks for `voyage`/`zeroentropyai`/`openai`.

## Workaround

Use the native `ollama:` recipe (e.g. `ollama:nomic-embed-text`, 768d) — fully local, no proxy, unaffected by this validation path.

---
*Reported from a local install used to back a personal knowledge wiki. Happy to test a patch.*
title: `litellm:` (and `llama-server:`) embedding setup is a catch-22: `--embedding-dimensions` is required yet rejected

body:
**What happened?**

The `ollama` embedding recipe pins a single `default_dims: 768` for the whole provider and provides no per-model dimension. As a result, two models that the docs themselves advertise as 1024-dimensional cannot be used at all:

- `ollama:bge-m3` — emits 1024d, but gbrain configures a 768d schema → embedding fails.
- `ollama:mxbai-embed-large` — `docs/integrations/embedding-providers.md` lists it as 1024d, but gbrain also configures 768d for it → same failure.

Two distinct dead ends:

1. **With `--embedding-dimensions 1024`** → `init` refuses outright:
   ```
   Refusing to init: Provider "ollama" model "bge-m3" does not support custom dimensions 1024
   (this model only emits its default vector size). Either drop --embedding-dimensions or pick a Matryoshka-aware model.
   ```
   (Same message for `ollama:mxbai-embed-large`.)

2. **Without `--embedding-dimensions`** → `init` silently sets a 768d schema, then `gbrain embed --stale` fails at runtime:
   ```
   Embedding dim mismatch: model bge-m3 returned 1024 but schema expects 768.
   ```

So there is no flag combination that lets a stock install use `ollama:bge-m3` (or `ollama:mxbai-embed-large`) end-to-end.

**Root cause (from source, v0.42.42.0)**

- `src/core/ai/recipes/ollama.ts` declares a single `touchpoints.embedding.default_dims: 768` with `models: ['nomic-embed-text', 'mxbai-embed-large', 'all-minilm']` and no `dims_options`. Every Ollama model inherits 768d regardless of its true output width.
- `src/core/ai/embedding-dim-check.ts` → `isCustomDimValidForProvider()` only allows a custom dimension when the provider is in a hardcoded Matryoshka allow-list (`voyage`, `zeroentropyai`, `openai`). For `ollama` it falls through to the Tier-3 rejection at ~L458 ("does not support custom dimensions"), so `--embedding-dimensions 1024` is always refused for Ollama models even when the model genuinely emits 1024d.

The Ollama OpenAI-compatible endpoint confirms the real width:
```
$ curl -s http://localhost:11434/v1/embeddings -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -d '{"model":"bge-m3","input":"test"}' | jq '.data[0].embedding | length'
1024
```

**What did you expect?**

`gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model ollama:bge-m3` should configure a 1024d schema (the model's native width), or at minimum `--embedding-dimensions 1024` should be accepted for Ollama models that actually emit 1024d. `mxbai-embed-large` should default to 1024d to match the documented value. Either way, `gbrain embed` should succeed without a dim mismatch.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `ollama pull bge-m3` (Ollama serving on `localhost:11434`).
2. `gbrain init --force --pglite --embedding-model ollama:bge-m3 --embedding-dimensions 1024`
   → refused: "does not support custom dimensions 1024".
3. Retry without the flag: `gbrain init --force --pglite --embedding-model ollama:bge-m3` → schema created at 768d. Then `gbrain import <dir> --no-embed && gbrain embed --stale` → fails: "model bge-m3 returned 1024 but schema expects 768".

**Suggested fix**

Give the Ollama recipe per-model dimensions (e.g. `bge-m3` → 1024, `mxbai-embed-large` → 1024, `nomic-embed-text` → 768, `all-minilm` → 384), and/or add `dims_options` so `isCustomDimValidForProvider()` (Tier 1) accepts the model's real width. Since the Ollama recipe is `implementation: 'openai-compatible'` and `user_provided_models` is effectively true for local servers, an alternative is to allow any explicit `--embedding-dimensions` for local providers (`ollama`, `llama-server`, `litellm`) the way `llama-server`/`litellm` (`default_dims: 0`) already intend — those two also hit the same Tier-3 rejection.

**Workaround used locally**

Added `'bge-m3'` to `models` and `dims_options: [768, 1024]` to the `embedding` touchpoint in `src/core/ai/recipes/ollama.ts`. After that, `gbrain init --embedding-model ollama:bge-m3 --embedding-dimensions 1024` works and `embedding_provider` reports `ollama:bge-m3 ✓ 1024 dims, DB aligned`. (Lost on `gbrain upgrade`.)

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.42.42.0
- OS: macOS 26.4.1 (build 25E253), arm64 (Apple Silicon)
- Bun version: 1.3.14
- Database: PGLite (local, `--pglite`)
- Ollama: 0.30.7
- Embedding endpoint: `http://localhost:11434/v1` (Ollama OpenAI-compatible)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**

> Note: captured *after* applying the local workaround, so embedding checks read healthy (`ollama:bge-m3 ✓ 1024 dims`). On a stock install the brain cannot reach this state for bge-m3 — see Steps to reproduce.

```json
{
  "schema_version": 2,
  "status": "warnings",
  "health_score": 65,
  "brain_checks_score": 85,
  "category_scores": { "brain": 85, "skill": 95, "ops": 95, "meta": 90 },
  "checks": [
    { "name": "connection", "status": "ok", "message": "Connected, 46 pages", "category": "ops" },
    { "name": "embeddings", "status": "ok", "message": "100% coverage, 0 missing", "category": "brain" },
    { "name": "embedding_provider", "status": "ok", "message": "ollama:bge-m3 ✓ 184ms, 1024 dims, DB aligned", "category": "brain" },
    { "name": "embedding_column_registry", "status": "ok", "message": "Registry healthy: 2 columns (embedding, embedding_image); active='embedding'", "category": "brain" },
    { "name": "embedding_width_consistency", "status": "ok", "message": "Schema width (1024d) matches gateway embedding_dimensions", "category": "brain" },
    { "name": "ze_embedding_health", "status": "ok", "message": "Configured embedding model \"ollama:bge-m3\" is not ZeroEntropy — skip.", "category": "ops" },
    { "name": "schema_version", "status": "ok", "message": "Version 116 (latest: 116)", "category": "meta" },
    { "name": "pgvector", "status": "warn", "message": "Could not check pgvector extension", "category": "ops" },
    { "name": "jsonb_integrity", "status": "warn", "message": "Could not check JSONB integrity", "category": "brain" },
    { "name": "brain_score", "status": "warn", "message": "Brain score 45/100 (embed 35/35, links 0/25, timeline 0/15, orphans 0/15, dead-links 10/10)", "category": "brain" },
    { "name": "embed_staleness", "status": "ok", "message": "No stale chunks", "category": "meta" },
    { "name": "search_mode", "status": "ok", "message": "Mode: conservative (no per-key overrides — mode bundle is canonical).", "category": "ops" }
  ],
  "top_issues": [
    { "name": "brain_score", "status": "warn", "tier": "symptom", "fix": "Brain score 45/100 (embed 35/35, links 0/25, timeline 0/15, orphans 0/15, dead-links 10/10)" },
    { "name": "jsonb_integrity", "status": "warn", "tier": "symptom", "fix": "Could not check JSONB integrity" },
    { "name": "pgvector", "status": "warn", "tier": "symptom", "fix": "Could not check pgvector extension" }
  ]
}
```
title: Ollama provider hardcodes 768d, rejects 1024d models (bge-m3, mxbai-embed-large)

body:
## Problem

gbrain has embeddings and pgvector similarity query (`<=>`), but no first-class command to **cluster** near-duplicate pages or **merge** them. Large corpora accumulate many pages that restate the same idea from different sources/angles. Today the only options are manual review or deleting duplicates — both lose information.

## Proposed capability

A `cluster` + `merge` workflow that turns redundancy into a salience signal instead of throwing it away.

### 1. `gbrain cluster`
- Group pages by cosine similarity above a threshold (default ~0.86) using existing pgvector embeddings.
- Scope by slug prefix / type filter.
- Connected-components or greedy agglomeration above threshold.
- Optional LLM **distinctness guard**: confirm cluster members are true duplicates of one mechanism vs. genuinely distinct ideas; split clusters that aren't. Similarity proposes, judgment disposes.
- Output clusters as JSON (member slugs, pairwise similarity, suggested canonical).

### 2. `gbrain merge`
Fold a cluster into a single canonical page (highest-quality member), preserving every framing:
- `merge_count` (int) in frontmatter — number of absorbed pages. **This is a salience metric**: a high count means the idea was independently re-derived many times, i.e. it matters.
- `backlinks` (list) — each absorbed page's source + the specific angle it contributed (`{source, angle, date}`).
- A `## Facets` body section — canonical statement up top, then short per-source variations beneath.
- Absorbed pages **moved** to a `_merged/` tree (reversible) rather than deleted, so the operation is fully undoable.

### 3. Emergent promotion
- When `merge_count` crosses a threshold, auto-promote the page from atom tier to concept tier. This makes the atom/concept boundary **emergent from usage** rather than hand-drawn — a recurring, heavily-referenced idea earns concepthood.

## Why
- Deletion is irreversible and discards the frequency signal.
- Merge-with-backlinks keeps every framing, captures "this idea keeps recurring" as a first-class metric, makes concept promotion data-driven, and is reversible.

## Notes
- Reuse existing embeddings; no re-embed needed beyond `embed --stale`.
- Thresholds should be knobs, calibrated on the actual similarity/merge_count distribution, not hardcoded.
- pgvector cosine: `SELECT slug, embedding <=> $1 AS dist FROM pages WHERE slug LIKE $2 ORDER BY dist LIMIT k;` (similarity = 1 - dist).
title: Feature: semantic clustering + dedup-by-merge command

body:
## Summary

`reindex --markdown` (and the import embed path it shares) hangs deterministically when it reaches a very large page of space-less CJK text. That page's embedding batch never returns, and because the sliding concurrency pool only advances when an in-flight batch completes, the whole pool stalls — every worker idles on the one stuck batch. In our run this froze all workers (~40 min, reproduced at concurrency 8/6/3) until killed. The embedding provider is healthy throughout (normal per-query embeds ~1.6s; live search unaffected).

## Environment

- v0.42.x deployment, embeddings via a third-party HTTP provider.
- Trigger pages: research-class documents ≥ ~50 KB of CJK text (largest ~614 KB), no whitespace word boundaries.

## Evidence

- A full sweep re-chunked ~19.7k normal pages cleanly, but deterministically froze each time it reached one of ~235 very large CJK pages.
- Reproducible and page-specific (same pages every time); excluding those 235 lets the sweep finish with zero failures.
- Provider healthy during the freeze (ad-hoc query embeds ~1.6s).
- No abort/timeout fires — the import embed call appears to lack an abort-signal/timeout, so one stuck batch hangs indefinitely and the sliding pool (batch size ~100) never drains.

## Expected vs actual

- **Expected:** a slow/oversized embedding batch should time out / abort (fail-safe), so one bad page can't starve the pool.
- **Actual:** no abort-timeout on the import/reindex embed path → a giant-CJK page hangs the batch indefinitely and freezes all concurrent workers.

## Possible directions

1. Thread an abort-signal/timeout through the import/reindex embed call (as the query path already bounds its embeds), so a stuck batch aborts and the page is retried/skipped instead of hanging the pool.
2. Make the embed batch size configurable (appears hardcoded ~100) and/or cap by total bytes, so one huge page can't form an unbounded batch.
3. Optionally split oversized chunks (or bound per-chunk bytes) before the embedding request.

## Note

`GBRAIN_PAGE_BLOCK_BYTES` can skip embedding for very large pages, but that removes them from the search surface (destructive) — not acceptable when the goal is to make large pages *retrievable*. The deterministic hang (not mere slowness) is the core issue.
title: reindex/import embed path hangs deterministically on very large (≥50KB) CJK pages — missing abort-timeout starves the embed pool

body:
## Summary

`synthesize_concepts` writes `concepts/` pages via `putPage`, but those pages end up with **0 `content_chunks` and a NULL embedding** — so they are invisible to `query` (vector retrieval) and effectively unreachable. This is despite `src/core/search/source-boost.ts` assigning `'concepts/': 1.3`, i.e. the retrieval layer is explicitly designed to rank concept pages *above* bulk content. In practice that boost is dead — there are no concept chunks to boost.

The CHANGELOG's own worked example shows the intended behavior (`concepts/widget-pattern` → "returned (rank 1)"), but a deployment whose concept pages are never embedded can never reproduce it.

## Environment

Observed on a v0.42.x deployment. Verified the gap still exists on `main` at **v0.42.42**: `src/core/cycle/synthesize-concepts.ts` still only calls `engine.putPage('concepts/${title}', …)` with no chunk/embed step, and the header comment still reads `// … dedup-by-embedding-similarity ship in v0.42+.`

## Evidence (deployment with ~1,700 concept pages)

- `SELECT count(*) FROM content_chunks c JOIN pages p ON p.id = c.page_id WHERE p.slug LIKE 'concepts/%'` → **0**
- Every `concepts/` page has `embedding_signature IS NULL`.
- A 20-question retrieval probe (questions chosen to match concept topics) returned a concept page in top-5 in **0/20** cases.
- FTS `search` also fails to surface them even on near-verbatim title queries: the pages *do* have `search_vector` populated, but being short they are out-ranked by longer pages and fall below the result limit.
- **Control:** `distilled/` and `dream-cycle-summaries/` pages (also dream-generated) *do* have chunks. So the `dream_generated` anti-loop guard is not the cause — it is specific to the `synthesize_concepts` (and `extract_atoms`) write path.

## Expected vs actual

- **Expected:** concept pages — the curated "distilled framework" output the schema treats as first-class and `source-boost.ts` prioritizes at 1.3× — should be in the retrieval surface (chunked + embedded) so `query`/`search` can return them.
- **Actual:** `synthesize_concepts` writes them but they are never chunked/embedded, so they are unreachable via the primary retrieval paths.

## Possible directions (maintainers to weigh)

1. Have `synthesize_concepts` enqueue chunking/embedding for the concept pages it writes. This is the natural home and it composes with the planned v0.42+ embedding-similarity dedup, which will need concept embeddings anyway.
2. Alternatively, document that concepts need a separate `reindex`/`embed` pass, and add a `doctor` check that flags "concept pages present but unchunked" so operators know to run it.

## Note

`atoms/` are intentionally substrate (no `source-boost` entry) and presumably should *not* be embedded — this report is specifically about `concepts/`.
title: synthesize_concepts writes concept pages that are never chunked/embedded → unreachable by retrieval despite source-boost 1.3×

body:
## Problem

The `gbrain extract all --ner --source db` command returns 0 results for `gbrain-base-v2` because the pack's `link_types` lack `inference.regex` fields.

In `src/core/extract-ner.ts` (line ~110), the NER path has a hard check:

```typescript
const hasRegex = pack.manifest.link_types.some(
    (lt) => lt.inference && typeof lt.inference === 'object' && 'regex' in lt.inference,
);
if (!hasRegex) return { pages: 0, created: 0, pack_unavailable: true };
```

This means **NER is completely disabled** for `gbrain-base-v2` users, because the bundled pack doesn't include any `inference` blocks.

## Context

- **Brain**: 223 pages, 28 entity pages (person/company)
- **Current link coverage**: 14% (35 links from frontmatter wikilinks only)
- **Target**: 70%
- **Timeline**: Fixed (0 → 147 after format correction)
- **Embedding**: ✅ 100%
- **Overall health**: 65/100 → 50/100 brain score

The existing `inferLinkType` function in `link-extraction.ts` already has production-quality regex matchers for `founded`, `invested_in`, `advises`, `works_at`, and `mentions` — but NER bypasses them entirely when the pack lacks `inference.regex`.

## Suggested Fix

Option A (preferred): Let NER fall through to the legacy `inferLinkType` when no pack regex is found, instead of returning 0. Update `src/core/extract-ner.ts` to call the legacy matcher as a fallback.

Option B: Add `inference.regex` blocks to `gbrain-base-v2.yaml` for the 4 typed verbs (works_at, founded, invested_in, advises) that already have production matchers in `link-extraction.ts`.

Option C: Allow home-config packs (`~/.gbrain/schema-packs/`) to be discovered and activated, enabling users to add their own regex patterns.

## Impact

This single check blocks the entire NER pipeline for all `gbrain-base-v2` users, preventing automated entity link extraction from the markdown body content.
title: [BUG] NER produces 0 links for gbrain-base-v2 — missing inference.regex hard check

body:
**What happened?**

`gbrain onboard --apply --auto --max-usd N --target-score N` returns `Brain at score X/100, target Y/100. Nothing to do.` even when `gbrain onboard --check --explain` lists several auto-eligible onboard-check recommendations (e.g. `extract-ner`, `extract-timeline-from-meetings`). The check path threads `extraRemediations` into the planner correctly; the apply path never receives them.

Diagnosis:

- `commands/onboard.ts:105-106` gathers `extraRemediations` via `runAllOnboardChecks(engine).flatMap(r => r.remediations)`.
- `commands/onboard.ts:109` (the `--check` branch) passes `{ targetScore, extraRemediations }` to `computeRemediationPlan`.
- `commands/onboard.ts:129` (the `--apply --auto` branch) calls `runRemediation(engine, { targetScore, maxUsd }, hooks)` with no `extraRemediations` field.
- Downstream, `core/remediation/run.ts:71` builds the pre-flight plan via `computeRemediationPlan(engine, { targetScore })` with no extras, and `core/remediation/run.ts:90` calls `computeRecommendations(initialHealth, ctx)` with the default empty `extraRemediations = []`. Only generic brain_score remediations (sync, embed, etc.) reach the runner. For a brain that's clean on those (0 stale pages, 0 missing embeddings), `recs.length === 0` and the runner fires `onNothingToDo`.

`RemediationOpts` (`core/remediation/types.ts`) has no `extraRemediations` field, so the gap is structural — even if the caller wanted to pass extras through `runRemediation`, the type signature does not accept them.

**What did you expect?**

`onboard --apply --auto` should submit jobs for every auto-eligible recommendation that `onboard --check --explain` would surface, scoped by `--max-usd` and `--target-score`. The two paths should agree on which remediations are in scope.

**Steps to reproduce (deterministic)**

1. `gbrain init` against a fresh PGLite (or Postgres) engine.
2. `gbrain sources add <id> --path <dir-with-markdown> --federated` against a corpus where `entity_link_coverage` and `timeline_coverage` will report below target after sync (e.g. markdown with meeting notes but no extracted entities or timeline).
3. `gbrain sync --source <id>`: drives `embed_coverage` to 100% but leaves entity/timeline coverage below target.
4. `gbrain onboard --check --explain`: lists `extract-ner` / `extract-timeline-from-meetings` (or similar) as auto-eligible.
5. `gbrain onboard --apply --auto --max-usd 5 --target-score 70`: prints `Brain at score X/100, target Y/100. Nothing to do.` instantly. No jobs queued in `minion_jobs`.

Observed on master at `4ee530f3` (v0.42.42.0), reproduced against both PGLite and Postgres 16 + pgvector 0.8.2.

**Environment**

- gbrain version: master at `4ee530f3` (v0.42.42.0)
- OS: Linux
- Bun: 1.3.14
- Database: PGLite + Postgres 16 / pgvector 0.8.2 (both reproduce)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**

Not material to the bug. The plan envelope returned by `computeRemediationPlan` is the same shape; the bug is that `runRemediation` calls the planner without the extras and stops before any work is dispatched. `doctor` itself is unaffected.

**Proposed fix**

Add `extraRemediations?: RemediationStep[]` to `RemediationOpts`, read it in `runRemediation` (`core/remediation/run.ts`), and thread it into both `computeRemediationPlan` (line 71) and `computeRecommendations` (line 90). Then update `commands/onboard.ts:129` to pass the already-gathered `extraRemediations` the same way line 109 (the `--check` branch) already does. Three-file, ~15-line patch.

A regression test would feed `runRemediation` an extra step that the brain_score generator never emits (e.g. `extract-ner`) and assert it appears in `result.submitted`.

**Adjacent observation (separate issue if confirmed worth)**

`onboard --apply --auto` has an undocumented prereq: a worker (`gbrain jobs work` or `gbrain autopilot`) must be running to claim queued jobs. With the patch above landed, the apply path correctly queues jobs; with no worker `attempts_started` stays 0 and onboard times out at 540s (`error: timeout after 540007ms waiting for job 1`). The failure mode reads as a broken handler. A heartbeat check on `minion_jobs` emitting a one-line hint when no row has `attempts_started > 0` within K seconds, or a docs touch in `gbrain onboard --help`, would close the gap. Happy to file a follow-up.

Paired PR: #2161.
title: gbrain onboard --apply --auto returns "Nothing to do" while --check --explain shows auto-eligible recommendations

body:
**What happened?**

`sources.config.{include_globs,exclude_globs}` had storage but no readers. `gbrain sources add` had no flag to populate them, and the two surfaces that walk a source directory (`gbrain sync` and `gbrain lint`) both ignored the field. The internal sync classifier in `src/core/sync.ts` (`SyncableOptions`, `unsyncableReason`, the `'include-glob-miss'` / `'exclude-glob-hit'` reason codes shipped in v0.41.13 / #1433) supported include + exclude globs from day one; the CLI surface did not. So an Obsidian-style vault with authoring scaffolding under `Templates/`, third-party documentation dumps under `Resources/api-specs/`, or older content under `Archive/` saw every page chunked, embedded, surfaced in search results, AND emitted as lint findings the user had no way to opt out of.

The workarounds all break the host tool: renaming `Templates/` confuses Obsidian's "create note from template" flow, hiding it with a dot-prefix breaks the template picker, and `.gitignore` is the wrong place (the file is meant to be in the vault, not the index).

On a mixed-content vault, a no-filter `gbrain lint --dry-run` reports an order of magnitude more findings than the user actually wants to triage; the gap is entirely from subtrees the user already considers out of scope (vendor API specs, historical scratch, scaffolding templates).

**What did you expect?**

`gbrain sources add vault --path ~/vault --exclude 'Templates/**' --exclude '.smart-env/**'` should persist those globs so every subsequent `gbrain sync` AND `gbrain lint <vault>` honors them. Same for the symmetric `--include 'people/**'` case (CRM vaults where only a subtree is brain material). The cycle's lint phase, which calls into the same path, should pick up the same scope without per-invocation flag plumbing.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `gbrain init` against a fresh engine.
2. `gbrain sources add vault --path /some/obsidian-vault --federated`.
3. Add an `obsidian-vault/Templates/Meeting.md` file.
4. `gbrain sync --source vault`.
5. `gbrain search "meeting template"`: the template's pages appear in results despite being scaffolding.
6. `gbrain lint --dry-run /some/obsidian-vault`: the template's pages appear in the issue list.

There is no CLI surface today to opt out of indexing or linting `Templates/**` short of renaming the directory or carrying a fork patch.

**Environment**

- gbrain version: 0.42.42.0 (reproduces against current `master` at 4ee530f3).
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu) and macOS both reproduce.
- Bun version: 1.3.14.
- Database: self-hosted Postgres 16, also reproduces on PGLite.

**Proposed fix**

Two-half change with no schema migration:

1. **Write side (`gbrain sources add`).** New repeatable `--include <glob>` / `--exclude <glob>` flags. Values land in `sources.config.include_globs` / `sources.config.exclude_globs` arrays at INSERT time.

2. **Read side, surface A (`gbrain sync`).** `commands/sync.ts` reads those arrays at the three sync entry points (`--all` fan-out's `runOne`, single-source CLI path, `syncOneSource`) and threads them into `SyncOpts.include` / `SyncOpts.exclude`. `performSync`'s filter at `commands/sync.ts:1454` constructs `syncOpts` with all three knobs and passes them into `isSyncable` / `unsyncableReason`.

3. **Read side, surface B (`gbrain lint`).** `runLintCore` lifts the same `sources.config.{include,exclude}_globs` arrays for the matching source row, threads them through `collectPages`. New `--include <glob>` / `--exclude <glob>` flags (repeatable, parallel to the sync surface) for ad-hoc scoping. The cycle's lint phase already passes `opts.engine`, so it gets auto-resolution without further wiring. Explicit caller-supplied flags always win over the source-row lift.

4. **Shared matcher.** Export `matchesAnyGlob` from `src/core/sync.ts` so lint reuses the exact same `globToRegex` semantics sync ships, rather than re-declaring the matcher. Keeps the two surfaces in lockstep.

Adjusting globs on an existing source is deliberately out of scope. The existing `gbrain sources set-cr-mode` is the precedent for a future narrow-verb `sources set-globs` surface; `UPDATE sources SET config = config || '...'::jsonb` is the documented escape hatch in the meantime.

PR ready for review: #2157.
title: sources: add --include / --exclude glob filters honored by sync AND lint

body:
**What happened?**

`parseMarkdown` (in `src/core/markdown.ts`) raises a `MISSING_CLOSE` validation error on a file whose YAML frontmatter
starts with one or more `#` comment lines, even when the closing `---` is present. The cause is the scanner at lines
~219-248: it walks the lines after the opening `---` and records the first `^#{1,6}\s`-shaped line as
`headingBeforeClose`. When the closing fence is later found, the scanner still flags the recorded "heading" as a
MISSING_CLOSE finding with the message `Heading at line N found inside frontmatter zone (closing --- comes after)`. The
line is a perfectly valid YAML comment, not a markdown heading.

YAML permits `#` comments anywhere outside string scalars, so a leading-`#` line inside the fence is a comment by
definition. The heading-shape heuristic was meant to catch unclosed frontmatter; using it inside a fence that DOES close
is the false-positive.

**What did you expect?**

The parser should accept the file (no `MISSING_CLOSE` finding) when the closing `---` is present, regardless of `#`
comment lines that appear between the fences.

**Steps to reproduce**

Create a markdown file with YAML comment lines at the top of its frontmatter:

```markdown
---
# Research Template
# This file serves as a template for all research findings

research_id: "R19"
title: "iOS App Clip security limitations"
---

body
```

Run `bun test --bail test/markdown-validation.test.ts` against a test that calls `parseMarkdown(md, undefined, {
validate: true })` on the content above and asserts the errors list does not contain `MISSING_CLOSE`. With current
master at `4ee530f3` (v0.42.42.0), the assertion fails. The errors list contains a `MISSING_CLOSE` with the message
`Heading at line 2 found inside frontmatter zone (closing --- comes after)`.

**Environment**

- gbrain version: master at `4ee530f3` (v0.42.42.0)
- OS: Linux
- Bun: 1.3.14
- Database: not relevant (the bug is in pure string parsing; no engine state involved)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**

Not material, because the bug is in `src/core/markdown.ts:collectValidationErrors` and reproduces against unit tests
without any engine state. The bug-report template's doctor-output paste block is included for completeness:

```text
(omitted — reproducible without a connected engine)
```

**Adjacent context**

- v0.37.5.0 (#1229) fixed the same parser's `NESTED_QUOTES` validator to stop flagging valid YAML: direct precedent for
  this fix's shape. The proposed fix in the paired PR follows the same "stop the false positive without weakening the
  genuine-error path" pattern.
- #1074 (`frontmatter audit: false-positive MISSING_OPEN on convention plain-markdown files`) is the closest open
  neighbor: same parser, different error code, same class of false-positive. The two fixes are independent and
  non-conflicting.

**Proposed fix**

When walking lines between the opening `---` and the closing `---`, do NOT track a `headingBeforeClose`. Once the
closing `---` is found, content between the fences is YAML by definition; `#` lines are comments. The heading hint
remains valuable in the genuine-missing-close branch (still surface the first heading-shaped line so the error message
points the user at the right line). Paired PR: #2153.
title: parseMarkdown flags MISSING_CLOSE on valid YAML # comment lines inside the frontmatter fence

body:
**What happened?**

On a brain that is below target AND cannot reach target autonomously, `gbrain doctor --remediation-plan` prints three
lines in sequence, two of which contradict each other:

```text
Brain score: 45/100 → target 90
Target unreachable: max with autonomous remediation is 70/100.
No remediations needed. Brain is at target.
```

The first two lines are correct (45 current, 70 ceiling, 90 target). The third is wrong: target is 90, brain is 45,
several remediations ARE applicable (they just can't reach the target without manual prereq config). The bad line makes
the operator think the brain is healthy when it is not, and it hides the real next step.

Diagnosis: `runRemediationPlan` (in `src/commands/doctor.ts` around line 7600+) prints `Target unreachable: max with
autonomous remediation is ${plan.max_reachable_score}/100` when `plan.target_unreachable` is true, then unconditionally
falls through to the empty-plan branch which prints `No remediations needed. Brain is at target.` The two messages were
never gated against each other.

**What did you expect?**

When the plan is empty AND the brain is below target, do NOT print the "Brain is at target" line. The unreachable
message above is the user-facing explanation; the `Blocked checks` block below the plan surfaces the manual gap.

**Steps to reproduce (deterministic)**

1. `gbrain init` against a fresh PGLite (or Postgres) engine.
2. `gbrain sources add <id> --path <dir-with-markdown> --federated`.
3. `gbrain sync --source <id>`: drives `embed_coverage` to 100% but leaves `link_density = 0` and `timeline_coverage =
   0`.
4. `gbrain doctor --remediation-plan`: prints the three contradictory lines above.

Observed on master at `4ee530f3` (v0.42.42.0), Postgres 16 + pgvector 0.8.2, brain_score reported as 45/100 (per the
formula in `postgres-engine.ts:4715`: 35 embed + 0 link_density + 0 timeline + 0 no_orphans + 10 no_dead_links).

**Environment**

- gbrain version: master at `4ee530f3` (v0.42.42.0)
- OS: Linux
- Bun: 1.3.14
- Database: PGLite + Postgres 16 / pgvector 0.8.2 (both reproduce)

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**

Not material to the bug, because the JSON-mode short-circuit in `runRemediationPlan` returns the plan envelope verbatim
and is unaffected. The bug lives in the human-render branch only.

**Adjacent context**

This is one of a handful of doctor diagnostics that quietly mislead operators. Others include `schema_version` reporting
`ok` when the DB is AHEAD of the client, `reranker_health` reporting `ok` while every rerank fails on auth, and
`orphan_ratio` recommending a fix that backlink counting structurally excludes. Same systemic class; each instance is a
small, scoped fix.

**Proposed fix**

Gate the "Brain is at target" line on `brain_score_current >= targetScore`. When the plan is empty AND the brain is
below target, leave the unreachable line as the explanation and let the `Blocked checks` section below surface the
manual gap.

A small refactor to make the renderer testable in isolation is included in the paired PR:
`renderRemediationPlanLines(plan, targetScore): string[]` is exported alongside `runRemediationPlan`. The latter joins
the lines and prints them through `console.log`, so behavior is byte-identical for every case other than the fixed
contradiction. The renderer extraction makes the regression test pure-functional (no `console.log` spy needed).

Paired PR: #2151.
title: gbrain doctor --remediation-plan prints contradictory verdict — "Target unreachable" followed by "Brain is at target"

body:
## Summary

Pages that yield **zero atoms** are rediscovered by `extract_atoms` forever. Idempotency is keyed on atom rows (`NOT EXISTS … atom.frontmatter->>'source_hash' = substring(p.content_hash, 1, 16)`), so a page the LLM judges un-atomizable leaves no marker — it re-enters the discovery window every run.

Two production consequences (v0.42.37.0, postgres engine, pack `gbrain-everything`):

1. **`--drain` wedges with a false `no_progress`.** Once the head of the discovery window (`ORDER BY p.updated_at DESC`, LIMIT 50) is mostly zero-yield pages, every batch reports `+0 atom(s)` and the drain loop stops (`stopped: no_progress`) while `remaining` sits frozen — ours froze at 2,013 after draining one source cleanly:
   ```
   [drain] batch 1: +4 atom(s), ~2017 remaining
   [drain] batch 2: +4 atom(s), ~2015 remaining
   [drain] batch 3: +5 atom(s), ~2015 remaining
   [drain] extracted 13 atom(s) across 3 batch(es); 2013 remaining (stopped: window)
   [drain] batch 1: +0 atom(s), ~2013 remaining
   [drain] extracted 0 atom(s) across 1 batch(es); 2013 remaining (stopped: no_progress)
   ```
2. **Every nightly cycle re-spends budget on the same pages.** Our last nightly: `extract_atoms: 2 atoms from 0/0 transcripts + 41/50 pages` — ~40 zero-yield pages re-processed (real Haiku calls), and the same pages will be back tomorrow.

## Proposed fix (PR to follow)

- On a **successful** LLM call that parses to zero atoms, stamp the source page: `frontmatter.atoms_scan_hash = substring(content_hash, 1, 16)`. LLM failures do NOT stamp, so transient errors stay retryable. Mirrors the existing per-page stamp precedent (`setPageEmbeddingSignature`).
- Discovery + `countExtractAtomsBacklog` exclude pages where `atoms_scan_hash` matches the *current* content hash prefix — content edits re-eligibilize the page, mirroring the atom-row staleness semantics.
- Drain `no_progress` only fires when the backlog count genuinely didn't shrink (a zero-atom batch that tombstones pages IS progress).

Related: #2123 / #2124 (extract_atoms → synthesize_concepts wiring); independent bug, same module.
title: extract_atoms: zero-yield pages are rediscovered forever — wedges --drain (false no_progress) and re-spends nightly budget

body:
## Summary

Authenticated HTTP MCP writes and reads still fall back to `source_id: default` even when the caller token is source-bound and the brain default source is changed to `personal` with `gbrain sources default personal`.

This makes source-bound collector writes hard to validate: `put_page` and `add_timeline_entry` succeed, but `get_page` reports the written page under `default`, and `query` with `source_id: default` also returns it.

## Environment

- gbrain version: `0.41.26.0`
- Runtime: GKE serve pod using `gbrain serve --http` behind an authenticated MCP URL
- `GBRAIN_HOME=/data`
- Database-backed brain with sources including:
  - `default` federated, 2 pages
  - `personal` federated, large existing corpus
- Auth token metadata only: OAuth client-credentials style bearer with `read write admin` scope. Token value was not printed or copied.

## Tool schema observation

`tools/list` shows:

- `put_page` accepts `slug`, `content`, `ingested_via`, `source_kind`, `source_uri`; no `source_id` argument.
- `add_timeline_entry` accepts `slug`, `date`, `summary`, `detail`, `source`; no `source_id` argument.
- `query` accepts `source_id`.
- `get_page` accepts no `source_id`.

So an MCP collector cannot route a write to a non-default source per call.

## Reproduction

1. Confirm source state:

```sh
gbrain sources list
```

2. Set default source:

```sh
gbrain sources default personal
# Output: Default source set to "personal".
```

3. Through authenticated HTTP MCP, call `put_page` for an existing test slug with frontmatter containing `source_id: "personal"`.

4. Through the same authenticated HTTP MCP token, call `add_timeline_entry` for that slug.

5. Read back through MCP:

```json
{
  "get_page": {"reported_source_id": "default"},
  "query_with_source_id_default": ["<test-slug>"],
  "query_with_source_id_personal": ["<test-slug>"]
}
```

The page is still reported as `source_id: default` after the default-source change and second write.

## Expected

One of these should be true:

- source-bound auth / allowedSources routes `put_page`, `add_timeline_entry`, and `get_page` to the bound source, or
- `gbrain sources default personal` changes the fallback used by the HTTP MCP server for authenticated requests, or
- MCP write/read tools expose an explicit `source_id` option for deterministic collectors.

## Actual

- `put_page` and `add_timeline_entry` succeed.
- `get_page` returns the page with top-level `source_id: default`.
- `query` scoped to `default` returns the test slug.
- Re-running after `gbrain sources default personal` still reports `source_id: default`.

## Impact

Deterministic collectors that are supposed to write into a source-bound personal corpus cannot prove source ownership through MCP. This can also create duplicate-looking pages across `default` and `personal` when migration/pilot scripts are run.

## Notes

No credential values, bearer headers, private message content, or customer/person content are included in this report.
title: HTTP MCP source-bound writes still land in default source

body:
## Improvement opportunity

Add GitHub artifact attestations for the compiled release binaries produced by `.github/workflows/release.yml`.

## Evidence inspected

Repo-local evidence:

- `.github/workflows/release.yml:27-33` runs `bun build --compile` for `gbrain-darwin-arm64` and `gbrain-linux-x64`, then uploads those binaries as workflow artifacts.
- `.github/workflows/release.yml:39-47` downloads those artifacts and publishes the same binaries into the GitHub Release.
- `.github/workflows/release.yml:7-8` only grants `contents: write`. There is no `id-token: write` or `attestations: write` permission, and no `actions/attest` / `actions/attest-build-provenance` step.
- Repository workflow scan found pinned action usage and least-privilege read permissions in the test workflows, so this is a focused release-provenance gap rather than a broad Actions-hardening request.

Current baseline:

- GitHub artifact attestations establish where and how build artifacts were produced.
- GitHub's documented binary flow requires `id-token: write`, `contents: read`, `attestations: write`, and an `actions/attest@v4` step with `subject-path` pointed at the built binary.
- Reference: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/using-artifact-attestations/using-artifact-attestations-to-establish-provenance-for-builds

## Expected impact

Users downloading `gbrain-darwin-arm64` or `gbrain-linux-x64` from a release should be able to verify the binary's provenance with `gh attestation verify ... -R garrytan/gbrain`. That gives release consumers a machine-checkable link from the downloaded binary back to the repository's release workflow run, improving supply-chain integrity without changing the CLI runtime.

## Suggested fix

Keep this scoped to the release workflow:

1. Move the release workflow permissions to job-level least privilege:
   - `build`: `contents: read`, `id-token: write`, `attestations: write`
   - `release`: `contents: write`
2. After each `bun build --compile ... --outfile bin/${{ matrix.artifact }}` step, add an attestation step for `bin/${{ matrix.artifact }}`.
3. Pin the attestation action consistently with the rest of this repo's workflows. The existing workflows pin `actions/checkout`, `setup-bun`, `upload-artifact`, `download-artifact`, and `softprops/action-gh-release` by commit SHA.
4. Optionally add a short release note or README snippet showing:

```bash
gh attestation verify ./gbrain-linux-x64 -R garrytan/gbrain
gh attestation verify ./gbrain-darwin-arm64 -R garrytan/gbrain
```

## Duplicate check

I inspected the required open items with `gh` before filing:

- `gh issue list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state open --limit 200 --json number,title,body,url,labels,updatedAt`
  - 200 open issues inspected, covering #2140 through #1520 in the returned set.
- `gh pr list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state open --limit 200 --json number,title,body,url,headRefName,isDraft,updatedAt`
  - 200 open PRs inspected, covering #2135 through #1276 in the returned set.

Title/body keyword scans for the specific release-provenance terms found no covering open issue or PR:

- `artifact attestation`
- `artifact attestations`
- `attest-build-provenance`
- `actions/attest`
- `build provenance`
- `release provenance`
- `binary provenance`
- `SLSA`

I also searched all open GitHub issues/PRs through `gh api -X GET /search/issues` for the same terms, including `repo:garrytan/gbrain state:open "artifact attestation"`, `"attest-build-provenance"`, `"actions/attest"`, `"build provenance"`, `"release provenance"`, `SLSA`, and `"binary provenance"`; those searches returned no covering items.

## Commands/files inspected

- `.github/workflows/release.yml`
- `.github/workflows/test.yml`
- `.github/workflows/e2e.yml`
- `.github/workflows/heavy-tests.yml`
- `package.json`
- `gh issue list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state open --limit 200 --json number,title,body,url,labels,updatedAt`
- `gh pr list --repo garrytan/gbrain --state open --limit 200 --json number,title,body,url,headRefName,isDraft,updatedAt`
- `gh api -X GET /search/issues ...` duplicate searches listed above
- repo content search for `attestation|attest-build-provenance|actions/attest|SLSA|dependency-review|OSV|Semgrep|Scorecard`
title: Add artifact attestations for release binaries

body:
## Summary

Pages of a second, `federated: true` source are fully indexed (chunks + embeddings verified in Postgres) but **never appear in default search** — CLI and MCP alike. The `sources add` output promises otherwise:

> `federated: true — appears in cross-source default search`

Additionally, passing the `__all__` sentinel as `source_id` makes results from the **default** source disappear — it returns *only* the non-default source. So `__all__` can't serve as a workaround default (e.g. via `GBRAIN_SOURCE`) either.

Possibly related to the multi-source family #1747 / #1503 (operations silently targeting `default`).

## Setup

- postgres engine, observed on **0.42.26.0**; the relevant code is unchanged in 0.42.40.0 (`ecd6ae8`)
- source `default`: ~1700 pages
- source `slack`: added via `gbrain sources add slack --path /srv/.../slack-archive --federated`, synced via `gbrain sync --source slack` (2 pages, 2 chunks, embedded — verified via psql: `content_chunks` rows exist with `embedded_at` set, `chunk_text` contains the search terms)

## Repro

1. `gbrain search "<unique keyword that only exists in the secondary source>"` → 0 hits from the secondary source (only `source_id=default` results, tested up to limit 30)
2. `gbrain call search '{"query":"<same keyword>","source_id":"slack"}'` → the pages ARE returned as top hits (score 0.86) — index is fine
3. `gbrain call search '{"query":"<keyword matching many default pages>","source_id":"__all__"}'` → returns ONLY the 2 secondary-source pages; all default-source results are gone
4. `gbrain call get_chunks '{"slug":"<secondary-source slug>"}'` → `[]`, while `resolve_slugs` finds the slug and psql shows 1 embedded chunk

## Where it pins to default (0.42.40 source)

- `src/mcp/server.ts:49` — `sourceId: process.env.GBRAIN_SOURCE || 'default'` is applied to every operation context
- `src/core/search/hybrid.ts:876` — `sourceId: opts?.sourceId` threads it into SearchOpts
- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:1579-1586` (and the vector twin at ~1847) — `AND p.source_id = $n` hard-filters every search SQL
- `src/core/operations.ts:2467` — `get_chunks` gets the same ctx filter, hence `[]` for non-default slugs
- `loadSources(..., { federatedOnly: true })` (`src/core/sources-load.ts:44`) exists but appears to have no caller in the search path — nothing ever expands the default search to federated sources

## Expected

Either of:
1. Default search resolves `sourceIds = [ctx.sourceId, ...sources where config.federated === true]` so federated sources actually appear in cross-source default search (matching the `sources add` message), or
2. at minimum, a working `__all__` sentinel that includes the default source, so operators can opt in via `GBRAIN_SOURCE=__all__`.

Happy to test a fix — the two-source repro is easy to re-run on our side.
title: Federated sources never appear in default search; `__all__` sentinel drops default-source results

body:
## Summary

`take_proposals` inserts use `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` against a unique key that is effectively **per page** (`source_id, page_slug, content_hash, prompt_version`). When the extractor returns multiple claims for one page, the first INSERT succeeds and every subsequent claim from that page is silently discarded — no error, no log, no proposal row.

## Impact

Multi-claim pages (often the richest ones — session summaries, decision logs) are systematically reduced to a single proposal. The data loss is invisible: the cycle reports success and the page is cached as done, so the dropped claims are never re-extracted.

## Repro

1. Ingest a page that clearly contains 2+ distinct take-worthy claims.
2. Run propose_takes.
3. `SELECT count(*) FROM take_proposals WHERE page_slug = ...` -> 1 row, regardless of how many claims the LLM returned (visible in the cycle's LLM output/logs).

## Suggested fix

Include a claim discriminator (claim hash or ordinal) in the unique key, or switch to an upsert keyed on (page, claim_hash). Observed on gbrain 0.42.40.0.

## Related

Filed alongside two companion issues from the same cost-runaway investigation of the propose_takes loop: the zero-claim cache miss and the cycle budget config disconnect.
title: propose_takes: `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` on the per-page unique key silently drops claim #2+ for multi-claim pages

body:
## Summary

`gbrain config set cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd <n>` writes to the **DB config store**, but cycle phases load their budget via `loadConfig()`, which reads only the local `~/.gbrain/config.json` file + env. The CLI accepts the command and reports success, but the budget cap is never applied.

## Impact

This is a silent failure on a **cost-control** setting — the worst place for one. I set `budget_usd 0.001` via `config set` as an emergency off-switch during a runaway-spend incident (see companion issue "propose_takes: pages with zero extracted claims never get an idempotency cache row") and it gated nothing; the loop kept billing $3–4/day. The working fix was adding the flat key directly to the file:

```json
// ~/.gbrain/config.json
{ "cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd": 2 }
```

## Repro

1. `gbrain config set cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd 0.001`
2. Run a propose_takes cycle over enough pages to exceed $0.001.
3. Cycle does not stop at the budget; spend continues.
4. Add `"cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd": 0.001` to `~/.gbrain/config.json` -> cycle respects it.

## Suggested fix

Either have `loadConfig()` merge the DB config store, or have `config set` write cycle keys through to the file — or at minimum make `config set` error/warn when setting a key the runtime will never read. (Verified on gbrain 0.42.40.0.)
title: `gbrain config set cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd` silently has no effect — cycle phases only read the flat key from the config FILE

body:
## Summary

In `src/core/cycle/propose-takes.ts`, the idempotency cache row for a scanned page is only written as a side effect of inserting proposal rows into `take_proposals`. When the extractor finds **zero claims** on a page, no INSERT happens, so no cache row is created — and the page is re-sent to the LLM on **every** subsequent cycle, indefinitely.

## Impact

On a brain with many low-signal pages this dominates cycle cost. In my case (~v0.42.40.0, autopilot on a launchd schedule) this contributed to ~1,500 Sonnet calls/day re-scanning the same pages with zero persisted output — roughly $3–4/day of pure waste. Combined with the companion issue "`gbrain config set cycle.propose_takes.budget_usd` silently has no effect", there was no effective gate on the loop.

## Repro

1. Ingest pages with content the take extractor will find no claims in (boilerplate, receipts, short notes).
2. Run the propose_takes cycle twice.
3. Observe the same pages produce LLM calls on both runs (no cache hits), while pages that yielded >=1 proposal are correctly skipped on run 2.

## Workaround I'm running locally

Patch in `propose-takes.ts`: after a zero-claim extraction, insert a sentinel row (`status='rejected'`, claim text `"(sentinel: no claims found)"`) so the per-page cache key exists. Verified end-to-end: run 1 = 100 pages / 79 LLM calls / 0 errors; run 2 = 99/100 cache hits, ~$0. Steady-state cost went from $3–4/day to cents/day.

## Suggested fix

Write the cache/idempotency row unconditionally after a page is scanned (decoupled from proposal INSERTs), or insert an explicit `no_claims` marker row. Happy to PR if you'd take either approach.
title: propose_takes: pages with zero extracted claims never get an idempotency cache row — re-billed on every cycle forever

body:
## Summary

#2122 closes the **in-cycle** staleness gap: consolidate-written takes now feed the `recompute_emotional_weight` union, so pages that gain takes during a dream cycle get their weight recomputed. But the union only ever contains pages the *cycle* touched. Takes created **outside the cycle** on pages that are never synced or synthesized still leave `emotional_weight` stale (typically stuck at the column default of `0`) until someone runs a manual full walk: `gbrain dream --phase recompute_emotional_weight`.

Active takes are a direct input to `computeEmotionalWeight` (`src/core/cycle/emotional-weight.ts` — take density / avg take weight / holder ratio, alongside tags), so a page with takes but weight `0` is mis-ranked by every salience-boosted consumer until the full walk runs.

## The out-of-cycle take-creation paths

**1. `gbrain takes add <slug>`** — `cmdAdd` in `src/commands/takes.ts` writes the take row to the page file and mirrors it to the DB via `engine.addTakesBatch`, then returns. No emotional-weight recompute, no marker for a later one. If the page never changes on disk again, no future incremental cycle ever puts it in the union.

**2. `take_proposals` acceptance** — #2122's own investigation note is explicit that this is out of scope for the cycle phase:

> `propose_takes` does **not** need to feed the union: it only writes to the `take_proposals` queue; acceptance is manual and outside the cycle (D17).

Correct for the phase — but it means the acceptance path (however it is surfaced) inherits exactly the same gap as `takes add`: an accepted proposal materializes an active take on a page the recompute union will never see.

## Current behavior

`doctor`'s `salience_health` check (`src/commands/doctor.ts`, check 11a-3) already detects exactly this state — it runs the SQL that counts pages with active takes and `COALESCE(emotional_weight, 0) = 0` — but the WARN it emits tells the user to run the **full-brain walk**, even though the check just computed the exact offending set. On a large brain that's an O(brain) remedy for an O(flagged) problem, and the drift recurs every time another out-of-cycle take lands.

(Observed on an operator brain: a handful of pages re-flagged by `salience_health` after the full walk had already cleared an earlier batch — the new stragglers were created via the out-of-cycle path.)

## Proposed fix

Two complementary pieces; either alone closes most of the gap:

1. **`doctor --fix` auto-repair scoped to the flagged slugs.** `salience_health` extends its query to return the offending slugs (not just the count) and, under the existing `--fix` flag (`src/commands/doctor.ts` already has the auto-fix convention), calls `runPhaseRecomputeEmotionalWeight(engine, { affectedSlugs })`. The phase already supports exactly this incremental shape (`src/core/cycle/recompute-emotional-weight.ts` — `affectedSlugs` non-empty ⇒ only those pages, two SQL round-trips, no LLM). Targeted, cheap, and the WARN message can advertise it.

2. **Mark-on-write at the take-creation sites.** `cmdAdd` (and the proposal-acceptance path when takes materialize from the queue) either recomputes the single affected page inline — the computation is pure and deterministic, so this is one extra round-trip — or enqueues the slug so the next cycle's `recompute_emotional_weight` picks it up. This closes the window entirely instead of waiting for the next doctor run.

## Alternatives considered

- **Full walk every cycle** — correct but O(brain) per cycle; exactly the cost #2122's incremental union exists to avoid.
- **Doctor auto-fix only (no mark-on-write)** — acceptable, but leaves the page mis-ranked between take creation and the next `doctor --fix`; mark-on-write is a one-line-ish addition at each write site.

Happy to send a PR for either or both pieces once #2122 lands (piece 1 builds directly on its `affectedSlugs` plumbing).
title: takes created outside the dream cycle (takes add / take_proposals acceptance) never get emotional_weight recomputed — stale until a manual full walk (residual gap after #2122)

body:
## Summary

On a Postgres-backed brain running stock gbrain v0.42.40.0, `gbrain extract --stale --catch-up` can process a historical page but leave it stale when the page's `updated_at` predates `LINK_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS`.

The stale predicate includes:

`links_extracted_at IS NULL OR links_extracted_at < LINK_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS OR updated_at > links_extracted_at`

but the stale extractor stamps processed pages with the row's read `updated_at_iso`. If that timestamp is older than the current extractor version watermark, the version arm remains true after a successful extraction pass.

## Why this matters

A deterministic maintenance loop can repeatedly report pages as needing extraction even after `extract --stale --catch-up` succeeds. This makes it hard to distinguish real missed extraction work from pages that are only stale because of the extractor version watermark.

## Expected behavior

After `extract --stale` successfully processes a page, the extraction watermark should clear both freshness conditions:

- it should remain safe for concurrent edits, so a page edited after the read still re-extracts later
- it should also clear the extractor-version condition for historical pages whose `updated_at` is older than `LINK_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS`

A possible approach is to stamp processed stale pages at `max(read_updated_at, LINK_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS)` rather than always stamping at `read_updated_at`.

## Observed code path

In stock v0.42.40.0:

- `LINK_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS` is defined in `src/core/link-extraction.ts`
- stale detection uses `countStalePagesForExtraction` / `buildStalePagesWhere` in `src/core/postgres-engine.ts`
- `extractStaleFromDB` in `src/commands/extract.ts` pushes `extractedAt: page.updated_at_iso` for processed pages

## Notes

I first opened a draft PR while investigating, then closed it because an issue is the more appropriate first step for this project.
title: extract --stale can keep old pages stale after extractor version bump

body:
## Summary

`extract_atoms` never writes the `concepts` frontmatter field that `synthesize_concepts` consumes, so the atoms → concepts pipeline is broken end-to-end: every cycle reports `synthesize_concepts: skipped — no atoms with concept refs`, no matter how many atoms accumulate.

## Evidence (v0.42.37.0 / current master)

**The consumer documents that the producer should stamp the field** — `src/core/cycle/synthesize-concepts.ts` header:

> 2. Group by `concepts:` frontmatter field on each atom (when the Haiku 3-check from extract_atoms decides "this atom is about concept X", it stamps the field).

and consumes ONLY that field (`synthesize-concepts.ts:92`):

```ts
concept_refs: r.frontmatter!.concepts!,
```

with atoms filtered by `Array.isArray(r.frontmatter?.concepts)`.

**The producer never stamps it** — `src/core/cycle/extract-atoms.ts` writes `type` / `atom_type` / `source_hash` / `source_slug` / `source_quote` / `lesson` / `virality_score` / `emotional_register` / `extracted_at` / `extracted_by`, but `grep -n "concepts" src/core/cycle/extract-atoms.ts` has zero hits in the frontmatter write path (page path and transcript path both).

## Reproduction (production brain, postgres engine, pack `gbrain-everything`)

1. Run cycles until atoms accumulate — we have **696 page-derived atoms** (`extracted_by: extract_atoms-v0.41.2.1`).
2. `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pages WHERE type='atom' AND frontmatter ? 'concepts'` → **0**.
3. Every `gbrain dream` reports `synthesize_concepts: skipped — no atoms with concept refs`.

## The consumer side works once fed

We validated the consumer by stamping `concepts` externally (1-3 kebab-case shared-vocabulary topic labels per atom, batch-labeled with one LLM call per 40 atoms): the very next `gbrain dream --phase synthesize_concepts` wrote **33 concept pages (T2=7, T3=26) from 60 stamped atoms**, $0.044, zero failures. So the fix is purely on the extractor side.

## Proposed fix (PR to follow)

Add `concepts` to the extractor's per-atom JSON contract — `"concepts": 1-3 kebab-case topic labels, reuse labels already seen in this run"` — validate kebab shape, and stamp the field in the same frontmatter write. One extra field in the existing extraction call; no new LLM round-trip. The shared-vocabulary instruction matters: labels must cluster (synthesize_concepts only materializes groups of ≥2), so the prompt should bias reuse-over-coinage.

Happy to send the PR — we're running this shape in production via an external backfill script and the tiering/synthesis downstream behaves exactly as the v0.41 design comment describes.
title: extract_atoms never stamps frontmatter.concepts — synthesize_concepts permanently skips ("no atoms with concept refs")
Test runs pollute the operator's real ~/.gbrain/sync-failures.jsonl (audit-ledger fix 7e84ed7e didn't cover the sync-failures ledger)

body:
### Version

gbrain 0.42.40.0 (clone @ `ecd6ae87`), Postgres engine.

### Summary

`gbrain config get <key>` reads only the DB plane: `src/commands/config.ts:101` calls `engine.getConfig(key)` and reports not-found when that returns null. There is no file-plane fallback, even though the merged-read helper already exists (`loadConfigWithEngine` at `src/core/config.ts:501`). The result is the mirror image of the `config set anthropic_api_key` issue (filed separately): for keys that the runtime resolves from `~/.gbrain/config.json`, `set` writes a plane the runtime ignores, and `get` ignores the plane the runtime reads. The one CLI surface an operator uses to answer "what key is in effect?" can contradict actual runtime behavior in both directions.

### Repro

1. Postgres engine. Put `"anthropic_api_key": "<your-key>"` in `~/.gbrain/config.json` (the runtime-effective location; verify with `cd /tmp && gbrain think "Reply with the single word: pong"` succeeding with no env key).
2. Ensure no DB-plane row for the key (`gbrain config unset anthropic_api_key` if one exists).
3. `gbrain config get anthropic_api_key`

### Observed

Not-found / unset -- while the runtime happily resolves and uses the file-plane key (step 1's `think` probe succeeds from a keyless cwd).

### Expected

`config get` reports the effective value with its provenance, e.g. resolve env > file plane > DB plane and print which plane supplied it (`anthropic_api_key = <redacted> (file: ~/.gbrain/config.json)`, ideally redacted for `*_api_key` keys). The plumbing for a merged read exists (`loadConfigWithEngine`); `get` just does not call it.

### Local mitigation

Operator runbook: never trust `config get` for key state; verify rotation with a real runtime call from a keyless cwd (`cd /tmp && gbrain think ...`). That works but is tribal knowledge -- the CLI answer should not contradict the runtime.

### Related

- Companion issue: `config set anthropic_api_key` writes the DB plane the runtime never reads for keys -- #2119.
- #1025 (closed) is the earlier generic report of set/show plane confusion.
title: `config get` reads only the DB plane -- a runtime-effective key in `~/.gbrain/config.json` reports not-found (`loadConfigWithEngine` exists but `get` does not use it)

body:
### Version

gbrain 0.42.40.0 (clone @ `ecd6ae87`), Postgres engine, macOS, launchd-supervised jobsworkers + autopilot.

### Summary

The runtime key path reads process env and the file plane only: `hasAnthropicKey()` (`src/core/ai/anthropic-key.ts:20`) checks `process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, then file-plane `loadConfig()` (`~/.gbrain/config.json`); the gateway env-mapping at `src/core/ai/build-gateway-config.ts:30` does the same. `gbrain config set anthropic_api_key ...` writes the **DB plane** (Postgres `config` table), which the runtime never consults for keys -- so for key configuration the command is a no-op that reports success.

The trap closes because the runtime's own error hint recommends it: when a provider check fails with no key, `src/core/ai/gateway.ts:2378` says

```
no Anthropic API key configured (set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or run: gbrain config set anthropic_api_key ...)
```

The source already knows about the split -- the comment at `src/core/config.ts:78-81` says capture settings are "Read via file-plane `loadConfig()` at process boot (NOT `gbrain config set` which writes the DB plane -- those are different stores). Edit `~/.gbrain/config.json` directly." The hint and the setter just haven't caught up for keys.

### How this bites in practice (observed 2026-06-11)

Bun auto-loads `.env` from the working directory, and gbrain has no dotenv loading of its own. launchd agents default to cwd `~` (an autopilot-spawned child worker ran from `/`), so an entire launchd fleet ran keyless while the operator believed the key was configured -- `config set anthropic_api_key` had printed success and `config get` echoed the value back (it reads the same DB plane, see companion issue #2120).

The failure is silent end-to-end: per-page `extract_atoms` / `propose_takes` failures in job results while autopilot cycles report ok/warn. Inline contexts (`gbrain jobs submit <name> --follow`, `dream --drain`) inherit the caller's env/cwd, and with no key resolvable, an extract-atoms drain completes "ok" with 0 atoms in ~71ms. We initially filed that under "inline contexts are broken" -- a re-test on 2026-06-12 in a controlled window (workers quiesced, key present in the file plane, key visibility probed from the same cwd) showed the inline drain behaving identically to the in-process worker (same pages, same yield, real LLM duration and spend). The "inline 0-atoms" symptom was entirely this key-plumbing trap.

### Repro

1. Postgres engine, no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env, no `anthropic_api_key` in `~/.gbrain/config.json`.
2. `gbrain config set anthropic_api_key <your-key>` -> prints success.
3. From a cwd with no `.env`: any LLM-phase call (e.g. `gbrain think "ping"`, or an `extract-atoms` drain on a brain with extractable backlog).
4. The call fails (or silently yields zero) with the no-key hint -- which recommends the command from step 2.

### Expected

Any of (in descending preference):

- `hasAnthropicKey()` / the gateway env-mapping also consult the DB plane, making the hint true;
- `config set` for `*_api_key` keys writes the file plane (mode 0600) instead of, or in addition to, the DB plane;
- minimally: the `gateway.ts:2378` hint stops recommending `gbrain config set anthropic_api_key` and says "set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or add anthropic_api_key to ~/.gbrain/config.json" (matching the `config.ts:78` guidance).

### Local mitigation

Key placed directly in `~/.gbrain/config.json` (mode 0600) plus `WorkingDirectory=<repo>` on the launchd plists so `.env` keeps flowing to launchd processes; the inert DB-plane row deleted to avoid future confusion. Documented as an operator runbook -- which every new operator has to re-learn the hard way, hence this issue.

### Related

- #1025 (closed) reported the set/show plane confusion generically; this is the sharper key-specific case where the runtime read path makes the setter a functional no-op and the error hint actively recommends it.
- #365 is the inverse mismatch for `openai_api_key` (embed reads env only, ignores config.json).
- #2088 (worker never refreshes gateway config when a key is absent) compounds the same outage class.
title: `config set anthropic_api_key` writes a config plane the runtime never reads for keys -- and the runtime's own no-key error hint recommends exactly that command

body:
### Version

gbrain 0.42.40.0 (clone @ `ecd6ae87`), Postgres engine (postgres.js client), macOS.

### Summary

On some `gbrain search` invocations, the post-print best-effort `[last-retrieved]` write-back fails with the postgres.js `UNDEFINED_VALUE` error: somewhere in the write-back path an `undefined` lands in a query parameter. Results print correctly; the failure is only the write-back. This is the same post-print seam as #1887, but a different failure: #1887 is the connection being torn down before the write (CONNECTION_ENDED / hang), while here the connection is alive and the write itself passes an undefined value.

### Repro

Reproduced twice on the same brain (before and after a pack migration, so not migration-related), with the same query both times:

```
$ gbrain search "acme freight" --limit 3
[0.9752] companies/acme-freight -- # Acme Freight

> Logistics company based in ...
[0.8636] people/jane-exampleton -- # Jane Exampleton

> Cloud architect at ...
[0.8236] people/sam-samplewood -- # Sam Samplewood

> Partner-program contact at ...
[last-retrieved] write-back failed (best-effort): UNDEFINED_VALUE: Undefined values are not allowed
```

(Page names above are synthetic; the real pages are ordinary company/person pages. The error line is verbatim.)

Not every query triggers it: on our brain, 5 other fixed queries run in the same sessions completed without the line, and this query produced it deterministically both times (identical result set both runs). That points at a property of the returned rows rather than timing: presumably one of the rows lacks a field the write-back binds without a null-coalesce.

### Observed

`[last-retrieved] write-back failed (best-effort): UNDEFINED_VALUE: Undefined values are not allowed` after correct results, exit 0.

### Expected

The write-back either binds defined values for every result row (coalescing optional fields to null) or skips rows it cannot bind, silently. A best-effort write should not surface a parameter-shape bug as a per-query error line.

### Local mitigation

None needed for correctness (results are unaffected); the line is noise in captured smoke-test output. Filing because UNDEFINED_VALUE indicates the write-back binds a field that can legitimately be absent, so some result sets never get their `last_retrieved` updated.

### Related

- #1887: same post-print write-back seam on Postgres, different failure (teardown race / hang). A fix that hardens the write-back path could address both.
title: `gbrain search` post-print [last-retrieved] write-back fails with `UNDEFINED_VALUE: Undefined values are not allowed` on Postgres (0.42.40)

body:
### Version

gbrain 0.42.40.0 (clone @ `ecd6ae87`), Postgres engine. Bundled pack `gbrain-base-v2@1.0.0`.

### Summary

Two gaps in the bundled `gbrain-base-v2` pack, found after migrating a brain from `gbrain-base@1.0.0` via `unify-types`:
