1. **No `phases:` key.** Cycle phases like `extract_atoms` run only when the active pack declares them. `gbrain-base-v2` ships without any `phases:` declaration, so on a v2 brain `extract_atoms` is silently pack-gated off: autopilot cycles report ok, doctor's `extract_atoms_backlog` grows, and `extract_health` degrades -- there is no signal pointing at the pack.
2. **Zero `link_types[].inference` regexes.** `gbrain-base` v1 ships link-inference rules (founded, works_at, invested_in, attended). v2 declares the link types but no inference regexes at all, so `gbrain jobs submit extract-ner` returns `pack_unavailable` on a brain whose active pack is the current bundled default.

The sharp edge: after the v2 migration, `gbrain onboard` recommends running atom extraction and extract-ner -- both inert against the very pack the same onboard flow recommends installing.

### Repro

1. Migrate a brain to `gbrain-base-v2` via the onboard-recommended `unify-types`.
2. `gbrain jobs submit extract-atoms-drain --follow` -> the phase is pack-gated; backlog does not drain. `gbrain doctor` shows `extract_atoms_backlog` warn with the detail "active pack does not run extract_atoms".
3. `gbrain jobs submit extract-ner --follow` -> `pack_unavailable`.

### Observed

On our brain: `extract_atoms` had never run post-migration (backlog 463 pages), `extract-ner` hard-skipped. Doctor `extract_health` accumulated a high halt rate with no hint that the pack was the cause.

### Expected

The bundled successor pack should preserve the cycle-phase participation and inference capability of the pack it migrates away from, or the migration should warn loudly about the capability delta. Concretely: a `phases: [extract_atoms]` declaration and the v1 inference regexes (or successors) in `gbrain-base-v2`.

### Local mitigation

A local pack copy (`gbrain-base-v2-hss`) that re-adds `phases: [extract_atoms]` and ports the 4 v1 inference regexes. Verified: `extract_atoms_backlog` flipped warn -> OK with "active pack runs extract_atoms each cycle", extract-ner produced 160 links. Cost: the local copy must be manually re-synced on every upstream pack release, which is why we are filing rather than keeping the divergence quietly.

### Related

- #2109 (v2 drops the `meeting` type that extract-timeline-from-meetings hardcodes) is a third capability regression in the same pack -- same theme: v2 silently sheds capabilities v1 users depend on.
- #1750 is the same end state (a bundled pack shipping inert) for `gbrain-recommended`, via a YAML-parser bug rather than missing keys.
title: `gbrain-base-v2` ships no `phases:` declaration and zero `link_types[].inference` regexes -- extract_atoms is silently pack-gated off and extract-ner returns `pack_unavailable`, making two of onboard's own recommendations inert

body:
### Version

gbrain 0.42.40.0 (clone @ `ecd6ae87`), Postgres engine, macOS, brain ~3.2k pages.

### Summary

`gbrain onboard --auto` executes its remediation plan by submitting each step to the job queue and polling for completion (`src/core/remediation/run.ts:271`: `waitForCompletion(queue, job.id, { pollMs, timeoutMs: (step.est_seconds + 60) * 1000 })`). It never checks whether a jobs worker is actually alive before submitting, and it has no inline-execution fallback. When the launchd jobsworkers are stopped (a deliberate quiesce during a maintenance window, or simply a machine where `gbrain jobsworker` was never installed), each step sits queued until its `est_seconds + 60` timeout expires and is marked aborted. From the operator's seat the command looks hung.

### Repro

1. Stop all jobs workers (e.g. `launchctl bootout gui/$UID/com.gbrain.jobsworker`), leaving the queue with no consumer.
2. Run `gbrain onboard --auto --max-usd 1`.
3. Watch the first remediation step submit, then poll silently until its timeout; subsequent steps repeat the pattern.

### Observed

Each step waits out its full per-step timeout with no progress and no diagnostic, then aborts. Observed live on 2026-06-11 during a pack-migration window with both jobsworkers deliberately booted out: the command appeared to hang, was killed by the operator, and the four planned actions were then run manually via `gbrain jobs submit <name> --follow` (the inline worker path), which executed them fine.

### Expected

Either of:

- A pre-flight worker-liveness check: if no worker heartbeat is visible, fail fast with "no jobs worker is running; start one or re-run with --inline", or
- An inline fallback (the machinery already exists; `jobs submit --follow` runs an inline worker in the caller's process).

Quiescing workers around maintenance is exactly when an operator runs `onboard` to verify state, so the combination is not exotic.

### Local mitigation

We run the planned remediation actions manually via `gbrain jobs submit <handler> --follow` whenever workers are quiesced, and treat `onboard --auto` as usable only while the launchd workers are up.

### Related

- #1306 is the PGLite-flavored cousin (dream synthesize hangs with no worker daemon); this one is the onboard/remediation path on Postgres, where workers exist but may be down.
title: `onboard --auto` submits remediation jobs with no worker-liveness check and no inline fallback -- with jobsworkers down, every step burns its full timeout and aborts

body:
## Version
gbrain 0.42.37.0

## Summary
When `models.dream.synthesize` / `models.subagent` is a model that emits reasoning content, the multi-turn subagent loop (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts` → gateway toolLoop) intermittently or deterministically fails on the *next* call with the AI SDK validation error:

```
Invalid prompt: The messages do not match the ModelMessage[] schema.
```

Evidence points to reasoning/thinking parts of the assistant message being persisted into `subagent_messages` and rebuilt into the conversation history in a shape the AI SDK rejects on the subsequent turn/attempt.

## Observed (dream synthesize jobs, via OpenRouter)
- `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` (thinking on by default): **deterministic** — 3/3 attempts fail with the schema error; job dead-letters after burning the full transcript input 3×.
- `google/gemini-3.5-flash` (thinking mandatory): **intermittent** — same job: attempts 1–2 fail with the schema error, attempt 3 succeeds cleanly (5 turns, end_turn). Retry roulette, ~2/3 of attempt budget wasted.
- `anthropic:` models (no reasoning emitted by default): never reproduced.

## Why this is distinct from (but related to) #1886 / #2039
#1886 covers dropped tool-result messages and #2039 covers crash-mid-round dangling tool-call tails. This report adds the **reasoning-content dimension**: with reasoning models the corruption occurs *without* any crash — a clean turn that includes reasoning parts is enough to poison the rebuilt history. Fix likely overlaps: strip/normalize reasoning parts when persisting and rebuilding history (most providers require either omitting reasoning or echoing it in a provider-specific field — e.g. DeepSeek's thinking mode returns 400 unless `reasoning_content` is handled per their spec).

## Repro sketch
1. `gbrain config set models.dream.synthesize openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`
2. Queue a synthesize job on any ≥2000-char transcript that triggers tool use (search + put_page).
3. Watch attempts 2..n fail with the ModelMessage[] schema error.
title: subagent toolLoop: reasoning-model output breaks next-turn history — "Invalid prompt: messages do not match the ModelMessage[] schema" (deterministic on DeepSeek V4, intermittent on Gemini 3.5 Flash)

body:
## Version
gbrain 0.42.37.0

## Summary
`src/commands/import.ts:463` runs `await engine.setConfig('sync.repo_path', dir)` as an unconditional side effect of every `gbrain import <dir>`. Any import from a directory that is *not* the canonical brain repo silently repoints write-through and sync at that directory.

## How we got bitten (real incident, 2026-06-12 03:30 nightly dream)
1. `sync.repo_path` correctly pointed at our brain repo (git repo A).
2. The dream cycle's sync phase ran against a different `--dir` (a transcript staging dir, git repo B). Its incremental anchor (`sync.last_commit`) referenced a commit that doesn't exist in repo B → anchor miss → **full reimport fallback**.
3. The full reimport path calls `gbrain import`-equivalent logic → import.ts:463 silently rewrote `sync.repo_path` to repo B.
4. From then on, every `put_page` write-through landed in the wrong directory; the canonical repo silently stopped receiving md mirrors (DB-only divergence). Nothing logged the config change.

## Expected
- `import` should not mutate `sync.repo_path` implicitly. Suggest: only set it when unset (first-run convenience), or behind an explicit flag (`--set-repo-path`), and always log the old → new value when it does change.
- The dream sync full-reimport fallback in particular should never repoint the configured repo.
title: gbrain import silently overwrites sync.repo_path — dream full-reimport fallback clobbers the configured brain repo

body:
## Version
gbrain 0.42.37.0

## Summary
`src/core/facts/extract.ts:182` hardcodes `maxTokens: 1500` for the extraction chat call, and the result path never inspects `finish_reason`. Models whose reasoning cannot be disabled and whose reasoning tokens count against the completion cap (e.g. `openrouter:google/gemini-3.5-flash` — the Gemini 3.x API rejects attempts to disable thinking with 400 "Reasoning is mandatory") burn ~1,400–1,500 tokens thinking, hit the cap, and return `finish_reason=length` with **empty content**. The 4-strategy JSON parser then finds nothing and the pipeline treats it as "no facts in this turn" — **silently**. No error, no log line distinguishing "model produced nothing" from "turn genuinely had no facts".

## Observed
- `facts.extraction_model = openrouter:google/gemini-3.5-flash`: 3/3 test calls died with `finish_reason=length`, reasoning tokens 1,437–1,496 of the 1,500 budget, zero visible output → 0 facts inserted, no error surfaced.
- Same prompt against the same model **without** a cap: completes fine, valid JSON.
- Result in production: ~30 pages ingested over 2 days extracted zero facts before we noticed (combined with the queue-abort issue #2108).

## Expected
Either of:
1. Check `finish_reason`. On `length` + empty/truncated content: log loudly (ingest_log), and ideally retry once with a raised cap.
2. Document that `facts.extraction_model` must be a non-reasoning (or reasoning-optional) model, and/or have `gbrain doctor` flag mandatory-reasoning models configured for capped call sites.

Related failure class: #1302 (judge phase truncated at a 4000-token cap).
title: facts extraction: finish_reason=length never checked — mandatory-reasoning models silently extract zero facts (maxTokens: 1500)

body:
## Summary
There is no supported CLI to release a stale **cycle** lock. `gbrain sync --break-lock` only covers `gbrain-sync:*` locks; cycle locks (`gbrain-cycle:<source>` in `gbrain_cycle_locks`) have no release path. The doctor flags them (`stale_locks` WARN) but the only remediation is hand-written SQL.

## Concrete case (v0.42.26.0, Postgres)
A source was renamed (`personal-second-brain` → `default`). The old `gbrain-cycle:personal-second-brain` row remained with a dead holder pid, 48h past its TTL. Because nothing ever re-acquires that lock id after the rename, TTL-takeover never fires and the row (and the doctor WARN) persists forever.

`gbrain sync --break-lock` reports: `Lock gbrain-sync:default is not held (nothing to break).` — it cannot see the cycle lock. There is no `gbrain lock` subcommand.

## Ask
A `gbrain lock list` / `gbrain lock release <id>` (or extend `--break-lock` to cycle locks) with the same dead-pid/age safety checks `runBreakLock` already implements. Bonus: garbage-collect lock rows whose lock id no longer corresponds to any configured source (rename leftovers).

## Workaround used
```sql
DELETE FROM gbrain_cycle_locks WHERE id = 'gbrain-cycle:personal-second-brain' AND holder_pid = <dead pid>;
```
after verifying the holder pid was dead.
title: No CLI to release stale cycle locks — sync --break-lock only covers sync locks; renamed sources leave gbrain_cycle_locks rows (and stale_locks WARN) forever

body:
## Summary
With `agent.use_gateway_loop=true`, subagent jobs running on `openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` die with:

```
[chat(openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash)] Tool results are missing for tool calls call_xxx, call_yyy.
```

The error message users hit first (`subagent job: resolved model ... is non-Anthropic but agent.use_gateway_loop is not enabled`) explicitly recommends enabling the gateway loop for non-Anthropic providers — but on deepseek the gateway loop then fails mid-conversation when the model emits parallel tool calls and the loop doesn't return results for all of them. Observed 6/8 dream-pipeline subagent jobs dead with this signature (v0.42.26.0).

## Repro
1. `gbrain config set models.subagent openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` (or models.default resolving there)
2. `gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true`
3. Let the dream cycle enqueue a subagent job (multi-turn, tool-using synthesis prompt)
4. Job dead-letters after retries with "Tool results are missing for tool calls …"

## Expected
gateway.toolLoop() should return a tool_result for every tool_call id in the assistant turn (including parallel tool calls), or the gateway loop docs/error text should not recommend providers it can't drive.

## Side note — config key inconsistency
While remediating: the subagent handler reads `models.subagent` (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts:211` → `resolveModel(..., configKey: 'models.subagent')`) but the `subagent_capability` doctor hint tells users to set `models.tier.subagent`. One of the two should be canonical. Also `gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true` warns `Nothing in gbrain reads this` (unregistered key) even though the subagent handler reads exactly that key via `engine.getConfig('agent.use_gateway_loop')`.

## Workaround used
Reverted the gateway loop and moved subagent jobs to `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6` (set on BOTH `models.subagent` and `models.tier.subagent`).
title: Gateway-native tool loop loses tool results on deepseek parallel tool calls; subagent model config keys inconsistent (models.subagent vs models.tier.subagent)

body:
## Summary
The `gbrain-base-v2` schema pack removes the `meeting` page type entirely (it is not among the 15 declared types, nor an alias of any), but `extract-timeline-from-meetings` hardcodes the type filter:

```
src/core/extract-timeline-from-meetings.ts:66   WHERE type = 'meeting'
src/core/extract-timeline-from-meetings.ts:88   AND pf.type = 'meeting'
```

After running the v0.42 `unify-types` migration (gbrain-base → gbrain-base-v2), all `meeting` pages are catch-all retyped to `note`, so the extractor silently scans **0 meetings** — while `onboard --check` keeps recommending `extract-timeline-from-meetings` as the remedy for low `timeline_coverage`. The feature dies with no error.

## Repro (v0.42.26.0, Postgres engine)
1. Brain with N pages of `type: meeting` (granola-style meeting pages under `meetings/`).
2. `gbrain jobs submit unify-types --allow-protected --params '{"target_pack":"gbrain-base-v2"}'` — completes cleanly, meetings retyped to `note` (`legacy_type: meeting` preserved).
3. `gbrain jobs submit extract-timeline-from-meetings` → result `{"meetings_scanned":0,"entries_created":0}`.
4. `onboard --check` still recommends the job.

Before the migration the same job scanned all meetings; after restoring `type='meeting'` (via a local pack fork that re-adds the type) it scanned 256.

## Expected
Either (a) gbrain-base-v2 keeps/declares a `meeting` type (it declares `email` and `slack` as temporal types — meetings are the same class), or (b) type-dependent features like `extract-timeline-from-meetings` resolve types through the active pack (alias/prefix expansion) instead of hardcoding `'meeting'`.

## Workaround used
Forked the bundled pack to `~/.gbrain/schema-packs/<fork>/pack.yaml`, re-added `meeting` (primitive `temporal`, prefix `meetings/`), restored the retyped rows from `legacy_type`. Works, but diverges from the bundled pack. Related papercut: `gbrain schema fork gbrain-base-v2 <new>` fails with "Source pack not found" even though the pack is bundled and active (`schema list` doesn't show it either) — the manual copy was required.
title: gbrain-base-v2 removes the meeting type but extract-timeline-from-meetings hardcodes type='meeting' — unify-types silently kills the feature

body:
## Summary

`put_page`-triggered `facts:absorb` jobs run fire-and-forget (`runFactsBackstop` mode `'queue'`). On process exit, the background-work drain waits only **1 second** (`drainPending` default, `src/core/facts/queue.ts:155`) before calling `abort()` on the facts queue (`src/core/background-work.ts:101-105`). Any in-flight `chat()` call slower than that window is killed and the extraction is **silently lost** — the page lands fine, but no facts are ever extracted for it.

With a slower expansion-tier chat model (`openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`) and proposal-sized pages (several KB of CJK text), the loss rate on a batch ingest was **100%**: 40/40 absorb attempts aborted across 30 distinct pages, and the `facts` table gained **zero rows** on two consecutive days of heavy ingest.

Version `0.42.37.0` @ `1eb430a2`.

## Observed

`ingest_log` over a ~4.5h batch-ingest window (2026-06-11 05:45–10:15 UTC), all 40 rows identical in shape:

```
pipeline_error: [chat(openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro)] The operation was aborted.
```

- 40 rows, 30 distinct `source_ref` pages (some pages aborted 2–3×).
- Affected pages were written via MCP `put_page` during batch knowledge ingest (full proposal documents, mostly Traditional Chinese, multi-KB content each).
- `SELECT date_trunc('day', created_at)::date, count(*) FROM facts GROUP BY 1` shows **0 facts** landed on either ingest day (most recent fact row predates the batch by 5 days). Pages, embeddings, links, search: all fine. Hot-facts layer: empty for the entire batch.

## Root cause (code-path walked)

1. `put_page` → `runFactsBackstop` mode `'queue'` — fire-and-forget by design (`src/core/facts/backstop.ts` header: "Caller's await is ~zero").
2. Host process eventually exits (one-shot CLI, or MCP server shutting down at session end). The background-work drain registry runs: `drainPending({ timeout })` with the **default 1000ms** (`src/core/facts/queue.ts:155`).
3. The comment at `queue.ts` says unfinished jobs "aren't aborted, they just continue running" under the singleton-still-alive contract — but on the CLI-exit path the registry then does `if (unfinished > 0 && d.abort) await d.abort()` (`src/core/background-work.ts:101-105`, marked `CLI-EXIT-ONLY` at line 84), which calls the facts queue `shutdown()` and aborts every in-flight job.
4. `extract.ts` re-throws aborts; `absorb-log.ts` files the `pipeline_error` row. Net: the absorb is logged as failed and never retried by any live path.

So the effective contract is: **an absorb job only survives if its LLM round-trip beats the 1s drain window** (or the process happens to stay alive long after the write). A long page + a non-instant chat model loses every time. Nothing about this is surfaced to the caller — `put_page` returns success.

## Aggravating interaction

The designed safety net for missed absorbs is the dream `extract_facts` phase ("MEDIUM waits for the dream cycle"). In our deployment that phase had been skipping nightly with `extract_facts: NN legacy v0.31 fact rows pending fence backfill — run gbrain apply-migrations` — so the fire-and-forget losses were never backfilled either, and the facts layer quietly flatlined while every other subsystem reported healthy. A deployment can sit in this state indefinitely; only `doctor`'s `facts_extraction_health` threshold warning (>10 absorb failures/24h) hints at it.

## Ask

Any of these would close the silent-loss class; the first two seem cheapest:

1. **Persist the absorb queue** (or at least the enqueued payload refs) so aborted-at-exit jobs are re-driven on next process start or by the dream cycle explicitly — abort would then mean "deferred", not "lost".
2. **Make the drain timeout configurable** (and/or scale it when the queue is non-empty: e.g. wait up to N seconds when jobs are in flight, where N covers one realistic LLM round-trip). 1s is below the floor of any chat completion on multi-KB content.
3. For batch surfaces (sync already passes `'high-only'`; MCP `put_page` bursts are the analogous case), offer an **inline/await-drain mode** so callers that just wrote 20 pages can opt into "don't exit until absorbs settle".
4. A `doctor` check that compares `pages written (eligible)` vs `facts landed` over a window — the 40-failures threshold caught this eventually, but a coverage-style check ("X eligible pages in 7d, 0 facts") states the impact directly.

## Environment

- `0.42.37.0` @ `1eb430a2`, macOS arm64 (Darwin 25.5), Bun, single host, single source (`default`).
- Supabase pooler `:5432`, `sslmode=require`.
- Chat routed via OpenRouter; expansion tier resolved to `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`. Embeddings `openai:text-embedding-3-large` (1536d) — embedding side unaffected.
- Brain: ~850 pages, schema pack `parkgo`, writes mostly via MCP `put_page` from agent sessions.

Happy to provide the full `ingest_log` dump, timing distribution, or run a patched build against this brain to verify a fix.
title: facts:absorb fire-and-forget jobs are aborted by the 1s exit-drain — silent 100% fact loss on batch put_page ingest with non-instant chat models

body:
**Type:** bug
**Component:** `dream` cycle / `propose_takes`
**Version:** gbrain `0.42.40.0` (master HEAD, commit `ecd6ae8`)
**Engine:** PostgreSQL

## Summary

`propose_takes` claims a content-hash idempotency contract — *"an unchanged page never re-spends LLM tokens"* — but that contract only holds for pages that produce **≥1 take**. A page that yields **zero** proposals never gets a `take_proposals` row, so its idempotency key is never stored, so it is **re-sent to the LLM on every cycle** in which it falls inside the scan window. In a steady brain, the *majority* of pages have no takeable claims (concept pages, stubs, transcripts, reference), so the phase spends the large majority of its tokens re-extracting pages that will never produce a take.

## The idempotency contract (and why it's false for zero-take pages)

`src/core/cycle/propose-takes.ts` JSDoc:

> *Idempotency contract (D17 schema spec): The unique index on (source_id, page_slug, content_hash, prompt_version) means an unchanged page never re-spends LLM tokens.*

The unique index does guarantee that — **but only because a row gets written.** The check and the write are asymmetric:

- **Check** (`propose-takes.ts:349`): skip the page iff a row exists for `(source_id, page_slug, content_hash, prompt_version)`:
  ```ts
  const cached = await engine.executeRaw(
    `SELECT id FROM take_proposals
     WHERE source_id = $1 AND page_slug = $2 AND content_hash = $3 AND prompt_version = $4
     LIMIT 1`, [sourceId, page.slug, ch, promptVersion]);
  if (cached.length > 0) { result.cache_hits += 1; continue; }
  ```
- **Write** (`propose-takes.ts:392-398`): the *only* writer of that key is the per-proposal loop:
  ```ts
  for (const p of proposals) {
    await engine.executeRaw(
      `INSERT INTO take_proposals (...) VALUES (...)
       ON CONFLICT (source_id, page_slug, content_hash, prompt_version) DO NOTHING`, [...]);
  }
  ```

If `proposals.length === 0`, the loop body never runs, **no row is written, and the page is a permanent cache-miss.** Nothing else writes that key (verified: `take_proposals` is referenced only by this producer + the schema DDL across the whole tree).

This compounds with the scan strategy: the phase scans `sort: 'updated_desc'`, `pageLimit` default **100** (`propose-takes.ts:308,325`), `skipPagesWithFence` default **false** (`:309`), and runs on the autopilot every cycle (it's in `ALL_PHASES`, gated only by `phases.includes('propose_takes')` at `cycle.ts:1962`, not pack-gated). Any churn that bumps `updated_at` (sync/embed/consolidate, ingest) keeps rotating the same zero-take pages back through the top-100 window, and each appearance is a fresh LLM call.

## Evidence (one production brain, Postgres, ~3 weeks)

- Total live pages: **2,677**; pages with a non-empty body but **zero** `take_proposals` rows: **1,659**.
- Rows ever written to `take_proposals`: **1,466** (across 1,038 distinct `page_slug`); single `prompt_version` (`v0.36.1.0-tuned-cat15`) — i.e. **no** prompt-version invalidation.
- `propose_takes` LLM submits over 3 weeks (from the dream-budget audit log): **~13,600** (one week peaked at 8,671; one hour peaked at 596 calls).
- So only **~11%** of calls produced a cacheable result; **~85–89%** were re-extraction of pages that yield no take and never cache. The *positive* cache works perfectly (no productive `(slug, content_hash)` pair is ever re-extracted across runs) — the entire leak is the missing **negative** cache.
- At the logged ~$0.012/call this is **~$100/week** of pure waste in this deployment; it also defeats the per-cycle budget cap on unpriced models (e.g. OpenRouter), since those calls log `submit_unpriced` and never project a cost to trip the cap.

## Reproduce

1. A brain with many body-bearing pages that contain no gradeable claims (concepts/reference/transcripts).
2. Run the dream cycle repeatedly (or let autopilot run).
3. Observe `take_proposals` row count stays flat while `propose_takes` LLM submits keep accruing each cycle; the zero-take pages are re-sent every run they fall in the `updated_desc` top-100 window.

## Suggested fix

Cache the **negative** result: after the extractor returns, if `proposals.length === 0`, still stamp the `(source_id, page_slug, content_hash, prompt_version)` key so the `:349` SELECT short-circuits next cycle. The composite key already self-invalidates correctly — a page whose body later changes gets a new `content_hash` (SELECT miss → re-extract), and a `PROMPT_VERSION` bump re-scans everything — so no legitimately-new take is ever suppressed.

Two implementation options:
- **Sentinel row in `take_proposals`** (no migration): insert one marker row on the zero-proposal path via the same `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` upsert. To keep it out of the operator's pending view (`take_proposals_pending_idx` is `WHERE status='pending'`), set `status='superseded'` (an already-legal CHECK value) and a recognizable marker (`kind='_skip'`, `claim_text=''`, `proposal_run_id` prefixed `propose-skip-…`). Satisfies the NOT NULL columns; adds nothing to the queue.
- **Dedicated marker** (cleaner, small migration): a `take_scan_marker(source_id, page_slug, content_hash, prompt_version, scanned_at)` table (or a `last_take_scan_hash` column), written unconditionally after each extract and consulted alongside the existing SELECT. Keeps `take_proposals` purely positive; costs a migration.

Either removes ~85% of `propose_takes` token spend with no loss of coverage.

## Related / adjacent issues

- [#1467 — Take proposal workflow: configurable provider, multiple proposals, review CLI](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/1467) — the missing consumer/review surface. *Different concern*: this report is purely the producer-side token waste and is orthogonal to whether a review surface exists.
- [#1412 — `propose_takes` phase times out on large corpus (3,125 pages); no configurable timeout or standalone invocation](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/1412) — related symptom (the phase is expensive / slow) but a different root cause.
- [#1781 — dream `extract_facts` non-idempotent: facts + fence rows duplicate each cycle (Postgres)](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/1781) — *sibling* class of "dream phase re-does work every cycle"; this is the `propose_takes` analogue.
- [#2088 — long-running jobs worker never refreshes AI gateway config; `propose_takes` hard-fails per page on the hardcoded anthropic default](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/2088) — same phase, adjacent failure mode (model resolution), independent of this caching bug.
title: propose_takes re-extracts every zero-take page on every cycle — no negative-result cache

body:
## Summary

`runLocalOp` in `src/cli.ts` installs the 10s `DISCONNECT_HARD_DEADLINE_MS` force-exit timer **before** `op.handler` runs (around line 366). The timer is unref'd but still fires 10 seconds after arming, calling `process.exit(process.exitCode ?? 0)`.

Consequence: any local-engine **shared op** that takes longer than ~10s total (op + drain + disconnect) is killed mid-work — and because `process.exitCode` is unset when the op hasn't errored, the process exits **0**, so the truncation looks like success to callers and scripts.

## Why it mostly goes unnoticed

- Ops routed to a remote MCP server (thin-client mode) don't take this path.
- The CLI_ONLY command path arms the same timer correctly — *after* the command completes, scoped around drain + disconnect (`cli.ts` ~line 1930).
- Most local shared ops (get_page, list, put of small pages) finish well under 10s.

But e.g. a local-engine `think`/`query` with an LLM call, or a `put_page` of a large multi-chunk page on a slow embed provider, can exceed 10s and gets silently killed with exit 0. The warning it prints ("engine.disconnect() did not return within 10000ms") is also misleading in that case — disconnect never ran.

## Suggested fix

Arm the timer in the `finally` block (immediately before the drain/disconnect sequence), matching the CLI_ONLY path's placement, so it bounds teardown rather than the whole op.

## Context

Found while debugging the facts:absorb abort class fixed in #2104 (the 10s cap is one of the reasons a longer exit-drain cannot rescue in-flight extraction work in one-shot CLI processes).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: cli.ts arms the 10s force-exit timer before the shared-op handler — local ops >10s are killed with exit 0

body:
**Version:** v0.42.40.0 (`ecd6ae87`), Linux x64, Bun 1.3.14, Postgres engine

**Summary:** `docs/integrations/embedding-providers.md` documents the LiteLLM escape hatch as: *"Set up LiteLLM, then `gbrain init --embedding-model litellm:<your-model-id> --embedding-dimensions <N>`."* That invocation cannot succeed — all three configuration paths dead-end:

1. **With `--embedding-dimensions N`:** `Provider "litellm" model "<id>" does not support custom dimensions N (this model only emits its default vector size).` — `src/core/ai/recipes/litellm-proxy.ts` declares `default_dims: 0` (*"user must declare --embedding-dimensions explicitly"*), but `validateDimAgainstTouchpoint` (`src/core/embedding-dim-check.ts`, the `requestedDims !== defaultDims` branch) treats any nonzero request as a custom dim, and litellm has no `dims_options`/Matryoshka allowlist, so it falls to the Tier-3 refusal.
2. **Without the flag:** `Provider litellm requires --embedding-dimensions <N>` (`src/commands/init.ts`, the `user_provided_models` guard). Catch-22 with (1).
3. **`gbrain config set embedding_model` / `embedding_dimensions`:** hard-blocked by design (`No --force escape...`), and its printed recipe points back at invocation (1).

**Repro:** run any LiteLLM proxy with an embedding model (e.g. `ollama/nomic-embed-text` behind it), set `LITELLM_BASE_URL`/`LITELLM_API_KEY`, then:

```
gbrain init --embedding-model litellm:your-embed-model --embedding-dimensions 768
```

**Suggested fix:** exempt `user_provided_models` recipes from the custom-dim refusal — for litellm/llama-server the explicit `--embedding-dimensions` *is* the declared schema width, not a Matryoshka truncation request.

**Workaround for others hitting this:** the `ollama` recipe is openai-compatible — `OLLAMA_BASE_URL=https://<your-litellm>/v1` + `OLLAMA_API_KEY=<vkey>` + `--embedding-model ollama:<model-name>` works (default 768 dims matches nomic-embed-text).

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: litellm embedding recipe: the documented init invocation is always rejected (default_dims: 0 vs custom-dimension validation)

body:
## Summary

The closing output of `gbrain init` tells the user to run a CLI command that has no implementation:

```
Soul audit: run `gbrain soul-audit` to customize agent identity
```

— [`src/commands/init.ts:1454`](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/blob/master/src/commands/init.ts#L1454)

soul-audit ships only as a prose interview **skill** (`skills/soul-audit/SKILL.md` — the 6-phase identity interview executed by the agent), not as a CLI verb. A user who follows the printed hint gets an unknown-command error.

## Evidence (checked at v0.42.26.0, `8058144`)

- `grep -rn "soul-audit" src/commands/` → the only hit is the `console.log` hint in `init.ts` itself (line 1454, plus the comment at line 1401). No `soul-audit.ts` command module exists.
- `grep -n "soul" src/cli.ts` → zero matches across the dispatcher's ~73 `case` routes — `gbrain soul-audit` has no route.

## Suggested fix (either direction)

1. **Reword the hint** to match reality — e.g. `Soul audit: ask your agent to "run a soul audit" to customize identity (skills/soul-audit)`, since the skill is invoked conversationally via the agent, not from the shell; **or**
2. **Add the command** — a thin `soul-audit` CLI entry that launches the agent with the soul-audit skill (consistent with the hint as printed).

Option 1 is a one-line change and removes the dead end immediately.

## Context

Found while adopting the soul-audit interview pattern for a persona factory downstream (morty-v3 LABS-97, 2026-06-11) — we initially scoped the work assuming the CLI verb existed because the `init` output advertises it.
title: `gbrain init` output advertises a `gbrain soul-audit` command that doesn't exist

body:
## Context

Some users run NVK `llm-wiki`-style, Obsidian-style, or multi-repo knowledge systems where a lightweight hub/root repo contains registry metadata, while each topic folder is its own git repo and should be treated as its own GBrain source.

Example shape:

```text
~/wiki/                              # lightweight hub/root repo
  _index.md
  log.md
  wikis.json
  docs/
  topics/
    topic-a/                         # separate topic repo, separate GBrain source
    topic-b/                         # separate topic repo, separate GBrain source
    topic-c/                         # separate topic repo, separate GBrain source
```

Each `topics/<slug>` is its own git repo/source boundary. The hub root is also version-controlled, but only for hub metadata: `_index.md`, `log.md`, `wikis.json`, docs, migration notes, and access-boundary policy.

The current GBrain primitives are close:

```text
brain   = DB/access boundary
source  = named repo/content boundary inside a brain
mount   = separate brain mounted beside the host brain
.gbrain-source + local_path = source routing by directory
```

But there does not appear to be a first-class primitive for a multi-source wiki hub where the root is a registry/metadata repo and child topic directories are separately registered sources.

## Problem

For llm-wiki / Obsidian / multi-topic knowledge systems, a user may want:

1. One hub root that contains metadata and registry files.
2. Many child topic repos/sources under `topics/<slug>`.
3. GBrain to understand the group as a coherent hub.
4. GBrain to avoid crawling child topic repos as part of the hub root metadata source.
5. GBrain to register/sync child sources from a manifest instead of manual one-by-one commands.

If the user simply registers the hub root as a normal source, there is a risk of duplicate/incorrect indexing because filesystem walkers may descend into nested topic repos. A root `.gitignore` is useful for git hygiene but should not be treated as a guaranteed GBrain crawl boundary.

## Proposed feature

Add a source-group / wiki-hub primitive, for example:

```bash
gbrain hubs add personal-wiki --path ~/wiki --manifest wikis.json
# or
gbrain sources add-hub ~/wiki --manifest wikis.json --id personal-wiki
```

The feature would treat the hub root as a source group, not a normal content source.

## Desired behavior

### 1. Parse a hub manifest

Support a manifest that maps child source ids to paths and display names. NVK llm-wiki already uses `wikis.json`, roughly:

```json
{
  "topic_wikis": {
    "topic-a": {
      "path": "topics/topic-a",
      "title": "Topic A",
      "description": "..."
    }
  }
}
```

GBrain could either support this shape directly or define its own `gbrain-hub.yml` that can be generated from it.

### 2. Register/sync child sources

A command should register each child topic as a GBrain source:

```bash
gbrain hubs sync-sources personal-wiki
```

Equivalent manual outcome:

```bash
gbrain sources add topic-a --path ~/wiki/topics/topic-a --federated
gbrain sources add topic-b --path ~/wiki/topics/topic-b --federated
```

### 3. Optional hub metadata source

Allow a narrow hub metadata source that indexes only root-level docs, not topic contents:

```text
_index.md
log.md
wikis.json or rendered registry docs
docs/**
```

But exclude:

```text
topics/**
```

This avoids duplicating all child topic pages under a `hub` source.

### 4. Source group query ergonomics

Allow the user/agent to query by source group:

```bash
gbrain search "example query" --source-group personal-wiki
gbrain sync --source-group personal-wiki
```

This should expand to the child sources in that group, respecting federation and any per-source config.

### 5. Crawl safety

A hub-root source should never accidentally crawl nested child repos unless explicitly configured. GBrain could detect nested `.git` directories or child sources under a registered hub and warn/fail closed.

### 6. Access-control alignment

For future team-agent use, the hub/source-group metadata should help construct scoped brains/endpoints:

```text
private brain: all child sources
team-a brain: shared + topic-a only
team-b brain: shared + topic-b only
team-c brain: shared + topic-c only
```

The goal is not necessarily per-user GBrains. The useful primitive is role/access-circle source grouping.

## Why this helps users

This is useful for anyone running:

```text
- NVK llm-wiki hubs
- Obsidian vaults split by topic/repo
- multi-repo personal knowledge bases
- company brains with many team/topic sources
- local-first/private knowledge systems avoiding GitHub/SaaS storage
```

It lets GBrain stay aligned with git boundaries and access boundaries without forcing a monorepo.

## Suggested acceptance criteria

- A hub manifest can register/update N child sources idempotently.
- Hub metadata can be versioned/indexed separately without crawling child topics.
- `gbrain sync --all` or `gbrain hubs sync <id>` works cleanly with child source repos.
- GBrain warns or refuses if a normal root source would duplicate nested registered sources.
- Source-group queries can expand to child sources explicitly.
- Agents can cite results as `source:slug` and optionally display the group/hub label.

## Non-goals

- Do not require GitHub.
- Do not require submodules.
- Do not force every team member to have a separate GBrain database.
- Do not make the hub root a security boundary by itself; actual access control should still use brain/source/OAuth boundaries.

## Interim workaround

For now, users can apply a bespoke rule:

```text
Root hub repo = git/versioned metadata only; not registered as a normal GBrain source initially.
Topic repos = GBrain sources.
```

This feature request is for making that pattern first-class and safer for future users.
title: Support multi-source wiki hubs / source groups for llm-wiki-style knowledge bases

body:
## Summary

`gbrain eval longmemeval` 404s **out of the box** — with pure defaults and a valid `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, every answer-generation call fails, and the trajectory extractor fails **open** (silently), so half the breakage is invisible in the output.

## Root cause

Two design decisions collide:

1. `resolveModel()` deliberately returns **provider-prefixed** ids (`DEFAULT_ALIASES.sonnet` → `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`) — required by the v0.40.8+ subagent queue's recipe resolution (`src/core/model-config.ts`).
2. `eval-longmemeval` feeds that resolved string **verbatim into a raw `new Anthropic()` SDK client** (`src/commands/eval-longmemeval.ts` ~473): `realClient.messages.create({ model: 'anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6', … })` → the Anthropic API has no model by that name → `404 not_found`.

The trajectory extractor is worse: it resolves tier-utility → `anthropic:claude-haiku-…` (prefixed), has **no CLI flag**, and `callExtractor` catches all errors and returns `null` (fail-open) — so trajectory extraction silently no-ops and recall quietly degrades with **zero errors reported**. Observable signature: `extractor.cache_hits: 0 / N sessions (0.0%, cached_bodies=0)`.

`think` already solved this at the identical call shape via `normalizeModelId()` + `probeChatModel()` (`src/core/think/index.ts` ~247); eval just doesn't use it.

## Repro (v0.42.36.0; code unchanged through 0.42.40.0)

```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=… gbrain eval longmemeval test/fixtures/longmemeval-mini.jsonl --limit 1
# → answer-gen 404s (model "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6" not found)
```

## Zero-code workaround (verified end-to-end)

Bare ids bypass the prefixing — the CLI flag and env var pass through alias resolution untouched when they aren't alias names:

```
GBRAIN_MODEL=claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 \
  gbrain eval longmemeval <fixture> --model claude-sonnet-4-6
# → 10/10 recall, 0 errors, extractor live (cached_bodies=N)
```

(`--model` fixes answer-gen; `GBRAIN_MODEL` is needed because the extractor has no flag and checks env before the prefixed tier fallback.)

## Ready-made fixes already exist as closed PRs

- #1401 — strip the prefix at the call site (minimal)
- #1681 — route answer-gen + extractor through the gateway (`tryBuildGatewayClient`, the canonical chat seam `think`/`synthesize` use); covers the extractor path; was verified end-to-end before closing for queue hygiene

Either can be reopened/cherry-picked; happy to refresh #1681 against current master on request.

## Same disease elsewhere (prefixed/hardcoded ids hitting provider APIs raw)

#1857 (`takes extract` — same silent fail-open, 0 claims), #1607 (`eval cross-modal` defaults / gateway 404s), #1307 (`brainstorm/lsd` hardcoded `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`). A `normalizeModelId` sweep at the provider-call boundary would close the class.
title: eval longmemeval 404s out-of-the-box: resolveModel's provider-prefixed ids hit the raw Anthropic SDK; extractor fails open (silent recall degradation)

body:
#1253 (v0.37.7.0) fixed `--source` routing for the **local-engine** path: `makeContext()` in `src/cli.ts` resolves the 6-tier chain (`--source` / `GBRAIN_SOURCE` / `.gbrain-source` / path-match / brain default / `default`) into `OperationContext.sourceId`, and ops scope from context. Verified working on a local caller.

The **thin-client** path never gets there. When a remote MCP is configured, `cli.ts` short-circuits to `runThinClientRouted(op, params, …)` before `makeContext` runs, and the parsed `--source` value (`params.source`) is never mapped to the op's `source_id` wire param. Result: `gbrain query "<q>" --source <X>` against a remote brain returns **identical, unscoped results for any `--source` value** — no error, no warning. (`gbrain search --source <X>` has the same UX plus a second gap: the `search` op schema has no source param at all.)

## Repro (remote-MCP thin client, multi-source brain)

1. `gbrain query "anything" --source <isolated-or-specific-source>` → results come from the federated pool, not the named source; a query whose answer exists only **outside** `<source>` still returns those outside hits.
2. Same call via the MCP `query` tool with `source_id: "<source>"` directly → scopes correctly (returns `[]` when the content genuinely isn't in that source).
3. Same CLI call on the brain host (local engine) → scopes correctly.

## Suggested fix

In the thin-client routed path, run the engine-free tiers of the resolution chain (flag / env / dotfile) and set `params.source_id` for ops that declare it (`query`, …); optionally add `source_id` to the `search` op schema for parity. A loud error for `--source` on an op with no source param would also prevent the silent-drop UX.

## Relationship to #1372

Complementary — #1372 governs whether a remote caller is **allowed** a given `source_id`; this issue is about the CLI never **sending** it. With both landed, `--source` from a thin client becomes both honored and authority-checked.

Seen on 0.42.36.0; routing code unchanged through 0.42.40.0 (`runThinClientRouted` at `src/cli.ts:347`/`452`; `makeContext` at `src/cli.ts:737`).
title: bug: thin-client routing drops --source — query/search from a remote-MCP CLI silently search unscoped

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

My usecase - a shared readonly mcp for all developers accessed via VPN. The brain would contain knowledgebase of multiple code repositories. The number of repositories may grow at a random moment or some repo might be dropped. Currently federated access has to list all sources and there does not seem to be a straightforward way to update access after the fact

**What does the solution look like?**

I propose prefix access support like `mycompany/codebase/*`, which would automatically allow devs access to all sources in `mycompany/codebase/app-a`, `mycompany/codebase/app-b` ... etc. Separating it from `mycompany/wiki`. We can still restrict pure wildcard access like `*`, although I do see some usecases in a trusted environment, where automatic access to all sources could be useful.

**Alternatives considered**

I could just fork and just implement it for us, but I think it will be useful for the whole community
title: prefix grant for federated access

body:
## Summary

`gbrain serve` (stdio) cannot answer the MCP `initialize` handshake until the engine is fully connected, because `cli.ts` awaits `connectWithRetry(engine, ...)` before command dispatch (`src/cli.ts` ~1992-1997). For the PGLite engine, "connected" means the full WASM Postgres boot **plus** advisory write-lock acquisition (`src/core/pglite-engine.ts:217` → `acquireLock`, which itself waits up to 30s on contention).

MCP hosts enforce a connect timeout on `initialize` — Claude Code's default is **30s**. Whenever PGLite boot + lock-wait exceeds that, the host kills the server and reports an opaque `connection timed out after 30000ms`. The user never sees the real reason (slow WASM boot, or "another serve holds the write lock").

Related but distinct from #677 (cooperative single-owner mode) and #2058 (no lock heartbeat / live-holder steal): this is about the *handshake ordering* — even with a perfect lock protocol, any engine boot slower than the host timeout makes stdio MCP registration unusable, and lock contention surfaces as a host-side timeout instead of an actionable error.

## Environment

- gbrain `0.42.37.0`, PGLite engine (~2k pages / 6.6k chunks)
- Bun 1.4.0, Windows 11
- MCP host: Claude Code (stdio transport via a `.bat` wrapper, 30s connect timeout)

## Observed timeline (real incident, UTC)

| Time | Event |
|---|---|
| 13:42:36 | Claude Code session A spawns `gbrain serve` (stdio), 30s connect timer starts |
| 13:42:38 | Claude Code session B (second window) spawns its own `gbrain serve` |
| 13:43:06 | Session B: `Connection timeout triggered after 30038ms` → host kills its server |
| 13:43:08 | Session A: same timeout → host kills its server |
| 13:43:16 | `.gbrain-lock/lock` written by one of the (now-orphaned) serve processes — **8-10s after both parents were killed** |
| later | Lock dir left behind; holder PID dead; next boot pays the stale-lock cleanup path |

Two engines booting PGLite WASM concurrently (CPU contention) plus the loser waiting in `acquireLock`'s retry loop pushed both past 30s. Neither ever got to answer `initialize`.

## Measurements

Time from spawn to `initialize` response on the same brain, same machine (warm FS cache, single instance):

| Config | time-to-initialize |
|---|---|
| Bun default (JIT on) | **~2.3s** |
| `BUN_JSC_useJIT=0` (interpreted WASM) | **~16s** |

The JIT-off row is a local quirk of this machine's wrapper, but it demonstrates the variance class: cold cache, large brains, slow disks, CPU contention, or a second session waiting on the lock can all push an *eager* connect past a host's handshake deadline. (For scale: `acquireLock`'s own default wait is 30s — identical to the host timeout, so a locked brain is *guaranteed* to lose this race.)

## Secondary bug: shutdown racing an in-flight connect leaks the lock dir

The 13:43:16 lock write above happened **after** shutdown had already begun. Sequence:

1. Host kills the `.bat`/`cmd.exe` wrapper → stdin `close` fires (and/or the #2049 parent watchdog catches it) → `beginShutdown` → `engine.disconnect()`.
2. But `engine.connect()` is still in flight: `this._lock` isn't set yet, so `disconnect()` releases nothing and the process exits.
3. The in-flight `acquireLock` then completes (or completed between the disconnect check and exit), writing a lock dir that no live process owns and nothing ever releases.

It self-heals on the next boot via the dead-PID stale check, but under Windows PID recycling the next boot can mistake the recycled PID for a live holder and burn the full 5-minute `STALE_THRESHOLD_MS` (or, post-#2058, whatever replaces it). `beginShutdown` should await/cancel an in-flight connect before calling `disconnect()` (or `connect()` should check a shutdown flag before and after `acquireLock` and release immediately).

## Proposed fix

Decouple the MCP handshake from engine readiness for the stdio path:

1. **Answer `initialize` (and `tools/list`) immediately.** The tool list is generated from `src/core/operations.ts` — no DB needed. Start the engine connect in the background when `serve` dispatches.
2. **First `tools/call` awaits engine-ready** (with a generous internal deadline). If the engine can't connect because the write lock is held, return a structured tool error — e.g. `brain is locked by pid N (command: ...)` — instead of letting the host time out the whole server. The host stays connected, the user sees an actionable message, and a later call can retry once the lock frees.
3. **Close the shutdown/connect race** so a killed-during-boot serve never leaves a lock dir behind.

This would also make the multi-session story (per-client stdio registration spawning N serves — the exact topology #2058 observed) degrade gracefully: extra sessions connect fine at the MCP layer and get a clear per-call error, rather than dying as opaque host timeouts.

Happy to PR this (follow-up to #2049, which this incident's lock-leak analysis builds on).
title: serve (stdio): engine connects eagerly before the MCP initialize handshake — slow PGLite boot / lock contention kills the host connection at its 30s timeout, and shutdown racing an in-flight connect leaks the lock dir

body:
## Summary

`takes.embedding` is **never written by any code path** — INSERTs into `takes` omit the column (postgres-engine.ts:4018 and :4393; pglite-engine.ts:3927) and no `UPDATE takes SET embedding` exists anywhere in the tree. The read side is fully built and shipping: `searchTakesByVector`, `getTakeEmbeddings` (postgres-engine.ts:4312), `countStaleTakes` (:4327), `listStaleTakes` (:4335), and the engine interface ("v0.28 stale-takes row", engine.ts:322/1429). `listStaleTakes`/`countStaleTakes` additionally have **zero callers** outside the engine layer — `embed --stale` covers pages/chunks only, and the dream cycle doesn't embed takes either, so there is no recovery knob. Verified identical on v0.42.37.0 and master (ecd6ae87, v0.42.40.0).

Consequences:

- `gbrain takes search` (vector-only) returns empty forever, on every install.
- `think`'s `takes_vec` gather arm (src/core/think/gather.ts:7) never contributes. With `takes_kw` inheriting #2077's AND-fragility for natural-language questions, `think` effectively runs **takes-blind** — observed `Takes: 0` in think's own stats line with 21 active takes in the DB, several directly relevant to the question asked.
- The synthesis then picks up take *summaries* from ordinary pages instead and can misstate claim text/scope (observed twice in one run: a take's meaning inverted, a narrow claim broadened) — the takes table is exactly the layer that was supposed to prevent this.

## Second defect: hardcoded dims

The takes DDL is static `vector(1536)` (schema-embedded.ts:289) while init's dim-substitution gives `content_chunks` the configured embedder dims — a ZE-configured live DB shows `content_chunks.embedding vector(1280)` and `takes.embedding vector(1536)` side by side. The init.ts:1017 comment acknowledges the static-substitution gap for Postgres. Net: even when a writer ships, any non-1536 embedder will fail the insert or the `embedding <=> query_vec` comparison until the column dims align with the configured embedder.

## Repro (v0.42.37.0, Postgres engine, any embedder)

1. `gbrain takes add` a few takes; run sync + a full dream + `gbrain embed --stale`.
2. `SELECT count(*) FROM takes WHERE active AND embedding IS NULL;` → equals the total active count, indefinitely.
3. `gbrain takes search "<distinctive term from a claim>"` → empty. `gbrain think "<question relevant to a take>"` → `Takes: 0`.

## Ask

1. Add the takes embed pass (claim text → embedding) — natural homes: the dream cycle's embed phase and/or `embed --stale`; the `listStaleTakes` machinery is already there waiting for a caller.
2. Create the takes column with the configured embedder dims (the same substitution `content_chunks` gets), and/or migrate existing `vector(1536)` columns on upgrade.

Cross-refs: #2077 (keyword-arm AND-fragility — currently the only live takes-retrieval arm), #2078/#2079 (takes CLI family).
title: takes.embedding has no writer — vector takes search (think takes_vec, takes search) is structurally dead; DDL also hardcodes vector(1536) regardless of configured embedder dims

body:
**Version:** 0.42.37.0 (1eb430a), Postgres engine, Linux VPS, autopilot installed as a systemd user service.

## Symptom

Every `autopilot-cycle` job logged one warning **per page** in the `propose_takes` phase:

```
extractor failed on <slug>: Anthropic chat requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.
```

~70–100 warnings per cycle, for ~36 hours. No Anthropic key exists on this brain — `chat_model` is `deepseek:deepseek-chat`, set both in DB config (`gbrain config set chat_model …`) and as `GBRAIN_CHAT_MODEL` in the env file sourced by the autopilot unit. `gbrain config get chat_model` returned the correct value the whole time, which made the failure very confusing to diagnose.

## What happened

1. The autopilot service (which spawns the `gbrain jobs work` child that executes cycles) had been **started before** `chat_model` was configured.
2. The AI gateway config is resolved once at process startup (`connectEngine` → file/env merge + DB merge + `reconfigureGatewayWithEngine`). A long-running worker never re-reads it.
3. With no `chat_model` resolved at boot, the gateway falls back to the hardcoded `DEFAULT_CHAT_MODEL = 'anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6'` (`src/core/ai/gateway.ts`). `instantiateChat` then throws `AIConfigError` for every page because `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is absent.
4. Net effect: `gbrain config set chat_model …` (or fixing the env file) has **no effect on already-running workers** — the per-page failures persist silently in job warnings until the operator thinks of restarting the service.

Restarting the autopilot service fixed it instantly: the same job (`autopilot-cycle` with `phases:["propose_takes"]`) went from ~70 per-page failures to `100 pages scanned, 0 warnings, proposals inserted` on deepseek.

## Why this is worth fixing

- The failure mode is a per-page hard-fail buried in job warnings — easy to miss for days (takes/atoms silently stop accruing while everything else looks healthy).
- The fallback targets a provider the operator may never have configured. Other phases gate cleanly when prerequisites are missing (e.g. `extract_atoms: not_in_active_pack — skipped`); chat-bearing phases could do the same.

## Suggestions (any subset helps)

1. Re-resolve gateway config at job start (or on a TTL) in the jobs worker, so `gbrain config set chat_model` applies without a service restart.
2. When the effective chat model resolves to a provider with no credentials, skip the phase once with an explicit status (like pack-gating) instead of emitting N per-page `AIConfigError`s.
3. `src/core/extract-takes-from-pages.ts` defaults to a hardcoded `'anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5'` (`opts.model ?? …`) — the same dormant failure for brains without an Anthropic key; defaulting to the configured `chat_model` would be consistent.

Happy to provide logs or more repro details.
title: Long-running jobs worker never refreshes AI gateway config — propose_takes hard-fails per page on hardcoded anthropic default when ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is absent

body:
## Summary

`enrich` can false-SKIP thin but fact-rich entity pages because `retrieveEvidence()` appends hybrid search chunks before backlinks and facts, while `renderEvidence()` packs whole evidence blocks into a fixed 12k character window in order. A few long hybrid chunks can consume the render window and push short, high-signal facts/backlinks out of the prompt.

## Minimal Reproduction Shape

1. Create a thin target page with a short body, e.g. a company/entity stub.
2. Add many extracted `facts` for that entity and inbound links with useful contexts.
3. Ensure hybrid search returns long chunks for the entity name before those facts/backlinks are appended.
4. Run `enrich --limit 1 --model <capable chat model>`.

Expected: facts/backlinks are visible to the model and the page is enriched.

Actual: rendered evidence may contain only the first long hybrid chunks; facts/backlinks are omitted, and the model can emit `SKIP`.

## Local Evidence

From a 2026-06-10 probe on our deployment (entity names anonymized):

- A thin company page (`companies/<entity-A>`) had DB evidence available: 20 fact rows, 12 backlinks, 7 hybrid hits.
- The rendered prompt contained only 3 hybrid source blocks because the 12k context cap was consumed before facts/backlinks.
- With the same retrieved facts/backlinks rendered first, the model produced a usable cited company profile instead of `SKIP`.
- A person page (`people/<person-B>`) showed the same failure shape: facts existed but were dropped behind long hybrid chunks.

Probe artifacts (rendered-prompt dumps + diagnosis) are archived locally; happy to share sanitized excerpts on request.

## Proposed Minimal Fix

1. Feed render evidence in priority order: facts, then backlinks, then hybrid search chunks.
2. Clamp individual hybrid chunks before rendering, e.g. ~2,000 characters per hybrid source, while preserving the `[Source:]` header at render time.
3. Split `pages_skipped_insufficient` into:
   - `pages_skipped_pre_llm`
   - `pages_model_skip`
   - `pages_empty_output`

Keep the legacy `pages_skipped_insufficient` as the sum for compatibility.

## Non-Goals

This issue does not propose prompt changes, retries, fan-out, CJK alias expansion, or different retrieval. Those should be evaluated separately after the evidence ordering fix is measured.

## Related

- #1700 (no batch/incremental enrichment primitive) — adjacent but distinct: this issue is about evidence ordering inside a single `enrich` run causing false SKIPs even when evidence exists.
title: enrich: thin pages can false-SKIP when hybrid evidence fills the 12k render window before facts/backlinks

body:
## Summary

For markdown (default-source) syncs, gitignored files are invisible to **incremental** sync (it enumerates via `git diff lastCommit..HEAD`) but ARE imported — and embedded — by **full reimports**: `collectSyncableFiles` (import.ts:532) prunes only dot-prefixed dirs, `node_modules`, `ops`, and `.raw`, never consulting `.gitignore`. Meanwhile `sync.respect_gitignore` already appears in doctor.ts (~line 2117) as a config nudge, but nothing implements it.

## Why the divergence is the problem

A gitignored `.md` under the synced tree behaves as "not indexed" for months of routine syncs — then a full reimport fires (first sync, a `CHUNKER_VERSION` bump on upgrade, or the sync anchor commit going missing after a history rewrite) and the file is silently imported and embedded. For users who use `.gitignore` as their "private/local-only" boundary, that's surprise egress of exactly the content they meant to exclude, triggered by an event they didn't initiate (an upgrade or a git history operation).

The two sync modes disagreeing about what's in the corpus also means a full reimport doesn't reproduce the incremental state — page counts jump and search results change after what should be a no-op rebuild.

## Ask

Implement `sync.respect_gitignore` for the markdown walker (filter `collectSyncableFiles` through `git check-ignore` / parsed gitignore rules when the flag is set). Default off is fine — the doctor nudge already implies the flag's existence, so the fix is mostly making the documented surface real.

Related: #1073 asks for the same gitignore awareness on the `--strategy code` walker. This issue is specifically about the markdown/default source path and the already-referenced-but-unimplemented config key.
title: markdown walker ignores .gitignore on full reimport (incremental sync skips those files) — sync.respect_gitignore referenced in doctor but unimplemented

body:
## Summary

`extract links` has working machinery for resolving bare wikilinks by basename across folders — `opts.globalBasename` (extract.ts ~307–313: "Ancestor walk misses + globalBasename on + basename hits → all matches") — but no CLI flag wires it, so it's unreachable in practice.

## Why it matters

Obsidian's default link style is bare `[[Page Title]]` regardless of folder. In a foldered vault (`10-people/`, `11-companies/`, `12-meetings/`, ...), most wikilinks point across folders, and the ancestor-walk-only default derives **zero** edges for them. Measured on a real vault: hundreds of dangling cross-folder refs that `extract links` couldn't resolve, all of which had unambiguous basename targets — the only path was scripting manual `add_link` calls.

## Ask

A `--global-basename` flag on `extract links` (opt-in, default off to preserve current behavior). Ambiguity policy can stay exactly as the existing code documents (return all matches / skip ambiguous).
title: extract links: globalBasename resolution exists but no CLI flag exposes it — cross-folder bare wikilinks underivable

body:
## Summary

Chunk text contains only the page **body** — the title and `aliases:` frontmatter never enter `content_chunks.chunk_text` / `search_vector`. Two consequences (v0.42.37.0, Postgres engine):

1. **Title-only / list-style pages are invisible to their own exact-title keyword queries.** A page titled "the ideal diet" whose body is a bare bullet list produces one chunk in which the phrase "ideal diet" appears nowhere — so the keyword arm can't match the page even for its verbatim title, while pages merely *linking* to it rank. (Empirically verified; adding a single H1 + one prose line naming the subject fixed retrieval immediately.)

2. **`aliases:` frontmatter can't act as a vocabulary bridge at the chunk level.** Adding natural-phrase aliases to a page (e.g. mapping a project codename to its plain-English description) has no effect on the keyword or vector arms, because aliases never reach chunk text or embeddings.

This hits exactly the note shapes a personal vault accumulates: short reference lists, stub pages, codename-titled project notes.

## Ask

Prepend the page title (and optionally aliases) to chunk text — or at least into `search_vector` — so a page is always reachable by its own name. If unconditional prepending is too invasive for long multi-chunk documents, first-chunk-only or a config knob would cover the failing cases.

(The hybrid title-phrase boost stage helps rank pages that some arm already retrieved, but it can't rescue a page no arm returned.)
title: page title + aliases never enter chunk text — title-only/list-style pages are invisible to their own exact-title queries

body:
## Summary

`gbrain takes list` prints `No takes on list.` even when the brain holds many takes. Root cause: the takes CLI has no `list` subcommand — `cmdList` treats `args[0]` as a **page slug** (takes.ts:110–115), so the word `list` is looked up as a page named "list", which has no takes.

```
$ gbrain takes list
No takes on list.          # ← "list" was parsed as a slug

$ gbrain call takes_list '{}'
[ ...all takes returned fine... ]
```

It's a natural thing to type (every other noun-style CLI surface supports `list`), and the failure mode reads like "the takes table is empty" — an agent or user reasonably concludes there are no takes and moves on.

## Suggested fix (either works)

- Support a global list: `gbrain takes list` (or bare `gbrain takes`) → all active takes, i.e. CLI parity with the `takes_list` operation; or
- Reserve `list` as a word and error with the usage string instead of silently slug-ifying it.

## Related papercut (same discovery session)

`gbrain eval --help` (and `gbrain eval suspected-contradictions --help`) prints the generic "run gbrain --help for the full command list" stub rather than the eval subcommand's actual usage — the only way to learn the `run/trend/review` sub-subcommands and the `--queries-file | --query | --from-capture` requirement is to read `src/commands/eval-suspected-contradictions.ts`. Happy to split this into its own issue if preferred.
title: `gbrain takes list` parses "list" as a page slug and prints "No takes on list."

body:
## Summary

`gbrain takes supersede` can never find an **active** take: `cmdSupersede` looks up the target with `engine.listTakes({ page_id: pageId, active: false, limit: 500 })` (takes.ts:274), and the engine treats `active: false` as `WHERE active = false`. Superseding any active take — the normal case; superseding is *for* active takes — fails with "Row #N not found".

## Repro (v0.42.37.0)

```
gbrain takes add <slug> --claim "..." --weight 0.8 ...   # creates active take, row N
gbrain takes supersede <slug> --row N --claim "..."      # → "Row #N not found"
```

## Workaround (verified twice)

```sql
UPDATE takes SET active = false WHERE id = <target>;
```
then run the CLI supersede — `engine.supersedeTake` itself doesn't check `active`, the fence renders correctly, and the end state is byte-identical to what a fixed CLI would produce.

## Fix

`active: true` in the lookup (or omit the filter and match by row number among all takes on the page, erroring on already-superseded targets with a clearer message).
title: takes supersede looks up its target with active:false — superseding any ACTIVE take fails "Row #N not found"

body:
## Summary

The keyword arm is `websearch_to_tsquery('english', $1)` hardcoded (postgres-engine.ts:1604/1608 and 1742/1747). `websearch_to_tsquery` ANDs all plain terms — so **one query term absent from a chunk zeroes the whole match**. More keywords = strictly worse recall, which inverts what users (and LLM agents writing queries) expect from a search box.

## Observed failure shape (v0.42.37.0, real corpus)

Measured during a retrieval eval against a known-answer set: for several misses, the target page ranked #1 in hybrid for a sensible ≤4-keyword query, but the examinee agent's natural 5–7-term query AND-missed because one incidental term ("payroll", "URL", "format") wasn't in the chunk. The pattern was consistent: every keyword-arm miss traced to a single absent term, never to ranking.

A related sharp edge for prose corpora: tsvector tokenizes `a/b` as a single `file`-type token, so a phrase like `thyroid/metabolic` is unmatchable by the word `thyroid` — slash-joined word pairs in prose silently break term matching.

## Ask

When the AND'd `websearch_to_tsquery` yields **zero rows**, retry with OR-joined terms (`to_tsquery` with `|`, or strip terms progressively) and rank with `ts_rank` as usual. Zero-row fallback preserves current precision when AND matches exist, and turns the all-or-nothing cliff into graceful degradation. Alternatively (or additionally): expose the keyword-arm query builder as a config knob.

This composes with — but is independent of — vector-arm quality: agents writing verbose natural queries currently get punished by both arms at once unless they happen to know the ≤3-distinctive-keywords trick.
title: keyword arm: websearch_to_tsquery ANDs all terms — one absent term zeroes the match; add zero-row OR-fallback

body:
## Summary

`embed_skip: true` frontmatter reads like "this page's content never gets embedded / never surfaces in search," but it is honored on only one path. Three verified gaps (v0.42.37.0, Postgres engine):

### 1. Incremental sync's FIRST import of a new file chunks AND embeds it despite the key

A brand-new `.md` carrying `embed_skip: true` in its frontmatter, picked up by incremental sync, gets chunked and embedded normally — egress to the embedding provider happens. The full-reimport path (`performFullSync` → `putPage`) honors the key (page row created, 0 chunks), so behavior diverges depending on which path first sees the file.

Empirical repro: import a new page with the key already set → `content_chunks` rows exist with non-NULL embeddings; the same file entering via a full reimport gets 0 chunks.

### 2. A stamp added AFTER first import never reaches the DB

The incremental modified-page path uses `refreshPageBody` (postgres-engine.ts:1104), a deliberately narrow UPDATE that skips the `frontmatter` column. So adding `embed_skip: true` to an already-imported page and syncing changes nothing — the DB row keeps the old frontmatter, and the chunks stay embedded. (`capture` behaves the same way.) The only working remediation today is direct SQL on `pages.frontmatter` plus NULLing the page's chunk embeddings.

### 3. Nothing at search time filters embed_skip'd content

All `embed_skip` predicates live in the embed-selection queries (postgres-engine.ts:2208–2396). The keyword arm's SQL (postgres-engine.ts:1604ff) matches `content_chunks` with no embed_skip or embedding-state condition, and the title-match path operates on page rows regardless. So once chunks exist via gap #1, the content is fully keyword-searchable and the page surfaces by title even after de-embedding.

Empirical repro: a page imported with `embed_skip: true` (chunked via gap #1) surfaces in `gbrain search` by its title.

## Why it matters

The natural reading of `embed_skip` is a privacy/exclusion contract ("keep this out of the index"). Users putting sensitive-ish content behind it get protection only if the file happens to enter via a full reimport and is never modified afterward. The gaps are silent — no error, no doctor warning.

## Suggested fixes

- Gap 1: check `embed_skip` in the incremental first-import path (same predicate the embed-selection queries use).
- Gap 2: either include `frontmatter` in `refreshPageBody`'s UPDATE, or detect an embed_skip transition on the modified-page path and de-embed (NULL embeddings / drop chunks) accordingly.
- Gap 3 (design decision): if `embed_skip` should also mean "not searchable," add the predicate to the keyword arm + title path; if it intentionally means only "not embedded," document that loudly — and gaps 1–2 still need fixing for the embedding half.

Happy to provide more detail on any of the repros.
title: embed_skip is honored only on the full-reimport path — first import embeds despite the key, later stamps never reach the DB, search doesn't filter it

body:
## Two contributions (patches below)

I couldn't open a PR — `POST /repos/garrytan/gbrain/forks` returns `403 "You cannot fork this repository at this time"` for my account even though the repo is public with `allow_forking: true`, and I don't have push access. So I'm filing the work as an issue with `git am`-ready patches. Happy to convert to a proper PR if you enable forking for my account (`cheRoma`) or add me as a collaborator.

Both patches apply on top of `master`. The `VERSION`/`CHANGELOG` bump in patch 1 (0.42.39.0) is illustrative — rebase to whatever the current release slot is.

---

### 1. `feat(cycle): link_chat` — auto-link orphan chat captures to per-project hubs

The SessionEnd capture hook writes raw transcripts to `chat/<date>-<project>-<id>` with no links, so every capture lands as an orphan and the orphan count climbs every session. `link_chat` is a deterministic, zero-model cycle phase (mirrors `enrich_thin`): it finds orphan `chat/` pages, derives the project from the slug, ensures a `projects/<project>/_index` hub, and adds a **hub → capture** link so the page gains an inbound link and stops being an orphan.

- Direction is load-bearing: orphan = no *inbound* link, so the hub links **to** the capture, not the reverse.
- The `/_index` suffix is already excluded by `shouldExclude` (commands/orphans.ts), so hubs never become orphans and there's no hub chain.
- Idempotency is the orphan filter itself — a linked page is no longer a candidate next tick.
- Reuses existing engine methods only (`findOrphanPages`/`getPage`/`putPage`/`addLink`), so **engine-parity is unaffected** (no new SQL shape).
- Opt-in: `cycle.link_chat.enabled` (default OFF), `cycle.link_chat.max_pages_per_tick` (default 50).
- Wired into `cycle.ts` (`CyclePhase`/`ALL_PHASES` between `enrich_thin` and `skillopt`, `PHASE_SCOPE='source'`, `NEEDS_LOCK`, dispatch). Pinned by `test/cycle-link-chat.test.ts` (TDD, 17 assertions).

Verified live: enabling it on a real brain dropped orphans 238 → 72 in one cycle and auto-created 12 project hubs.

### 2. `chore(test): guard the test suite from wiping a developer's personal brain`

The e2e helpers (`test/e2e/helpers.ts:setupDB`) and ~10 direct connectors run `initSchema` + `TRUNCATE ... CASCADE` against `process.env.DATABASE_URL`. On a dev box, `~/.gbrain/.env` exports `DATABASE_URL` pointing at the real brain (`:5434/gbrain`), so a bare `bun test` truncates it. (It bit me: 336 pages → 6 rows mid-run; recovered from the markdown source of truth.)

Fix: a `bun test` preload clears `DATABASE_URL`/`GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL` when they resolve to the same `host:port/db` as the configured personal brain (`~/.gbrain/config.json`), so the e2e suite skips real-Postgres tests instead of truncating. **CI is unaffected** (no `~/.gbrain/config.json` to match); `GBRAIN_ALLOW_PROD_TEST=1` opts back in. Pinned by `test/prod-brain-guard.test.ts` (TDD, 12 assertions). Proven end-to-end: with `DATABASE_URL`=brain, `test/e2e/postgres-bootstrap.test.ts` now 4-skips and leaves the brain untouched.

---

### Apply

```
git am 0001-*.patch 0002-*.patch
```

<details>
<summary>Patch 1 — link_chat (feat)</summary>

From 77bfa92b3d29bbc57521a230cd1fa95bf49aa971 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001

Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 20:09:31 +0000
Subject: [PATCH 1/2] =?UTF-8?q?v0.42.39.0=20feat(cycle):=20link=5Fchat=20?=
 =?UTF-8?q?=E2=80=94=20auto-link=20orphan=20chat=20captures=20to=20per-pro?=
 =?UTF-8?q?ject=20hubs?=
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

Tier 0 chat distiller: a deterministic, zero-model cycle phase that gives raw
SessionEnd chat captures an inbound link from an auto-created per-project hub
(projects/<project>/_index) so they stop being orphans. Idempotent via the
orphan filter itself; the /_index suffix is already orphan-excluded so hubs
never become orphans. Opt-in via cycle.link_chat.enabled (default OFF).

Wired into cycle.ts (CyclePhase/ALL_PHASES between enrich_thin and skillopt,
PHASE_SCOPE=source, NEEDS_LOCK, dispatch). Reuses existing engine methods only,
so engine-parity is unaffected. Pinned by test/cycle-link-chat.test.ts.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
---

 VERSION                        |   2 +-
 docs/architecture/KEY_FILES.md |   1 +
 package.json                   |   2 +-

 test/core/cycle.serial.test.ts |   6 +-
 test/cycle-link-chat.test.ts   | 156 +++++++++++++++++++++++
 8 files changed, 442 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
 create mode 100644 src/core/cycle/link-chat.ts
