1. **Key not registered.** `conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled` (and `conversation_parser.llm_polish`) do not appear anywhere in `src/core/config.ts`. So `config set` writes it as an unknown key (hence `--force` is required), and no validated config surface exposes it.

2. **Live parser is sync + regex-only.** `parseConversation()` (`src/core/conversation-parser/parse.ts:436`) returns `ParseResult` synchronously and only scores/selects regex pattern candidates. There is no branch that consults the flag or falls back to an LLM when zero messages match.

3. **The fallback function is dead code.** `runLlmFallback()` (`src/core/conversation-parser/llm-fallback.ts:68`) is exported but has **zero callers** in `src/` (grep: the only hit is its own definition). The live paths — `conversation-parser scan` and the conversation-fact extractor — call the regex parser directly and never invoke it.

4. **Same for polish.** `runLlmPolish()` (`src/core/conversation-parser/llm-polish.ts:88`) is likewise exported but never wired into any live parse/scan/extract path, and `llm_polish` is also absent from `config.ts`.

5. **Doctor recommends the no-op.** `src/commands/doctor.ts:4529`, in the `conversation_format_coverage` warn branch, tells users to run `gbrain config set conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled true` — i.e. it points users straight at the dead flag, implying LLM fallback is an available remedy when it does nothing.

## Impact

- Users hit the `conversation_format_coverage` warning, follow doctor's advice, set the flag, and silently get no behavior change — the parser still returns `no_match` on unrecognized formats.
- The scaffolding (`runLlmFallback` / `runLlmPolish` / the LLM base helpers) gives the impression the feature exists when it's unreachable.

## Suggested fix (one of)

- **Wire it up:** make `parseConversation` (or its callers in `scan` / fact extraction) async and, on zero regex matches with the flag enabled, invoke `runLlmFallback()` (and `runLlmPolish()` where applicable); register both keys in `src/core/config.ts`; **or**
- **Stop advertising it:** if LLM fallback isn't ready to ship, remove the doctor recommendation at `doctor.ts:4529` (and gate/hide the keys) so users aren't directed to a no-op.

Repro:
```
gbrain config set conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled true --force   # writes unknown key, no effect
gbrain conversation-parser scan <unmatched-slug> --json                   # still phase: no_match; LLM never invoked
```
title: conversation_parser.llm_fallback_enabled / llm_polish are dead config (never read; doctor recommends the no-op)

body:
Summary

After gbrain query/search prints its (correct, fast) results, gbrain performs a best-effort "last-retrieved" write-back. On the native Postgres storage engine, the DB connection/pool is closed before that write-back runs, so it races teardown. Two observed outcomes from the same race:

Fast-fail (common): the write-back hits a dead socket and the process exits, printing [last-retrieved] write-back failed (best-effort): write CONNECTION_ENDED localhost:5432. Results are correct; the line is noise, but it signals the ordering bug.
Hang (intermittent): when the write-back instead blocks on the pool rather than erroring, the CLI pegs one CPU core at ~100% and never exits — must be SIGKILLed (timeout/killpg; rc 137).

This is the post-print teardown that PR #1259 ("drain last-retrieved writes before CLI disconnect") fixed for PGLite (#1247 / #1269 / #1290 / #1343, all PGLite, #1343 Closed). On the Postgres engine adapter the drain ordering isn't honored — the connection is ended before the last-retrieved write is drained. So the fix is engine-specific and the Postgres path regressed / was never covered.
Note: this is not #1368 (the pre-output expansion-path spin, where no results print). Here results are printed (fast, correct), the failure is in teardown, and it reproduces with --expand false, so the expander is ruled out.
Observed (live, 2026-06-05, gbrain 0.41.0.0, Postgres engine)
$ gbrain query "test" --expand false
[0.8326] meetings/2026-04-08-... -- # Insight Spine — ...
[0.8190] people/richard-jerrett -- # Richard Jerrett ...
... (20 correct, well-ranked rows in ~1-3s) ...
[0.5132] people/melody-achi -- # Melody Achi ...
[last-retrieved] write-back failed (best-effort): write CONNECTION_ENDED localhost:5432
$            # exited this run; on other runs the same point hangs at ~100% CPU until killed

Steps to reproduce

Brain on the native Postgres engine (engine: postgres, Homebrew postgresql@17 + pgvector 0.8.0; config per the #1671 migration). Healthy: gbrain doctor → brain_score 100/100, connection: Connected, pgvector: Extension installed.
Run gbrain query "<any query returning at least one page>" --expand false
Results print in ~1-3s, then either (a) the [last-retrieved] write-back failed ... write CONNECTION_ENDED localhost:5432 line prints and the process exits, or (b) the process pegs one core at ~100% CPU and never exits (intermittent — the two branches of the same teardown race).
Reproduces with --expand false (not the #1368 expander) on a freshly-rebuilt, integrity-clean brain (not corruption-dependent).

Environment

gbrain version: 0.41.0.0 (local git checkout, bun link; runs src/cli.ts directly — no dist/)
Storage engine: native PostgreSQL 17.10 (Homebrew postgresql@17, launchd) + pgvector 0.8.0 (built from source)
OS: macOS 26.5.1 / Darwin 25F80 (arm64), Mac mini M4 Pro
Bun: 1.3.14 · Node: v22.22.2
Embedding: llama-server:daniel-embed (bge-m3 Q8_0, 1024d) via llama-swap on 127.0.0.1:11435
gbrain doctor: brain_score 100/100; 194 pages / 411 chunks; schema v93

Likely cause (evidenced)

The CONNECTION_ENDED localhost:5432 confirms the last-retrieved write-back fires after the Postgres connection/pool is closed. #1259 drains the last-retrieved writes before CLI disconnect for the PGLite path; the Postgres engine adapter evidently tears the connection down first (or doesn't await the same drain), so the write-back either errors on a dead socket (fast-fail, process exits — observed) or blocks on the pool, keeping the event loop alive and spinning a core (the ~100% CPU non-exit hang).
Fix direction: ensure the last-retrieved write is awaited and drained before pool.end() / connection close on the Postgres engine, or make the drain hook engine-agnostic (shared shutdown) rather than wired to the PGLite disconnect.
Impact

The CONNECTION_ENDED line is cosmetic (results are correct) but signals the bug; the intermittent hang breaks any harness/cron that waits for process exit — every batch invocation must wrap each call in a timeout/SIGKILL and parse stdout.
Affects native-Postgres deployments, which on macOS 26 (Tahoe) is the only working engine (PGLite WASM aborts there — #223/#1670) — exactly the users forced onto Postgres by the OS update.

Workaround

Wrap each invocation in a kill-after-output bound (own process group, SIGKILL after results print, keep stdout; treat rc 137 as success).
Happy to add a process.getActiveResourcesInfo() dump from a hang instance if needed, or open a PR if the fix is awaiting the last-retrieved drain before pool.end() on the Postgres adapter.
title: Postgres engine: post-print [last-retrieved] write-back races connection teardown (write CONNECTION_ENDED) → best-effort failure + intermittent ~100% CPU non-exit hang. #1259's drain doesn't cover the Postgres adapter

body:
## Repro on pure-upstream v0.42.25.0 (SHA 9a0bae8d)

Box: clean install, **zero local patches**, schema migrated to 113.

```bash
gbrain jobs submit subagent --queue noah-wellbeing --params '{
  "prompt":"Generate a 3-bullet morning summary based on briefings/2026-06-04-morning. Return ONLY the 3 bullets, no other text.",
  "model":"openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro",
  "max_turns":5
}'
```

Result: **dead after 3 attempts**, identical error each attempt:

```
[chat(openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro)] Tool result is missing for tool call call_<id>.
```

Token shape: ~4520 in / ~85 out per attempt. The loop appears to make the first tool-call, then loses the tool-result on the way to the next turn.

## Pattern

- Manifests when DeepSeek emits a `tool_use` that the model expects a `tool_result` for on the next turn.
- The `messages.push({ role: 'user', content: toolResultBlocks })` at `src/core/ai/gateway.ts:3054` (around `toolLoop`) DOES push the tool-result blocks to the in-memory array, BUT the message does NOT reach `persistMessage` storage.
- On retry/replay (or sometimes within the same attempt), the next `chat()` call doesn't include a tool-result for the prior tool-call, and the model rejects the conversation.
- v0.42.11.0 + v0.42.19.0 (PR #1759 / #1809) covered the schema-conversion side (`toModelMessages` at the chat() call site) — but did not add the persistence layer.

## A related error class — same family, possibly same root

Occasionally the same loop emits a broader AI-SDK validation error:

```
[chat(openrouter:deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro)] Invalid prompt: The messages do not match the ModelMessage[] schema.
```

Both originate in the `ai` package (AI SDK):
- "Tool result is missing for tool call" → `MissingToolResultError` (tool_use without matching tool_result).
- "messages do not match the ModelMessage[] schema" → Zod validation on the full messages array (broader: any structural violation).

Same family, both triggered by a structurally invalid messages array reaching `generateText()`. Possibly same root (the tool_use/tool_result pairing is one schema concern), but the schema-mismatch error is the broader catch-all and may also surface from other causes (content-block ordering, role/content mismatch on tool messages).

Our local persistence fix resolves the "Tool result is missing" case empirically — and most "schema mismatch" cases — but we've observed at least one job (a different subagent with a more complex tool sequence) hit the schema-mismatch error where in-loop retry could not recover. Suggests additional schema-validation paths the persistence fix doesn't cover.

## Suggested fix

Add an `onToolResults?: (messageIdx: number, blocks: ChatBlock[]) => Promise<void>` callback to `ToolLoopOpts` and call it right before the tool-result `messages.push` in `toolLoop`. Subagent-handler callers persist via `persistMessage`. (Mirrors the existing `onToolCallComplete` shape.)

We've been carrying this exact patch locally since 2026-06-01 — verified it fixes both error patterns in production. Happy to file the PR if useful.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.42.25.0 (9a0bae8d)
- Bun runtime
- Supabase Postgres backend (schema v113)
- DeepSeek-v4-pro via OpenRouter, native tool-use
title: multi-turn subagent loop drops tool-result user message → "Tool result is missing for tool call" with DeepSeek (and some other providers)

body:
On a Supabase/pooler thin-client brain, the dream `synthesize` phase repeatedly fails to process large session transcripts: the per-transcript subagent ingests the whole file, times out, and writes nothing. The nightly dream summary shows e.g. `Children: 0 completed, 2 failed/timeout. Pages written: 0` for consecutive nights.

**Concrete case:** a session-corpus transcript of ~5 MB (~1.4M tokens, 82k lines). The synthesize subagent (`anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`) is handed the full file, ingests it, and times out before producing output. Because the corpus entry is keyed by `(file_path, content_hash)` for idempotency, the same oversized file is retried every night and fails every night — so it never gets smaller and never succeeds.

**The cost angle is the real issue:** each failed attempt still pays for the ingested input (~$/MB of input tokens), recurring nightly, for zero pages written. And a timed-out child is not recorded in `subagent_messages` (only completed children are), so the spend is invisible in the brain's own accounting (`minion_budget_log` / `budget_ledger` are empty) — you only see it on the provider bill.

**Ask:** a configurable size guard in the synthesize phase, e.g.:
- Skip + warn (record a skip-reason in the dream summary / `dream_verdicts`) when a transcript exceeds a token/byte threshold, instead of handing it to the subagent and timing out.
- Or pre-chunk / map-reduce oversized transcripts (synthesize per chunk, then merge), so large inputs still produce pages without a single oversized call.
- Either way, never retry the same oversized transcript indefinitely with no backoff or cap.

This is defense-in-depth: producers should keep transcripts lean too, but the synthesize phase should not silently burn tokens on inputs it cannot complete.
title: dream synthesize: oversized session transcripts silently burn tokens on timeout (need a size guard / pre-chunk)

body:
## Summary

Add a **dynamic workflow** layer on top of the existing Minions subagent/aggregator handlers — a declarative multi-phase DAG with deterministic completeness guarantees. This replicates (and improves on) the "Claude Dynamic Workflows" feature Anthropic shipped for Claude Code, but on our durable, Postgres-backed, restart-surviving queue instead of a session-bound runtime.

Reference / prior art: Artem Zhutov, "Claude Dynamic Workflows for Your Second Brain" (https://x.com/artemxtech/status/2062582596190498864). The native feature has Claude write a JS orchestration script that spawns isolated-context subagents in parallel or pipeline form. The script being a *real deterministic program* is the entire value: it kills agent laziness (quitting before all N items done), self-preferential bias (isolated context per child), and goal drift over long sessions.

## Why

We already have ~60% of this:
- `gbrain agent run --fanout-manifest` = N children + 1 aggregator that claims only after every child terminates (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts`, `subagent-aggregator.ts`).
- Parent-child DAGs with failure policies, mid-flight steering, token accounting, structured progress.

What's missing is the workflow layer and the completeness guarantee.

## Gaps to close

### 1. Multi-phase pipelines (barriers between phases)
Today: one fanout + one aggregator. Needed: phase 1 (draft, parallel) → barrier → phase 2 (check) → barrier → phase 3 (synthesize). We have the parent-child primitive but no workflow spec that chains 3+ phases with barriers.

### 2. Deterministic completeness driver (highest value)
A driver that **pins the unit list at submit time** and refuses to mark the workflow complete until `processed == total`. No early stop, no "I'll do a representative sample," no agent laziness. This is the single most valuable piece — it structurally fixes the recurring "stops mid-batch" failure mode instead of relying on the orchestrator remembering to continue.

### 3. Pattern templates (cheap once #1 + #2 exist)
- **classify-and-act** — route each item to a handler by classification
- **fan-out + synthesize** — already have, formalize as a template
- **tournament** — N attempts → judge scores each → final pick
- **loop-until-done** — schedulable (e.g. daily 7am digest)
- **claim-extractor verification** — claim extractor → per-claim source-check workflow → final verified report

## Proposed shape

New command: `gbrain workflow run <spec.json>`

- `spec.json` = small declarative DAG: ordered phases, each phase = `fanout | pipeline | map`, with a barrier between phases.
- A thin compiler translates the spec into the existing `subagent` / `subagent_aggregator` handlers. **No queue rewrite** — workflow is a layer on top.
- The "Claude writes the JS for you" UX becomes "the spec.json is generated from a plain-English ask" — more inspectable and diffable than generated JS.
- Completeness driver enforces `processed == total` as a hard gate before the workflow can terminate `succeeded`.

### Example spec (sketch)
```json
{
  "name": "mine-sessions-for-corrections",
  "units": { "source": "sessions", "last": 50 },
  "phases": [
    { "id": "extract", "type": "map", "subagent": "correction-extractor", "model": "haiku" },
    { "id": "cluster", "type": "fanout", "subagent": "correction-clusterer", "model": "opus" },
    { "id": "reconcile", "type": "pipeline", "subagent": "skill-reconciler", "model": "opus" }
  ],
  "completeness": { "require": "processed == total" }
}
```

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `gbrain workflow run <spec.json>` submits a multi-phase DAG to Minions and survives a worker restart mid-run.
- [ ] Barriers enforced: phase N+1 does not claim until every phase N child terminates.
- [ ] Completeness driver: workflow cannot reach `succeeded` unless every pinned unit is addressed; partial completion surfaces as `failed`/`partial` with the unaddressed unit list.
- [ ] At least the `fan-out+synthesize`, `map`, and `tournament` templates ship with tests.
- [ ] Progressive-batch-gate semantics preserved/compatible (eval receipts per phase where applicable).
- [ ] Docs: `docs/guides/minions-workflows.md` + skill update to `skills/minion-orchestrator`.

## Non-goals
- Not rewriting the Minions queue or the shell-job lane.
- Not replacing native subagents for simple single-shot work.
- Not adopting Claude Code's session-bound runtime — durability via Minions is the differentiator.

## Notes
- Code lives read-only to Wintermute; implementation will be a fork → branch → PR.
- Prior art brain page: `media/x/artemxtech/claude-dynamic-workflows-second-brain.md`.
title: Minions: dynamic workflow layer (multi-phase DAG + deterministic completeness)

body:
## Summary
`gbrain doctor` **always exits 0**, even when checks are `[FAIL]` (e.g. `cycle_freshness`, connection down). There's no non-zero exit code and no top-level severity field, so a monitoring wrapper has no signal to alert on — a brain can be badly degraded while the health command "succeeds." This makes real problems silent.

## Why it matters (real incident)
A daily cron ran `gbrain doctor --json` to "watch" the brain, but the only thing it could key off was "did the command crash / is the JSON valid." So:
- **61h of stale dreaming** (`cycle_freshness` FAIL) → never alerted.
- **A corrupted PGLite engine** (doctor degraded to "filesystem checks only") → never alerted; doctor still exited 0.

Both went unnoticed for days precisely because `doctor` reports FAILs in its body but signals success to the shell.

## Repro
1. Put a brain in a state with a failing check (e.g. let a source go stale → `cycle_freshness` FAIL).
2. `gbrain doctor; echo "exit=$?"` → prints `[FAIL] cycle_freshness ...` but `exit=0`.

## Suggested fix (any of)
1. **Non-zero exit on FAIL** — exit 1 (or a documented code) when any check is `status: fail`. Simplest; standard for health/CI tooling.
2. A `--fail-on <warn|fail>` flag for opt-in strictness (keeps default exit 0 for back-compat).
3. In `--json`, add a top-level `{ "ok": false, "failed_checks": [...], "worst_severity": "fail" }` so wrappers can alert without grepping human text.

Any of these lets a cron/CI actually detect an unhealthy brain. Right now monitoring `gbrain doctor` is effectively impossible without scraping its prose output (which also varies, e.g. the misleading "Overall health score" vs `brain_score`).

## Context
Filed alongside #1869–#1872 from the same debugging session.
title: gbrain doctor always exits 0 even on [FAIL] checks — no exit code / severity signal for monitoring (degradation stays silent)

body:
## Summary
Stopping the autopilot service with `systemctl --user stop gbrain-autopilot` (SIGTERM) shuts PGLite down **uncleanly and corrupts the engine**: every subsequent `gbrain` invocation then fails with `PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime / Aborted()`. Recovery required a full `reinit-pglite` + resync.

## Environment
- Linux/WSL, PGLite engine, gbrain 0.42.x, autopilot run as a systemd **user** service.

## Repro
1. Run `gbrain autopilot` on a PGLite brain (e.g. via the systemd service) so it holds the DB and cycles.
2. `systemctl --user stop gbrain-autopilot` (SIGTERM) while it's alive.
3. `gbrain doctor` / `gbrain search` → `PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime. Aborted()`, persistently.
4. Verified NOT a stale lock: no `gbrain`/`bun` process alive (`pgrep`, `lsof +D <datadir>` empty), removing `postmaster.pid` doesn't help. The data dir itself is corrupted.
5. Recovery: `gbrain reinit-pglite --embedding-model <p:m> --embedding-dimensions <N> --yes` (backs up to `*.bak`) then `gbrain sync --repo <brain>`.

## Root cause (suspected)
The autopilot process doesn't trap SIGTERM to close the PGLite WASM Postgres cleanly, so a normal `systemctl stop` kills it mid-write → corrupted data directory.

## Impact
A routine `systemctl stop` corrupts the brain engine. Content survives (markdown is source of truth) but the index must be rebuilt. Combined with PGLite being single-connection (autopilot also blocks all queries while running), this makes **autopilot effectively unusable on PGLite**.

## Suggested fix
- Trap SIGTERM/SIGINT in autopilot and perform a clean PGLite shutdown (close the connection / checkpoint) before exit.
- Set a sane `TimeoutStopSec` / `KillSignal` in the generated systemd unit and document graceful-stop.
- Consider refusing/​warning when `autopilot` is run against a PGLite engine (recommend Postgres for long-running autopilot).
title: systemctl stop of autopilot (SIGTERM) corrupts the PGLite engine — no graceful shutdown

body:
## Summary
On a healthy PGLite brain, `gbrain doctor`'s `oversized_pages` and `scraper_junk_pages` checks report `No database connection: connect() has not been called` even though the `connection` check in the same run reports `[OK] Connected, N pages`. This drags the "Overall health score" down with false skips.

## Environment
- Linux/WSL, PGLite engine, gbrain 0.42.x. Freshly rebuilt + synced brain (64 pages), connection healthy, embeddings 100%, brain_score 78.

## Symptom (single doctor run)
```
[OK] connection: Connected, 64 pages
[OK] oversized_pages: Skipped (No database connection: connect() has not been called. Fix: Run gbrain init --supabase or gbrain init --url <connection_string>)
[OK] scraper_junk_pages: Skipped (No database connection: connect() has not been called. ...)
```
The connection clearly succeeds for other checks but these two act as if `connect()` was never called.

## Impact
Misleading "Overall health score" (these skips count against it) and the remediation text wrongly implies you must move to Supabase/URL, when the PGLite connection is fine.

## Suggested fix
Have `oversized_pages` / `scraper_junk_pages` reuse the engine connection established for the rest of the run (or call `connect()` in their path) on PGLite.

## Related
#1422 (dream DB phases swallowed by try/catch) — same family of "connection not established / swallowed" issues, different surface.
title: gbrain doctor: oversized_pages & scraper_junk_pages report 'connect() has not been called' on PGLite despite a healthy connection

body:
## Summary
When PGLite fails to initialize, the error hardcodes a macOS-specific cause even on Linux, sending users to debug the wrong thing.

## The message
```
PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime.
  Most common cause: the macOS 26.3 WASM bug
  (https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/223).
  Run `gbrain doctor` for a full diagnosis.
  Original error: Aborted().
```
Shown verbatim on **Linux/WSL**, where #223 (macOS 26.3) is irrelevant.

## Actual causes we hit (Linux)
1. **Another process already holds PGLite's single connection** (e.g. `gbrain serve`, `gbrain autopilot`, or a concurrent command). PGLite is single-connection; the second opener aborts.
2. **Data-dir corruption from an unclean shutdown** (SIGTERM-ing a process that held PGLite mid-write) — persistent abort until `reinit-pglite`.

Neither is the macOS WASM bug, but the message points only there.

## Impact
Wasted debugging on the wrong surface (chased the macOS bug + lock files before realizing it was contention, then corruption).

## Suggested fix
- Gate the macOS-26.3 hint on `process.platform === 'darwin'`.
- On other platforms, surface the likelier causes: "another process may hold the PGLite connection (single-connection engine)" and "the data dir may be corrupted from an unclean shutdown — see reinit-pglite".
- Bonus: detect a held connection vs a corrupt data dir and message accordingly.
title: PGLite init-failure message hardcodes 'macOS 26.3 WASM bug' on Linux, hiding the real cause (connection contention / unclean-shutdown corruption)

body:
## Summary
`gbrain dream --dir <path>` completes a cycle (exit 0, real work) but **never stamps per-source `cycle_freshness`**, so `gbrain doctor` reports the brain as perpetually stale even though it's being dreamed on schedule. `gbrain dream --source <id>` DOES stamp it.

## Environment
- Linux/WSL, PGLite engine, gbrain 0.42.x, single source `default`.

## Symptom
- A daily cron running `gbrain dream --dir ~/brain` succeeds every night (exit 0, propose_takes/consolidate/etc. all run).
- Yet `gbrain doctor` shows `[FAIL] cycle_freshness → Source 'default' last cycled NNh ago` indefinitely (observed 61h).

## Repro
1. `gbrain dream --dir ~/brain` → exit 0, "Dream cycle (partial)".
2. `gbrain doctor` → `cycle_freshness` still FAIL / unchanged.
3. `gbrain dream --source default` → exit 0.
4. `gbrain doctor` → `[OK] cycle_freshness: All sources cycled recently`. ✅

## Impact
Highly misleading: operators see "brain hasn't dreamed in N hours" and assume dreaming is broken, when it's running fine nightly — the `--dir` invocation just doesn't update the source's `last_cycled_at`. Cost us a long debugging detour.

## Suggested fix
Either stamp `cycle_freshness` on the `--dir` path too (resolve `--dir` to its source and update `last_cycled_at`), or have `--dir` print a clear notice that it does not stamp per-source freshness (and point to `--source`). Ideally the docs/`doctor` remediation text should mention this distinction.
title: gbrain dream --dir <path> never stamps cycle_freshness (only --source does) → brain shows perpetually stale

body:
## Summary
`gbrain autopilot --install` generates a launcher (`~/.gbrain/autopilot-run.sh`) that assumes **macOS/zsh** and never puts the `bun` bin dir on `PATH`. On Linux (incl. WSL) with a bun-linked gbrain install, the systemd **user** service then crash-loops: exec'ing the gbrain binary triggers its `#!/usr/bin/env bun` shebang, `bun` isn't on the service's `PATH`, and it dies instantly. systemd auto-restarts → infinite loop. **Net effect: autopilot never runs, so the brain never auto-cycles** (`cycle_freshness` stays perpetually stale), and it's easy to miss because manual `gbrain dream`/cron backstops mask it.

## Environment
- Linux (WSL2), bash login shell (no zsh, no `~/.zshenv`/`~/.zshrc`)
- gbrain installed via `bun link` (binary resolves to `~/.bun/bin/gbrain`, a `#!/usr/bin/env bun` script)
- gbrain `0.42.24.0`
- systemd **user** service `gbrain-autopilot.service`

## Symptom
- `systemctl --user status gbrain-autopilot.service` → `activating (auto-restart)`, never `active (running)`
- `~/.gbrain/autopilot.err` fills with (hundreds of KB):
  ```
  env: 'bun': No such file or directory
  env: use -[v]S to pass options in shebang lines
  ```
- `gbrain doctor` → `[FAIL] cycle_freshness → Source 'default' last cycled NNh ago`

## Root cause
The generated `~/.gbrain/autopilot-run.sh`:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Auto-generated by gbrain autopilot --install
# Sources shell profile for API keys, then runs autopilot.
# zshenv is the canonical place for env vars in zsh on macOS ...
[ -f ~/.zshenv ] && source ~/.zshenv 2>/dev/null
source ~/.zshrc 2>/dev/null || source ~/.bashrc 2>/dev/null || true
exec '/home/<user>/.bun/bin/gbrain' autopilot --repo '/home/<user>/brain'
```
On Linux: `~/.zshenv`/`~/.zshrc` don't exist; `~/.bashrc` (non-interactive) typically doesn't export `~/.bun/bin` onto `PATH`. systemd user services start with a minimal `PATH`. So when the kernel runs the `exec`'d gbrain script, its `#!/usr/bin/env bun` shebang can't find `bun` → `env: 'bun': No such file or directory`.

## Repro
1. Linux/WSL, bash. Install gbrain via `bun link`.
2. `gbrain autopilot --install`
3. `systemctl --user status gbrain-autopilot.service` → crash-looping; check `~/.gbrain/autopilot.err`.

## Suggested fix
In the generated launcher (and/or the systemd unit):
1. **Prepend the bun bin dir to `PATH`** — derive it from the resolved bun path (e.g. `dirname "$(command -v bun)"`) at install time, or `export PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH"`.
2. **Don't hardcode zsh** — detect the shell/OS, or just source `~/.gbrain/keys.env` directly for API keys instead of relying on shell rc files (keys already live there).
3. Optionally invoke bun explicitly (`exec bun <gbrain-entry>`) so the shebang isn't load-bearing.

Manual workaround that resolved it: add `export PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH"` (and `source ~/.gbrain/keys.env`) to `autopilot-run.sh`, then `systemctl --user restart gbrain-autopilot.service`.

## Related
- #135 (closed) — a different `autopilot --install` wrapper-generation bug (`process.execPath` returns bun). This one is distinct: macOS/zsh assumption + missing `bun` on `PATH` on Linux.
title: autopilot --install: generated launcher assumes macOS/zsh and omits bun from PATH → systemd service crash-loops on Linux

body:
### Environment
  - gbrain 0.42.23.0 (installed from github default-branch HEAD)
  - Engine: direct Postgres (localhost, no pooler/Supabase), schema v113
  - Personal single-source brain, `default` is repo-backed

  ### Summary
  Facts deposited via **remote MCP `extract_facts`** (`remote=true`) land in the DB with `row_num IS NULL` and are **never written into
  their entity page's `## Facts` fence**. The only code that fences DB→page is the **one-time `v0_32_2` Phase B** migration, which is
  registry-terminal and cannot be re-run. So the "legacy v0.31 facts pending fence backfill" counter is not a one-time historical backlog
  — it **grows with every remote deposit** (observed 587 → 707 over a couple of days), and the `extract_facts` cycle phase stays
  permanently guard-skipped.

  ### Why it happens (root cause)
  1. v0.32.2 contract: new facts write fence-first, then stamp the DB index. But the trust boundary suppresses markdown writes when
  `remote=true` (`src/mcp/server.ts` / `src/core/operations.ts`), so remote `extract_facts` facts are DB-only with `row_num IS NULL`.
  2. The cycle guard (`src/core/cycle/extract-facts.ts:112`) counts `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM facts WHERE row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS
  NOT NULL` and **refuses** the reconcile phase while any exist, pointing the operator to `gbrain apply-migrations --yes`.
  3. But `v0_32_2` is marked `complete` in `~/.gbrain/migrations/completed.jsonl`. `statusForVersion`
  (`src/commands/apply-migrations.ts:145`) returns `complete` if **any** entry is complete — checked **before** the `retry` branch — so
  `--force-retry` cannot regress it, `--force-orchestrator` only resets `wedged` migrations, and `--migration 0.32.2` still buckets it as
  `applied`. The runner only executes `[...partial, ...pending]` (`apply-migrations.ts:420`). There is no supported re-run path.
  4. No standalone fence-backfill command exists (`gbrain backfill` is unknown in this build); the only DB→fence logic is
  `__testing.phaseBFenceFacts` inside the migration.

  Net: remote-deposited facts accumulate as `row_num`-NULL forever, and the prescribed remedy is unreachable. Self-reinforcing: the guard
  message tells you to run a migration that won't run.

  ### Impact
  - `gbrain dream` `extract_facts` phase is permanently skipped (`legacyRowsPending > 0` guard).
  - Facts remain in the DB and stay queryable, but are **never mirrored into the markdown system-of-record**, so a rebuild purely from the
  repo (`gbrain rebuild`) would silently drop them. This breaks the v0.32.2 "DB is fully derivable from the repo" promise for any brain
  that deposits via remote MCP.
  - The backlog and the guard-skip are unbounded over time.

  ### Repro
  1. Postgres brain, v0_32_2 already applied (any normal upgrade).
  2. Deposit several facts via the remote MCP `extract_facts` op (`remote=true`).
  3. `SELECT count(*) FROM facts WHERE row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL;` → grows by each deposit.
  4. `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` → "All migrations up to date." `--migration 0.32.2 --dry-run` → "Already applied. Nothing to do." No
  way to fence them.

  ### Suggested fixes (any one)
  - A first-class, re-runnable command, e.g. `gbrain facts fence-backfill [--dry-run]`, exposing `phaseBFenceFacts` outside the migration
  registry (it's already idempotent on `row_num IS NULL`, atomic per-page, dirty-tree-guarded).
  - Have the cycle `extract_facts` phase fence orphan `row_num`-NULL rows itself (DB→fence) instead of only refusing, so the steady-state
  reconciler closes the loop.
  - Make remote `extract_facts` write its fence row on a subsequent trusted pass (e.g. the `*/15` conveyor or `gbrain extract all --source
  db`) so remote deposits don't perpetually re-arm the guard.

  ### Workaround (what I'm doing meanwhile)
  Parking the backlog (facts stay live/queryable/consolidating) and holding a deliberate out-of-band invocation of
  `__testing.phaseBFenceFacts` (backup → dry-run → apply → verify) as a pre-rebuild escape hatch.
title: Remote `extract_facts` deposits are never re-fenced — `row_num`-NULL backlog grows unboundedly; v0_32_2 is one-time and   un-rerunnable

body:
## `takes extract --from-pages` hardcodes `anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5`, silently returns 0 claims on OpenAI-only brains

### Summary
`gbrain takes extract --from-pages` returns `0 claim(s) from N page(s)` (no error) on a brain configured for OpenAI that has no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, even though the chat gateway is healthy and `facts.extraction_model` is `openai:gpt-4.1-mini`.

### Root cause
`src/core/extract-takes-from-pages.ts` hardcodes the model on the per-page classifier call:

```ts
// src/core/extract-takes-from-pages.ts:177
response = await chat({
  model: opts.model ?? 'anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5',
  ...
});
```

But the documented contract for that param says it should default to the configured model:

```ts
// src/core/extract-takes-from-pages.ts:51
/** Model override; defaults to facts.extraction_model. */
model?: string;
```

Neither caller sets `opts.model`:
- `src/commands/takes.ts` `cmdExtract` (the `takes extract --from-pages` CLI handler)
- `src/commands/jobs.ts` (the `extract-takes-from-pages` Minion handler)

So every invocation resolves to the hardcoded Anthropic id regardless of `facts.extraction_model`.

### Why it fails silently
1. The top-level `isAvailable('chat')` guard checks the gateway's configured chat model (OpenAI here), so it passes.
2. Per page, `chat({ model: 'anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5', ... })` is attempted against Anthropic, which has no key configured.
3. The per-page `try/catch` swallows the failure and `continue`s → 0 claims for that page.
4. The function returns `{ pages_scanned: N, claims_extracted: 0, llm_unavailable: false }`. `llm_unavailable` stays false because the gateway-level guard passed, so the CLI never prints the "chat gateway unavailable" message.

Result: `takes extract --from-pages: 0 claim(s) from N page(s)` with no indication anything went wrong.

### Repro
On a brain with `facts.extraction_model = openai:gpt-4.1-mini` and **no** `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`:

```
gbrain config set takes.bootstrap_enabled true --force
gbrain takes extract --from-pages --source-id default --max-pages 5 --yes
# -> "takes extract --from-pages: 0 claim(s) from 5 page(s)"
```

Set an Anthropic key and the same command extracts claims, confirming the model routing is the cause.

### Suggested fix
Honor `facts.extraction_model` as the JSDoc already promises, keeping the Anthropic Haiku id only as the last-resort fallback for brains that never set it (backward compatible):

```ts
const model =
  opts.model ??
  (await engine.getConfig('facts.extraction_model')) ??
  'anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5';
```

Resolving in `extractTakesFromPages` itself fixes both the CLI and the Minion handler in one place. The interactive consent prompt in `cmdExtract` ("...sends ... page content to Haiku.") should also be updated since the model is now configurable.

PR to follow.

<!-- rod-agent: id=CCVPS4 session=cc-4 host=srv912889 tmux_session=cc-4 tmux_window=1:claude tmux_pane=2:%11 run=20260602T235259Z -->
title: takes extract --from-pages hardcodes anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5, silently returns 0 claims on OpenAI-only brains

body:
Hi — running gbrain on an FS/git-canonical brain (markdown on disk is the committable source of truth, `sync.repo_path` set, DB derived). I hit two related timeline issues and traced both to root cause. Versions:
**0.42.23.0 → 0.42.25.0** (master `9a0bae8`).


---

## Bug 1 — `extractTimelineFromContent` fragments summary-first bullets (I have a tested fix ready)

`gbrain sync` and `extract --source fs` populate `timeline_entries` from
markdown via `extractTimelineFromContent` (`src/commands/extract.ts`). Its
Format-1 parser:

```js
/^-\s+\*\*(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})\*\*\s*\|\s*(.+?)\s*[—–-]\s*(.+)$/gm
// → { source: match[2], summary: match[3] }
```

assumes `**date** | SOURCE — SUMMARY` and splits the text after `|` on the
**first** `[—–-]`. That class includes a plain hyphen, so it mis-splits the
summary-first bullets gbrain actually writes (`**date** | <summary> [Source: …]`):

| Bullet on disk | Stored `summary` (corrupted) |
|---|---|
| `… \| Draft media-release copy went to review. [Source: …]` | `release copy went to review. …` (hyphen in "media-release") |
| `… \| YouGov delivered results. [Source: email, 2026-03-09]` | `03-09]` (hyphen inside the embedded ISO date) |
| `… \| Earliest instance — predating the prior anchor.` | `predating the prior anchor.` (prose em-dash) |

So every `sync` writes fragmented rows. Meanwhile the **DB-source** path
(`extract --source db`) uses `parseTimelineEntries` (`src/core/link-extraction.ts`),
which is line-anchored, takes the whole post-`|` text as the summary, and
parses the same bullets correctly. So there are effectively **two timeline
parsers with divergent semantics**, and the FS/sync one corrupts real data.

**Proposed fix:** have `extractTimelineFromContent`'s Format-1 delegate to
`parseTimelineEntries` (already imported in `extract.ts`), so FS and DB
extraction agree; leave the `### date — title` Format-2 path as-is. This
*does* change the `SOURCE — SUMMARY` split behavior (the text after `|` becomes
the summary verbatim, `source: ''`) — but that heuristic can't distinguish a
delimiter em-dash from a prose em-dash or a hyphen, so unifying on the
summary-first parser seems right. I have this implemented with the two existing
assertions updated and regression tests added (hyphenated word, embedded ISO
date, prose em-dash); `extract.test.ts` + `link-extraction.test.ts` + `tsc`
green. **Glad to open a PR if you're happy with the direction.**

---

## Issue 2 — `add_timeline_entry` / `timeline-add` writes DB-only and strands on FS-canonical brains

`add_timeline_entry` writes only the `timeline_entries` table, never the page
markdown. On an FS-canonical brain the entry is then invisible to `git`, absent
from `gbrain get <slug>`, and **lost on any FS→DB rebuild** (no source row in
the canonical markdown to reconstruct it from). `put_page` already
write-throughs to disk via `writePageThrough` when `sync.repo_path` is set;
`add_timeline_entry` is the one mutating op that doesn't. I see the intent
(`gbrain-allow-direct-insert: … the explicit canonical surface for manual
timeline entries`) — correct for DB-authoritative deployments — but on an
FS-canonical one it silently strands manual entries. (Observed: ~96 manual
`timeline-add`/MCP calls over ~2 weeks all stranded.)

**Why it's not a one-line write-through:** the page keeps timeline in two
representations — `page.timeline` (markdown text, what `writePageThrough`
serializes) and the `timeline_entries` table (unique index
`idx_timeline_dedup (page_id, date, summary, source)`). `add_timeline_entry`
updates only the table, so a post-hoc `writePageThrough` would serialize
`page.timeline` (which lacks the new row). And "insert row **and** append a
bullet" duplicates: the row is `(summary, source)` in columns, while the bullet
re-parses to `summary = "<summary> [Source: …]"`, `source = ''` — a different
dedup tuple.

**Options (your call on architecture):**
1. When `sync.repo_path` is set, append the rendered bullet to the page's
   `## Timeline` on disk (date-ordered, atomic) and skip the direct table
   insert; let the extract cycle reconcile the table. Dup-free, FS-canonical.
2. Splice into `page.timeline` text, persist the page row, then
   `writePageThrough`; table reconciled by the extract cycle.
3. Render `## Timeline` from the table in `serializePageToMarkdown` (bigger
   change to the serialize contract).

Gating on `sync.repo_path` keeps DB-only deployments unaffected; preserving the
subagent-sandbox DB-only path (`viaSubagent` without `allowedSlugPrefixes`,
as `put_page` does) seems right either way.
title: Timeline entries on FS/git-canonical brains: `sync` fragments summary-first bullets, and `add_timeline_entry` writes DB-only (strands on disk)

body:
## Bug description

`gbrain doctor` still reports an Anthropic-only `subagent_capability` warning even when the provider-neutral gateway loop is already enabled via `agent.use_gateway_loop=true`.

This makes the runtime look Anthropic-only even though `src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts` supports the gateway-native loop when the config flag is enabled.

## Environment

- GBrain version: `0.42.10.0`
- Engine: Postgres
- Active schema pack: `gbrain-base-v2@1.0.0+b9bebaa4`
- Local checkout/source inspection: `src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts` routes subagent jobs through `gateway.toolLoop()` when `agent.use_gateway_loop` is true.

## Reproduction

```bash
$ gbrain config get agent.use_gateway_loop
true

$ gbrain doctor --json
```

Relevant doctor check:

```json
{
  "name": "subagent_capability",
  "status": "warn",
  "message": "chat_model is \"deepseek:deepseek-chat\" (non-Anthropic) and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set. Subagent features (gbrain dream, gbrain agent run, gbrain autopilot) will fail at job submission unless agent.use_gateway_loop=true. Chat alone (gbrain think) still works. Either set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or enable: `gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true`."
}
```

But the suggested fix is already applied:

```bash
$ gbrain config get agent.use_gateway_loop
true
```

## Expected behavior

If `agent.use_gateway_loop=true`, `doctor` should not emit the Anthropic-only warning for non-Anthropic `chat_model` / missing `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.

Instead, it should either:

1. report `subagent_capability: ok` if the resolved subagent/gateway model has tool-loop capability; or
2. warn about the actual resolved gateway/subagent model if it lacks tools / is unknown / has degraded capabilities.

## Actual behavior

`doctor` appears to check only:

- `chat_model` is non-Anthropic
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is missing

and returns the warning without checking whether `agent.use_gateway_loop` is already enabled.

## Likely location

`src/commands/doctor.ts::checkSubagentCapability()` around the branch that emits:

```ts
chat_model is "..." (non-Anthropic) and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set...
unless agent.use_gateway_loop=true
```

The check should read `agent.use_gateway_loop` before returning that warning.

## Related context

This is related to, but distinct from, #1454. In #1454 the CLI reportedly rejected `agent.use_gateway_loop`; in this case the key exists and returns `true`, but `doctor` still warns as if it were not enabled.
title: doctor subagent_capability warns about Anthropic even when agent.use_gateway_loop=true

body:
## Summary

`gbrain link <from> <to> --type <T>` reports `status: ok` but stores the row with `link_type = ''` instead of the supplied type. The edge is created, but it's invisible to any downstream consumer that filters or routes on `link_type` (e.g. `gbrain graph-query --type <T>`). Confirmed against `gbrain 0.32.1.0` on a Postgres engine.

The `extract links` path stores `link_type='mentions'` correctly, so the bug is isolated to the manual `link` CLI / `addLink` write — the type argument reaches the layer that returns `ok`, but not the row that lands in `public.links`.

## Repro

In any synced brain with two pages `wiki/people/alice` and `wiki/companies/acme`:

```bash
gbrain link wiki/people/alice wiki/companies/acme --type works_at
# → { "status": "ok" }
```

Inspect:

```sql
SELECT id, link_type
FROM links
WHERE from_page_id = (SELECT id FROM pages WHERE slug='wiki/people/alice')
  AND to_page_id   = (SELECT id FROM pages WHERE slug='wiki/companies/acme');
-- expected: link_type='works_at'
-- actual:   link_type=''
```

## Observed behavior

Eight separate `gbrain link --type T` calls across `works_at | previously_at | founded_by | mentions` all returned `{"status":"ok"}` and all stored with empty `link_type`:

```
 id  | link_type
-----+-----------
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
 ... | ''
```

In the same brain, `extract links` correctly persists the type for edges it builds — `link_type='mentions'` on every row. So the bug is on the explicit `link` write path only.

## Impact

- Manual graph curation via `gbrain link --type` is effectively impossible — every typed edge collapses to the empty bucket.
- `gbrain graph-query <slug> --type <T>` returns nothing for any manually-curated typed edge.
- Workaround is direct SQL `UPDATE links SET link_type=... WHERE id=...`.

## Version

`gbrain 0.32.1.0`
title: `link --type T` silently drops the type; stored `link_type` is empty string

body:
## Summary

`gbrain extract links` (both `--source fs` and `--source db`, `gbrain 0.32.1.0`) inconsistently materializes edges from bare-name `[[wikilinks]]` even when the target slug is unambiguous and the source/target pages are both fully synced into the same source. Some bare-name targets resolve, others don't, with no obvious pattern based on slug shape, file location, or page age.

The same brain shows the resolver working for *some* bare-name links — e.g. `[[acme]]` reliably resolved to `wiki/companies/acme` — while structurally identical links to other pages were silently dropped. Manual `gbrain link` succeeds against the same slugs, so the pages exist and are reachable; the gap is in extract-time wikilink resolution.

## Repro shape

In a brain with these pages (all in the same source, all synced):

```
wiki/companies/acme.md
wiki/companies/widgetco.md
wiki/people/alice.md
wiki/people/bob.md
wiki/people/carol.md
wiki/meetings/alice-2026-02-20.md
```

Each page contains bare-name wikilinks pointing at the others — e.g. `acme.md` has `[[alice]]`, `[[bob]]`, `[[carol]]`, `[[widgetco]]`; person pages have `[[acme]]` and `[[widgetco]]`. Wikilinks live in normal body prose (both prose lines and `- list items`).

Run:

```bash
gbrain sync
gbrain extract links
# Expected: ~10–12 edges materialized across the wikilinks
# Actual:   ~4 edges; ~8 silently dropped
```

`gbrain backlinks <slug>` on the orphaned targets confirms the missing edges aren't there. A second `extract links` run is a no-op (idempotent), so re-running doesn't recover.

## Observed pattern

The resolver succeeded for `[[acme]]` and `[[widgetco]]` (company slugs) but consistently failed for `[[alice]]`, `[[bob]]`, `[[carol]]` (person slugs) in the same brain — including failing links *from* `acme.md`, where on the same line `[[carol]]` was dropped but `[[widgetco]]` later in the line was kept. So it's not a per-page issue; it's a per-target-slug issue.

A path-form wikilink in the same body (`[[meetings/alice-2026-02-20]]`) resolved correctly. Switching the failing bare-name links to `[[wiki/people/alice]]` is a workaround but breaks the established bare-name convention used elsewhere in the brain.

There's also a possible relationship to slug-prefix ambiguity: `bare-name = alice` matches both `wiki/people/alice` *and* the longer `wiki/meetings/alice-2026-02-20`. If the resolver rejects on ambiguity, that would explain `alice` failing. But it does NOT explain `bob` and `carol` failing, where the only candidate slugs were `wiki/people/bob` / `wiki/people/carol`. So if ambiguity-rejection is the rule, it's overshooting.

## Side observation: phantom slugs from prior sync

In the same brain I found three phantom pages with stripped-prefix slugs (`companies/acme`, `people/carol`, `acme-freelance-offer-2026-06`) sitting alongside the real `wiki/companies/...` / `wiki/people/...` pages. These appeared post a clean prune run, were live (not soft-deleted), but pointed at no file on disk. Soft-deleting them did *not* fix the extract gap — the bare-name resolver still missed the same 8 edges afterward — but their existence suggests sync may sometimes write a slug-stripped duplicate alongside the canonical one. Possibly a slug-normalization regression worth a separate investigation.

## Impact

- Graph extraction is partial in a way that's hard to detect without an audit, because edges silently don't materialize.
- Any workflow relying on bare-name `[[link]]` convention for `type: person` / `type: company` pages gets an incomplete graph.
- Manual `gbrain link` is the workaround, but interacts with a separate bug (link --type silently drops the type — filed separately).

## Version

`gbrain 0.32.1.0`
title: `extract links` bare-name wikilink resolver inconsistently drops edges on unambiguous targets

body:
## Summary

_First off — gbrain is great; I've been building a Lark/Feishu → gbrain ingestion integration on top of it. While doing that I hit a reproducible write-corruption bug and root-caused it against the v0.42.2.0 source. Sharing a tight repro + findings below, and I'd be glad to open a PR for any of the suggested directions._

When a `gbrain serve` (MCP, holding the PGLite `.gbrain-lock`) is running, a concurrent
`gbrain capture` / `put_page` against the same brain intermittently fails mid-write. Two
symptoms:

- **A** — with frontmatter `tags`: `put_page failed: addTag failed: page "<slug>" (source=default) not found` — even though `putPage(<slug>)` ran in the *same transaction* immediately before.
- **B** — once a slug has failed this way, **every** subsequent write to that slug permanently fails with `put_page failed: Page not found: <slug> (source=default)`, even when the lock is free and the content is valid. The slug is effectively bricked.

The exact same content written under a **brand-new slug** succeeds. So content/frontmatter are fine; the slug-level state is corrupted.

## Environment

- `gbrain 0.42.2.0`, PGLite engine (embedded Postgres), single-source brain, macOS.
- A long-lived `gbrain serve --stdio-idle-timeout 120` (MCP) is running against the same `~/.gbrain`.

## Reproduce

1. Have `gbrain serve` running (holds `~/.gbrain/brain.pglite/.gbrain-lock`).
2. Run a batch of `gbrain capture --file X.md --slug s-N` in quick succession (e.g. a loop of 6).
3. Observe several fail with `addTag failed: page not found` (if tags present) or `Page not found`.
4. Retry a failed slug **after** the lock is free → still `Page not found` (permanent).
5. Same content, **new** slug → succeeds.

Single, spaced-out captures (lock idle, no concurrency) succeed every time — only concurrent / rapid-batch writes fail.

## What I ruled out

- **Not a `transaction()` bug.** `PGLiteEngine.transaction` (`src/core/pglite-engine.ts:802`) correctly hands the callback a `txEngine` whose `db` getter is redirected to the PGLite transaction handle, so `putPage` + `addTag` + `upsertChunks` in `importFromContent` (`src/core/import-file.ts:612`–`693`) all run on the same `tx`. The implementation is correct.
- **Not tags-specific.** Dropping `tags` changes symptom A→B but doesn't fix it; the slug still bricks.
- **Not a stale-lock / source-id mismatch.** `source=default` is consistent on both write and read paths in the single-source case.

## Hypothesis (root cause)

Multi-process PGLite concurrency. PGLite is an embedded single-writer; `serve` and the CLI
`capture` are separate processes opening the same datadir. When they race (serve holds/re-acquires
the lock while a CLI write is in flight), the in-transaction `addTag` SELECT (`pglite-engine.ts:2922`)
can't see the just-written page, and the partial write leaves orphan state under that slug that
`get_page` reports as `page_not_found` yet blocks future `put_page` upserts. This is likely the same
class tracked in `TODOS.md` as "PGLite serve/sync concurrency".

## Impact

- Batch ingestion (the core need for any ingestion recipe/integration) is unreliable whenever a
  `serve`/MCP is attached to the same brain.
- Worse than a transient failure: a single failed slug is **permanently** unwritable until renamed —
  a transient race is amplified into durable data corruption.

## Code pointers (v0.42.2.0)

- `src/core/import-file.ts:612`–`693` — `engine.transaction(...)` wrapping `putPage` + `addTag` + `upsertChunks`.
- `src/core/pglite-engine.ts:802`–`808` — `transaction()` (correct tx redirection).
- `src/core/pglite-engine.ts:2918`–`2933` — `addTag` (throws `addTag failed: ... not found`).

## Suggested directions

1. **Fail-fast on concurrent access** — CLI `capture`/`put_page` should refuse (clear error) or
   queue when a `serve` holds the lock, instead of proceeding into a partial write.
2. **No partial state on failure** — ensure a failed `put_page` rolls back fully so the slug stays
   clean and retryable (no "dirty slug" brick).
3. **Self-heal** — `gbrain doctor` / a repair op could detect & purge orphan slug state so a bricked
   slug becomes writable again without renaming.

## Minimal repro

Prereq: an initialized gbrain (PGLite engine). Most reliable with a `gbrain serve` attached
to the same brain in another shell (holds the lock); parallel CLI writes alone also contend
the single-writer lock.

```bash
N=8
# Baseline — serial writes: all succeed
for i in $(seq 1 $N); do echo "serial $i" | gbrain capture --stdin --slug r-serial-$i --quiet; done
# Race — parallel writes contend the single-writer PGLite lock
for i in $(seq 1 $N); do echo "conc $i" | gbrain capture --stdin --slug r-conc-$i --json 2>&1 | head -1 & done; wait
# Dirty-slug — a slug that failed once stays permanently unwritable
echo retry | gbrain capture --stdin --slug r-conc-1 --json 2>&1 | head -1   # often: Page not found
echo fresh | gbrain capture --stdin --slug r-fresh  --json 2>&1 | head -1   # succeeds
```

The concurrency race is intermittent, but the **dirty-slug bricking is deterministic once
triggered** — observed verbatim in a real run (identical content; dirty slug bricks, fresh
slug works):

```
$ gbrain capture --file doc.md --slug larkdoc-RzQE…      # serve running concurrently
gbrain capture: put_page failed: Page not found: larkdoc-RzQE… (source=default)

$ gbrain capture --file doc.md --slug probe-full-001     # SAME file, fresh slug
{ "slug": "probe-full-001", "status": "created_or_updated", "chunks": 1, … }   # succeeds
```

(Context: hit while building a Lark/Feishu → gbrain ingestion integration. Happy to refine the repro or pair on a fix.)
title: PGLite: concurrent gbrain capture during serve corrupts writes (addTag failed / Page not found) and leaves permanently unwritable dirty slugs

body:
## Summary

`nightly_quality_probe_health` can log an `error` for missing `test/fixtures/longmemeval-nightly.jsonl` when the probe resolves the repo root to the vault/source directory instead of the installed GBrain repo/package root.

## Environment

- GBrain CLI: 0.42.1.0
- Runtime: WSL Ubuntu via Windows host
- Source/vault directory: `D:/cicada3301/lost9999` / `/mnt/d/cicada3301/lost9999`
- GBrain repo/package checkout: `/home/lost9999/gbrain`

## Observed

Audit row detail:

```text
nightly fixture not found at D:/cicada3301/lost9999/test/fixtures/longmemeval-nightly.jsonl
```

But the fixture exists in the GBrain repo/package:

```text
/home/lost9999/gbrain/test/fixtures/longmemeval-nightly.jsonl
```

Doctor then warns for 7 days because `nightly_quality_probe_health` treats any non-`pass` audit row in the window as warning.

## Expected
n
The nightly probe should resolve `test/fixtures/longmemeval-nightly.jsonl` from the GBrain package/repo root, not from the user's vault/source directory. If the fixture is not shipped in packaged installs, either ship it or make the probe fail with a clearer install/package diagnostic.

## Related code

- `src/core/cycle/nightly-quality-probe.ts`
- `NIGHTLY_FIXTURE_REL_PATH = 'test/fixtures/longmemeval-nightly.jsonl'`
- `runNightlyQualityProbe(... deps.resolveRepoRoot())`

## Why this matters

This is not a user data problem; the committed fixture exists in the codebase. The current diagnostic points users at creating a fixture inside their vault, which would be the wrong layer.
title: nightly quality probe resolves longmemeval fixture from vault root instead of package root

body:
## Summary

`gbrain doctor` reports all image assets missing on a Windows + WSL setup when `files.storage_path` contains Windows drive paths such as `D:/...`, even though the files exist and are readable when translated to `/mnt/d/...`.

## Environment

- GBrain CLI: 0.42.1.0
- Runtime: WSL Ubuntu
- Vault/source path from `gbrain sources list`: `/mnt/d/cicada3301/lost9999`
- Stored `files.storage_path` sample: `D:/cicada3301/lost9999/...jpg`
- DB image rows: `248`

## Observed

Doctor warning:

```text
image_assets warn 248 of 248 image(s) missing from disk (e.g. D:/cicada3301/lost9999/...). Fix: restore from git, or `gbrain sync --skip-failed` to acknowledge.
```

Direct verification:

- `select count(*) from files where mime_type like 'image/%'` => `248`
- All sampled/translated paths exist on disk when `D:/...` is resolved as `/mnt/d/...` under WSL.
- Windows/Git-bash can also read the `D:/...` path form.

## Expected

Doctor should not classify valid Windows drive paths as missing when running under WSL. It should either:

1. normalize `^[A-Za-z]:/` paths to `/mnt/<drive>/...` when WSL is detected; or
2. resolve asset paths relative to the source root; or
3. use the same path-normalization layer used by sync/ingest.

## Why this matters

The current warning suggests data loss and recommends restore/sync acknowledgement, but the assets are present. Rewriting DB paths from `D:/...` to `/mnt/d/...` locally is risky because Windows-side tools may depend on the Windows path form.
title: doctor image_assets treats Windows D:/ paths as missing under WSL

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

The following changes would improve agentic use. The goal is for agents to spend less tokens on CLI discoverability and use.

**What does the solution look like?**

- Distinct exit codes for error classes: no exit code documentation found in help text
       → Document and use distinct exit codes (e.g., 1=error, 2=usage, 4=auth) so agents can classify failures without parsing stderr.

- JSON output support: no --output/--format/--json/-o flag with JSON support found in any command
       → Add `--output json` flag to all commands that produce output.

**Alternatives considered**

None
title: [Agent] Reduce CLI token consumption for agents at discovery stage

body:
## Summary

The company-brain tutorial documents a multi-source setup where each source has its own checked-out git repo via `sources.local_path`:

```bash
gbrain sources add shared --path /srv/brain-repos/shared --name "Shared company wiki"
gbrain sources add customers --path /srv/brain-repos/customers --name "Customer notes"
gbrain sources add internal --path /srv/brain-repos/internal --name "Internal-only"
```

In current `put_page` / capture write-through code, disk persistence appears to use the global `sync.repo_path` config instead of the target source's `sources.local_path`.

That leaves source-aware write-through ambiguous for the documented separate-repo company-brain layout.

Related broader issue: #1732.

## Observed behavior

Current upstream `src/core/write-through.ts` resolves the write-through root with:

```ts
const repoPath = await engine.getConfig('sync.repo_path');
```

Then it writes the page with `resolvePageFilePath(repoPath, slug, sourceId)`, which routes non-default sources to:

```text
<sync.repo_path>/.sources/<source_id>/<slug>.md
```

That works for a single global brain repo using the `.sources/<source_id>/...` layout, but it does not write to the separate source repo registered for the target source.

## Expected / question

For a company-brain setup using separate checked-out repos, should a write scoped to source `shared` write through to:

```text
/srv/brain-repos/shared/<slug>.md
```

by looking up `sources.local_path` for `shared`?

If yes, `writePageThrough` could prefer the target source's `sources.local_path` and fall back to the current `sync.repo_path` behavior for legacy/single-repo layouts.

Rough behavior:

1. If the target source has `sources.local_path`, write `<local_path>/<slug>.md`.
2. Else, if `sync.repo_path` exists, preserve current behavior: `<sync.repo_path>/.sources/<source_id>/<slug>.md` for non-default sources.
3. Else, keep current DB-only skip behavior.

## Why this matters

The README says captures land in the database and on disk, and the company-brain tutorial shows each source path as a checked-out git repo. A user following that tutorial may reasonably expect MCP/capture writes scoped to a source to materialize in that source's repo path.

Currently, in a separate-repo company-brain deployment with no global `sync.repo_path`, `put_page` writes are DB-only. If `sync.repo_path` is set to one of the source repos, write-through can land in the wrong layout for the multi-repo setup.

## Environment where observed

- GBrain: `0.42.8.0`
- Upstream commit checked: `0bfe0d0c7ebda6f2ab706bbddc023b8c8db21647`
- Backend: Postgres
- Layout: multiple company-brain sources with separate git repos under `/srv/brain-repos/<source-id>`

## Notes

This is narrower than #1732. The remote host does have source checkouts; the gap is that write-through does not appear to use the source-specific checkout path.

This issue is mainly asking for intended-behavior clarification. If source-aware write-through is intended, I can help with a small PR and tests.
title: Clarify/source-aware write-through for company-brain sources.local_path repos

body:
**Type:** Performance / feature
**Version observed:** gbrain 0.42.10.0
**Severity:** Medium — the routine cycle/extract embed path runs embedding batches **serially**, so multi-batch embeds (long pages, fact extraction, contextual reindex) are gated on sequential API round-trips even though `embed-stale.ts` already proves bounded parallelism works elsewhere.

## TL;DR

`embedBatch()` in `src/core/embedding.ts` chunks `texts` into `BATCH_SIZE` slices and `await`s each slice **one at a time** (a serial `for` loop). For any caller with more than one batch worth of inputs, total time = sum of per-batch API latencies, when it could be `~max` of them with bounded concurrency. The infra to do this right already exists in `src/core/embed-stale.ts` (a `concurrency` worker-pool, default 20) — it's just not used by the general `embedBatch()` path that the cycle/extract callers go through. Request: give `embedBatch()` bounded parallelism (shared with / modeled on `embed-stale.ts`), configurable, default conservative.

## What's serial today

`src/core/embedding.ts` (~line 91):

```ts
export async function embedBatch(texts, options = {}) {
  if (!texts?.length) return [];
  // fast path: single batch
  if (texts.length <= BATCH_SIZE && !options.onBatchComplete) {
    return gatewayEmbed(texts, gwOpts);
  }
  const results = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < texts.length; i += BATCH_SIZE) {     // <-- serial
    const slice = texts.slice(i, i + BATCH_SIZE);
    const out = await gatewayEmbed(slice, gwOpts);          // <-- one round-trip at a time
    results.push(...out);
    options.onBatchComplete?.(results.length, texts.length);
  }
  return results;
}
```

Hot callers that hit the multi-batch path:
- `src/core/cycle/extract-facts.ts:238` — `const embeddings = await embed(texts)` in the routine cycle.
- contextual-retrieval / reindex paths that embed many chunks per page.

## Why this matters (measured context)

#1207 already documented the macro symptom: serial per-file embedding makes `gbrain import` run at ~2.1 s/file because each file is one API round-trip. That issue targets `import` batching. **This issue is the complementary, narrower gap:** even once you batch, `embedBatch()` dispatches the batches *sequentially*. On a provider that accepts 2048 inputs/call but where each call is ~300–2000 ms, a page/extract job that produces, say, 6 batches pays 6× the latency instead of ~1–2× with a concurrency of 3–4.

This is **not** "add batching" (that exists) and **not** the `embed-stale.ts` path (that's already concurrent). It's specifically: the general-purpose `embedBatch()` that the cycle/extract/contextual paths call is the one place still serial.

## Proposed implementation

1. **Bounded-parallel slice dispatch in `embedBatch()`.** Replace the serial `for` with a worker-pool of size `concurrency` (mirror `embed-stale.ts`'s `numWorkers = Math.min(concurrency, keys.length)` pattern). Preserve input order in `results` (index slices, don't rely on completion order). Keep `onBatchComplete` firing as each slice resolves.
2. **Config knob.** `EmbedBatchOptions.concurrency` (default e.g. 4 — conservative to respect provider rate limits), overridable via env `GBRAIN_EMBED_CONCURRENCY`. Single-batch fast path unchanged.
3. **Rate-limit / 429 safety.** Reuse the existing retry/backoff in `gatewayEmbed`; ensure the pool honors `abortSignal` at the same three sites `embed-stale.ts` documents (batch claim, retry sleep, HTTP body). On repeated 429s, the pool should back off effective concurrency rather than hammer.
4. **Share the primitive.** Ideally factor the bounded-concurrency map out of `embed-stale.ts` into a small shared helper so both paths use one implementation (DRY; one place to get backpressure right).

## Acceptance

- A multi-batch `embedBatch()` call with concurrency N completes in ~`ceil(batches/N)` API-latency units instead of `batches` units, order-preserving.
- `extract-facts` / contextual reindex throughput improves proportionally on multi-chunk pages, with no increase in 429 error rate at the default concurrency.
- `abortSignal` cancels in-flight and queued slices promptly.
- Single-batch callers see byte-identical behavior (fast path untouched).

## Related

- #1207 — `gbrain import` per-file embedding batching (the macro case; complementary, not duplicate).
- #1199 / #970 / #1080 — provider per-batch size limits; the concurrency pool must respect these existing `max_batch_*` recipe fields when sizing slices.
- `src/core/embed-stale.ts` — existing bounded-concurrency implementation to model on / extract from.

---
_Filed 2026-06-03 alongside the connection-pooler investigation. The DB round-trip is a minority of sync wall-clock; embedding parallelism is the higher-leverage speedup for embedding-bound jobs._
title: embedBatch() dispatches batches serially — give it bounded parallelism (the embed-stale.ts concurrency pool the cycle/extract path doesn't use)

body:
**Type:** Feature request / performance
**Version observed:** gbrain 0.42.10.0 (Postgres engine, Supabase Supavisor **transaction** pooler, port 6543, `prepare:false`)
**Severity:** Medium — leaves a measured ~2x DB round-trip speedup on the table for Supabase Pro+ users, and the current "always disable prepared statements" posture is correct for the *shared* pooler but wrong for the *dedicated* one.

## TL;DR

gbrain currently hard-requires `prepare:false` (no prepared statements) whenever it talks to a Supabase pooler, because the **shared** Supavisor transaction pooler (`<region>.pooler.supabase.com:6543`) genuinely can't support them — connections swap between transactions, so a prepared plan on one backend isn't there on the next. That's the right call for the shared pooler.

But Supabase **Pro+ plans ship a co-located Dedicated Pooler (PgBouncer ≥1.21) that supports prepared statements *in transaction mode***. On that endpoint, gbrain could keep transaction-mode connection multiplexing (no session-mode connection-exhaustion risk) **and** turn prepared statements back on. Request: detect/support the Dedicated Pooler endpoint and enable prepared statements when connected to it (or via an explicit opt-in), instead of a blanket `prepare:false`.

## Background / measured data

On a real brain (Supabase Pro, us-east-1), a controlled A/B of 200 sequential parameterized `SELECT`s through the `postgres` client:

| Endpoint | Mode | prepare | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `<region>.pooler.supabase.com:6543` (shared) | transaction | false (current) | **125 ms/query, 8 q/s** |
| `<region>.pooler.supabase.com:5432` (shared) | session | true | **63 ms/query, 16 q/s** |

So ~2x on raw DB round-trips. **Important caveat:** most of that 2x is the extra Supavisor proxy hop in transaction mode, not prepared statements per se — and for embedding/LLM-bound jobs (sync/import) the DB slice is a minority of wall-clock, so end-to-end this is more like a 10–20% win there. It matters most for query-heavy paths (search, graph traversal, fact extraction) that do many small round-trips.

## Why NOT just flip to session mode (:5432)

The obvious "free" fix — point at the session-mode port so prepared statements come back — reintroduces a failure class gbrain already has open bugs for. Supabase's own docs warn session mode dedicates one backend connection per client until released; with a supervisor + N concurrent workers + autopilot + every one-off CLI invocation, that exhausts the pool. See the spirit of #1163 (session-pool exhaustion). gbrain deliberately uses transaction mode + `prepare:false` to get connection multiplexing — correctly. **The point of this issue is to keep transaction-mode multiplexing while recovering prepared statements via the Dedicated Pooler, not to trade one for the other.**

## The Dedicated Pooler (the actual "solved problem")

Supabase Management API for a Pro project returns a dedicated PgBouncer config:

```
GET /v1/projects/<ref>/config/database/pgbouncer
{
  "pool_mode": "transaction",
  "connection_string": "postgres://postgres:[PW]@db.<ref>.supabase.co:6543/postgres",
  ...
}
```

This endpoint is co-located PgBouncer ≥1.21, which tracks named prepared statements at the protocol level — i.e. **transaction mode *with* working prepared statements.** (Caveat from our box: the `db.<ref>.supabase.co` host resolved IPv6-only and `:6543` was unreachable from an IPv4 path, so reachability/IPv6 has to be validated per-deploy — see "Open questions".)

## Proposed implementation

1. **Endpoint-aware prepare policy in the connection manager (`src/core/db.ts`).** Today the prepare flag is effectively pinned off for poolers. Make it a function of the *endpoint shape*:
   - host matches `*.pooler.supabase.com` (shared Supavisor) → `prepare:false` (unchanged, required).
   - host matches `db.<ref>.supabase.co` with `pool_mode=transaction` (dedicated PgBouncer ≥1.21) → `prepare:true` allowed.
   - direct `db.<ref>.supabase.co:5432` / session mode → `prepare:true` allowed.
2. **Explicit override env** `GBRAIN_PREPARE=auto|true|false` (default `auto` = the policy above). `auto` removes the footgun where a user sets `GBRAIN_PREPARE=true` against the shared transaction pooler and gets `prepared statement does not exist` errors. (Today the kill-switch handling is already fragile — see #1268.)
3. **Doctor surfacing.** `gbrain doctor` should report the detected pooler type + effective prepare setting (e.g. `pooler=dedicated-pgbouncer prepare=true` vs `pooler=shared-supavisor-tx prepare=false`), so the operator can see whether prepared statements are actually on.
4. **Docs.** A short "Supabase connection modes" note: shared transaction pooler (default, prepare off), dedicated pooler (Pro+, prepare on, recommended for query-heavy brains), session mode (prepare on but connection-exhaustion risk — discouraged for multi-worker setups).

## Acceptance

- Against the shared `:6543` pooler, `prepare` stays false with no behavior change.
- Against the dedicated PgBouncer endpoint, prepared statements are enabled and a query-heavy workload shows the expected round-trip reduction with no `prepared statement (does not exist|already exists)` errors.
- `GBRAIN_PREPARE=auto` (default) picks correctly from the endpoint; explicit `true`/`false` overrides and is honored on first connect (guards against the #1268 first-connect bypass).
- `doctor` reports pooler type + prepare state.

## Open questions

- Dedicated Pooler endpoint was IPv6-only / unreachable from at least one IPv4 deploy — does it need an IPv4 add-on, and should gbrain fall back to shared `:6543` (prepare off) with a clear `doctor` warning when the dedicated endpoint is unreachable?
- Worth confirming the installed PgBouncer version exposes `max_prepared_statements > 0` before flipping prepare on, rather than trusting the endpoint shape alone.

## Related

- #1268 (`GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL` ignored on first connect) — same connection-manager surface; the `auto` prepare policy should land in coordination with that.
- #1163 (session-pool exhaustion) — the reason session mode is *not* the answer.

---
_Filed from a connection-tuning investigation, 2026-06-03. Conclusion: don't flip to session mode globally; recover prepared statements via the dedicated pooler instead._
title: Recover prepared statements via Supabase Dedicated Pooler (transaction mode) instead of blanket prepare:false — endpoint-aware prepare policy + GBRAIN_PREPARE=auto
isAvailable('embedding') returns false for llama-server/litellm even with a concrete model configured → hybridSearch silently runs keyword-only

body:
### Summary

On a Postgres-engine brain, the `extract_facts` phase of `gbrain dream` appears **non-idempotent**: re-running the cycle on unchanged content re-inserts the same facts as new rows (and new `## Facts` fence rows), so duplicates accumulate roughly linearly with the number of cycle runs. Over weeks of a nightly `dream`, the `facts` table grows to many multiples of the distinct fact set, and individual entity-page fences end up containing dozens of byte-identical rows.

### Environment

- gbrain `v0.42.8.0` (also observed on the state inherited from `v0.37.2.0`)
- Engine: Postgres (`postgres-engine`), pgvector, `text-embedding-3-large` @ 1536d
- Multi-source brain (`default` + one federated source), nightly `gbrain dream` via cron
- Schema version 112

### Observed

```
SELECT count(*) total,
       count(DISTINCT (entity_slug, fact)) distinct_facts,
       count(*) - count(DISTINCT (entity_slug, fact)) dupes
FROM facts;
-- total=4360  distinct_facts=929  dupes=3431
```

A single fact's text repeats as many identical **fenced** rows on its entity page (`row_num IS NOT NULL`), e.g. one company page's `## Facts` fence held ~85 byte-identical rows of the same claim. ~3142 of the 3431 duplicate rows are fenced (in the markdown system-of-record), not just stray DB rows.

The duplicate count tracks the number of `dream` runs (≈ one extra copy of each fact per cycle), which is consistent with `extract_facts` re-extracting + re-appending facts that already exist rather than treating them as already-known.

`gbrain sync` alone (no `dream`) does **not** add duplicates — only cycles that run `extract_facts` do.

### Expected

Re-running `dream` on unchanged content should be idempotent: an already-extracted fact (already present in the DB and/or the page's `## Facts` fence) should not be re-inserted / re-appended. The fence's row-level dedup (the `(claim, source)` key used by `v0.32.2`'s `upsertFactRow`) does not appear to be applied on the `extract_facts` write path.

### Impact

- `facts` table and entity-page fences grow unbounded over nightly cycles.
- Search returns many byte-identical fact chunks for a single query (diversity loss, wasted context tokens), and every duplicate is embedded (cost).
- There is no `gbrain facts dedup` command to recover, and a DB-only dedup would desync the markdown fences (which contain the duplicates), risking re-wedging the `v0.32.2` verify.

### Questions

1. Is `extract_facts` intended to dedup against existing facts (by `content_hash` / `(claim, source)`)? If so, what conditions cause it to miss exact-text repeats?
2. Is there a supported way to dedup an existing brain's facts + fences in lockstep?

Happy to provide more detail or a minimal repro brain.
title: dream extract_facts non-idempotent: facts + fence rows duplicate each cycle (Postgres engine)

body:
Summary

gbrain already lets me choose my embedding provider from 16 recipes — I run llama-server:<model> against a local llama.cpp endpoint and it works great. But the reasoning LLM (multi-query expansion, extract-facts, the dream cycle, the Minions/jobs work worker) appears hardcoded to Anthropic Claude — ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is required and config show exposes no chat/reasoning model key. This asks for the reasoning tier to be configurable the same way embeddings already are.

Use case

The reasoning calls split cleanly into two latency classes:

Hot / interactive (query expansion when a human is waiting) — wants a fast, high-quality hosted model. Anthropic is a great default here.
Latency-tolerant batch (dream, extract-facts, overnight enrichment via the worker) — nobody is waiting. For these I want to run a local or cheaper model (e.g. a quantized OSS model behind an OpenAI-compatible endpoint like llama.cpp llama-server / llama-swap) for cost control and data locality, while keeping Anthropic for the hot path.

Right now the only way to get any of gbrain's cognition off Anthropic is to not use it — there's no "parallel"/secondary reasoning model.

Proposal

Mirror the embedding-provider design on the reasoning side:

Add a reasoning_model config key (optionally a separate expansion_model) resolved through the same recipe system that already powers embedding_model (llama-server:, openai:, litellm:, ollama:, …). Default = the current anthropic:claude-haiku-* so existing behavior is unchanged.
Honor an OpenAI-compatible base-URL override for the chat endpoint, exactly as embeddings already do (the LLAMA_SERVER_BASE_URL pattern).
Route expansion / extract-facts / dream / the Minions worker through the configured reasoning model instead of the hardcoded client.

Why this is low-risk

Default unchanged — Anthropic stays the out-of-box reasoning provider; this is purely additive and opt-in.
Same pattern you already shipped — the recipe + base-URL machinery exists for embeddings; this reuses it for chat/completions rather than inventing anything.
Unblocks the local-batch + cost + data-locality story without touching the interactive quality bar.

Precedent
You already did exactly this for embeddings — 16 provider recipes including local llama-server and ollama. This is the same treatment for the reasoning tier.
Happy to open a PR if you're open to the shape above.
title: Make the reasoning/expansion LLM configurable via provider recipes (parity with embedding providers)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain check-backlinks check` reports false "missing back-link" gaps for pages that **are** correctly, bidirectionally linked. The root cause is a substring check that requires a `.md` file extension, while gbrain's own link convention writes links **without** the extension.
