As a side effect, `check-backlinks fix` is the *only* thing that produces links the checker will subsequently accept — and it does so by injecting a `| Referenced in …` timeline-row format that does not match the convention used anywhere else in a brain.

Version: **gbrain 0.42.1.0**

## Root cause (code-level)

`src/commands/backlinks.ts`:

```ts
/** Check if a page already contains a back-link to a given source file */
export function hasBacklink(targetContent: string, sourceFilename: string): boolean {
  return targetContent.includes(sourceFilename);   // sourceFilename = basename WITH ".md"
}
```

`sourceFilename` is `basename(page.relPath)` — e.g. `venture-x.md` (line ~102). So a target page is only credited with a back-link if its raw text contains the literal substring `venture-x.md`.

But gbrain links are written **without** the `.md` suffix, e.g.:

```md
a fellow **[Venture X](programs/venture-x)** participant
- [Venture X](programs/venture-x)
```

`targetContent.includes("venture-x.md")` is therefore `false` even when the page links to that slug multiple times. Every correctly-linked pair is reported as a gap.

## Reproduction

Two pages that link to each other with standard markdown:

- `people/ben-gottlieb.md` → links to `[Venture X](programs/venture-x)` (4 occurrences, including a `## See Also`-style bullet)
- `programs/venture-x.md` → links back to `[Ben Gottlieb](people/ben-gottlieb)` (3 occurrences)

```
$ grep -c 'venture-x\.md'      people/ben-gottlieb.md   # 0  (links omit .md)
$ grep -c 'programs/venture-x' people/ben-gottlieb.md   # 4  (actual links)

$ gbrain check-backlinks check
...
  people/ben-gottlieb.md <- programs/venture-x.md
    "Ben Gottlieb" mentioned in "Venture X"
...
Found 76 missing back-link(s)
```

The pair is fully linked in both directions, yet flagged as missing. On a real brain this produces a large, permanent, non-actionable count (76 here) that never goes down no matter how correctly the brain is linked.

### Confirming it's the checker, not the brain

- Adding correct `## See Also` `[Text](slug)` links **increases** the reported count (each new mention adds a one-way gap that the no-`.md` link can't satisfy).
- The brain's own quality scorers disagree with `check-backlinks`: `gbrain doctor` reports `links 25/25`, `dead-links 10/10`, ~45% inbound coverage, integrity OK.

## Secondary bug: `fix` injects a non-convention format

The only thing `hasBacklink` *will* accept is a path containing `.md`, which is exactly what `fix` writes via `buildBacklinkEntry`:

```ts
return `- **${date}** | Referenced in [${sourceTitle}](${sourcePath})`;  // sourcePath ends in .md
```

Consequences:
- This `| Referenced in …` timeline-row format appears in **zero** organically-authored brain pages (the convention is inline `[Text](slug)` links and a `## See Also` section).
- Because it's the only `.md`-bearing form, `fix` is effectively self-justifying: it "resolves" gaps by writing rows in a shape no other tooling or convention emits, polluting page bodies / Timeline sections.
- It also force-creates a `## Timeline` section on pages that don't have one (line ~177).

## Suggested fix

Make `hasBacklink` (and the gap detection) match gbrain's actual link convention instead of a `.md`-suffixed basename. Concretely, credit a back-link when the target page contains a markdown link to the source **slug** (with or without `.md`), e.g. compare against the extension-less slug `programs/venture-x` and/or parse links via the same `extractEntityRefs`/`link-extraction.ts` path already used for the forward direction. Reusing the canonical link extractor for *both* directions would make the check symmetric and convention-aware, and would make `check`'s count actually reach zero on a well-linked brain.

If desired I can open a PR.
title: check-backlinks: standard links (no .md suffix) never credited → false "N missing back-links"; fix injects non-convention rows

body:
## Summary

`gbrain brainstorm` and `gbrain lsd` throw a hard error when `models.default` is set to a non-Anthropic model (e.g. `deepseek:deepseek-chat`), instead of falling back gracefully.

## Error

```
brainstorm.lsd: no pricing entry for model "deepseek:deepseek-chat" (kind=chat). Add it to src/core/anthropic-pricing.ts or drop --max-cost.
```

Passing `--max-cost 999` does not help — the error fires before cost evaluation.

## Steps to reproduce

```bash
gbrain config set models.default deepseek:deepseek-chat
gbrain lsd "any topic" --yes
# → hard error, exits 1
gbrain brainstorm "any topic" --yes
# → hard error, exits 1
```

## Context

- **gbrain version**: v0.42.8.0
- **Platform**: macOS (home-mac)
- `gbrain think` works fine with the same DeepSeek config (routes correctly, produces output).
- `gbrain dream` also works (extract_atoms runs with DeepSeek).
- Only `brainstorm` and `lsd` hit this code path.

## Root cause (from source inspection)

`src/core/anthropic-pricing.ts` only has Anthropic model entries. The comment in that file says:

> non-Anthropic models (gemini, gpt, anything not in this map) bypass the budget gate with a `BUDGET_METER_NO_PRICING` warn once per process.

But `brainstorm`/`lsd`'s cost-guard path does **not** use that bypass — it throws instead. The two code paths are inconsistent.

## Expected behavior

Either:
1. Add DeepSeek pricing to the table (DeepSeek V3 / deepseek-chat: ~$0.27/$1.10 per 1M tokens as of 2026-06), or
2. Apply the same `BUDGET_METER_NO_PRICING` bypass that dream uses for unknown providers, so brainstorm/lsd continue (with a warning) instead of aborting.

Option 2 seems more robust since it handles any future provider automatically.
title: brainstorm/lsd: hard error for non-Anthropic models due to missing pricing entry

body:
## Summary

`gbrain doctor` / `gbrain check-resolvable` appears to treat all skills under an OpenClaw workspace `skills/` directory as if they are part of the gbrain skill resolver surface. That creates false hard failures for ClawHub/OpenClaw workspace skills that are runtime integrations rather than gbrain memory-workflow skills.

In my case, two ClawHub-installed OpenClaw skills caused `resolver_health` to fail:

- `agentmail`
- `printing-press-library`

Both were installed under the OpenClaw workspace skill root and had ClawHub install metadata, but lacked gbrain-style `triggers:` frontmatter and `AGENTS.md` / `RESOLVER.md` routing rows.

The issue is not that these two particular skills need better metadata. The broader problem is that gbrain is assuming "installed workspace skill" means "must be routable by gbrain's resolver."

## Environment

- gbrain: `0.42.1.0`
- Engine: PGLite
- Host: macOS / OpenClaw workspace
- Workspace-local skills directory contained only two ClawHub skills:
  - `skills/agentmail`
  - `skills/printing-press-library`

Both had `.clawhub/origin.json` metadata, e.g. registry `https://clawhub.ai`, slug, installed version, installed timestamp.

## Repro shape

1. In an OpenClaw workspace, install one or more ClawHub skills into the workspace-local skill root.
2. Do not add gbrain-specific `triggers:` frontmatter or resolver rows for those skills.
3. Run:

```bash
gbrain doctor --json --fast
# or
gbrain check-resolvable --json
```

Observed resolver output included:

```json
{
  "type": "unreachable",
  "skill": "agentmail",
  "message": "Skill 'agentmail' is in manifest but has no trigger row in RESOLVER.md or AGENTS.md"
}
```

and:

```json
{
  "type": "mece_gap",
  "skill": "agentmail",
  "message": "Skill 'agentmail' has no triggers: field in its SKILL.md frontmatter"
}
```

Same pattern for `printing-press-library`.

Doctor moved from `unhealthy` to `warnings` only after manually adding:

- `triggers:` frontmatter to both ClawHub skills
- a small routing table in `AGENTS.md` pointing to those two skills

That workaround makes the checker happy, but it feels semantically wrong: an OpenClaw email integration should not necessarily become a gbrain "brain operations" route just because it exists in the workspace skill directory.

## Why this matters

GBrain's resolver-health model makes sense for gbrain's own skillpack. Those skills are operating procedures for memory work: query, capture, ingest, enrich, maintain, citation fixing, etc. For those, checking that every skill is reachable is useful.

But OpenClaw workspace skills are a broader category. Some are runtime capabilities or app integrations, not gbrain workflow skills. Examples from this case:

- `agentmail` is tied to OpenClaw config / SecretRefs / an agent email identity.
- `printing-press-library` is a tool-discovery/catalog skill.

Treating every ClawHub workspace skill as resolver-required means any ClawHub skill without gbrain-specific metadata can make `gbrain doctor` fail, even if gbrain itself is otherwise healthy.

This also pushes gbrain-specific frontmatter expectations into the broader ClawHub ecosystem, which seems backwards: ClawHub skills should not need to carry gbrain resolver metadata unless they explicitly participate in gbrain's skill router.

## Expected behavior

One of these would be less surprising:

1. `resolver_health` only audits skills in gbrain's configured skill roots / skillpack manifest, not arbitrary OpenClaw workspace skills.
2. Workspace/ClawHub skills are treated as external or optional unless explicitly opted into gbrain resolver checking.
3. Missing resolver metadata on ClawHub workspace skills is a warning, not a hard `resolver_health` failure.
4. `check-resolvable` distinguishes "installed tool/runtime skill exists" from "gbrain workflow skill must be routable."

## Related

This is related to #386, but a different failure mode. #386 is about overlay skill roots and avoiding local-edit conflicts. This issue is specifically about resolver-health scope: gbrain is auditing OpenClaw/ClawHub workspace skills as if they are required gbrain-routable skills.

## Suggested direction

My preference would be to narrow the resolver-health input set or add an explicit opt-in/opt-out boundary for workspace skills, e.g.:

- only skills installed by `gbrain skillpack ...` are strict resolver-health participants;
- ClawHub/OpenClaw workspace skills are ignored or soft-warned by default;
- users can explicitly opt a workspace skill into resolver checking when it is intended to act as a gbrain workflow skill.
title: doctor resolver_health treats ClawHub workspace skills as required gbrain-routable skills

body:
## Environment

- `gbrain serve --http` on a remote host (Railway), Supabase Postgres backend, single user.
- No local brain checkout on the client (thin client; all ops route via MCP/CLI to the remote engine).
- Active source is a **named source** (not `default`).
- The brain (~1.3k pages) uses **flat namespaces**: `people/`, `companies/`, `concepts/`, `originals/`, etc. No `wiki/` pages exist.

## Symptom: synthesize has never produced a page here

Every dream-cycle run for two weeks writes the same summary:

```
# Dream cycle YYYY-MM-DD
**Children:** 0 completed, 2 failed/timeout.
**Pages written:** 0.
```

- 13 consecutive runs, 2026-05-16 → 2026-05-28, all identical: **2 children dispatched, 0 completed, all failed/timeout, 0 pages written.**
- So there WAS corpus to process (children were dispatched), but the synthesis children never complete.
- The dream-cycle **summary page itself is written correctly** (to the named source), so the orchestrator + summary writer work; it is the **synthesis child subagents** that fail.

## Likely root cause (hypothesis, not confirmed)

This strongly correlates with **#1471**, whose title literally describes the mechanism: *"resolveLintContentSanity disconnects shared module-level db singleton, killing the cycle's main engine connection."* The cycle runs `lint` before `synthesize`; if the synthesize children inherit/share the engine connection that the lint phase tears down, a fail/timeout with 0 writes is the expected shape. (Happy to pull worker logs if useful.)

## Compounding issues — synthesize would mis-file even if children succeeded

Even after the fail/timeout is fixed, synthesize output would not land usefully on this deployment:

1. **Hard-coded `source_id: 'default'` (#1586).** The child put_page slugs are returned with `source_id: 'default'` (`src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts`, ~L871: `.map(slug => ({ slug, source_id: 'default' }))`), and a code comment acknowledges the gap (`...default to default for current dream-cycle product behavior`). On a brain whose active source is not `default`, synthesized pages would land in the wrong source (a multi-source-drift symptom; see also #1123 on the missing remediation CLI surfaces). The orchestrator's own summary writer does NOT have this problem (it used the correct source).

2. **Hard-coded `wiki/` output paths, not configurable.** `buildSynthesisPrompt` instructs children to write to literal `wiki/personal/reflections/...` and `wiki/originals/ideas/...` (`src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts`, ~L989/L992). The allow-list (`_brain-filing-rules.json` → `dream_synthesize_paths.globs`) is also `wiki/`-only and is enforcement-only (it does not redirect the prompt). On a brain that files into flat namespaces (`originals/`, `people/`, `concepts/`), this means synthesized content lands in a separate `wiki/` namespace with **no reconciliation** (verified: `(source_id, slug)` is the unique key; `slug_aliases` only handles explicit registered redirects; no automatic prefix collapse). Result: a permanently fragmented brain (`wiki/originals/x` vs `originals/x` are two unrelated pages). There is currently **no way to align synthesize's output paths to a brain's filing convention without patching `buildSynthesisPrompt`** (the prompt uses literals, not config).

## Requests

1. Investigate the child fail/timeout (is it #1471, or a separate child-timeout)? This is the blocker — synthesize produces nothing.
2. Make synthesize **source-aware** (resolve the active source instead of hard-coding `default`) — #1586.
3. Make synthesize **output paths configurable** (read target prefixes from `_brain-filing-rules.json` / config in `buildSynthesisPrompt`) so deployments with flat namespaces don't fragment. At minimum, document that synthesize requires the `wiki/` convention.
title: dream-cycle synthesize writes 0 pages on a remote/Supabase deployment — children fail/timeout (likely #1471); compounded by source=default (#1586) and hard-coded wiki/ output paths

body:
## Summary

During `gbrain dream`, the `extract.links_fs` / `extract.timeline_fs` phases lose link/timeline batches when the DB connection drops mid-cycle, because the batch retry path never re-acquires a connection. On a pooled connection (Supabase Supavisor) this means the graph layer silently never materializes, and the cycle is marked `partial` so `last_full_cycle_at` never updates and `cycle_freshness` fails in doctor.

## Repro

Run against a Supabase **session-pooler** connection (transaction/`prepare:false` mode), e.g. a brain whose `DATABASE_URL` points at `*.pooler.supabase.com`:

```
GBRAIN_POOL_SIZE=2 gbrain dream --json
```

Observed:

```
[extract.links_fs] connection blip, retrying 100 rows in 500ms (No database connection: connect() has not been called. Fix: Run gbrain init --supabase or gbrain init --url <connection_string>)
  batch error (100 link rows lost): No database connection: connect() has not been called.
Links: created 0 from N pages
```

The `gbrain init` hint is misleading — init is done. It's a connection-state issue, not config.

## Root cause

`withRetry` in `src/commands/extract.ts:59-68`:

```ts
export async function withRetry<T>(fn, opts) {
  try { return await fn(); }
  catch (firstErr) {
    if (!isRetryableConnError(firstErr)) throw firstErr;
    opts.onRetry?.(1, firstErr);
    await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, opts.delayMs ?? 500));
    return await fn(); // single retry against the SAME (still-disconnected) engine
  }
}
```

On a retryable connection error it waits 500ms and re-invokes `fn()` against the **same engine**, which is still disconnected — so the retry fails with `"connect() has not been called"` and the batch is dropped. The retry never re-establishes the connection.

Contributing trigger: `idle_timeout: 20` is hardcoded in `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:146`. Across a multi-minute cycle (notably the LLM-bound `synthesize` phase, or gaps between extract batches) the pooled connection idles past 20s and is closed by the pooler; the next extract batch then hits the dead connection, and the no-reconnect retry can't recover.

## Impact

- `extract.links_fs`/`timeline_fs` report `partial` → `cycle.extract` reports `partial` → cycle marked `partial` → `last_full_cycle_at` never set → `cycle_freshness` doctor check fails (−20 health). On our deployment the brain sits at health ~40-47 with an empty graph (`page_links` ≈ 3 across 600+ pages) despite content ingesting + retrieving fine.

## Suggested fixes

1. **Reconnect on retry.** In `withRetry` (or the batch caller), re-acquire/reconnect the engine before the second attempt rather than reusing the dead handle. A single reconnect+retry would resolve the dominant case.
2. **Make `idle_timeout` configurable** (env or config) and/or raise the default for pooled connections — 20s is shorter than the gap between some cycle phases, so connections idle out mid-cycle.

Happy to test a patch against a Supavisor-pooled deployment.
title: dream cycle: extract.links withRetry never reconnects after a dropped pooled connection (loses link/timeline batches)

body:
## Summary

The always-on `signal-detector` (the ambient capture sub-agent that fires per inbound message) is expensive per capture, and the cost is dominated by **context re-read across a multi-turn back-link-resolution loop** — not by the SKILL.md or the page it writes. Request: a configurable **cheap/bounded capture mode** so ambient capture stays economically viable for cost-conscious or high-volume single-user deployments.

## Measured cost (one active project, one day)

- 25 capture runs (Sonnet): **~$9.74 total**, avg **$0.389/run**, max **$0.823/run**.
- Extrapolated for a daily-active project: **~$200–290/month** just for ambient capture.

## Where the cost actually goes (one 754K-token run, 18 turns)

| Component | Tokens | Share |
|---|---|---|
| Initial context (system prompt + tool schemas + spawn prompt) | ~20K | fixed |
| SKILL.md (re-read each run) | ~1K | negligible |
| **`cache_read` accumulated across 18 turns** | **~585K** | **78%** |

Tool calls in that run: `query` ×4 (heavy hybrid search + synthesis), `get_page` ×1, `put_page` ×2, `add_link`, `add_timeline_entry`.

The driver: the back-link-resolution loop issues several heavy `query` calls; each result stays in context and is re-read every subsequent turn (context grows 20K → 57K), so 18 turns × growing context ≈ 585K `cache_read`. The richness of the back-linking (what makes captures good) is exactly what inflates the loop. `search.mode: conservative` helps (smaller result payloads → less accumulation), but the structural cost (turns × heavy-query results) remains.

## Request: a bounded "lite" capture mode

A config knob — e.g. `signal_detector.mode = full | lite`, or `signal_detector.max_lookups` / `max_turns` — that bounds the loop. Possible implementations:

1. **Lighter ops for back-link discovery** — `search` (keyword) / `get_backlinks` / `resolve_slugs` instead of repeated `query` (hybrid + synthesis).
2. **Cap lookups and turns** — e.g. one search + a single-pass write with minimal links, no multi-turn chase.
3. **Defer deep back-linking to the nightly cycle** — live capture writes the page + the verbatim signal cheaply; the `extract` / `backlinks` dream-cycle phases add the dense links in batch (deterministic, ~free). (Related to #1732 — for a remote/checkout-less brain the nightly cycle currently can't see MCP-authored pages to enrich them, so this lever depends on that gap being closed too.)

## Justification

Ambient capture runs on **every** message. At ~$0.39/capture × hundreds of messages/month, the spend is dominated by context re-read in a multi-turn loop, not by the value delivered (the page + signal). A bounded mode preserves the capture (page + verbatim signal + a few links) while cutting the expensive back-link chase. For cost-conscious or high-volume single-user deployments, "full" mode makes always-on capture economically painful; a "lite" mode keeps always-on capture viable. Quality-sensitive users keep "full", and the nightly cycle can backfill link depth either way.

Related: #1732 (DB→git reverse-sync — same capture-architecture surface; the "defer back-linking to nightly" lever depends on it).
title: Feature request: a cheap/bounded capture mode for the signal-detector (per-capture cost dominated by multi-turn back-link loop)

body:
## Symptom

`gbrain-recommended` — a bundled pack and the documented "operational brain" starter — resolves to **0 page_types**, though its YAML declares 12 (deal/meeting/concept/project/source/daily/personal/civic/original/place/trip/writing). They're dropped silently, no error:

```
parseYamlMini(gbrain-recommended.yaml).page_types  ->  undefined
Object.keys(parsed)  ->  [api_version, name, version, gbrain_min_version, extends, description]
                        ^ capture stops at `description`; page_types + every later key gone
parseYamlMini(gbrain-base-v2.yaml).page_types      ->  [15]   OK
parseYamlMini(gbrain-investor.yaml).page_types     ->  [2]    OK
```

`gbrain schema use gbrain-recommended` succeeds and reports an active pack while providing zero types.

## Root cause

`recommended.yaml` uses `description: |` (a YAML block scalar). `parseYamlMini` (`src/core/schema-pack/loader.ts`) reads `description` as the literal `"|"`, then the indented block body breaks its top-level-key tracking, so `page_types` and every subsequent top-level key are dropped. `z.array(PageTypeSchema).default([])` (`manifest-v1.ts:338`) turns the missing field into `[]` — no error.

The parser's own header (`loader.ts:85`-`86`) lists block scalars under **"Rejected by design"** — but it doesn't actually *reject* (throw); it silently mis-parses. So a shipped pack uses a documented-unsupported construct and loads broken.
Permalink: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/blob/eefe8b5741c27e59bf65198d46e3dfe5bfa70ce9/src/core/schema-pack/loader.ts#L85-L93

## Proposed fixes (any subset)

1. **Fail loud on unsupported constructs.** `parseYamlMini` should throw a clear error when it hits a documented-rejected construct (block scalars `|`/`>`, flow style, ...) instead of silently dropping every key after it. The silent partial-parse is the real footgun — it truncates the rest of *any* manifest and will bite the next pack author who reaches for `|`.
2. **Support `|`/`>` block scalars** in `parseYamlMini` (multi-line descriptions are natural in manifests).
3. **One-line pack fix:** change `gbrain-recommended.yaml`'s `description: |` -> inline `description: "..."`.

## Verified fix

Inlining that one description, nothing else changed:

```
BEFORE (description: |):  page_types = undefined
AFTER  (inline desc)   :  page_types = [12]   (deal, meeting, concept, project, source, ...)
```

## Why this matters

Of the 7 bundled packs, only `recommended` trips it today — but the failure is general: a single unsupported YAML construct silently truncates the rest of the manifest with no diagnostic. A pack author gets a "successfully activated" pack that's quietly empty. Making the parser fail loud turns a silent footgun into a one-line error at load time. (Note: even with this fixed, `recommended` would still need #1749 — extends-merge — to inherit `gbrain-base`'s types.)

## Repro

```
$ gbrain schema use gbrain-recommended && gbrain schema active
Page types: 0
```
```ts
import { parseYamlMini } from 'src/core/schema-pack/loader.ts';
const raw = parseYamlMini(readFileSync('src/core/schema-pack/base/gbrain-recommended.yaml','utf8'));
raw.page_types   // undefined
raw.description  // "|"
```

gbrain 0.42.1.0 (master HEAD `eefe8b5`), Bun 1.3.14. Related: #1749, #1574, #1726.
title: parseYamlMini silently drops every key after a block scalar — gbrain-recommended ships with 0 page_types

body:
## Symptom

Activating any `extends`-based schema pack strips the brain's taxonomy to that pack's own (often tiny) declared types — the parent `gbrain-base` types are never inherited (v0.42.1.0):

```
gbrain-investor    (2 own + extends gbrain-base[25])   ->  Page types: 2    (expected 27)
gbrain-recommended (extends gbrain-base)               ->  Page types: 0
gbrain-everything  (extends investor + borrow lenses)  ->  Page types: 0
gbrain-base-v2     (extends: null, 15 inline)          ->  Page types: 15   OK
gbrain-base        (extends: null, 25 inline)          ->  Page types: 25   OK
```

Only `extends: null` packs work. Every lens/domain pack (`recommended`, `investor`, `creator`, `engineer`, `everything`) is structurally non-functional as a taxonomy: activating `investor` loses `person`/`company`/etc., and `gbrain schema explain person` returns *"not in active pack."* The packs *load* (identity hashes compute, phases declare) while their **types come back empty** — silently replacing the working base taxonomy.

## Root cause

`resolvePack` (`src/core/schema-pack/registry.ts`) walks the extends chain only to enforce the depth cap + build the cache snapshot, then constructs the resolved pack from the **child's own manifest verbatim**. The merge is an explicit, never-landed TODO:

> `registry.ts:278` — `// Full extends-merging (child-wins) is the v0.41+ T20 follow-up.`

The `while (cursor?.extends)` loop (`registry.ts:262`-`282`) loads each parent via `loadByName` but discards it — parents are used only to push names onto `chain`. No parent `page_types`/`link_types`/`takes_kinds`/`calibration_domains` are ever merged.
Permalink: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/blob/eefe8b5741c27e59bf65198d46e3dfe5bfa70ce9/src/core/schema-pack/registry.ts#L262-L282

## Proposed fixes (any subset)

1. **Implement the deferred merge in `resolvePack`.** Compose `page_types`, `link_types`, `takes_kinds`, `calibration_domains` base->child (child-wins on name), resolve `borrow_from` the same way, build `ResolvedPack` from the merged result. The core fix.
2. **`doctor` signal for an own-only active pack.** When the active pack declares `extends` but resolves to <= its own declared count, warn loudly — today a brain can silently run on a lens pack with zero inherited types and nothing surfaces it.
3. **Fail-loud on activation.** Reject/warn when a pack with `extends:`/`borrow_from:` resolves to a type set that excludes the parent's, so a broken lens pack can't be silently activated.

## Why this matters

The lens packs are a headline feature (`docs/architecture/lens-packs.md`; the `gbrain-everything` "YC-president pattern"). As shipped, activating any of them is *worse than nothing* — it silently swaps the working 15/25-type base taxonomy for an empty or 2-type one. This is the schema-layer instance of "imported != curated": the pack *looks* active (identity, phases) while the taxonomy it promises is absent, with no health signal.

## Repro

```
# investor = 2 own types + extends: gbrain-base (25). Set schema_pack: gbrain-investor in
# ~/.gbrain/config.json (it isn't in the `schema use` allow-list - see #1726), then:
$ gbrain schema active
Active pack: gbrain-investor v1.0.0
Page types: 2                       # expected 27
$ gbrain schema explain thesis      # own -> recognized
$ gbrain schema explain person      # from extends: gbrain-base
Type `person` not in active pack `gbrain-investor`.
```
```ts
// or directly, no CLI: resolvePack(gbrain-investor) -> resolved.manifest.page_types == the 2 own only
```

gbrain 0.42.1.0 (master HEAD `eefe8b5`), Bun 1.3.14, Supabase Postgres. Related: #1574, #1726.
title: Schema-pack extends/borrow_from never merges parent page_types — lens packs silently resolve to own-types-only

body:
## Summary

On v0.42.1.0 (PGLite), `gbrain extract` produces **0 link edges** for a federated source that was populated via `gbrain import --source-id <id>`, even though the pages are dense with Obsidian `[[wikilink]]`s whose targets are existing page slugs **in the same source**. `graph` and `backlinks` consequently return `[]`. Hybrid retrieval (`query`/`think`) works perfectly on the same data, so ingestion + embedding are fine — only graph/link extraction is affected.

Strongly suspect the link-resolution path resolves target slugs against the `default` source instead of the page's own `source_id` (the multi-source "silent collapse to default" class).

## Repro

```bash
gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model ollama:nomic-embed-text
gbrain sources add wiki --path ./wiki --federated
# ./wiki/*.md contain bodies with [[other-page]] links; targets match real slugs in ./wiki
gbrain import ./wiki --source-id wiki          # imports fine, chunks created
gbrain extract all --source db                  # -> "Links: created 0 from N pages (db source)"
gbrain extract all --source db --source-id wiki # -> still 0
gbrain backlinks other-page                     # -> []
gbrain graph some-hub-page --depth 2            # -> node with "links": []
```

`extractEntityRefs` clearly matches the unqualified `[[path]]` form (link-extraction.ts), and the target slugs match imported page slugs exactly, yet `addLinksBatch`'s JOIN finds no targets.

## Expected

`extract` creates `[[wikilink]]` edges scoped to the source the pages live in; `backlinks`/`graph` traverse them.

## Notes / context

- Source was created with `--federated`. Pages route correctly (`sources list` shows the right per-source counts), and content is searchable via hybrid retrieval scoped to `--source wiki`. So the page rows carry the right `source_id`; only link extraction comes up empty.
- This setup uses `import` (not `sync`) because the vault is a subdirectory of a larger git repo and `gbrain sync` requires a git-repo root — possibly relevant if extraction's slug resolution keys off sync-populated state.
- Interim UX nit: `Links: created 0 from N pages` reads like success. When N pages carry wikilinks but 0 resolve, a warning ("0 of N wikilink-bearing pages resolved — targets may be in another source") would surface this instead of looking clean.

Env: gbrain 0.42.1.0, engine=pglite, macOS, embeddings ollama:nomic-embed-text.
title: extract: 0 wikilink edges on a non-default source populated via `import --source-id` (link resolution appears to target `default`)

body:
## Problem

DeepSeek v4-flash (and potentially other providers in the future) defaults to thinking/reasoning mode, producing ~50% extra output tokens as reasoning traces. For gbrain's non-reasoning use cases (embeddings, entity extraction, chat), these tokens are pure waste — they increase latency and cost with no benefit.

## Proposal

Add `{ thinking: { type: 'disabled' } }` to `providerOptions` for all openai-compatible providers in `gateway.chat()`:

```typescript
if (recipe.implementation === 'openai-compatible') {
    providerOptions.openaiCompatible = {
        ...((providerOptions as any).openaiCompatible ?? {}),
        thinking: { type: 'disabled' as const },
    };
}
```

The `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` provider spreads `providerOptions[recipe.id]` into the request body. Providers that don't recognize the `thinking` parameter silently ignore it — 100% safe.

## Scope

- Applies to all openai-compatible providers (DeepSeek, Ollama, OpenRouter, Groq, Together, llama-server, etc.)
- Does NOT affect native providers (OpenAI o-series, Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini) which use different parameter names for reasoning control
- Only affects the chat touchpoint (not embeddings or other API calls)

## Benefit

~50% reduction in output tokens for DeepSeek v4-flash chat calls, with zero downside for any other provider.
title: feat: option to disable thinking/reasoning for openai-compatible providers

body:
## What happened?

`gbrain init` with a correctly configured llama-server embedding model fails with `user_provided_model_unset`, even though the model string is fully specified.

Example: `llama-server:text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b`

## Root Cause

In `diagnoseEmbedding()` (gateway.ts ~line 671), the guard checks:

```typescript
if (
    Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
    tp.models.length === 0 &&
    (recipe.id === 'litellm' || isUserProvided)
)
```

This only inspects `tp.models` (the touchpoint's static model list) but ignores `parsed.modelId` (the user's actual model name from the config string). When the user specifies a complete model string like `llama-server:text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b`, `parseModelId()` correctly extracts the modelId, but the guard unconditionally rejects because the touchpoint has an empty static models array.

This is distinct from #1674 (dimensions mismatch error) — this bug triggers even when dimensions are correctly specified.

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
gbrain init --embedding-provider llama-server \
  --embedding-model llama-server:text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b \
  --embedding-dimensions 1024
```

Result: `user_provided_model_unset` error despite having a valid modelId.

## Expected Behavior

Should proceed to model instantiation when the user has provided a valid model string with a parseable modelId.

## Fix

Add `!parsed.modelId` to the condition so it only rejects when the user truly hasn't supplied a model:

```typescript
if (
    Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
    tp.models.length === 0 &&
    (recipe.id === 'litellm' || isUserProvided) &&
    !parsed.modelId  // only reject when modelId is truly absent
)
```

## Environment
- gbrain version: v0.42.1.0
- Provider: llama-server or any openai-compatible with `user_provided_models: true`

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: Duplicate of another issue or PR

title: embedding preflight: user_provided_model_unset rejects valid user-specified modelId

body:
## What happened?

When `agent.use_gateway_loop=true` is enabled for non-Anthropic providers (DeepSeek, OpenAI, OpenRouter, etc.), subagent tool loops fail with:

```
[chat(deepseek:...)] schema is not a function
```

## Root Cause

In `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` line 2460, the `chat()` function passes tool definitions to `generateText()` using:

```typescript
inputSchema: { jsonSchema: t.inputSchema } as any,
```

The Vercel AI SDK expects the `parameters` key with `jsonSchema()`-wrapped values. The `inputSchema` key is silently ignored for openai-compatible providers, causing the SDK to fall through to calling `.schema()` on `undefined`.

## Expected Behavior

Non-Anthropic subagent tool loops should work correctly with the gateway loop.

## Fix

```typescript
// Import jsonSchema from 'ai'
import { embed as aiEmbed, embedMany, generateObject, generateText, jsonSchema } from 'ai';

// Use parameters key with jsonSchema() wrapper
parameters: jsonSchema(t.inputSchema),
```

## Environment
- gbrain version: v0.42.1.0 (bug present since v0.38+ when gateway_loop was added)
- Provider: any non-Anthropic (DeepSeek, OpenAI, OpenRouter, Groq, etc.)
- Config: `agent.use_gateway_loop: true`

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: Duplicate of another issue or PR

title: Non-Anthropic subagent tool loops fail with 'schema is not a function'

body:
## Summary

Pages authored via MCP `put_page` on a remote/Supabase-backed brain (served by `gbrain serve --http` on a host with no local checkout) land in the DB but never reach the git system-of-record repo. There's no canonical way to mirror them back, so the DB accumulates pages git never sees — which breaks two documented guarantees: disaster recovery and multi-machine sync.

## Context

`docs/architecture/system-of-record.md` is explicit: git markdown is the system of record, the DB is a derived cache, and disaster recovery is "rebuild the DB from the repo." That assumes every user-knowledge page has a markdown artifact.

## The gap

On a checkout-less remote deployment, `put_page` returns `write_through: { written: false, skipped: "repo_not_found" }` (`src/core/write-through.ts`). The DB row is durable, but no `.md` is created. MCP-authored pages (ambient capture agents, anything writing via the `put_page` op) live only in the DB.

**Impact:**
1. The documented "rebuild DB from repo" can't restore these pages — a Supabase reset loses them unless the DB is separately backed up.
2. A second machine running `gbrain sync` from the repo never sees them.

## What exists today (and why it's not enough)

- `writePageThrough` writes a `.md` when `sync.repo_path` is valid, but doesn't commit/push (by design) and needs a persistent checkout the remote host may not have.
- `gbrain export` (non-`--restore-only`) **overwrites every file** — unsafe against a live checkout with hand-authored markdown. `--restore-only` is scoped to storage-tier `db_only` paths, not general user-knowledge.
- The dream-cycle reverse-renders + commits its **own** synthesize outputs, not arbitrary MCP-authored pages.
- No `gbrain sync --push`, no bidirectional mode, no periodic DB→git reconciliation phase.

## Requested (rough preference order)

1. **`gbrain sync --push` / bidirectional** — after git→DB, additively serialize DB-only pages, commit + push. Idempotent, never clobbers hand-authored files.
2. **An autopilot/dream-cycle phase** doing the same on a schedule for remote brains.
3. **`gbrain export --missing-only`** — additive export safe against a live checkout, as a composable building block.

**Design question (the edit case):** pages modified via MCP (e.g. backlinks added by a capture agent) need a policy on whether git mirrors the DB or stays hand-authored. Additive-only cleanly covers new pages but not edits; a clear "git mirrors DB for MCP-authored pages" rule or a per-page provenance flag would resolve it.

## Workaround (for anyone hitting this now)

One-time additive reconciliation, zero risk: `gbrain export` to a temp dir, copy only files that don't already exist in the checkout (skip derived/ephemeral dirs), `git add && commit && push`. Never touches existing files.
title: Feature request: canonical DB→git reverse-sync for checkout-less (remote/Supabase) brains

Preview: `gbrain onboard --check --explain`","category":"meta"},{"name":"type_proliferation","status":"ok","message":"2 distinct typed values (pack declares 25)","category":"meta"},{"name":"dangling_aliases","status":"ok","message":"No dangling aliases","category":"meta"}]}```
 
**Additional notes:**

  - Schema version is 111.
  - gbrain doctor reports DB connection OK.
  - gbrain doctor reports cycle_freshness failure because default has never completed a full cycle.
  - The suspected area is PostgresEngine.reconnect(): its comment says it should no-op for module-singleton mode, but  _savedConfig appears to be set for the main CLI engine, so a retry path may call this.disconnect() on the load-bearing module singleton mid-cycle.
title: gbrain dream disconnects module Postgres singleton mid-cycle before conversation_facts_backfill

body:
Spun out of [garrytan/gstack#1802](https://github.com/garrytan/gstack/issues/1802), where gstack's `/sync-gbrain` resume path `rm -rf`'d a user's repo because `import-checkpoint.json`'s `dir` field was the repo root.

## What happened downstream

gstack stages prepared pages to `~/.gstack/.staging-ingest-*`, runs `gbrain import <stagingDir>`, and on completion deletes that dir. Its resume feature reused the checkpoint's `dir` as the staging dir. When an autopilot `gbrain import` was SIGTERM'd on timeout **while the working directory was the user's repo**, the persisted checkpoint `dir` resolved to the repo root rather than the import-target staging dir. The next run adopted it and deleted it.

gstack is fixing its own `rm -rf` boundary (fail-closed ownership guard — gstack#1802 / its PR). But the durable fix is here: **a checkpoint should only ever name a path gbrain created and owns.**

## Requests

1. **Persist `dir` as the absolute, resolved import-target path captured at import start** — never re-derive it from CWD on interrupt/SIGTERM. The poison arose because the interrupted write resolved `dir` to the process CWD instead of the argument passed to `gbrain import`.
2. **Self-describing checkpoint** — consider a `schema_version` plus an `owner`/`kind` field so consumers can validate a checkpoint before acting on it, and reject malformed/old-format files rather than parsing garbage.
3. **Document the checkpoint contract** in the sync docs: what `dir` means, who owns cleanup of it, and the invariant that it is always a gbrain-minted path.

## Severity

`rm -rf` of a user's working tree (including untracked/uncommitted work) with no confirmation. Commits survived on the remote, but the blast radius is user data. High.

## Repro shape

1. `gbrain autopilot` / `gbrain import <dir>` interrupted via SIGTERM mid-run while CWD is a repo.
2. Inspect `~/.gbrain/import-checkpoint.json` → `dir` is the repo root, not the import target.

Filed from the same investigation as gstack#1802; happy to provide the `fs_usage` trace and the gstack-side mitigation PR for cross-reference.
title: import-checkpoint.json `dir` can resolve to CWD/repo root on SIGTERM — enforce staging-first checkpointing

body:
## Summary

A thin-client (Topology 2) user who ALSO keeps the full repo cloned at `~/gbrain` (for doctrine + as a deploy vector to their Railway brain-host) hits a large hidden context cost: every Claude Code subagent inherits the repo's `CLAUDE.md` (~549 KB / ~137K tokens), inflating each subagent invocation to ~200K tokens.

This is a real combination the docs don't address, so I'm filing it as a docs/topology gap rather than a code bug.

## The mechanism (verified against official Claude Code docs)

1. The user's global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` follows gbrain's canonical guidance: read `~/gbrain/AGENTS.md` + `~/gbrain/skills/RESOLVER.md` at session start.
2. Per Claude Code's memory docs, CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories of the cwd are lazy-loaded *"when Claude reads files in those subdirectories."* Reading any file inside `~/gbrain/` pulls `~/gbrain/CLAUDE.md` into the main conversation's context.
3. Per Claude Code's sub-agents docs, a non-fork subagent inherits *"every level of the memory hierarchy the main conversation loads."* So every signal-detector / Task subagent carries the 549 KB CLAUDE.md.

The user never asked to load `CLAUDE.md` — their doctrine points at `AGENTS.md` + `RESOLVER.md`. The 549 KB load is collateral from the lazy-load rule.

## Why this combination isn't covered

`docs/architecture/topologies.md` describes Topology 2 (thin-client) assuming the user installs via `bun install -g` and does NOT have the repo cloned. But a real use case needs BOTH:
- the cloned repo (doctrine via `git pull`, and a fork-push deploy vector to Railway), AND
- thin-client MCP consumption from Claude Code.

For that user, gbrain's own context-hygiene principle applies — `docs/ethos/THIN_HARNESS_FAT_SKILLS.md` already warns that a fat CLAUDE.md degrades attention ("my CLAUDE.md was 20,000 lines... the model's attention degraded... the fix: about 200 lines"). The repo's CLAUDE.md is dev documentation (architecture, src/core, tests), not user doctrine, so loading it into a consuming agent is pure overhead.

## Suggested doc addition

A short note in `docs/architecture/topologies.md` (Topology 2) for the "cloned repo + thin-client" case, covering one or more of:
- Keep the clone outside the home cwd tree so the lazy-load rule doesn't fire, or
- Use Claude Code's `claudeMdExcludes` setting to skip `<clone>/CLAUDE.md` from the memory hierarchy, or
- Note that `CLAUDE.md` is developer documentation and consuming agents only need `AGENTS.md` + `skills/RESOLVER.md`.

Happy to send a docs PR if the direction is useful.
title: Docs/topology gap: cloned-repo + thin-client users load the 549KB dev CLAUDE.md into every subagent (~137K tokens overhead)

body:
## Summary

Two small observability/config gaps surfaced while auditing a Railway+Supabase thin-client deployment on v0.42.1.0. Neither breaks the engine; both make the system harder to reason about. Filing together since both are one-line-ish fixes.

## Bug A — `list_schema_packs` MCP op hardcodes the bundled list

`src/core/operations.ts` (the `list_schema_packs` handler) has:

```ts
const bundled = ['gbrain-base', 'gbrain-recommended'];
```

But `src/core/schema-pack/base/` actually ships **7** packs:

```
gbrain-base.yaml  gbrain-base-v2.yaml  gbrain-recommended.yaml
gbrain-creator.yaml  gbrain-engineer.yaml  gbrain-investor.yaml  gbrain-everything.yaml
```

So `list_schema_packs` (and `get_active_schema_pack`'s sibling discovery) under-reports: an agent asking "what packs can I activate?" sees 2 of 7. The packs that declare the `extract_atoms` / `synthesize_concepts` phases (`gbrain-creator`, `gbrain-everything`) are invisible through the MCP surface, so a user can't discover that those nightly phases even exist without reading the repo. The loader itself reads the real directory; only the reporting list is frozen. Suggest deriving `bundled` from a directory read (same source the loader uses) instead of the literal array.

## Bug B — no persistent config key for the calibration holder (asymmetric with emotional_weight)

`src/core/config.ts` registers `emotional_weight.user_holder` (line ~688) so salience can be pointed at a non-default holder. There is **no** parallel `calibration.user_holder`. The calibration holder defaults to `'garry'` hardcoded in three places:

- `src/core/cycle/calibration-profile.ts:229` — `const holder = opts.holder ?? 'garry'`
- `src/commands/calibration.ts:161` and `:243` — same `?? 'garry'`

The only way to change it is the per-invocation `--holder` flag / MCP `holder` param. For a self-hosted brain owned by someone other than `garry`, the nightly `calibration_profile` phase always queries holder `garry`, finds 0 resolved takes, and skips — silently, forever — even though the user's takes live under their own holder slug. Setting `emotional_weight.user_holder` does NOT help (different subsystem). Suggest a `calibration.user_holder` config key that the cycle's `calibration_profile` phase reads, symmetric to `emotional_weight.user_holder`, so the nightly phase can target the brain owner without a flag.

Happy to send a PR for either if useful.
title: Observability: list_schema_packs hardcodes 2 of 7 bundled packs; no persistent config key for calibration holder

body:
## Summary

`gbrain check-backlinks fix` claims to repair backlinks but instead **injects a junk format that does not match the brain's actual linking convention**, polluting every page it touches. The injected content has to be manually reverted.

## Version

`gbrain 0.42.1.0`

## What happens

Running `check-backlinks fix` adds fabricated timeline-style rows to pages, e.g.:

```
| Referenced in <page> …
```

This format appears in **zero** organically-authored brain pages. The brain's real backlink convention is a clean `## See Also` section with markdown links — not pipe-delimited "Referenced in …" rows. The fixer also introduces bad spacing.

## Impact

- Caught it polluting **10 pages** in a single run during a routine sync.
- It's a disk-only write (the engine never ingests the injected text), so the damage is invisible to hybrid search and only shows up when you read the raw page — easy to miss and commit by accident.
- Net effect: the "fix" makes pages *worse* and creates manual cleanup work.

## Expected

`check-backlinks fix` should either:
1. Emit backlinks in the canonical `## See Also` format (clean markdown links), or
2. Do nothing if it can't produce that format — never inject the `| Referenced in …` rows.

## Workaround

Don't run `check-backlinks fix` blind. Inspect its diff and revert before committing.
title: check-backlinks fix injects a junk "| Referenced in …" format that doesn't match the brain's `## See Also` convention

body:
**Version:** gbrain 0.41.11.1 (Postgres engine, `gbrain autopilot` continuous daemon)

## TL;DR

When the Postgres connection drops under a long-running `gbrain autopilot` worker (e.g. a Supabase Supavisor session-pooler closing the socket), the autopilot-managed child worker (`gbrain jobs work`) never re-establishes the connection in-process. Its background interval loops (`promoteDelayed`, stall-detection) and the failure-recording path (`failJob`) just throw `No database connection` repeatedly, the original job error gets masked, and the worker exits `code=1` and respawns. On a brain that sees periodic pooler closes this becomes a crash-loop.

This is **not** the RSS watchdog — no `[watchdog rss=…]` events occur. The initiating failure is a socket/pooler close.

## Observed (sanitized)

Continuous `gbrain autopilot` daemon, child worker `gbrain jobs work --max-rss 2048`, Supabase Supavisor **session pooler** (`<project>.pooler.supabase.com:5432`).

```
[extract.links_fs] connection blip, retrying 7 rows in 500ms (No database connection: connect() has not been called…)
Promotion error: write CONNECTION_CLOSED <project>.pooler.supabase.com:5432
Stall detection error: write CONNECTION_CLOSED <project>.pooler.supabase.com:5432
  batch error (7 link rows lost): No database connection: connect() has not been called…
```

Over the daemon's lifetime: `No database connection` ×6,350, `Promotion error` ×20,064 (background interval loop spamming the dead pool), `worker exited code=1` ×547, `crashCount` reaching 5/5. The errors spray across *every* cycle phase (lint, sync, extract, embed…), confirming a global pool-state failure, not a single bad phase.

## Root cause

1. The DB connection is a single mutable module-level pool singleton: `let sql` (`src/core/db.ts:7`). `getConnection()` throws `No database connection` when it's null (`db.ts:151`).
2. A raw `CONNECTION_CLOSED` does **not** null the singleton — the object stays non-null but its underlying socket is dead. And `connect()` is a **blind no-op when `sql` is already set** (`db.ts:162`), so nothing repairs a dead-but-non-null pool.
3. The autopilot child is plain `gbrain jobs work` — **not** `MinionSupervisor` — so it lacks the reconnect health logic in `supervisor.ts:589`. Its background paths only log:
   - `promoteDelayed()` — `src/core/minions/worker.ts:432`
   - stall-detection timer — `src/core/minions/worker.ts:242`
   - `failJob()` → `engine.transaction()` (`src/core/minions/queue.ts:855`) → `this._sql || db.getConnection()` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:764`)

   None of these call `connect()`/`reconnect()`. `failJob` recording a failure on the dead pool throws again and **masks the original job error**, taking the worker down.
4. Retry coverage is fragmented: `retry-matcher.ts` (`/connection.*closed/i`, would match `CONNECTION_CLOSED`) is not called by these paths; and `connectWithRetry()` uses a separate older list (`db.ts:255`) that lacks `connection.*closed`.

A `PostgresEngine.reconnect()` already exists (`postgres-engine.ts:4146`) — it just isn't wired into the child-worker's background loops.

## Proposed minimal fix

1. `worker.ts:432` + `worker.ts:242`: on `isRetryableConnError(e)`, call `engine.reconnect()` and retry once for these idempotent queue-maintenance operations.
2. `worker.ts:831`: wrap `queue.failJob()` so a retryable DB failure reconnects + retries once; if it still fails, log **both** the original job error and the failure-recording error rather than masking the original.
3. `db.ts:162`: `connect()` should not be a blind no-op when the singleton exists but may be dead; `reconnect()` should be the standard repair path for worker daemons.
4. Unify `connectWithRetry()` (`db.ts:255`) with `retry-matcher.ts` so the startup matcher and the runtime matcher agree (the startup list currently misses `CONNECTION_CLOSED`).

Happy to open a PR for (1)+(2) (the highest-leverage pair) if the direction looks right.
title: autopilot worker crash-loops on pooler CONNECTION_CLOSED: background queue loops have no in-process reconnect

body:
**Version**: gbrain 0.41.14.0 (Postgres engine)

## Summary

After `gbrain embed --all` / `gbrain embed <slug>` / `gbrain import`, every embedded chunk's `content_chunks.model` column is written as `DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MODEL` (`zeroentropyai:zembed-1` in v0.36+) **regardless of the model that actually produced the vector**. The vectors themselves are correct (they go through `getEmbeddingModel()`); only the stored `model` label is wrong.

## Root cause

`src/commands/embed.ts` builds the `ChunkInput[]` for `upsertChunks` **without a `model` field** (e.g. `embedPage` ~336-342, `embedOnePage` ~435-441):

```ts
const updated: ChunkInput[] = chunks.map(c => ({
  chunk_index: c.chunk_index,
  chunk_text: c.chunk_text,
  chunk_source: c.chunk_source,
  embedding: embeddingMap.get(c.chunk_index) ?? undefined,
  token_count: c.token_count || Math.ceil(c.chunk_text.length / 4),
  // model: <omitted>
}));
```

`upsertChunks` (`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:1868`) then writes `chunk.model || DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MODEL`, so the column is mislabeled with the gateway default. This is misleading for `gbrain doctor`, for mixed-model detection, and for anyone reading the `model` column to learn which embedding space a chunk lives in (it can read e.g. `zeroentropyai:zembed-1` while the vector is actually OpenAI `text-embedding-3-large`).

## Suggested fix

Thread the resolved model (`getEmbeddingModel()`, the same value used for the batch) into the `model` field of the upserted chunks.
title: Embedding: `gbrain embed`/`import` mislabels content_chunks.model as the gateway default (v0.41.x)

body:
**Version**: gbrain 0.41.14.0 (Postgres engine, macOS, Bun 1.3)

## Summary

With `embedding_model = litellm:<model>` correctly configured (DB + file plane) and `LITELLM_BASE_URL` + `LITELLM_API_KEY` set — `gbrain providers list` shows `litellm … ✓ ready` — `gbrain embed` still refuses:

```
Provider "litellm" requires a specific model name to be configured.
  Set one: gbrain config set embedding_model litellm:<model-name>
```

…which is exactly what is already configured. The `litellm` recipe is effectively unusable for embeddings even when set up correctly.

## Root cause

`src/core/ai/gateway.ts:668-682` (`diagnoseEmbedding`):

```ts
const isUserProvided = (tp as any).user_provided_models === true;
if (
  Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
  tp.models.length === 0 &&
  (recipe.id === 'litellm' || isUserProvided)
) {
  return { ok: false, reason: 'user_provided_model_unset', model: modelStr, provider: parsed.providerId, recipeId: recipe.id };
}
```

The `litellm` recipe ships `touchpoints.embedding.models: []`, so this check fires **whenever the recipe is litellm**, regardless of whether a concrete model was configured (and registered via `registerExtendedModel` at gateway configure-time). `validateEmbeddingCreds()` (`src/core/embed-preflight.ts`) then throws `user_provided_model_unset`.

## Suggested fix

Consult the resolved/registered model (or the configured `embedding_model`'s model part) and only fail `user_provided_model_unset` when it is genuinely absent — not unconditionally for the litellm recipe.

## Workaround

Use the native `openai` recipe + `OPENAI_BASE_URL` pointed at the OpenAI-compatible endpoint (the `openai` recipe has a non-empty models list, so the check is skipped).

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: Embedding: `litellm` recipe is unusable — diagnoseEmbedding rejects it unconditionally (v0.41.x)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain doctor --json` emits stderr warnings for several doctor checks whose names are not registered in `src/core/doctor-categories.ts`:

```text
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'embed_staleness' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'entity_link_coverage' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'timeline_coverage' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'takes_count' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'pack_upgrade_available' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'type_proliferation' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
[doctor-categories] unknown check name 'dangling_aliases' — defaulting to 'meta'. Add it to src/core/doctor-categories.ts.
```

This looks like a doctor check/category registry drift: `doctor` emits these check names, but `doctor-categories.ts` does not include them in `BRAIN_CHECK_NAMES`, `OPS_CHECK_NAMES`, or `META_CHECK_NAMES`.

## Environment

- Installed CLI: `gbrain 0.41.38.0`
- Checked latest upstream source: `origin/master` at `eefe8b57`, `VERSION` = `0.42.1.0`
- Engine: Postgres + pgvector
- Command:

```bash
gbrain doctor --json > /tmp/gbrain-doctor.json 2> /tmp/gbrain-doctor.err
rg 'doctor-categories' /tmp/gbrain-doctor.err
```

## Observed behavior

Doctor completes, but stderr logs the unknown-check warnings above. The JSON output then defaults those checks to category `meta`.

## Expected behavior

Every doctor check emitted by `doctor` should be registered in `src/core/doctor-categories.ts`, or the categorizer should intentionally recognize that group without warning. A normal doctor run should not produce unknown-check warnings for first-party checks.

## Impact

- No data loss.
- No install blocker.
- Noisy stderr for operators/agents parsing doctor output.
- Category reporting can be misleading because these checks silently fall through to `meta`.

## Suggested fix

Add these emitted check names to the appropriate category set in `src/core/doctor-categories.ts`, likely:

- `embed_staleness` → brain
- `entity_link_coverage` → brain
- `timeline_coverage` → brain
- `takes_count` → brain or meta, depending intended score semantics
- `pack_upgrade_available` → meta or brain, depending intended score semantics
- `type_proliferation` → brain or meta
- `dangling_aliases` → brain or meta

A regression test that runs the doctor check-name inventory against the category registry would prevent future drift.
title: bug: doctor-categories missing emitted check names, causing unknown-check warnings

body:
See trace below. Reproduced on 0.42.1.0.

## Summary
On a multi-source (federated) PGLite brain, `gbrain query "<q>" --source __all__` always returns **"No results"**, even though the same query against a specific source returns good hits. The documented cross-source sentinel `__all__` is silently downgraded to the empty `default` source.

## Version
gbrain 0.42.1.0 (git clone + `bun link`), engine: pglite, schema v111, embedding `openai:text-embedding-3-small`, 3 federated sources (none of them `default`).

## Reproduce
```
$ gbrain query "Who is Jeff Woodrow?" --source __all__ --no-expand
No results.

$ gbrain query "Who is Jeff Woodrow?" --source scott-brain --no-expand
[0.8765] people/jeff-woodrow -- # Jeff Woodrow
...
```

## Root cause
`--source __all__` is rejected by the source-id regex; the resulting throw is swallowed and the context falls back to the empty `default` source:

1. `src/core/source-resolver.ts:78-79` — `if (!SOURCE_ID_RE.test(explicit)) throw new Error(...Must match [a-z0-9-]{1,32}...)`. `__all__` contains underscores, so the regex rejects it.
2. `src/cli.ts` `makeContext` — `sourceId = await resolveSourceId(engine, explicit)` throws; `catch { sourceId = undefined }` swallows it (the comment intends the `undefined` → cross-source back-compat path), but the return then does `sourceId: sourceId ?? "default"`, overriding `undefined` back to `"default"`.
3. The query is scoped to `WHERE source_id = "default"`, which has 0 pages → "No results".

The operation-layer cross-source branch in `src/core/operations.ts` (`sourceIdParam === "__all__" ? {} : { sourceId }`) is never reached for CLI callers because `makeContext` corrupts the value first.

## Suggested fix
Special-case the sentinel before the regex check so it threads through to the operation handler, and stop `makeContext` from overriding the cross-source intent with `"default"`:
```ts
const explicit = (params.source as string | undefined) ?? null;
if (explicit === "__all__") {
  sourceId = "__all__"; // or a dedicated cross-source sentinel the op handler honors
} else {
  sourceId = await resolveSourceId(engine, explicit);
}
```

## Workaround
Pass an explicit single `--source <id>` and route per query at the app layer.
title: CLI `query --source __all__` returns "No results": sentinel rejected by source-id regex, falls back to empty `default` source

body:
## Repro (gbrain 0.41.18.0)

Running `gbrain sync --source <id> --no-pull --no-embed --json` against a
registered source completes import cleanly but throws on the post-import phase:

```
$ gbrain sync --source ai-cos --no-pull --no-embed --json
...
Import complete (0.6s):
  1 pages imported
  1028 pages skipped (1028 unchanged, 0 errors)
  0 chunks created
The "data" argument must be of type string or an instance of Buffer, TypedArray, or DataView. Received undefined
$ echo $?
0
```

Manual CLI exits 0 (success) — the throw happens **after** the import is reported complete.

## Impact

When `gbrain` runs the same sync as a Minions job dispatched by `gbrain autopilot` (`auto_embed_backfill=true`, `embed_reason=autopilot_freshness`), the thrown error is captured by the job runner and the job is marked **DEAD** after 2 attempts.

Observed on a healthy local brain: **288 DEAD sync jobs / 24h**. Job stats show 100% sync failure rate even though the sources are actually updating correctly:

```
$ gbrain jobs stats
  Type           Total   Done    Failed   Dead   Avg Time
  sync           288     0       0        288    7.6s
  autopilot-cycle 1152    1152    0        0      8.4s
  recompute_emotional_weight 24    24    0       0    4.3s
```

Net effect: autopilot's dead-job metric is misleading and the platform-level shadow-IT/health audits flag it as a regression every cycle.

## Suspected location

- `src/commands/sync.ts` — JSON envelope writer at the end of the run
- `src/core/embed-backfill-submit.ts` — D22 extended sync handler
- `src/commands/autopilot.ts:459` — `autopilot_freshness` dispatch path

The error message comes from Node's internal fs (`writeFile` / `writeFileSync` receiving `undefined` for the `data` argument). Likely a code path that conditionally populates an output buffer when `--json` is set, but doesn't populate it on the `auto_embed_backfill` path.

## Environment

- gbrain `0.41.18.0`
- engine: Postgres (local, port 5433)
- multi-source brain with 7 registered sources
- autopilot running as a systemd user service

Affected sources observed: `ai-cos`, `claude-sessions`, `signal-backfill-2026-05`, `truthlake`, `truthlake-raw`.

---

Found via the workspace nightshift Minions adoption sweep — context: https://github.com/michaeladair44/zeus-openclaw-workspace/pull/11
title: autopilot sync jobs fail with "data argument undefined" in post-import phase

body:
## Bug Report

**gbrain version:** 0.41.38.0 (master)
**Engine:** PGLite (also reproducible on Postgres by code inspection)

### Problem

\`getLinks\` and \`getBacklinks\` do not filter \`pages.deleted_at IS NULL\` on the JOIN to pages, so during the 72h soft-delete recovery window:

- A page that \`delete_page\` soft-deleted still appears in \`get_backlinks(target)\` for any page it linked to
- A page whose link target was soft-deleted still appears in \`get_links(source)\`

This is the same class of bug as #1021 (\"orphans: soft-deleted pages still counted in orphan scan\"), fixed by PR #1033. PR #1033 only patched orphans + the \`dead_links\` counter in \`get_health\`; the read-side \`getLinks\` / \`getBacklinks\` queries in both \`src/core/pglite-engine.ts\` and \`src/core/postgres-engine.ts\` still need the same filter.

### Reproduction

\`\`\`bash
# In an MCP session (or via gbrain CLI):
gbrain put inbox/test-orphan-link <<'MD'
---
title: "Test"
type: note
---
Linking [[concepts/some-target]] for repro.
MD

gbrain delete inbox/test-orphan-link
# Page is soft-deleted (recoverable for 72h)
gbrain backlinks concepts/some-target
# Expected: empty (or no row from inbox/test-orphan-link)
# Actual:   row still present, naming the deleted source
\`\`\`

Or via MCP:

\`\`\`json
// 1. put_page with a wikilink
{ \"method\": \"tools/call\", \"params\": { \"name\": \"put_page\",
  \"arguments\": { \"slug\": \"inbox/test\", \"content\": \"... [[concepts/x]] ...\" }}}

// 2. delete_page (= softDeletePage)
{ \"method\": \"tools/call\", \"params\": { \"name\": \"delete_page\",
  \"arguments\": { \"slug\": \"inbox/test\" }}}

// 3. get_backlinks on the target
{ \"method\": \"tools/call\", \"params\": { \"name\": \"get_backlinks\",
  \"arguments\": { \"slug\": \"concepts/x\" }}}
// Returns inbox/test even though the source page is soft-deleted.
\`\`\`

### Expected Behavior

\`get_links\` and \`get_backlinks\` should hide rows whose endpoint pages are soft-deleted, matching the contract already enforced by \`get_page\` / \`list_pages\` / \`search\`. \`restore_page\` should bring them back into view. Hard delete (autopilot \`purgeDeletedPages\`) is already correct because the FK on \`links\` has \`ON DELETE CASCADE\`.

### Schema confirms this is a bug, not by design

\`src/schema.sql\` lines 401-402:

\`\`\`sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS links (
  ...
  from_page_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  to_page_id   INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  ...
);
\`\`\`

And the \`purgeDeletedPages\` comment at \`src/core/postgres-engine.ts:1004\`:

> The DELETE cascades through content_chunks, page_links, chunk_relations via FKs.

The schema author clearly intended links to disappear when their endpoint page does — that contract is satisfied on hard delete via FK cascade, but the soft-delete read path leaks them.

### Impact

- \`get_backlinks\` reports stale \"who links to me\" for 72h after a delete
- \`get_links\` reports outbound links to invisible targets
- Graph / recommendation surfaces that consume these queries inherit the staleness
- For agents that programmatically create-then-delete pages over MCP (test harnesses, scratch pages, autopilot-cycle subagent writes), the link table appears to accumulate orphans even though the underlying schema is correct

### Affected sites

By code inspection, both queries have the same omission in both engines:

- \`src/core/pglite-engine.ts\` — \`getLinks\` (line ~2430), \`getBacklinks\` (line ~2464)
- \`src/core/postgres-engine.ts\` — \`getLinks\` (line ~2477), \`getBacklinks\` (line ~2508)

The graph-traversal queries (\`traverseGraph\`, the v0.20.0 graph-query path) JOIN pages on \`l.to_page_id\` for the recursive step too — those probably want the same filter for consistency, but they are out of scope for this issue/PR to keep the change reviewable.

### Patch

PR coming separately. Mirrors PR #1033's pattern: add \`AND f.deleted_at IS NULL AND t.deleted_at IS NULL\` to the page JOINs in both branches (with and without \`opts.sourceId\`) of both functions in both engines. New regression test \`test/links-soft-deleted.test.ts\` covers backlinks + links + restore.

### Workaround

Wait 72h for autopilot \`purgeDeletedPages\` to hard-delete soft-deleted pages and let the FK cascade clean up the links. Or manually \`remove_link\` after every \`delete_page\`.
title: get_links / get_backlinks return links for soft-deleted endpoints (sibling of #1021)

body:
## Summary

On a **remote Postgres / Supabase pooler**, a single `gbrain query` / `gbrain search` issues **~85 separate `SELECT value FROM config WHERE key = $1` round-trips**. Each is a network round-trip; serial, they dominate query latency (~5s) and push the op handler past `cli.ts`'s hard-coded **10s disconnect force-exit timer** (`DISCONNECT_HARD_DEADLINE_MS`), which fires `process.exit(0)` **mid-handler** → **empty stdout** + a misleading `engine.disconnect() did not return within 10000ms` line (disconnect was never reached).

Invisible on local PGLite (sub-ms reads); only manifests on remote DBs.

## Evidence (gbrain 0.41.38.0, Supabase pooler, 2486-page brain)

Instrumented the engine (Proxy timing every method) during one `hybridSearch`:
- `getConfig()` called **~85×** (`loadConfigWithEngine` reads 15 keys and is invoked 2× per query; mode/cache/intent/rerank/graph-signals resolvers add more), each ~165ms on the pooler.
- Per-stage SQL is fast: `searchVector` 0.75s, `searchKeyword` 0.6s, `embed()` 0.31s — but full `hybridSearch` ~7–8s. The delta is config N+1.

Validated fix (preload whole `config` table in ONE query, serve `getConfig` from a Map):
```
preloaded 11 config rows in ONE query
handler returned 5 results in 3.71s (getConfig served from cache 85x)
```
Handler **~8s → 3.7s**; `gbrain query` end-to-end ~11s → ~5s, removing the force-exit/empty-stdout failure mode.

## Proposed fix

Process-lifetime config cache on `PostgresEngine.getConfig()`: one batch `SELECT key, value FROM config` on first read → Map; write-through on `setConfig`/`unsetConfig`; coarse TTL (`GBRAIN_CONFIG_CACHE_TTL_MS`, default 30s, 0 disables). Fixes all 56 `getConfig` callers. Precedent already exists in `query-cache.ts` (`WHERE key = ANY($1)`).

## Secondary (related) — force-exit timer scope

`cli.ts` arms the unref'd 10s force-exit timer **before** the op handler, so a legitimately slow remote handler (>10s) is killed mid-flight with an empty result. Consider arming it only around `engine.disconnect()` (its documented purpose), or making the deadline env-configurable, so a slow-but-correct handler isn't truncated.

Patch available; happy to open a PR.
title: perf(config): engine.getConfig issues ~85 round-trips per query; batch+cache to fix >5s latency + empty-stdout on remote Postgres

Trying to initialize gbrain using a local llama-server with the Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF model.

**What did you expect?**

Initialization to succeed.

**Steps to reproduce**
```

  --embedding-provider llama-server \

```
```
gbrain init --pglite   --embedding-provider llama-server   --embedding-model llama-server:Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF
Provider llama-server requires --embedding-dimensions <N> when using --embedding-model llama-server:Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF.
User-driven-model recipes (litellm, llama-server) have no default dimension.

Setup: Build llama.cpp, then `llama-server --model <gguf-path> --embeddings`. Set --embedding-model llama-server:<id> + --embedding-dimensions <N>.
```
