body:
### Summary

`diagnoseEmbedding()` in `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` always returns `user_provided_model_unset` for any provider with `user_provided_models: true` and an empty `models` array, even when the user has specified a model name via `embedding_model`.

### Reproduction

1. Set `embedding_model: "llama-server:text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b"` in config.json
2. Start llama-server / LM Studio with the model loaded
3. Run `gbrain embed --stale`
4. Observe: `Provider "llama-server" requires a specific model name to be configured.`

### Root cause

`src/core/ai/gateway.ts` lines 670-682 checks `tp.models.length === 0 && isUserProvided` but never inspects `parsed.modelId`. For `llama-server:text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b`, `parseModelId()` correctly extracts `modelId = "text-embedding-qwen3-embedding-0.6b"`, but the guard ignores it.

### Fix

Add `!parsed.modelId` to the condition so `user_provided_model_unset` only fires when the model name is genuinely absent:

```diff
if (
  Array.isArray(tp.models) &&
  tp.models.length === 0 &&
  (recipe.id === "litellm" || isUserProvided) &&
+ !parsed.modelId
) {
```

### Workaround

Use `--no-embed` on sync/import operations until fix is merged.

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: Duplicate of another issue or PR

title: llama-server embedding always returns user_provided_model_unset even when model name is configured

body:
## Bug Report

### Summary

`gbrain init --pglite` crashes on macOS 26.x (Tahoe) on Apple Silicon (arm64) due to a WASM runtime bug in the embedded PGLite engine. The crash occurs at engine initialization time, before any user data is processed.

### Environment

- **macOS**: 26.5 (Build 25F71) — Apple Silicon (arm64)
- **Bun**: 1.3.14
- **gbrain**: 0.41.29.0
- **Chip**: Apple M-series (arm64)

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Install gbrain on macOS 26.x (Tahoe) with Apple Silicon
2. Run `gbrain init --pglite`
3. Observe WASM crash during PGLite engine initialization

### Expected Behavior

PGLite should initialize successfully and create the local database.

### Actual Behavior

The process crashes with a WASM-related error during PGLite engine startup. The crash is in the WASM runtime layer, not in gbrain code itself. This appears to be a known issue with WASM execution on macOS 26.x.

### Workaround: Use Native PostgreSQL

A complete workaround is to bypass PGLite and use a native Homebrew PostgreSQL installation instead. Full step-by-step setup:

```bash
# Install PostgreSQL + pgvector
brew install postgresql@17
brew services start postgresql@17
createdb gbrain

# Build pgvector from source
cd /tmp && git clone --branch v0.8.0 https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector.git
cd pgvector && make && make install
psql gbrain -c "CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;"

# Configure gbrain to use native Postgres
cat > ~/.gbrain/config.json << 'EOF'
{
  "engine": "postgres",
  "database_url": "postgresql://localhost:5432/gbrain",
  "schema_pack": "gbrain-base-v2"
}
EOF

# Initialize and verify
gbrain apply-migrations --yes
gbrain doctor
```

All 102 migrations pass. `gbrain doctor` reports clean status. Full brain functionality verified: `put`, `search`, `link`, `files upload-raw`, etc.

### Impact

This affects all macOS 26.x (Tahoe) users on Apple Silicon using the default `--pglite` install path. The README recommends PGLite as the primary method ("2 seconds; no server"), so new users on the latest macOS hit this immediately.

### Suggested Fix

1. **Short-term**: Add docs about the macOS 26.x WASM incompatibility and the native Postgres workaround (PR incoming)
2. **Medium-term**: Have `gbrain init` detect macOS 26.x + arm64 and auto-suggest the Postgres engine path
3. **Long-term**: Track the upstream WASM fix (likely in Bun or the underlying WASM engine)

### Reporter

Saurav Roy — gbrain v0.41.29.0 user, verified on macOS 26.5 arm64.
title: PGLite crashes on macOS 26.x (Tahoe) due to WASM runtime bug

body:
## Summary

Redesign the brain taxonomy around **six record-intent primitives** with a deterministic precedence gate, split the monolithic recommended pack into a universal **`gbrain-core`** + a thin per-org **domain pack**, give every page a stable **ulid identity decoupled from its path**, and make filing **idempotent** via natural-key dedup + cached/versioned subject derivation.

This is the design-of-record. It came out of a 4-rev cross-modal design study (Opus 4.7 / GPT-5.5 / DeepSeek V4 Pro as independent judges); the winning rev scored 8.6/10 (MECE 9.0, DRY 8.7, idempotency 9.0). The skill-side routing changes already shipped downstream; this issue tracks the **gbrain code + schema-pack work** needed to make it real.

## Motivation (the bug class)

The current `gbrain-recommended` pack routes pages **by subject**: "an article about Acme Corp" gets filed under `companies/acme/`. That's a MECE failure — the page is a *capture* (inbound content), not an *entity*. Subject-routing produces:

- **Mis-filed records.** Articles, reports, transcripts about an entity land *inside* that entity's directory, polluting the entity namespace and making "all my source material" unenumerable.
- **Path = identity coupling.** Renaming a page (or an entity slug) breaks every inbound link. Link-rot is structural, not accidental.
- **Non-idempotent re-imports.** Re-ingesting the same tweet/URL/file mints a fresh page because there's no natural-key dedup. Migrations and re-syncs duplicate.
- **44 historical types** with overlapping semantics (5 concept-ish types, 5 tweet-ish types, 4–5 raw-input buckets) — no single canonical home for a given artifact, so the same thing lands in different places depending on who filed it.

## The design

### 1. Six primitives, deterministic precedence

A page is classified by **what it IS as a record**, not what it's about. Subject becomes a frontmatter property (`subjects[]`), never the routing key.

| # | Primitive | Record-intent question | Bright-line test |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ENTITY | Is this page itself a thing-in-the-world? | Could you point and say "this page IS that person/org/place/product/thing"? |
| 2 | ANNOTATION | Does this page depend on a parent page? | Frontmatter has `parent_ids[]`. |
| 3 | EVENT | A record of one bounded occurrence? | Has `occurred_at` + ≤1 real-world origin. |
| 4 | CAPTURE | Inbound content not authored by us? | Has external `source` + `inbound=true`. |
| 5 | WORK | Did we author/iterate this? | Has `author_ids[]` ∋ self/org. |
| 6 | CONCEPT | Durable, source-independent idea? | Survives unlinking from all sources. |

**Precedence (single-pass, deterministic):** `ANNOTATION → ENTITY → EVENT → CAPTURE → WORK → CONCEPT`. Every page hits exactly one primitive.

### 2. 29 structural types, one canonical path each, total selection

44 → 29 types (−34%). Each primitive declares a **catch-all leaf** (`thing` / `event` / `document` / `writing` / `concept` / `synthesis`) so `select_type()` is **total within a primitive** — it never returns "no match." Escalation is only for genuine two-way ambiguity, never for no-match (the old behavior, which produced silent closest-fit mis-files).

Counts: ENTITY=5, EVENT=7, CAPTURE=5, WORK=7, CONCEPT=2, ANNOTATION=3 = **29**.

New types added: `thing`, `event`, `decision`, `task`, `ticket`, `metric-snapshot` (the last four make the core generalize to any company brain — to-dos, ADRs, external-system issues, KPI snapshots). 21 legacy types retire by consolidation/aliasing. The complete 44→29 destination map (with backfill fields per row) is the migration spec.

### 3. Identity ≠ location

- Every page gets an immutable **ulid** `id`, persisted to `_index/by-id/{id}` at first sight.
- **Path is a pure function of frontmatter** — `canonical_path(frontmatter)`. A `taxonomist heal` pass recomputes paths; renames are free.
- Renames write an `aliases.json` entry + a permanent `.redirect` stub at the old path. **Links resolve by id, never break.**
- Collision resolution is **id-ordered**: lower ulid keeps the bare slug, higher gets a `--{id6}` suffix. Order-independent, replay-safe.

### 4. `subjects[]` on every non-ENTITY page → O(1) findability

```yaml
subjects:
  - { id: 01ABC..., role: primary,   confidence: 1.00, evidence_span: "title" }
  - { id: 01DEF..., role: about,     confidence: 0.93, evidence_span: "body:142-188" }
  - { id: 01GHI..., role: mentioned, confidence: 0.71, evidence_span: "body:991-1003" }
subjects_meta:
  resolver_version: "subject-resolver-v3.0.0"
  content_hash: "sha256:7a3f..."
```

Roles: `primary | about | setting | mentioned | author | counterparty | source`, each with defined query semantics. Confidence is required; auto-write threshold 0.85, below that → `_system/subject-review-queue.jsonl`. Builds `_index/by-subject/{id}/` — the O(1) "find all data on subject X" mechanism (the thing subject-routing was *trying* to give us, done correctly).

**Idempotency:** `derive_subjects()` is cached + versioned on `(content_hash, resolver_version)`. Same content + same resolver = byte-identical `subjects[]`. Only a resolver-version bump re-derives. This is what makes a migration re-runnable.

### 5. Natural-key dedup before ulid

Types with a stable external ID declare a `natural_key` (`x:{tweet_id}`, `url_sha256:{canonical_url}`, `sha256:{file_sha256}`, `{system}:{external_id}`). Persisted to `_index/natural-keys/{ns}/`. Re-importing resolves to the existing ulid instead of minting a new page.

### 6. Core pack + domain pack split

```
gbrain-core@1.0.0    # universal: 6 primitives, 29 types, path/id/index/precedence machinery, zero domain specifics
  └── extends → your-org-brain@x.y.z   # domain pack: org_kind, report_kind, concept.domain, doc_kind, medium enums
```

Adding a new **structural type** requires a core-pack escalation + design review. Most real "new needs" are new *subtype values* (a new `org_kind`, a new `report_kind`, a new `concept.domain`) and live entirely in the domain pack — no core change. This is what makes the core generalize to any company brain instead of being forked per-org.

## Proposed work (gbrain code)

New schema packs:
- [ ] `packs/gbrain-core/v1.0.0.yaml` — 6 primitives, 29 types, `precedence_order`, `primitive_catch_all`, per-type `required[]` + `natural_key`
- [ ] domain-pack template (`extends: gbrain-core@1.0.0`) with `domain_subtypes` for `org`/`concept`/`report`/`writing`

New CLI:
- [ ] `gbrain natural-key {ns}:{key}` — resolve a natural key to a ulid (null if unknown)
- [ ] `gbrain taxonomist heal [--dry-run]` — idempotent self-heal pass (recompute paths, derive missing `subjects[]`, rebuild indices)
- [ ] `gbrain migrate --plan` / `--apply` — pre-flight + execute the 44→29 migration (per-commit revertable, ~10 commits)
- [ ] `gbrain doctor --post-migration` — assert acceptance criteria

New write-path invariants (`gbrain page write`):
- [ ] Reject writes missing `id`, `type`, or any `required[]` field (no silent default-fill)
- [ ] Auto-fill `id` from natural-key if known, else generate ulid; persist to `_index/by-id` + `_index/natural-keys`
- [ ] Auto-derive `subjects[]` (with `resolver_version`, `content_hash`, `evidence_spans`) on non-ENTITY pages if missing; allow explicit override

Indices:
- [ ] `_index/by-id/{id}` (ulid → current path)
- [ ] `_index/by-subject/{id}/` (subject → pages, role-filtered)
- [ ] `_index/natural-keys/{ns}/` (natural key → ulid)
- [ ] `aliases.json` + `.redirect` stubs (rename trail)

## Acceptance criteria

`gbrain doctor --post-migration` must report:
- `untyped_pages = 0`
- `invariant_failures = 0` (every page satisfies its type's `required[]`)
- `orphan_types = 0` (no page on a type not in the active pack)
- `by-subject` coverage: every subject id in every page has a `_index/by-subject/{id}/` entry
- `natural-key` coverage: every CAPTURE with a natural key has an index entry
- Idempotency: a second `taxonomist heal` immediately after a clean run is a **no-op** (zero file changes)

## Migration risk

This physically reorganizes paths across the full brain. The plan is **per-commit revertable** (~10 commits), runs after the pack + indices land, and is gated behind an explicit go. Single-threaded runtime estimate for a large brain (~150–200K pages): ~6–8h (embeddings + link checks + subject backfill + git history rewrite); ~1–1.5h with 8-way parallelism on the embedding + subject-derivation hot paths.

## Notes / open judgment calls

A few routing decisions are right for a media/research/investing brain but a different org might override in its domain pack: `contract → document` (vs a dedicated type), `incident → event` or `ticket`, `dataset → media-asset`, `code → document` (vs `reference`), `board-deck → report`. These are soft as *universal* claims; for the reference brain they're settled. The messiest legacy mapping is generic `note` → 3-way split (project / concept / take), which needs a manual review queue during migration rather than a blind rule.

Full design study (all 4 revs + cross-modal eval receipts + concrete skill/pack deltas) is archived and available on request — happy to attach the implementable spec + the 44→29 map as a follow-up comment.
title: Brain taxonomy redesign: 6 record-intent primitives, gbrain-core/domain-pack split, ulid identity, idempotent filing

body:
## What happened

Following `docs/ai-providers/llama-server-reranker.md` to point gbrain at a local
`llama.cpp` reranker, the reranker never receives requests — `gbrain query` silently
falls back to un-reranked order. Only the `LLAMA_SERVER_RERANKER_BASE_URL` env var
actually routes the request; the **documented** `gbrain config set
provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker …` has no effect.

This looks like the same root-cause class as #919 (native-openai recipe ignores
`provider_base_urls`), extended to the **reranker** touchpoint + the
`llama-server-reranker` recipe. Filing separately because (a) it's a different
recipe/touchpoint and (b) `docs/ai-providers/llama-server-reranker.md` step 4 explicitly
instructs `gbrain config set provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker …`, which is ignored.

## Repro

1. Run a local reranker on a **non-default** port:
   ```
   llama-server --model bge-reranker-v2-m3-Q8_0.gguf --alias bge-reranker-v2-m3 \
     --reranking --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8091 -ub 8192
   ```
2. Configure gbrain exactly per the docs:
   ```
   gbrain config set provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker http://127.0.0.1:8091/v1
   gbrain config set search.reranker.model llama-server-reranker:bge-reranker-v2-m3
   gbrain config set search.reranker.enabled true
   gbrain config set search.cache.enabled false   # so the query cache doesn't mask repeats
   ```
3. Confirm the configured server is up + reachable: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8091/health` → `{"status":"ok"}`.
4. `gbrain query "<a novel query that retrieves several docs>"`

## Expected

Rerank request `POST`s to `http://127.0.0.1:8091/v1/rerank`; results are reranked.

## Actual

`gbrain query` logs a fail-open entry to `~/.gbrain/audit/rerank-failures-*.jsonl`:

```json
{"reason":"network","model":"llama-server-reranker:bge-reranker-v2-m3","doc_count":30,
 "error_summary":"rerank: Unable to connect. Is the computer able to access the url?"}
```

The configured `:8091` server is up and reachable, but **receives zero requests** during
`gbrain query` (its access log shows no `POST`, while a direct `curl …/v1/rerank` does
appear). The request is going to the recipe default `http://localhost:8081/v1` instead of
the configured `:8091`.

## Proof it's the config that's ignored (not something else)

With the 8091 server **up and reachable** and `provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker`
set to it:

- **config-set only (no env):** 3/3 `gbrain query` runs → "Unable to connect" failures
  (the request never reaches the up+reachable 8091).
- **`LLAMA_SERVER_RERANKER_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8091/v1 gbrain query …`:** 0 failures,
  rerank succeeds (server log grows by ~600 lines for the same query).

Since the configured target is provably reachable, a connect failure can only mean the
configured `provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker` is not consulted on the reranker
path; only the env var is.

## Likely location

`gateway.rerank()` resolves the base URL via `applyOpenAICompatConfig(recipe, cfg)`. That
path uses the recipe `base_url_default` + the `LLAMA_SERVER_RERANKER_BASE_URL` env
passthrough (`src/cli.ts`), but does not appear to consult the DB-plane
`provider_base_urls.<recipe-id>` key that `gbrain config set` writes. (Cf. #919 for the
chat/embedding/expansion equivalent.)

## Workaround

Use the env var instead of `config set`:

```
export LLAMA_SERVER_RERANKER_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8091/v1
```

## Suggested fix

Either (a) have the reranker base-URL resolution consult
`provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker` from config (parity with the env passthrough and
with what the docs promise), or (b) update `docs/ai-providers/llama-server-reranker.md`
step 4 to instruct the env var instead.

## Environment

- gbrain: v0.41.30.0
- OS: macOS 15 (Darwin 24.6.0), arm64 (Apple M2)
- Bun: 1.3.11
- Engine: PGLite
- Embedding: `ollama:nomic-embed-text` (768d)
- Reranker: `llama-server-reranker:bge-reranker-v2-m3` (`llama.cpp --reranking`)
- Search mode: `balanced` (reranker on by default)

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: `provider_base_urls.llama-server-reranker` is ignored — reranker uses the recipe default, not the configured URL (docs instruct `config set`)

body:
## Summary
On a **Postgres**-engine brain containing `links` rows with `link_source='embedding'` (created by an earlier embedding-/similarity-links feature; these carry `link_type='semantic_similar'`), the schema upgrade is permanently blocked at **migration 95** (`links_link_source_check_includes_mentions`).

## Environment
- gbrain **0.41.29.0**, engine **postgres** (self-hosted PG17), macOS.
- DB at schema **94**; binary expects **107**.

## Symptom
`gbrain stats` / `serve` / `autopilot` all print:
```
Schema version 94 → 107 (12 migration(s) pending)
[95] links_link_source_check_includes_mentions...
Schema probe failed: check constraint "links_link_source_check" of relation "links" is violated by some row
```
The schema never advances — serve/autopilot auto-migrate **silently aborts at 95 on every connect**, pinning the DB at 94. `gbrain apply-migrations --yes` is a **no-op stub** (prints "All migrations up to date" while still at 94); the working migrator is `gbrain init --migrate-only`, which also aborts at 95.

## Root cause
- Migration 95 sets the `links.link_source` CHECK to `ARRAY['markdown','frontmatter','manual','mentions']` — it **omits `embedding`**.
- Pre-existing rows have `link_source='embedding'`. Replaying 95 fails validation.
- The **final (107) constraint also omits `embedding`**, and **no migration converts or removes** the pre-existing `embedding` rows — so there is **no upgrade path** for any brain that used the embedding-links feature.

## Impact
Any brain with `link_source='embedding'` rows is stuck at schema 94 and cannot upgrade via the supported path; CLI, `serve`, and `autopilot` all silently fail to advance.

## Repro
1. Postgres-engine brain at schema <95 with ≥1 `links` row where `link_source='embedding'`.
2. Upgrade gbrain to a build containing migration 95.
3. `gbrain init --migrate-only` → aborts at `[95] ... check constraint "links_link_source_check" ... violated by some row`.

## Suggested fix
Add a data-migration that runs **before** 95 tightens the constraint, e.g.:
```sql
-- if the similarity-links concept is retired (it appears unreferenced in 0.41.29 source):
DELETE FROM links WHERE link_type = 'semantic_similar';
-- or, to preserve them as plain edges:
UPDATE links SET link_source = 'manual' WHERE link_source = 'embedding';
```
…or keep `embedding` in the `link_source` CHECK. Either way the migration set should self-heal existing `embedding` rows rather than abort.

## Manual workaround
Back up first (`pg_dump`), stop autopilot/serve, then `UPDATE links SET link_source='manual' WHERE link_source='embedding';`, run `gbrain init --migrate-only` (reaches 107). Since `link_type='semantic_similar'` is no longer produced by current code, the relabeled rows can then be deleted as vestigial: `DELETE FROM links WHERE link_type='semantic_similar';`.
title: migrate: migration 95 (links_link_source_check) has no upgrade path for brains with link_source='embedding' rows

body:
## Summary

`gbrain dream --source <id>` fails DB-dependent phases (`sync`, `synthesize`, `embed`) on Postgres/pgvector with "No database connection: connect() has not been called." PGLite works fine. Doctor, sync, embed --stale commands work fine (different code path).

## Environment

- gbrain 0.41.28.0 (also confirmed on 0.41.0.0)
- Engine: postgres (self-hosted pgvector/pg17)
- Embedding: llama.cpp bge-m3-Q4_K_M (1024-d)
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 / macOS 15

## Error

Every DB-dependent phase in `gbrain dream --source default` fails:

```
✗ sync        sync phase failed
    [InternalError/UNKNOWN] No database connection: connect() has not been called.
    Fix: Run gbrain init --supabase or gbrain init --url <connection_string>
✗ synthesize  synthesize phase failed
    [InternalError/SYNTH_PHASE_FAIL] No database connection: connect() has not been called.
```

Phases that work (local filesystem only): lint, backlinks, extract, resolve_symbol_edges, recompute_emotional_weight, consolidate, schema-suggest, purge.

## Diagnosis

- `gbrain doctor` connects to Postgres successfully (Connected, 2904 pages)
- `gbrain sync --source default` works (with --no-embed)
- `gbrain embed --stale` works (with OPENAI_API_KEY set)
- `gbrain sources list` shows all sources with correct paths
- `gbrain_cycle_locks` table shows cycles are attempted but not completing

The issue is in the PostgresEngine connection pool. Worker engines spawned during the dream cycle lose the parent connection manager reference. The retry-matcher.ts already documents this pattern since v0.41.2.1:

```typescript
// v0.41.2.1: gbrains own GBrainError thrown by getConnection() when
// the singleton pool was nulled (engine.disconnect mid-cycle, or
// postgres.jss auto-recovery between queries).
/No database connection/i,
```

The cycle creates PostgresEngine workers but they either:
1. Dont inherit the parent`s ConnectionManager properly (worker engines inherit via constructor option `parent` per connection-manager.ts)
2. The singleton pool gets nulled during the cycle

## Steps to reproduce

```bash
gbrain init --url "postgres://user:pass@host:5432/gbrain"
gbrain doctor  # confirms connection OK
gbrain dream --source default  # fails on all DB-dependent phases
```

## Expected behavior

All phases should complete against Postgres, matching PGLite behavior.

## Additional context

- `sync_freshness` and `cycle_freshness` health checks both FAIL as a consequence
- Brain score 81/100 despite the failures
- 11 sources all show "never completed a full cycle"
- Workaround: manual `gbrain sync --source <id>` and `gbrain embed --stale` work individually
title: dream --source <id> fails on Postgres: workers lose connection pool mid-cycle (PGLite OK)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain init --url <pooler-url>` against Supabase fails with `getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND` during the schema-migration step on IPv4-only networks, even when the caller explicitly provided a working Session pooler URL. The cause: `src/core/connection-manager.ts:147` derives a *direct* URL (`db.<project-ref>.supabase.co:5432`) from the pooler URL and uses it for the migration's direct-pool path. Supabase direct connections are **IPv6 only** unless the paid IPv4 add-on is enabled, so the lookup fails for every user behind an IPv4-only NAT.

The undocumented escape hatch `GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1` (line 175) works around it, but it's not surfaced in `gbrain --help`, `gbrain init --help`, the README, or the warning emitted on direct-connection URLs. New users have to read source to find it.

## Environment

- macOS 26.5 (also reproduces on 26.3 per [#223](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/223))
- Bun 1.3.13
- gbrain 0.41.29.0 (`bun install -g github:garrytan/gbrain`)
- Supabase project on `ap-northeast-2` (Seoul), free tier, no IPv4 add-on
- ISP is IPv4-only (\`bun --print 'await Bun.dns.lookup(\"db.<ref>.supabase.co\")'\` → \`DNSException: ENOTFOUND\`; same hostname's pooler resolves fine)

## Reproduction

1. Provision a fresh Supabase project on an IPv4-only network.
2. From the dashboard, switch the Connection-string panel to **Session pooler** and copy the URL (e.g. \`postgresql://postgres.<ref>:<pw>@aws-1-ap-northeast-2.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres\`).
3. Run:
   \`\`\`
   gbrain init --url \"<that exact URL>\" --force
   \`\`\`

### Expected

Init succeeds against the Session pooler. \`gbrain config show\` reports \`engine: postgres\`, \`database_url\` points at the pooler hostname, \`gbrain doctor\` is green.

### Actual

\`\`\`
Connecting to database...
pgvector extension not found. Attempting to create...
pgvector extension created successfully.
Running schema migration...
getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND
\`\`\`

The initial connection + pgvector creation succeed (pooler works). Then \`engine.initSchema()\` triggers \`connection-manager.ts → deriveDirectUrl()\` which rewrites the host to \`db.<ref>.supabase.co\` for the \`CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY\` direct-pool path. DNS for that hostname fails. Config is never saved (init exits before \`saveConfig\`), so \`gbrain config show\` still reports the pre-existing PGLite state — masking what actually happened.

## Root cause

\`\`\`ts
// src/core/connection-manager.ts:132-168
export function deriveDirectUrl(url: string): string | null {
  // ...
  if (refMatch && refMatch[1] && isPoolerHost) {
    directHost = \`db.\${refMatch[1]}.supabase.co\`;  // ← line 147
    directUser = 'postgres';
  }
  // ...
}
\`\`\`

The function unconditionally constructs a direct host for any Supabase pooler URL. There's no probe to check whether that direct host is actually reachable from the current network.

## Workaround (works today)

\`\`\`
GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1 gbrain init --url \"<pooler URL>\" --force
\`\`\`

Then persist:
- For the autopilot daemon: add \`export GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1\` to \`~/.gbrain/autopilot-run.sh\` (or set the launchd plist's \`EnvironmentVariables\` dict)
- For stdio MCP via Claude Code: \`claude mcp add gbrain -e GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1 -- gbrain serve\`
- For interactive use: \`export GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1\` in \`~/.zshenv\`

## Suggested fixes (any one would close it)

1. **Probe direct-host reachability before swapping.** Best-effort \`dns.lookup\` on the derived direct host; on \`ENOTFOUND\` / \`ENETUNREACH\` / IPv6-only, log a one-line note and fall back to the pooler URL for the CONCURRENTLY path. Session pooler on port 5432 supports session-mode features including the prepared-statement and extension semantics CONCURRENTLY needs.
2. **Auto-detect IPv4-only environments at init time** and set the kill-switch implicitly (with a stderr breadcrumb).
3. **At minimum, surface the kill-switch** in the existing direct-connection warning (\`src/commands/init.ts:1080\`) and in \`gbrain init --help\`. The warning already detects \`db.*.supabase.co\` URLs; it could append \"Or for pooler URLs that fail mid-migration with ENOTFOUND, set GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1.\"

Option 1 is the right long-term fix; option 3 unblocks every new IPv4-only user immediately.

## Why this matters

The current behavior funnels every IPv4-only user toward either (a) paying for the Supabase IPv4 add-on ($4/mo Pro plan), (b) abandoning the install, or (c) digging into gbrain source. Most carriers in Asia-Pacific deploy CGNAT with no public IPv4-on-IPv6 fallback, so this hits a meaningful slice of the user base. The escape hatch already exists in code — it just needs to be discoverable.

Happy to send a PR for option 3 (warning-text + \`--help\` update) if it'd be helpful.
title: Pooler→direct hostname swap in connection-manager.ts breaks IPv4-only networks during `gbrain init --url`

body:
## Problem

The mention scanner (`--by-mention`) cannot extract CJK entity names because:

1. `TOKEN_RE = /[a-zA-Z0-9]+/g` skips all CJK characters (line 118)
2. `MIN_NAME_LENGTH = 4` filters out 2-3 character CJK names (line 42)
3. `tokenizeTitle()` returns empty array for pure CJK titles, excluding them from gazetteer

This affects any brain containing Chinese, Japanese, or Korean named entities (people, companies, concepts).

## Reproduction

```bash
# Create a CJK entity page
echo '---
title: 纳瓦尔
type: person
---
Naval Ravikant, in Chinese context.' | gbrain put people/naval

# Scan for mentions — finds 0 links even though 纳瓦尔 appears in other pages
gbrain extract links --by-mention --source db
```

## Proposed Fix

PR #1637 adds CJK detection helpers, lowers min name length for CJK titles to 2 chars, handles pure CJK titles in the tokenizer, and adds a substring matching pass for CJK entities in `findMentionedEntities`.

## Verified

```
$ gbrain extract links --by-mention --source db
Mentions: created 27 links from 456 pages against gazetteer of 3 first-token buckets
```

The code comment at line 109-111 explicitly notes CJK was deferred: `"Non-ASCII (CJK, accented) is deliberately not tokenized in v1"` — this PR implements that deferred support.

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: Duplicate of another issue or PR

title: CJK (Chinese/Japanese/Korean) entity names not extracted by mention scanner

body: > Not a project issue. Please use the appropriate venue for IP correspondence. _Originally posted by @garrytan in [#1114](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/1114#issuecomment-4481491027)_ Kindly address this in the manner I've requested or provide the avenue to to reach out. This is Prior Art. 17 months old (Jan 2025), we made a first post of our work publicly and applied to some VCs alongside including YC. https://github.com/aevov/NeuroSymbolic-Cognition-for-Physical-Thermodynamic-Organism-Substrate I disclosed when i was ready and built further to address the need of an actual brain architecture that no one else has precedence on. Additional definitive proof of Neural Substrate implying our prior art as a Brain architecture. Integration is here: https://github.com/aevov/Aevov-NeuroSymbolic-NeuroResonance-root/blob/master/Aevov%20AI%20Whitepaper%20(developing).md Excerpt: "The BLOOM Engine (The Neural Substrate): This is the foundational layer of the architecture, responsible for processing raw data and identifying patterns. It is a distributed network of "tensor processing units" that can be scaled horizontally to handle massive amounts of data. The Aevov Pattern Sync Protocol (The Symbolic Overlay): This layer sits on top of the BLOOM engine and is responsible for orchestrating the flow of information and for performing symbolic reasoning. The Aevov Language (The Lingua Franca): This is a purpose-built programming language that is used to define the patterns, rules, and ontologies that are used by the system. The BLOOM Engine: A Decentralized Neural Substrate The foundation of the Aevov architecture is the BLOOM engine, a decentralized network of processing nodes designed for high-throughput pattern recognition. Unlike traditional, centralized AI models, the BLOOM engine is designed to be a distributed system from the ground up, inspired by the principles of swarm intelligence and decentralized computing." Fully fledged sentiencecloud.one - available as the world's only engineered brain architecture with https://github.com/aevov/Afolabi-LBM-170B-Lattice/tree/main Once again requesting an updated to what is an actual substrate that we created before anyone else with a Neuro-Symbolic foundation. Regards!
title: This is Prior Art.

## Summary

`gbrain query "<q>" --source __all__` (and `--source-id __all__`, and the MCP `query`/`search` tools with `source_id: "__all__"`) **intermittently returns "No results" for queries that single-source scoping returns reliably.** The same `__all__` query is **non-deterministic run-to-run** — it returned a full result set on one run and "No results." on a later identical invocation in the same session.

Crucially, **single-source queries are 100% reliable and deterministic** for the exact same query strings. So the corpus is present and embedded; only the cross-source (`__all__`) fan-out/merge path is flaky.

This also **contradicts an assumption stated in #1484**, which says *"the same query against `--source __all__` does work (cross-source pipeline runs correctly), so the routing logic is fine."* In this brain, `__all__` is **not** a reliable workaround.

## Environment

- gbrain **0.41.14.0**
- **bun 1.3.14** (so this is NOT #1610 — that truncation bug is `bun < 1.1.27`; this brain is also 100% embedded)
- Engine: postgres (Supabase pooler, port 6543, PgBouncer transaction mode — prepared statements disabled)
- Embedding: `zeroentropyai:zembed-1` (2560d)
- Multi-source brain: `wiki` (33 pages), `outputs` (10), `interview` (13), plus an empty `default` (0 pages). 56 pages total, 478 chunks, **100% embedded** per `gbrain stats`.

## Repro

`__all__` — flaky / non-deterministic:

```
$ gbrain query "acquisitions 2026 pipeline" --source __all__ --limit 2
# run 1: ~13 good results across wiki/interview/outputs
# run 2 (identical command, same session): No results.

$ gbrain query "data warehouse retention pipeline" --source __all__ --limit 3
No results.

$ gbrain search "Comulate" --source __all__
No results.
```

Single-source — 100% reliable for the *same* query strings:

```
$ gbrain query "data warehouse retention pipeline" --source wiki --limit 3
[0.86] themes/ai-tooling-roadmap ...
[0.86] operations/systems-and-data ...

$ gbrain query "data warehouse retention pipeline" --source interview --limit 3
[0.89] union-bay-board-meeting-...-transcript ...
[0.55] union-bay-ai-strategy-meeting-...-transcript ...

$ gbrain search "Comulate" --source wiki
[0.37] log ...
[0.33] themes/ai-tooling-roadmap ...
[0.24] _index ...
```

## Why I think it's the cross-source path specifically

If this were a query-side embedding failure (e.g. an intermittent `embed([query])` error), **single-source queries would be equally flaky** — they use the same query embedding. They are not: single-source is deterministic and correct every time. Only the `__all__` fan-out/merge varies run-to-run.

Suspected areas (cross-source path in `core/search/hybrid.ts` or the source fan-out/merge):
- A score-threshold/cutoff applied **after** the cross-source merge that can zero out the whole set depending on per-source score normalization.
- A race / partial-failure when fanning out sub-queries across N sources where one source's failure (or an empty intermediate) collapses the merged result to `[]` instead of degrading gracefully.
- Per-source result normalization that occasionally drops all rows below cutoff.

## Possibly related (not duplicates)

- **#1484** — silent default to the empty `default` source (the *separate* default-routing bug; this report is specifically about `__all__` itself being unreliable, which #1484 assumes is fine).
- **#1469** — hybrid `query` vector path returning identical results regardless of input. Different symptom, but both implicate the hybrid/vector query path; may share a subsystem.
- **#1610** — ruled out here (bun ≥ 1.1.27, 100% embedded).

## Expected behavior

`--source __all__` should be **deterministic** and return the union of what each single-source query returns (modulo ranking). At minimum, a cross-source query should never return fewer results than the best single-source query for the same input, and identical invocations should return identical results.
title: `--source __all__` cross-source query/search intermittently returns "No results" (non-deterministic); single-source is reliable

body:
## Summary

On a Postgres (Supabase) brain-host running the nightly `gbrain dream` cycle, the `extract` phase reliably writes **zero** links and timeline entries:

```
[extract.links_fs] connection blip, retrying (attempt 1/3): No database connection: connect() has not been called.
[extract.links_fs] connection blip, retrying (attempt 2/3): No database connection: connect() has not been called.
[extract.links_fs] connection blip, retrying (attempt 3/3): No database connection: connect() has not been called.
  batch error (N link rows lost): No database connection: connect() has not been called.
Links: created 0 from 346 pages
Timeline: created 0 entries from 346 pages
```

The cycle's `sync` phase (earlier in the same process) works fine, and a separate `gbrain extract all` invocation (fresh process) writes the links correctly. So the brain repo + content are fine — the problem is the cycle's shared DB connection going null before the `extract` phase.

## What's certain

`PostgresEngine.sql` getter returns `this._sql ?? db.getConnection()`. For the module-style engine `gbrain dream` uses, `_sql` is null by design, so it falls through to `db.getConnection()` (`src/core/db.ts:151`), which throws `connect() has not been called` when the module singleton `sql` (`src/core/db.ts:7`) is null.

`addLinksBatch` → `batchRetry` → `withRetry` re-invokes `_addLinksBatchOnce`, which re-reads `const sql = this.sql` on **every** attempt. Since both `_sql` and the singleton are null, all 3 retries hit the identical error and the batch is dropped. The v0.41.19.0 / v0.41.2.1 retry work only recovers a *transient* pool drop where postgres.js reconnects the existing `sql` object — it structurally cannot recover a permanently-nulled singleton (retry-on-null re-reads the same null).

The extract phase also swallows the per-batch error and reports `created 0` rather than failing loudly (`src/commands/extract.ts`), so the cycle exits 0 and the data loss is silent.

## Likely trigger (hypothesis)

Within a single `gbrain dream` process there is no explicit `db.disconnect()` in the cycle subtree. The most plausible cause is the `synthesize` phase: it submits subagent jobs and `waitForCompletion`s on a separate `gbrain jobs work` process, leaving this process's DB connection idle for a long time; the pooler / postgres.js drops the idle connection and the singleton ends up null. The comment at `src/core/retry-matcher.ts:23-27` already names this exact shape ("engine.disconnect mid-cycle, or postgres.js's auto-recovery between queries").

## Proposed fix

**Reconnect-on-null**, not just retry-on-null: when the module singleton is null, `getConnection()` (or the batch writers) should call `db.connect(savedConfig)` to re-establish it before failing. `db.ts` would need to retain the last-used config; `postgres-engine.ts` already keeps `_savedConfig` for the instance path via `reconnect()`. Secondary: make extract batch failures fatal (or surface them in the cycle exit code) so total data loss isn't reported as `created 0` with exit 0.

## Workaround

Running each maintenance phase as its own short-lived process (the pattern in `docs/guides/cron-schedule.md`) sidesteps it — a fresh `gbrain extract all` gets a fresh module singleton that nothing disconnects for that process's lifetime.

## Environment

- gbrain 0.41.26.0
- Postgres engine (Supabase), session-mode pooler (`:5432`)
- Headless brain-host on Railway, nightly `gbrain dream --dir $REPO --pull`

Key files: `src/core/db.ts` (7, 151-160, 227-233), `src/core/postgres-engine.ts` (sql getter, `_addLinksBatchOnce`), `src/core/retry-matcher.ts` (14-28), `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts`, `src/commands/extract.ts`.
title: gbrain dream: extract phase silently drops all link/timeline batches when the module-singleton DB connection is null mid-cycle (retry-on-null can't recover)

body:
Sustained disconnect-call audit failures in batch_retry_health (v0.41.28.0, Supabase transaction pooler)

## Per v0.41.28 guidance

> If batch_retry_health shows disconnect calls > 0, file an issue with that data so v0.41.28+ can target the right ownership boundary.

## Environment

- gbrain version: 0.41.28.0
- Install method: bun-link (from source clone)
- Database backend: Supabase Postgres (pgvector)
- Connection path: Supabase transaction pooler on port 5432
- `GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1` (direct connection disabled; pooler is the only path)
- OS: macOS
- Node/Bun runtime: bun 1.3.14

## Doctor output — batch_retry_health

```json
{
  "name": "batch_retry_health",
  "status": "fail",
  "message": "1008 exhausted batch retries in last 24h (worst: extract.links_fs = 1008). Sustained circuit-breaker incident. Fix: check pooler status; consider raising GBRAIN_BULK_MAX_RETRIES or moving to direct-connection. Disconnect-call audit: 12 call(s) in 24h (most recent caller: at disconnect (<home>/gbrain/src/core/postgres-engine.ts:207:7))."
}
```

- `connection` check: **ok** (database reachable)
- `stale_locks`: **ok**
- `queue_health`: **ok**
- All 1008 exhausted retries are concentrated in `extract.links_fs`.

## Key observation

The disconnect-call audit is naming `postgres-engine.ts:207` as the caller — this is internal to gbrain's own PostgreSQL engine module, not an external timeout or network-layer disconnect. The connection check passes, yet the retry circuit-breaker is sustaining 1008 exhausted retries in a single operation class over 24h.

## Expected behaviour

Either the retry logic recovers after the disconnect, or the disconnect origin is correctly attributed so the ownership boundary can be addressed.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Run on Supabase transaction pooler with `GBRAIN_DISABLE_DIRECT_POOL=1`.
2. Allow dream cycle to reach the link extraction phase.
3. Observe `batch_retry_health` via `gbrain doctor --json`.
title: Sustained disconnect-call audit failures in batch_retry_health (v0.41.28.0, Supabase transaction pooler)

body:
Thank you for maintaining gbrain. I noticed a small docs mismatch around the MCP trust boundary.

In `docs/mcp/DEPLOY.md`, the `Scopes and localOnly` section says these operations are rejected over HTTP regardless of scope:

- `sync_brain`
- `file_upload`
- `file_list`
- `file_url`

That matches `src/commands/serve-http.ts`, where HTTP MCP tools are built from `operations.filter(op => !op.localOnly)`. It also matches `docs/mcp/CHATGPT.md`, which says the same four operations are rejected over HTTP.

Later in `docs/mcp/DEPLOY.md`, the `Operations` section says all operations are available remotely, including `sync_brain` and `file_upload`, and describes remote `file_upload` confinement. That looks stale relative to the current HTTP behavior.

Suggested fix: update or remove the later `Operations` paragraph so it says HTTP remote clients do not see `localOnly` operations, and point local users to stdio or CLI for those local filesystem surfaces.

I did not open a PR because I wanted to confirm the intended docs wording first, but this looks like a documentation-only correction.
title: Docs: DEPLOY.md contradicts localOnly remote availability

body:
## Summary
`gbrain embed` fails with `[embed(zeroentropyai:zembed-1)] Invalid JSON response` for any page that produces **2 or more chunks**. Single-chunk pages embed fine. The same failure mode applies to the Voyage compat path. Root cause is a known bun bug in `Response.clone()` (oven-sh/bun#6348) that truncates large cloned bodies on bun < 1.1.27.

## Environment
- gbrain 0.41.26.1
- bun 1.0.36
- macOS (Apple M1 / arm64) — but note the bug is **architecture-independent** (it's in bun's `Response.clone()` implementation; reproduces on any platform with bun < 1.1.27)
- provider: `zeroentropyai:zembed-1` @ 1280d, Supabase pgvector brain

## Symptom
- `gbrain embed <multi-chunk-slug>` → `[embed(zeroentropyai:zembed-1)] Invalid JSON response`
- `gbrain embed <single-chunk-slug>` → succeeds
- In a 611-chunk brain, ~62% embedded; the remaining 28 pages (all ≥2 chunks) all failed consistently.
- The ZeroEntropy API itself is fine: a direct `curl`/`fetch` of the **exact** multi-input request returns HTTP 200 with valid JSON (~124 KB for 5 chunks). This is **not** rate limiting, **not** the API key, **not** payload size, **not** content-specific.

## Root cause
`src/core/ai/gateway.ts` — both `zeroEntropyCompatFetch` and `voyageCompatFetch` read the upstream response via:

```ts
const json = await resp.clone().json();
```

On bun < 1.1.27, `Response.clone()` truncates large in-progress streaming bodies (oven-sh/bun#6348, oven-sh/bun#4765; fixed in **bun v1.1.27**). The clone yields a truncated body → `JSON.parse` throws `Unexpected end of JSON input` → the shim's `catch` silently does `return resp` (a now-consumed/truncated response) → the AI SDK reports `Invalid JSON response`.

Single-chunk responses (~25 KB) stay under the truncation threshold, which is why they slip through and the bug looks intermittent / size-dependent.

Confirmed by injecting a log into the shim's `catch`:
```
[ZEDBG] catch: Unexpected end of JSON input | name: SyntaxError
```

## Suggested fix
Avoid `clone()` in the compat shims — read the body once:

```ts
const text = await resp.text();
const json = JSON.parse(text);
// ... rewrite results -> data, usage ...
return new Response(JSON.stringify(json), { status: resp.status, statusText: resp.statusText, headers });
```

When returning the rewritten body, also drop the now-stale `content-length` / `content-encoding` / `transfer-encoding` headers (the rewritten body length differs from the original).

Alternatively / additionally, document a minimum supported bun version (>= 1.1.27).

I'm happy to open a PR with the `clone()` removal if that's preferred.

## Repro
1. bun < 1.1.27
2. A page that chunks into ≥2 chunks
3. `gbrain embed <slug>` with a ZeroEntropy (or Voyage) embedding provider
title: ZeroEntropy/Voyage embeddings fail with "Invalid JSON response" for multi-chunk pages on bun < 1.1.27 (Response.clone() truncates large bodies)

body:
## Summary

A cluster of related staleness/validation issues surfaced while running `gbrain eval cross-modal` (v0.41.23.0). Net effect: the genuine multi-provider eval is hard to run because the shipped defaults and native-provider allowlists have drifted behind both the live provider APIs and gbrain's own model registry.

## 1. `eval cross-modal` default slot models are all dead

Defaults (`src/commands/eval-cross-modal.ts`): `openai:gpt-4o` / `anthropic:claude-opus-4-7` / `google:gemini-1.5-pro`.
- `gpt-4o` → rejected by gbrain's own OpenAI recipe allowlist (`Model "gpt-4o" is not listed for OpenAI chat`).
- `claude-opus-4-7` → **404 at the Anthropic API** (deprecated).
- `gemini-1.5-pro` → 404 at the Google API + needs a key.

So an out-of-the-box `eval cross-modal` returns `inconclusive: 0 of 3 models parseable`.

## 2. Native-provider chat allowlists are deprecated

- `recipes/openai.ts` chat: `['gpt-5.2', 'gpt-4o-mini']` (rejects `gpt-4o`).
- `recipes/google.ts` chat: `['gemini-2.0-flash-exp', 'gemini-2.0-flash', 'gemini-1.5-pro']` — **all three 404 at the live API** ("no longer available to new users"), while current models (`gemini-2.5-flash`, `gemini-flash-latest`) work at the API but are **rejected by the allowlist** ("not listed"). Net: native Google chat is unusable for new keys.
- The registry alias `gemini → google:gemini-3-pro` (from `gbrain models`) also disagrees with the recipe allowlist.

## 3. Native-Anthropic gateway 404s a valid key+model

`anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6` via the gateway → `[chat(anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6)] Not Found`. The same key + model via a direct `POST https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages` (anthropic-version 2023-06-01) → **HTTP 200**. So the key and model are valid; gbrain's native-Anthropic chat path (via `@ai-sdk/anthropic`) is 404ing. Routing the same model through OpenRouter (`openrouter:anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6`) works, which points at the native path specifically (SDK version / endpoint).

## 4. `gbrain providers list` reports unvalidated keys as "✓ ready"

A 4-character placeholder `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` showed `openrouter … ✓ ready`, then every call returned `400 Bad Request` (empty body). Readiness is apparently a non-empty-env-var check, not a format/auth check — misleading when debugging which providers actually work.

## Suggested fixes

- Refresh `eval cross-modal` default slots to current models (e.g. `openai:gpt-5.2` / a current `claude-*` / a current `gemini-*`).
- Refresh the native chat recipe allowlists to current models, or make the allowlist **advisory (warn-not-throw) for native providers too** (it's already non-enforced for the openai-compat tier), letting provider 404s surface naturally.
- Investigate the native-Anthropic gateway 404 (likely `@ai-sdk/anthropic` version or endpoint construction) — a valid key+model 404s only through the native path.
- Strengthen `providers list` readiness: validate key format (or do a cheap auth probe) rather than reporting any non-empty var as ready.

Filed from a real skillify-adoption effort (FoundryLabs/morty-v3 LABS-93) where the only way to get a multi-provider `eval cross-modal` running was to route everything through OpenRouter passthrough.
title: eval cross-modal defaults + native-provider chat allowlists are stale; native-Anthropic gateway 404s valid models; providers list reports unvalidated keys as ready

body:
## Summary

`gbrain skillify check` hardwires TypeScript-repo conventions for three **required** items, so a well-tested Python skill structurally cannot pass them — the score caps well below the threshold even when the work genuinely exists.

## Evidence

Running against a mature Python skill in a Python repo (morty-v3 `repo-sync-poll`):

- It has **34 passing unit/cycle tests** (`scripts/test_repo_sync_poll.py`) + an isolated E2E smoke (`scripts/repo-sync-poll-smoke.sh`), yet:
  - **Unit tests (required): FAIL** — detail: `no matching *.test.ts in <root>/tests`
  - **E2E test (required): FAIL** — detail: `try /qa or test/e2e/`
  - **Resolver entry (required): FAIL** — repo uses `triggers:` frontmatter + an `AGENTS.md`, not `skills/RESOLVER.md`
- Final structural score: **5/12**, with the gap dominated by these three tooling mismatches, not missing work.

## Root cause (from source)

In `src/commands/skillify-check.ts`:
- `findRelatedTests()` only matches `*.test.ts` / `*.test.mjs` / `*.test.js` (lines ~148-154); no `test_*.py` / `*_test.py`.
- `detectTestDir()` only looks at `<root>/{test,__tests__,tests,spec}` — not `scripts/` or skill-local `tests/`.
- `--help` exposes only `[--recent] [--json]` — no flag to specify test path, glob, or language.
- `isInResolver()` keys off `RESOLVER.md` / `AGENTS.md`; projects that retired a central RESOLVER in favor of per-skill `triggers:` frontmatter can't satisfy it.

## Suggested fix

- Detect Python tests (`test_*.py`, `*_test.py`) and scan `scripts/` + skill-local `tests/` in addition to the TS conventions; OR
- Add `--test-glob` / `--test-dir` / `--lang` flags so non-TS repos can point the checker at their real tests; and
- Treat `triggers:` frontmatter as a valid resolver-entry signal (or make the resolver item language/routing-agnostic).

Filed from a real skillify-adoption effort (FoundryLabs/morty-v3 LABS-93) where this caps every Python skill's structural score regardless of test quality.
title: gbrain skillify check: required Unit/E2E/Resolver items are unpassable for Python/non-TS skills

body:
## Suggestion: Make `BULK_RETRY_OPTS` configurable via `gbrain config set` (not just env vars)

### Motivation

v0.41.19.0 (#1537) introduced `BULK_RETRY_OPTS` (`maxRetries: 3`, `delayMs: 1000`, `delayMaxMs: 10000`, decorrelated jitter) to handle transient connection drops from Supavisor/PgBouncer poolers. The retry count and timing are tunable via three env vars (`GBRAIN_BULK_MAX_RETRIES`, `GBRAIN_BULK_RETRY_BASE_MS`, `GBRAIN_BULK_RETRY_MAX_MS`), but there is no `gbrain config set` surface for these values.

### Issue

1. **3 retries / ~12s total may not cover all pooler recovery windows.** In our deployment, `addLinksBatch` exhausted all 3 retries and dropped 66 link rows in a single batch. While the Supavisor circuit breaker typically recovers in 5-10s, some configurations may take longer.

2. **Env-only tuning is hard to discover.** A user encountering `batch error (N rows lost)` in their stderr has no obvious path to tuning — `gbrain config set bulk.max_retries` doesn't exist, and `GBRAIN_BULK_MAX_RETRIES` is only documented in `retry.ts` source comments and the CHANGELOG.

3. **`gbrain doctor --json` reports `batch_retry_health`** but does not surface the current effective retry configuration.

### Suggestion

Add `gbrain config set` keys for bulk retry tuning, e.g.:
- `bulk.max_retries` (default 3)
- `bulk.retry_base_ms` (default 1000)
- `bulk.retry_max_ms` (default 10000)

Have `resolveBulkRetryOpts()` check these config keys as a fallback if the env vars are not set, keeping env vars as an escape hatch for containerized deployments where `gbrain config set` is impractical.
title: Suggestion: Make BULK_RETRY_OPTS configurable via gbrain config set (not just env vars)

body:
# Dream synthesize: hardcoded 30-min subagent timeout fails on dense session transcripts

## Summary

`gbrain dream --phase synthesize` consistently fails with `wall-clock timeout exceeded` on transcripts that exceed ~20k tokens, even when the subagent is making valid progress. The 30-minute per-chunk timeout is **hardcoded as a literal** in `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts` (line 481) rather than being exposed as a config knob, so users cannot raise it without forking.

## Environment

- gbrain: v0.41.14.0 (Bun global install)
- Engine: Supabase (PostgreSQL 17.6, pgvector, transaction pooler @ port 6543)
- macOS arm64, Bun 1.3.14
- Embedding: `openai:text-embedding-3-small` (1536d)
- Subagent model: `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`

## Reproduction

1. Configure `dream.synthesize.session_corpus_dir` to a directory of `.txt` transcripts, where each transcript is a dense Telegram/chat-style log (~20k–50k tokens).
2. Run `gbrain dream --dir <brain> --phase synthesize --json`.
3. Watch jobs queue as `subagent` rows; each gets the hardcoded `timeout_ms: 30 * 60 * 1000`.
4. Every job runs to wall-clock timeout, retries 3x, ends `dead`. Zero `wiki/personal/reflections/*` pages get written.

In my run, 6/6 worthwhile transcripts (per the verdict pass) hit `wall-clock timeout exceeded` on all 3 attempts.

## Relevant code

`src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts:481`
```ts
const submitOpts: Partial<MinionJobInput> = {
  max_stalled: 3,
  on_child_fail: 'continue',
  idempotency_key,
  timeout_ms: 30 * 60 * 1000, // 30 min per chunk    <-- hardcoded literal
};
```

`src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts:502`
```ts
const job = await waitForCompletion(queue, jobId, {
  timeoutMs: 35 * 60 * 1000,                          <-- also hardcoded
  pollMs: 5 * 1000,
});
```

Attempting `gbrain config set subagent.timeout_ms 600000` returns `[config] No similar known key`, confirming there is no published override path.

## Why 30 min is not enough for some users

The subagent is allowed `max_turns: 30`. With ~25k–50k input tokens per transcript:
- Sonnet spends most turns on `search` + `get_page` exploring existing brain content before calling `put_page` (the prompt mandates wikilink discovery first).
- Each tool round-trip can be 30–90 sec on Anthropic.
- A 25–30 turn read-heavy session can legitimately blow past 30 min wall-clock even when the subagent is making real progress.

The result is **silent data loss**: the verdict pass identifies worthwhile transcripts, but the brain never receives the synthesized pages.

## Proposed fix

Promote both literals to config keys with sane defaults:

```ts
const submitTimeoutMs =
  Number(await engine.getConfig('dream.synthesize.subagent_timeout_ms')) ||
  30 * 60 * 1000;

const waitTimeoutMs =
  Number(await engine.getConfig('dream.synthesize.subagent_wait_timeout_ms')) ||
  35 * 60 * 1000;
```

Register both keys in the config schema validator so `gbrain config set …` accepts them without `--force`.

Optional follow-ups (lower priority):
- Surface `dream.synthesize.max_turns` (currently fixed at 30 inline in the synthesize prompt builder).
- Emit a structured event when a synthesize subagent is **terminated by timeout while still making tool calls** so operators can distinguish "timed out but progressing" from "stuck/looping".

## Workaround

Disable the phase entirely:
```bash
gbrain config set dream.synthesize.enabled false
```
…which loses the whole feature.

## Will to contribute

Happy to PR this if you want — straightforward 10-line patch + a unit test that asserts the override is honored. Tag me if you'd prefer it shipped under a different config namespace.
title: Dream synthesize: hardcoded 30-min subagent timeout silently drops transcripts

body:
## Summary

`gbrain sync --break-lock --max-age N` exists and self-heals wedged sync locks. 
The cycle lock (`gbrain-cycle` in `gbrain_cycle_locks`) has no equivalent CLI 
escape hatch. When a dream/autopilot cycle gets stuck (cancelled job, embed phase 
ignoring abort signal, timeout), the cycle lock stays held indefinitely and every 
subsequent `gbrain dream` skips with `cycle_already_running`.

This is documented in TODOS.md as a known issue. Garry hits it daily on his 
production brain.

## Reproduction

1. Run `gbrain dream` — starts an autopilot-cycle job
2. Cancel the job mid-run (or let it timeout during embed phase)
3. The `gbrain_cycle_locks` row stays held (ttl_expires_at in the future, 
   or the autopilot daemon refreshes it before TTL expires)
4. Every subsequent `gbrain dream` → `cycle_already_running` → skipped
5. `gbrain sync --break-lock` only targets `gbrain-sync`, not `gbrain-cycle`
6. No CLI command exists to break the cycle lock

## Current workaround

Manually `DELETE FROM public.gbrain_cycle_locks` in the database, then 
immediately run `gbrain dream --phase X` for individual phases. Race the 
autopilot daemon which re-acquires the lock within seconds.

## Proposed fix

One or both of:

### Option A: `gbrain dream --break-lock [--max-age N]`
Mirror the `gbrain sync --break-lock --max-age N` pattern for the cycle lock.
Clears any `gbrain-cycle` lock in `gbrain_cycle_locks` where `last_refreshed_at` 
is older than N seconds, then proceeds with the dream cycle.

### Option B: Autopilot self-heals stale locks on startup
When the autopilot daemon starts (or on each tick), check if the held lock's 
`last_refreshed_at` is older than 2× the TTL. If so, break it and proceed. 
This makes the autopilot self-healing without operator intervention.

### Option C: Embed phase respects abort signals (TODOS.md fix)
The root cause from TODOS.md: "Embed phase ignores signal.aborted between 
batches today. Job wall-clock timeout fires → handler keeps running → cycle's 
finally block unreachable → gbrain_cycle_locks row stays held indefinitely."
Adding `signal.aborted` checks between embed batches would prevent the lock 
from getting stuck in the first place.

## Environment

- gbrain: v0.41.20.0
- Engine: postgres (Supabase EU/Ireland, Session pooler)
- Schema: v103
- Deployment: Railway (gbrain serve --http)
- Autopilot: via Minions queue (autopilot-cycle handler)

## Impact

The cycle lock bug means the brain never self-maintains. No backlinks, no 
orphan resolution, no enrichment, no pattern extraction. The brain score 
degrades over time. The whole value proposition of "the brain gets smarter 
while you sleep" breaks.
title: feat(dream): add --break-lock for gbrain-cycle (parity with sync --break-lock)

body:
## Summary

In gbrain v0.18.2, running `gbrain sync --repo <path> --source <id>` correctly resolves and stores the `--source` value as a sync anchor, but the imported page rows themselves end up with `source_id = 'default'` instead of the requested source id. The multi-source registration (`gbrain sources add/list/attach`) and the schema (`pages.source_id`) are wired up correctly — only the sync → import call drops the source id.

## Affected version

- gbrain `0.18.2` (`gbrain check-update --json` reports no newer release as of 2026-05-28)
- macOS 26.3 (Darwin 25.3.0), PGLite engine

## Root cause (in this repo's source)

1. `src/core/pglite-schema.ts:46` — `pages.source_id TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'default'`. The column exists, the default is `'default'`. ✓
2. `src/commands/sync.ts:545-555` — `runSync` resolves the flag correctly:
   ```ts
   const explicitSource = args.find((a, i) => args[i - 1] === '--source') || null;
   let sourceId: string | undefined = undefined;
   if (explicitSource || process.env.GBRAIN_SOURCE) {
     const { resolveSourceId } = await import('../core/source-resolver.ts');
     sourceId = await resolveSourceId(engine, explicitSource);
   }
   ...
   const opts: SyncOpts = { repoPath, dryRun, full, noPull, noEmbed, skipFailed, retryFailed, sourceId };
   ```
   `opts.sourceId` is populated. ✓
3. `src/core/import-file.ts` — `importFromFile()` and `importFromContent()` only accept `{ noEmbed?: boolean }`:
   ```ts
   export async function importFromContent(engine, slug, content, opts: { noEmbed?: boolean } = {}): Promise<ImportResult>
   export async function importFromFile(engine, filePath, relativePath, opts: { noEmbed?: boolean } = {}): Promise<ImportResult>
   ```
   They never receive `sourceId`, so the page INSERT statement omits the column, and the schema default `'default'` kicks in. ✗

`opts.sourceId` from `SyncOpts` ends up reaching `readSyncAnchor` / `writeSyncAnchor` (so per-source `last_commit` works), but never the page-insert path.

## Reproduction

```bash
# Fresh PGLite brain, no pages
gbrain sources add personal-brain --path ~/dev/personal-brain --federated
gbrain sources add tesseract     --path ~/dev/tesseract     --no-federated
gbrain sync --repo ~/dev/personal-brain --source personal-brain
gbrain sources list
```

### Observed

```
SOURCES
───────
  default            federated   6 pages  never synced
  personal-brain     federated   0 pages  last sync 2026-05-28T07:41:14Z
                     ~/dev/personal-brain
  tesseract          isolated    0 pages  never synced
                     ~/dev/tesseract
```

The 6 imported pages live under `source_id = 'default'`. The `personal-brain` source's `last_commit` anchor updates correctly, but page count remains 0.

### Expected

```
SOURCES
───────
  default            federated   0 pages  never synced
  personal-brain     federated   6 pages  last sync 2026-05-28T07:41:14Z
                     ~/dev/personal-brain
  ...
```

All 6 imported pages should have `source_id = 'personal-brain'`.

## Why this matters

The v0.18.0 sources work is a major capability: federated vs isolated sources, kebab-context-style `attach`, per-source default search. With this gap, sources only function as labels for sync state, not for the pages themselves — so federation/isolation semantics silently don't apply to actual page data. Multi-source brains end up with all pages in `'default'` regardless of which `--source` was passed.

Two concrete downstream consequences observed:

1. **Federation/isolation broken in practice.** `gbrain query "..."` returns hits regardless of source attribution because all hits live in `'default'` (which is federated). An `--no-federated` source can't actually hide its pages from default search because no pages are actually attributed to it.
2. **Slug collision risk.** With `pages_source_slug_key UNIQUE (source_id, slug)`, syncing a second repo (e.g. one that also has a top-level `readme`) collides on `('default', 'readme')` instead of creating two distinct `(personal-brain, readme)` and `(tesseract, readme)` rows.

## Suggested fix

Thread `sourceId` from `SyncOpts` through to the import path:

1. Widen the `importFromFile` / `importFromContent` options:
   ```ts
   opts: { noEmbed?: boolean; sourceId?: string } = {}
   ```
2. Default `sourceId` to `'default'` inside the import functions, then include it in the page INSERT (both `pglite-engine.ts` and `postgres-engine.ts` paths).
3. In `runSync`, pass `sourceId: opts.sourceId` into the import call.
4. Add a test asserting `pages.source_id === '<requested source>'` after `sync --source <requested>`.
5. Backfill helper: a one-shot command (or migration) to move existing pages from `'default'` to a target `sourceId` for users who already ran a v0.18.x sync against the wrong source. Otherwise upgraders will silently keep their pages mis-attributed until they re-sync.

Happy to send a PR for items 1-4 if useful.

## Workaround for users until fixed

- Don't rely on the sources subsystem for actual page isolation in v0.18.x.
- Use a single `'default'` source and namespace pages by directory prefix (`personal/...`, `deals/...`) until the fix lands.

Filed from a setup where personal-brain (federated) and tesseract-ventures (isolated) were configured and only personal-brain was synced; the report above is from the live state.
title: v0.18.2: `sync --source <id>` doesn't tag imported pages — they fall back to schema default `source_id='default'`

body:
## Summary

`gbrain dream --phase synthesize --dir <brain-dir>` writes the synthesized
reflection/idea pages to the `default` source, even when `<brain-dir>` is a
registered source (its `.gbrain-source` dotfile names the source id). Result:
persistent `multi_source_drift` warnings in `doctor`, and the named source's
`last_sync` never advances ("stale" forever).

## Root cause

In `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts`, `collectChildPutPageSlugs()` (~L870)
hardcodes `source_id: 'default'` for all subagent-written pages. The comment at
L849-854 acknowledges this is a known gap:

> Subagent put_page tool schema doesn't expose source_id (subagents are scoped
> to a single source); default to 'default' for the current dream-cycle product
> behavior. Threading the source_id through reverseWriteRefs guarantees getPage
> targets the correct (source, slug) row...

So the `brain_put_page` write path never honors the resolved source from the
6-tier `resolveSourceId` chain (`--source` / `GBRAIN_SOURCE` / `.gbrain-source`
/ path-match / brain-default / 'default').

## Impact

- `doctor` → `multi_source_drift`: pages live at `default` but their intended
  source is the named brain source.
- `doctor` → `sync_freshness`: the named source is flagged stale forever,
  because dream writes land in `default` and never advance the source's sync.
- Workaround forces operators to `git commit && gbrain sync --source <id>` to
  re-home the pages, which leaves duplicate copies (one in `default`, one in the
  named source). Search dedupes by slug so retrieval is unaffected, but
  `page_count` / `chunk_count` inflate.

## Repro

1. `gbrain sources add mybrain --path ~/mybrain` (so `~/mybrain/.gbrain-source`
   = `mybrain`)
2. `gbrain dream --phase synthesize --dir ~/mybrain`
3. `gbrain doctor` → `multi_source_drift` lists the new reflection/idea pages as
   intended=`mybrain` but present at `default`.

## Suggested fix

Thread the resolved sourceId into the subagent put_page write path and through
`collectChildPutPageSlugs` / `reverseWriteRefs`, so synthesized pages are
attributed to the correct source at write time instead of the hardcoded
`'default'`.

## Env

gbrain 0.35.8.0, postgres engine, multi-source setup (multiple federated
sources).
title: dream synthesize writes synthesized pages to 'default' source, ignoring the resolved brain source

body:
## Summary

Two facts about `unify-types` disagree:

1. **Handler interface** in [`src/core/schema-pack/unify-types-handler.ts:41-50`](src/core/schema-pack/unify-types-handler.ts#L41-L50) documents `apply?: boolean` as:
   > `/** Apply mutations. Default false (dry-run). */`

2. **Worker registration** in [`src/commands/jobs.ts:1644`](src/commands/jobs.ts#L1644) explicitly does:
   ```ts
   apply: data.apply ?? true,            // worker invocation defaults to apply
   ```

The worker default wins for the canonical operator invocation, i.e., **the CLI invocation applies destructively by default**:

```
gbrain jobs submit unify-types --allow-protected --params '{"target_pack":"X"}'
```

There's also no `--dry-run` (or `--apply`) ergonomic flag on `gbrain jobs submit`. The only way to opt into dry-run is to pass it inside `--params`:

```
gbrain jobs submit unify-types --allow-protected --params '{"target_pack":"X","apply":false}'
```

…which isn't discoverable from `gbrain jobs submit --help`.

## Impact

First-time operators reading the handler interface (or any internal issue template that quotes its doc-comment) reasonably expect dry-run on first invocation — that's what the function-level documentation says is the default. The actual behavior is a one-shot destructive migration.

In a recent morty-v3 pack-activation session (2026-05-27, LABS-102), a planned dry-run checkpoint between preview and apply was lost because the CLI applied on first invocation. The outcome matched the operator's confirmed scope, but the operator-checkpoint was gone. The destructive default was clearly intentional (the inline comment "worker invocation defaults to apply" is explicit), but the disagreement between handler-level documentation and worker behavior is friction.

## Possible fixes (pick one — they're not mutually exclusive)

### A. CLI ergonomic only (smallest patch)

Add a `--dry-run` shortcut to `gbrain jobs submit` for `unify-types` (or all PROTECTED handlers) that injects `apply: false` into params. Default behavior unchanged; the safer mode becomes discoverable via `--help`.

### B. Realign default to match handler doc (safest)

Flip [`jobs.ts:1644`](src/commands/jobs.ts#L1644) to `apply: data.apply ?? false`, and add a `--apply` shortcut for the operator-friendly destructive mode. Aligns with the handler interface doc-comment and biases the system toward "show me what you'd do, I'll confirm."

### C. Just update the doc (smallest semantic change)

Edit the handler interface comment at [`unify-types-handler.ts:45`](src/core/schema-pack/unify-types-handler.ts#L45) from:

```ts
/** Apply mutations. Default false (dry-run). */
apply?: boolean;
```

to:

```ts
/**
 * Apply mutations. Default false in pure-function usage; the CLI worker
 * defaults to true. Pass `apply: false` in `--params` for dry-run.
 */
apply?: boolean;
```

Acknowledges the intentional UX choice without changing behavior.

## Context

Surfaced during a morty-v3 pack-activation session (LABS-102, 2026-05-27). The operator hit this with `gbrain jobs submit unify-types --allow-protected --params '{"target_pack":"morty"}'`. The job applied on first invocation; the planned dry-run review didn't happen. Output matched expectations (0 explicit retypes, 190 catch-all retypes to `note` w/ `legacy_type` stamps), but the checkpoint was lost.
title: `unify-types` CLI applies by default — handler interface says "Default false (dry-run)" but worker registration flips it to true
schema fork/show/diff/lint/validate `gbrain-base-v2` fails with "Unknown pack" — `packPathByName` only resolves `gbrain-base`

body:
**Reproduced on:** v0.41.2.0
**Related issue:** #1426 (read-path hang)
**Severity:** sync unusable on brains >3k files

---

## Summary

`gbrain sync --no-pull --no-embed --yes` wedges deterministically after ~3100 imported files on a brain with ~56k markdown files. The wedge is reproducible across `--workers 1`, `--workers 8`, and `--full` invocations, and across both fresh and resume modes.

Two distinct hang signatures observed depending on entry point:

1. **Fresh mode (cold start)**: ~8 hours of forward progress 0 → 3100, then process pegs at 100% CPU with RSS shrinking from ~390MB to ~10MB. `sample <pid>` shows **85% of CPU samples in `clock_gettime` / `mach_absolute_time` via JIT-compiled JS** — consistent with `vm.runInContext`'s timeout watchdog polling while a native regex `.exec()` is deep in catastrophic backtracking.

2. **Resume mode (with non-empty `import-checkpoint.json`)**: <2 min, no files imported, no log output. `sample <pid>` shows **main thread in deep JIT recursion (40+ frames)**, RSS climbs to ~390MB then halves. Trace-instrumentation of `importFromFile` never fires.

The resume-mode wedge prevents any further progress: a fresh sync would have to re-import 3100 records (~8 hours) only to hit the fresh-mode wedge at the same point.

---

## Repro

```bash
# Brain with ~56k markdown files (mixed types: note, person, org, conversation, etc.)
gbrain sync --source <id> --workers 8 --no-pull --no-embed --yes
# After ~8h: checkpoint reaches ~3100, then hangs indefinitely at 100% CPU
# Subsequent resume attempts wedge within minutes in pre-import phase
```

Smaller brains (500 records) sync cleanly in <30s. Reproducing at scale requires a large corpus; happy to extract a minimal anonymized repro if helpful.

---

## Diagnostic evidence

### Fresh-mode wedge (stack sample at 3h elapsed)

`sample <pid> 10` output (8512 samples, ~85% on time-reading):

```
8512 Thread_X   DispatchQueue_1: com.apple.main-thread  (serial)
+ ... 40 frames of bun JIT ...
+   <recursive JIT calls ×3>
+     8000 ??? (in <unknown binary>) — JS regex.exec call site
+       7882 ??? (in <unknown binary>)
+         7599 ??? (in bun) — interpreter time-check tick
+           6281 ??? (in bun) — Date.now() / performance.now()
+             5978 clock_gettime (in libsystem_c.dylib) + 60
+               5665 clock_gettime_nsec_np (in libsystem_c.dylib) + 384
+                 5624 mach_absolute_time (in libsystem_kernel.dylib)
```

Other threads (Bun Pool 0-3, JSC libpas scavenger, Wasm Worklist Helper) are parked on `_pthread_cond_wait`.

### Resume-mode wedge (stack sample at 1 min elapsed)

Main thread same depth of JIT recursion but **without** the `clock_gettime` hot loop — sustained ~99% CPU on deep recursive JS calls. `importFromFile` never reached (verified via instrumented `appendFileSync` trace).

### Code pointer

`src/core/schema-pack/redos-guard.ts:131` uses `vm.runInContext(code, ctx, { timeout })`. Per Node docs, `vm` `timeout` only checks between code operations; it cannot preempt a single native regex `.exec()` mid-backtrack. Fresh-mode wedge signature is consistent with this.

Resume-mode wedge is harder to pin — appears to be in pre-import phase (walk, diff, or pack reconciliation), but instrumentation did not surface a specific function.

---

## Mitigations attempted

| Attempt | Result |
|---|---|
| Apply read-path hang patches from #1426 | Sync still wedges (patches cover query/search, not sync writes) |
| `--workers 1` | Wedges in resume mode at same checkpoint |
| `--workers 8` | Wedges in resume mode at same checkpoint |
| `--full --workers 4` | Wedges in resume mode; checkpoint unchanged (does `--full` actually wipe the checkpoint file?) |
| `--no-embed` | Doesn't help — wedge is pre-embed |
| Kill stale `gbrain serve` MCP processes to free PGLite lock | Lock release works (and is needed!), but doesn't fix the import hang |

---

## Asks

1. **Replace `vm.runInContext` with `worker_threads.Worker` in `redos-guard.ts`.** Worker threads are kill-able mid-execution; `vm` timeout is not, per the Node docs' guidance for regex. This would harden the fresh-mode wedge.
2. **Surface a flag to skip schema-pack regex extraction during sync** (`--no-schema-pack` or similar). Lets users complete sync on suspect corpora and re-run extraction selectively later.
3. **Investigate resume-mode pre-import recursion.** Possibly the diff/walk/pack-reconcile phase has O(N²) or unbounded recursion on large checkpoints. Happy to provide additional samples if helpful.
4. **Document concurrency contract between `gbrain serve` and `gbrain sync`.** PGLite single-writer + concurrent serve instances = race condition. Either serve should yield on sync-pending signal, or docs should require stopping serve before sync.

Sample files (`/tmp/gbrain-hang-sample.txt`, `/tmp/gbrain-prewedge-sample.txt`) available on request.
title: sync wedges deterministically at ~3100 files on large brain (56k files) — two hang signatures

body:
## Context

We're building a multi-tenant SaaS product (IVI) that uses GBrain as a per-user knowledge graph backend. Each end-user of our SaaS gets isolated memory pages stored in a shared GBrain database, identified by `frontmatter.user_id` in the page record.

## Problem

The current CLI `gbrain call <op>` (verified on `v0.41.18`) does not expose a `--user-id` filter for read operations (`query`, `search`, `recent`, etc). Without it, we can't safely route queries to a single tenant without leaking another tenant's data.

Today we work around this by reading directly via `asyncpg` with `WHERE frontmatter->>'user_id' = \$1` parameterized queries. This works but couples us to your storage schema (we have to know that `user_id` lives inside `frontmatter` JSONB), and we duplicate logic that conceptually belongs in GBrain (RLS-like read filtering).

## Proposed solution (one of)

**Option A — CLI flag:**

\`\`\`
gbrain call query --user-id <uuid> --limit 5 "<query text>"
gbrain call search --user-id <uuid> "<query text>"
\`\`\`

Filter is applied server/CLI-side before the result set is returned. Same semantics as our manual `WHERE frontmatter->>'user_id'` clause.

**Option B — SDK / Python API equivalent:**

\`\`\`python
client.query(text="...", limit=5, user_id="<uuid>")
client.search(text="...", user_id="<uuid>")
\`\`\`

We currently consume via a wrapper we wrote locally. Native support would let us drop the wrapper.

**Option C — RLS-style source filtering as first-class concept:**
A more general design where read ops can take a `--filter "frontmatter.X=Y"` (or programmatic equivalent) so that any indexed JSONB key can be the multi-tenant anchor. `user_id` would be the most common use case.

## Why this matters for the GBrain ecosystem

- **Multi-tenant SaaS adoption:** today, anyone building user-facing AI products on top of GBrain has to roll their own read-side RLS. Native support would lower that barrier.
- **Security:** a centralized filter is less likely to leak than each consumer hand-rolling SQL.
- **Composability:** with \`--user-id\`, downstream tools (memory adapters, agent contexts) can compose GBrain ops without knowing internals.

## What we'd need to migrate

Once any of A/B/C ships, we'd:
1. Drop our custom user-filter wrapper
2. Drop direct \`asyncpg\` dependency on GBrain's schema layout
3. Pin \`gbrain>=<the version where this lands>\` in our \`pyproject.toml\`

Happy to discuss any of the design options above. Also happy to PR the CLI flag side if that's helpful — let us know if there's a preferred design direction first.

---

Filed by an operator running a multi-tenant SaaS on GBrain.
title: Feature request: CLI flag --user-id (and equivalent SDK param) for query/search ops to enable multi-tenant SaaS use

body:
Environment
    - OS: Windows 10 x64
    - Bun: 1.3.14 (via npm)
    - gbrain: 0.41.18.0 (git clone + bun install + bun link)
    - DEEPSEEK_API_KEY configured for chat

    Steps to reproduce
    bash
    git clone https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain.git
    cd gbrain && bun install && bun link
    gbrain init --pglite --no-embedding   # succeeds, 98 migrations applied
    gbrain capture "test content"          # succeeds, page stored
    gbrain search "test"                   # "No results."

    gbrain think "test" → "no LLM available" (separate ANTHROPIC_API_KEY issue)

    Root cause
    PGLite WASM binary on Windows x64 does not include the pgvector extension:

    error: could not access file "$libdir/vector": No such file or directory


    The content_chunks table has an embedding USER-DEFINED column that requires pgvector. Any SELECT on this table fails, making search/think non-functional even with --no-embedding.

    Difference from #939
