## Problem

Facts stub page writing can produce or overwrite page frontmatter/metadata in ways that are too brittle for downstream page hygiene. A downstream patch hardens `src/core/facts/fence-write.ts` and adds tests in `test/fence-write.test.ts`.

## Expected behavior

- Facts stub page writer preserves safe existing metadata where appropriate.
- Generated stubs include consistent required metadata.
- Rewrites should avoid clobbering unrelated frontmatter fields.
- The behavior should be covered by focused regression tests.

## Current downstream patch

A downstream patch updates `src/core/facts/fence-write.ts` and `test/fence-write.test.ts` to make stub metadata handling more robust.
title: Facts stub page writer should preserve safe metadata

body:
## Feature request

The take-proposal pipeline would be easier to operate if it supported a complete review workflow rather than a single implicit proposal path. A downstream patch carries three related changes:

- make `propose-takes` provider/model configurable
- allow multiple take proposals per page in schema/code
- add a `takes` review CLI for inspecting and acting on proposals

## Motivation

Different deployments may want cheaper or provider-specific models for proposal generation, and pages can contain multiple independent candidate takes. Operators also need a CLI review surface before accepting/declining generated proposals.

## Expected behavior

- Proposal generation provider/model can be configured separately from default model paths.
- Multiple proposals can be stored per page without overwriting each other.
- CLI review commands expose pending proposals and allow operator review.

## Current downstream patch

Downstream changes touch `src/core/cycle.ts`, `src/core/cycle/propose-takes.ts`, schema files, `src/commands/takes.ts`, and `test/propose-takes.test.ts`.
title: Take proposal workflow: configurable provider, multiple proposals, review CLI

body:
## Problem

Fact extraction and `gbrain think` fallback paths can fail noisily or lose useful fallback behavior when extraction/model calls return unexpected shapes. A downstream patch hardens `src/core/facts/extract.ts` and `src/core/think/index.ts` with focused tests.

## Expected behavior

- Fact extraction should degrade gracefully on malformed or partial model output.
- `think` should fall back through the gateway adapter consistently rather than failing on provider-specific response shape assumptions.
- Tests should cover malformed extraction results and gateway fallback adapter behavior.

## Current downstream patch

A downstream patch updates `src/core/facts/extract.ts`, `src/core/think/index.ts`, `test/facts-extract.test.ts`, and `test/think-gateway-adapter.test.ts`.
title: Harden fact extraction and think fallback error handling

body:
## Problem

Gateway tool-loop replay needs provider-neutral normalization so non-Anthropic models such as DeepSeek can complete multi-tool interactions reliably. A downstream patch hardens `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` and subagent handling for DeepSeek V4-style replay, cyclic tool outputs, audit-only fields, and schema-normalized tool result messages.

## Expected behavior

- Tool results are replayed as provider-compatible `role: tool` turns.
- Nested `undefined` values are normalized safely instead of leaking invalid provider payloads.
- Cyclic tool outputs are handled deterministically instead of crashing/retrying forever.
- Provider-specific audit-only fields are stripped before prompt validation.
- DeepSeek/V4 style tool-loop runs should work without forcing Anthropic-only paths.

## Current downstream patch

Downstream commits harden gateway replay and add adversarial coverage in `test/ai/gateway-tool-loop.test.ts`, including cyclic outputs and DeepSeek replay behavior.
title: Provider-neutral gateway tool-loop replay for DeepSeek and cyclic outputs

body:
## Problem

Doctor freshness can stay unhealthy even when autopilot is active if full-cycle freshness is not attributed to the specific source that completed work. A downstream patch aligns dream freshness with source-scoped cycles and hardens source attribution through synthesize/minion paths.

Related downstream changes touch `src/commands/dream.ts`, `src/commands/autopilot.ts`, `src/core/cycle.ts`, `src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts`, and minion/subagent plumbing.

## Expected behavior

- `gbrain dream --source <id>` should clearly run and record a source-attributed full cycle.
- Autopilot should treat stale per-source freshness as actionable full-cycle work.
- Synthesis/minion failures should retain enough source context for doctor/admin surfaces.
- Doctor should not report a stale source merely because global service liveness is healthy.

## Current downstream patch

A downstream patch adds source-scoped dream freshness handling and propagates `source_id` through cycle/minion plumbing so failures and absorb logs remain attributable.
title: Source-scoped dream cycles should update freshness and preserve attribution

body:
## Problem

`gbrain sync` currently treats local filesystem remotes/mirrors too narrowly. A downstream patch allows local mirror pulls during sync by hardening `src/core/git-remote.ts` and adding coverage in `test/git-remote.test.ts`.

Local filesystem mirrors are useful for offline/devbox setups, local source-of-truth mirrors, and controlled sync tests where network Git remotes are not desirable.

## Expected behavior

- Sync should accept safe local mirror pull paths where the source is explicitly configured as a local Git remote/mirror.
- It should still reject ambiguous or unsafe remote shapes.
- Tests should cover local-path remotes and normal network remotes.

## Current downstream patch

A downstream patch updates `src/core/git-remote.ts` and `test/git-remote.test.ts` to allow local mirror pulls while preserving existing remote validation.
title: Allow sync to pull from local filesystem mirrors

body:
## Bug Description

`facts:absorb` is intended to make best-effort facts/backstop failures observable via `ingest_log` reason-coded rows. In at least the page-write / CLI facts-backstop queue path, the observability path itself can fail because `writeFactsAbsorbLog()` calls `engine.logIngest()` on an engine handle that is not connected.

The user-facing page write succeeds, but instead of a durable `ingest_log` row, stderr prints a one-off warning:

```text
[facts:absorb] suppressed: 'No database connection' fires on a separate engine handle (known WARN-4 in v0.38; subsequent occurrences silent this process). First-occurrence trace for v0.38.4 diagnosis:
GBrainError: No database connection: connect() has not been called. Fix: Run gbrain init --supabase or gbrain init --url <connection_string>
    at getConnection (.../src/core/db.ts:153:15)
    at logIngest (.../src/core/postgres-engine.ts:4014:17)
    at writeFactsAbsorbLog (.../src/core/facts/absorb-log.ts:81:18)
    at <anonymous> (.../src/core/facts/backstop.ts:173:15)
    at async runEntry (.../src/core/facts/queue.ts:166:19)
```

## Steps to Reproduce

Observed on `gbrain 0.41.11.1` during a normal page write where facts backstop was queued:

```bash
gbrain put personal/local-favorites < /tmp/gbrain-local-favorites.md \
  && gbrain get personal/local-favorites
```

The `put` response included successful durable write-through plus queued facts backstop:

```json
{
  "status": "created_or_updated",
  "facts_backstop": { "queued": true },
  "write_through": {
    "written": true,
    "path": "/home/.../brain/knowledge/personal/local-favorites.md"
  }
}
```

Then the warning above printed from the facts absorb logger.

## Expected Behavior

- The page write should continue to succeed. ✅
- If the optional facts backstop hits a failure / no-op / absorbed condition, the reason should be durably observable as an `ingest_log` row with:
  - `source_type='facts:absorb'`
  - stable reason-coded `summary` (`gateway_unavailable`, `parse_failure`, `embed_failure`, `no_candidates`, `pipeline_error`, etc.)
  - the relevant source/page/session ref
- Doctor/admin surfaces should be able to read those rows.
- No repeated noisy stderr spam.

## Actual Behavior

- The page write succeeds. ✅
- The facts backstop logger attempts to call `engine.logIngest(...)`.
- That call throws `GBrainError: No database connection: connect() has not been called` because the facts subsystem / queue path is holding a disconnected engine handle.
- The observability event is therefore not written to `ingest_log`; only the first occurrence is printed to stderr and subsequent occurrences are suppressed.

## Why this matters

This is not data loss for the canonical page write, but it defeats the purpose of facts absorb observability: failures become less inspectable by `doctor` / admin dashboards exactly when the system needs structured reason codes.

## Suspected Root Cause

`src/core/facts/backstop.ts` queue-mode error handling calls:

```ts
await writeFactsAbsorbLog(ctx.engine, parsedPage.slug, reason, msg, ctx.sourceId);
```

`src/core/facts/absorb-log.ts` then calls:

```ts
await engine.logIngest({ source_type: 'facts:absorb', ... });
```

But in this path, `ctx.engine` can be a separate/disconnected engine handle.

## Proposed Solution Shape

Preferred upstream-shaped fix:

1. Ensure the facts queue/backstop logger uses the same connected engine lifecycle as the page-write caller; or
2. Make `writeFactsAbsorbLog()` safely obtain/connect a valid engine before `logIngest`; or
3. If DB logging is impossible, write a structured fallback diagnostic that `doctor` can inspect, rather than only stderr.

Keep the existing behavior that observability failures must not break the page write.

## Regression Test Requirement

Add a test that simulates facts backstop queue logging with a disconnected engine handle and verifies:

- the page write / caller path is not failed by absorb logging;
- facts absorb observability is durably captured somehow (`ingest_log` preferred; structured fallback acceptable if DB unavailable);
- stderr does not repeatedly spam on subsequent occurrences.

## Environment

- GBrain version: `0.41.11.1`
- Engine path in trace: `postgres-engine.ts`
- CLI path: `gbrain put ...` with `facts_backstop.queued=true`
title: facts:absorb logging can fail on disconnected engine handle

body:
## Summary

`gbrain embed --stale` can report progress above 100% even while the embed is making real progress and eventually succeeds.

Observed on `gbrain 0.41.11.1` with the Postgres engine after switching embeddings to ZeroEntropy and running a full stale re-embed.

## Observed output

The run started with this progress denominator:

```text
2026-05-25T23:32:00+00:00 starting gbrain embed --stale
[embed.pages] start
[embed.pages] 26/31993 (0%)
```

During the run, the displayed numerator exceeded the total:

```text
[embed.pages] 39169/31993 (122%)
```

The run eventually completed successfully, still above 100%:

```text
[embed.pages] 43048/31993 (134%)
Embedded 45255 chunks across 43048 pages
[embed.pages] 43048/31993 (134%) done
2026-05-25T23:47:27+00:00 gbrain embed --stale exited rc=0
```

A concurrent dry-run showed real progress, so this did not appear to be a hang:

```text
[dry-run] Would embed 9124 stale chunks
[dry-run] Would embed 7803 stale chunks
[dry-run] Would embed 7243 stale chunks
[dry-run] Would embed 6984 stale chunks
[dry-run] Would embed 5047 stale chunks
[dry-run] Would embed 0 chunks (0 stale found)
```

## Expected behavior

Progress should not exceed 100%. The label/units should match the values being displayed, for example:

- chunks embedded / initial stale chunk count, or
- page groups processed / estimated page group count, or
- unknown total progress without a percentage.

## Suspected cause

From the current source at commit `552ff4ed`:

- `src/commands/embed.ts:507` uses `engine.countStaleChunks()` for the initial denominator.
- `src/commands/embed.ts:574-581` groups stale rows by `source_id::slug`.
- `src/commands/embed.ts:614-618` calls `onProgress` once per processed key/page group, using an estimated total derived from the stale chunk count and current batch key count.
- `src/commands/embed.ts:238-244` ignores the callback's `done` / `embedded` values and calls `progress.tick(1)`.
- `src/core/progress.ts:131-137` renders `done/total (%)` without clamping, so the mismatch can display values like `134%`.

So the visible denominator starts from stale chunk count, but the visible numerator is driven by callback ticks/page groups. The run can be healthy but the progress display becomes misleading.

## Environment

```text
gbrain: 0.41.11.1
engine: postgres
embedding_model: zeroentropyai:zembed-1
embedding_dimensions: 1280
command: gbrain embed --stale
```

## Impact

Mostly cosmetic, but operationally confusing. It looks like the embed job is looping or stuck after 100%, even though stale count is falling and the command exits successfully.
title: bug: embed --stale progress can exceed 100% despite successful completion

body:
## Symptom
Doctor's `subagent_capability` warn message recommends `gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true` as alternative to setting ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. The config CLI rejects it:

```
$ gbrain config set agent.use_gateway_loop true
[config] Unknown config key "agent.use_gateway_loop".
```

## Diagnosis
Either:
- The key name has drifted from what doctor advertises
- The config CLI needs `--force` to accept new keys (then doctor should say so)
- The feature was renamed/removed but doctor message wasn't updated

## Suggested fix
Either restore the config key OR update doctor's recommendation to match the current config surface.
title: doctor advertises agent.use_gateway_loop=true as fix, but config CLI rejects it

body:
_Filed via brain-janitor from `Learning and Strategy/Wiki/reference/gbrain-known-bugs.md` (bug #11)._

- **Filed**: 2026-05-25 (pending upstream filing — not yet posted)
- **Symptom**: `gbrain sync --source studiovault` runs against a working tree containing one new page. Output shows `[sync.imports] 1/1 (100%) done` and `Synced <a>..<b>: +1 added`. A warning line earlier in the output reads `Warning: skipped <path>: ZeroEntropy embedding requires ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY.` Subsequent `gbrain get <slug>` returns `page_not_found`; `gbrain list --type <T>` does not show the page. Re-running with `--skip-failed` does not recover — the failure is acknowledged in `~/.gbrain/sync-failures.jsonl` but the page is still absent from the index. The "+1 added" counter is a lie.
- **Root cause** (suspected, same family as #6 and #8): the embedding step is a non-optional gate in the sync pipeline even though ZeroEntropy is an external service. When the embedding fails (missing key, network error, quota), the per-page transaction rolls back but the sync orchestrator reports success against the commit hash. Next sync sees the same commit as "already synced" and refuses to retry. Functionally: a single transient embedding-service issue can cause permanent silent dropouts.
- **India's encounter**: 2026-05-25 during Step 7/8 of the agent-factory rollout. Synced the new `agent-factory.md`; sync output looked clean (`1/1 done`, `+1 added`). Five minutes later, `gbrain query` against the agent-factory page returned no results. Inspection: `gbrain get` returned `page_not_found`; `gbrain list --type project --source studiovault` showed phase-6 and phase-7 but not agent-factory. The `ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY` was present in `~/.gbrain/env` but the daemon-equivalent (here: the CLI shell context where the user ran `gbrain sync`) didn't have it loaded. After sourcing `~/.gbrain/env` and re-running, gbrain reported "Already up to date" — the previous commit was marked synced. Only `gbrain put <slug> < file.md` (direct write path, bypassing sync) successfully landed the page.
- **Workaround**: (a) Always `source ~/.gbrain/env` before invoking `gbrain sync` so the embedding key is in scope; (b) if a sync run shows a `ZeroEntropy embedding requires…` warning, do NOT trust the "+N added" counter — verify with `gbrain get <slug>` for each new page; (c) when sync has already marked a commit as synced but the page didn't land, use `gbrain put <slug> < <path-to-md>` to push directly. The `put` path appears to be embedding-tolerant (succeeds with or without ZeroEntropy).
- **Status**: Pending upstream filing. This is a third instance of the broader "sync orchestrator reports success on partial failure" pattern (compare #6, #8). The right fix is for the sync orchestrator to (a) defer marking the commit synced until ALL pages in the diff are queryable, and (b) downgrade ZeroEntropy embedding from a hard gate to a deferred optional step (insert the page without embedding; queue the embedding for retry). The CLI should also exit non-zero when any page in the diff fails — currently exit code is 0 even when the warning fires.
title: `gbrain sync` reports `+1 added` but page never persists when ZeroEntropy embedding step errors

body:
Supabase database linter (advisor lint 0014, `extension_in_public`) on a fresh local install (v0.41.2.0, Supabase CLI local stack) flags 2 extensions:

```
Extension `vector` is installed in the public schema. Move it to another schema.
Extension `pg_trgm` is installed in the public schema. Move it to another schema.
```

Both are WARN-level SECURITY findings. Supabase's [best practice doc](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/database/database-linter?lint=0014_extension_in_public) recommends installing extensions in a dedicated `extensions` schema for namespace hygiene.

### Reproduction

1. `gbrain init --supabase` against a fresh Supabase CLI local stack (e.g. `supabase init && supabase start`)
2. Open Supabase Studio → SQL editor → run `select * from supabase.database_linter()` (or use the Advisors panel)
3. Two `extension_in_public` WARN rows surface

### Source

`src/schema.sql` runs `CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector` + `CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm` without an explicit `WITH SCHEMA extensions` clause, so they land in `public` (the default).

### Suggested fix

```sql
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS extensions;
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector WITH SCHEMA extensions;
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm WITH SCHEMA extensions;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA extensions TO postgres, authenticated, anon, service_role;
```

Plus a migration to move the existing-install extensions:

```sql
ALTER EXTENSION vector SET SCHEMA extensions;
ALTER EXTENSION pg_trgm SET SCHEMA extensions;
```

`ALTER EXTENSION ... SET SCHEMA` updates references inside the extension itself, but any user code that referenced `public.<type>` or `public.<function>` directly needs to be updated to `extensions.<...>` or have `extensions` added to `search_path` first.

### Local impact

WARN-only on single-user Postgres. Not a blocker, but every gbrain install running Supabase Studio's advisor sees the warning.

Companion to issue #171 (function_search_path_mutable) — same advisor sweep, different lint code. Filing separately so they can be addressed independently if scope differs.

### Linter raw output

```json
{"name":"extension_in_public","level":"WARN","detail":"Extension \`vector\` is installed in the public schema. Move it to another schema."},
{"name":"extension_in_public","level":"WARN","detail":"Extension \`pg_trgm\` is installed in the public schema. Move it to another schema."}
```
title: Supabase linter: pgvector + pg_trgm extensions installed in 'public' schema (extension_in_public)

body:
# Draft: GitHub issue for garrytan/gbrain

**Title:** `gbrain query` hangs indefinitely after results render — `awaitPendingSearchCacheWrites` fix at v0.36.1.1 covers only `cache.store`, other fire-and-forget writes still block disconnect

---

## Environment

- **gbrain version:** 0.41.2.0 (also reproduced on 0.35.8.0)
- **Engine:** PGLite (`engine: pglite`, `~/.gbrain/brain.pglite`)
- **Runtime:** Bun 1.x on macOS Darwin 25.4 (arm64, M-series)
- **Embeddings:** ollama:nomic-embed-text, 768d (local Ollama healthy, 42ms doctor probe)
- **Brain size:** 500 pages, 706 chunks (imported via `gbrain import` from markdown directory, all `gbrain doctor` checks pass)
- **No remote services:** PGLite local-only, no Supabase, no thin-client mode

## Symptom

`gbrain query <text>` (with or without `--no-expand`) on this PGLite brain:

1. Renders results to stdout within ~1 second.
2. Process continues running, ~0% CPU, never exits.
3. `perl -e 'alarm 90; exec @ARGV' gbrain query …` reliably exits with status 142 (SIGALRM) at 90 seconds.

`gbrain search` (tsvector path) on the **same brain** returns and exits in <1 second.

## Reproduction

```bash
gbrain init  # PGLite default
gbrain import ~/some-markdown-dir  # 500+ markdown files works
# Wait for embed completion. Then:
perl -e 'alarm 90; exec @ARGV' gbrain query "any query text" --no-expand --limit 5
# → output prints fully, process hangs ~90s, exit 142
```

Independent of: `--limit 1/5/20`, `--no-expand`, `--detail low/medium/high`, `--salience off`, `--recency off`, `GBRAIN_OUTPUT=json`, `GBRAIN_CONTRIBUTOR_MODE=0`, `--use-cache false`, `gbrain config set search.track_retrieval false`, `gbrain config set search.cache.enabled false`.

## Diagnosis

**Process sample during hang** (5s into hang, `sample <pid>`):
- Main thread idle in `start (in dyld)` → event loop suspended.
- 4× `Bun Pool` threads, 1× `Wasm Worklist Helper`, 1× `HTTP Client` thread — all parked.
- 0% CPU. Process is waiting on unresolved Promises.

**Root cause:** `engine.disconnect()` at `cli.ts` runs immediately after `op.handler` returns + `formatResult` writes stdout. PGLite `_db.close()` blocks until in-flight writes drain. Multiple fire-and-forget (`void ...`) calls fire per query but are NOT tracked by `awaitPendingSearchCacheWrites`:

| Call site | What it writes |
|---|---|
| `src/core/search/hybrid.ts:1192` (covered) | `cache.store` — semantic query cache writeback |
| `src/core/last-retrieved.ts:81` (UNCOVERED) | `bumpLastRetrievedAt` — `UPDATE pages SET last_retrieved_at = NOW()` on every result. Called from `operations.ts:1225` (query) and `operations.ts:1425` (search). |
| `src/core/operations.ts:3761` (UNCOVERED) | `recordSpend` — cost meter write |
| `src/core/search/query-cache.ts:183` (UNCOVERED) | `bumpHit` — UPDATE hit counter on cache hits |
| `src/core/search/telemetry.ts:103, 179` (UNCOVERED) | `this.flush` — search telemetry batch flush |
| `src/core/operations.ts:1231, 1435` (UNCOVERED) | `captureEvalCandidate` — fires only when `GBRAIN_CONTRIBUTOR_MODE=1` |

The v0.36.1.1 PR (#1182, commit `1bc57991`) added `pendingCacheWrites: Set<Promise>` + `trackCacheWrite` + `awaitPendingSearchCacheWrites` and wired the cli await before disconnect. **But it only wraps `cache.store`.** Every other fire-and-forget write in the query path remains unguarded.

Why doesn't `gbrain search` hang? Search produces fewer results faster — the `bumpLastRetrievedAt` UPDATE completes before disconnect races; cache.store is bypassed in search; telemetry threshold isn't crossed. The race exists but search wins. `query` is slower and loses.

## Verification steps I ran

1. Upgraded `gbrain` v0.35.8.0 → v0.41.2.0: hang persists.
2. Confirmed `awaitPendingSearchCacheWrites` is in installed `cli.ts:194-195` and `hybrid.ts:36-46, 1192`.
3. Disabled retrieval tracking: `gbrain config set search.track_retrieval false` — hang persists.
4. Disabled cache: `gbrain config set search.cache.enabled false` — hang persists.
5. `gbrain doctor`: all checks green except routing-eval-fixture warnings unrelated to retrieval.

## Suggested fix

Replicate the `pendingCacheWrites` pattern with a generic per-operation drain registry. Either:

**A) Single shared drain:** export a single `awaitPendingWrites()` from a new `src/core/pending-writes.ts`; have every `void` callsite that touches PGLite wrap its promise via `trackPendingWrite`. Call once in `cli.ts` before disconnect.

**B) Op-scoped drain:** attach a `pendingWrites: Promise<unknown>[]` to `OperationContext`. Helpers push into it. `cli.ts` awaits `Promise.allSettled(ctx.pendingWrites)` before disconnect. Cleaner separation; survives long-running daemons.

Either way, the fix needs to cover **every** untracked `void` PGLite write reachable from a CLI op, not just `cache.store`.

## Why this matters

PGLite ingestion at scale: 500-record brain reproduces 100% of the time. Larger brains (we have a 22k-record vault waiting to migrate) would be unusable. The current fix gives false confidence — released as `fix-wave: community PR triage` but the fix only addressed the most visible symptom.

## Workaround for users

`gbrain search` (tsvector keyword) returns fine. For semantic+keyword RRF, no workaround currently — `query` is unusable on PGLite until this is resolved.

---

*Reporter: Noevu GmbH, building Jarvis (personal AI chief-of-staff). Happy to test patches against the 500-record reproducer.*
title: gbrain query hangs after results render — v0.36.1.1 fix covers only cache.store, other fire-and-forget PGLite writes still block disconnect

body:
## Summary

On Postgres engine, the `autopilot --inline` daemon silently loses link rows during the `extract.links_fs` phase, and `engine.getHealth()` then throws `No database connection: connect() has not been called` every cycle. Affects v0.41.0.0 (current at time of filing). Not the same class as #1162 / PR #465 (which is about the reconnect loop crashing the daemon) — this fires even when the daemon stays alive.

## Repro

Stock v0.41.0.0 on a Postgres-engine brain. From an interactive shell with your `GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL` / `ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY` etc. loaded:

```bash
gbrain dream --dir /path/to/your/brain --json 2>&1 | tee /tmp/dream.log
```

Output around the `extract` phase will contain:

```
[cycle.extract] start
[extract.links_fs] 3/3 (100%)
  batch error (2 link rows lost): No database connection: connect() has not been called. Fix: Run gbrain init --supabase or gbrain init --url <connection_string>
[extract.links_fs] 3/3 (100%) done
```

The cycle continues, every subsequent phase that calls `this.sql` and falls back to the module-level `db.getConnection()` will fail the same way (in autopilot's main loop this surfaces as `[health] ERROR ...` after the cycle completes, but the daemon does not crash — `logError` catches it).

## Root cause

`PostgresEngine.connect(config)` without a `poolSize` arg goes to the module-level singleton branch (`postgres-engine.ts:175-189`) — `db.connect(config)` runs, but the engine instance's own `_sql` stays `null`. From then on, `this.sql` returns `db.getConnection()` (the module singleton).

Somewhere in the cycle's call graph (still narrowing — observed firing during the `extract.links_fs` phase, after `synthesize` completes — even on a brain with no transcripts so synthesize is a near-noop), the module-level `sql` in `db.ts` gets nulled. Once that happens, every later engine call routed through the getter throws `connect() has not been called`. The lost-rows behavior in `extract.ts:614-628` is structural — the `flush()` function catches and logs the throw, but `batch.length = 0` in the `finally` clears the un-inserted rows regardless.

This is the same bug class the source comment at `src/commands/auth.ts:52` acknowledges for auth commands:

> v0.32: createEngine returns a disconnected instance. PostgresEngine's `sql` getter falls back to `db.getConnection()` (the module-level singleton) when `_sql` is unset, which throws "connect() has not been called" when `db.connect()` was never invoked either.

The auth-command instance was fixed by having `withConfiguredSql` call `engine.connect()`. Long-running daemons that hold the engine across many cycle phases have a different exposure: SOMETHING during the cycle nulls the singleton out from under them.

I did not isolate the exact line that nulls `sql` in `db.ts` (the only sites that set `sql = null` are `connect()`'s catch path on line 216 and `disconnect()` on line 230 — neither of which any cycle phase appears to invoke directly). It could be a transient pool error that triggers the catch-path null, or a code path I missed. Filing this as a bug first so a maintainer who knows the cycle code better can localize the source.

## What this affects

- **`gbrain autopilot --inline` on Postgres**: silent row loss in every `extract` phase that has links to insert. Status reads as `partial`, but the bare counts shown in the human log line (`extracted=N`) look correct because they reflect *intent*, not actual rows persisted. Operators trusting the log line will believe the daemon is healthy.
- **`gbrain autopilot` Minions-dispatch path on Postgres**: same class, fires when the dispatched job's engine routes through the singleton (per-job worker engines using `poolSize` are immune; the autopilot's *parent* engine handling intra-tick orchestration is not).
- **Adaptive `engine.getHealth()` health check** post-cycle: throws `connect() has not been called` and `logError('health', e)` writes the line, daemon survives, no adaptive backoff occurs because `interval` stays at `baseInterval`.

## Relationship to PR #465

PR #465 (open, ~1 month no review) fixes the `(engine as any).connect?.()` no-config-arg bug in the autopilot reconnect path. That's a real fix and lands when this issue lands. But applying #465 alone leaves THIS bug fully present — I confirmed it by patching #465 locally and re-running. The daemon stays alive (good — that's what #465 ships), but `extract.links_fs` still loses rows on every cycle and `getHealth` still throws.

So this is a separate fix wave.

## Suggested fix

Two reasonable shapes; I'm filing PR for the second one because it's the narrowest scope and survives architectural disagreement about the first.

**Option A — engine-level**: Have `PostgresEngine.connect(config)` always create its own `_sql` regardless of `poolSize`. Treat `poolSize` as a sizing hint, not a "should we own a pool" toggle. This kills the singleton-fallback class entirely. Risk: every existing CLI caller now opens a pool instead of sharing one — small extra connection overhead, but it's the architecturally cleaner answer.

**Option B — autopilot-specific** (what my PR does): At autopilot startup, after `connectEngine()` returns, force `engine.connect({...savedConfig, poolSize: 5})` so the autopilot's engine moves to instance-owned pool. The long-running daemon is the affected blast radius; one-shot CLI commands are unaffected by the same race because they exit before the singleton has time to get nulled.

I'm not opening Option A as a PR because it's a behavior change for every caller and reviewing it deserves a separate thread.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.41.0.0 (commit at install: `bun install -g github:garrytan/gbrain` ~2026-05-24)
- Engine: postgres (local Postgres 17.10 via Homebrew)
- macOS 26.4.1, Bun 1.x via global bun install
- ZeroEntropy embeddings (`zeroentropyai:zembed-1`, 1280-dim)
- Brain has 15 pages, ~50 chunks

## Companion PR

(linked in a moment)
title: autopilot: silent link-row loss + getHealth() breakage from module-singleton null mid-cycle (separate class from #1162 / #465)

body:
Running gbrain dream --json on a corpus of 3,125 pages / 85,720 chunks. The propose_takes phase consistently times out at 600s (SIGTERM) before completing.
Environment:

GBrain: 0.40.2.0
Schema: v89
Database: PostgreSQL 18.4
Corpus: 3,125 pages, 85,720 chunks, 100% embedded
Embedding model: zeroentropyai:zembed-1 (1280d)
OS: CachyOS Linux

What happens:

All other phases complete cleanly (lint, backlinks, sync, synthesize, extract, extract_facts, resolve_symbol_edges, patterns, recompute_emotional_weight, consolidate)
propose_takes calls claude-sonnet-4-6 at ~$0.012/batch, ~7s per batch, 209 batches total
At ~600s the phase is killed by SIGTERM
Zero takes are written — nothing commits before the timeout fires
Stale locks left in gbrain_cycle_locks (cleared manually via DELETE)
Total cost consumed with zero output: ~$2.51 across two attempts

What I tried:

gbrain dream --help — only accepts --dry-run and --json, no timeout flag
gbrain propose-takes --help — unknown command
gbrain takes --help — recognized but no help output (internal only)
No timeout config found in ~/.gbrain/config.json or env files

Expected behaviour:
Either a configurable per-phase timeout, or propose_takes exposed as a standalone command so it can be run independently without the dream cycle orchestrator killing it.
Workaround:
None currently. Brain score stuck at 46/100 (links 1/25, timeline 0/15) because propose_takes cannot complete.
title: gbrain dream propose_takes phase times out on large corpus (3,125 pages) — no configurable timeout or standalone invocation

body:
The `llama-server` embedding-provider recipe in v0.41.0.0 has a structural gap that makes it unusable with any custom dim. `gbrain reinit-pglite` and `gbrain init --pglite` both refuse — with-flag AND without-flag — creating a paradox.

## Repro

```bash
$ gbrain --version
gbrain 0.41.0.0

# 1. With the flag (and a value matching the model's native dim):
$ LLAMA_SERVER_BASE_URL=http://localhost:11435/v1 \
  gbrain reinit-pglite --embedding-model llama-server:my-model \
    --embedding-dimensions 1024 --yes

Refusing to init: Provider "llama-server" model "my-model" does not support
custom dimensions 1024 (this model only emits its default vector size).
Either drop --embedding-dimensions or pick a Matryoshka-aware model.

# 2. Without the flag (as the error suggests):
$ LLAMA_SERVER_BASE_URL=http://localhost:11435/v1 \
  gbrain reinit-pglite --embedding-model llama-server:my-model --yes

--embedding-dimensions <N> is required.
```

Same behavior with `gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model ... --embedding-dimensions ...`.

The recipe's own docstring (`src/core/ai/recipes/llama-server.ts`) says:

> They MUST pass `--embedding-model llama-server:<id>` and `--embedding-dimensions <N>`. The wizard refuses to pick implicit defaults.

— so the user IS expected to pass dims, but the validation rejects every value they pass.

## Root cause

`src/core/ai/recipes/llama-server.ts` declares `default_dims: 0` and `user_provided_models: true`, but does **not** declare `dims_options` on the embedding touchpoint.

`src/core/embedding-dim-check.ts:validateDimAgainstTouchpoint()` has a 3-tier check:

1. Tier 1 — recipe-declared `dims_options`. (highest precedence: "recipe author knows their backend")
2. Tier 2 — provider-specific Matryoshka allow-lists (Voyage / ZeroEntropy / OpenAI text-embedding-3-*).
3. Tier 3 — reject custom dims.

Because llama-server declares no `dims_options` (Tier 1 miss) and isn't in any Tier-2 allow-list, every positive dim value falls to Tier 3 → reject. The Voyage recipe's pairing with `VOYAGE_VALID_OUTPUT_DIMS` in `src/core/ai/dims.ts` is the structurally identical precedent for how llama-server should be wired.

## Suggested fix

Declare `dims_options` on the recipe's embedding touchpoint with the common embedder sizes a user-launched llama-server might serve:

```typescript
// src/core/ai/recipes/llama-server.ts, inside touchpoints.embedding:
dims_options: [256, 384, 512, 768, 1024, 1280, 1536, 2048, 3072],
```

This covers bge-m3 (1024), nomic-embed-text (768), all-MiniLM (384), ZE zembed-1 sizes (1280), OpenAI 3-small/3-large (1536/3072), Voyage steps (256/512/1024/2048), and various smaller models. Anything outside the list still rejects with a clear error referencing the allowed list — same UX as the Voyage / ZE recipes.

Happy to send a PR if useful. Tested locally on macOS (Mac mini M4 Pro) with bun-installed gbrain + bge-m3 Q8_0 via llama-swap at 1024d — patched recipe accepts the flag, schema lands at VECTOR(1024), `gbrain import` round-trips through llama-server cleanly (0.9973 cosine on a smoke search).

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: llama-server recipe is missing `dims_options` — both `reinit-pglite` and `init` refuse any custom-dim use (v0.41.0.0)

body:
**Bug:** `brainstorm` / `lsd` print a misleading "Saved to wiki/ideas/..." message when the configured engine is non-filesystem (e.g. `engine: "postgres"`)

**Version:** gbrain 0.40.7.2

**Repro:**
1. `~/.gbrain/config.json` has `"engine": "postgres"` (page store is Postgres, not the filesystem)
2. Run `gbrain brainstorm "..."`
3. CLI prints: `_Saved to `wiki/ideas/2026-05-24-brainstorm-<slug>`._`
4. No file exists at that path. Nothing was written to disk. The page lives only as a row in the Postgres `pages` table.

**Why this is wrong:**
The "Saved to ..." message in `src/commands/brainstorm.ts` (line 329) is hard-coded to show the slug as if it were a filesystem path, regardless of which engine `putPage` actually wrote to. Users go looking for the file, can't find it, and assume the save failed (or the CLI is lying — both bad).

**Fix:**
Make the success message reflect the actual storage backend. Options:
- For `engine: "postgres"`: print `_Saved to page `<slug>` (postgres engine — view with `gbrain page show <slug>`)._`
- For `engine: "filesystem"` (or whatever the FS engine key is): keep the current `wiki/ideas/...` path message but resolve it to the absolute on-disk path so users can `cat` it directly.
- Generic: have `engine.putPage` return a `{ location, kind }` descriptor (`{ kind: "file", path: "/abs/path.md" }` or `{ kind: "row", table: "pages", slug: "..." }`) and let the CLI format the message from that.

**Bonus:** add a `gbrain page show <slug>` (or `gbrain ideas list` / `gbrain ideas show <slug>`) command so users on the Postgres engine have an obvious way to read what was just "saved." Right now the only way to see the output is to query Postgres directly, which most users won't do.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.40.7.2
title: `brainstorm` / `lsd` print a misleading "Saved to wiki/ideas/..." message when the configured engine is non-filesystem (e.g. `engine: "postgres"`)

body:
**Bug / UX:** `gbrain brainstorm` has no "top N" output mode — prints all passed ideas

**Version:** gbrain 0.40.7.2

**Behavior:** `brainstorm` prints every idea that passed the judge thresholds (`onlyPassed: true`) and saves the full corpus (passed + failed) to `wiki/ideas/<date>-brainstorm-<slug>.md`. There is no way to ask for just the top N ranked ideas.

**Expected:** A flag like `--top 3` (or `--limit N`) that prints the N highest-scoring passed ideas to stdout, sorted by composite judge score. The full corpus should still be saved to the wiki page for triage.

**Why it matters:** Most prompts ("top 3 startup ideas for me") expect a small ranked shortlist, not a 50–200 idea brain dump. Users currently have to open the saved file and eyeball it.

**Suggested fix:**
- Add `--top N` flag (default unset = current behavior)
- When set, sort `result.passed` by composite score desc and slice to N before passing to `formatBrainstormMarkdown`
- Saved file behavior unchanged (still full corpus)

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.40.7.2
title: `gbrain brainstorm` has no "top N" output mode — prints all passed ideas

body:
**Bug:** `--to supabase` flag is misnamed — the underlying functionality isn't Supabase-specific

**Version:** gbrain 0.40.7.2

**Problem:**
The `--to supabase` flag implies Supabase-only behavior, but under the hood it just targets a standard Postgres connection (connection string, schema, RLS handling, etc.). There's nothing Supabase-specific about the code path — it works against any Postgres instance (self-hosted, RDS, Neon, Postgres.app, plain Docker).

This is misleading because:
- Users on non-Supabase Postgres assume the flag doesn't apply to them and skip it
- Users on Supabase assume it does Supabase-specific things (auth, storage, edge functions, RLS policies) that it doesn't
- Docs/help text reinforce the wrong mental model

**Fix:** Rename the flag to reflect what it actually does.

Suggested: `--to postgres` (or `--to pg`), with `--to supabase` kept as a deprecated alias that prints a warning and forwards to `--to postgres`.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `--to postgres` works against any Postgres connection string
- `--to supabase` still works but logs a deprecation notice pointing to `--to postgres`
- README / `--help` / examples updated to use `--to postgres`
- Remove or correct any Supabase-specific language in the docs for this command (unless there's actual Supabase-specific behavior, in which case split it into a separate `--to supabase` path)

**Environment**
gbrain 0.40.7.2
title: `--to supabase` flag is misnamed — the underlying functionality isn't Supabase-specific

body:
**Where:** `src/core/brainstorm/judges.ts`

**Root cause:**
- `DEFAULT_JUDGE_CHUNK_SIZE = 100` (line 362)
- `maxTokens: 4000` (line 451)
- Each scored idea is ~80–120 tokens (5 scores + note), so 100 ideas ≈ 8000–12000 tokens of output
- Model hits the 4000-token cap mid-JSON and returns truncated `{ "ideas": [ ...` with no closing `]}`
- `parseJudgeJSON` (line 241) tries 3 strategies (raw `JSON.parse`, markdown-fence extraction, brace-match + trailing-comma repair) — none can reconstruct truncated JSON, so it throws
- A 72-idea run sits right on the edge: most ideas come back, but the closing JSON gets cut

**Symptom:** `brainstorm` runs fail with a judge JSON parse error, or silently drop ideas, whenever the chunk size × per-idea token cost exceeds `maxTokens`.

**Fix:** Bring chunk size and `maxTokens` into agreement. Suggested:
- `DEFAULT_JUDGE_CHUNK_SIZE`: `100` → `25`
- `maxTokens`: `4000` → `8000`

This keeps each judge call comfortably under the output cap with headroom, instead of relying on the parser to reconstruct truncated JSON.

**Better long-term fix:** size `maxTokens` from chunk size (e.g. `chunkSize * 150` plus overhead), or stream/append ideas instead of requiring one JSON blob per chunk.

**Environment**
gbrain 0.40.7.2
title: Judge JSON parse fails because chunks of 100 ideas exceed the 4000-token output cap - 0.40.7.2

body:
**What happened?**

On a freshly-rebuilt PGLite brain, default `gbrain query \"<question>\"` (expansion enabled) hangs at ~100% CPU and never produces output. Adding `--no-expand` returns results promptly on the same brain / same query, so the failure mode is in the expansion path *before* any results are produced.

This is **distinct from the post-print hang** covered by #1247 / #1269 / #1290 / #1343 / fix PR #1259 — those issues describe results being printed promptly followed by hung CLI cleanup. Here, no output is produced; the CPU spin happens before any output and persists indefinitely.

**What did you expect?**

Default `gbrain query \"<q>\"` to either complete the expansion step and produce ranked results, or fail with a diagnosable error message. Not silently spin at 100% CPU.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Freshly-rebuilt PGLite brain (in our case `gbrain init --pglite` followed by `gbrain import` of ~1,800 markdown pages — see Environment below).
2. Confirm brain is healthy: `gbrain doctor --fast` → exits 0.
3. Run default query:
   ```bash
   gbrain query \"test query\"
   ```
   Observed: no output, ~100% CPU. Killed via SIGKILL after a 176-minute spin.
4. Run with `--no-expand`:
   ```bash
   gbrain query \"test query\" --no-expand
   ```
   Observed: ranked results print in <1s (top-hit score ~0.86 on a relevant query). The process then exhibits the post-print hang described in #1247/#1290, but that is a separate (and already-tracked) bug; the *results themselves* are correct.

The bug reproduces on a freshly-rebuilt brain — it is not corruption-state-dependent. The 100% CPU rules out a network wait on an LLM expansion call (which would be 0% CPU); the symptom is consistent with a compute-bound loop in the expansion path.

**Environment**

- gbrain version: 0.40.2.0
- OS: macOS Darwin 25.x (`sw_vers -productVersion` reports 26.1)
- Bun version: 1.3.13
- Database: PGLite (`~/.gbrain/brain.pglite`, freshly built via `gbrain init --pglite`)
- Brain contents: 1,886 pages across two sources (1,836 in a federated `default` source, 50 in an isolated `doe` source)
- Embedding: `openai:text-embedding-3-small` (1536d)

**Possibly related**

- #1156 (Query expansion silently disabled on OpenAI-compatible endpoints) — touches the same expansion code path. That issue describes a *silent fallback* to `[query]` after a schema-validation failure; here the symptom is a *hang*, not a fallback. Could share root cause if the validation failure path has degenerated to a loop on v0.40, but is observably a different failure mode from the operator side.
- #1259 (fix: drain last-retrieved writes before CLI disconnect) — fixes the *post-print* hang behavior covered by #1247/#1269/#1290. Would not address a pre-output expansion hang.

**Impact**

`gbrain query` is effectively unusable without `--no-expand` on this brain. Workaround documented locally; filing for visibility because default expansion is the documented behavior per CLI help text, and a 176-minute silent CPU spin with no diagnostic output is hard to debug from the operator side.

**\`gbrain doctor --json\` output**

Health score: 90, schema v89, PGLite connection OK, embedding probe confirms OpenAI text-embedding-3-small (1536d) reachable, 1,886 pages all embedded. Full \`gbrain doctor --json\` available on request.
title: PGLite: gbrain query without --no-expand hangs at ~100% CPU before producing output (v0.40.2.0)

body:
## Problem

On Windows, the default pipe buffer size is limited (approximately 45KB). When passing large content (e.g., files >45KB) to `gbrain` via stdin/pipe, the data is silently truncated or fails without any error message.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a markdown file larger than 45KB
2. Run: `gbrain put_page --title "Test" < large_file.md`
3. The content is silently truncated or the operation fails

### Expected behavior

`gbrain` should be able to handle files of any size reliably.

### Actual behavior

Content >45KB gets silently truncated on Windows due to pipe buffer limitations. No error message is shown to the user.

## Proposed Solution

Add a `--file` (or `--input-file`) parameter that accepts a file path and reads the content directly from the file instead of stdin. This completely bypasses the pipe buffer limitation.

### Example usage

```bash
# Instead of (fails on Windows for large files):
gbrain put_page --title "Test" < large_file.md

# Use new --file parameter:
gbrain put_page --title "Test" --file large_file.md
```

## Additional Context

- This issue is **Windows-specific** — Unix systems have much larger pipe buffers so the problem is less visible there
- The `--file` parameter workaround has been manually patched locally and works reliably
- This would make `gbrain` more robust on Windows and also improve performance for large files on all platforms

## Environment

- OS: Windows 10/11
- gbrain version: (please fill in)
- Python version: 3.13

---

Would you be open to accepting a PR that implements this feature?
title: Add --file parameter to bypass Windows pipe buffer limitation
`gbrain put <slug>` fails on Windows with ENOENT: /dev/stdin

- R2 lifecycle policy on the bucket itself (via Cloudflare API)

Found during external validation 2026-05-23.

Environment:
- GBrain: 0.36.3.0
- Runtime: Bun 1.3.14
- OS: Ubuntu 22.04, aarch64 (Oracle Cloud ARM)
- Engine: Supabase

Issue:
gbrain init --supabase falls back to db.[ref].supabase.co:5432 (IPv6 direct) 
during schema migration even when Session pooler URL (port 6543) is provided.

Pooler connects successfully. Migration step resolves the Supabase project ref 
and constructs a direct connection URL internally, ignoring the provided pooler URL.

Steps to reproduce:
1. ARM Ubuntu VM (IPv4 only, or IPv6 where port 5432 is firewalled)
2. Run: gbrain init --supabase
3. Enter Session pooler URL: postgresql://postgres.[ref]:[pw]@aws-1-[region].pooler.supabase.com:6543/postgres
4. Observe: connects via pooler, then migration fails with ECONNREFUSED on IPv6 port 5432

Expected: all connections including migrations use the provided pooler URL
Actual: migrations fall back to direct connection db.[ref].supabase.co:5432

Workaround: none found. PGLite also fails on ARM (WASM runtime crash).
Related: #223 (macOS WASM bug), #218
title: Supabase init falls back to direct IPv6 connection during migrations when pooler URL provided

body:
## Summary

Add a `--name-prefix <prefix>` flag to `gbrain skillpack scaffold` that namespaces the scaffolded skills (directory names, frontmatter `name:` fields, and inter-skill cross-references in skill bodies) under the given prefix.

Example:
```bash
gbrain skillpack scaffold --all --workspace ~/.claude --name-prefix gbrain
```

Result: `~/.claude/skills/gbrain-query/SKILL.md` (frontmatter `name: gbrain-query`, body cross-references like `delegate to ingest` rewritten to `delegate to gbrain-ingest`).

## Use case

gbrain ships ~48 skills with short, generic names (`query`, `ingest`, `enrich`, `search`, `maintain`, `briefing`, `setup`, etc.) — sensible defaults when gbrain is the only skill suite in an OpenClaw-style workspace, but problematic when scaffolded into a mixed Claude Code `~/.claude/skills/` that already hosts other skillpacks.

On a typical user machine, `~/.claude/skills/` ends up holding skills from several sources:
- `estack-*` (a personal skill collection)
- `gstack-*` (build/ship tooling)
- `document-skills:*` (Anthropic-bundled)
- `superpowers:*`, `impeccable:*`, `cloudflare:*`, plugin packs
- ...and after `gbrain skillpack scaffold --all`, ~36 unprefixed gbrain skills

Without a prefix, generic names like `query`, `search`, `setup`, `migrate`, `testing`, `reports` collide visually (and potentially semantically) with skills from other authors. The other packs above all use directory-name prefixes for exactly this reason; gbrain is the outlier.

## Precedent

The convention is already established across the ecosystem:
- `estack-chris-voss`, `estack-customer-discovery`, etc. — directory `estack-chris-voss`, frontmatter `name: estack-chris-voss`
- `gstack-investigate`, `gstack-ship`, `gstack-design-review`, etc.
- `document-skills:docx`, `document-skills:pptx`, etc. (plugin-namespaced form)
- `superpowers:brainstorming`, `cloudflare:agents-sdk`, etc.

Today the only way to get a gbrain prefix is to manually rename after scaffolding (36+ dirs, 36 frontmatter edits, plus all inter-skill body references — `\b<skillname>\b` find-and-replace across all bodies for the renamed set). The manual approach is also fragile: a subsequent `gbrain upgrade` or re-scaffold reintroduces the unprefixed versions, doubling skills.

## Proposed behavior

`gbrain skillpack scaffold [<name|source>] --name-prefix <prefix> [other flags]`

Validation: `<prefix>` matches `^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$` (kebab-case, leading letter), terminating dash optional but auto-added if absent so `--name-prefix gbrain` and `--name-prefix gbrain-` both produce `gbrain-query`.

For each scaffolded skill `<name>`:
1. Write to `<workspace>/skills/<prefix>-<name>/SKILL.md` (and paired source files) instead of `<workspace>/skills/<name>/`.
2. Replace the `name: <name>` line in frontmatter with `name: <prefix>-<name>`.
3. Rewrite inter-skill body references: for every other scaffolded-in-this-run skill `<other>`, find-and-replace `\b<other>\b` → `<prefix>-<other>` in the body (NOT in code fences or quoted literals — same restraint the upstream regex needs). Limit rewrites to references to skills being scaffolded in this run; don't touch names not in the rename set, so prose words that happen to overlap (`search`, `query` as verbs, `ingest` as noun) aren't false-positive rewritten when those names ARE being renamed.
4. The `subagent`, `delegate-to`, or similar structured-reference fields in frontmatter (if any exist) get the prefix applied too.
5. `RESOLVER.md` and `manifest.json` written into the workspace get the prefixed names.

Sticky-prefix idea (nice-to-have): persist `--name-prefix` per-workspace in a `<workspace>/.gbrain-skillpack-config` (or `gbrain.yml` under `skillpack.scaffold.name_prefix`), so subsequent `gbrain skillpack scaffold <name>` and `gbrain skillpack reference <name>` calls in the same workspace automatically use the same prefix without re-specifying. `gbrain skillpack reference <prefix>-<name>` should map back to the unprefixed upstream `<name>` for the diff lens.

## Backwards compatibility

Opt-in flag; absent prefix preserves today's exact behavior. `gbrain skillpack reference <name>` continues to work for unprefixed installs. The diff lens (`reference`) needs a small update to strip the prefix when comparing against upstream.

## Tradeoffs / open questions

1. **Cross-reference rewriting in skill bodies is the subtle bit.** Many gbrain skills mention each other by name in prose (`query` skill mentions `search`, `signal-detector` mentions `ingest`, etc.). Word-boundary find-and-replace works for unambiguous names like `signal-detector`, but `query`, `search`, `ingest`, `maintain` are also common English words. Conservative approach: only rewrite when the name appears in a structured form like a bullet list `- skill-name`, a markdown link `[query](...)`, a code-fence reference, or following a known verb pattern like "delegate to query" / "see query skill". Aggressive approach: rewrite all word-bounded occurrences in the rename set. Worth designing the rewrite contract explicitly — maybe a `cross_refs: []` field in each `SKILL.md` frontmatter that lists which skills it depends on, so the scaffolder rewrites those references unambiguously.

2. **`OVERLAP_WHITELIST`** (`ingest`, `signal-detector`, `brain-ops`) is referenced by the resolver-health check. With prefixed names those entries would need to be `<prefix>-ingest`, `<prefix>-signal-detector`, `<prefix>-brain-ops` — either the check needs prefix-aware whitelist matching or the whitelist becomes per-workspace config.

3. **Routing fixtures** (`test/fixtures/routing-eval.jsonl`) reference skills by unprefixed names. They'd need either parallel prefixed fixtures or a prefix-strip pass before evaluation.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.40.8.0
- Filing this after running `gbrain skillpack scaffold --all --workspace ~/.claude` on Windows and noticing the gbrain skills mix visually with the surrounding ecosystem's prefixed packs.
title: Feature: `gbrain skillpack scaffold --name-prefix <prefix>` to disambiguate gbrain skills in mixed workspaces

body:
## Problem

The `dream` cycle's synthesize phase (`src/core/cycle/synthesize.ts`) has a significance judge that hardcodes `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` as the only authentication mechanism:

```typescript
function makeHaikuClient(): JudgeClient | null {
  if (!process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) return null;  // <- hardcoded
  const client = new Anthropic();
  return { create: client.messages.create.bind(client.messages) };
}
```

When `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is unset, **all** conversation transcripts are unconditionally marked as `worth: false` with reason `"no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY for significance judge"`, making the synthesize phase non-functional for users who rely on alternative LLM providers.

This is increasingly common as high-quality, cost-effective models like DeepSeek v4 Flash, Gemini, and local endpoints become viable alternatives to Claude Haiku for lightweight judging tasks.

## Impact

- Users without Anthropic access cannot use the dream synthesize phase at all
- The significance judge (a lightweight JSON-classification task) does not require the specific capabilities of Claude Haiku — any capable LLM can perform it
- The hardcoded provider check prevents innovation in the GBrain ecosystem

## Suggested solution

Replace `makeHaikuClient()` with a `makeJudgeClient()` that:
1. Tries `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` first (preserving original behavior)
2. Falls back to `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, or `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` via an OpenAI-compatible fetch adapter
3. Supports configurable base URLs via `DEEPSEEK_BASE_URL` / `OPENAI_BASE_URL` environment variables

I have a working implementation at https://github.com/justemu/gbrain/pull/1 that has been tested with DeepSeek v4 Flash — 3/5 real-world transcripts correctly judged as worth processing, 2/5 correctly filtered as routine operations.

## Alternatives considered

- **Configuration-based model routing**: More flexible but requires more extensive refactoring of the judge infrastructure. The env-var fallback approach is minimal-risk and backward-compatible.
- **agent.use_gateway_loop**: The gbrain doctor suggests this for non-Anthropic setups, but the synthesize judge has a separate code path that ignores this config and hardcodes the Anthropic SDK.
title: Dream synthesize significance judge hardcodes ANTHROPIC_API_KEY — no fallback for other LLM providers
pglite-engine.ts:connect() swallows Aborted() but never calls NodeFS.repairWal() from the PR #994 overlay

body:
## Summary

Two small CLI ergonomics gaps in v0.30.2:

1. **`restore_page` is reachable only via `gbrain call restore_page '{"slug":"..."}'`** — the operator-friendly CLI surface is missing. `gbrain pages purge-deleted` already exists as the operator escape hatch for the autopilot purge phase, but the restore counterpart is MCP-only.
2. **`pages` is missing from `CLI_ONLY` in `src/cli.ts`** — `gbrain pages purge-deleted` currently exits with `Unknown command: pages` because the early shared-ops dispatch short-circuits before the large `case 'pages':` switch (`src/cli.ts:723`) can run.

## Repro

```console
$ gbrain pages purge-deleted
Unknown command: pages
Run gbrain --help for available commands.

$ gbrain pages restore concepts/foo
Unknown command: pages

# Current workaround for restore:
$ gbrain call restore_page '{"slug":"concepts/foo"}'
{"status": "restored", "slug": "concepts/foo"}
```

## Context

I'm running v0.30.2 with PGLite as the personal brain for an autonomous learning loop. The loop's sync script iterates `concepts/*` pages and discovered a foot-gun: when a page is soft-deleted (via `gbrain delete` or the MCP `delete_page` op), a subsequent `put` against the same slug returns `{"status":"created_or_updated"}` but is a no-op — the page stays hidden because it's still soft-deleted. The shadow is invisible to `gbrain get` / `list` and only surfaces with `--include-deleted`.

The clean recovery path is `restore_page` then re-`put`. From a Bash automation context, one short CLI line (`gbrain pages restore <slug>`) is significantly cleaner than `gbrain call restore_page '{"slug":"<slug>"}'` (JSON escaping in shell quoting, dependent on `call`'s long-term stability).

## Proposed change

Two-file, ~30-line patch. Already implemented and locally verified against a production `~/.gbrain` PGLite DB. Mirrors the existing `runPurgeDeleted()` shape (JSON flag, `--help` integration, exit codes). Thin wrap over `engine.restorePage(slug)` (already on `BrainEngine` since v0.26.5).

```
 src/cli.ts            |  2 +-
 src/commands/pages.ts | 29 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++--
 2 files changed, 28 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
```

Full patch:

```diff
diff --git a/src/cli.ts b/src/cli.ts
--- a/src/cli.ts
+++ b/src/cli.ts
@@ -27 +27 @@
-const CLI_ONLY = new Set(['init', ..., 'sources', 'mounts', ...]);
+const CLI_ONLY = new Set(['init', ..., 'sources', 'pages', 'mounts', ...]);

diff --git a/src/commands/pages.ts b/src/commands/pages.ts
--- a/src/commands/pages.ts
+++ b/src/commands/pages.ts
@@ -24,6 +24,26 @@ function parseOlderThanHours(args: string[]): number {
+async function runRestore(engine: BrainEngine, args: string[]): Promise<void> {
+  const json = args.includes('--json');
+  const slug = args.find((a) => !a.startsWith('--'));
+  if (!slug) {
+    console.error('Usage: gbrain pages restore <slug> [--json]');
+    process.exit(2);
+  }
+  const restored = await engine.restorePage(slug);
+  if (json) {
+    console.log(JSON.stringify({ slug, status: restored ? 'restored' : 'not_deleted' }, null, 2));
+    return;
+  }
+  if (restored) {
+    console.log(`Restored: ${slug}`);
+  } else {
+    console.error(`No soft-deleted page found at slug: ${slug}`);
+    process.exit(1);
+  }
+}
+
 async function runPurgeDeleted(engine: BrainEngine, args: string[]): Promise<void> {

@@ -63,14 +83,18 @@ function printHelp(): void {
 Subcommands:
+  restore <slug> [--json]           Restore a soft-deleted page (clear deleted_at).
+                                    Thin CLI wrap of the existing `restore_page` MCP tool.
+                                    Exits 1 if the slug is not soft-deleted.
   purge-deleted [--older-than HOURS|Nd] [--dry-run] [--json]
                                     ...

 Notes:
-  Soft-delete a page via the MCP `delete_page` op. Restore via `restore_page`.
-  This command is the manual operator escape hatch — the autopilot cycle's
+  Soft-delete a page via `gbrain delete <slug>` (or the MCP `delete_page` op).
+  Restore via `gbrain pages restore <slug>` (this command) or the MCP `restore_page` op.
+  `purge-deleted` is the manual operator escape hatch — the autopilot cycle's

@@ -80,6 +104,7 @@ export async function runPages(engine: BrainEngine, args: string[]): Promise<voi
   switch (sub) {
+    case 'restore': return runRestore(engine, rest);
     case 'purge-deleted': return runPurgeDeleted(engine, rest);
```

## Verification

Run locally against `~/.gbrain` (PGLite):

```console
$ bun run src/cli.ts delete concepts/foo
{"status": "soft_deleted", "slug": "concepts/foo", "recoverable_until": "now + 72h via restore_page"}

$ bun run src/cli.ts get concepts/foo >/dev/null 2>&1; echo $?
1                                            # hidden, as expected

$ bun run src/cli.ts pages restore concepts/foo
Restored: concepts/foo

$ bun run src/cli.ts get concepts/foo >/dev/null 2>&1; echo $?
0                                            # back

$ bun run src/cli.ts pages restore concepts/foo
No soft-deleted page found at slug: concepts/foo   # idempotent failure, exit 1

$ bun run src/cli.ts pages restore concepts/foo --json
{
  "slug": "concepts/foo",
  "status": "restored"
}

$ bun run src/cli.ts pages --help
... shows restore + purge-deleted side by side ...
```

## Why CLI vs MCP for this op

The MCP `restore_page` tool is correctly scoped (`scope: write`) and stays. The CLI wrap is for:

- Bash automation contexts (shell scripts, cron jobs) where shell quoting `--json` payloads is brittle
- Local operator recovery, with discoverability via `gbrain pages --help`
- Parity with `gbrain delete` and `gbrain pages purge-deleted` (the existing operator escape hatch for the same lifecycle)

The bonus `pages` → `CLI_ONLY` registration also unblocks `gbrain pages purge-deleted`, which is currently silently broken at the CLI level (the autopilot purge phase still works because it goes through the library function directly).

Happy to send a PR if this seems desirable. The patch deliberately stays minimal — no new types, no new tests beyond what the existing `restore_page` op handler path already covers.
title: Add `gbrain pages restore <slug>` CLI subcommand (plus `pages` CLI_ONLY registration fix)

body:
## Problem

GBrain has three documents that each claim to define "canonical page types," and they disagree:

1. **`skills/_brain-filing-rules.json`** — 22 canonical `kind` values. Includes `conversation`, `daily`, `media-format`. The user-facing spec.
2. **`src/core/types.ts` `PageType`** — 22 different types. Includes `yc`, `hardware`, `note`, `email`, `slack` — none of which appear in the filing-rules JSON.
3. **Workspace tooling** (e.g. brain-integrity-auditor in OpenClaw skills) — uses 19 canonical + 16 operational = 35 acceptable, missing `conversation`/`daily`/`media-format`.

The downstream symptom: my brain has 104 distinct page-types against a canonical ~22. The brain-integrity-auditor flags ~900 real non-canonical pages but ALSO false-flags ~1200 `conversation` pages because that type is canonical in the JSON but not in the auditor's hardcoded list.

## What enforces today

Nothing at write time. `src/core/markdown.ts` `parseMarkdown` casts `frontmatter.type` to `PageType` without enum validation. Any string is accepted. That's how `[object Object]` ends up in the DB.

## What I'd love

1. **Pick one SoT.** Personal vote: `_brain-filing-rules.json` (it's the spec; types.ts can derive from it via codegen or a contract test).
2. **Add write-time validation** in `parseMarkdown` (chokepoint covering both file imports and MCP `put_page`). Default `warn`, with `GBRAIN_STRICT_TYPES=1` to reject.
3. **(Optional but ideal)** Open question: should `type` be an enum at all, or should we move to tags + a small `kind` (5-7 values)? Enums drift; tags don't.

## Context

Workspace-side, I'm shipping a stopgap today (fix 4 offender skills + bulk SQL remap of existing non-canonical pages, reversible via backup). Not waiting on upstream for that. But the structural fix has to land upstream — happy to draft the PR if you have a preference on direction.

Posted from OpenClaw platform agent; verified no in-flight taxonomy work in 60d of upstream commits before filing.
title: Three sources of truth for page type — which is authoritative?

body:
## Description

When using Ollama (local) as embedding provider with gbrain, imports fail with `[embed(ollama:nomic-embed-text)] Not Found` error, even though:

1. The Ollama server is running and accessible at localhost:11434
2. `nomic-embed-text` model is pulled and verified working
3. `gbrain providers test --model ollama:nomic-embed-text` succeeds (returns 768 dims)

## Environment
- gbrain: v0.40.2.0
- Ollama: v0.24.0
- Model: nomic-embed-text (768d)
- Config: `"embedding_model": "ollama:nomic-embed-text"`

## Reproduction Steps

1. Start Ollama with `ollama serve`
2. Pull model: `ollama pull nomic-embed-text`
3. Configure gbrain: `gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model ollama:nomic-embed-text --embedding-dimensions 768`
4. Try to import: `gbrain import /path/to/markdown`

Expected: Pages imported with embeddings
Actual: `[embed(ollama:nomic-embed-text)] Not Found` error

## Investigation

Manual API testing shows:
- `curl .../v1/embeddings -d "{\"model\":\"nomic-embed-text\",\"input\":\"hello\"}"` → SUCCESS (returns embeddings)
- `curl .../v1/embeddings -d "{\"model\":\"ollama:nomic-embed-text\",\"input\":\"hello\"}"` → FAIL (model not found)

It appears the code may be passing the model name WITH the provider prefix (e.g., "ollama:nomic-embed-text") when the API expects just "nomic-embed-text".

## Suggested Fix

Either:
1. Strip the provider prefix before passing to the embedding API for Ollama,
2. Or normalize model IDs consistently in model-resolver.ts

Happy to help debug further.
title: Ollama embedding fails with Not Found - model prefix handling issue
