Fresh PGLite installs can initialize the schema with the legacy OpenAI 1536-dim embedding column while the runtime gateway defaults to ZeroEntropy `zembed-1` at 1280 dimensions. The first write/embed then fails with a dimension mismatch.

## Observed behavior

On a fresh-ish local PGLite brain with no pages:

```text
gbrain v0.37.1.0
config.json:
{
  "engine": "pglite",
  "database_path": "/home/opc/.gbrain/brain.pglite"
}
```

`gbrain doctor --json` reports the effective embedding provider as ZeroEntropy:

```text
embedding_provider: ok / skipped
Model: zeroentropyai:zembed-1
connection: Connected, 0 pages
```

But a first `gbrain put personal/goals.md` fails when embedding:

```text
expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280
```

The brain is empty, so this is not an old-data migration problem. The schema itself appears to have been initialized with `vector(1536)`, while the active embedding path is producing 1280-dim ZeroEntropy vectors.

## Why this looks like a default mismatch

The current runtime gateway defaults appear to be ZeroEntropy 1280:

```ts
// src/core/ai/gateway.ts
const DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MODEL = 'zeroentropyai:zembed-1';
const DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS = 1280;
```

But several schema/init paths still appear to have legacy OpenAI defaults:

```ts
// src/core/pglite-schema.ts
export function getPGLiteSchema(dims: number = 1536, model: string = 'text-embedding-3-large'): string
```

Also found similar legacy fallbacks in PGLite/Postgres initialization paths:

```text
src/core/pglite-engine.ts: let dims = 1536
src/core/postgres-engine.ts: let dims = 1536
src/core/schema-embedded.ts: embedding vector(1536)
```

This creates a split-brain default:

```text
schema default: 1536 / OpenAI-style
runtime gateway default: 1280 / ZeroEntropy
```

## Expected behavior

A new PGLite brain should be internally consistent.

Either:

1. The schema initializes to ZeroEntropy's default dimensions:

```text
embedding_model = zeroentropyai:zembed-1
embedding_dimensions = 1280
content_chunks.embedding = vector(1280)
```

or:

2. If the schema intentionally remains 1536 for compatibility, the effective runtime embedding model/dimensions should also resolve to 1536 until the user explicitly runs a provider switch.

## Suggested fix

Centralize the embedding defaults used by:

- gateway runtime config
- PGLite schema creation
- Postgres schema creation
- config seed rows
- doctor/schema dimension checks

At minimum, `gbrain init --pglite` should seed `embedding_model` and `embedding_dimensions` consistently with the schema it creates.

`doctor` should also catch this before first embed/write by comparing:

```text
actual schema vector width
configured/effective embedding_dimensions
configured/effective embedding_model
```

## Reproduction outline

```bash
gbrain init --pglite
# with no explicit --model / --embedding-model args
# configure ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY or otherwise let runtime resolve to zeroentropyai:zembed-1

gbrain put personal/goals.md < some-file.md
```

Actual:

```text
expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280
```

Expected:

```text
put succeeds, or doctor/init catches and fixes the mismatch before embedding
```

## Impact

This breaks the first write for a new PGLite brain even when the brain has 0 pages, because the schema width and runtime embedding provider dimensions disagree.
title: Fresh PGLite init can mismatch ZeroEntropy 1280 runtime with 1536 schema

body:
## Summary

Add a GBrain provider recipe for Kimi / Moonshot AI as an OpenAI-compatible chat + expansion provider.

Kimi exposes an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint and can be used for GBrain's non-embedding LLM surfaces such as query expansion, chat, facts extraction, and dream synthesis.

## Motivation

GBrain already has a recipe architecture for OpenAI-compatible providers such as DeepSeek, Groq, Together, Zhipu, DashScope, and others. Kimi / Moonshot is a common frontier model choice for agent workloads and should be configurable through the same provider mechanism.

This would allow configs such as:

```bash
gbrain config set models.tier.utility kimi:kimi-k2.6
gbrain config set models.tier.reasoning kimi:kimi-k2.6
gbrain config set models.tier.deep kimi:kimi-k2.6
gbrain config set models.expansion kimi:kimi-k2.6
gbrain config set models.chat kimi:kimi-k2.6
gbrain config set facts.extraction_model kimi:kimi-k2.6
gbrain config set models.dream.synthesize kimi:kimi-k2.6
```

## Proposed provider recipe

Add something along these lines:

```ts
export const kimi: Recipe = {
  id: 'kimi',
  name: 'Kimi (Moonshot AI)',
  tier: 'openai-compat',
  implementation: 'openai-compatible',
  base_url_default: 'https://api.moonshot.ai/v1',
  auth_env: {
    required: ['MOONSHOT_API_KEY'],
    setup_url: 'https://platform.moonshot.ai/',
  },
  touchpoints: {
    expansion: {
      models: [
        'kimi-k2.6',
        'kimi-k2.5',
        'kimi-k2-turbo-preview',
        'kimi-k2-thinking',
      ],
    },
    chat: {
      models: [
        'kimi-k2.6',
        'kimi-k2.5',
        'kimi-k2-turbo-preview',
        'kimi-k2-thinking',
      ],
      supports_tools: true,
      supports_subagent_loop: false,
      supports_prompt_cache: false,
      max_context_tokens: 256000,
    },
  },
  setup_hint: 'Get an API key at https://platform.moonshot.ai/, then `export MOONSHOT_API_KEY=...`',
};
```

Expected code changes:

- Add `src/core/ai/recipes/kimi.ts`
- Register it in `src/core/ai/recipes/index.ts`
- Surface `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` in provider env discovery/listing
- Add provider recipe smoke/contract coverage if applicable

## Scope

This issue is intentionally scoped to adding the Kimi provider recipe for chat + expansion.

It does not attempt to solve `models.tier.subagent` routing. That is covered by related issues such as:

- #1157
- #621

Once the subagent provider abstraction is generalized, Kimi can be evaluated separately for subagent/tool-loop support. For this issue, `supports_subagent_loop` can stay `false` to avoid overclaiming compatibility.

## References

- https://platform.moonshot.ai/docs/guide/kimi-k2-6
- https://platform.kimi.com/docs/api/overview

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: Add Kimi / Moonshot AI provider recipe

body:
## Problem

GBrain v0.37 has a useful multimodal embedding path, but the native Google recipe only exposes `gemini-embedding-001` as a text embedding model. `embedMultimodal()` currently rejects the Google recipe because `supports_multimodal` is not set, and the error message points operators to Voyage.

Google has shipped Gemini Embedding 2 / `gemini-embedding-2-preview`, a native multimodal embedding model that maps text, images, video, audio, and documents into a single embedding space.

## Evidence

Google docs: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/embeddings

- `gemini-embedding-2` / `gemini-embedding-2-preview` supports multimodal input: text, images, video, audio, PDF.
- Output dimensions support 128-3072, recommended 768/1536/3072.
- Current GBrain Google recipe only lists `gemini-embedding-001` under embedding.
- Current `embedMultimodal()` error says: Today: `voyage:voyage-multimodal-3`.

## Local validation

LiteLLM proxy in front of Gemini can expose `gemini-embedding-2-preview` and text embeddings work:

```text
POST /v1/embeddings model=gemini-embedding-2-preview input="hello world"
→ OK, 3072 dims
```

But the OpenAI-compatible multimodal content-array shape that GBrain sends through `embedMultimodalOpenAICompat()` fails via LiteLLM:

```text
POST /v1/embeddings
input=[{type:"image_url", image_url:{url:"data:image/png;base64,..."}}]
→ 400 INVALID_ARGUMENT
Invalid value at 'requests[0].content.parts[0]' (text), Starting an object on a scalar field
```

So the LiteLLM path is not currently sufficient as a drop-in bridge for Gemini multimodal embeddings.

## Desired behavior

Allow configs like:

```bash
gbrain config set embedding_multimodal true
gbrain config set embedding_multimodal_model google:gemini-embedding-2-preview
# or google:gemini-embedding-2 when stable
```

Then image ingestion / `gbrain reindex --multimodal` / cross-modal query paths should use Google native multimodal embeddings instead of requiring Voyage.

## Why this matters

- Single-vendor deployment for users already on Gemini.
- Native all-modal support: text, images, video, audio, documents.
- Avoids Voyage free-tier 429s during vault image ingestion.
- Aligns with GBrain's provider-agnostic embedding architecture.

## Implementation sketch

- Extend `src/core/ai/recipes/google.ts` with Gemini Embedding 2 model(s).
- Add Google native `embedMultimodal` request serialization for `models/*:embedContent` with `parts` containing text and inline image data.
- Handle output dimensions consistently with GBrain schema / `embedding_image` and `embedding_multimodal` columns.
- Add tests for text-only, image-only, text+image request payloads and model validation.
title: feat: support Gemini Embedding 2 as native multimodal embedding provider

body:
Hi Garry and GBrain maintainers,

I have been following gstack and gbrain because they feel unusually honest about what agent work is becoming: memory, product judgment, review, QA, shipping, and eventually real operational authority.

I am building SINT Protocol, an open source runtime gate for agent actions. The core loop is simple:

```text
agent intent
capability token
policy gateway
allow, deny, or escalate
proof receipt
```

The part I am trying to sanity check here is the syscall boundary for agents.

If an agent can call MCP tools, spend money, start jobs, touch infra, or operate robots, I think it needs a boring and inspectable control point that sits below the agent brain and above the tool call.

SINT currently focuses on:

1. Attenuated capability tokens, where delegated permissions can only narrow.
2. Consequence tiers for observe, prepare, act, and commit actions.
3. Signed proof receipts for policy decisions.
4. A tamper evident evidence ledger.
5. Conformance fixtures across MCP, ROS 2, Open RMF, OPC UA, Sparkplug, and related physical AI surfaces.

The first integration idea is small:

1. Add an optional adapter around GBrain or OpenClaw tool execution.
2. Route selected MCP or job actions through a SINT policy gateway.
3. Return a proof receipt with each allow, deny, or escalation.
4. Keep the first demo tiny enough to review in one sitting.

The useful outcome from this issue would be a sharp technical critique:

Is a runtime policy gate for agent syscalls a useful primitive at this layer, or is that boundary better placed somewhere else in the stack?

Repo for context:

https://github.com/sint-ai/sint-protocol
title: Design discussion: runtime policy gates for agent tool actions

body:
## Observation

`gbrain import` against ~2000 staged markdown files runs at ~2.1 seconds per file. After 35 minutes the wall-clock had embedded 1000 of 1989 files:

```
~/.gbrain/import-checkpoint.json:
{
  "totalFiles": 1989,
  "processedIndex": 1000,
  "completedFiles": 1000,
  "timestamp": "2026-05-19T19:30:05.008Z"   ← 35 min after start
}
```

2100s ÷ 1000 files = **2.1 s/file**.

That number is unmistakable: it's a single OpenAI `text-embedding-3-large` (or similar) API round-trip per file. CPU work on the file itself is microseconds. The throughput is bottlenecked entirely on serial calls to OpenAI's `/v1/embeddings` endpoint.

## What gstack expected

gstack's `bin/gstack-gbrain-sync.ts:1063` cites an estimate:

> "Per ED2: ~25-35 min for ~11.7K transcripts = ~150ms/page synchronous"

150ms/page would be plausible if embedding calls were batched (per-batch round-trip cost amortized over many files). 2100ms/page is what you see when each file is a single API call.

## The fix

OpenAI's `/v1/embeddings` accepts **up to 2048 inputs per request** and a max payload of ~8M tokens. Batching the embedding step at the chunk level (or even at the file level, for short files) yields the kind of speedup that lets a one-time `gbrain import` of a personal archive finish in minutes instead of hours:

| Batch size | Embedding calls for 1989 files | Time @ ~2s/call |
|---|---:|---:|
| 1 (current) | 1989 | 35 min+ |
| 32 | 63 | ~2 min |
| 256 | 8 | ~16 s |

(Rough; assumes one chunk per file for the table — adjust for multi-chunk files.)

## Why this matters now

The personal-brain use case has gotten an order of magnitude bigger in the last month — e.g. one user (me) just landed ~166k Gmail markdown files in `~/brain/email/` after wiring the iCloud + Gmail LaunchAgents. At 2.1s/file, that's a **97-hour** initial embed at sequential rate. Multi-day. With batching at size 256, the same archive embeds in ~22 minutes.

The 35-minute SIGTERM wall in gstack (filed separately at garrytan/gstack#1611) is partially a downstream symptom of this — if gbrain batched, the wall would stop biting big-brain users.

## Where to look

I haven't poked at gbrain's embedder pipeline directly, but a quick grep for the OpenAI client call site in `src/embedding/` or wherever the per-chunk loop lives would be the place. Two paths:

1. **Tactical:** wrap the per-call site in a batcher that collects N items then flushes (back-pressure-safe via a bounded queue).
2. **Structural:** rewrite the import stream to fan inputs into a batched embedder with a configurable batch size (env: `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_BATCH_SIZE`, default 256).

Either way, the same code path needs to respect OpenAI's token budget per request (~8M; conservative default 1M is plenty).

## Environment

- macOS 15.x, Apple Silicon
- gbrain v0.33.1.0
- gstack v1.40.0.0 (invoking `gbrain import` via `gstack-memory-ingest --bulk`)
- Engine: Supabase Session Pooler (postgres)
- OPENAI_API_KEY present in env; OpenAI API responding normally

## Verifying the diagnosis

If anyone wants to confirm this is single-call (not some other pathology), run with `OPENAI_LOG=debug` or wrap the embedder call in a logger that prints per-batch input count. A "1 input per call" log == this issue.

Happy to PR the batcher if it's a welcome change.
title: `gbrain import` makes per-file OpenAI embedding calls (~2s/file); batching would give 10-50× speedup

body:
## Environment

- gbrain `0.26.0` (bun script install via `bun install -g gbrain`)
- macOS, PGLite engine (`~/.gbrain/config.json` `engine: pglite`)
- Autopilot installed via `gbrain autopilot --install` → launchd plist at `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.gbrain.autopilot.plist` with `KeepAlive=true` and `RunAtLoad=true`
- Autopilot repo: a git working tree that another tool may force-push or hard-reset (in my case, `~/.claude/skills/gstack` which gets `git reset --hard origin/main` during gstack auto-upgrades)

## Trigger

1. `gbrain autopilot` is running normally against `--repo <X>`. It has cached a sync anchor commit somewhere in its sync state (this case: `17d8df4d`).
2. Something outside gbrain does `git reset --hard origin/main` (or any other history-rewriting operation) inside `<X>`. The cached anchor commit is no longer reachable.
3. Next autopilot cycle: `git cat-file <anchor>` fails. Autopilot logs:
   ```
   fatal: git cat-file: could not get object info
   Sync anchor commit 17d8df4d missing (force push?). Running full reimport.
   ```
4. Full reimport starts, never converges. Cycle counter eventually hits "cycle-failure-cap" and logs `Autopilot stopping (cycle-failure-cap).`
5. Process exits.
6. launchd restarts the process within seconds (`KeepAlive=true`).
7. New process inherits the same stale anchor, repeats from step 3.

The loop is CPU-bound (~99%) because each reimport is a hot loop reading + chunking + extracting 100+ markdown files.

## What actually broke for me

Because PGLite is single-process and autopilot held the database file open continuously, **`gbrain serve` (the MCP entry point) could not acquire the database and blocked at startup**. Claude Code's MCP client timed out the connection, surfacing as `mcp__gbrain__* disconnected` in every session for hours. The autopilot loop was invisible from gbrain doctor (`--fast --json` showed `status: warnings, health_score: 90`) because doctor checks didn't try to acquire the DB.

This is the lock-contention case that #677 anticipates, with autopilot as the antagonist holding the lock.

## Evidence

`~/.gbrain/autopilot.err` (excerpt across multiple respawns):

```
fatal: git cat-file: could not get object info
Sync anchor commit 17d8df4d missing (force push?). Running full reimport.
[gbrain import] Skipping symlink: …
[import.files] start
[import.files] 4/123 (3%) imported=4 skipped=0 errors=0
…
[import.files] 78/123 (63%) imported=78 skipped=0 errors=0
[cycle.lint] start
[cycle.lint] done
[cycle.backlinks] start
…
[cycle.sync] start
fatal: git cat-file: could not get object info
Sync anchor commit 17d8df4d missing (force push?). Running full reimport.
…
```

`~/.gbrain/autopilot.log` (showing cycle-failure-cap exit and immediate respawn):

```
[cycle-inline partial] lint=0 backlinks=0 synced=0 extracted=0 embedded=0 orphans=124
[cycle] score=10 elapsed=1s next=150s
Autopilot stopping (cycle-failure-cap).
Autopilot starting. Repo: /Users/…/.claude/skills/gstack, interval: 300s
[autopilot] running steps inline (engine=pglite)
Running full import of /Users/…/.claude/skills/gstack...
Found 123 markdown files
Stale lock file found (>10 min). Taking over.
```

Process info during the loop:

```
PID    %CPU  ETIME    COMMAND
7995   99.0  15:21    bun /Users/…/.bun/bin/gbrain autopilot --repo /Users/…/.claude/skills/gstack
```

## Expected behavior

- A missing sync anchor commit should trigger **at most one** full reimport, after which a new anchor is established at current HEAD.
- If full reimport cannot converge, autopilot should exit with a non-zero status and a clear log message; under `KeepAlive=true` the OS will restart it, but at the very least the loop should make forward progress.
- Alternatively: `cycle-failure-cap` should write a sentinel file or set state such that autopilot self-disables on next launch until manually re-enabled, instead of being respawned indefinitely by launchd.
- `gbrain doctor` should surface "autopilot loop" or "DB held by long-running process" as a check (related to #677's cooperative-single-owner direction).

## Actual behavior

- Full reimport runs every cycle but never updates the sync anchor (or updates it to a value that doesn't survive a process restart).
- `cycle-failure-cap` exits the process but launchd respawns it. The "stopping" message is misleading — autopilot is not actually stopped, the OS supervises it back up.
- MCP server is silently unavailable for as long as the loop runs.

## Workaround

```bash
launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.gbrain.autopilot.plist
pkill -9 -f "gbrain autopilot"
rm -f ~/.gbrain/autopilot.lock
```

Once autopilot is unloaded, `gbrain serve` acquires the DB cleanly and MCP works. Re-enabling autopilot would re-trigger the loop, so I'm leaving it off pending this fix.

## Suggested fixes (non-prescriptive)

1. When `git cat-file <anchor>` fails, after the full reimport completes, write the new anchor as the **current HEAD of the repo**, not the previously-cached value. The current code seems to keep the old anchor across reimports (or never write a new one until incremental sync would have).
2. Make `cycle-failure-cap` write a persistent sentinel (`~/.gbrain/autopilot-disabled` or similar) that the autopilot entrypoint checks before doing any work. Refuse to run until cleared manually. This breaks the launchd respawn loop without requiring the user to unload the plist.
3. Consider whether `gbrain autopilot --install` should generate a plist with `KeepAlive` set to a dictionary (`KeepAlive = { SuccessfulExit = false }`) instead of `true`, so that a clean `cycle-failure-cap` exit (which is intentional, not a crash) doesn't get respawned.
4. Address #677 so that `gbrain serve` can coexist with autopilot on PGLite.

## Related

- #677 PGLite MCP server and maintenance commands need a cooperative single-owner mode (the architectural root)
- #1162 autopilot: reconnect loop after 5 consecutive failures (sibling failure mode, different trigger)
- #1078 Autopilot/dream sync fails with FK constraint error (adjacent)
title: autopilot: cached sync anchor commit invalidated by upstream force-push / hard-reset triggers indefinite full-reimport loop; cycle-failure-cap fails to exit because launchd plist sets KeepAlive=true

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

The README does not clearly explain the beginner setup flow after running:

```bash 
gbrain init --pglite
```

As a new user, it is difficult to understand:

* where API keys should be configured
* which providers are required
* how to do first document ingestion

This creates onboarding friction for first-time users.

---

**What does the solution look like?**

Add a small “Quick Start” section with:

* API key configuration example

```bash id="9wyz2r"
gbrain config set openai.api_key YOUR_KEY
```

* Minimal ingestion example

```bash id="66w9gf"
gbrain import .
gbrain search "example query"
```

This would help users get their first successful setup much faster.

These work eventually, but a minimal end-to-end example in the README would make onboarding much smoother.
title: README Improvement Suggestion — Missing API Key / First-Time Setup Guidance

body:
## Symptom

When `gbrain import` (or any path that calls `embedBatch` with > 10 texts in one call) hits DashScope `text-embedding-v3`, the API returns:

```
[embed(dashscope:text-embedding-v3)] <400> InternalError.Algo.InvalidParameter:
  Value error, batch size is invalid, it should not be larger than 10.: input.contents
```

Individual files that produce > 10 chunks are silently skipped. On my 43-file `gbrain import`, **7 files (the long ones) failed for this reason** while shorter files imported fine.

## Root cause

DashScope's OpenAI-compatible endpoint enforces a per-request limit of **10 `input.contents`** (it's a hard count cap, not just a token cap).

`src/core/ai/recipes/dashscope.ts` only declares the token cap:

```ts
embedding: {
  ...
  max_batch_tokens: 8192,
  chars_per_token: 2,
}
```

There's no `max_batch_count` field, and grepping the gateway confirms it has no general per-recipe content-count cap mechanism:

```bash
$ grep -rn "max_batch_count\|maxBatchCount" src/core/ai/
# no results
```

Meanwhile `src/core/embedding.ts` (v0.36.3.0) ships with:

```ts
const BATCH_SIZE = 100;
```

So `embedBatch` sub-batches to 100 texts before handing off to the gateway, and the gateway has no way to know it should further split to 10 for DashScope.

## Reproduce

- v0.36.3.0 (also reproduces on v0.35.x — recipe unchanged)
- DashScope `text-embedding-v3` via OpenAI-compatible recipe
- `provider_base_urls.dashscope: https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1` (mainland-China key path)
- `embedding_dimensions: 1024`
- Import a directory where at least one file chunks into > 10 pieces (long markdown dumps, PDF-derived pages, etc.)

Excerpt from real `gbrain import`:

```
Warning: skipped daily/2026-05-18-2318.md: [embed(dashscope:text-embedding-v3)] <400> InternalError.Algo.InvalidParameter: Value error, batch size is invalid, it should not be larger than 10.: input.contents
Warning: skipped daily/2026-05-17.md:    (same error)
Warning: skipped daily/2026-05-17-1626.md: (same error)
Warning: skipped daily/2026-05-17-1625.md: (same error)
Warning: skipped daily/2026-03-23.md:    (same error)
[import.files] 43/43 (100%) done
  35 pages imported
  8 pages skipped (0 unchanged, 8 errors)
```

## Suggested fix

Two minimal changes:

1. **`src/core/ai/recipes/dashscope.ts`** — add `max_batch_count: 10`:
   ```ts
   embedding: {
     ...
     max_batch_tokens: 8192,
     max_batch_count: 10,  // DashScope OpenAI-compat API caps at 10 inputs/call
     chars_per_token: 2,
   }
   ```

2. **`src/core/ai/gateway.ts`** (or wherever sub-batching lives) — read `recipe.touchpoints.embedding.max_batch_count` and cap each outbound request to `min(currentBatch, maxBatchCount)`.

`embedding.ts` `BATCH_SIZE = 100` can stay as the outer paginator for progress callbacks — the actual cap belongs at the recipe / gateway layer where it's per-provider.

Worth checking other recipes too:
- Zhipu has its own per-call limits
- MiniMax is 32
- Voyage has limits at the high end too

## Workaround (what I'm running)

Local patch to `src/core/embedding.ts`:

```diff
-const BATCH_SIZE = 100;
+const BATCH_SIZE = 10;
```

Re-importing the failed 7 files completes cleanly. Blunt (slows OpenAI/Voyage paths to 10/batch too), but reliable until the per-recipe cap lands.

## Context

Running on a mainland-China JD Cloud Ubuntu ECS, brain is PGLite, embed is DashScope (no API egress to international endpoints), chat is Bedrock through a local LiteLLM proxy. Same environment as #1065 (which v0.36.3.0 resolved — thanks for that). Happy to send a PR for the recipe + gateway change if useful, but figured the issue first so you can decide whether `max_batch_count` is the right field name / where the gateway split should live.

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: dashscope: text-embedding-v3 rejects batches > 10 (`InvalidParameter`) — recipe has no `max_batch_count` field

body:
## Summary

Fresh `gbrain init --pglite` on v0.36.3.0 lands in a state where `embedding_model` config says `openai:text-embedding-3-large` but the embed pipeline tries ZeroEntropy (`zembed-1` 1280d), causing every page to fail with `expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280`.

The state is sticky — wiping `~/.gbrain/brain.pglite` and re-running `init` does NOT reset it. Same error persists even after `unset ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY`.

## Environment

- **gbrain version**: 0.36.3.0
- **Repo commit**: 1d5f69fe
- **bun**: 1.x (installed via Homebrew)
- **macOS**: 26.3.1 (M-series Mac)

## Steps to reproduce

```bash
git clone https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain.git ~/sandbox/gbrain
cd ~/sandbox/gbrain
bun install && bun link

export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-proj-...
export ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY=ze_...

gbrain init --pglite
gbrain import /some/markdown/dir/ --no-embed   # 31 files, 234 chunks ok
gbrain embed --stale
```

Result:

```
Error embedding entities/nvda: expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280
Error embedding concepts/ai-model-release-cycle: expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280
...
[embed.pages] 31/31 (100%)
Embedded 0 chunks across 31 pages
```

## Investigation

- `gbrain config get embedding_model` returns `text-embedding-3-large` (no provider prefix)
- `gbrain config get embedding_dimensions` returns `1536`
- `gbrain config set embedding_model openai:text-embedding-3-large` succeeds, but embed pipeline still tries ZE
- `gbrain providers list` shows openai + zeroentropyai both ready
- `gbrain doctor` warns `embedding_provider: zeroentropyai:zembed-1 responds (925ms, 1280 dims) but: DB dimension mismatch: column is vector(1536) but provider returns 1280-dim`

## Workarounds tried (none worked)

| Attempt | Result |
|---------|--------|
| `gbrain config set embedding_model openai:text-embedding-3-large` | embed still routes to ZE |
| `unset ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY` before embed | error switches to `ZeroEntropy embedding requires ZEROENTROPY_API_KEY` (still ZE-routed) |
| `rm -rf ~/.gbrain/brain.pglite && gbrain init --pglite` | same state persists across reinits |
| `gbrain init --pglite --embedding-dimensions 1280` | flag silently ignored, dimensions still 1536 |
| `gbrain ze-switch` | command exists but produces no output, no state change |
| `gbrain config set ze_switch_declined_at "<iso>"` | doesn't unstick the embed path |

## Suspected root cause (from reading source)

`src/core/retrieval-upgrade-planner.ts` defines a ZE-as-default switch flow (v0.36.0.0). On fresh init when `OPENAI_API_KEY` is set, `isLegacyDefault=true` triggers `zeSwitchOffered=true`, but `ze_switch_applied` is never written, leaving the embed pipeline in a half-migrated state where it routes to `ZE_TARGET_EMBEDDING_MODEL` even though `gbrain config get embedding_model` returns the OpenAI default.

`loadConfigWithEngine` in `src/core/config.ts` doesn't merge `embedding_model` from the DB plane (only `embedding_multimodal`, `embedding_columns`, `search_embedding_column`, etc.), so user `config set embedding_model` writes to the DB but the embed code reads the gateway default.

## Impact

Cannot complete fresh PGLite install following INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS.md.

## Suggested fix directions (no PR yet)

1. `loadConfigWithEngine` should merge `embedding_model` from DB plane the same way other `embedding_*` keys are merged.
2. `gbrain init --pglite` should either complete the ZE switch (if ZE key set) or hard-refuse it (if dim mismatch), not leave half-state.
3. `gbrain ze-switch` should print what it's doing (currently silent, no diagnostic output).
4. `gbrain init --embedding-dimensions N` flag should be honored or removed from CLI help.
title: v0.36.3.0 — ZE-switch state stuck mid-migration on fresh PGLite init (1280/1536 dim mismatch)

body:
## Bug

`gbrain serve --http` running on a stateless container (Railway, Fly, Docker, etc.) where the brain's `content_chunks.embedding` column is `vector(1536)` from a pre-v0.36.2.0 OpenAI install will silently fall back to `DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MODEL = 'zeroentropyai:zembed-1'` (1280d) after upgrade. Every `put_page` then fails with:

```
{"error":"internal_error","message":"expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280"}
```

Reads continue to work because `searchVector` doesn't embed at query time when the cache is warm — so the issue surfaces only when writes start failing silently (signal-detector, ingestion skills, MCP `put_page` callers).

## Root cause

`src/core/config.ts:236-238`:

> "fields (embedding_model, etc.) keep their file/env-only loading because they..."

`embedding_model` and `embedding_dimensions` are NOT read from the DB `config` table — only from `~/.gbrain/config.json` (file plane) or `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_MODEL` / `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` env vars.

In a Railway / Fly / Docker deployment:
- `~/.gbrain/config.json` doesn't exist (stateless container, no persistent home dir).
- No env var is set by default.
- → `configureGateway` resolves both fields to `undefined` and applies `DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MODEL` (which v0.36.2.0 flipped to `zeroentropyai:zembed-1` / 1280d).
- The vector emitted at write time is 1280d; the column is `vector(1536)`; pgvector rejects the insert.

The v0.36.2.0 release notes describe the TTY-only ze-switch prompt and `gbrain ze-switch --resume` for recovery, but the failure mode for stateless server deployments isn't mentioned in the release notes or migration skill (`skills/migrations/v0.36.2.0.md`). The prompt skips in non-TTY by design, which is correct, but there's no fallback path that pins the existing model when the prompt is skipped.

## Reproducer

1. Provision a Supabase brain on v0.35 with default OpenAI `text-embedding-3-large` (1536d).
2. Deploy `gbrain serve --http` on Railway / Fly / Docker without `~/.gbrain/config.json` and without `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_MODEL`.
3. Upgrade the deployed binary to v0.36.2.0+.
4. Call `put_page` via the MCP. Observe `expected 1536 dimensions, not 1280` on every write.

## Suggested fixes

Three options, not mutually exclusive:

1. **In `loadConfigWithEngine()`, also read `embedding_model` and `embedding_dimensions` from the DB config plane** (with file/env still winning by precedence). The existing comment justifying the file/env-only path for these keys is from a different era and isn't obviously load-bearing now. Stateless hosts can then set the values once via `gbrain config set` against the remote DB.

2. **In `gbrain serve --http` startup, refuse to start when the brain's `content_chunks.embedding` column width doesn't match the resolved `embedding_dimensions`** (the existing `embedding_width_consistency` doctor check has the logic — fire it at startup instead of waiting for `gbrain doctor` to be invoked). Fail loud, paste-ready fix hint.

3. **In the v0.36.2.0 migration skill, add a section for stateless host deployments** explaining that they need to set `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_MODEL` + `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS` env vars OR run `gbrain ze-switch` against the host before the upgrade, or writes will silently break.

The first option is the structural fix; the second is the defense-in-depth; the third is the documentation patch.

## Workaround (for anyone hitting this now)

Set both env vars on the host service and redeploy:

```bash
# Railway example
railway variables --set GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_MODEL=openai:text-embedding-3-large \
                   --set GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_DIMENSIONS=1536 \
                   --service gbrain-http
# Railway auto-redeploys when env vars change
```

After redeploy, `put_page` works again. No re-embed, no data loss, no schema change.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.36.3.0 (also reproducible on v0.36.2.0)
- Topology 2 (cross-machine thin-client + remote `gbrain serve --http`)
- Host: Railway with Supabase backend (pre-v0.36.2.0 brain, `vector(1536)` column)
- Client: macOS thin-client, no local engine

Happy to test a candidate fix or open a PR for any of the three suggestions if useful.
title: Stateless host deployments fall through to ZeroEntropy default after v0.36.2.0, breaking writes against existing vector(1536) brains

body:
## Summary

After upgrading from v0.33.0 → v0.35.4.0 on macOS **15.6** (not 26.3), PGLite WASM aborts on subsequent autopilot launches and the brain.pglite data directory becomes unrecoverable after a single autopilot cycle. The diagnostic message points to issue #223 (macOS 26.3 WASM bug), but that does not apply — we are several major macOS versions behind that boundary. Downgrading back to v0.33.0 with the same data directory works cleanly.

## Environment

- macOS 15.6 (build 24G84), arm64
- Bun v1.3.13
- gbrain v0.35.4.0 (commit `0c6fcab5`), upgraded from v0.33.0 (commit `17b190e2`)
- Engine: pglite (default)
- Brain size: ~700 pages, ~1.6k embedded chunks

## Reproduction

Starting from a healthy v0.33.0 brain (schema 54):

1. `git checkout 0c6fcab5` (v0.35.4.0)
2. `bun install`
3. `gbrain apply-migrations` — schema goes 34 → 66, but one migration partially fails with `column "source_id" does not exist`. Autopilot still works; brain_score 89/100.
4. Autopilot runs one healthy cycle (`Embedded 1650 chunks across 844 pages`, `score=89`, ~27s).
5. Cycle ends with `cycle-failure-cap` (orphans=849), supervisor restarts the process.
6. On the next `PGlite.create()` open, PGLite WASM aborts:
   ```
   RuntimeError: Aborted(). Build with -sASSERTIONS for more info.
     at abort (.../@electric-sql/pglite/dist/index.js:1:77811)
     at invoke_viiiiii (.../index.js:3:265750)
     at callMain (.../index.js:3:270645)
     at at (.../index.js:3:283568)
     at async create (.../index.js:3:272669)
   ```
7. `KeepAlive` respawns the process and the brain enters a permanent crash loop (4,500+ aborts in ~24h).

The data directory is now unrecoverable — direct `PGlite.create({ dataDir })` from a one-liner reproduces the same `Aborted()` error. Even removing the stale `postmaster.pid` doesn't help (that's the May 14 wasm-fix recipe in the codebase — it only works when the issue is a stale lock, not corruption-on-recovery).

## Diagnostic noise

The error message:

> "This is most commonly the macOS 26.3 WASM bug: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/223"

is misleading when the host OS is < 26.3. Suggest adding an OS version check before printing this hint, or include both possibilities in the message ("if macOS < 26: data dir corruption — try restoring a backup; if macOS >= 26.3: see #223").

## What worked

- Stop launchd-managed `com.gbrain.autopilot`.
- Restore a pre-v0.32 backup (`brain.pglite.pre-v0.32.0-upgrade-*`) — opens cleanly under v0.33.0.
- Downgrade `git checkout 17b190e2` (v0.33.0) + `bun install`.
- `gbrain doctor` reports schema 54, brain_score 89/100, 695 pages, all healthy.
- Autopilot ran a clean cycle in ~10s (`Embedded 444 chunks across 246 pages, score=87`), zero WASM aborts since.

## Suspected interaction

The schema 34 → 66 migration leaves the database in a state that survives one cycle but cannot be reopened. Specifically, the partial-failure on `column "source_id" does not exist` may leave one of the v0.34/v0.35 migrations half-applied, and a subsequent autopilot writes data the WASM reader cannot parse on restart.

I have backups of:
- The corrupted v0.35 brain (`brain.pglite.crashed-20260517-093400`, `brain.pglite.crashed-20260518-2333`) — both `Aborted()` on open.
- The pre-v0.35 brain (`brain.pglite.pre-v0.35-upgrade-20260517-092938`) — also `Aborted()` (caught mid-corruption).
- The pre-v0.32 brain — opens cleanly.

Happy to share dumps or run targeted reproductions if it helps narrow down the offending migration. Most likely candidates given the `source_id` column error are the migrations in the v0.34 series (cdfc210e, 3325b405, 488e4824) or one of the v0.35 schema rebuilds (baf1a477 ZeroEntropy, dd1cc121 contradiction probe).

## Workaround

Stay on v0.33.0 for users with existing brains until the migration path is repaired and/or the data-corruption mode is fixed. Embedding works via OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 with an OpenRouter key (default config in our setup).

## Asks

1. Fix the migration so it doesn't leave the DB in an unreopenable state.
2. Tighten the "macOS 26.3" hint to only fire on actual macOS 26.3 hosts.
3. Optionally: ship a `gbrain rollback-schema` command for users who hit this.
title: PGLite WASM `Aborted()` on macOS 15.6 after v0.33 → v0.35 upgrade — diagnostic misattributes to macOS 26.3 bug (#223)
Gbrain

body:
## Summary

Fresh PGLite brain initialized with ZeroEntropy `zembed-1` at 2560 dimensions can become wedged when `gbrain apply-migrations` / migration schema replay runs.

The DB itself remains structurally intact, but the migration ledger records repeated partials and doctor reports a wedged migration. The failure appears to be the migrate/schema replay path using default 1536-dim gateway state instead of the configured `embedding_dimensions: 2560`, causing schema replay to emit the HNSW chunk index even though pgvector HNSW caps at 2000 dims.

Related but not identical: #1141. That issue is Postgres/Supabase schema v66 -> v67. This report is the PGLite fresh-install/apply-migrations surface.

## Environment / config

Current upstream inspected while triaging:

- `master`: `3a0e1116e76b1be45a136cf184d1a49d431b6b60`
- `VERSION`: `0.36.1.0`
- Engine: PGLite
- Pages: `0`

Config shape:

```json
{
  "engine": "pglite",
  "database_path": "~/.gbrain/brain.pglite",
  "embedding_model": "zeroentropyai:zembed-1",
  "embedding_dimensions": 2560
}
```

## Observed

Doctor / state after failure:

- `schema_version` remained current / unchanged
- `connection: Connected`
- `pages: 0`
- `search_mode: balanced`
- config preserved
- migration ledger had repeated `partial` entries for `0.11.0`, later also `0.12.0`
- doctor reported `WEDGED MIGRATION(s)` after repeated partials

Representative underlying failure:

```text
column cannot have more than 2000 dimensions for hnsw index
```

## Expected

All schema replay / migration paths should apply the same HNSW policy as fresh schema generation:

- if embedding dimensions <= 2000: create `idx_chunks_embedding` HNSW index
- if embedding dimensions > 2000: skip HNSW index and rely on exact vector scan

No migration should wedge a 2560-dim brain by trying to create the chunk HNSW index.

## Suspected root

`init --migrate-only` loads config and calls `engine.initSchema()`, but does not appear to configure the AI gateway from config before schema generation.

PGLite `initSchema()` reads dimensions from the gateway and falls back to 1536 when the gateway is not configured. That makes `getPGLiteSchema()` apply the <=2000 HNSW branch even though the actual brain config/column is 2560.

This may also affect the in-process PGLite schema path in the open #1182 fix-wave unless that path configures the gateway before `eng.initSchema()`.

## Related

- #1141: same HNSW/dimension class, Postgres/Supabase upgrade path
- #1100 / #1182: PGLite apply-migrations wedge, but lock/connect-path oriented rather than 2560-dim HNSW policy
- #640 / #697: fresh schema fixed to skip HNSW when dims >2000, but migrate/schema replay path still appears unsafe

## Workaround

Use `zembed-1` at 1280 dimensions for PGLite fresh installs until the migrate/schema replay path is fixed.

Happy to close this as a duplicate of #1141 if maintainers prefer, but wanted to preserve the PGLite-specific repro surface.
title: bug: PGLite apply-migrations wedges with zembed-1 2560d due HNSW index emitted on migrate path

body:
## Summary

The `gbrain dream` cycle runs through its `cycle.extract` phase without error but extracts 0 links, even when the vault has thousands of `[[wikilinks]]`. Running `gbrain extract links` manually on the same vault works correctly.

## Environment

- gbrain version: 0.36.0.0
- Engine: postgres (Supabase Free, us-west-1)
- Platform: WSL2 / Ubuntu 24.04 on Windows 11
- Bun: 1.3.13
- Vault: ~826 markdown pages, ~3293 chunks, ~3800 `[[wikilink]]` occurrences across the corpus

## Steps to reproduce

1. `gbrain init` against a fresh Supabase database
2. `gbrain import <vault-dir> --no-embed --workers 1` (imports 826 pages, 0 errors)
3. `gbrain embed --stale` (embeddings populate)
4. `gbrain dream --json`

## Expected

After the dream cycle, `Links: NNN` in `gbrain stats` should reflect extracted wikilinks (hundreds-plus given the vault size).

## Observed

- `gbrain stats` shows `Links: 0`
- Dream cycle log shows the extract phase started and ended with no warnings:
  ```
  [cycle.extract] start
  [cycle.extract] done
  ```
- No errors, no skipped-count, no rate limit
- Orphan count is 826/827 (every page looks orphaned) because no links were extracted

## Workaround

Running `gbrain extract links` directly works:

```
[extract.links_fs] 803/803 (100%) done
Links: created 816 from 803 pages
Done: 816 links, 0 timeline entries from 803 pages
```

After the manual run, backlinks, graph traversal, and graph-query all work correctly.

## Hypothesis

The dream cycle's `extract` phase may be guarded by a 'only run if stale' check that fails silently when no specific pages are flagged stale, or it may be invoking a different code path than the standalone `gbrain extract` command.

## Impact

Significant for users running `gbrain dream` as their primary maintenance path (e.g., via cron). Without external prompting, they'll have a brain with no graph edges and silently degraded backlinks/orphan/graph-query functionality.
title: Dream cycle's link extraction phase silently no-ops (0 links extracted)

body:
## Summary

Migration `v66 / embed_stale_partial_index` in `src/core/migrate.ts` wraps `DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_chunks_embedding_null` inside a `DO $$ ... EXECUTE '...' END $$` block. Postgres rejects `CONCURRENTLY` inside *any* function context — including statements run via `EXECUTE` in a `DO` block. So when the EXISTS guard triggers (because a prior `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` left an `indisvalid = false` index), the migration fails:

```
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot be executed from a function
```

The migration completes fine on brains that never had a failed `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` for that index name — the EXISTS guard returns false and `EXECUTE` is never called. The bug only manifests for brains carrying invalid-index leftovers.

## Repro

On a Postgres-backed brain:

```sql
-- Create an invalid leftover (simulates a prior failed CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY)
CREATE INDEX idx_chunks_embedding_null ON content_chunks (page_id, chunk_index) WHERE embedding IS NULL;
UPDATE pg_index SET indisvalid = false WHERE indexrelid = 'idx_chunks_embedding_null'::regclass;
```

Then run `gbrain init --migrate-only` (with schema version <= 65). Migration v66 fails.

I hit this organically while upgrading from v0.32.0 → v0.35.8.0 on a Supabase-backed brain — the invalid index was already present (presumably from a much earlier failed migration attempt). Workaround was:

```sql
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_chunks_embedding_null;
```

then re-run `gbrain init --migrate-only`. Worked cleanly.

## Why the current shape doesn't work

The migration's intent is correct — the comment block explicitly anticipates the leftover-invalid-index case ("A failed CONCURRENTLY leaves an invalid index with the target name; the handler pre-drops any invalid remnant via pg_index.indisvalid"). But the implementation uses `DO $$ ... EXECUTE 'DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY ...' END $$`, and Postgres parses `EXECUTE`'s dynamic SQL inside the function call's transactional scope. `CONCURRENTLY` is rejected at runtime, not parse time, so the EXISTS guard does its job but the EXECUTE that follows fails.

## Suggested fix

Two options:

1. **Drop the `DO` wrapper for the invalid-cleanup step** and issue the DROP as a top-level statement that runs only when needed. Requires the migration runner to support multiple statements. The handler is already engine-aware (uses `runMigration` separately for the DROP and the CREATE) — could split the conditional check from the DROP and run them as separate top-level calls.

2. **Drop without `CONCURRENTLY` for the invalid-leftover case**. An invalid index has no useful index data; a plain `DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_chunks_embedding_null` would take an AccessExclusiveLock briefly on `content_chunks` but the lock duration is negligible because there's nothing to clean up beyond the catalog entry. The subsequent `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` can stay as-is.

Option 2 is mechanically simpler and matches the actual intent (clean up a known-broken artifact, then create the real index concurrently).

## Environment

- gbrain v0.35.8.0
- Postgres (Supabase, pgbouncer transaction mode on :6543, session pooler on :5432 for DDL)
- Brain previously on v0.32.0; upgrade path included v51–v67 via `gbrain init --migrate-only`
- Invalid index `idx_chunks_embedding_null` confirmed via `SELECT indisvalid FROM pg_index ... WHERE indexrelid = ...`

Happy to PR a fix if you want — option 2 is a ~3-line change.
title: bug: migration v66 (embed_stale_partial_index) fails with 'DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot be executed from a function' when invalid index exists from prior failed run

body:
On a fresh source, `gbrain sync` emits log lines that
read as contradictory at a glance:

```
Import complete (5.9s):
  7 pages imported
  0 pages skipped (0 unchanged, 0 errors)
  7 chunks created
[gbrain phase] sync.fullsync.import done 5913ms imported=7 skipped=0 errors=0
Embedded 0 chunks (0 stale found)
First sync complete. Checkpoint: 20dff88a
  7 file(s) imported, 7 chunks, 7 pages embedded
```

The "Embedded 0 chunks (0 stale found)" line is followed
immediately by "7 chunks, 7 pages embedded" — which
appears to contradict it.

The actual semantics: the first line refers to the
*stale embedding refresh* step (which had nothing to
refresh because everything was new). The second line
refers to the *initial embedding* step (the one that
actually embedded the 7 chunks). The two lines describe
two distinct phases but the wording reads as if the
first contradicts the second.

**Expected**: the log lines clearly distinguish the two
phases. Even just a clarifying parenthetical would help.

**Suggested fix** (one option): revise the stale-refresh
line to:
```
Re-embedded 0 stale chunks (no prior embeddings to refresh on first sync)
```
OR omit the "Embedded 0 chunks (0 stale found)" line
entirely when the source has just been initialized
(no prior chunks → nothing could be stale).

**Severity**: low. Cosmetic confusion in the log output;
no functional impact.
title: 'Embedded 0 chunks (0 stale found)' log line is misleading on first sync

body:
`gbrain sources remove <id>` without
`--confirm-destructive` exits non-zero with the message:

```
To proceed, pass --confirm-destructive (or use
soft-delete: gbrain sources archive ...)
```

The `--confirm-destructive` gating is intentional and
good. The problem: the soft-delete alternative
`gbrain sources archive <id>` is NOT listed in
`gbrain --help` under the SOURCES block. The user only
discovers it by trying `sources remove` first and
reading the error.

**Expected**: `gbrain --help` lists `sources archive` in
the SOURCES section alongside `sources add`,
`sources list`, `sources remove`. Users who want to
soft-delete a source without destructive intent should
be able to find the command directly.

**Suggested fix**: add `sources archive <id>` to the
SOURCES block in the help output. One-line addition.

**Reproduction**:
```bash
gbrain --help | grep -A20 SOURCES
# observed: no mention of `sources archive`
gbrain sources remove some-id
# error message mentions sources archive — only discoverable here
```

**Severity**: low. Discoverability gap, not a functional
bug. Easy fix.
title: gbrain sources archive is undocumented in --help (soft-delete discoverability gap)

body:
The `--no-expand` flag on `gbrain query` is documented
as disabling LLM-driven query expansion. It does NOT
disable query-string EMBEDDING (used for pgvector
similarity ranking).

Without `OPENAI_API_KEY` in the environment, the
embedding step fails silently. The command then returns
"No results" with no error message — the user has no
signal that the failure was due to missing credentials
rather than a genuinely empty result set.

**Expected**: either
- The command emits a clear error about missing
  embedding credentials when `OPENAI_API_KEY` is unset
  AND embedding is required, OR
- `--no-expand` ALSO disables embedding and falls back
  to tsvector-only keyword search.

The current behavior is the worst of three: it appears
to succeed (exit 0) but returns useless output.

**Suggested fix**: add a `--keyword-only` flag that
explicitly skips both expansion AND embedding (pure
tsvector lookup). The current `--no-expand` flag could
either keep its current behavior (just disable expansion,
require API key) with a clear error on missing
credentials, OR be renamed to clarify it doesn't fully
go API-key-free.

**Reproduction**:
```bash
unset OPENAI_API_KEY
gbrain query "users table" --no-expand --source <id>
# observed: "No results" (exit 0); no signal that embedding silently failed
```

**Severity**: medium. Affects users trying to run gbrain
in keyword-only mode for cost or privacy reasons; the
silent failure is misleading.
title: gbrain query --no-expand silently returns 'No results' without OPENAI_API_KEY

body:
`gbrain sync` ingests files and assigns slugs to each
page. The slug strips file extensions:

- `docs/auth.md` → `docs/auth`
- `README.md` → `readme`
- `_superstack/docs/code-map.md` → `_superstack/docs/code-map`
- `src/auth/users.ts` → `src-auth-users-ts` (different
  shape entirely — flattened to dashes)

The TypeScript case is more aggressive: directory
separators become dashes AND the extension becomes part
of the slug (`-ts` suffix).

This makes slug-pattern filtering brittle. A consumer
wanting "all markdown documentation pages" can't filter
by `*.md` because the slug doesn't have `.md`. They have
to either:
- Filter by negation (`-ts`, `-json`, `-yaml` slug
  suffixes for code/config files)
- Cross-reference against the page-type column from
  `gbrain list` (which reports `code`, `note`, `concept`)
- Detect prose-vs-code by inspecting chunk preview
  content (Superstack's `schema-tracker` v2 settled on
  this approach)

**Expected**: slugs preserve the original file
extension, OR the ingested page exposes a `mime_type`
or `extension` field queryable via `gbrain list`.

**Suggested fix** (least disruptive): add an
`extension` column to the `pages` table populated from
the source file. `gbrain list` could surface it as a
flag; query results could include it in JSON output.
The existing slug shape stays for backwards-
compatibility.

**Reproduction**:
```bash
echo "# Test" > /tmp/test.md
mkdir /tmp/test-project && cp /tmp/test.md /tmp/test-project/
cd /tmp/test-project && git init -q && git add -A && git commit -qm init
gbrain sources add slug-demo --path /tmp/test-project
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-... gbrain sync --source slug-demo --strategy auto
gbrain list --source slug-demo
# observed: slug is "test", not "test.md"
```

**Severity**: low individually; medium when combined
with downstream parsers that need to distinguish prose
from code (which Superstack's gbrain-using subagents do).
title: sync flattens file extensions in page slugs (docs/auth.md → docs/auth)

body:
`gbrain query` accepts `--json` (per `gbrain --help`) but
the flag has no effect in v0.32. Output is always the
text format:
```
[0.6090] src-billing-customers-ts -- [TypeScript] src/...
```

Programmatic consumers that pass `--json` for stable
parsing get the text format and the regex-parse path,
without warning.

The asymmetric case: `gbrain code-def` and `gbrain code-refs` DEFAULT to JSON output (regardless of
`--json`). This means parsers handling both surfaces need
two strategies — text-regex for `query`, JSON-jq for
`code-def`/`code-refs`. The asymmetry is undocumented.

**Expected**:
- Either `gbrain query --json` produces JSON, OR
- `gbrain query` rejects `--json` with a clear "not
  supported" error.

The silent fallback is the worst-of-three.

**Suggested fix**: implement `--json` for `gbrain query`
to emit:
```json
{"results": [{"score": 0.6090, "slug": "...", "preview": "..."}]}
```
matching the shape of `code-def`/`code-refs`.

Alternatively, document the asymmetry prominently in the
help text and reject `--json` on `query` with a clear
error until implemented.

**Reproduction**:
```bash
gbrain query "auth" --json --no-expand --limit 5 --source <id>
# observed: text format, --json ignored
```

**Severity**: medium for consumers building tools on top
of gbrain (e.g., Superstack's subagents).
title: gbrain query --json silently emits text format

body:
Consumers of `gbrain reindex-code` benefit from knowing
the version of the chunker used to produce existing
embeddings. If the chunker upgrades (different chunk
boundaries, different metadata), prior embeddings may need
reindexing.

The natural surface is:
```bash
gbrain config get chunker_version
```

In v0.32, this returns `key not found` regardless of the
running chunker version. The chunker IS versioned
internally (the code-page chunks carry stable metadata),
but the version is not exposed.

**Expected**: `gbrain config get chunker_version` returns
the current chunker's version string (e.g., `0.32.0`,
or a semver matching the bundled tree-sitter-wasms
version).

**Suggested fix**: expose the chunker version via the
existing config-get surface. Add a `gbrain config list`
that enumerates all queryable config keys would help
discoverability.

**Reproduction**:
```bash
gbrain config get chunker_version
# observed: "key not found"
```

**Severity**: low for end users; medium for tools that
want chunker-aware reindex behavior (Superstack's
`code-mapper` v3 had to fall back to age-only reindex
decisions because of this gap).
title: chunker_version config key missing — blocks chunker-aware reindex decisions

body:
## Summary

Each invocation of \`gbrain serve\` from an interactive terminal spawns a long-lived bun process that holds its own Postgres client pool. When the user closes the parent terminal (iTerm pane, tmux pane, VS Code integrated terminal, etc.) without explicitly \`Ctrl-C\`-ing or \`exit\`-ing first, **the serve process is not killed** — it orphans and stays alive indefinitely, still holding open Postgres connections.

Over days of normal operation (open new terminals, launch \`gbrain serve\`, close terminals later) the user's machine accumulates 4-6 orphaned serve processes. With Supabase's free-tier session pooler capped at 15 client connections, this saturates the pool and produces \`(EMAXCONNSESSION) max clients reached in session mode\` errors on legitimate MCP and CLI calls.

## Snapshot from my machine just now

```
$ ps aux | grep 'bun.*gbrain.*serve' | grep -v grep
  7541   Sun06AM   bun run /Users/me/Developer/gbrain/src/cli.ts serve
14092   Mon10:14AM bun run /Users/me/Developer/gbrain/src/cli.ts serve
41783   Sat04PM   bun run /Users/me/Developer/gbrain/src/cli.ts serve
59980   Fri08AM   bun run /Users/me/Developer/gbrain/src/cli.ts serve
70116   Sat05PM   bun run /Users/me/Developer/gbrain/src/cli.ts serve
```

Five serves spanning four days, only one of which (the Monday 10:14AM one) was attached to an active terminal. The other four are orphans from closed iTerm panes. Each was holding active TCP sessions toward \`aws-0-us-west-2.pooler.supabase.com:5432\`.

## Repro

1. Open terminal A, run \`gbrain serve &\` (or just \`gbrain serve\` in a tmux pane).
2. Note the serve PID.
3. Close terminal A's window without \`exit\` or \`Ctrl-C\` first (just close the iTerm tab / X-button the window).
4. \`ps aux | grep gbrain.*serve | grep <pid>\` — serve still running.
5. Repeat in 4 more terminals over a few days.
6. \`(EMAXCONNSESSION) max clients reached in session mode - max clients are limited to pool_size: 15\` shows up on subsequent legitimate calls.

## Diagnosis

The CLI doesn't register a SIGHUP trap or use process-group cleanup, and there's no PID-file-based singleton check that would prevent a second serve from starting (or kill the predecessor) when a new one is invoked. Once orphaned, the only thing keeping the serves running is bun's lack of a parent-terminal dependency — they happily continue serving (a dev-friendly feature) even when no client will ever connect to them again.

## Suggested fix directions

Three options, roughly in order of complexity:

1. **PID-file singleton**: \`gbrain serve\` writes \`~/.gbrain/serve.pid\` on start. New \`serve\` invocations check the PID, send TERM if alive, then take over. One serve at a time, ever. Caveat: doesn't help users who run multiple intentional serves on different ports (does that exist?).

2. **SIGHUP trap**: \`gbrain serve\` traps SIGHUP and exits cleanly. Most terminals send SIGHUP to children when the parent shell exits — but only if the child wasn't backgrounded with \`nohup\` semantics, and bun's default may not honor SIGHUP the same way as node. Worth testing.

3. **Process group + setsid handling**: explicitly join the parent shell's process group so terminal close terminates the serve via the standard Unix mechanism. Requires CLI flag changes.

(1) is the safest and most useful for the typical interactive-dev case. (2) is the right Unix-y default. (3) is for power users with multi-serve setups.

## Companion issue

This issue is the upstream root cause of #1162 (autopilot reconnect loop). When pool exhaustion eventually kills the autopilot's cycles, the reconnect bug in #1162 then prevents the autopilot from recovering. Fixing this issue removes the most common trigger for #1162.

## Workaround (verified 2026-05-18)

```bash
# Kill all orphaned serves except the currently-active one
ps aux | grep 'bun.*gbrain.*serve' | grep -v grep | grep -v <active-pid> | awk '{print \$2}' | xargs -I {} kill -TERM {}

# Verify
ps aux | grep gbrain.*serve | grep -v grep
```

After cleanup, Supabase pool capacity is restored within ~30 seconds (Supavisor drains the stale sessions).

## Environment

- gbrain: 0.33.1.1
- Bun: 1.3.11
- Platform: macOS Darwin 25.5.0 (arm64)
- Terminal: iTerm2 (also reproduced with tmux pane close)
- Engine: postgres (Supabase, session-mode pooler port 5432)

Happy to PR option 1 if the maintainer team agrees on the direction.
title: serve: terminal-launched processes accumulate across sessions, exhaust Supabase session pool over days

body:
## Problem

The subagent loop in `isAnthropicProvider()` (src/core/model-config.ts) hardcodes provider detection to only recognize `claude-*` model names and `anthropic:` prefix. This prevents using Anthropic-compatible APIs (e.g., Zhipu GLM-5.1 via `https://open.bigmodel.cn/api/anthropic`) for the dream synthesize and subagent phases.

Error when using GLM-5.1 with Anthropic-compatible endpoint:
```
subagent job rejected: data.model "glm-5.1" is non-Anthropic. The subagent loop is Anthropic-only (Messages API + prompt caching). Pass an Anthropic model id (e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6) or omit data.model to use the configured default.
```

## Current Workaround

Patch `isAnthropicProvider()` to add custom model prefixes:
```diff
-  return trimmed.toLowerCase().startsWith('claude-');
+  return trimmed.toLowerCase().startsWith('claude-') || trimmed.toLowerCase().startsWith('glm-');
```

This gets wiped on every `git pull` update.

## Proposal

Add a config option like `models.anthropic_compatible_prefixes` that allows users to specify additional model prefixes that should pass the Anthropic provider check:
```bash
gbrain config set models.anthropic_compatible_prefixes '["glm-"]'
```

This way users of Anthropic-compatible APIs (Zhipu, etc.) can use them without patching source code on every update.

## Environment
- gbrain v0.35.7.0
- Anthropic-compatible endpoint: `https://open.bigmodel.cn/api/anthropic`
- Model: `glm-5.1` (supports Messages API + prompt caching)

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: Feature: Allow configurable Anthropic-compatible provider list for subagent loop

body:
## Summary

On OpenAI-compatible endpoints (`provider: openai` with a custom
`OPENAI_BASE_URL`), `gbrain query` silently runs without expansion even
when the expansion model is healthy and reachable. There is no warning
in stderr, and `gbrain query` returns the same hits it would with
`--no-expand`.

Reproduced on two unrelated OpenAI-compatible providers:

- **oMLX** at `http://127.0.0.1:8888/v1` serving `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-6bit`
- **DeepSeek V4 Flash** at `https://api.deepseek.com/v1`, model `deepseek-v4-flash`

## Environment

- gbrain v0.35.7.0 (also verified against current `master`, file SHA `e65b0894`)
- macOS, Apple Silicon
- `expansion_model: "openai:<model>"`
- `provider: openai` (Vercel AI SDK `@ai-sdk/openai`) with custom `OPENAI_BASE_URL`

## Root cause

`src/core/ai/gateway.ts` (master) defines at line 1608:

```ts
const ExpansionSchema = z.object({
  queries: z.array(z.string()).min(1).max(5),
});
```

…and the prompt at lines 1626–1632:

```ts
prompt: [
  'Rewrite the search query below into 3-4 different, related queries that would help find relevant documents.',
  'Return ONLY the JSON object. Do NOT include the original query in the result.',
  'Each rewrite should emphasize different aspects, synonyms, or framings.',
  '',
  `Query: ${query}`,
].join('\n'),
```

The prompt never names the JSON key. When the underlying provider does not
enforce the schema server-side (see "Why this only hits OpenAI-compat"
below), the model picks the prompt-salient noun — `rewrites` — and emits:

```json
{"rewrites": ["…", "…", "…"]}
```

Schema validation against `{ queries: string[] }` fails, the error is a
schema-validation error (not an `AIConfigError`), and the `catch` at
lines 1645–1652 silently falls back to `[query]`:

```ts
} catch (err) {
  // Expansion is best-effort: on failure, fall back to the original query alone.
  const normalized = normalizeAIError(err, 'expand');
  if (normalized instanceof AIConfigError) {
    console.warn(`[ai.gateway] expansion disabled: ${normalized.message}`);
  }
  return [query];   // ← silent --no-expand on schema-validation failures
}
```

## Why this only hits OpenAI-compatible endpoints

`generateObject({ schema })` from the AI SDK tries strict modes first:

- `response_format: { type: "json_schema", json_schema: {...} }` — the
  server enforces key names. OpenAI and Anthropic adapters do this.
  **DeepSeek rejects it** outright (`"This response_format type is
  unavailable now"`), and many self-hosted OpenAI-compat gateways
  don't implement it.
- Forced tool calling — DeepSeek's reasoner rejects `tool_choice: required`
  (`"deepseek-reasoner does not support this tool_choice"`).

When strict modes fail, the call degrades to
`response_format: { type: "json_object" }`, which only enforces JSON
validity, not the key names. At that point the prompt is the only
signal — and the prompt says "rewrite" three times without naming `queries`.

## Reproduction

Direct `curl` against either endpoint with the in-tree prompt and
`response_format: {"type": "json_object"}`:

```bash
# Endpoint A: oMLX at http://127.0.0.1:8888/v1, model Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-6bit
# Endpoint B: DeepSeek at https://api.deepseek.com/v1, model deepseek-v4-flash
# Both return:
{"rewrites": ["...", "...", "..."]}
```

End-to-end:

```bash
gbrain query "test query"             # with expansion: latency ≈ --no-expand
gbrain query "test query" --no-expand # baseline
# stderr is clean on both — no [ai.gateway] warnings emitted.
```

## Proposed fix

One-line change to the prompt naming the required key. A pull request
follows.

## Adjacent context

PR #1135 (open) — *Allow explicit `expansion_model` to fall back to
chat-capable providers* — touches the same expansion code path. The two
changes are independent (different layers: capability gate vs. prompt),
but together they widen who can successfully use expansion on
OpenAI-compatible providers.
title: Query expansion silently disabled on OpenAI-compatible endpoints (prompt does not constrain JSON key name)

body:
### Summary
Once the symbol-edge backfill has fully drained (every `content_chunks.edges_backfilled_at` is non-NULL and `>= EDGE_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS`), each autopilot `resolve_symbol_edges` cycle still performs a **sequential scan of `content_chunks`** that returns zero rows. On large brains this is continuous, unbounded disk I/O for no useful work.

### Root cause
Resume predicate — `src/core/chunkers/symbol-resolver.ts` ~L141-144:

```sql
cc.edges_backfilled_at IS NULL OR cc.edges_backfilled_at < $2::timestamptz
```

(`$2` = `EDGE_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS`, L73). The only supporting index is `idx_content_chunks_edges_backfill ... WHERE edges_backfilled_at IS NULL` (partial, IS-NULL-only). The `< EDGE_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS` arm has no usable index, so post-drain the planner can't satisfy the OR via the partial index and falls back to a full heap scan of `content_chunks` every cycle.

### Impact
On a `content_chunks` table of hundreds of thousands of rows / multi-GB, every idle cycle reads the entire heap. Visible in `pg_stat_statements` as a recurring

```sql
SELECT cc.id, cc.page_id FROM content_chunks cc JOIN pages p ON p.id = cc.page_id
WHERE p.source_id = $1 AND (edges_backfilled_at IS NULL OR edges_backfilled_at < $2)
```

with high `shared_blks_read` and zero rows produced. Easily sustains I/O above small managed-Postgres compute-tier baselines.

### Repro
1. Brain with backfill fully complete (all `edges_backfilled_at >= EDGE_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS`).
2. Let autopilot run normally.
3. `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)` the resume query → `Seq Scan on content_chunks`, large `Buffers: shared read=…`, `rows=0`.

### Suggested fix
Replace the partial index with a plain btree on `content_chunks (edges_backfilled_at)` (covers both the `IS NULL` and `< ts` arms) via a `transaction: false` migration using `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` — same pattern already used for `idx_pages_updated_at_desc`. Optionally also short-circuit the cycle phase when a cheap indexed probe returns 0 candidates.

### Secondary note (not a bug, worth documenting)
Bumping `EDGE_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS` re-stales the entire `content_chunks` table, triggering a full re-walk. On small compute tiers that is a large one-time I/O spike per release that bumps the constant — worth pacing or documenting for resource-constrained installs.
title: resolve_symbol_edges: idle autopilot cycles seq-scan content_chunks (resume predicate's "< EDGE_EXTRACTOR_VERSION_TS" arm is unindexed)

body:
## Context

Running gbrain with multiple sources (one per project area) for noise isolation: project A doesn't surface project B's content, personal queries don't surface business context. Federation via `shared/` catches the always-on cross-cutting material.

But there's a real failure mode for which I haven't found a built-in answer: **scoped queries silently miss cross-source hits.**

Example: working in `tovi` scope, query for "subscription pricing patterns." There is an excellent pattern in `pdfmux/notes/pricing-experiments.md`. The pdfmux source isn't federated, so it doesn't surface. No way to know that pattern exists unless I remember to re-run with `--source-id __all__`.

In practice this means I either:
- (a) Type `--source-id __all__` defensively on every query, which defeats the point of scoping, or
- (b) Live with a recall hit on cross-cutting queries and don't know what I'm missing

Order-of-magnitude estimate: 15-25% of my queries are cross-cutting. The cost of the miss is invisible.

## Proposal: penumbra results

When a query is source-scoped, also return a small set of "halo" results from outside the scope, clearly marked.

```
gbrain --source-id tovi query "subscription pricing patterns"

→ [0.91] products/tovi/pricing/founder-pricing-thesis  (scope=tovi)
  [0.84] products/tovi/research/competitor-pricing      (scope=tovi)
  [0.81] products/tovi/decisions/free-tier-removal      (scope=tovi)
  --- may be relevant (from outside scope) ---
  [0.86] products/pdfmux/notes/pricing-experiments      (scope=pdfmux, foreign)
  [0.79] shared/wiki/patterns/freemium-to-paid          (scope=shared, federated)
  [0.77] products/desta/pricing/seat-vs-usage           (scope=desta, foreign)
```

Default: on for `query`, off for `search` (search is exact-keyword; the cost of a noisy result is higher).

Configurable knobs:
- `--penumbra-k <N>` (default 3) — number of cross-source hits to surface
- `--no-penumbra` — disable for a single call
- `sources.penumbra.default: false` config — disable globally for users who want strict isolation

## Implementation sketch

In `hybrid.ts` after the scoped retrieval completes:
1. If results count > 0 and `penumbra_k > 0`, fire a second query with `sourceIds: undefined` (cross-source) excluding the current scope.
2. Take top-K from cross-source results.
3. Append to result list with `cross_source: true` and `source_id` field set.
4. Skip if the second query returns nothing.

Adds one extra retrieval per scoped query. Caching/dedup against the primary result set is the only nuance worth getting right.

Cost: ~1 day to implement + tests. Latency adds 1 vector search round-trip per scoped query (probably +20-50ms on a small instance).

## Why ask before sending a PR

This is more opinionated than the foreign-edge graph fix. You may have a different design in mind — semantic source-affinity routing, or a smarter signal than "top-K from outside" (e.g. only surface penumbra when scoped retrieval's max score is below a threshold, suggesting the answer might live elsewhere). Or you may prefer this stays out of core and lives in a wrapper.

What I'd like to know:

1. Is this a real shape you'd accept upstream? (If no, I'll wrap it externally.)
2. If yes, what's your preferred default? On for `query` only? Behind an opt-in flag entirely?
3. Is there a different existing mechanism I'm missing that solves the same problem?

Happy to send a PR if there's interest.

— Nameet

## Related

I'm also filing a separate issue on `graph-query` foreign-edge severance — same underlying problem (source isolation removing cross-cutting visibility), different surface area.
title: [Design discussion] Soft cross-source results under source-scoped queries — 'penumbra' pattern

body:
# Bug: `brain_score` timeline component scores 2/15 when `get_health` reports 93% timeline coverage

## Summary

The `brain_score` doctor check reports timeline at **2/15** points (~13% of max) on a brain whose `get_health` simultaneously reports `timeline_coverage: 0.93` (93%). The two numbers should agree directionally; instead they disagree by ~80 percentage points. Looks like an off-by-many-multiplier in the brain_score formula's timeline component.

## Live evidence

From `gbrain doctor` on a Postgres-engine v0.35.7.0 install:

```
[OK] graph_coverage: Entity link coverage 96%, timeline 93%
[OK] brain_score: Brain score 77/100 (embed 35/35, links 25/25, timeline 2/15, orphans 5/15, dead-links 10/10)
```

Same brain, same run. The `graph_coverage` line says timeline is 93%; the `brain_score` formula awards 2 of 15 timeline points.

If timeline 93% → 14/15 (round of 0.93 × 15), the brain_score would be 89/100 instead of 77/100. That's a 12-point phantom loss.

## Affected

- `gbrain` v0.35.7.0 (commit `1dadd9e`), Postgres engine, Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64
- Pages: 3376, Chunks: 6590, Timeline entries: 2775
- `graph_coverage.timeline = 0.93` (from get_health internals)

## Likely root cause (speculation)

I'd guess the timeline component of the brain_score formula is treating raw timeline-entries-per-page (which can be 0–N depending on page type) as if it were a 0–1 coverage ratio, OR it's normalizing against a wrong denominator. The fact that `embed`, `links`, and `dead-links` components all hit their max (35/35, 25/25, 10/10) suggests those formulas are tuned reasonably; only `timeline` and `orphans` are short. `orphans` being short is real for our brain (2150 source pages without inbound links). `timeline` being short despite 93% coverage is the puzzle.

## Suggested fix

If you can point at where `brain_score`'s timeline component is computed (probably in `src/commands/doctor.ts` near the other graph-coverage maths), I'm happy to verify against our live brain that whatever formula change closes the gap. Even just inverting / normalizing it to match `graph_coverage.timeline` directly would fix this.

## Why filing

This isn't blocking — the brain is operationally fine and `get_health` shows the truth. But the brain_score is what doctor reports as the headline number, and a 12-point phantom loss creates false signals about brain health (we kept thinking the brain needed work when actually it was the score formula). Worth fixing for everyone running v0.27+ that surfaces brain_score in their ops dashboards.

## Filing command

```bash
gh issue create -R garrytan/gbrain \
  --title "Bug: brain_score timeline component scores 2/15 when get_health reports 93% timeline coverage" \
  --body-file /tmp/upstream-issue-timeline-scoring.md
```
title: Bug: brain_score timeline component scores 2/15 when get_health reports 93% timeline coverage

body:
## Summary

On Windows with default `core.autocrlf=true`, `gbrain doctor --fast --json` reports every skill not in `OVERLAP_WHITELIST` as a `mece_gap` warning, claiming the skills are missing `triggers:` arrays in their frontmatter — even when those arrays are clearly present.

The same `gbrain doctor` run also reports `skill_conformance: 42/42 skills pass`, so the two checks contradict each other within one report.

## Repro

1. Windows machine with default Git config (`git config --global core.autocrlf` returns `true`).
2. `git clone https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain` — SKILL.md files are checked out with CRLF line endings.
3. `gbrain doctor --fast --json` (any version that uses `src/core/check-resolvable.ts`'s `extractTriggers` function).

Result:

```json
{
  "name": "resolver_health",
  "status": "warn",
  "message": "39 issue(s): 0 error(s), 39 warning(s)",
  "issues": [
    {
      "type": "mece_gap",
      "skill": "query",
      "action": "Add a triggers: array to the frontmatter of skills/query/SKILL.md"
    }
    // ... 38 more
  ]
},
{
  "name": "skill_conformance",
  "status": "ok",
  "message": "42/42 skills pass"
}
```

`skills/query/SKILL.md` and 38 others already have valid `triggers:` blocks. Verifiable with:

```bash
grep -l "^triggers:" skills/**/SKILL.md | wc -l   # 42
```

## Root cause

`src/core/check-resolvable.ts` lines 166 and 169:

```ts
const fmMatch = skillContent.match(/^---\n([\s\S]*?)\n---/);
// ...
const triggersMatch = fm.match(/^triggers:\s*\n((?:\s+-\s+.+\n?)*)/m);
```

Both regexes anchor on literal `\n` (LF). On Windows the file content is `---\r\n` and `triggers:\r\n`, so `^---\n` never matches. The outer frontmatter match fails, `extractTriggers` returns `[]`, and every skill outside `OVERLAP_WHITELIST` (`ingest`, `signal-detector`, `brain-ops`) is flagged. 42 − 3 = 39 — exactly the count observed.

Hexdump of the first bytes of `skills/setup/SKILL.md` on Windows confirms CRLF:

```
0000000   -   -   -  \r  \n   n   a   m   e   :       s   e   t   u   p
```

## Why CI doesn't catch it

`.github/workflows/*.yml` all use `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`. Ubuntu checkouts preserve LF, so the regex works and the test at `test/check-resolvable.test.ts:258-283` ("v0.22.4 regression — actual repo skills/ has 0 errors") passes.

## Suggested fix

One-line CRLF normalization at the top of `extractTriggers`:

```ts
const content = skillContent.replace(/\r\n/g, '\n');
```

Preserves all existing LF behavior. PR coming with patch + unit test for CRLF input.

## Environment

- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 10.0.26200
- gbrain: 0.35.7.0 (master)
- Bun: 1.3.13
- git: `core.autocrlf=true` (Windows default after Git-for-Windows installer)
title: gbrain doctor reports 39 false mece_gap warnings on Windows (CRLF) — extractTriggers regex requires LF

**What problem does this solve?**
Creates an easy way for people to specify what they don't want the "brain" to ingest.
For example: "vendor", "storage", "some-custom-folder"

**What does the solution look like?**
Just like a .gitignore file

There aren't any at present.
