The classifier default appears to be concept. This is a reasonable fallback for genuinely-novel pages but a poor default for round-trip puts. A second
guard worth considering: if the put body is a near-identical match to the previous version (e.g., content_hash differs by less than N%), assume edit-not
classify and preserve type unconditionally
title: put: silent type: concept regression on round-trip when frontmatter omits explicit type: field

body:
## Problem

The DB-side wall-clock detector in `handleWallClockTimeouts` uses `lockDuration` as a multiplier for the dead-letter threshold for jobs that don't set `timeout_ms` explicitly:

```sql
EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - started_at)) * 1000 >
  CASE WHEN timeout_ms IS NOT NULL THEN timeout_ms * 2
       ELSE lockDurationMs * 2 * GREATEST(max_stalled, 1) END
```

At gbrain defaults (`lockDuration=30000`, `max_stalled=3`) that cap is **180s = 3 minutes**, and `gbrain jobs work` doesn't expose either `--lock-duration` or a `GBRAIN_*` env var to bump it.

In practice this is too tight for heavy `claude --print` collector jobs (vision-heavy PDF extraction, multi-page bundle scrapes, orchestrators that fan out serially). On my queue, the slow-tail of successful jobs runs 125-167s — operating with literal seconds of headroom against the cap. Across one overnight cycle I had 7 collector jobs dead-letter with `attempts_made=0` and `error_text='wall-clock timeout exceeded'`. Since wall-clock kills go straight to `status='dead'` (no retry path — they don't increment `attempts_made`), the work is lost; the only recovery is whatever the cron's next fire happens to pick back up.

The workaround today is per-job `timeout_ms` at submit time, but that means touching every job submitter (~10 cron scripts across multiple sibling repos) and remembering to do it for any new wrapper. An operator-side global lever is the right hammer.

## Proposed fix

Add `--lock-duration MS` to `gbrain jobs work`, validated identically to `--health-interval` (positive integer milliseconds, reject suspicious sub-1000ms values that read as unit-confusion typos). Pass through to the existing `MinionWorker` constructor option that's already accepted (`opts?.lockDuration ?? 30000` in `worker.ts`). Startup banner mentions the active value.

```
gbrain jobs work --concurrency 4 --lock-duration 90000
```

Cap then becomes 90s × 2 × 3 = 540s = 9 minutes for non-`timeout_ms` jobs; lock-renewal correctness unchanged.

## Local patch

I have a working local patch (one commit, +35 / -2 in `src/commands/jobs.ts`) running in production for the last hour with `--lock-duration 90000`. Banner reports correctly, validation errors fire on bad input, dead-letter rate dropped to zero overnight.

Happy to open a PR if the shape is welcome — or if you'd prefer a different flag name (`--lock-ttl`?), an env var (`GBRAIN_LOCK_DURATION`?), or to expose `--max-stalled` instead since it's the third multiplier, let me know and I'll re-shape before submitting.
title: feat: --lock-duration flag on `gbrain jobs work` to tune the wall-clock dead-letter cap

body:
I’m looking at GBrain as a self-hosted shared brain: one server-hosted GBrain instance used by multiple agents from different environments over HTTP MCP/OAuth.

This is not asking GBrain to become a managed service or move away from local-first. The question is narrower:

What is already solid for self-hosted shared use, what is still local-first by design, what is contradictory in the docs/code, and what small improvements would make the hosted path easier to operate safely?

## What already seems in place

GBrain already has a lot of the necessary foundation:

- `gbrain serve --http` exposes remote MCP over HTTP.
- OAuth 2.1 support exists: client credentials, auth-code + PKCE, refresh tokens, optional DCR.
- `--public-url` exists so the issuer matches the public reverse-proxy/tunnel URL.
- The admin dashboard exists for client registration, request logs, activity, and health.
- Operations have scopes such as `read`, `write`, `admin`, `sources_admin`, `users_admin`.
- `localOnly` exists for operations that should not cross the remote boundary.
- Thin-client mode exists via `gbrain init --mcp-only` and `remote_mcp`.
- Topology docs already describe a cross-machine brain host with remote agents.
- Multi-source brains exist through `source_id`, source federation, and source-scoped rows.
- Request logging includes client identity, operation, latency, status, and redacted params by default.

So the core answer seems to be: yes, GBrain can already be hosted and consumed remotely.

## What seems rough or incomplete

### 1. Per-agent isolation is not fully connected

This is already covered well in #960.

The hosted/multi-agent story needs a way to bind an OAuth client to a default source/workspace/project. The code already has hints of this shape: HTTP dispatch tries to read `authInfo.sourceId`, and operations can receive `ctx.sourceId`, but OAuth clients do not appear to persist or populate that field yet.

For a self-hosted multi-agent setup, the desired behavior is probably:

- agent/client A sees its own source plus federated/shared sources
- agent/client B sees its own source plus federated/shared sources
- admin clients can see/manage everything
- isolated sources do not leak into normal search/query for other clients

Related: #960.

### 2. Hosted docs contradict themselves around `localOnly`

`docs/mcp/DEPLOY.md` says remote HTTP rejects `sync_brain`, `file_upload`, `file_list`, and `file_url` as `localOnly`.

Later in the same doc it says all operations are available remotely, including `sync_brain` and `file_upload`.

The source appears to agree with the first statement: `serve-http.ts` filters operations with `operations.filter(op => !op.localOnly)`.

This is worth cleaning up because it directly affects self-hosted expectations.

### 3. Hosted vs local command parity is unclear

Thin-client mode intentionally refuses a number of commands that require the host’s local engine or filesystem: `sync`, `embed`, `extract`, `migrate`, `apply-migrations`, `sources`, `files`, some code lookup commands, etc.

That is probably correct, but the product boundary could be clearer:

- what should always run on the host?
- what should route over remote MCP?
- what should never be remote?
- what is planned for parity later?

A hosted operator needs this as a matrix, not scattered command errors.

### 4. Reverse proxy / production deployment guidance is partial

There are docs for ngrok/tunnels and remote MCP, but a self-hosted server setup would benefit from one blessed production recipe:

- systemd service
- Docker or compose example
- Caddy/nginx reverse proxy config
- TLS expectations
- `--public-url`
- backup/restore
- health checks
- log retention
- upgrade/migration flow
- recommended Postgres/Supabase/self-hosted Postgres posture

Related: #912 shows at least one reverse-proxy/admin-dashboard edge case.

### 5. Security docs may describe the older HTTP transport more than the OAuth server

`SECURITY.md` documents env vars like `GBRAIN_HTTP_CORS_ORIGIN`, `GBRAIN_HTTP_RATE_LIMIT_IP`, `GBRAIN_HTTP_RATE_LIMIT_TOKEN`, and `GBRAIN_HTTP_TRUST_PROXY`.

But the current OAuth `serve-http.ts` path appears to use open `cors()` on OAuth/MCP routes, Express rate limiting mainly on `/token` and admin magic links, and `app.set('trust proxy', 'loopback')`.

Maybe I’m reading this wrong, but for hosted deployments it would help to clarify which hardening knobs apply to the current OAuth HTTP server versus the older legacy bearer transport.

### 6. OAuth correctness still has active hosted-impacting issues

There are open issues around OAuth behavior that matter more for hosted deployments than local use:

- #803 auth-code client secret comparison issue
- #935 invalid bearer token returning 500 instead of 401
- related OAuth/bearer issues visible in the tracker

A self-hosted readiness doc could link these as known blockers/footguns by client type.

### 7. Destructive MCP operations may need a hosted policy story

Scopes help, but a shared hosted brain may still need operational guardrails:

- hide destructive tools from normal agents
- require approval for deletes/reverts
- rate limit writes per client
- set per-client operation allowlists
- inspect recent write activity by client/source

Related: #84.

## Hosted vs local gap summary

| Area | Local GBrain | Hosted/shared GBrain |
|---|---|---|
| Auth | OS/user boundary | OAuth clients, scopes, tokens |
| Filesystem access | direct local access | should be blocked or host-mediated |
| Sync/import | local command | host-side job or remote trigger |
| Source selection | cwd/env/dotfile/default | should be per-client/default-source aware |
| Admin | local operator | dashboard/API/reverse proxy/health checks |
| Safety | user sees local command | agents can call tools repeatedly/remotely |
| Observability | terminal output | request logs, client logs, health, traces |
| Backups | local file/Supabase | explicit production runbook needed |

## Possible improvements

1. Land per-OAuth-client source scoping, likely via #960.
2. Add a hosted/local capability matrix to the docs.
3. Fix the `DEPLOY.md` contradiction around `localOnly`.
4. Clarify which `SECURITY.md` hardening knobs apply to `serve-http.ts`.
5. Add a blessed self-hosted deployment guide: systemd + Docker/Compose + Caddy/nginx + backups.
6. Add explicit host-side workflows for thin clients: trigger sync/import/extract/embed as jobs where safe.
7. Add optional per-client operation allowlists or tool-domain filtering.
8. Add better admin observability for client/source activity.
9. Link known OAuth/proxy issues from the deploy guide until resolved.

## Open questions

- Is “self-hosted shared brain for many agents” an intended first-class deployment shape?
- Should source scoping be the main isolation primitive, or should there be a separate workspace/project/tenant abstraction?
- Should hosted deployments prefer Postgres/Supabase over PGLite, even though HTTP OAuth works with PGLite?
- Should destructive tools be controlled by scopes only, or also by per-client policy/allowlists?
- Should thin clients eventually route more CLI commands over MCP, or is host-only the intended boundary?
- Should GBrain ship an official Docker/systemd deployment target, or keep that in docs only?

## Related issues

- #960 — Per-OAuth-client source scoping for multi-agent isolation
- #976 — Memory as a coherent operation surface / hosted gbrain.io context
- #84 — Policy enforcement for destructive MCP tools
- #912 — Admin dashboard SSE behind reverse proxy
- #935 — Invalid bearer token returns 500 instead of 401
- #803 — OAuth auth-code client secret compare issue
title: RFC: self-hosted shared GBrain readiness for multi-agent use

body:
I came across newer context/memory tools like HydraDB, and they seem to point at an interesting product/API direction: memory is not just vector search, and recall is not just “nearest chunks.” Their framing combines long-term memory, graph context, metadata scoping, user/agent preferences, recency, async ingestion, and explainable context assembly.

GBrain already has many of the hard pieces locally: hybrid search, embeddings, graph links, timelines, MCP tools, eval capture, and a local-first Postgres/PGLite engine. So this is not a request to replace GBrain’s storage layer or add HydraDB as a dependency. It is more of an exploratory question:

Could GBrain expose a more explicit native “recall layer” on top of its existing primitives?

**What problem does this solve?**

Right now, users can search/query GBrain, and the engine already combines keyword, vector, graph, and ranking signals. But the user-facing mental model is still mostly “search my brain.”

A recall layer could make the product feel more like:

- remember durable user/project preferences
- retrieve relevant knowledge and memories separately
- include graph context when useful
- scope recall by workspace/project/agent/person
- support fast vs deeper “thinking” retrieval modes
- show why something was recalled
- improve over time through evals and feedback

In other words: move from “hybrid search over stored pages” toward “context assembly for agents.”

**What does the solution look like?**

Maybe something like:

```ts
brain.recall({
  query,
  mode: "fast" | "thinking",
  scopes: ["project", "user", "workspace"],
  includeGraphContext: true,
  includePreferences: true,
  recencyBias,
  metadataFilters,
  maxResults
})
```

Internally, this could compose existing GBrain pieces rather than replace them:

- keyword + vector search
- graph/backlink expansion
- timeline/recency signals
- metadata filters
- memory/preference records
- eval capture
- reranking/context packing

One small first step could be to add a higher-level `recall()` API that wraps existing hybrid search and returns a structured context packet, not just search results.

A second step could be first-class memory types:

- `knowledge`: source-backed docs/pages/chunks
- `preferences`: user or agent preferences
- `decisions`: project/team decisions
- `events`: timeline facts
- `corrections`: superseded or invalidated memories

A later step could add recall traces, so users can inspect why a result appeared:

- keyword score
- vector score
- graph boost
- recency boost
- preference match
- metadata filter match
- final fused rank

**Alternatives considered**

One alternative would be integrating HydraDB directly, but that seems like the wrong first move for GBrain. GBrain’s local-first Postgres/PGLite architecture is a major part of its appeal, and it already has much of the necessary retrieval machinery.

Another alternative would be to keep this entirely inside the current `query`/hybrid search path. That may be enough, but a named recall layer might make the concept easier to expose through MCP, docs, evals, and agent-facing APIs.

**Non-goals**

This does not need to mean:

- adding HydraDB as a dependency
- replacing pgvector/PGLite/Postgres
- moving away from local-first operation
- rewriting the engine layer
- turning GBrain into a hosted service

The interesting part is the product/API direction: making GBrain’s existing search, graph, timeline, and eval machinery feel like an intentional memory/recall system for agents.

**Open questions**

- Would `recall()` be meaningfully different from today’s `query`/hybrid search path?
- Should preferences and durable memories be modeled separately from normal pages/chunks?
- Should graph context be returned as a structured part of the response?
- Should GBrain support fast/deep recall modes?
- Could recall traces become part of BrainBench/eval workflows?
- Is this better as a new API surface, a new MCP tool, or just an evolution of existing query behavior?

Curious if this direction fits the GBrain roadmap. HydraDB is a useful external signal here, but the stronger idea may be: GBrain can provide a local-first, inspectable version of this richer agent recall model using primitives it already has.
title: Proposal: explore a native recall layer inspired by newer context systems

body:
> **Posting as RFC** — feedback / redirection welcome. If the framing is wrong, the most helpful response is "no, here's how I think about it instead." Trying not to waste your cycles on a thread that's pointed at the wrong star.

## TL;DR

`core/operations.ts` is the foundation; `mcp/server.ts` is generated from it. That's the right spine. The question this RFC raises: **is the operation set internally organized around "memory" as a coherent noun, or is it a mix of memory-shaped operations alongside control-plane things (skills, signals, minions, doctor, takes)?** From the outside it looks like the second. If you agree, we'd love to talk about naming the planes explicitly — purely additive, doesn't change behavior for any current client.

Related PR (smaller, low-controversy): #975 — adds the legacy SSE MCP transport to `serve --http` so SSE-pinned clients (openclaw, older MCP SDK) can connect. That's mostly plumbing. This issue is the bigger design question we wanted to surface separately.

## Context — gbrain.io

We're building **[gbrain.io](https://gbrain.io)** — managed gbrain hosting for workgroups. A team signs up, we provision a Postgres-backed `gbrain serve --http` per workgroup (Fly Machines under the hood), and members point their agents (Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, openclaw, future Hermes, …) at one URL: `https://gbrain.io/brains/<id>/mcp`.

The shape we're enforcing on ourselves:

- **Upstream gbrain runs unchanged.** Same release artifact a DIY user would deploy. Per-tenant Fly app, per-tenant Postgres, per-tenant volume.
- **The mothership is a transport + auth layer, not a tool layer.** It maps a per-org bearer token to the right brain's upstream OAuth `client_credentials`, routes over Fly's private 6PN to `gbrain-app-<id>.flycast:18789/mcp`, and forwards `tools/list` + `tools/call` verbatim.
- **Real LLM provider keys never reach a tenant.** A separate inference proxy on the mothership swaps a per-tenant token for the real `sk-ant-...` / `sk-proj-...` and forwards. The tenant VM sees only the per-tenant token.
- **No tool ever lives on the mothership.** If we feel pulled to add one, that's the smell: the right home is upstream, where every gbrain deployment gets it.

So the "what tools does my agent see when it connects to gbrain.io" question is really **"what tools does upstream `serve --http` expose"** — which is everything in `operations.ts`.

## The dream end state

> A workgroup admin drops `https://gbrain.io/brains/47/mcp` + a bearer token into Claude Desktop / openclaw / Hermes / their custom MCP client. The agent now has the tools to **put stuff into the brain** and **pull stuff out of the brain.** That's it.

We think the spine to deliver that already exists — `operations.ts` → generated MCP. The wobble is on the *operation surface itself.*

## The wobble — read/write isn't actually the cleanest cut

Initial instinct: split operations into "writes" and "reads." Walked away from it because the seam blurs the moment you look hard at it:

- Every write triggers reads (dedup search, similarity link, contradiction check).
- `think`-style reflective writes happen *during* reads (the brain notices a pattern, writes a take).
- Cron-driven consolidation writes happen with no external read or write.

The interesting axis isn't read vs write — it's **who has the judgment**. Three modes, not two:

1. **Primitive write** — *"store this page, here's the markdown, here's the frontmatter."* Caller already decided. This is `put_page` today.
2. **Adjudicated intake** — *"here's a blob (md / pdf / jpg / arbitrary bytes), decide if it matters, store it correctly if so."* The brain does dedup-search, suggests tags from its existing taxonomy, optionally calls its own judgment LLM, and returns one of: `{stored, path, why}` / `{merged, into, why}` / `{skipped, reason}` / `{needs_input, questions}`. This is **the thing we keep wanting and don't see clearly in the current op surface.**
3. **Recall** — *"given a query / time window / tag, surface relevant memory."* Today's `search` / `list_pages` / `get_page`.

Mode 2 is "the brain has opinions about its own intake," which is what makes it a *brain* and not a doc store. The agent uploaded a blob; the brain came back with a decision plus a receipt.

## The bigger wobble — operations.ts contains multiple domains

When we sit with the operation list, what we keep noticing: not everything in there is *about memory*. Some operations are memory (the data plane); others are machinery that acts on or alongside memory (skills, signals, takes, consolidation, doctor) or admin (OAuth clients, request log).

A plausible classification (purely a naming pass — no behavior change):

| Plane | What lives here | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Data plane (memory)** | The noun. State the brain holds. | `put_page`, `get_page`, `search`, `list_pages`, `add_tag` … + `intake` (new) |
| **Control plane** | Verbs that act *on* memory. Machinery. | run a skill, fire/observe signals, takes, consolidation, doctor, dream-cycle |
| **Admin plane** | Operating the system itself. | OAuth clients, scopes, request log, magic-link mint, sign-out-everywhere |

**What this would change:**

1. **`operations.ts` gains a `domain` tag per op** — `:memory | :control | :admin`. Doesn't break anything; gives generators something to filter on.
2. **The generated MCP server keeps current behavior** (flat tool list) by default, but `serve --http` can now accept e.g. `--mcp-domains=memory,control` (default: all). Clients that want everything still get everything.
3. **The "drop URL into Claude Desktop, get the right tools" UX cleans up.** A workgroup setting their agent up for *memory* gets the memory tools, not a tool list that includes "run skill" / "fire signal" / "view request log."
4. **Hosted gbrain.io gets a real subset to expose.** Today we forward `tools/list` verbatim. With domains tagged, we can default to memory-only and let power users opt in to control-plane exposure per agent.

This is meant to be **additive and reversible.** If you disagree with the cut, the easy escape is "no, drop the tags, keep the flat surface." We lose nothing.

## On the function-as-contract pattern (which we love about gbrain)

You've already built the right thing here: `operations.ts` is THE contract, every transport (CLI, MCP stdio, MCP streamable HTTP, the SSE one in #975) is generated from it. That means:

- A new operation (e.g., `intake`) → CLI, every MCP transport, and any future transport (an HTTP API for non-MCP clients, a Hermes-shaped wire protocol) gets it for free.
- Sync vs async is a caller's decision, not a contract-shape decision. A CLI user runs `intake` synchronously and waits. An Oban worker on our mothership wraps the same function with retries + rate limits + dead-letter. A "minion" inside gbrain calls the same function from a background process. **The function is the contract; the queue is one possible caller.**

For *most* of intake (< 30s for typical blobs), the synchronous-in-MCP-tools-call shape is fine. If/when a particular workload exceeds that ceiling, an opt-in async mode (`intake(blob, async: true) -> {:job, id}` + `intake_status/1`) is a second mode, not a redesign. We'd rather not preempt that until something actually needs it.

## What we're proposing (lowest-cost version)

Three small things, each landable independently:

1. **Operation domain tags** — add `domain: :memory | :control | :admin` to each entry in `operations.ts`. PR-able as a single mechanical pass; we can draft the classification and submit it for your review/edits.
2. **`intake(blob, opts)` as a memory-domain operation** — the adjudicated path. Spec-design first (a doc + a discussion), implementation second.
3. **`serve --http --mcp-domains=...` filter** — optional flag to scope the generated MCP tool surface. Default: all (today's behavior). Off the critical path; nice-to-have once (1) lands.

## Concrete questions

1. **Does the three-plane cut match how you already think about operations.ts**, or do you see a different seam?
2. **Is `intake(blob, opts)` an operation you want upstream**, or is your model that the agent owns judgment and the brain just stores what it's told?
3. **Anything you'd want us NOT to do upstream because it's hosted-specific** — so we keep the right things in our Phoenix layer and don't pollute yours?
4. **For the operations.ts classification audit** — would you rather see a draft as a discussion in this thread, or as a PR you can review/edit/reject in place?

## Where this hooks into our side

For full context (and to make it easy to audit that we're not asking you to compromise upstream for hosted-specific reasons):

- Mothership source: https://github.com/overtonxyz/gbrain (Phoenix, Elixir, LiveView).
- The MCP proxy is `GBrainWeb.Plugs.MemoryProxy` — ~30 lines of "route, swap bearer for OAuth `client_credentials` access token, forward."
- Per-tenant runtime: same Docker image as upstream `gbrain serve --http`, no patches; just stamped with mothership-set env vars (per-tenant inference token, OAuth issuer URL).
- Inference proxy: separate from MCP; mothership-only; holds the real Anthropic / OpenAI keys. Tenant SDKs send a mothership-minted `gbi_*` token shaped like an API key.

If anything in there looks like we're working around an upstream gap that should just *be* upstream, please call it out — we'd rather close the gap than carry the workaround.

---

**Most useful thing you could say in response:** "The cut is right / the cut is wrong / here's where you're missing something." We'll adjust the proposal based on the answer rather than push through. No code in flight on this yet beyond #975.
title: RFC: Memory as a coherent operation surface — three-plane cut of operations.ts

body:
## Summary

The Google Gemini embedding recipe declares `gemini-embedding-001` but does not set `max_batch_tokens`, so startup/runtime warnings can complain that the embedding batch cap is missing.

## Evidence

Google's Gemini Embedding model page documents the `gemini-embedding-001` input token limit as 2,048 tokens:

https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models/gemini-embedding-001

## Expected

The recipe should encode the documented cap so the gateway can pre-split batches and avoid the missing-`max_batch_tokens` warning.

## Local patch tested

I added:

```ts
max_batch_tokens: 2048,
```

to `src/core/ai/recipes/google.ts` under the embedding touchpoint.

Verification:

```bash
bun test test/recipes-contract.test.ts
# pass
```
title: Add max_batch_tokens for gemini-embedding-001 recipe

body:
## Summary

`gbrain sync --source <id>` can return `up_to_date` without refreshing `sources.last_sync_at`. That makes `gbrain doctor` continue warning that the source is stale even immediately after a successful no-op sync.

## Repro

1. Have a configured source whose repo HEAD matches `sync.last_commit`.
2. Set `sources.last_sync_at` to an old timestamp.
3. Run:
   ```bash
   gbrain sync --source default --no-embed --no-extract
   gbrain doctor --json
   ```

## Observed

`gbrain sync` reports up-to-date / no content changes, but `gbrain doctor` still reports:

```text
sync_freshness: Source 'default' last synced <N>h ago. Run `gbrain sync --source <id>` to refresh
```

## Expected

A successful source sync attempt should refresh `sources.last_sync_at` even if there are no content changes, so the doctor freshness check reflects that the source was checked.

## Local patch tested

I added a small helper in `src/commands/sync.ts` to update `sources.last_sync_at = now()` when `performSync` returns `status: 'up_to_date'` for a source-backed sync, plus a regression test in `test/sync.test.ts`.

Verification:

```bash
bun test test/sync.test.ts
# pass

gbrain sync --source default --no-embed --no-extract
gbrain doctor --json
# healthy / 100 locally
```
title: Refresh source last_sync_at on up-to-date sync

body:
## Summary
When running \`gbrain sync\` in a repo with a long basename, the helpful error message suggests an ID that is then rejected by \`gbrain sources add\` for being too long.

## Environment
- gbrain 0.33.1.0
- macOS 26.5

## Repro
```bash
$ cd /Users/me/some-repo-with-a-longer-name
$ gbrain sync --no-pull
Source "gstack-code-some-repo-with-a-longer-name-77a69214-c23834" not found.
Available sources: run `gbrain sources list` to see registered sources,
or `gbrain sources add gstack-code-some-repo-with-a-longer-name-77a69214-c23834` to create it.

$ gbrain sources add gstack-code-some-repo-with-a-longer-name-77a69214-c23834 --path . --federated
Invalid source id "gstack-code-some-repo-with-a-longer-name-77a69214-c23834".
Must be 1-32 lowercase alnum chars with optional interior hyphens.
```

The suggested 43-char ID violates the 32-char limit. Same for any repo name long enough.

## Impact
- Users follow the verbatim hint, get rejected, have to guess the right truncation. The error doesn't say "try this 32-char version".
- For some shorter repos, the same auto-derivation truncates AGGRESSIVELY and uses a 4-char hash prefix like `vice` (e.g. `gstack-code-vice-64c1a6f8-5d464a` for a repo named `core-service`). This loses the repo name entirely — searching `gbrain sources list` for "core-service" returns nothing.

## Suggested fix
Either:
1. Auto-derive an ID that fits 32 chars deterministically (e.g., `gstack-code-<truncated-name>-<6-char-hash>`, capped at 32). Use this same ID in both the "not found" error and the auto-creation path.
2. Or accept the long ID. The 32-char limit looks arbitrary — bump it to 64.

Either way, the two surfaces (sync's hint + sources add's validator) should agree on what's a valid source ID.
title: gbrain sync's 'sources add <id>' hint suggests IDs that exceed the 32-char limit

body:
## Summary
`connection-manager.ts::deriveDirectUrl()` maps a Supavisor pooler URL to the direct DB hostname `db.<ref>.supabase.co`. On Supabase Free-tier projects (and possibly some Pro projects), that hostname **only has an IPv6 (AAAA) record — no IPv4 (A) record**. On networks without IPv6 routing (a large fraction of consumer ISPs + many corporate networks), Bun's DNS resolver returns `getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND` with an undefined hostname in the error message, and every DDL operation that goes through the direct pool fails.

This blocks: `gbrain init --migrate-only`, schema migrations (which spawn `gbrain init --migrate-only` via execSync in v0.29.1's orchestrator), `apply-migrations`, and any other code path that calls `connectionManager.ddl()`.

## Environment
- gbrain 0.33.1.0
- macOS 26.5 (Darwin 25.5.0, arm64)
- Bun 1.3.11
- Engine: Postgres on Supabase Free tier (region sa-east-1)
- Network: IPv4-only (typical consumer ISP)

## Repro
```bash
# Configured brain points at the Session Pooler:
$ cat ~/.gbrain/config.json
{"engine":"postgres","database_url":"postgresql://postgres.<ref>:***@aws-1-sa-east-1.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres"}

# This fails:
$ gbrain init --migrate-only
getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND

# Drill-down via stack trace shows the failure is in initDirectPool:
#   at sql (.../postgres/src/index.js:112:15)
#   at initDirectPool (src/core/connection-manager.ts:350:13)

# The derived direct URL points at a host with no IPv4:
$ host db.<ref>.supabase.co
db.<ref>.supabase.co has IPv6 address 2600:1f1e:...

$ nslookup db.<ref>.supabase.co
*** Can't find db.<ref>.supabase.co: No answer    # no A record

# Workaround: pin GBRAIN_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL to the pooler URL itself:
$ export GBRAIN_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL="$GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL"
$ gbrain init --migrate-only
# → Schema up to date (engine: postgres). [success]
```

## Impact
- Hard-blocks first-time setup on macOS/Linux IPv4-only networks for users following the documented Supabase auto-provision path.
- Hard-blocks every subsequent upgrade that requires `apply-migrations` (e.g., 0.28.x → 0.33.x).
- Silently hidden because the error message has no hostname — users see `getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND` with no clue what host. Took ~30 minutes of code-walking through `connection-manager.ts` + a manual DNS check to find the IPv6-only host.
- Compounds with the fact that the postinstall hook tries to apply migrations and reports `Migration vX.Y.Z reported status=failed` without surfacing the underlying ENOTFOUND.

## Suggested fixes

In order of preference:

1. **Try IPv4 first, fall back to IPv6 (or vice versa) in `deriveDirectUrl`.** When the direct hostname has no A record, fall back to the pooler URL with a debug-log note. Don't fail closed on a hostname the user didn't even configure.

2. **Surface the actual hostname in the error.** Catch the ENOTFOUND in `initDirectPool` and re-throw with `Error(\`getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND ${this.hostOnly(this._directUrl)} (derived direct host; original pooler URL is reachable)\`)`. The current error message strips the host, which makes the bug nearly invisible.

3. **Make the direct-pool optional.** If `_directUrl` doesn't resolve or doesn't accept connections, fall back to running DDL through the read pool with a startup-log note ("running in single-pool mode — DDL via pooler"). The `// single (no-direct-url)` branch on line 420 already handles this case when `_directUrl` is null — extend it to handle "resolution failed" too.

4. **Document the `GBRAIN_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL` override prominently.** It's mentioned in connection-manager.ts comments but not surfaced in the error message, the setup docs, or the doctor output.

## Detection in doctor
Worth adding to `gbrain doctor`: a `direct_url_dns` check that calls `dns.resolve4(host)` on the derived `_directUrl` and warns when only AAAA records exist. One-line fix surface that prevents the silent hours-long debug.
title: deriveDirectUrl produces an IPv6-only hostname, breaking DDL on IPv4-only networks (getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND)

body:
## Summary
The `mcp__gbrain__query` tool exposes `near_symbol`, `symbol_kind`, `lang`, and `walk_depth` parameters whose descriptions imply they operate over a parsed-code index ("Anchor retrieval at this qualified symbol name", "Filter to chunks where content_chunks.symbol_type matches"). In v0.28.12 they operate over transcript prose, not actual code. Symbol lookup returns Claude Code conversations that *mention* the symbol, not the file/line where it's defined.

The CLI subcommands referenced in the v0.19.0 / v0.20.0 release notes for "Cathedral II" code indexing — `gbrain code-def`, `gbrain code-refs`, `gbrain code-callers`, `gbrain code-callees` — also don't appear in `gbrain --help` output despite being listed in the help under "CODE INDEXING".

## Environment
- gbrain 0.28.12
- macOS 26.5

## Repro
Code with a function `seedExampleSystemRoles` defined at `server/db/seed/roles.ts:59`. Look it up via each path:

```bash
# Ground truth (works, ~0.14s)
$ grep -rn "function seedExampleSystemRoles\|export.*seedExampleSystemRoles" server/ shared/
server/db/seed/roles.ts:59:export async function seedExampleSystemRoles(...)
server/db/seed/index.ts:214:    seedExampleSystemRoles,

# gbrain query with near_symbol (3s wall, wrong answer)
$ gbrain call query '{"query":"seedExampleSystemRoles","near_symbol":"seedExampleSystemRoles"}'
# → top hit: transcripts/claude-code/...  (score: 0.304)
#   This is a conversation that mentions the symbol, not the definition.

# gbrain query with symbol_kind: function (returns [])
$ gbrain call query '{"query":"PERMISSION_META source of truth","symbol_kind":"function"}'
# → []
```

## Impact
- `code-def` / `code-refs` / `code-callers` / `code-callees` are advertised at the top of `gbrain --help` as headline v0.20.0 features. They appear to not exist as CLI subcommands in v0.28.12.
- The `near_symbol` / `symbol_kind` params on `query` give the false impression of code-aware retrieval. They silently fall back to text matching against the transcript corpus.
- For any "where is X defined / who calls X" question, Grep beats gbrain by 10-100× and is 100% accurate. There's no signal that gbrain should be tried first.

## Expected
Either:
1. Ship the documented code index — parse code files into `content_chunks` with `symbol_type` populated, build `code_edges`, expose `code-def` / `code-refs` / `code-callers` subcommands as the help text implies, and have `near_symbol` actually anchor retrieval at the symbol's chunk.
2. OR: remove the symbol-flavored params from `query` and remove "CODE INDEXING (v0.19.0 / v0.20.0 Cathedral II)" from the `--help` output. False advertising erodes trust more than the missing feature does.

## Actual
Symbol-related machinery exists as parameter surface only; underneath, it's keyword-search over the transcript corpus.
title: near_symbol / symbol_kind / lang parameters operate on transcript prose, not parsed code — no real code index in v0.28.12

body:
## Summary
`gbrain doctor --fast` prints `Health score: 90/100. All checks OK (some warnings).` while simultaneously emitting `[WARN] connection: Skipping DB checks (--fast mode...)`. The aggregate score is computed across checks that didn't run. Looks reassuring while hiding the fact that the most important check (DB reachability) was skipped.

## Environment
- gbrain 0.28.12
- macOS 26.5

## Repro
```
$ gbrain doctor --fast
[WARN] resolver_health: Could not find skills directory
[WARN] connection: Skipping DB checks (--fast mode; remove --fast for full)
[OK]   pgvector: Extension installed
[OK]   schema_version: Version 39 (latest: 39)
...
Health score: 90/100. All checks OK (some warnings).
```

The user sees 90/100 and assumes the brain is healthy. The brain might be unreachable.

## Impact
- Agents and humans use `gbrain doctor --fast` as a quick health probe (e.g., in shell-init checks). A misleading 90 invites false positives.
- Compounds with the broader pattern of silent-degrade in gbrain (`--source` ignored, `query` returns [], `auto_links` skipped) — every "looks OK on the surface" footgun erodes trust.

## Expected
Either:
1. Don't compute a score in `--fast` mode (just print check statuses)
2. Compute score from only the checks that actually ran, AND label it differently (e.g., `Health score (fast mode, no DB): 8/9 checks OK`)
3. Cap score at the count of checks that ran (5/10 ran, max possible is 50/100)

## Actual
Score includes skipped-check slots as if they passed.

## One-liner suggested fix
```diff
- const score = (ok / total) * 100;
+ const ran = ok + warn + err;
+ const score = ran === 0 ? 0 : Math.round((ok / ran) * 100);
+ const label = skipped > 0 ? `Health score (partial, ${skipped} skipped)` : 'Health score';
```
title: gbrain doctor --fast reports 'Health score: 90/100' while skipping DB connection check

body:
## Summary
`mcp__gbrain__search` and `mcp__gbrain__query` with `--limit 20` and a common term (e.g., "permission", "user") produce 60-90KB responses that exceed Claude Code's MCP tool output cap. The response gets persisted to disk and the agent has to read it back, doubling the round-trip cost. Effectively forces all agent MCP queries to `limit ≤ 5`.

## Environment
- gbrain 0.28.12
- macOS 26.5
- Engine: Postgres on Supabase
- Transport: stdio MCP

## Repro
```jsonc
{"name":"search","arguments":{"query":"user","limit":20}}
// → response is 58.8KB, exceeds MCP tool output cap, persists to disk for read-back

{"name":"query","arguments":{"query":"permission","limit":20}}
// → response is 90KB, same outcome
```

## Impact
- Agents must pre-emptively pass `limit ≤ 5` for any common-term search or pay the read-back cost.
- Useful queries that legitimately need more candidates (e.g., "find all docs about permissions, I'll re-rank in my context") become expensive.
- Discoverability suffers: an agent that wants to scan 20 candidates and pick the most relevant has to do 4× sequential queries with offset.

## Suggested
Server-side options:
1. **`detail` parameter respected**: `detail: "low"` should return slugs + scores + ≤1 line of preview each, sized for agent consumption. Currently `detail: medium` is the practical max but already too large at limit=20.
2. **Chunk-preview truncation**: cap each result's snippet to ≤200 chars when `limit > 5`.
3. **Pagination headers**: when result set is large, return first N + a continuation token instead of the full payload.

The MCP transport has a hard cap; the tool should fit inside it for reasonable parameter ranges, not force callers to discover the limit empirically.

## Workaround
Document `limit ≤ 5` as the recommended ceiling for MCP `search` / `query` calls in the README and the tool descriptions.
title: MCP search/query responses exceed token cap on common-term queries (--limit 20 → 90KB persisted to disk)
LongMemEval harness: missing configureGateway() prevents hybrid search

body:
## Summary

`gbrain init --pglite --fresh` and every subsequent DB-touching command (`list`, `import`, `query`) abort during PGLite WASM runtime init on Windows 11. The error text mentions the macOS 26.3 WASM bug (#223) but the symptom reproduces on Windows under Bun 1.3.13, so this is either a separate root cause or a broader runtime issue.

## Environment

- gbrain: **0.33.1.0** (fresh `gbrain upgrade` + `bun install --ignore-scripts && bun link` from `~/Development/gbrain`)
- Bun: **1.3.13** (bf2e2cec)
- OS: **Microsoft Windows 11 Enterprise**, Build 26200 (10.0.26200)
- Engine: pglite (default, no Supabase config)
- Install method detected by `gbrain upgrade`: `bun-link`

## Reproduction

```
$ gbrain init --pglite --fresh
Setting up local brain with PGLite (no server needed)...
PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime.
  This is most commonly the macOS 26.3 WASM bug: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/223
  Run `gbrain doctor` for a full diagnosis.
  Original error: Aborted(). Build with -sASSERTIONS for more info.
```

Same abort on `gbrain list`, `gbrain doctor` (DB layer), and any command that opens the engine. `gbrain doctor` filesystem checks pass; skill conformance 42/42; DB section reports:

```
[WARN] connection: Could not connect to configured DB (URL from config-file-path); filesystem checks only
Health score: 90/100. All checks OK (some warnings).
```

Pre-upgrade build (0.32.x) had identical behaviour, so this is not a regression introduced by 0.33.1.0 — just confirming it survived the upgrade.

## Notes

- The postinstall step in `gbrain upgrade` also fails on Windows with a bash redirect parse error (`expected a command or assignment but got: "Redirect"` on `1>&2`). Worked around with `bun install --ignore-scripts && bun link`. Likely a separate small issue worth its own report.
- Build with `-sASSERTIONS` in the PGLite WASM module would help pin the actual abort cause.
- Happy to provide more diagnostics if useful.
title: PGLite WASM runtime aborts on init (Windows 11 + Bun 1.3.13, gbrain 0.33.1.0)

body:
## TL;DR

There is already a working downstream reference implementation of this package in Eva Brain at `plugins/gbrain-codex`. The upstream task is not to invent a Codex Desktop adapter from scratch; it is to upstream/adapt that thin package so stock GBrain can be installed into Codex Desktop with one repeatable repo-owned path instead of hand-editing MCP JSON.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Codex["Codex Desktop"] --> Plugin["gbrain-codex plugin package"]
  Plugin --> Launcher["launch-gbrain-serve.mjs"]
  Launcher --> CLI["local gbrain serve"]
  CLI --> Core["canonical GBrain MCP tools + database"]
```

## Why This Matters

Codex Desktop users should not need to manually wire MCP config to use a local GBrain. A repo-owned plugin package gives maintainers and agents a stable install target:

- Codex gets the same MCP tools every other stdio host gets.
- The launcher handles GUI PATH issues and local checkout binaries.
- The plugin can ship a rehearsal smoke proving Codex can start local `gbrain serve`.
- The package stays a wrapper around GBrain core, not a forked runtime.

## Existing Reference Implementation

Downstream reference package:

```text
plugins/gbrain-codex/
├── .codex-plugin/plugin.json
├── .mcp.json
├── README.md
├── package.json
├── assets/gbrain-codex.svg
└── scripts/
    ├── launch-gbrain-serve.mjs
    └── rehearsal.mjs
```

The current Eva implementation also has a repo installer script that links this package plus the current skill tree into Codex Desktop. For upstream, the first PR should keep the package thin and GBrain-branded.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Codex plugin launches local `gbrain serve` over stdio MCP.
- No second GBrain runtime is embedded in the plugin.
- No host-specific auth claims are made by the package.
- Rehearsal script can create a temp `GBRAIN_HOME`, initialize PGLite, connect to MCP, and list/exercise tools.
- README explains install, restart, troubleshooting, and safety boundary.
- Metadata is upstream-branded as GBrain, not Eva Brain.

## Agent Implementation Notes

Start by adapting the existing Eva package, not by generating a new architecture. Replace downstream branding/repo URLs with upstream GBrain equivalents. Keep this packaging-only; provider auth/OAuth and host-native extraction belong in separate adapter/auth issues.
title: feat: upstream the existing GBrain Codex Desktop plugin package

body:
## TL;DR

`gbrain embed --stale --source <id>` should only count, list, read, and write stale chunks for that source. Today the stale fast path accepts `--source` at the CLI level but the SQL stale-count/list queries are global, so multi-source brains can do unnecessary work and same-slug pages can still be routed through the wrong source unless every downstream call is carefully re-scoped.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["multi-source brain"] --> B["default: shared-slug"]
  A --> C["openclaw-support-kb: shared-slug"]
  D["gbrain embed --stale --source openclaw-support-kb"] --> E["count/list stale chunks"]
  E -->|"current: global"| F["sees stale work outside requested source"]
  E -->|"desired: source-scoped"| G["only KB stale chunks"]
  G --> H["getChunks/upsertChunks with source_id=openclaw-support-kb"]
```

## Why This Matters

This is both correctness and cost control:

- **Correctness:** duplicate slugs are valid across sources. Any stale-embed path must treat `(source_id, slug)` as the page identity.
- **Cost:** recurring jobs often want to refresh one source, such as a support KB, without scanning or embedding unrelated user docs.
- **Operator trust:** if a user asks for `--source openclaw-support-kb`, the command should not count or process stale chunks from every source.

## Related Work

This complements existing source-isolation/source-sync issues such as #891 and egress work such as #775. It is narrower than a full sync rewrite: this issue is specifically the stale embedding path.

## Root Cause

`runEmbed()` parses `--source`, but the stale path calls:

```ts
engine.countStaleChunks()
engine.listStaleChunks()
```

without passing a source filter. The engine methods also do not accept source-scoped options yet.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `BrainEngine.countStaleChunks(opts?: { sourceId?: string })` supports optional source scoping.
- `BrainEngine.listStaleChunks(opts?: { sourceId?: string })` supports optional source scoping.
- Postgres and PGLite implementations filter by `pages.source_id` when `sourceId` is provided.
- Image chunks are not treated as stale text embeddings.
- `embed --stale --source <id>` passes source scope into stale count/list queries.
- Stale rows are grouped by `(source_id, slug)` before `getChunks`/`upsertChunks`.
- Tests prove duplicate slugs do not fall back to `default` and source-scoped stale queries are actually scoped.

## Agent Implementation Notes

Recommended small patch shape:

- update the `BrainEngine` interface method signatures
- update `PostgresEngine.countStaleChunks/listStaleChunks`
- update `PGLiteEngine.countStaleChunks/listStaleChunks`
- update `src/commands/embed.ts` so `embedAllStale()` receives and forwards `sourceId`
- add one focused test file that mocks `embedBatch` and asserts `getChunks`/`upsertChunks` receive the stale row's `source_id`

Do not broaden this PR into all source-isolation fixes. Keep it to stale embedding behavior.
title: bug: embed --stale --source should scope stale chunk queries by source_id

body:
## TL;DR

Migration `v0.32.2` can fail before doing any useful work because Phase B dirty-checks every registered source before it knows whether any legacy fact rows actually need fenced writes. A dirty, unrelated source can block a no-op migration or a migration whose writes touch only a different source.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["apply migration v0.32.2"] --> B["Phase B: fence legacy facts"]
  B --> C["Current: load all sources"]
  C --> D["dirty-check every local_path"]
  D --> E["fail if any source is dirty"]
  E --> F["even when legacy facts = 0\nor dirty source has no writes"]

  B --> G["Safer order"]
  G --> H["query legacy facts first"]
  H --> I{"any fenceable groups?"}
  I -->|"no"| J["complete no-op"]
  I -->|"yes"| K["dirty-check only sources that will be written"]
  K --> L["write fenced facts safely"]
```

## Why This Matters

This is a migration reliability issue, not a feature request. A user can have a healthy database and no legacy facts to migrate, but the migration still reports failed because an unrelated registered source has uncommitted work. That leaves the install in a confusing partial/wedged state and makes doctor output harder to interpret.

The dirty-tree refusal is the right safety behavior before writing markdown. The bug is scope and timing: refusal should apply only when the migration will actually write into that source.

## Related Issue

This complements #921. #921 covers the poor CLI error surface when v0.32.2 fails. This issue covers one concrete false-failure path that should not fail in the first place.

## Root Cause

Phase B currently does this order:

1. load all sources
2. dirty-check every `local_path`
3. query legacy facts
4. group facts into fenced writes

That order means unrelated source state can block a no-op migration.

## Acceptance Criteria

- If no legacy facts need fencing, Phase B completes without dirty-checking unrelated source directories.
- If legacy facts exist but none are fenceable, Phase B completes without refusing dirty unrelated source directories.
- If fenceable facts target source A, Phase B dirty-checks source A before writing.
- If source B is dirty but has no fenceable writes in this migration, source B does not block source A.
- Unexpected git probe errors fail closed; only known "not a git repo" / `git` unavailable cases are treated as not dirty.
- Tests cover no-op, unfenceable-only, unrelated dirty source, and targeted dirty source refusal.

## Agent Implementation Notes

Keep the implementation inside `src/commands/migrations/v0_32_2.ts`:

- query legacy rows before loading/dirty-checking sources
- early-return complete for `legacy.length === 0`
- build `(source_id, entity_slug)` groups before deciding which source IDs need dirty checks
- dirty-check only grouped source IDs with pending writes
- keep the existing refusal behavior for targeted dirty sources

Do not weaken the dirty-tree guard. Narrow it to the sources that would be mutated.
title: bug: v0.32.2 facts migration dirty-checks unrelated sources before no-op detection

body:
## Bug

When migration `v0.32.2` ("facts join the system-of-record") fails any of its phases, the CLI output is:

```
=== Applying migration v0.32.2: Facts join the system-of-record — your hot memory now lives in markdown, indexed by the DB ===

=== v0.32.2 — facts join the system-of-record invariant ===

Migration v0.32.2 reported status=failed.
```

That's it. No mention of which phase failed (A schema / B fence_facts / C verify), no detail string, no recovery hint. The actual error detail is collected by the orchestrator into `OrchestratorResult.phases[*].detail` (see `src/commands/migrations/v0_32_2.ts:154-198` for the most operator-relevant case: "source X has uncommitted changes in <path>. Commit or stash, then re-run.") — but this never reaches the user.

Worse: after 3 consecutive partials with no completion, doctor flags the migration as WEDGED and recommends `gbrain apply-migrations --force-retry 0.32.2`. But force-retry plus another `apply-migrations --yes` reproduces the exact same silent failure, putting the user in a debugging loop with no signal.

## Repro

```bash
# In any git repo registered as a gbrain code source
cd /path/to/repo
git status --porcelain  # has ANY untracked file or modification
gbrain apply-migrations --yes
# Output: "Migration v0.32.2 reported status=failed." — no detail
```

Root cause discoverable only by reading `src/commands/migrations/v0_32_2.ts` directly.

## Suggested fix

In `apply-migrations.ts`'s runner, after calling each migration's orchestrator, print the `phases` array's failure detail before writing to the ledger:

```ts
if (result.status === 'failed' || result.status === 'partial') {
  for (const phase of result.phases) {
    if (phase.status === 'failed') {
      console.error(`  Phase ${phase.name}: FAILED`);
      console.error(`    ${phase.detail ?? '(no detail)'}`);
    }
  }
}
```

Same `--yes` behavior, just surfaces what the orchestrator already collected. No new schema, no new fields — just print what's already in the in-memory `OrchestratorResult`.

Bonus: if a phase's detail string contains an actionable recovery command (e.g. "Commit or stash, then re-run"), highlight it as a fix hint with a leading `→`.

## Env

- macOS 26.3
- gbrain 0.33.0 (also reproed on 0.27.0)
- The specific failure that prompted this report: untracked `.gbrain-source` in the code source's local_path triggered Phase B's `isLocalPathDirty()` check, but the user only saw "status=failed". (Filed as a separate issue.)
title: Migration v0.32.2 silently reports status=failed at the CLI surface — no actionable error detail

body:
## Bug

When `/sync-gbrain` (or `gstack-gbrain-sync.ts`) registers a code source against a git repo, it writes the kubectl-style worktree pin file `.gbrain-source` into the repo root. The file is per-machine (contains a locally-generated source ID like `gstack-code-<hash>-<id>`) and should never be in version control.

But gbrain doesn't add `.gbrain-source` to `.gitignore` when it drops the pin. So:

- `git status` shows it as untracked forever
- It silently fails migration v0.32.2's dirty-tree refusal check (Phase B in `src/commands/migrations/v0_32_2.ts:151`), because that check fires on ANY non-empty `git status --porcelain` output — including untracked files
- New users who run `/sync-gbrain` then `gbrain upgrade` get a wedged migration with no obvious root cause

## Repro

```bash
cd /path/to/some/git/repo
gbrain init --pglite  # or have an existing brain
# Some path that triggers /sync-gbrain to register this repo as a code source
git status --porcelain
# Output: ?? .gbrain-source
```

Then any subsequent migration with a dirty-tree refusal phase will fail.

## Suggested fix

When `sources add` (or wherever the `.gbrain-source` pin gets written) runs, also:

1. Check whether the repo has a `.gitignore`
2. If `.gbrain-source` isn't already in it, append `.gbrain-source` (one line)
3. Surface the change in stdout so the user knows their `.gitignore` was touched (analog of how `gbrain skillpack install` reports managed-block changes)

Idempotency: if `.gbrain-source` is already gitignored, no-op. If `.gitignore` doesn't exist, create it with just that one line. Atomic write via `.tmp` + rename per the existing fence-write pattern in `facts-fence.ts`.

Alternative: don't modify the user's `.gitignore` at all, but ship a `.git/info/exclude`-style worktree-local ignore (no commit needed, fully invisible). Either is fine.

## Env

- macOS 26.3 (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64)
- Bun 1.3.x
- gbrain 0.27.0 → 0.33.0 (upgraded today; behavior was identical on both)
- Discovered while debugging issue #223 fallout. After rebuilding `brain.pglite` cleanly per intenseandnoble's 2026-05-02 comment, migration v0.32.2 still failed silently 3+ times in a row before the dirty-tree check became obvious.
title: /sync-gbrain should add .gbrain-source to .gitignore when registering a code source

body:
## Bug Description

The `native-openai` recipe implementation in `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` does **not** read `provider_base_urls[\"openai\"]` when instantiating models for expansion, embedding, or chat. This causes `createOpenAI()` to always use the default `api.openai.com` endpoint, ignoring any custom base URL configured by the user.

## Affected Code

Three locations in `src/core/ai/gateway.ts`:

**Line ~677 (embedding):**
```typescript
const client = createOpenAI({ apiKey });
// Missing: baseURL: cfg.base_urls?.["openai"]
```

**Line ~1129 (expansion):**
```typescript
return createOpenAI({ apiKey }).languageModel(modelId);
// Missing: baseURL: cfg.base_urls?.["openai"]
```

**Line ~1332 (chat):**
```typescript
return createOpenAI({ apiKey }).languageModel(modelId);
// Missing: baseURL: cfg.base_urls?.["openai"]
```

In contrast, the `openai-compatible` implementation correctly reads the base URL via `applyOpenAICompatConfig(recipe, cfg)`.

## Impact

Users who want to use OpenAI-compatible endpoints (e.g., DashScope coding endpoint, Azure OpenAI, LiteLLM) **with the `openai:` model prefix** cannot do so. The custom base URL is silently ignored, and requests go to `api.openai.com`, causing timeouts or auth failures.

## Reproduction Steps

1. Configure `provider_base_urls: { openai: "https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1" }`
2. Set `expansion_model: "openai:qwen3.6-plus"` and `OPENAI_API_KEY`
3. Run `gbrain query "test"`

**Expected:** Request goes to `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`
**Actual:** Request goes to `api.openai.com` → timeout (401 or 403 if key is rejected)

## Suggested Fix

Add `baseURL: cfg.base_urls?.["openai"]` to all three `createOpenAI()` calls. This makes `native-openai` consistent with how `openai-compatible` handles custom endpoints, while preserving the default `api.openai.com` when no override is set.

## Environment

- gbrain version: 0.32.0
- OS: macOS 26.2
- Engine: PostgreSQL + pgvector

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: native-openai recipe ignores provider_base_urls for expansion/embedding/chat

body:
## Summary

The admin dashboard's "Live Activity" panel cannot establish its SSE connection when GBrain is served behind a reverse proxy on the same hostname. The `EventSource` is constructed without `withCredentials: true`, so the browser refuses to send the `gbrain_admin` session cookie on the SSE request, and `/admin/events` returns 401 (followed by `ERR_INCOMPLETE_CHUNKED_ENCODING` in the browser console).

## Environment

- GBrain v0.31.10 (HTTP MCP mode)
- Running in Docker (custom Dockerfile, Bun runtime)
- Reverse proxy: nginx-proxy-manager → `gbrain:3131`
- Public hostname: `http://gbrain.sentinel` (tailnet-internal, HTTP only — Issuer remains the default `http://localhost:3131`)
- Browser: Chrome 148 on macOS

## Reproduction

1. Deploy GBrain in HTTP mode behind any reverse proxy at `http://<hostname>/admin/`
2. Log in successfully at `/admin/` with the bootstrap admin token
3. Dashboard loads; counters populate via `/admin/api/stats` and `/admin/api/health-indicators` (cookie sent, 200 / 304 returned)
4. Live Activity panel shows "● connecting..." indefinitely

## Server-side observations

NPM access logs:

```
GET /admin/api/stats              200 / 304   cookie: gbrain_admin=...
GET /admin/api/health-indicators  200 / 304   cookie: gbrain_admin=...
GET /admin/events                 401          (no cookie header on request)
```

Browser devtools console:

```
admin/events:1  Failed to load resource: net::ERR_INCOMPLETE_CHUNKED_ENCODING
```

## Root cause

In the admin SPA bundle (`admin/dist/assets/index-*.js`):

```js
new EventSource("/admin/events")
```

Per the [HTML Living Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html#eventsource), `EventSource` defaults to `withCredentials = false`, which sets `credentials = "same-origin"` on the underlying fetch. This blocks cookies on requests going through reverse-proxy chains, even on the same host as observed by the browser — most reproducibly when:

- The cookie has `Secure` flag but the request hits an HTTP scheme
- The cookie has `SameSite: Strict` (which `adminCookie()` sets)

`/admin/login` server-side sets the cookie via `res.cookie('gbrain_admin', sessionId, adminCookie(req, 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000))` with `sameSite: 'strict'`. Regular `fetch()` calls from the SPA correctly inherit credentials via the browser's same-origin rules. `EventSource` does not, by default.

## Suggested fix

Change the EventSource constructor in the admin SPA from:

```js
new EventSource("/admin/events")
```

to:

```js
new EventSource("/admin/events", { withCredentials: true })
```

Purely client-side; the server already correctly authenticates cookies on the receiving side via `requireAdmin` middleware. No server change needed.

## Workarounds attempted

- Adding `proxy_buffering off`, `chunked_transfer_encoding off`, `proxy_read_timeout 24h`, `proxy_set_header Connection ""` to the nginx location block — addresses the `ERR_INCOMPLETE_CHUNKED_ENCODING` but the underlying 401 remains because the cookie never reaches the server
- Modifying cookie `sameSite` or `secure` from `'strict'` to `'lax'` or `false` would require patching server source
- Patching the SPA bundle directly works but gets clobbered on every container rebuild

## References

- Cookie config: `src/commands/serve-http.ts` `adminCookie()` function
- Auth middleware: `src/commands/serve-http.ts` `requireAdmin()`
- SSE handler: `src/commands/serve-http.ts` `app.get('/admin/events', requireAdmin, ...)`
- SPA source: `admin/src/.../*.tsx` — wherever the dashboard's Live Activity hook is implemented
- Spec: https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html#dom-eventsource
title: Admin dashboard "Live Activity" panel stuck on "connecting…" when serving via reverse proxy — SSE EventSource missing withCredentials
Incremental extract fails to extract timeline and links for new pages
Ranking results inverted - most relevant content gets lowest score

body:
## Bug

In gbrain v0.18.x, running `gbrain sync` without `OPENAI_API_KEY` set produced per-chunk warnings (`Error embedding ...: OpenAI embedding requires OPENAI_API_KEY`) but the sync continued, importing pages and creating chunks without embeddings. This left the brain in a usable FTS-only state — `gbrain search` worked on literal terms even without semantic vectors.

In v0.32.0 this graceful fallback is gone. Without `OPENAI_API_KEY`, sync hard-errors and imports zero pages.

## Repro

```bash
unset OPENAI_API_KEY
gbrain sync --source-id <some-source> --full
```

Expected (v0.18 behavior):
```
[import.files] N/N (100%) imported=N skipped=0 errors=0
N pages imported, N chunks created
Embedded 0 chunks (no API key)
```

Actual (v0.32):
```
[import.files] N/N (100%) imported=0 skipped=N errors=N
0 pages imported, N pages skipped (0 unchanged, N errors)
```

## Why this matters

Some content shouldn't ship to OpenAI's embedding API for compliance reasons (employee compensation data, regulated PII, sensitive financial intel). v0.18 let users index those pages locally with FTS-only search as the tradeoff. v0.32 forces the binary choice: install with OpenAI processing, or don't index at all.

A `--no-embed` flag on `sync` (or restoring the soft-fail behavior) would re-enable the FTS-only use case without forcing users back to v0.18.

## Workaround

None currently. Either set `OPENAI_API_KEY` and accept that chunks transit OpenAI, or use `gbrain put` to manually insert pages one at a time (no sync automation).

## Environment

- gbrain 0.32.0 (commit pinned at v0.32 tag)
- macOS 26.3, PGLite engine
- Node/Bun runtime via gstack installer
title: v0.32 regression: gbrain sync hard-errors without OPENAI_API_KEY (was soft-fail in v0.18)

body:
Hi Garry,

Long-time fan of gbrain and the OpenClaw / Hermes work. I noticed your agents sometimes hit context exhaustion on multi-cluster refactors — compaction kicks in, the agent forgets a convention from earlier in the session, and you end up starting over. A small, regeneratable structural pack might help reduce that.

Here's what I have in a local branch: `docs/agent-onboarding/` — 16 dense markdown files, ~5 KB total. The idea is that an agent loads it at the start of a structural task and trades 2 KB of read time for ~30 KB of orientation savings (skip the Glob + Read + Grep dance).

Contents (snapshot, all auto-derivable from `src/` at any commit):

- `module-index.md` — 10 architectural clusters with anchor symbols + responsibility + decision tree
- `cluster-A-engine.md` through `cluster-J-skillpack.md` — one dense page per cluster
- `schema-erd.md` — 29 tables with read/write hotness
- `api-surface.md` — 47 MCP ops with `file:line` citations + scope + localOnly
- `trust-boundary.md` — `OperationContext.remote` semantics + 4 fail-closed sites (codified from v0.26.9 hardening)
- `hub-functions.md` — top 20 symbols by inbound caller count
- `naming-conventions.md` — camelCase / PascalCase / snake_case rules
- `SKILL.md` — routing stub for OpenClaw/Hermes (when to load which doc)
- `README.md` — entry point with task-to-doc routing table

This is different from what CLAUDE.md does — CLAUDE.md is for an agent editing code (your style, ship rules, conventions). This pack is for an agent orienting before editing. Complementary surface, not a replacement.

On provenance: I generated this via ArgosBrain's `/argos-corporate-delivery` skill against commit `410c697`. The structural data (counts, anchors, file:line) is verifiable from `src/`. The narrative was composed by Claude — citations are exact, prose may need your eyes.

I'd love to submit this as a PR if you're interested. Two options I'd defer to you on:

1. Just the auto-derivable files first (`module-index.md`, `schema-erd.md`, `api-surface.md`, `hub-functions.md`, `naming-conventions.md`, `SKILL.md`, `README.md` — 7 files, low-prose). If those land, I can iterate on the cluster docs separately.

2. The full pack as `docs/agent-onboarding/` (16 files, ~5 KB total).

Not married to either. Happy to close this if it doesn't fit your direction for the project — you know gbrain way better than a single PR can capture.

A few questions before I open a PR:

- Would you want this regenerated by CI on a schedule (e.g. weekly via `argosbrain delivery render`)? Or hand-curated?
- Is the `SKILL.md` routing stub useful, or do you handle agent orientation differently in OpenClaw/Hermes?
- Should the structural data live elsewhere — e.g. `gbrain --tools-json` extended to emit cluster + anchor info as JSON?

Thanks for the work on gbrain. Happy to take whatever direction you point.

- @CataDef
title: Proposal: agent-onboarding/ — dense structural context to reduce agent compaction crashes

body:
## Summary

Proposal: add an optional [PageIndex](https://github.com/VectifyAI/PageIndex) / [OpenKB](https://github.com/VectifyAI/OpenKB) path for long-document retrieval and wiki compilation.

This should not replace GBrain's database-backed operating brain. The useful split is narrower:

- GBrain keeps the DB for operations: page registry, graph, jobs, auth, eval, audit, sources, and MCP surfaces.
- PageIndex handles long-document navigation: document trees, page ranges, figures, tables, and section-level evidence.
- OpenKB informs the compiler pattern: raw docs become summaries, concept drafts, backlinks, index updates, and log entries.

The problem is not that chunks and vectors are useless. They are useful for short notes, fuzzy recall, and cold starts. The problem is making them the default shape for every document.

## Why this matters

GBrain already has the hard parts of an agent brain runtime: skills, resolver, MCP/CLI/API, typed graph, jobs, eval, import/export, and brain-first workflows.

The current hybrid retrieval path is still useful, but long professional documents expose a different retrieval problem. A filing, contract, manual, book, deck, or table-heavy report is usually not best represented as a pile of embedding chunks. Structure matters: headings, exhibits, tables, figures, page ranges, and cross-references.

PageIndex gives a better primitive for those sources: build a document tree, let the agent reason over the tree, then fetch tight page ranges. OpenKB shows the companion compiler loop: raw documents become summaries, concept pages, cross-links, index, and log.

## Proposed direction

Add a retrieval-provider layer:

```ts
interface RetrievalProvider {
  id: string
  canHandle(query, context): Promise<boolean>
  retrieve(query, context): Promise<RetrievalEvidence[]>
}
```

Initial providers:

- `markdown`: current page/wiki lookup
- `graph`: typed-link and backlink traversal
- `pageindex`: long documents and page-range retrieval
- `code`: Cathedral/code-symbol search
- `vector`: fallback, not the default for every source

Then make query routing explicit:

```text
query
  -> route
  -> compiled wiki / graph / PageIndex / code / vector fallback
  -> answer with citations
```

## Smallest useful MVP

A first version could avoid changing the default query path:

1. Add a `pageindex build` command that writes a tree manifest for one document.
2. Add a `pageindex query` command that returns page-range evidence.
3. Add no automatic routing yet.
4. Add one eval fixture comparing current hybrid retrieval vs PageIndex on a long document.

If that works, routing can come later.

## Suggested implementation path

### 1. Add a PageIndex adapter

Commands:

```bash
gbrain pageindex build <file-or-doc-id>
gbrain pageindex query <doc-id> "<question>"
gbrain sources add <path> --strategy pageindex
```

Store the tree and extracted page assets under a deterministic path:

```text
.gbrain/indexes/pageindex/<doc-id>/tree.json
.gbrain/indexes/pageindex/<doc-id>/pages/
.gbrain/indexes/pageindex/<doc-id>/images/
```

The DB stores metadata only: `doc_id`, `source_id`, `raw_path`, `content_hash`, `mime_type`, `page_count`, `index_kind`, `index_path`, and `status`.

### 2. Add router support

`gbrain query` should choose PageIndex when a query points at a long document, or when the relevant source is `index_kind=pageindex`.

### 3. Add an OpenKB-style compiler mode

Do not take a hard dependency on OpenKB at first. Copy the pattern, not the product:

```text
raw document
  -> summary draft
  -> concept create/update plan
  -> concept drafts
  -> backlinks
  -> index/log update
  -> human or agent acceptance gate
```

Generated concept pages should land as drafts unless the install explicitly allows canonical writes.

### 4. Keep vectors as fallback

Vectors still make sense for short notes, semantic recall, cold-start search before summaries exist, and fuzzy lookup. They should not be the mandatory retrieval substrate for every long document.

## Evaluation

Before making this default, compare four routes:

- current GBrain hybrid retrieval
- PageIndex only
- compiled wiki only
- router: wiki + graph + PageIndex + vector fallback

Measure:

- answer accuracy
- citation precision
- page-range precision
- latency
- token cost
- index/build cost
- maintenance cost after source changes
- failure type

Pass condition:

The router improves long-document accuracy and citation precision without increasing median cost or latency for short wiki/entity queries.

## Non-goals

- Do not replace GBrain with OpenKB.
- Do not make PageIndex the whole brain.
- Do not remove the graph, jobs, eval, MCP/API, auth, skills, or resolver.
- Do not make LLM tree search the default for simple slug, tag, graph, or timeline queries.
- Do not auto-rewrite canonical pages from OpenKB-style synthesis.

## Why this fits GBrain

This makes GBrain less dependent on the weakest part of classic RAG while keeping the parts that make it distinct.

Target shape:

```text
OpenKB/PageIndex = document intelligence layer
GBrain = operating brain runtime
```

The DB remains the operating substrate. PageIndex becomes an optional evidence path for documents where structure matters more than chunk similarity.
title: Add optional PageIndex/OpenKB-style retrieval and compiler path

body:
## What

`deriveDirectUrl()` in `src/core/connection-manager.ts` correctly rewrites the
pooler URL host:port to the direct host:port for Supabase, but **preserves the
pooler-style username** (`postgres.<project-ref>`). Direct Supabase connections
require the bare `postgres` user — they reject the dotted form with
`password authentication failed for user "postgres.<ref>"`.

## Repro

Run `gbrain init --migrate-only` (or anything that goes through the schema
probe / DDL path) on a Supabase project where:
- `GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres.<ref>:<pw>@aws-1-<region>.pooler.supabase.com:6543/postgres`
- `GBRAIN_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL` is unset (so the manager calls `deriveDirectUrl`)

Observed:

```
[gbrain] Prepared statements disabled (PgBouncer transaction-mode convention on port 6543).
password authentication failed for user "postgres.<ref>"
```

Reading the derived URL with a debugger confirms the username is
`postgres.<ref>` against host `db.<ref>.supabase.co:5432`.

## Expected

When `deriveDirectUrl` swaps the host from `*.pooler.supabase.com` →
`db.<ref>.supabase.co`, it should also rewrite the user from
`postgres.<ref>` → `postgres`. Supabase's docs explicitly call this out
([direct vs. pooler connection users](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/database/connecting-to-postgres#direct-vs-pooler-connections)):
direct uses `postgres`, transaction-pooler uses `postgres.<ref>`.

## Suggested fix

```ts
// inside deriveDirectUrl, after computing directHost
if (refMatch && refMatch[1] && isPoolerHost) {
  directHost = `db.${refMatch[1]}.supabase.co`;
  parsed.username = 'postgres';  // ← swap user when swapping host
}
```

i.e. the same `refMatch && isPoolerHost` branch that already determines we're
moving to the direct host should also normalize the username.

## Workaround

Set `GBRAIN_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL` explicitly to your direct URL
(`postgresql://postgres:<pw>@db.<ref>.supabase.co:5432/postgres`). The env
override (line 211: `opts.directUrl ?? envOverride ?? deriveDirectUrl(opts.url)`)
takes precedence over derive.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.32.0 (fresh upgrade from v0.30.2 today; bug present at least back
  to v0.31.x where dual-pool routing landed)
- Supabase Postgres on `pooler.supabase.com:6543`
- bun runtime
- macOS 25.3.0
title: deriveDirectUrl() keeps pooler-style user when swapping to direct host

body:
## Summary

When the very first `gbrain sources sync --strategy code` import for a code source runs without a working embedding provider (e.g. `OPENAI_API_KEY` unset), pages get imported successfully but their chunks have no embeddings. Once the env is fixed, there is **no surfaced CLI path** to backfill embeddings for those pages — every available command either ignores the code source's ID or refuses to re-run because content hasn't changed.

The only workaround I can find is destructive: `gbrain sources remove <id>` followed by a fresh sync, which throws away version history, chunk-level metadata, and any cross-source links the page already participates in.

## Repro

```bash
# Initial import without an embedding key
unset OPENAI_API_KEY
cd /path/to/repo
gbrain sources add gstack-code-myrepo --path "$(pwd)" --strategy code
gbrain sync --source gstack-code-myrepo --strategy code
# -> Pages imported, every embedding errors with "OpenAI embedding requires OPENAI_API_KEY"

# Now fix the env and try to backfill
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

# Attempt 1: embed --stale
gbrain embed --stale
# -> Embeds pages in source=default fine, but for every code-source slug:
#    Error embedding modules-foo-json: Page not found: modules-foo-json (source=default)

# Attempt 2: embed a specific code-source slug
gbrain embed modules-foo-json
# -> Page not found: modules-foo-json (source=default)

# Attempt 3: re-run sync against the code source
gbrain sync --source gstack-code-myrepo --strategy code
# -> ' +0 added, ~0 modified ' — content unchanged, nothing re-imported, nothing re-embedded

# Attempt 4: reindex-code with --yes
gbrain reindex-code --source gstack-code-myrepo --yes
# -> reindex-code: 0 reindexed, 7 skipped, 0 failed
#    Content-hash gated; no --force flag visible in --help.
```

## Observed

1. **`gbrain embed --stale` / `embed <slug>` ignore code-source pages.** They look up the slug in `source=default` and throw `Page not found: <slug> (source=default)`. Confirmed in source — `src/commands/embed.ts`'s `EmbedOpts` exposes `slug`, `slugs`, `all`, `stale`, `dryRun`, `onProgress`. There is no `source` field, and no `--source` parsing in `runEmbed()`.
2. **`gbrain reindex-code --source <id> --yes` is content-hash gated** with no force / re-embed flag. Pages whose chunks have null embeddings are still classified as 'skipped' because the source content is unchanged from import time.
3. **`gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code`** likewise only acts on git-diff changes, so a no-op sync produces a no-op embed.

Net effect: the only way back from a key-less initial import is to remove the source and re-add it.

## Expected

At least one of:

- `gbrain embed` accepts a `--source <id>` flag so callers can scope a backfill to the code source whose pages need vectors.
- `gbrain reindex-code` accepts a `--force` flag (or a more targeted `--embed-only` / `--missing-embeddings` flag) that re-runs the embed step for pages already imported.
- `gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code` re-runs the embedding pass for pages whose chunks have null embeddings even when content is unchanged.

Any of the three would unblock recovery without losing the imported pages.

## Related issues

- #710 — MCP `search` tool only searches default source. Same shape (source-scope assumed = default), different surface (MCP search vs CLI embed). A fix that threads source IDs through embed paths would likely benefit both.
- #711 — `gbrain sources list` reports 0 pages for federated sources after successful sync. Related federation/source-scope plumbing.
- #712 — `code-stage sync writes pages with malformed frontmatter; reindex-code reports 'No code pages to reindex'`. Different root cause but same user-facing 'can't recover from failed code import' family.
- #767 — `sync --strategy code` dropped on first sync via `performFullSync`. Adjacent reliability issue on the same code path.

## Workarounds (for users hitting this today)

- Destructive: `gbrain sources remove <id> && gbrain sources add <id> ... && gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code` with the API key set. Loses any source-specific history.
- Live with keyword-only matches on the code source's pages until a fix lands. `gbrain search` still returns BM25-style hits (score 0.0000), just no vector relevance.

## Environment

- gbrain: 0.31.3 (commit 9c60b3a, master)
- Engine: postgres (managed)
- Bun: 1.3.11
- Platform: Linux 6.8.0-111-generic x86_64

## Separate observation (probably its own issue)

`CODE_EXTENSIONS` in `src/core/sync.ts:46` does not include `.tf`, `.tfvars`, or `.hcl`. On a Terraform repo with 163 `.tf` + 51 `.tfvars` files (plus 20 `.md`), `gbrain sync --strategy code` matched 7 files (6 JSON + 1 Python) and indexed nothing else. This mirrors #709 (`.astro` missing) and is probably worth a follow-up issue that tracks an extensible / pluggable extension list, since Terraform/HCL, Bicep, Pulumi, Kubernetes manifests, and Helm templates all sit in the same gap.
title: No recovery path when initial code-source import fails to embed (e.g. missing OPENAI_API_KEY at first sync)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain extract links --source fs --dir <vault>` can discover RAZSOC/Obsidian wikilinks in dry-run mode, but inserts very few or no links when the synced page slugs differ from raw markdown relative paths.

In this vault, synced pages use slugified lowercase path segments, while the extractor appears to resolve links against raw file-ish paths. The result is that GBrain can have thousands of pages and many Obsidian `[[wikilinks]]`, but low link count / high orphan count after native extraction.

## Observed impact

On a large Obsidian vault migration:

- Native extraction dry-run reported thousands of candidate wikilinks.
- Actual DB link count stayed near baseline because most candidates did not match synced page slugs.
- A local slugify-aware bridge resolved ~7.6k unique wikilink pairs and restored graph health.

Current post-bridge baseline:

- Pages: 7,091
- Links: 7,637
- Raw wikilinks scanned: 16,480
