- Unique resolved page pairs: 7,615
- Remaining unresolved: 76
- Ambiguous: 0

## Expected behavior

Native fs wikilink extraction should resolve targets using the same slugification/canonicalization that sync uses for page slugs, including:

- lowercase path segments
- spaces -> hyphens
- stripped punctuation/diacritics
- basename/title/alias fallback where appropriate

## Repro shape

1. Sync an Obsidian vault where filenames/paths include spaces, mixed case, punctuation, and aliases.
2. Run `gbrain extract links --source fs --dir <vault> --dry-run`.
3. Run native extraction without dry-run.
4. Compare DB link count / orphan count with the number of detected wikilinks.

## Local workaround

I added a RAZSOC-local bridge script that walks markdown, resolves links against GBrain `pages.slug`, and inserts `wikilink` edges idempotently. It is intentionally local, but the same resolution logic probably belongs in upstream extraction.

Related PR: #871 fixes a separate large-migration failure where `git diff --name-status` can exceed Node's default exec buffer.
title: fs wikilink extraction should resolve against synced slugified page slugs

body:
On macOS, `gbrain migrate --to supabase` always attempts to connect to the IPv6 Direct Connection address `2600:1f18:...:5432`, regardless of `--url <session-pooler-url>` flag or SUPABASE_DB_URL/SUPABASE_URL/POSTGRES_URL env vars.

macOS doesn't have IPv6 connectivity by default, so this fails with ECONNREFUSED.

Workaround: stay on PGLite.

Expected: --url flag should override the default URL construction and use the provided Session Pooler URL (port 5432 or 6543 via `*.pooler.supabase.com`).

Repro:
1. Configure gbrain with PGLite engine, ingest data
2. Set DATABASE_URL to Supabase Session Pooler URL
3. Verify psql "$DATABASE_URL" -c "SELECT 1" succeeds
4. Run `gbrain migrate --to supabase --url "$DATABASE_URL"`
5. Observe ECONNREFUSED to IPv6 address despite the override flag
title: `gbrain migrate --to supabase --url <url>` ignores the --url flag, constructs IPv6-only Direct Connection that fails on macOS

body:
## Summary

Running the `vector-grep-rrf-fusion` adapter from [gbrain-evals](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain-evals) against gbrain `0.31.3` fails with `AIConfigError: AI gateway is not configured. Call configureGateway() during engine connect.` The graph-only `gbrain` adapter works fine; the issue is specifically on the embedding path.

The error message itself points back at gbrain (`fix: "This is a gbrain bug — file an issue at https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues"`), so opening here. Happy to switch repos if it actually belongs in gbrain-evals.

## Repro

```sh
git clone https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain-evals.git
cd gbrain-evals
bun install
bun run eval:run:dev
```

The first adapter (`gbrain`, graph-only) completes:
```
P@5 49.1%, R@5 97.9%, 248/261 correct (run 1)
```

The second adapter (`vector-grep-rrf-fusion`) crashes during `init()`:

```
AIConfigError: AI gateway is not configured. Call configureGateway() during engine connect.
   fix: "This is a gbrain bug — file an issue at https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues"

      at requireConfig (node_modules/gbrain/src/core/ai/gateway.ts:161:11)
      at embed (node_modules/gbrain/src/core/ai/gateway.ts:457:15)
      at importFromContent (node_modules/gbrain/src/core/import-file.ts:271:30)
      at async init (eval/runner/adapters/vector-grep-rrf-fusion.ts:65:15)
      at async scoreOneRun (eval/runner/multi-adapter.ts:339:31)
```

## Environment

- gbrain: `0.31.3` (commit `ef7794f`, pulled via `github:garrytan/gbrain#master`)
- gbrain-evals HEAD: `ef7794f fix(longmemeval-chart): headline as horizontal bar chart, no overflow`
- Bun: `1.3.13`
- Node: bundled with Bun
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (Linux 6.8.0-111-generic, x86_64)

`OPENAI_API_KEY` was unset for this run (default for `eval:run:dev`). Setting it doesn't change the error — the `embed()` path is reached before any gateway call would short-circuit on missing keys.

## Likely root cause

`importFromContent()` calls `embed()` from `core/ai/gateway.ts`, which calls `requireConfig()`. Nothing on the engine's `connect()` path calls `configureGateway()` — the contract `requireConfig()` describes ("Call configureGateway() during engine connect") isn't actually wired.

The graph-only adapter works because it never reaches `embed()`; only the embedding-bearing adapters trip the missing config.

## Workaround

Open question — would `configureGateway({ embed: <noop>, complete: <noop> })` from the adapter's `init()` be the supported way to wire fakes for tests/benchmarks, or is the gateway expected to auto-configure when the engine connects? Either is fine for downstream consumers, just want to know which path you'd take.

## Why we care

We're spiking `gbrain-evals` against our pinned gbrain to use as a regression gate (and downstream as the methodology for a per-customer "brain self-test" feature on each VM). The graph-only baseline is reproducing your published numbers exactly — we'd like to score the full multi-adapter comparison as well.

Thanks for shipping this — really useful work.
title: vector-grep-rrf-fusion adapter throws AIConfigError when run via gbrain-evals

body:
## Version
gbrain v0.22.6.1

## Symptom
Queries against a corpus with a healthy HNSW index and populated embeddings still route through tsvector GIN keyword search by default. P95 query latency observed at approximately 9000ms, versus an estimated 2800ms with pure vector similarity search.

## Reproduction
Run a query phrase that benefits from semantic similarity against an embedded corpus (e.g., a phrase with synonym variation). Compare timing:
- Current: gbrain query "phrase with synonym" (routes via GIN tsvector + RRF)
- Desired: gbrain query --vector-only "phrase with synonym" (HNSW only)

## Diagnostic evidence
- `gbrain doctor --json` reports: HNSW index healthy, embeddings 100% populated
- `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` on the current query path shows GIN scan instead of HNSW probe
- This is a valid use case when semantic relevance is preferred over keyword matching

## Proposed fix
Expose one of:
- `--vector-weight N` (0-100) to control the balance between vector and keyword scoring in Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
- `--vector-only` flag to bypass tsvector and use HNSW similarity exclusively
- Configuration option in `~/.gbrain/config.json` to set the default ranking strategy

## Impact
This would allow users to optimize for latency-sensitive workloads where vector similarity is the primary ranking signal. It enables production deployments with different ranking needs without workarounds.
title: Expose --vector-weight or --vector-only flag for ranking strategy control

body:
## Problem

`gbrain query` and `gbrain search` return cross-source results with no way to filter by source_id. When a gbrain brain has multiple ingested sources (wiki articles, user diaries, external docs), queries intended for one source surface results from others.

## Current workaround

We filter results client-side by maintaining a slug allowlist of wiki article filenames and discarding any gbrain result whose slug is not in the allowlist. This works but requires N+1 filesystem reads and doesn't scale to sources with non-slug-based identifiers.

## Requested

A `--source <source_id>` flag on `gbrain query`:

```bash
gbrain query "how does Atlas route dispatch events" --source wiki --limit 5
```

And equivalently on `gbrain search`. The `source_id` should match the source identifier used in `gbrain sync --source <id>`.

## Context

- gbrain version: 0.30.2
- Brain has 11 sources, ~3,350 pages; wiki is ~50 pages; watson-diary is ~2,554 pages (76% of total). Cross-source bleed kills wiki recall on infrastructure queries.
- Related: PR #774 (--src-subpath for monorepo subdir sources)
title: CLI: add --source filter to `query` and `search` commands

body:
## Summary

OAuth 2.1 token exchange always fails with `invalid_client` because `GBrainClientsStore.getClient` returns `client_secret_hash` in the `client_secret` field. The MCP SDK's `authenticateClient` middleware compares the stored `client.client_secret` to the incoming `client_secret` via plain string equality (`!==`). Hash never equals plaintext, so every confidential-client token request fails before reaching `exchangeAuthorizationCode`.

Net effect: any MCP client that uses the standard authorization-code grant (claude.ai web/iOS custom connectors, Anthropic's hosted connector backend, anything wired through `mcpAuthRouter` from the SDK) cannot complete OAuth against gbrain. Only the legacy bearer path and `client_credentials` clients with `token_endpoint_auth_method=none` work.

## Reproduction

1. `gbrain serve --http --port 8090 --public-url https://your-host --enable-dcr`
2. Add the URL as a custom connector in claude.ai web (or any DCR-capable client that uses `client_secret_post`).
3. Browser flow completes consent, the client receives an auth code and POSTs to `/token`.
4. Client sees `invalid_client` (or whatever the SDK's `InvalidClientError` translates to upstream).
5. Inspect database:
   - `oauth_clients`: rows present (DCR succeeded)
   - `oauth_codes`: rows present (authorize succeeded)
   - `oauth_tokens`: **empty** (token endpoint never issued anything)

## Evidence from the SDK

```js
// node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/dist/cjs/server/auth/middleware/clientAuth.js:45-50
if (client.client_secret) {
    if (!client_secret) {
        throw new InvalidClientError('Client missing client_secret');
    }
    if (client.client_secret !== client_secret) {
        throw new InvalidClientError('Invalid client_secret');
    }
```

Plain string equality. No hashing.

## Root cause in gbrain

```ts
// src/core/oauth-provider.ts:134-153 (v0.30.0)
async getClient(clientId: string): Promise<OAuthClientInformationFull | undefined> {
    const rows = await this.sql`
      SELECT client_id, client_secret_hash, ...
      FROM oauth_clients WHERE client_id = ${clientId}
    `;
    ...
    return {
      client_id: r.client_id as string,
      client_secret: r.client_secret_hash as string | undefined,  // <-- bug
      ...
    };
}
```

`registerClient` correctly stores `hashToken(clientSecret)` in `client_secret_hash`, but `getClient` returns that hash where the SDK expects the plaintext value it'll string-compare against.

## Suggested fixes

Three options, depending on the security stance:

1. **Override `authenticateClient`**: provide a custom middleware that hashes the incoming `client_secret` with the same `hashToken` before comparing to the stored hash. Keeps secrets hashed at rest. Requires intercepting `mcpAuthRouter` to swap the middleware.

2. **Store plaintext under encryption**: keep `client_secret_hash` for verification *and* add `client_secret_encrypted` (e.g. AES-GCM with a key from env) so `getClient` can decrypt and return plaintext to the SDK. Heavier, more secure than option 3.

3. **Store plaintext directly**: drop the hashing column and store the raw secret. Simplest; matches what the upstream SDK appears to assume. Acceptable if the database is treated as a secret-bearing store (which it already is for tokens, refresh tokens, etc).

The MCP SDK's [`authenticateClient` docs](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk) seem to assume option 3 (the SDK never hashes), which is why upstream provider implementations (the in-memory examples) use plaintext.

## Confirmed working: legacy bearer path

`gbrain auth create <name>` plus `Authorization: Bearer <token>` works correctly via `verifyAccessToken` (which uses `hashToken` properly). So all `gbrain auth test` runs pass and Claude Desktop with bearer auth is fully functional. Only the OAuth 2.1 confidential-client path is broken.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.30.0 (commit `1399e51`)
- @modelcontextprotocol/sdk: bundled version
- Server reachable at `https://brain.taussig.ai/mcp`, OAuth metadata at `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`, resource metadata at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`
- Engine: postgres (Supabase)
- Tested clients: claude.ai web (`Claude` client_name, `https://claude.ai/api/mcp/auth_callback` redirect_uri, `client_secret_post` auth method) and `mcp-remote@0.1.37` (`MCP CLI Proxy`, `none` auth method — ironically works through DCR but trips a separate mcp-remote bug at code-exchange time)

Happy to PR option 1 or 3 if there's appetite — wanted to file the diagnostic first.
title: OAuth 2.1 token exchange always fails: getClient returns client_secret_hash where SDK expects plaintext

body:
## Problem

gbrain v0.18.2 hard-codes OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large in src/core/embedding.ts. Builders who don't use OpenAI as a vendor are forced to either add OpenAI just for embeddings or run lexical-search-only mode (semantic similarity ranking and hybrid query/ask degrade to keyword matching).

This pushes builders toward vendor sprawl. Many of us run on Gemini (Google), Anthropic (Claude), or local-first stacks deliberately.

## Proposal

Abstract the embedding provider behind an interface and add provider switching:

- Default: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (1536 dims) — current behavior, no breaking change
- Add: Gemini text-embedding-004 (768 dims, free tier on Google AI Studio, generous limits)
- Add: Voyage voyage-3-lite (512 dims, Anthropic's recommended embedding partner, free tier)
- Add: Ollama nomic-embed-text (768 dims, fully local, $0 cost, privacy-preserving)

Selected via:
- gbrain init --embedder gemini
- gbrain config set embedder gemini  
- Or env: GBRAIN_EMBEDDER=gemini

Schema change: store embedding model + dimensions per chunk. Allows mixed-dimension corpora during migration. Migration helper: gbrain reindex --embedder gemini --all.

## Why this matters

- Removes vendor lock to OpenAI for builders who chose other AI stacks deliberately
- Lowers the barrier to entry for users who don't have OpenAI billing
- Aligns with the broader gstack ethos of "use what you have"
- Local Ollama option preserves privacy for sensitive corpora

## Willing to contribute

Happy to discuss design before submitting a PR. The lift is roughly:
- Refactor src/core/embedding.ts to an EmbeddingProvider interface
- Add 3 implementations (Gemini, Voyage, Ollama)
- Schema migration to track model + dims per chunk
- Update gbrain init flow to ask which embedder
- Tests against fixture corpus per provider

Estimated ~150-200 LOC plus migration. Open to alternative designs.
title: Feature request: Pluggable embedding providers (Gemini, Voyage, Ollama)

body:
## Symptoms

After `gbrain sync --strategy code` walks a multi-language repo (1882 source files: Java + Python + C + TypeScript + JavaScript + bash + CSS + HTML + YAML + JSON, etc.), the chunks land in `content_chunks` with the chunk text correctly tagged in the body (e.g., `[Java] dview/.../DstreamView.java:5-5 import java.awt.C`), but the `language`, `symbol_name`, `symbol_type`, and `symbol_name_qualified` columns are NULL on essentially every chunk.

`code-def <symbol>` returns 0 hits as a result — the query filters on `symbol_name` which is unpopulated. `code-refs` still works because it's a text-scan over `chunk_text`.

## Concrete numbers from a freshly-synced nvr repo (~31k chunks)

After `gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code` finishes successfully, plus the autopilot daemon doing a few maintenance cycles plus a manual `gbrain extract all`:

| Source language (inferred from slug) | chunks | with language | with symbol_name | with symbol_type |
|--------------------------------------|--------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| java | 16,663 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| python | 10,541 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| c | 3,024 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| javascript | 692 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| c-header | 461 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| bash | 234 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| json | 117 | 104 | 0 | 104 |
| html | 59 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| yaml | 52 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| css | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| cpp | 14 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| typescript | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

JSON's 104 chunks with `symbol_type` are the only language with consistent metadata; everything else is empty.

The chunker code path looks plausible:
- `src/core/chunkers/code.ts:542-579` calls `extractSymbolName(node)` and `normalizeSymbolType(node.type)` to populate metadata.
- `src/core/import-file.ts:127, 470` reads `c.metadata.symbolName` and writes to the `symbol_name` column.
- The chunkable-node Sets at `src/core/chunkers/code.ts:296-300` for Java look right (`method_declaration`, `class_declaration`, `interface_declaration`, etc.).

So either the chunker is computing metadata but not persisting (some pipeline disconnect), or the chunker isn't actually invoking the language grammar for code files (silent fallback to a non-AST chunker), or a downstream process (extraction, autopilot maintenance) rewrites chunks and strips metadata.

## Reproduction

1. Register a code source pointing at a real multi-language repo:
   ```bash
   gbrain sources add my-code --path /path/to/repo --federated
   ```
2. Set `strategy=code` in the source's stored config (until #767's PR lands, this is needed to make sync walk code files):
   ```sql
   UPDATE sources SET config = '{"federated":true,"strategy":"code"}' WHERE id='my-code';
   ```
3. First sync:
   ```bash
   cd /path/to/repo
   gbrain sync --source my-code --strategy code --no-embed
   ```
4. Verify chunks landed:
   ```sql
   SELECT COUNT(*) FROM content_chunks WHERE chunk_text LIKE '[Java] %';
   -- many
   SELECT COUNT(*) FROM content_chunks WHERE language = 'java' OR symbol_name IS NOT NULL;
   -- zero or near-zero
   ```
5. Confirm `code-def` is empty for known symbols:
   ```bash
   gbrain code-def <a-class-name-from-the-repo>
   # {"symbol": "...", "count": 0, "results": []}
   ```

## Suspected interaction with `gbrain extract all` / autopilot

A pre-extract snapshot showed `__init__: 325, os: 133, stdMsg: 102` populated in Python `symbol_name` (from `gbrain stats` with strategy filter). After running `gbrain extract all` plus a few autopilot maintenance cycles, those counts dropped to single digits or zero. Suspect the extract / autopilot path may be rewriting `content_chunks` rows (e.g., for backlink reconciliation or timeline extraction) without preserving the original symbol metadata.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.30.1 (`dffb607`) with PR #768 applied locally (the strategy fix; doesn't touch chunkers or import-file)
- Engine: Postgres 16.13 (pgvector + pg_trgm) via Docker
- bun 1.3.10
- macOS 26.3.1 (build 25D771280a)

## Why I'm filing as an observation rather than a fix

Root-causing this requires instrumenting the chunker output before DB write to see whether `c.metadata.symbolName` is populated on the way in (chunker bug vs persistence bug) and tracing the extract / autopilot paths to see if either rewrites chunks without metadata. The chunker pipeline is yours; faster for you to triage than for me to debug-explore. Happy to run any diagnostic queries or share more data.
title: Code chunks land in DB with NULL language / symbol_name / symbol_type across all languages

body:
## Summary

`gbrain sync --strategy code --source <id>` silently does a markdown-only import on first sync (no anchor commit yet). The `--strategy` flag is parsed correctly but dropped when `performFullSync` calls `runImport`, which is hardcoded to walk markdown only via `collectMarkdownFiles`. As a result, registering a fresh code source and running first sync produces 0 code pages — no error, just silent under-coverage. `code-def` / `code-refs` / `code-callers` / `code-callees` then return 0 hits for every symbol in the repo.

## Steps to reproduce

```bash
# Start with a fresh code source
gbrain sources add gstack-code-myrepo --path /path/to/myrepo --federated

# First sync with --strategy code
cd /path/to/myrepo
gbrain sync --source gstack-code-myrepo --strategy code --no-embed

# Output: 0 pages imported, N skipped (where N = the number of .md files
#         that already exist as duplicates in default source)
# Expected: thousands of code pages from .ts/.py/.java/.c/etc.
```

Verify:
```bash
gbrain code-def <known_symbol>  # returns count: 0
```

## Root cause

`src/commands/sync.ts:847` — `performFullSync` calls `runImport` from `./import.ts`:

```ts
const { runImport } = await import('./import.ts');
const importArgs = [repoPath];
if (opts.noEmbed) importArgs.push('--no-embed');
if (fullConcurrency > 1) importArgs.push('--workers', String(fullConcurrency));
const result = await runImport(engine, importArgs, { commit: headCommit });
```

Note that `opts.strategy` is in scope but never threaded into `importArgs`. `runImport` then uses `collectMarkdownFiles(repoPath)` which only enumerates `*.md`. The `walkSyncableFiles(strategy)` path that `incremental` mode uses (sync.ts:111-134) is bypassed entirely on first sync.

The dry-run branch above (sync.ts:858) has the same drop — `collectMarkdownFiles` regardless of `opts.strategy`.

## Affected behavior

- First-time `gbrain sources add <id> --path <repo>` followed by `gbrain sync --strategy code` produces no code pages.
- Subsequent invocations of `sync --strategy code` after the first still hit the same path because `last_commit` was never advanced.
- Workaround `gbrain reindex-code --source <id> --yes` prints `No code pages to reindex` (chicken-and-egg — needs code pages first).
- `setting strategy=code` directly in the source's config row in postgres doesn't help — the code path of performFullSync ignores the source config too because it uses `runImport`.

## Suggested fix

Thread `opts.strategy` through to `runImport` (or call a strategy-aware walker directly inside `performFullSync` instead of delegating to the markdown-only `runImport`). The incremental path (`walkSyncableFiles(repoPath, cb, strategy)`) already does the right thing; ideally `performFullSync` would use the same walker.

Same fix needs to apply to the dry-run branch.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.30.1 (commit `dffb607`)
- Engine: postgres 16.13 (pgvector + pg_trgm)
- macOS 26.3.1 (build 25D771280a), bun 1.3.10
- Use case: Dividia NVR repo (~7000 files: Java + Python + C + TypeScript + bash). Goal was enabling `mcp__gbrain__code_def` for cross-codebase symbol lookup.

## Workarounds tried

1. `gbrain sources add ... --strategy code` — no `--strategy` flag on `sources add`.
2. Setting `config.strategy = 'code'` directly in postgres — ignored by `runImport`.
3. `gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code --full --no-pull` — same bug.
4. `gbrain reindex-code --yes --source <id>` — needs pre-existing code pages.

Currently no clean workaround short of writing a custom TS script that imports `importCodeFile` from gbrain internals and walks the repo manually.
title: sync --strategy code dropped on first sync via performFullSync

body:
## Summary

For monorepo-style brains where a single git repo contains multiple logical sources (each in its own subdirectory), `gbrain sync` currently can't operate per-source — sync requires `local_path` to be a git root. This forces an architectural choice: either restructure the repo into N separate repos (operationally painful) OR fall back to `gbrain import` which is deliberately default-only (loses per-source isolation).

## Concrete repro / use case

I'm running gbrain on an "atlas" knowledge brain that has 1 git repo with 9 agent-specific subdirectories, each holding that agent's diary entries. Each agent should be its own source (per-source isolation for source-aware ranking). Plus a separate repo (`external-kb`) for VeeFriends KB content.

```bash
~/brain/                            # ← one git repo
├── agents/
│   ├── watson/memory/              # ← logical source: watson-diary
│   ├── librarian/memory/           # ← logical source: librarian-diary
│   ├── terminal/memory/            # ← logical source: terminal-diary
│   ├── augur/memory/               # ← logical source: augur-diary
│   └── ... (5 more)
└── shared/wiki/                    # ← logical source: wiki

~/projects/external-kb/           # ← separate git repo (works fine with sync today)
```

```bash
gbrain sources add watson-diary --path ~/brain/agents/watson/memory --no-federated
gbrain sync --source watson-diary --full
# Error: Not a git repository: ~/brain/agents/watson/memory.
#        GBrain sync requires a git-initialized repo.
```

`commands/sync.ts:374` enforces:
```js
if (!existsSync(join(repoPath, '.git'))) {
  throw new Error(`Not a git repository: ${repoPath}. GBrain sync requires a git-initialized repo.`);
}
```

For this case, the git repo is at `~/brain` (the parent), the source is at `~/brain/agents/watson/memory` (a subdir).

## Proposed fix

Add `--src-subpath <path>` flag (or rename `--repo` to `--root`, with `--source-path` semantics) so sync can operate on a subdirectory while reading git history from the repo root. Concretely:

```bash
gbrain sync --source watson-diary --root ~/atlas --src-subpath agents/watson/memory --full
```

Or, more elegant: when a source's `local_path` is registered, sync walks UP from `local_path` to find the nearest `.git` directory (longest-prefix git-root discovery), then uses THAT as the git context but only sync's files under `local_path`. No new flag needed; just smarter resolution.

`isSyncable()` filtering already runs per-file via `walkSyncableFiles()`, so the sync-scope-vs-git-scope split is a small lift.

## Workaround today

For my use case I fell back to `gbrain import <subdir>` for the 10 atlas-internal sources (which writes to `default` since import is deliberately default-only) + `gbrain sync --source external-kb --full` for the 1 source that IS its own git repo. Loses per-source isolation for 91% of my content.

After PR #707 lands (which I tested locally + adopted via a rebase — see comment on that PR), only the sync-vs-import surface still has this gap.

## Why now

The combination of:
- PR #707 fixing source_id routing in the write path
- gbrain v0.30.1 having a fully-formed `sources` table + resolver chain
- My use case (and probably others adopting gbrain for monorepo-style brains, e.g., docs+code+notes in one repo)

makes this the natural next gap to close. The 1-repo-per-source assumption seems to be the only remaining structural barrier to wider adoption for monorepo-style brains.

## What I'm NOT requesting

- Changing the `gbrain import` default-only design (that's deliberate per PR #707's design notes)
- Adding magic "auto-detect what's a source" — explicit `gbrain sources add` registration is the right contract
- A 1-repo-per-source restructure on the user's side (I'd PR a fix here before doing that)

Happy to take a stab at this if it sounds right to you. Wanted to surface the use case first.
title: feat: sync should support subdir-of-git-repo as a source (atlas-style monorepo with N logical sources)

body:
## Environment

- OS: Windows 11
- Shell: PowerShell 7.6.1
- Bun: 1.3.13
- gbrain: 0.30.0 (compiled binary, fresh install)
- Claude Code: latest
- Brain: PGLite at `C:\Users\espge\.gbrain\brain.pglite`
- Brain repo: `D:\R0-BRAIN`
- Schema: v43 (fresh init, all 39 migrations applied clean)

## Problem

`gbrain serve` starts and prints `Starting GBrain MCP server (stdio)...` but exits 
immediately. Claude Code never sees the MCP tools (`mcp__gbrain__*` not available).

This happens on both:
- A brain upgraded from v0.28.10 (schema v39, migration v0.29.1 blocked — see below)
- A **fresh brain** initialized from scratch with `gbrain init --pglite` (schema v43, 
  zero pages, no migration issues)

The MCP server is consistently unavailable regardless of schema state.

## Reproduction

```powershell
# Fresh brain, schema v43, no migration issues
cd D:\R0-BRAIN
$env:GBRAIN_REPO = "D:\R0-BRAIN"
C:\Users\espge\.bun\bin\gbrain.exe serve
```

Output:
[ai.gateway] recipe "google" declares an embedding touchpoint without max_batch_tokens...
[ai.gateway] recipe "ollama" declares an embedding touchpoint without max_batch_tokens...
[ai.gateway] recipe "litellm" declares an embedding touchpoint without max_batch_tokens...
Starting GBrain MCP server (stdio)...

Process exits. Does not block. Claude Code reports `GBRAIN_UNAVAILABLE` — 
`mcp__gbrain__*` tools never appear.



## Additional diagnostic: exit code

After `gbrain serve` prints "Starting GBrain MCP server (stdio)..." and exits:

```powershell
echo "Exit code: $LASTEXITCODE"
# Output: 0
```

Exit code 0 — the process believes it completed successfully. This confirms 
the server is not crashing — it thinks its job is done. The stdio event loop 
is not registering a keep-alive, so the process exits cleanly instead of 
blocking for incoming MCP connections.

Hypothesis: on Windows, Bun's compiled binary may not treat an open stdio 
pipe as a reason to keep the event loop alive. The server registers its 
handlers and then the process exits because nothing holds the loop open.

Possible fix direction: add an explicit `process.stdin.resume()` or 
equivalent keep-alive in `src/mcp/server.ts` before the startup log line.


## What was tried

1. **Fix `.exe` in `.mcp.json`** — command path changed from `gbrain` to 
   `C:\Users\espge\.bun\bin\gbrain.exe`. No change.

2. **Pre-launching `gbrain serve` before opening VS Code** — Claude Code still 
   launches its own stdio process and times out. Does not attach to the running one.

3. **HTTP mode** (`gbrain serve --http --port 3001`) — fails with 
   `No database connection: connect() has not been called. Fix: Run gbrain init 
   --supabase or gbrain init --url`. HTTP mode requires Postgres/Supabase, 
   not compatible with PGLite.

4. **Global `.mcp.json`** at `C:\Users\espge\.claude\settings.json` — Claude Code 
   does not read global MCP config on Windows; only reads `.mcp.json` from the 
   open repo directory.

5. **Fresh reinstall** — deleted `brain.pglite` and `D:\R0-BRAIN`, ran 
   `gbrain init --pglite` from scratch (schema v43 clean). Same result.

6. **`gbrain init --mcp-only`** — requires `--issuer-url` (remote server). 
   Not applicable for local PGLite setup.

## Related: migration bug on upgrade path (separate but related)

On upgrade from v0.28.10, `apply-migrations --yes` fails before the fresh-init 
workaround:
=== v0.29.1 — backfill effective_date for existing pages ===
column "effective_date" does not exist
Migration v0.29.1 reported status=failed.

This is also reported in #737. The fresh init workaround bypasses this but does not 
fix the underlying stdio MCP issue.

## Expected behavior

`gbrain serve` should block (stay alive) listening on stdio, allowing Claude Code to 
connect and use `mcp__gbrain__*` tools.

## Hypothesis

The process exits immediately after printing the startup message because PGLite 
initialization either fails silently or completes and closes the event loop before 
the stdio transport registers a keep-alive. On Unix this may not reproduce because 
process lifecycle on Windows with Bun compiled binaries behaves differently.

## Impact

GBrain MCP is completely non-functional on Windows with PGLite + Claude Code. 
The CLI works correctly (`gbrain put`, `gbrain search`, `gbrain doctor` all function). 
Only the stdio MCP transport is broken.

`--http` mode as a workaround requires Postgres which adds a hard dependency 
(Docker always running) for a use case that PGLite
title: gbrain serve (stdio MCP) never connects to Claude Code on Windows — PGLite cold start exits before handshake completes

body:
## Problem

`gbrain sync` derives page slugs from repo-relative Markdown file paths, but the current slugification strips non-ASCII characters. A Markdown file whose path segment is entirely Chinese can collapse to an empty slug and fail sync:

```text
Invalid slug: "". Slugs cannot be empty, start with /, or contain path traversal.
```

Observed examples from sync failure logs:

```text
场景设计路线图.md
工具使用.md
技术验证/技术方案/数据工程看板.md
资源/通用规范汉字表.md
```

This is painful for non-English brain repos because Markdown filenames are also the human-facing navigation layer. Asking users to rename every file to English/pinyin makes the repo less readable, while keeping Chinese filenames can break sync.

## Current behavior

From `src/core/sync.ts`, `slugifySegment()` keeps only:

```text
a-z
0-9
.
_
-
spaces
```

All Chinese characters are removed. For a pure Chinese filename, the generated slug can become empty.

## Expected behavior

GBrain should support Unicode/non-English Markdown paths without producing empty slugs.

Possible fixes:

1. Preserve Unicode letters/numbers in slugs instead of stripping them.
2. Add a deterministic fallback when slugification produces an empty segment, for example `u-<short-hash>`.
3. Support explicit stable slugs through frontmatter for sync files, if the declared slug passes validation.
4. Add a first-class `.gbrainignore` or documented include/exclude sync config so human-readable source files can coexist with machine-safe indexed files.

## Why this matters

For Chinese users, readable Markdown filenames are normal:

```text
当前产品方案.md
架构方案.md
任务状态.md
```

But today those names are unsafe for sync. The workaround is to use English filenames like `product-current.md` and maintain a Chinese `README.md` mapping table. That works, but it is awkward and surprising for a Markdown-first memory system.

## Environment

- GBrain version: `0.28.12`
- Engine: PostgreSQL + pgvector
- Sync source: Git-backed Markdown brain repo
title: Support Unicode Markdown paths in sync slug generation

body:
## Problem

\`gbrain extract links\` (both \`--source fs\` and \`--source db\`) hardcodes the entity-dir whitelist at the START of the link path: \`people/\`, \`companies/\`, \`meetings/\`, etc.

This silently produces 0 links for any wiki that nests entity dirs under a domain prefix. For example, a multi-domain personal wiki structured like:

\`\`\`
shared/people/reza-rastegar.md
shared/people/dusty-jolitz.md
gyc/companies/dlp-capital.md
gyc/companies/avenir-aviation.md
\`\`\`

…has zero entity links extracted, even when wiki pages explicitly reference each other via standard markdown links like \`[Reza](shared/people/reza-rastegar.md)\`.

The result: \`gbrain extract all\` reports success but \`gbrain doctor\` shows \`Entity link coverage 0%\`. Users have no idea why their entity graph is empty unless they read the regex source.

## Proposal

Add a config knob \`entity_dir_prefixes\` that defaults to \`[]\` (current behavior, fully backwards compatible). When non-empty, the extractor allows the listed prefixes before the entity dir name in both the \`ENTITY_REF_RE\` and the bare-slug regex.

\`\`\`json
{
  \"entity_dir_prefixes\": [\"shared\", \"gyc\", \"co-living\", \"pwh\"]
}
\`\`\`

After this, \`[Reza](shared/people/reza-rastegar.md)\` extracts \`shared/people/reza-rastegar\` as the canonical slug (matches what's stored in \`pages.slug\`).

## Current workaround

Local patch maintained at https://github.com/PascalWagner/pascal-wiki/blob/main/shared/ai-tooling/patches/gbrain-link-extraction-prefix-dirs.patch — happy to convert to a proper PR with the config knob design.

## Version

\`gbrain 0.28.3\`. Wiki has 2,141 pages, all matching the prefix pattern; extractor finds 0 links until patched.
title: Link extractor only matches whitelisted top-level entity dirs — breaks for prefixed wikis

body:
## Problem

`gbrain` doesn't pass any custom HTTP headers when calling LLM providers. For users who route through OpenRouter, this means:

- All requests show up as **\"Unknown\"** in https://openrouter.ai/logs
- No way to attribute spend to gbrain vs other apps using the same OpenRouter key
- Can't group requests by day/feature

OpenRouter's app attribution and session grouping use three headers:
- `HTTP-Referer` — app identity (e.g. `https://github.com/PascalWagner/pascal-wiki`)
- `X-Title` — app label (e.g. `gbrain: embed`)
- `X-Session-Id` — groups requests in the Sessions tab (e.g. `gbrain-2026-05-07`)

Other openai-compatible providers (LangSmith, Helicone, etc.) similarly read custom headers for telemetry.

## Proposal

Mirror the existing `provider_base_urls` config knob with a `provider_headers` knob:

```json
{
  \"provider_base_urls\": {
    \"litellm\": \"https://openrouter.ai/api/v1\"
  },
  \"provider_headers\": {
    \"litellm\": {
      \"HTTP-Referer\": \"https://github.com/me/my-brain\",
      \"X-Title\": \"my-brain\"
    }
  }
}
```

Ideally the gateway would also auto-populate `X-Title` based on the touchpoint (`embed` / `expansion` / `chat`) so users get per-touchpoint breakdowns without configuring three labels each.

## Current workaround

I'm patching `src/core/ai/gateway.ts` locally to inject the three headers in `instantiateEmbedding`, `instantiateExpansion`, `instantiateChat`. Patch maintained at https://github.com/PascalWagner/pascal-wiki/blob/main/shared/ai-tooling/patches/gbrain-openrouter-session-labels.patch — happy to convert to a proper PR.

## Version

`gbrain 0.28.3`.
title: Add provider_headers config so OpenRouter (and similar) traffic can be labeled

body:
## Problem

After `gbrain migrate --to supabase`, the resulting `~/.gbrain/config.json` is rewritten with only `engine` + `database_url`. Any custom `provider_base_urls`, `embedding_model`, and `embedding_dimensions` set previously in the config file are silently dropped.

This breaks setups that route through OpenRouter (or any other openai-compatible proxy) via the `litellm` recipe — after migrate, requests start hitting the default endpoints instead and fail with the wrong API key.

## Repro

1. Set up `~/.gbrain/config.json` with `provider_base_urls.litellm = "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` and `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_MODEL=litellm:openai/text-embedding-3-small` (or persisted in config).
2. Verify `gbrain embed --stale` works — traffic flows through OpenRouter.
3. Run `gbrain migrate --to supabase --url postgresql://...`.
4. After completion, `cat ~/.gbrain/config.json` — `provider_base_urls` is gone.
5. `gbrain embed --stale` now fails with a 401 from `api.openai.com` (because OPENAI_API_KEY is actually an OpenRouter key).

## Expected

`gbrain migrate` should preserve all top-level config keys other than `engine` / `database_url` / `database_path` from the source config. Equivalently: merge the new engine config INTO the existing file, don't replace it.

## Proposed fix

In `gbrain migrate`, instead of writing a fresh `{engine, database_url}`, load the existing config, update only the engine-related fields, and persist the merged object.

Happy to send a PR if helpful — small change in `src/cli.ts` migrate handler.

## Version

`gbrain 0.28.3`, observed on macOS 15 with PGLite → Supabase migration.
title: gbrain migrate should preserve provider_base_urls / embedding_model / embedding_dimensions

body:
## Problem

When importing pages from a directory structure like `people/shaambhav.md`, `gbrain import` flattens the path to just slug `shaambhav`, discarding the directory prefix.

This means:
1. Wikilinks like `[[people/shaambhav]]` don't resolve (the slug is just `shaambhav`)
2. Two files like `people/john.md` and `companies/john.md` would collide
3. The directory structure the user organized is lost

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
mkdir -p /tmp/test-import/people
echo '---\ntype: person\ntitle: John\n---\n\nTest' > /tmp/test-import/people/john.md
gbrain import /tmp/test-import
gbrain get john  # Works, but slug is 'john' not 'people/john'
```

## Expected Behavior

Either:
- Preserve the directory prefix in the slug (`people/john`), OR
- Add a `--preserve-path` flag to opt into path-preserving slugs

## Current Workaround

Use `gbrain put <slug> --content <content>` individually instead of `gbrain import`, which preserves whatever slug you provide.

## Environment
- gbrain v0.28.7
- PGLite backend
title: Bug: gbrain import flattens dir/slug paths to bare slugs
v0.28.6 OAuth /token always returns "Invalid client_secret" with --enable-dcr (DCR completely broken)

body:
**Repro**:

1. `gbrain serve` (stdio MCP server, no flags)
2. From MCP client (Claude Code), call `mcp__gbrain__whoami`

**Expected**: `{transport: "local", scopes: []}`

**Actual**:
```
{
  "error": "unknown_transport",
  "message": "whoami called over a remote transport that did not thread ctx.auth. This is a transport bug — every remote call site must populate ctx.auth or set ctx.remote === false."
}
```

**Other tools work fine** over the same stdio transport — `find_orphans`, `takes_list`, `sources_list`, `get_stats`, `list_pages` all return correct responses. Only `whoami` mis-detects the transport.

**Likely cause** (from quick grep on `src/mcp/server.ts`):
- Line 36 sets `remote: true` (HTTP server context)
- Line 58 sets `remote: false` (stdio context)

The `whoami` handler probably checks `ctx.remote === true` first and bails into the unknown_transport branch before reaching the local-shape return — OR the stdio entry point doesn't actually reach line 58 in some code path.

**Environment**: gbrain v0.28.6 (commit b325f28) / Bun 1.3.13 / Linux (Ubuntu 22.04, Vertex AI ADC fork) + Windows 11 client. Both clients exhibit the same error over their respective stdio MCP server.

Happy to PR a fix if you confirm the diagnosis direction.
title: whoami returns unknown_transport over stdio MCP transport (v0.28.6)

body:
**What happened?**

After running code-stage sync against 7 separate repos, `gbrain doctor` reports **1237 frontmatter MISSING_OPEN issues** across all 7 code sources. As a downstream consequence, `gbrain reindex-code --yes` returns `"No code pages to reindex"` even though the sync output explicitly says pages were imported and embedded.

Inspection: pages imported from code-stage sync end up with `type=note` in the brain (presumably the default fallback when frontmatter parsing fails to find a type), instead of `type=code` — so `reindex-code` (which presumably filters on `type=code`) finds nothing to do.

**What did you expect?**

1. Code-stage sync should write valid frontmatter with opening `---` delimiter so the parser recognises `type: code`.
2. Pages imported via `--strategy code` should be queryable via `reindex-code`.
3. Doctor's `frontmatter_integrity` should not warn about content the tool itself wrote.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. `cd <git-repo-with-origin>`
2. `gbrain sources add my-code --path . --federated && gbrain sync --strategy code --source my-code`
3. Sync reports e.g. `17 file(s) imported, 75 chunks, 17 pages embedded`
4. Run `gbrain doctor --json | jq '.checks[] | select(.name=="frontmatter_integrity")'` → see MISSING_OPEN count for that source
5. Run `gbrain reindex-code --yes` → `"No code pages to reindex"`
6. Run `gbrain stats` → page-type breakdown shows new pages classified as `note`, not `code`

Across my 7 code sources I get:
| Source | MISSING_OPEN |
|---|---|
| gstack-code-tixtrack-pro-822357 | 366 |
| gstack-code-iva-ticketing-140d80 | 446 |
| gstack-code-tix-workspace-b6a666 | 82 |
| gstack-code-il-mcp-server-721c7a | 90 |
| gstack-code-garrytan-gbrain | 236 (also 2 NESTED_QUOTES) |
| gstack-code-ocial-content-dcd58d | 12 |
| gstack-code-nal-assistant-5c840f | 4 |
| gstack-code-dev-research-d88794 | 1 |
| **Total** | **1237** |

**Likely root cause** (guess from outside)

The code page writer probably emits something like:

```
type: code
language: typescript
---
<file body>
```

…missing the leading `---` line that opens the YAML frontmatter block. The parser then can't find an opening delimiter, falls back to treating the whole thing as body, and the `type: code` line ends up as content rather than metadata. `frontmatter validate --fix` workaround is mentioned in the doctor output but I haven't tried it yet.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.28.6
- OS: macOS Darwin 25.3.0
- Bun version: 1.3.12
- Database: PGLite

**`gbrain doctor --json` excerpt**

```json
{
  "name": "frontmatter_integrity",
  "status": "warn",
  "message": "1237 frontmatter issue(s) across 8 source(s). gstack-code-dev-research-d88794: 1 (MISSING_OPEN=1); gstack-code-garrytan-gbrain: 236 (MISSING_OPEN=234, NESTED_QUOTES=2); gstack-code-il-mcp-server-721c7a: 90 (MISSING_OPEN=90); gstack-code-iva-ticketing-140d80: 446 (MISSING_OPEN=446); gstack-code-nal-assistant-5c840f: 4 (MISSING_OPEN=4); gstack-code-ocial-content-dcd58d: 12 (MISSING_OPEN=12); gstack-code-tix-workspace-b6a666: 82 (MISSING_OPEN=82); gstack-code-tixtrack-pro-822357: 366 (MISSING_OPEN=366). Fix: gbrain frontmatter validate <source-path> --fix"
}
```

**Possible fix path**

- Add `---\n` prefix to whatever code-stage sync's page-write helper emits before the YAML block
- After fix, run `gbrain frontmatter validate <each-source> --fix` on existing brains to retro-fix
- After retro-fix, `reindex-code` should find these pages and presumably move them to `type=code`
title: code-stage sync writes pages with malformed frontmatter (MISSING_OPEN); `reindex-code` reports 'No code pages to reindex'

body:
**What happened?**

After `gbrain sync --strategy code --source <id>` reports successful import (e.g. "17 file(s) imported, 75 chunks, 17 pages embedded"), `gbrain sources list` continues to show that source as `0 pages`. Meanwhile the `default` source page count grows by exactly the imported amount, suggesting the per-source counter is broken even though pages are written and queryable.

**What did you expect?**

`gbrain sources list` should attribute imported pages to the source that received the import. Either the source-level counter is incorrect, OR pages are being written to `default` instead of the requested source — both are bugs.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Create a federated code source: `cd <repo-with-origin> && bun gstack-gbrain-sync.ts --code-only` (this calls `gbrain sources add` + `gbrain sync --strategy code`)
2. Sync output: `… 17 file(s) imported, 75 chunks, 17 pages embedded …`
3. Check `gbrain sources list`:

```
SOURCES
───────
  default                                  federated   347 pages
  gstack-code-tixtrack-pro-822357          federated     0 pages   last sync 2026-05-07T14:43:48Z
                                           /Users/.../tixtrack-pro
  ... (7 other code sources, all 0 pages, all with valid last-sync timestamps)
```

`default` shows 347 (which is exactly the cumulative count from all syncs added to the original 296), but the per-source count never moves off 0.

**Additional: `--source` filter on `gbrain list` ignored**

Related but might be a separate issue: `gbrain list --source <code-source-id>` returns the same set of pages regardless of which source ID is passed — appears to ignore the filter and always return default's pages. Confirmed by passing 5 different source IDs and getting byte-identical output.

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.28.6
- OS: macOS Darwin 25.3.0
- Bun version: 1.3.12
- Database: PGLite

**`gbrain doctor --json` relevant excerpts**

```
"connection": "Connected, 347 pages"
"frontmatter_integrity": "1237 frontmatter issue(s) across 8 source(s).
  gstack-code-tixtrack-pro-822357: 366 (MISSING_OPEN=366);
  gstack-code-iva-ticketing-140d80: 446 (MISSING_OPEN=446); ..."
```

The frontmatter issues per source confirm pages **are** attributed to the right source internally (since doctor counts per source) — but `sources list` and `list --source` both fail to surface that attribution.
title: `gbrain sources list` reports 0 pages for federated sources after successful sync

body:
**What happened?**

The MCP tool `mcp__gbrain__search` returns `[]` for queries that find hits when run via `gbrain search` CLI. Concretely, content imported into a federated source via `gbrain sync --strategy code` is searchable via CLI but invisible to MCP search.

I have 8 federated sources registered (1 default + 7 code-stage `gstack-code-*` from various local repos). 347 total pages reported by both `gbrain stats` and `gbrain sources list` (default).

**What did you expect?**

MCP `search` should return the same results as CLI `gbrain search` for the same query — both should search across all federated sources, OR provide a `source` filter parameter so MCP clients can explicitly choose.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Set up gbrain with at least one code-stage federated source: `gbrain sources add <id> --path <repo-path> --federated && gbrain sync --strategy code --source <id>`
2. Run via MCP: `mcp__gbrain__search` with `query="<term known to be in that source>"` → returns `[]`
3. Run via CLI: `gbrain search "<same term>"` → returns hits with score > 0.4

Concrete example from my brain:
- `gbrain search "OPENTIX 票房"` → `[0.6351] pr_desc_v240 -- # v2.40：事件標註系統 Phase B …` ✅
- `mcp__gbrain__search { query: "TixTrack Pro Activa Productions OPENTIX 票房" }` → `[]` ❌

**Environment**
- gbrain version: 0.28.6
- OS: macOS Darwin 25.3.0
- Bun version: 1.3.12
- Database: PGLite (`~/.gbrain/brain.pglite`)

**`gbrain doctor --json` summary**

```
status: warnings
health_score: 65
connection: 347 pages
embeddings: 100% coverage, 0 missing
schema_version: 38 (latest)
```

(Full doctor output available on request — main warns are: resolver_health 37 routing-misses, sync_failures 180 unacknowledged, frontmatter_integrity 1237 issues across code sources — see related issues.)

**Workaround**

Use CLI `gbrain search` directly when MCP returns empty for what should be present.
title: MCP `search` tool only searches default source — federated source content missed

body:
## Summary

`.astro` files are skipped by `gbrain sync --strategy code` because the extension isn't in `CODE_EXTENSIONS` at `src/core/sync.ts:46-75`. Astro projects (a popular static-site framework — official template count is in the low thousands on GitHub) have most of their app surface in `.astro` files; gbrain currently treats them as invisible.

## Repro

```bash
$ find . -name "*.astro" ! -path "*/node_modules/*" | wc -l
253

$ gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code --no-embed
Found 14 markdown files       # 0 .astro
…
$ gbrain code-def InsightLayout
{ "symbol": "InsightLayout", "count": 0, "results": [] }
```

(`InsightLayout` is defined in `src/layouts/InsightLayout.astro` — invisible to the walker.)

## Root cause

`src/core/sync.ts:46-75` declares the allowlist:

```ts
const CODE_EXTENSIONS = new Set<string>([
  '.ts', '.tsx', '.mts', '.cts',
  '.js', '.jsx', '.mjs', '.cjs',
  '.py', '.rb', '.go', '.rs', '.java', '.cs',
  '.cpp', '.cc', '.cxx', '.hpp', '.hxx', '.hh',
  '.c', '.h',
  '.php', '.swift', '.kt', '.kts', '.scala', '.sc',
  '.lua', '.ex', '.exs', '.elm', '.ml', '.mli',
  '.dart', '.zig', '.sol',
  '.sh', '.bash',
  '.css', '.html', '.htm',
  '.vue',                       // ← Vue SFC supported
  '.json', '.yaml', '.yml', '.toml',
]);
```

Note `.vue` is included (Vue single-file components). `.astro` is the structural equivalent for Astro: a top frontmatter block (TypeScript) plus a JSX-like template body. Same single-file-component shape, same need for code-aware indexing.

The comment at line 35-44 says this allowlist is "kept as-is for now for `isAllowedByStrategy` fast-path + tests" while `detectCodeLanguage` is the long-term source of truth — so `.astro` may also need adding to whatever map `detectCodeLanguage` uses, depending on which one wins at runtime.

## Suggested fix

Add `.astro` to `CODE_EXTENSIONS`:

```ts
'.vue', '.astro',
```

Tree-sitter doesn't ship a first-party Astro grammar, but the existing fallback path that handles `.vue` and `.html` should chunk `.astro` reasonably (frontmatter as TS-ish, body as HTML-ish). If a richer parse is wanted later, [`@astrojs/compiler`](https://github.com/withastro/compiler) exposes a tokenizer that returns spans usable by the chunker.

## Why it matters

Astro is the framework behind many docs sites and Field-CTO-style personal hubs (this repo is one — bradshaw.cloud). Without `.astro` support, `gbrain code-def` / `code-refs` / `code-callers` return empty for the bulk of the app code, even after the source-routing bug in #457 is fixed.

## Environment

- gbrain: v0.28.7
- Bun: 1.3.13
- Database: Aiven managed Postgres
- Repo: https://github.com/johnybradshaw/bradshaw.cloud (Astro 6, 253 `.astro` files)

## Related

- #457 (source-routing + `--strategy code` ignored). My v0.28.7 reproduction comment on that issue surfaced this gap as adjacent. Filing separately per the maintainer's labeling cadence — this one is small enough to be a good-first-issue candidate.
title: bug: .astro extension missing from CODE_EXTENSIONS — Astro projects' SFCs invisible to code sync

body:
# `init --migrate-only` crashes on pre-v36 brains: `column "provider_id" does not exist`

### Summary

Upgrading a brain from <v0.27 to v0.27+ via `gbrain init --migrate-only` aborts before the v36 migration runs, because `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL` is replayed at startup and creates indexes referencing columns that only the v36 migration adds.

### Repro

1. Brain at `schema_version = 35`, gbrain CLI v0.26.x.
2. `git pull` to v0.27+ (introduces v36 migration: `subagent_provider_neutral_persistence_v0_27`, adds `subagent_messages.provider_id` and `schema_version`, plus matching columns on `subagent_tool_executions`).
3. `gbrain init --migrate-only`

### Result

```
error: column "provider_id" does not exist
   at ... CREATE INDEX … ON subagent_messages (job_id, provider_id)
```

The startup bootstrap aborts before `runMigrations()` gets a chance to add the column.

### Root cause

`src/core/pglite-engine.ts::applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` is the existing pattern for reconciling `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL` with not-yet-applied migrations (it ALTERs the legacy tables to add columns the bootstrap SQL expects). When v36 added new `provider_id` / `schema_version` columns to `subagent_messages` and `subagent_tool_executions` and `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL` was extended to index them (see line ~339 of the schema string), `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` was not extended to cover the new columns.

Result: any brain that was created on v0.26 or earlier, then upgraded to v0.27+ before running migrations, hits this on every subsequent `init`.

### Workaround (manual)

```sql
ALTER TABLE subagent_messages
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS schema_version INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS provider_id    TEXT;

ALTER TABLE subagent_tool_executions
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS schema_version INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS provider_id    TEXT;
```

Then `gbrain init --migrate-only` succeeds and v36 records itself as applied.

### Suggested fix

Extend `applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` to add the v36 columns the same way it handles the older forward refs. Optionally: have the bootstrap derive the required columns from the migration list rather than hard-coding them, so future migrations don't repeat this.

### Environment

- gbrain v0.28.3 (also reproduces against any v0.27.x → v0.28.x upgrade from a pre-v36 brain)
- macOS 15 / arm64
- bun 1.x
- PGLite (default engine)
title: init --migrate-only crashes on pre-v36 brains: column "provider_id" does not exist

body:
## Environment

- gbrain `0.18.2`
- Linux (PGLite backend)

## Reproducer

```
$ gbrain --version
gbrain 0.18.2

$ gbrain doctor --json 1>/tmp/doctor.stdout 2>/tmp/doctor.stderr

$ head -3 /tmp/doctor.stdout
{"schema_version":2,"status":"warnings","health_score":70,"checks":[{"name":"resolver_health","status":"warn", ... }]}

$ head -5 /tmp/doctor.stderr
[doctor.db_checks] start
[doctor.db_checks] connection
[doctor.db_checks] pgvector
[doctor.db_checks] pgbouncer_prepare
[doctor.db_checks] rls
```

```
$ gbrain doctor --json 2>&1 | jq -r '.status'
parse error: Invalid numeric literal at line 1, column 7
```

## Expected

`--json` is a machine-readable mode. With `--json` set, stdout has the JSON object (works today), and stderr is silent — or at minimum, structured (e.g., NDJSON progress events) rather than `[doctor.db_checks] connection` style human prose.

The common shell idiom `cmd --json 2>&1 | jq` should not break.

## Actual

`gbrain doctor --json` emits 13 `[doctor.db_checks] ...` progress lines to stderr alongside the JSON object on stdout. Scripts that redirect stderr to `/dev/null` work fine; scripts that combine streams (or end users running the command interactively) see the noise. The `--fast --json` variant is silent — only the full DB-check path is noisy.

```
$ gbrain doctor --fast --json 2>/tmp/fast.stderr
$ wc -l /tmp/fast.stderr
0 /tmp/fast.stderr
```

## Suggested fix

Either:
1. Gate the `[doctor.db_checks] *` writes on `!opts.json` (simplest), or
2. Convert them to NDJSON progress events on stderr when `--json` is set so structured consumers can stream them.

Option 1 matches the silent behavior of `--fast --json` and is what most CLIs do (`--json` implies quiet).
title: `gbrain doctor --json` writes progress lines to stderr; `--json` should be silent

body:
## Feature Request

Please support using a subscription-backed OpenAI/Codex OAuth provider for gbrain embeddings and semantic search, instead of requiring `OPENAI_API_KEY` and separate OpenAI API billing in every local install.

## Context

Some agent hosts, such as Hermes, can already authenticate with OpenAI through a ChatGPT/Codex OAuth flow. In that setup, normal agent chat/model calls are backed by the user's ChatGPT/Codex subscription and plan limits rather than a separate `platform.openai.com` API key.

However, gbrain currently requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` for embeddings and hybrid/semantic search. That means a user can have:

- Hermes/MCP agent replies working through OpenAI Codex OAuth / ChatGPT subscription quota
- gbrain MCP registered and usable
- local PGLite brain working
- but embeddings blocked unless they also create an API key and accept separate API billing

This is surprising from a user point of view because both paths are OpenAI-backed, but only one can use the existing subscription-backed OAuth session.

## Desired Outcome

Allow gbrain to use a non-API-key OpenAI provider for embeddings/search when the host already has an authenticated OpenAI/Codex OAuth session.

Ideally, local users could configure something like:

```sh
gbrain config set embedding_provider openai-codex
```

or:

```sh
gbrain config set embedding_provider host
```

and then `gbrain embed --stale`, `gbrain query`, dream-cycle maintenance, etc. would route embedding/model work through the host's subscription-backed provider when available.

## Possible Design Directions

- Add an `openai-codex` provider adapter for embeddings/search if the Codex OAuth surface supports the needed endpoint.
- Let gbrain delegate embedding requests to a host-provided provider bridge, e.g. Hermes/Codex OAuth, instead of directly calling the OpenAI API.
- Add an MCP/stdio provider contract where the host exposes `embed(texts)` and gbrain consumes it.
- Add a clear `embedding_provider` abstraction so users can choose `openai-api`, `openai-codex`, `host`, `local`, etc.
- If OpenAI/Codex OAuth cannot legally or technically expose embeddings, document that explicitly and suggest the best local/subscription-friendly alternative.

## Why This Matters

The current behavior adds an unexpected second billing surface for users who already pay for ChatGPT/Codex and already authenticated their agent through OAuth. For local-first personal AI setups, this makes gbrain feel less native: chat works through the subscription, but the brain's most important search feature still requires a separate API key.

A subscription-backed or host-delegated embedding provider would make gbrain much easier to adopt in Hermes/Codex-style setups, especially for users trying to keep the stack simple and low-maintenance.
title: Support subscription-backed OpenAI/Codex OAuth for embeddings instead of requiring OPENAI_API_KEY

body:
## Feature Request

Please make the full gbrain feature set usable on a local PGLite install, without requiring users to migrate to Supabase/Postgres for always-on behavior.

Today PGLite is the excellent zero-config default, but several of the most compelling gbrain features appear to become second-class or awkward locally:

- `gbrain autopilot --install`
- `gbrain sync --watch`
- native always-on autopilot
- minions / background jobs
- dream-cycle automation while an MCP server is live

The practical guidance ends up being: local PGLite is great for getting started, but true always-on native autopilot/minions wants Postgres/Supabase. That makes sense architecturally today, but it weakens the local-first story.

## Desired Outcome

A local user should be able to run a fully capable local gbrain with:

1. a live MCP server,
2. continuous or scheduled sync/watch,
3. autopilot/dream-cycle automation,
4. background job/minion-style orchestration where possible,
5. embeddings and extraction,
6. no required cloud database.

In other words: the local install should be able to use the “full power” path, even if the implementation has different concurrency semantics than Postgres.

## Possible Design Directions

Some possible approaches:

- Make `gbrain serve` the single local owner and let it run scheduled maintenance internally.
- Expose local maintenance operations through MCP, so the already-running server performs autopilot/dream/embed/extract jobs.
- Add a PGLite local daemon mode that owns the DB and serves both MCP and maintenance from one process.
- Implement a local job queue mode for PGLite that runs inline/cooperatively inside the owning process rather than requiring a separate worker daemon.
- Make `sync --watch` register with the owner process instead of opening a second competing PGLite process.
- Add a local-first `gbrain install-local-agent` or similar that wires MCP + watch + dream + embeddings together safely on macOS/Linux.

## Why This Matters

For personal AI / local agent setups, PGLite is the natural first choice: no cloud account, no database admin, no monthly cost, and good privacy ergonomics. But the value proposition of gbrain is not just storage; it is that the brain compounds automatically.

If local users need Postgres/Supabase to get first-class always-on autopilot/minions/watch, then the most exciting parts of gbrain are harder to trial locally. A PGLite-native “full power” mode would make gbrain feel complete from day one, and users could still migrate to Supabase later for multi-machine or production scale.

Related: #677 covers the concrete single-owner conflict between `gbrain serve` and maintenance commands. This issue is the broader product request: make the full feature set available and ergonomic on local PGLite installs.
title: Support full autopilot/minions/sync-watch features on local PGLite installs

body:
## Summary

PGLite is the zero-config default, but real agent hosts often keep `gbrain serve` running continuously as an MCP subprocess. Because PGLite has single-process ownership, that live MCP server can prevent maintenance commands from running in a second process.

In a local agent setup, the only reliable workaround was to add a host-specific wrapper that:

1. stops the agent gateway,
2. waits for the `gbrain serve` subprocess to exit,
3. runs `gbrain extract` / `gbrain embed` / `gbrain dream`,
4. restarts the gateway.

That works, but it is brittle and causes avoidable agent downtime. Ideally a PGLite-backed install should not need host-specific stop/start orchestration just to get the full gbrain maintenance loop.

## Environment

- gbrain `0.18.2`
- macOS / launchd
- Engine: PGLite
- MCP config runs `gbrain serve` as the long-lived server process
- Maintenance desired: `dream`, `embed --stale`, extraction, and/or autopilot

## Reproduction Shape

1. Initialize a local PGLite brain.
2. Start `gbrain serve` as an MCP server and leave it running under an agent host.
3. From another process, run maintenance commands such as:

```sh
gbrain extract all --source db --json
gbrain embed --stale
gbrain dream --dry-run --json --dir <repo>
gbrain autopilot --status
gbrain sync --watch
```

4. The second process may block or time out waiting for PGLite ownership. In one observed case, even `gbrain autopilot --help` / `--status` hit `GBrain: Timed out waiting for PGLite lock` because the CLI path tried to open the engine before answering.

## Actual Behavior

- `gbrain serve` and maintenance commands compete for PGLite ownership.
- `gbrain autopilot --install` can install a separate long-lived daemon on macOS, but on PGLite that daemon competes with the already-running MCP server.
- The documented inline fallback is useful once autopilot owns the process, but it does not solve the common host topology where `gbrain serve` is already the long-lived owner.
- Host agents need custom wrappers that temporarily stop their gateway before maintenance.

## Expected Behavior

PGLite mode should offer a first-class cooperative path where the live gbrain owner can also run maintenance, or where installing maintenance refuses / guides instead of creating a competing owner.

Possible fixes:

- Add a maintenance scheduler inside `gbrain serve`, e.g. `gbrain serve --with-maintenance` or config-driven dream/autopilot scheduling.
- Expose maintenance commands through MCP/tools so the existing `serve` process runs `dream`, `embed`, `extract`, etc. inside the active PGLite owner.
- Make `gbrain autopilot --install` detect PGLite and either install a PGLite-safe cooperative mode or refuse with a clear instruction.
- Make `autopilot --help`, `--status`, and other non-mutating informational commands avoid opening/locking the PGLite engine.
- Add `doctor` guidance when PGLite is configured and both `gbrain serve` and a maintenance daemon/watch loop are likely to coexist.

## Why This Matters

The current behavior creates a sharp edge for the most attractive local setup: zero-config PGLite plus a live agent/MCP server. Users can have a live MCP brain or autonomous maintenance, but not both without host-specific process choreography.

A native cooperative maintenance path would let local PGLite users get the full gbrain value proposition -- MCP access, embeddings, dream cycle, extraction, and autopilot -- without writing launchd wrappers that stop and restart their agent.
title: PGLite MCP server and maintenance commands need a cooperative single-owner mode

body:
## Bug Description

`gbrain autopilot --status --json` reports `installed:false` on Linux when autopilot is installed and running as a systemd user service.

This makes a canonical Linux install look unmanaged even though `gbrain-autopilot.service` is enabled and active.

## Steps to Reproduce

On a Linux host with systemd user services:

1. Install autopilot with the canonical systemd path, for example `gbrain autopilot --install --repo /path/to/brain`.
2. Verify systemd owns the service:
   - `systemctl --user is-enabled gbrain-autopilot.service`
   - `systemctl --user is-active gbrain-autopilot.service`
3. Run:
   - `gbrain autopilot --status --json`

## Expected Behavior

Status should report installed when the systemd user unit exists, is enabled, or is active. Ideally JSON should include enough detail to distinguish installation target and runtime state, for example:

```json
{"installed":true,"target":"systemd","active":true,"last_log":"..."}
```

## Actual Behavior

`gbrain autopilot --status --json` returns:

```json
{"installed":false,"last_log":"[cycle] score=81 elapsed=1s next=300s"}
```

The `last_log` is fresh because systemd is writing `~/.gbrain/autopilot.log`, but the installed flag is false.

## Root Cause

In `src/commands/autopilot.ts`, `showStatus()` checks:

- macOS: launchd plist
- non-macOS: crontab entries containing `gbrain autopilot`

It does not check Linux systemd user units, even though `installSystemd()` creates and enables `gbrain-autopilot.service`.

## Environment

- OS: Linux
- GBrain: `0.27.0`
- Install target: systemd user service

## Proposed Fix

Update `showStatus()` to detect Linux systemd installs by checking the systemd user unit before falling back to crontab detection. A minimal version would inspect:

- `~/.config/systemd/user/gbrain-autopilot.service`
- `systemctl --user is-enabled gbrain-autopilot.service`
- `systemctl --user is-active gbrain-autopilot.service`

This would make the status command align with the install path.
title: autopilot status reports installed=false for systemd user service

body:
## Bug

`src/core/chunkers/recursive.ts` `countWords()` returns `(text.match(/\S+/g) || []).length`. For CJK paragraphs that lack whitespace (Chinese / Japanese / Korean prose), this counts the entire paragraph as **1 "word"**.

With `chunkSize=300`, a 5000-character Chinese page returns `1 word ≤ 300`, so `chunkText` returns the entire content as ONE chunk. That single chunk then goes to OpenAI embeddings which hard-rejects input > 8192 tokens.

**Result in our brain**: 158 pages have chunks created with `embedding = NULL` — vector search misses them entirely, BM25 still works.

The same repo already has CJK-aware word counting in `src/core/search/expansion.ts` — proven by tests in `test/search.test.ts` *"CJK word count in expansion > CJK characters are counted individually"*. So the fix mirrors that existing logic.

## Why fixing `countWords` alone is INSUFFICIENT

A third-perspective review (Codex) flagged that just patching word-counting still leaves the chunker broken:

1. Even with CJK-aware word count, the `DELIMITERS` hierarchy is **ASCII-only** (`. `, `! `, `? `, `; `, `: `, `, `). Dense CJK prose has **none** of these — it has `。！？；：，、` and CJK newline variants. So the chunker correctly detects "too big" but has no boundary to split on.
2. The level-4 fallback `splitOnWhitespace` **also fails** on no-space CJK — same `\S+` regex, same problem.
3. **Critical**: 158 existing affected pages will NOT auto-recover. `importFromContent` short-circuits on unchanged `content_hash` (line 227), and `embed --stale` reuses existing chunks. Need a `PROSE_CHUNKER_VERSION` constant folded into the markdown content_hash so existing pages re-chunk on next sync.

## Proposed 4-piece fix

1. **`countWords()` mirror `expansion.ts:56` logic** — CJK chars count individually
2. **`DELIMITERS` add CJK punctuation** — `。`, `！`, `？`, `；`, `：`, `，`, `、` + newline variants
3. **Replace level-4 `splitOnWhitespace` fallback with script-aware fallback** — whitespace-words for spaced languages, character-/codepoint-slicing for CJK / no-space text
4. **Add `PROSE_CHUNKER_VERSION = 2` constant** — fold into `importFromContent`'s hash so existing CJK-affected pages auto-re-chunk on next sync

This is one focused PR. Test fixtures: dense CJK paragraph (no spaces), mixed CJK+English, emoji + symbol edge cases. After fix, our 158 pages re-chunk and recover embeddings on next notion-sync run.

---

> Tracking: chengzehsu/gbrain has a fork running this fix as a separate branch; happy to send a PR rebased on upstream master once you confirm scope. Ref: garrytan/gbrain#638 covers two unrelated improvements (server-side tier boost, split-hash mapper-only fast-path) — this CJK chunker fix is a third independent item.
title: fix(chunker): CJK-aware recursive prose chunker (current breaks on no-space CJK paragraphs, produces chunks > 8192 tokens)

body:
## TL;DR

Normalized media evidence is still useful, but it should be an adapter interchange contract that feeds upstream-native GBrain media storage, not a parallel media subsystem. Host runtimes such as OpenClaw, Codex Desktop, Claude Code, or Hermes may perform OCR/image/video/text extraction differently; GBrain should accept a stable normalized payload and write searchable pages/files/evidence using the native media primitives upstream already owns.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Media["image / video / audio / PDF / transcript"] --> Host["host extraction adapter"]
  Host --> Payload["gbrain.media-extraction.v1"]
  Payload --> Importer["GBrain import/write path"]
  Importer --> Native["upstream-native pages/files/raw_data/chunks"]
  Native --> Search["search / query / query --image"]
```

## Why This Matters

Different hosts have different model access and file-handling abilities. Some can call an OAuth-backed runtime; some can use API-key providers; some can only pass text/transcripts. A normalized payload lets those hosts interoperate with GBrain without each one inventing a different evidence shape.

At the same time, upstream GBrain should keep one canonical storage/search model. The adapter payload should not fork the database model.

## Scope

Good upstream target:

- define or document `gbrain.media-extraction.v1`
- accept normalized media evidence from host adapters
- convert it into upstream-native pages/files/raw data/chunks
- keep payload validation strict
- avoid claims of full binary image/video understanding unless the host/provider path actually supports it

Not the target:

- a competing media table model
- OpenClaw-only product claims in core docs
- a generic model-completion route in GBrain core
- forcing all hosts to support binary media

## Acceptance Criteria For A Future PR

- Add a small importer or documented adapter path for normalized media evidence.
- Validate schema version and required fields.
- Preserve source identity and file identity.
- Preserve existing tags/metadata on re-import unless explicitly overwritten.
- Store searchable text/evidence in upstream-native page/chunk/raw-data structures.
- Include fixtures for image, PDF/text, and video-transcript payloads.
- Clearly document that binary understanding depends on host/provider capability.

## Agent Implementation Notes

Do not revive PR #646 as-is. Rebuild against current upstream media primitives. Start with the storage/write mapping first; extraction routes and host-specific OAuth behavior belong in host adapters, not core.
title: feat: normalized media evidence import

body:
## TL;DR

GBrain needs a documented host-adapter boundary so integrations for OpenClaw, Codex Desktop, Claude Code, Hermes, and future runtimes can be powerful without making GBrain core host-specific. Core should own database/search/sync/MCP/provider primitives; host adapters should own native plugin manifests, host auth/profile routing, HTTP routes, rate limits, and UI/runtime behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Core["GBrain core\nDB, search, sync, MCP, schemas"] --> Contract["Stable contracts\nCLI, MCP, normalized JSON"]
  Contract --> Adapter["Host adapter package"]
  Adapter --> OC["OpenClaw tools/routes"]
  Adapter --> Codex["Codex Desktop plugin"]
  Adapter --> Claude["Claude Code / MCP helper"]
  Adapter --> Hermes["Hermes runtime module"]
  OC --> Auth["host-owned auth, UI, queues, rate limits"]
  Codex --> Auth
  Claude --> Auth
  Hermes --> Auth
```

## Why This Matters

GBrain is becoming useful across multiple agent hosts. Each host has a different extension model:

- MCP stdio config
- native plugin manifests
- HTTP plugin routes
- host-managed OAuth/profile auth
- GUI install paths
- local runtime throttling and queue limits

Without a boundary, every host feature risks becoming a special case in core. With a boundary, upstream can accept stable contracts while host packages move quickly outside the database/search engine.

## Human Example

A user who is already logged into an agent host should not have to understand every GBrain internals path just to install search/query tools. The host adapter can expose native commands like `gbrain_search` or `/plugins/gbrain/extract`, while core GBrain only sees validated inputs and normalized outputs.

## Engineering Boundary

Core GBrain should own:

- pages/chunks/files/facts/takes schema
- sync/import/search/query behavior
- MCP tool server
- provider gateway primitives
- normalized media/evidence contracts

Host adapters should own:

- native host plugin manifests
- host-specific install/update commands
- tool registration shape
- HTTP route names
- host auth/profile/OAuth lookup
- extraction queue/rate limits
- host UI/runtime health checks

## Acceptance Criteria

- Add a guide that defines host adapters and their responsibilities.
- Preserve the existing GBrain plugin/subagent contract; do not conflate it with host-native plugins.
- Include examples for OpenClaw/Codex/Claude Code/Hermes-style hosts without making any one host mandatory.
- Document minimum tool contracts: status/search/query.
- Document optional extraction route shape and safety requirements.
- Document auth boundary: adapters may use host-owned auth but must not leak OAuth tokens/API keys into tool payloads or logs.

## Agent Implementation Notes

This should be docs-only:

- add `docs/guides/host-adapters.md`
- add a pointer from `docs/guides/plugin-authors.md`
- do not add runtime code or package files in this PR
- keep examples generic and host-agnostic, with OpenClaw as one example, not a dependency
title: docs: define host-native adapter/plugin package contract

body:
Hi! I run a fork (`chengzehsu/gbrain`) deployed on Zeabur that ships frontmatter through a notion-sync mapper. Two improvements I built and tested locally that solve real production pain — would love your guidance on how to integrate (or whether to integrate at all).

## Problem 1 — retrieval ranking is purely cosine + compiled_truth boost; no tier signal at server level

When a brain has Sales-FAQ-style tier=1 pages alongside high-volume tier=3 task pages with similar lexical surface, hybridSearch's RRF + cosine score buries the tier=1 FAQ. We had a Slack QA bot return task-pipeline pages 37 times in a row for "方案導入時，基線怎麼計算？" while the actual tier=1 FAQ ranked 5th.

**Proposed fix** ([chengzehsu/gbrain#1, MERGED locally](https://github.com/chengzehsu/gbrain/pull/1)): add `BrainEngine.getTiersForSlugs(slugs)` reading `pages.frontmatter->>'tier'` (regex-guarded int cast) + `applyTierBoost` in `hybrid.ts` (tier=1: 1.5x, tier=2: 1.15x, tier=3/0: identity), called via `Promise.allSettled` alongside `applyBacklinkBoost`. Env-gated default ON (`GBRAIN_TIER_BOOST=false` to disable). 8 tests including a regression guard.

Codex review thread: 019df37c-a25f-7f91-b5de-a160cc5bfc0d. Round 1 ⛔ found 3 P1 + 1 P2 (SQL cast on bad frontmatter, missing keyword-only path coverage, Promise.all coupling) — all addressed in round 2 ✅.

## Problem 2 — content_hash invalidates on every mapper schema change → mass re-embed

`importFromContent` hashes `{title, type, compiled_truth, timeline, frontmatter, tags}`. When notion-sync mapper adds frontmatter fields (we recently added `trust_tier`, `confirmed_by`, `draft_status` for a trust-pipeline rollout), every page's hash changes → mass re-chunk + re-embed across thousands of pages → 4h+ slow sync + significant OpenAI cost, even though no embedding-relevant content actually changed.

**Proposed fix** ([chengzehsu/gbrain#2, MERGED locally](https://github.com/chengzehsu/gbrain/pull/2)): add `pages.body_hash TEXT` (migration v33) hashing only body fields (title + type + compiled_truth + timeline + tags, EXCLUDING frontmatter). New status `frontmatter_only` returned when content_hash differs but body_hash matches — updates only frontmatter + hashes, skips chunking/embedding/tag-reconcile/codeRefs/chunk-upsert. `sync.ts` and `import.ts` push `frontmatter_only` slugs into `pagesAffected`/`importedSlugs` so post-sync `extractLinksForSlugs` / `extractTimelineForSlugs` still run on frontmatter-derived edges (`key_people`, `attendees`, `related`).

Codex review thread: 019df72a-22d0-7010-82ba-5d50602c4bb1. Round 1 ⛔ found 2 P1 + 1 P2 + 1 Nit — all addressed in round 2 ✅.

## Integration questions

1. Do these align with your roadmap? (especially #2, which adds a column to pages and a new ImportResult status)
2. v0.27 master has `ai/gateway.ts isAvailable('embedding')` + v35 RLS event trigger — both intersect lightly with these PRs (cherry-pick produces conflicts in `hybrid.ts` keyword-only branch + `postgres-engine.ts` migration sequence). Happy to rebase if useful, or you can lift the design from the chengzehsu PRs and integrate however fits best.
