3. Migration version: my fork uses v33 for `pages_body_hash`. You'd want to renumber post-v35.

Both PRs have full test coverage and codex review trails. Code link: https://github.com/chengzehsu/gbrain/tree/master . Happy to refactor / split / squash / rebase per your preferences.

Thanks for gbrain!
title: Proposal: server-side tier-aware boost + split content_hash fast-path for mapper-only writes

body:
## Summary

`gbrain autopilot` and `gbrain jobs supervisor` both have worker-lifecycle behavior today, which can make the intended production deployment shape ambiguous.

From local docs/code, the clean split seems to be:

- `gbrain autopilot`: schedules/dispatches maintenance cycles, e.g. `autopilot-cycle` jobs.
- `gbrain jobs supervisor`: owns the durable `gbrain jobs work` process, restart semantics, pid file, and doctor health signal.

## Why this matters

If an operator runs autopilot in default worker-spawning mode and also runs a standalone/systemd supervisor, they can accidentally create duplicate workers. If they only run autopilot with its child worker, `gbrain doctor` may warn that the supervisor is not running even though there is a worker process.

## Proposed docs / behavior clarification

Document or enforce one recommended production shape:

```text
gbrain autopilot --repo <brain-repo> --no-worker
gbrain jobs supervisor --concurrency <n> --max-rss <mb>
```

In that shape:

- autopilot dispatches maintenance cycles;
- supervisor owns the worker;
- doctor's supervisor check maps to the actual worker owner;
- duplicate worker lanes are avoided.

Potential code/doc improvements:

1. Make `gbrain autopilot --install` optionally install dispatch-only mode when a supervisor service is present.
2. Add docs warning not to run autopilot's managed child worker and standalone supervisor simultaneously.
3. Consider doctor messaging that distinguishes "no supervisor, but autopilot has a child worker" from "no worker owner at all".

## Local validation

A local systemd setup using this split produced:

```text
gbrain jobs supervisor --concurrency 2 --max-rss 2048
gbrain jobs work --concurrency 2 --queue default --max-rss 2048
gbrain autopilot --repo /home/shan/brain/knowledge --no-worker
```

`gbrain doctor` then reports:

```text
supervisor: ok
queue_health: ok
```
title: clarify autopilot vs jobs supervisor production deployment shape

body:
## Summary

`gbrain doctor` resolver health currently depends too heavily on the process cwd and `RESOLVER.md` table rows. In an installed CLI setup, running doctor outside the source checkout can fail to find the installed `skills/` directory. Separately, routing evals can report false misses when the natural routing signal lives in `SKILL.md` frontmatter `triggers:` rather than a literal `RESOLVER.md` row.

## Why this matters

Doctor should validate whether GBrain can find and route its own skills. If the installed CLI cannot discover its own skillpack from normal operator cwd, the health check is brittle. If resolver health ignores skill frontmatter triggers, it can flag healthy skills as unreachable/missed even though the skillpack itself contains the intended trigger model.

## Proposed fix

Two small changes have tested well locally:

1. Add an installed-root fallback for doctor resolver discovery:
   - keep cwd walk behavior;
   - if cwd discovery fails, walk upward from the installed module/CLI location and find `skills/RESOLVER.md` or equivalent resolver files.

2. For resolver-health routing evals, build the trigger index from both:
   - `RESOLVER.md` table rows;
   - each skill's `SKILL.md` frontmatter `triggers:`.

Then keep legitimate co-routing explicit in `routing-eval.jsonl` via `ambiguous_with`, rather than weakening the checker.

## Local validation

A local patch stack against recent `origin/master` passes:

```bash
bun test test/check-resolvable.test.ts test/repo-root.test.ts test/routing-eval.test.ts
# 74 pass, 0 fail
```

Local doctor result after the patch:

```text
resolver_health: ok — 39 skills, all reachable
skill_conformance: ok — 39/39 skills pass
```

## Local commits available as reference

- `0275e35` — Fix doctor resolver discovery outside repo cwd
- `3671f28` — Make doctor resolver health use installed skill triggers

Happy to turn this into a PR from a fork if useful.
title: doctor resolver health should discover installed skills and use SKILL.md triggers

body:
## Summary

After upgrading from v0.22.4 → v0.26.7 (master tip), `gbrain` CLI commands ignore `~/.gbrain/config.json`'s `database_url` property and connect to a default/empty Postgres, surfacing as `relation "pages" does not exist` / `relation "sources" does not exist` errors.

Setting `GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL=postgresql://...` env-var resolves it.

## Reproduction

1. Have a working gbrain on Postgres (engine: `postgres`, populated DB at custom port, e.g. `:5433`).
2. `~/.gbrain/config.json` contains:
   ```json
   {
     "engine": "postgres",
     "database_url": "postgresql://<user>@localhost:5433/gbrain"
   }
   ```
3. Run `gbrain list -n 1` (or `sources list`, `put`, `doctor`, etc.) without `GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL`/`DATABASE_URL` set.
4. Result:
   ```
   relation "pages" does not exist
   ```
5. Run again with `GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL="postgresql://<user>@localhost:5433/gbrain" gbrain list -n 1` → works.

## Diagnosis

`getDbUrlSource()` in `src/core/config.ts` correctly reports `config-file`. `loadConfig()` returns the right URL. But the CLI's actual DB connection driver doesn't wire the config-file URL into the connection it opens. Setting the env-var is the only path that takes effect.

This appears to be a regression vs v0.22.4, where a config-only setup worked.

## Workaround

Persistent fix in `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
export GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL="postgresql://<user>@localhost:5433/gbrain"
```

`~/.gbrain/autopilot-run.sh` already sources `~/.zshrc`, so the autopilot picks it up.

## Environment

- macOS 26.3
- gbrain 0.26.7 (from master, `git pull && bun install && bun link`)
- Postgres 18.3 (Homebrew)
- bun 1.3.13
- Schema v34
title: config.json database_url not honored by CLI without GBRAIN_DATABASE_URL env-var (regression in v0.26.x)

body:
## Summary

While enabling GBrain dream synthesis in an automated environment, I hit a few places where the current implementation assumes a direct Anthropic SDK/API-key path and short argv-sized prompts. I patched these locally to make dream synthesis and subagent workers run through an existing local LLM route, and wanted to file the changes upstream so they can be generalized cleanly.

## Motivation

GBrain's dream engine is most useful when it can run unattended from existing agent infrastructure. In that environment, the system may already have a working LLM route/provider configured, but may not expose `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` directly to the GBrain process or systemd worker.

The current behavior made a few otherwise-valid paths fail or degrade:

- `dream --phase synthesize` significance judging is coupled to direct Anthropic client construction.
- subagent/minion workers are also coupled to direct Anthropic unless a client is injected in-process.
- long transcript prompts can be too large or fragile when passed as a CLI argv string to a helper.
- service/worker environments may have a stripped `PATH`, so helper CLIs can be missing even though they work interactively.
- reverse-writing synthesized pages from child jobs can miss slugs depending on whether tool input is stored as a JSON object or JSON string.

## Local changes that worked

I made local patches that validated the desired behavior:

1. **Provider/helper fallback for synthesize significance judging**
   - Keep the existing Anthropic SDK path when `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` exists.
   - Otherwise call a local LLM helper and adapt the output to the minimal Anthropic-like `messages.create` response shape expected by the existing code.

2. **Provider/helper fallback for subagent workers**
   - Keep Anthropic SDK when directly configured.
   - Otherwise route subagent prompts through the same local helper.
   - Add a PATH guard so detached/systemd workers can find the helper CLI.

3. **Prompt-file support for long prompts**
   - Instead of passing large prompts as argv, write the prompt to a temporary file and pass `--prompt-file <path>` to the helper.
   - This made long dream synthesis prompts much more reliable under unattended workers.

4. **Synthesis prompt size cap**
   - Cap transcript content in the synthesis prompt to keep downstream jobs stable.
   - The corpus file still remains the source; this only bounds a single job prompt.

5. **Reverse-write slug collection fix**
   - `subagent_tool_executions.input` may be a JSON object or a JSON string containing JSON.
   - The local query now handles both forms before extracting `slug`, so markdown reverse-write catches the pages that were actually created by child jobs.

## Validation

After these local changes:

- `bun run typecheck` passed.
- `gbrain dream --phase synthesize --dry-run --json` ran without a direct Anthropic key present.
- A real synthesize pass over a cleaned Hermes/OpenClaw-style transcript corpus completed successfully.
- Result: 1 transcript processed, 4 pages written, 4 reverse-written markdown files, summary slug created.
- Autopilot/service path is active with one worker and native cooldown working.

## Proposed upstream shape

Rather than upstreaming a Hermes-specific helper, I think the clean version is one of:

- A small LLM client abstraction used by both dream synthesis and minion/subagent handlers.
- Configurable command/provider fallback for `messages.create`-style calls.
- Prompt passing via stdin or prompt files for any external helper/provider command.
- More robust service environment handling for helper binaries.
- Reverse-write slug extraction that supports both JSON object and JSON-string stored tool input.

## Environment

- GBrain: `0.26.6`
- Runtime: Bun/TypeScript
- Use case: unattended dream synthesis over cleaned transcript corpus

Happy to turn the local patch into a PR if this direction makes sense.
title: Make dream synthesis and subagent LLM calls provider/helper-friendly

body:
## Summary

On `gbrain 0.26.6`, `gbrain doctor --fast --json` reports `resolver_health` warnings from shipped GBrain skills even when the runtime is otherwise healthy.

Observed warning set in a Railway/Postgres deployment pinned to upstream `9e2093fc9bb6cb46520e58b0c95b807e788d9606`:

- `37 issue(s): 0 error(s), 37 warning(s)`
- warning type: `routing_miss`
- affected shipped skills include `archive-crawler`, `brain-pdf`, `perplexity-research`, `voice-note-ingest`, `strategic-reading`, `concept-synthesis`, `book-mirror`, `media-ingest`, `academic-verify`, and `article-enrichment`
- recommended action in doctor output: `Update routing-eval.jsonl fixture or broaden resolver triggers in RESOLVER.md`

## Why this matters

For production readiness automation, `doctor --json` is the natural gate. A deployment can have green DB, pgvector, RLS, schema, embeddings, queue, and supervisor checks, but still cannot honestly claim strict FULL PASS because `status` remains `warnings`.

## Expected path

One of these would unblock strict readiness gates:

1. Update the affected shipped `skills/*/routing-eval.jsonl` fixtures so structural routing passes.
2. Broaden `skills/RESOLVER.md` and/or skill frontmatter triggers for the affected intents.
3. If these are expected until an LLM tie-break layer exists, classify them separately from production-readiness doctor warnings or document a stable allowlist/status code.

## Notes

The changelog already mentions that some new-skill routing warnings are real and that `routing-eval --llm` is currently a placeholder. This issue is specifically about the operational implication: `doctor --json` cannot be used as a strict green readiness gate while shipped skill fixtures produce warnings.
title: doctor: shipped skill routing fixtures produce resolver_health warnings on 0.26.6

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

Currently, the schema for pages is hard coded: `export type PageType = 'person' | 'company' | 'deal' | 'yc' | 'civic' | 'project' | 'concept' | 'source' | 'media' | 'writing' | 'analysis' | 'guide' | 'hardware' | 'architecture' | 'meeting' | 'note' | 'email' | 'slack' | 'calendar-event' | 'code';`

GBrain can be made useful for many other use cases, other than an "executive assistant", but this schema (and other use-case-specific code) prevents that.

**What does the solution look like?**

See below (created by my agent after we discussed it).

**Alternatives considered**

Using hard-coded types as is, but interpreting them as something else. It sort of works, but it is hacky and probably not possible for other use cases, like hardcore science agent memories.

**Detailed analysis/proposal**

Make GBrain a **schema-pack driven brain engine**, not a fixed personal-CRM ontology.

GBrain’s database is already flexible because `pages.type` is `TEXT`, `frontmatter` is JSONB, and links are typed strings. The rigidity is mostly in TypeScript, extractors, docs, and defaults. The next step is to let users define a domain schema without forking core code.

**Concrete Proposal**

Make page types configurable via a repo-local schema file, for example:

```yaml
schema:
  page_types:
    - id: paper
      directory: entities/
      title: Paper
      aliases: [publication, preprint]
    - id: claim
      directory: concepts/
    - id: method
      directory: concepts/
    - id: dataset
      directory: entities/
    - id: research_update
      directory: reports/

  relationship_types:
    - id: supports
      from: paper
      to: claim
    - id: weakens
      from: paper
      to: claim
    - id: introduces
      from: paper
      to: method
    - id: evaluates_on
      from: paper
      to: dataset

  frontmatter_links:
    - page_type: paper
      field: supports
      to: concept
      relationship: supports
    - page_type: paper
      field: benchmarks
      to: entity
      relationship: evaluates_on

  search_boosts:
    concepts/: 1.3
    entities/: 1.2
    reports/: 1.1
    sources/: 0.6
```

Then core GBrain can ship a default `personal-ops` schema pack containing the current `person/company/deal/meeting` ontology, but other users can install or define schema packs like:

- `research-state`
- `legal-case`
- `sales-crm`
- `health-record`
- `product-feedback`
- `hiring-pipeline`
- `investment-memo`
- `lab-notebook`
- `policy-intelligence`
- `software-architecture`

**Specific Hardcoded Spots To Generalize**

The main issue is not only:

```ts
export type PageType = 'person' | 'company' | ...
```

It is also the surrounding assumptions:

- `PageType` should become `string` or a generated union from config.
- `inferType()` should read directory/type mappings from schema config.
- `DIR_PATTERN` in link extraction should come from configured directories.
- `FRONTMATTER_LINK_MAP` should be loaded from schema config.
- `inferLinkType()` should support configurable relationship inference rules, with the existing person/company regexes as defaults.
- Search boosts should come from config, not fixed prefixes like `people/`, `companies/`, `meetings/`.
- Filing rules should be machine-readable and consumed consistently, not only markdown skill guidance.
- CLI/MCP tool descriptions should expose discovered schema types.

**Why This Matters**

The current ontology is excellent for an executive-assistant brain: people, companies, deals, meetings, emails, relationships. But the underlying architecture is much more powerful than that. For Paperzilla, the natural domain objects are papers, claims, methods, datasets, benchmarks, systems, evidence atoms, and research-update reports. Those should not have to masquerade as `concept`, `source`, or `analysis`.

The general design principle:

> GBrain core should manage pages, chunks, search, timeline, provenance, links, jobs, and MCP. The domain schema should be user-configurable.

**A Good Minimal Change**

1. Change `PageType` to `string` at the engine boundary.
2. Add a `schema.page_types` config.
3. Replace hardcoded directory/type inference with config lookup plus current defaults.
4. Move `FRONTMATTER_LINK_MAP` into configurable defaults.
5. Add `gbrain schema validate` to catch invalid page types, relationship types, and frontmatter links.

That would let GBrain stay opinionated out of the box while becoming useful for domains that are not personal CRM.
title: Add schema pack support

body:
## Hi 👋

First off — thank you for `gbrain`. I've been running it as the persistence layer for a personal ops assistant for the last few months and it's been delightful: the engine swap (PGLite ↔ Postgres), the chunker discipline, the Cathedral migrations, the strict-but-friendly `doctor` output. It's rare software.

This issue isn't a bug report, more a context note for four PRs I just opened. They emerged from running gbrain in a Portuguese-language brain on top of a self-hosted Postgres, and wanting to point the embedder at a local Ollama. Each PR stands alone but they share a common shape:

> *Most users will never touch this knob. The default behavior must not change. But for the small set of users who need to change it, source-patching is the only option today.*

So each PR turns one hardcoded value into an env var with a validated default that matches the historical behavior exactly.

## The four PRs

### #580 — `feat(search): make FTS language configurable via GBRAIN_FTS_LANGUAGE`

The smallest one, conceptually. `to_tsvector('english', ...)` is hardcoded in `postgres-engine.ts` and `pglite-engine.ts`. For an English brain it's correct; for any other language it tokenizes incorrectly, so search misses obvious matches (Portuguese stemming, German compound splitting, etc.).

This PR:

- Adds `src/core/fts-language.ts` with `getFtsLanguage()` (reads `GBRAIN_FTS_LANGUAGE`, defaults to `'english'`)
- Validates against `/^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$/` — the regex matches Postgres's own naming rules and prevents SQL injection since the value is interpolated, not parameterized (FTS config names can't be `$1`'d)
- Refactors both engine search methods to use it
- 14 unit tests, including injection attempts

Standalone — no dependencies on the other PRs.

### #581 — `feat(search): apply configured FTS language in trigger functions (v33)`

Once search reads the configured language, the *trigger functions that populate the FTS columns* still write `to_tsvector('english', ...)`. So queries find no matches because the index was built with a different analyzer than the query.

This PR:

- Adds migration v33 that recreates both trigger functions (`pages_fts_trigger`, `content_chunks_fts_trigger`) using `getFtsLanguage()` at apply-time
- Handler-based rather than literal SQL because the language is determined at runtime
- Idempotent
- Tested live: 4372 chunks reindexed, Portuguese terms now discoverable

Depends on #580.

### #582 — `feat(cli): gbrain reindex-search-vector for on-demand FTS rebuild`

v33 stamps once. If you change `GBRAIN_FTS_LANGUAGE` later, you need a way to re-apply triggers + backfill without re-running the migration.

This PR:

- Adds `gbrain reindex-search-vector [--language pt_br] [--dry-run] [--json] [--yes]`
- Modeled on the existing `reindex-code` command (same flag conventions, same JSON envelope)
- Backfills 2782 pages + 4372 chunks in 8.2s on a real brain

Depends on #581.

### #583 — `feat(embed): support local models + configurable dims/cost via GBRAIN_EMBED_*`

The largest one. Embedding service hardcodes model, dims, baseURL, API key, batch size, and cost. For users hitting OpenAI rate limits, running air-gapped, or using cheaper alternatives (Cohere v3, Together's BGE, Ollama's nomic-embed at $0), there's no escape hatch.

This PR:

- Adds `GBRAIN_EMBED_{MODEL,DIMENSIONS,URL,KEY,COST_PER_1K,BATCH,MAX_CHARS}`
- Skips the OpenAI-specific `dimensions` request param when targeting a custom baseURL (Ollama and most local servers reject it)
- Schema migration v33 that resizes `content_chunks.embedding` when the env dim differs from default — but only if no embeddings exist yet (pgvector can't cast between dims, so silent resize would corrupt data; refuses with actionable error otherwise)
- Keeps the old `EMBEDDING_MODEL`/`DIMENSIONS`/`COST_PER_1K_TOKENS` exports as `@deprecated` module-load snapshots so all 12 existing callers work unchanged

Independent of #580–#582 (different surface, different migration version conflict to resolve at merge time).

## Shared design principles

Across all four:

- **Defaults match pre-PR behavior exactly.** All-unset env = byte-identical behavior.
- **Validate before trusting.** Regex on FTS config names, regex on model ids, URL parsing on endpoints, range clamps on numerics. Invalid values fall back to default with a one-time warning so a typo can't silently corrupt the brain.
- **Schema changes are gated.** v33 (in #581) is structural and idempotent. v33 (in #583, will need renumbering on merge) refuses to act if it would lose data.
- **Tests with the same shape as existing ones** — `bun:test`, no new dependencies.

## Merge order suggestion (no rush)

If you find them useful at all:

1. #580 (independent, smallest)
2. #581 (depends #580)
3. #582 (depends #581)
4. #583 (independent, but its migration is also numbered v33 — happy to renumber to v34 once the others land)

## A note on scope

I deliberately kept each PR focused. There's a longer wishlist that didn't make it in:

- Cost-table per provider (Together, Cohere, Voyage) — the env var is fine for single-provider setups, a table would help multi-tenant
- Embedding endpoint health probe at startup (so a misconfigured `GBRAIN_EMBED_URL` fails fast instead of at first sync)
- A `gbrain config` subcommand that prints the resolved config (env > defaults) for debugging

If any of those would land better than the current PR shape, let me know and I'll restructure.

## And again — thank you

The codebase made all of this possible. The migration framework with named handlers, the engine interface, the JSON envelopes on every CLI command, the existence of `reindex-code` to model `reindex-search-vector` after — none of this required guesswork. I only had to look for the existing pattern and follow it.

Whether or not these PRs land as-is, gbrain is going to keep being my brain. ❤️

— Rafael ([@rafaelreis-r](https://github.com/rafaelreis-r))
title: Context for PRs #580–#583: i18n + local model support (a thank-you note)

Hi — we found a security-relevant issue in the v0.26 HTTP MCP OAuth path while reviewing GBrain's source code.

Your `SECURITY.md` directs reporters to use [GitHub private security advisories](https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/security/advisories/new), but private vulnerability reporting is not currently enabled on this repository, so external reporters cannot submit advisories.

Could you enable it under **Settings → Code security → Private vulnerability reporting**? Once enabled, we'll submit the full report through the proper channel.

The issue affects the trust boundary between local and remote MCP callers. We're holding details until a private channel is available.

Thank you for GBrain — it's genuinely well-built software.
title: Security: please enable private vulnerability reporting
bug: `gbrain sync --help` fires a live sync instead of printing help (CLI_ONLY commands without per-command help-handling fall through to live execution)

body:
## Question

`gbrain` import / sync paths only walk `*.md` files. Per-skill telemetry that lives as JSONL — e.g. `learnings.jsonl`, `*-reviews.jsonl`, `timeline.jsonl`, `builder-profile.jsonl` from gstack — gets pushed to GitHub via `gstack-brain-sync` but stays unsearchable inside gbrain.

## Why I'm asking

This may be **intentional** design separation:
- Markdown = narrative knowledge (CEO plans, designs, retros) → goes into the brain, indexable, searchable
- JSONL = operational telemetry (learnings, reviews, timeline) → local-format only, skill-consumed

If that's the design, I think it's defensible — the format reflects the data shape and changing it would break append performance, privacy classification, and the consuming skills' parsers.

## What would help

Either:
1. **Document this distinction explicitly** in README or docs so it stops being surprising. ("gbrain indexes Markdown by design; JSONL artifacts in the same repo stay format-of-record but are not ingested. To make JSONL searchable, generate a derived `*.md` summary.")
2. Or, if it's not intentional, add JSONL ingestion (with some structure-extraction strategy: one page per record? one page per file? merge by `key` field?).

## Workaround if intentional

Build a derived-markdown exporter for JSONL data when searchability is needed (don't change the canonical format). I'd be happy to discuss specs if you want this productized.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.27.0
- gstack 1.25 federation setup
title: question: is .jsonl file ingestion intentionally unsupported? (skill telemetry stays unsearchable in the brain)

## Summary

When `provider_base_urls` is set in `~/.gbrain/config.json`, `gbrain config show` prints the literal string `[object Object]` for that field instead of the JSON content. Missing `JSON.stringify()` somewhere in the config-show formatter.

## Repro

```bash
# config.json contains:
# {
#   "provider_base_urls": {
#     "ollama": "http://localhost:11434"
#   }
# }

gbrain config show
```

Output (snippet):
```
provider_base_urls: [object Object]
```

## Expected

```
provider_base_urls: { "ollama": "http://localhost:11434" }
```

or pretty-printed JSON.

## Fix

One-line change — wrap the field's render with `JSON.stringify(value, null, 2)` (or your existing object-render helper). Happy to send a PR if pointed at the relevant printer file.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.27.0
title: bug: `gbrain config show` prints `[object Object]` for `provider_base_urls` field

body:
## Bug Description
The compiled Bun GBrain executable is killed immediately on Roger's M5 MacBook Pro. Source-mode CLI works, but the compiled binary cannot even print version.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. From `/Users/rogergimbel/Projects/gbrain`, build the compiled CLI:
   ```bash
   bun run build
   ```
2. Run:
   ```bash
   ./bin/gbrain --version
   ```

## Expected Behavior
The compiled executable should start and print the GBrain CLI version, matching source-mode behavior:

```bash
bun src/cli.ts --version
# gbrain 0.10.1
```

## Actual Behavior
The compiled executable is killed by the OS/runtime:

```text
Killed: 9
exit 137
```

Observed command output during the Step 6 GBrain upgrade:

```text
bun version: 1.3.12
source CLI version: gbrain 0.10.1
compiled binary: exit 137
```

## Environment
- Machine: Roger's M5 MacBook Pro
- Bun: `1.3.12`
- Repo: `RogerGimbel/gbrain`
- Branch when observed: `roger/gbrain-v019-staged-port-2026-04-23`
- Known-good source-mode GBrain commit: `1371730 feat: promote reviewed dream packets`
- Build script: `bun build --compile --outfile bin/gbrain src/cli.ts`

## Current Workaround
Use source-mode CLI:

```bash
bun src/cli.ts <command>
```

Do not rely on:

```bash
./bin/gbrain <command>
```

## Impact
Not blocking current Step 6 work because source-mode CLI is healthy, but it blocks packaging/release/daemon/launchd/cron/MCP-wrapper usage that depends on `./bin/gbrain`.

## Notes
Keep this separate from the dream-synthesis Step 6 implementation lane. This is a packaging/runtime bug, not a blocker for guarded source-mode promotion tooling.
title: Diagnose Bun compiled GBrain binary exit 137 on M5

body:
**What happened?**

`gbrain put` and `mcp__gbrain__put_page` fail with a cryptic PostgreSQL error:

```
Error: there is no unique or exclusion constraint matching the ON CONFLICT specification
```

All write paths break — the CLI (`gbrain put <slug>`), the MCP server (`put_page` tool), and any cron/automation that writes pages. Reads continue to work normally, so the failure is silent until a write is attempted.

`gbrain doctor` does NOT catch this. The install scored 60/100 ("unhealthy") but gave no indication that writes were completely broken.

**What did you expect?**

`put_page` should succeed regardless of whether the `pages_source_slug_key` constraint was applied by schema.sql or by an external migration. Or at minimum, `gbrain doctor` should detect the missing constraint and flag it as a hard failure.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Have a GBrain install connected to Supabase Postgres where the `pages` table was created or migrated by a process that uses a different unique constraint naming scheme (e.g., `UNIQUE(brain_id, slug)` instead of `UNIQUE(source_id, slug)`)
2. Run `echo '---\ntype: person\n---\n# Test\n' | gbrain put test-page`
3. Observe: `Error: there is no unique or exclusion constraint matching the ON CONFLICT specification`
4. Run `gbrain doctor --json` — writes are completely broken but doctor does not flag it

**Root cause**

`src/core/postgres-engine.ts` line 306 uses:
```sql
ON CONFLICT (source_id, slug) DO UPDATE SET ...
```

This requires a unique constraint on `(source_id, slug)`. The schema.sql (line 74) defines this as `CONSTRAINT pages_source_slug_key UNIQUE (source_id, slug)`. If that constraint was not applied during `gbrain init` — or was dropped and recreated with a different name by an external migration — the `ON CONFLICT` clause has nothing to match and PostgreSQL throws.

The same pattern exists in `src/core/pglite-engine.ts` line 258.

**Why doctor misses it**

`gbrain doctor` checks: connection, pgvector, RLS, schema version, embedding coverage, graph coverage, integrity samples, JSONB integrity, queue health. None of these verify that the unique constraints expected by the engine's upsert queries actually exist on the `pages` table.

**Fix suggestions**

1. **Short-term:** `gbrain doctor` should verify that `pages_source_slug_key UNIQUE (source_id, slug)` exists and flag its absence as a critical failure (not just a warning)
2. **Defensive:** `putPage` in `postgres-engine.ts` could fall back to a `SELECT`-then-`INSERT`/`UPDATE` pattern when the upsert fails, or run `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS` before the upsert
3. **Migration hardening:** `gbrain init` and `gbrain migrate` should assert the constraint exists after setup, not just trust that `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` created it

**Environment**

- gbrain version: 0.22.6.1
- OS: macOS 15.3 (arm64)
- Bun version: 1.3.8
- Database: Supabase (managed Postgres + pgvector)

<details>
<summary>gbrain doctor --json output</summary>

```json
{"schema_version":2,"status":"unhealthy","health_score":60,"checks":[{"name":"resolver_health","status":"warn","message":"Could not find skills directory"},{"name":"connection","status":"ok","message":"Connected, 732 pages"},{"name":"pgvector","status":"ok","message":"Extension installed"},{"name":"rls","status":"fail","message":"3 table(s) WITHOUT Row Level Security: trigger_rules, trigger_fires, schema_migrations."},{"name":"schema_version","status":"ok","message":"Version 29 (latest: 29)"},{"name":"embeddings","status":"warn","message":"37% coverage, 586 missing."},{"name":"graph_coverage","status":"warn","message":"Entity link coverage 33%, timeline 93%."},{"name":"brain_score","status":"warn","message":"Brain score 66/100"},{"name":"integrity","status":"ok"},{"name":"jsonb_integrity","status":"ok"},{"name":"markdown_body_completeness","status":"ok"},{"name":"frontmatter_integrity","status":"ok"},{"name":"queue_health","status":"ok"}]}
```
</details>
title: put_page fails with "no unique or exclusion constraint" — writes broken when pages_source_slug_key is missing, doctor doesn't catch it

body:
Hi, Garry — using gbrain in an MCP-driven agent deployment and ran into a shape question. Curious if this is a real gap or if I'm missing something already in the API.

The pattern: in agent loops, the most common use of gbrain_query is the "what's relevant?" probe — agent gets a question, queries the brain to figure out which pages matter, then fetches the ones it needs in full via gbrain_get. For that probe step, the agent doesn't need the full chunk text — just slug, score, maybe a one-line summary, source.

The current shape: gbrain_query always returns full chunk_text (typically 2-4K chars per chunk). At default limit: 20, a single query is 30-60K tokens of payload. The existing detail: low | medium | high parameter filters chunk type (compiled-truth vs all) but not chunk size.
What I noticed: the CLI already shows a terse form — 1. concepts/do-things-that-dont-scale (score: 0.94) / PG's argument that... / [Source: paulgraham.com, 2013-07-01]. That shape is exactly what an agent wants at the discovery step. It just isn't exposed via the MCP/op surface.

Proposal: new optional param format: 'full' | 'snippets' (or whatever name fits the codebase). Default full = current behavior, no breaking change. snippets returns slug + score + ≤200-char summary + source, no chunk_text. ~10x smaller payload for the same discovery-step usefulness.

Before I write a PR:

Is this already on the roadmap or in flight?
Preferred parameter name? (format, compact, verbosity, something else?)
Where does the CLI's one-line summary get derived — would prefer to reuse that path rather than duplicate logic.
Anything obvious I'm missing about why the API is shaped this way today?

Happy to draft the PR if you're open to it — wanted to validate the direction before burning cycles on implementation.
Context: surfaced while diagnosing chat-window pressure in a production agent deployment using gbrain via MCP proxy. Full proposal with implementation sketch + test plan is drafted if useful, but figured a short issue first is the right shape.
Thanks for shipping gbrain — it's load-bearing for what we're building.
— Sean
title: Add format: snippets parameter to query op for cheaper agent discovery loops

body:
## TL;DR

GBrain should support host-provided credential sources for providers without making any one host a hard dependency. The active path should build on the current recipe/auth architecture and #698 rather than the stale resolver PR: core provider recipes stay provider-neutral; host adapters translate OpenClaw/Codex/Hermes/etc. auth state into an explicit credential source without leaking tokens into tool payloads, logs, or generated context.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Host["agent host\nOpenClaw / Codex / Hermes / Claude Code"] --> Auth["host-owned auth/profile/OAuth"]
  Auth --> Adapter["GBrain host adapter"]
  Adapter --> Resolver["credential-source resolver"]
  Resolver --> Recipe["Recipe.resolveAuth / provider recipe"]
  Recipe --> Provider["OpenAI / Voyage / Gemini / local provider"]
```

## Why This Matters

Users often already authenticated inside their agent host. Requiring every GBrain install to also collect and store provider API keys creates friction, duplicate billing/config work, and a worse default for managed user fleets.

The product goal is not "hide API keys with another API-key wrapper." The goal is a clean boundary:

- host owns login/session/profile state
- GBrain core owns provider calls, capability detection, and readiness checks
- adapter/resolver passes only minimum credential-source metadata
- secrets never appear in model-visible payloads, CLI output, route responses, or logs

## Concrete Examples

- OpenClaw can expose an OAuth-backed model runtime to a GBrain adapter without requiring the user to paste `OPENAI_API_KEY` into GBrain.
- Codex Desktop can launch local `gbrain serve` while the host plugin contributes credential-source metadata through a narrow adapter contract.
- Claude Code or Hermes can implement the same contract later without core GBrain importing host-specific SDKs.
- CI and headless servers can keep using plain env vars, with process env remaining the highest-precedence escape hatch.

## Current State

- The old provider-auth resolver PR was closed as stale because upstream now has the v0.32 recipe/auth architecture.
- #698 is the active Codex OAuth provider direction and should inform this work.
- `Recipe.resolveAuth` is the right upstream seam to plug into; new work should not bypass it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Define a provider-neutral credential-source shape that recipes can consume.
- Integrate with `Recipe.resolveAuth` / existing provider recipes instead of creating a parallel auth path.
- Keep process env as the highest-priority override for regular CLI/server installs.
- Expose redacted readiness metadata for commands such as `gbrain providers explain`, `list`, or `test`.
- Include fake-token tests proving secrets are never printed, logged, serialized into route payloads, or included in agent-visible context.
- Include at least one host-adapter fixture proving the core seam works without importing a real host runtime.

## Non-Goals

- Do not make OpenClaw, Codex Desktop, Hermes, or Claude Code required for GBrain.
- Do not print OAuth access tokens or refresh tokens.
- Do not claim subscription-backed embeddings unless the provider/runtime actually supports embeddings.
- Do not merge stale PR #642 as-is.

## Agent Implementation Notes

Start from the current recipe/auth files on `master`. Build the smallest seam that lets a host adapter resolve credentials before the provider recipe creates its client. Keep host code outside core. Use fake credentials in tests and assert redaction at every public boundary.
title: umbrella: OpenClaw/Codex auth support for GBrain providers

body:
## Summary

After upgrading gbrain v0.10.1 → v0.22.16, **four observable defects**, one of which is data-loss not just display:

1. **`get_health.link_coverage` returns 0** despite `get_stats.link_count > 0` (display bug)
2. **`get_health.timeline_coverage` returns 0** despite `get_stats.timeline_entry_count > 0` (display bug)
3. **`gbrain doctor` recommends `Run: gbrain link-extract && gbrain timeline-extract`**, but those commands don't exist in v0.22.16. The actual command is `gbrain extract <links|timeline|all>`. (CLI/doctor-message inconsistency)
4. **`gbrain extract all` loses timeline entries silently due to a missing schema constraint.** Stderr emits: `batch error (27 timeline rows lost): there is no unique or exclusion constraint matching the ON CONFLICT specification`. The final-line summary `Done: 0 timeline entries from 501 pages` is misleading — partial earlier batches DO land (timeline_entry_count went from 1948 → 2056, +108 in this run), but 27 from the failing batch are dropped on the floor with no retry. **This is data-loss, not display.** (HIGH severity)

## Repro steps (all from a v0.22.16 install with a populated brain)

```bash
# 1. Confirm DB has links + timeline entries
gbrain stats | jq '{link_count, timeline_entry_count}'
# {"link_count": 6057, "timeline_entry_count": 1948}

# 2. Confirm health surface reports both as 0%
gbrain health | jq '{link_coverage, timeline_coverage}'
# {"link_coverage": 0, "timeline_coverage": 0}

# 3. Confirm doctor recommends the stale CLI
gbrain doctor --json | jq '.checks[] | select(.name == "graph_coverage")'
# {"name":"graph_coverage", "status":"warn", "message":"Entity link coverage 0%, timeline 0%. Run: gbrain link-extract && gbrain timeline-extract"}

# 4. Confirm the recommended commands don't exist
gbrain link-extract
# Unknown command: link-extract
gbrain timeline-extract
# Unknown command: timeline-extract

# 5. Confirm correct command DOES exist
gbrain --help | grep -A1 'extract '
# extract <links|timeline|all>       Extract links/timeline (idempotent)
```

## Hypothesis (root cause guesses)

**For (1) and (2):** the v0.10.x → v0.22.x migration likely renamed/recomputed how `link_coverage` and `timeline_coverage` are derived in the `get_health` query. Either:
- The aggregation query in `get_health` reads from a column that's now empty (migration moved data to a new table/column without updating the rollup query)
- The denominator changed (e.g. now divides by total page count vs. eligible page count, both fields need wiring up)
- The percentage rollup is computed only on a subset of pages and the subset is empty

**For (3):** doctor's recommendation text was hard-coded with the v0.10.x command names. v0.22.16 renamed the commands but didn't update the doctor message. Single-line fix — find the string `link-extract && gbrain timeline-extract` in the doctor source and replace with `extract all`.

## Workaround (works today)

Run `gbrain extract all` directly. The data layer is fine; only the `% display` is broken. Confirmed via `get_stats` showing the link_count + timeline_entry_count rising correctly after each extract run.

## Repro for defect (4) — extract data-loss

```bash
gbrain extract all
# ...
# [extract.timeline_fs] 501/501 (100%)
#   batch error (27 timeline rows lost): there is no unique or exclusion constraint matching the ON CONFLICT specification
# [extract.timeline_fs] 501/501 (100%) done
# Timeline: created 0 entries from 501 pages
# Done: 0 links, 0 timeline entries from 501 pages
```

Pre/post stats observed on production substrate:
- `timeline_entry_count`: 1948 → 2056 (+108 actually landed)
- 27 explicitly reported as "lost" in stderr
- 366 entries from the 501-page scan are unaccounted for (neither landed nor explicitly errored — silent drop from the missing-batch chain)

## Hypothesis (defect 4)

The v0.22.16 extract pipeline issues a SQL `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (column) DO NOTHING` (or `DO UPDATE`) against the `timeline_entries` table. The migration that landed v0.22.16's schema either:
- Removed the `UNIQUE` constraint that `ON CONFLICT (column)` referenced, OR
- Added a new column that the ON CONFLICT clause should reference but the query wasn't updated, OR
- Renamed the constraint and the query reference is stale

Suggested fix (defect 4):
1. Inspect the extract pipeline's INSERT query for `ON CONFLICT (...)`.
2. Inspect the current `timeline_entries` table DDL — confirm the unique/exclusion constraint exists with the exact column the ON CONFLICT references.
3. Either restore the missing constraint OR update the query to match the actual constraint.
4. Add a regression test that runs `extract all` on a 100-page corpus and asserts `Done: <N> timeline entries from <N> pages` matches expected count, with zero "batch error" lines in stderr.

## Severity

**HIGH — defect (4) is data-loss.** Brain is mostly healthy at the data level for entries that survive. But:
- `brain_score` calculation includes `link_density_score` and `timeline_coverage_score`, so `brain_score` reports artificially low (~80 instead of ~95) because of these zero-display bugs.
- Operators reading the doctor output get incorrect health signal + a stale CLI recommendation that fails on first use.
- This shows up daily in the SOAO gate output across the Nous AGaaS substrate (8 hosts, 4-target HEAD parity check, 8 active agent lanes).

## Affected versions

Confirmed: **v0.22.16** (current). Likely also affects v0.20.x / v0.21.x given the rename window.

## Suggested fix

1. **For (1) + (2):** find the `get_health` SQL/ORM query that computes `link_coverage` and `timeline_coverage`. Verify it reads from `links` table (or wherever post-migration link data lives) and divides correctly. Add a regression test asserting `get_health.link_coverage > 0` when `get_stats.link_count > 0`.

2. **For (3):** grep the gbrain source for the literal string `gbrain link-extract` in any error/warn message, replace with `gbrain extract links` (or `extract all` for the combined recommendation).

## Evidence captured

This issue is observed in the wild on the Nous AGaaS production substrate. Full diagnostic: *(internal Nous AGaaS finding doc — full diagnostic available on request)* (companion finding from session 100 lane-A). All MCP tool outputs reproducible against the same DB.

## How this matters to the Nous AGaaS retrieval-quality program

The *(internal: AP-61 supersession-metadata spec)* spec proposes adding a `superseded_pages` counter to `get_health` once the supersession-metadata frontmatter contract ships. That spec depends on gbrain's `get_health` surface being trustworthy. As long as `link_coverage` and `timeline_coverage` lie about being 0%, any new metric we add will be similarly suspect.

Fixing this also helps any agent reading `get_health` to make routing decisions trust the output.
title: v0.22.16: extract data-loss + display bugs (4 defects with repro)

body:
## Summary

The `NESTED_QUOTES` lint check (`gbrain frontmatter validate` and `gbrain doctor`) flags any line in YAML frontmatter with **3 or more unescaped double quotes** as nested-quote violations. This false-positives on the legitimate YAML pattern `key: ["A", "B", "C"]` (flow-sequence array of quoted strings), which is valid YAML and parses correctly.

## Reproduce

```yaml
---
type: law
related: ["LAW-001", "LAW-009", "LAW-013"]
---
```

```bash
$ gbrain frontmatter validate file.md
[NESTED_QUOTES]:3 Nested double quotes in YAML value (use single quotes for the outer)
```

## Why this is a FP

- The line is valid YAML (parses cleanly to `["LAW-001", "LAW-009", "LAW-013"]`)
- The lint description suggests `key: "value with "inner" quote"` (a single value with malformed escaping), which is genuinely broken
- The detector counts raw `"` characters per line; arrays of quoted strings naturally have ≥4 quotes per line

## Impact

Surfaces 16+ false-positive WARN-level lint findings on legitimate vault content. Brain owners cannot reach `Health 100/100` without rewriting valid YAML to unquoted form (e.g., `[LAW-001, LAW-009, LAW-013]`), which then breaks if any value contains a colon or starts with a special char.

## Suggested fix

Detect actual nested-quote pattern (single-value with unescaped inner quotes) rather than counting per-line quote count. Alternatively, parse the YAML value first and only flag if a string value contains an unescaped `"`.

## Environment

- gbrain v0.22.16
- Source: `src/core/markdown.ts:219` and `src/core/brain-writer.ts:115`
- Surfaced via Nous AGaaS substrate audit (~16 FPs across LAW/AMENDMENT files with `related: ["..."]` arrays)
title: [FP] NESTED_QUOTES lint false-positives on flow-sequence arrays of quoted strings

body:
## Summary

  Following the README's recommended install path (`git clone && bun install && bun link`), `bun install` fails inside the cloned repo because Bun's built-in mini-shell cannot
  parse the `postinstall` script's redirect syntax. The script never gets a chance to execute its intended logic, and `bun install` exits with code 1.

  This is a *different* Bun pitfall from the one already documented in the README (which warns about global installs blocking the postinstall hook). Here, the hook *does* fire —
  it just fails at parse time.

  ## Environment

  - OS: Windows 11
  - Bun: 1.3.12 (700fc117)
  - Install path: `git clone` + `bun install` (the README-recommended path)

  ## Reproduction

  ```sh
  git clone https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain.git
  cd gbrain
  bun install
  ```

  ## Actual output

  ```
  $ command -v gbrain >/dev/null 2>&1 && gbrain apply-migrations --yes --non-interactive || echo '[gbrain] postinstall skipped. ...' 1>&2
  error: Failed to run script D:\...\gbrain\[eval] due to error expected a command or assignment but got: "Redirect"
  error: postinstall script from "gbrain" exited with 1
  ```

  ## Root cause

  Bun does not invoke the system shell for `package.json` scripts; it uses its own POSIX-ish mini-shell written in Zig. That parser does not currently accept the combination used
  in the postinstall:

  ```sh
  command -v gbrain >/dev/null 2>&1 && gbrain apply-migrations --yes --non-interactive \
    || echo '[gbrain] postinstall skipped...' 1>&2
  ```

  Specifically, the chained redirects (`>/dev/null 2>&1` ... `1>&2`) combined with `&&` / `||` trip the parser. The exact same line runs fine under bash / zsh / Git Bash.

  ## Suggested fix

  Move the postinstall logic into a small Node script and invoke it without shell features:

  ```json
  "scripts": {
    "postinstall": "node scripts/postinstall.mjs"
  }
  ```

  Inside `scripts/postinstall.mjs`, use `child_process.spawnSync` to:

  1. Check whether `gbrain` is on PATH.
  2. If yes, run `gbrain apply-migrations --yes --non-interactive`.
  3. If no, print the existing skip message to stderr.

  This avoids the entire shell-compatibility surface and works identically under bun / npm / pnpm / yarn on every platform.

  ## Workaround for users hitting this today

  ```sh
  bun install --ignore-scripts
  bun link
  gbrain apply-migrations --yes
  ```

  Happy to send a PR if useful.
title: bun install fails: postinstall script unparseable by Bun's built-in shell

body:
## Summary

The npm package name `gbrain` is registered by an unrelated graph/RL library (currently at v1.3.1). Because gbrain is installed via `git clone + bun link` (not npm), users who run the natural upgrade command `bun add -g gbrain@latest` will silently install the wrong package and break their working binary.

## Why this matters

gbrain is an AI agent tool with:
- Filesystem access
- Credentials in `~/.gbrain/config.json` (Supabase DB URL)
- Runs in Claude Code / OpenClaw sessions with broad permissions

A malicious actor who controls `gbrain` on npm could:
1. Publish a poisoned version
2. Wait for users to run `bun add -g gbrain@latest` as a reflex upgrade
3. Exfiltrate `~/.gbrain/config.json`, shell history, API keys, or worse

This is a classic **dependency confusion / package squatting** attack. The install path (`bun link` from git) is actually safer by design — the risk is the gap between the install method and the intuitive upgrade command.

## Reproduction

```bash
bun add -g gbrain@latest
# Installs https://www.npmjs.com/package/gbrain (v1.3.1 — graph/RL library)
# Breaks ~/.bun/bin/gbrain symlink
gbrain --version  # fails: broken symlink
```

## Recommended fix

Register `gbrain` on npm defensively with a stub package that prints a warning and exits 1:

```js
// index.js
console.error("⚠️  This is not the gbrain knowledge brain tool.");
console.error("    Install from source: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain");
process.exit(1);
```

This locks out bad actors and guides confused users to the right install path. Many popular tools do this (e.g. `node-fetch` stub, `leftpad` successors).

## Workaround (for existing users)

Add a shell guard to `~/.zshrc`:

```zsh
function bun() {
  if [[ "$*" == *"add"*"-g"*"gbrain"* ]]; then
    echo "❌ Blocked: 'gbrain' on npm is squatted. Upgrade via: cd ~/gbrain && git pull && bun install && bun link"
    return 1
  fi
  command bun "$@"
}
```

Flagging this as a security issue — happy to keep it public since the attack vector is already obvious to anyone who checks npm.
title: Security: npm package name `gbrain` is squatted — dependency confusion attack vector

body:
## Bug

`gbrain check-update --json` returns a `no_releases` error with an empty `latest_version`, instead of either succeeding or failing with a clearer message.

## Repro (gbrain 0.22.2)

```console
$ gbrain check-update --json
{
  "current_version": "0.22.2",
  "current_source": "package-json",
  "latest_version": "",
  "update_available": false,
  "upgrade_command": "bun update gbrain",
  "release_url": "",
  "changelog_diff": "",
  "published_at": "",
  "error": "no_releases"
}
```

## Expected

Either:
- (a) Successfully fetch the latest published version (npm registry or GitHub releases) and report it
- (b) If the API source is unreachable, return a clearer error like `network_error` or `api_unavailable` so users can debug

## Notes

- Install source is `package-json` (i.e., installed via npm/bun, not git)
- The `release_url` and `changelog_diff` fields are empty too — suggests the lookup never returned a hit, not that it returned a hit with empty fields

## Probable cause

If the check is hitting GitHub releases on `garrytan/gbrain`, there don't appear to be tagged releases (or the lookup target is wrong). For npm-installed users, the npm registry would be a more reliable source.

---
Reported via /cso security audit. gbrain 0.22.2 on macOS, bun 1.3.13.
title: check-update --json returns no_releases with empty latest_version

body:
## Bug

`gbrain check-backlinks check [dir]` and `gbrain check-backlinks fix [dir]` both ignore the optional `[dir]` argument and walk the cwd (or parent of cwd) instead. When invoked from a directory whose siblings include unreadable folders (`~/Pictures/Photo Booth Library` on macOS), the walker dies with `EPERM`.

## Repro (gbrain 0.22.2 on macOS)

```console
$ pwd
/Users/me

$ gbrain check-backlinks check ~/brain
[backlinks.scan] start
[backlinks.scan] done
EPERM: operation not permitted, scandir 'Pictures/Photo Booth Library'
```

Same with the explicit dir arg:

```console
$ gbrain check-backlinks check /Users/me/brain
EPERM: operation not permitted, scandir 'Pictures/Photo Booth Library'
```

Workaround — `cd` into the brain dir first, omit the arg:

```console
$ cd ~/brain && gbrain check-backlinks check
[backlinks.scan] start
[backlinks.scan] done
No missing back-links found.
```

## Expected

The `[dir]` argument should override cwd. With the explicit arg, the walker should stay scoped to `<dir>` and never traverse siblings.

## Why this matters

- New users running `gbrain check-backlinks fix` from outside their brain dir get a confusing macOS-specific error and bail.
- Documentation/`--help` says `[dir]` is the brain dir to check, so the current behavior is surprising.

## Probable fix area

The CLI flag parser for `check-backlinks`. Pass the resolved `dir` into the file-walker rather than letting it default to `process.cwd()` or `path.resolve('.')`.

Happy to send a PR if you can point me at the right call site.

---
Reported via /cso security audit on a 31k-page brain. gbrain 0.22.2 on macOS 25.4.0 (Darwin), bun 1.3.13.
title: check-backlinks <check|fix> [dir] ignores the directory argument

body:
## Repro

Fresh install on macOS, `gbrain` v0.10.1, Postgres engine pointing at a Supabase project (Postgres 17.6, pgvector 0.8.0, pg_trgm 1.6).

Vault: 7 markdown pages, 10 chunks, all embedded with `text-embedding-3-large`.

```
$ gbrain query "folder conventions"
[NaN] claude -- # helm-vault — rulebook
[NaN] claude -- ## 1. Folder conventions
[NaN] index -- # helm-vault index
[NaN] log -- # log
[NaN] memory/memory -- # vault memory index
```

Every result line gets `[NaN]` as the score prefix. Ranking order itself looks correct (the `claude` chunks containing the matching content rank above unrelated pages), so this looks like a display / scoring-pipeline bug rather than a retrieval bug.

Reproduces with both literal-token and concept-style queries, and with `--no-expand`.

## Where it surfaces

`src/cli.ts:174`:

```ts
return results.map(r =>
  `[${r.score?.toFixed(4) || '?'}] ${r.slug} -- ${r.chunk_text?.slice(0, 100) || ''}${r.stale ? ' (stale)' : ''}`,
).join('\n') + '\n';
```

The formatter has an operator-precedence trap: when `r.score` is `NaN`, `NaN.toFixed(4)` returns the string `"NaN"`, which is truthy, so the `|| '?'` fallback never fires. Easy fix:

```ts
const s = Number.isFinite(r.score) ? r.score.toFixed(4) : '?';
return `[${s}] ${r.slug} -- ${r.chunk_text?.slice(0, 100) || ''}${r.stale ? ' (stale)' : ''}`;
```

That's the cosmetic patch. But the real question is **why the score is `NaN` to begin with** — that's almost certainly upstream in `src/core/search/` (RRF + multi-query expansion + dedup). Likely candidate: division by zero in the RRF normalizer when one of the source rankings is empty, or NaN propagation through the compiled-truth boost on a small corpus.

## Environment

- gbrain `0.10.1`
- bun `1.3.10`, node `22.22.0`
- macOS (Darwin arm64)
- Engine: Postgres on Supabase (transaction pooler, port 5432)
- 7 pages / 10 chunks / 100% embedded
- Schema version 4

## Doctor output

```
[OK] connection: Connected, 7 pages
[OK] pgvector: Extension installed
[OK] rls: RLS enabled on all tables
[OK] schema_version: Version 4 (latest: 4)
[OK] link_integrity: No dead links
```

Happy to test a fix against this Supabase install if useful.
title: [NaN] score prefix in `gbrain query` output (v0.10.1, Postgres engine)

body:
**What happened?**

Code indexing does not work correctly for source-based repo syncs.

There appear to be two related bugs:

1. `gbrain sync --source <id>` ignores `sources.config.strategy`
2. full / first sync still uses the markdown-only importer even when `--strategy code` is passed

In practice, this means a repo source configured for code indexing still reports:

```text
Running full import of /path/to/repo...
Found 19 markdown files
```

and no code symbols become queryable via `code-def` / `code-refs`.

**What did you expect?**

If a source is configured with `config.strategy = "code"`, then:

- `gbrain sync --source <id>` should honor that strategy automatically
- `gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code` should perform a full code-aware sync on first/full sync
- code symbols from the repo should become available through `code-def`, `code-refs`, `code-callers`, and `code-callees`

**Steps to reproduce**
1. Register a repo source:
   ```bash
   gbrain sources add presswork --path /home/luna/code/presswork
   ```
2. Set `sources.config.strategy` for that source to `code` (or pass `--strategy code` explicitly to sync)
3. Run:
   ```bash
   gbrain sync --source presswork --strategy code
   ```
4. Observe output like:
   ```text
   Running full import of /home/luna/code/presswork...
   Found 19 markdown files
   ```
5. Check symbol retrieval:
   ```bash
   gbrain code-def <KnownSymbol>
   gbrain code-refs <KnownSymbol> --json
   ```
6. Observe that code symbols are missing / empty

**Environment**
- gbrain version: (`0.21.0`, technically `0.22.0` but you didn't change the `package.json` version on release for that)
- OS: Linux
- Bun version: (`1.3.13`)
- Database: self-hosted Postgres

title: bug: code indexing doesn't work as expected (or at all)

body:
**What happened?**

The README currently tells users:

```bash
gbrain sources add <repo> --strategy code
```

but `sources add` does not support `--strategy`, so the documented command is invalid.

Separately, the current docs imply that `sync --strategy code` is enough to index a repo as code, but first/full sync still routes through the markdown importer, so the documented workflow does not work as written.

**What did you expect?**

The docs should either:

- describe the currently supported behavior accurately, or
- only document code-repo indexing once the underlying full-sync path actually supports it

At minimum, the docs should not suggest a `sources add --strategy code` flag that does not exist.

**Steps to reproduce**
1. Read README instruction:
   ```text
   Run `gbrain sources add <repo> --strategy code` to index a repo
   ```
2. Run it
3. Observe `sources add` rejects `--strategy`
4. Try the implied alternative:
   ```bash
   gbrain sync --source <id> --strategy code
   ```
5. Observe first/full sync still reports markdown import behavior

**Environment**
- gbrain version: (`0.21.0`, technically `0.22.0` but you didn't change the `package.json` version on release for that)
- OS: Linux
- Bun version: (`1.3.13`)
- Database: self-hosted Postgres

title: docs: incorrect code indexing command

body:
## Problem
`isSyncable()` in `src/core/sync.ts` hardcodes which paths sync. Users with upstream tooling that owns directory conventions (e.g. Docusaurus blog with required `slug:` frontmatter that gbrain rejects) cannot exclude those dirs without:

1. Patching the hardcoded list (lost on upgrade)
2. Renaming to hidden dir (breaks the upstream tool)
3. Spamming `--skip-failed` forever (noise + still re-fails when files edit)

## Concrete case
`docs-site/blog/*.md` in a Docusaurus repo. Each post needs explicit `slug:` for clean URLs. gbrain rejects with `Frontmatter slug X does not match path-derived slug Y`. Repo-wide sync wants to skip the entire blog dir.

## Proposed
Either:
- **`.gbrainignore`** at repo root, gitignore syntax. Filter inside `isSyncable()` after the hardcoded checks.
- **`sync.exclude: string[]`** in gbrain config (glob patterns).

Either lets users opt-out paths without forking core.

## Version
gbrain 0.18.2
title: Feature: path exclusion for `gbrain sync` (.gbrainignore or sync.exclude)

body:
## Bug

`addLinksBatch` in both `PostgresEngine` and `PGLiteEngine` uses `unnest(::text[])` to batch-insert links. When any slug or context string contains `{`, `}`, `"`, or `,`, PostgreSQL interprets these as text[] array delimiters, producing a malformed array literal. The `unnest()` then yields an empty set and the entire batch INSERT returns zero rows — **with no error raised**.

## Impact

This is a silent data loss bug. In a CJK-heavy vault (~500 pages), approximately 60% of link rows were silently dropped during `gbrain extract links`. The link graph becomes incomplete, degrading search, backlinks, and brain-score accuracy.

Characters that trigger this:
- `{}` — extremely common in JSON snippets, code blocks, template syntax
- `"` — ubiquitous in quoted text, frontmatter, markdown
- `,` — present in nearly all natural language

Any vault with non-ASCII content or code blocks is affected.

## Reproduction

1. Create two pages with slugs containing no special characters
2. Add a link between them with a `context` field containing `{"key": "value"}`
3. Run `gbrain extract links`
4. Observe: the link is not inserted, but no error is logged

## Root Cause

`unnest($1::text[], $2::text[], ...)` requires each parameter to be a valid PostgreSQL text array literal. The postgres-js driver (and PGLite's query interface) serialize JavaScript arrays into the `{elem1,elem2,...}` format. When an element itself contains `{`, `}`, `"`, or `,`, the serialized literal becomes ambiguous and PostgreSQL silently misparses it.

## Suggested Fix

Replace `unnest(::text[])` with individual parameterized INSERTs using scalar `$N` parameters. Scalar parameters are immune to array-syntax parsing. Each link is inserted independently; a missing page only skips that single row.

Tradeoff: more round-trips per batch (N queries instead of 1), but typical batch sizes (50-200 links) complete in <100ms on local PostgreSQL.
title: addLinksBatch: unnest(::text[]) silently drops rows when content contains array-syntax characters

body:
### Summary

`put_page` fails when given a slug containing uppercase characters. The page row is created with the lowercased slug (via `validateSlug`), but the chunk-upsert step in the same transaction queries by the original mixed-case slug, returns 0 rows, and throws `Page not found: <originalSlug>`. The transaction rolls back. From the caller's perspective, a `put_page` call returns a `Page not found` error, which is misleading — the operation is a write, and the page row was created (and rolled back) inside the same transaction.

### Reproduction

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8000/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  --data '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"put_page","arguments":{"slug":"Test/Mixed-Case-Slug","content":"---\ntype: note\ntitle: t\n---\n\nbody"}}}'
```

**Expected:** page is created (and chunked) at the canonical lowercased slug.

**Actual:**

```
{"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Error: Page not found: Test/Mixed-Case-Slug"}],"isError":true},"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1}
```

A subsequent `list_pages` shows no `test/mixed-case-slug` page either — the row was created and rolled back, not persisted.

### Root cause

Inside one `importFromContent` transaction, `putPage` and `upsertChunks` handle the slug differently:

- `src/core/utils.ts:8-13` — `validateSlug(slug)` returns `slug.toLowerCase()`.
- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:131-132` — `putPage` calls `slug = validateSlug(slug)` before the INSERT.
- `src/core/postgres-engine.ts:393-399` — `upsertChunks` does **not** call `validateSlug`. It runs `SELECT id FROM pages WHERE slug = ${slug}` against the caller-supplied (mixed-case) slug.
- `src/core/import-file.ts:269-296` — `importFromContent` calls `tx.putPage(slug, ...)` and then `tx.upsertChunks(slug, chunks)` with the same `slug` variable, never lowercased at the importer level.

The same pattern exists in `src/core/pglite-engine.ts` (`putPage` validates, `upsertChunks` doesn't).

**Suggested fix**

Lowercase consistently. Three options, in order of strictness:

1. **Mirror `validateSlug` in `upsertChunks`** so it queries with the same canonical form `putPage` writes. Smallest change.
2. **Push slug-validation up into the importer/transaction wrapper** so all engine ops see the canonical form.
3. **Reject mixed-case slugs at the API surface** with `invalid_slug` instead of silently lowercasing. Catches the bug at the boundary; surfaces it loudly to clients that may be silently mutating their slugs today.

Option 1 is non-breaking and the smallest diff. Option 3 is the strictest and most useful for downstream consumers — silent slug mutation can hide real client-side bugs.

**Test sketch**

```ts
test('put_page accepts a mixed-case slug and persists chunks', async () => {
  const result = await put_page({
    slug: 'TestNamespace/Page-Name',
    content: '---\ntype: note\ntitle: t\n---\n\nbody text',
  });
  expect(result.status).not.toBe('failed');

  const page = await get_page({ slug: 'testnamespace/page-name' });
  expect(page).toBeTruthy();

  const chunks = await get_chunks({ slug: 'testnamespace/page-name' });
  expect(chunks.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
});
```

**Why this is easy to miss**

The MCP error response carries `isError: true` inside `result.content`, separate from the JSON-RPC `error` envelope. Clients that only inspect the JSON-RPC envelope treat the failure as a successful response and read the error text as data. A discardable caller (`_ = await client.put_page(...)`) sees no exception, no error code, and no log line — the silent failure surface depends entirely on whether the client checks `result.isError`.
title: `put_page` silently corrupts mixed-case slugs: page row created with lowercased slug, but `upsertChunks` queries with the original case and throws `Page not found`

body:
**What happened?**

gbrain documents three always-on conventions that are supposed to fire automatically on every Claude Code session:
1. **Signal detection on every inbound message** (`skills/signal-detector/SKILL.md`)
2. **Brain-first lookup before external APIs** (`skills/conventions/brain-first.md`)
3. **Quality conventions on every brain write** (`skills/conventions/quality.md`)

The `skills/setup/SKILL.md` Phase D ("Brain-First Lookup Protocol") and Phase E ("Load the Production Agent Guide") describe these as conventions to **inject into AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md** — i.e. as written instructions the agent is supposed to read and follow. The `INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS.md` Step 5 says "Read `~/gbrain/skills/RESOLVER.md`. Save this to your memory permanently."

But Claude Code's actual enforcement primitive is **hooks in `~/.claude/settings.json`** (UserPromptSubmit / PreToolUse / PostToolUse). The setup skill never touches settings.json. There's no `gbrain` command that writes hook entries.

So the conventions rely on the agent reading documentation and remembering to apply it — which is unreliable across sessions and across different Claude Code instances. The conventions are *documented* but not *automated*.

**What did you expect?**

A `gbrain setup-claude-code` command (or equivalent in `gbrain init`/`gbrain setup`) that writes appropriate hook entries to `~/.claude/settings.json` so the always-on conventions are enforced mechanically:

- `UserPromptSubmit` hook → capture user message via signal-detector + (optionally) inject brain-priors into context
- `PreToolUse` hook on `WebSearch`/`WebFetch`/`Agent` → run `gbrain search` against the query and inject results before the external call
- `PostToolUse` hook on `Edit`/`Write` to a brain-repo path → trigger `gbrain sync`
- `PostToolUse` hook on `mcp__gbrain__put_page` → lint freshly-written pages against `quality.md` (citations, cross-links, output-rules)

The shipped hooks could be standard scripts at `~/.gbrain/hooks/` that delegate to `gbrain` CLI commands. Users could override them by editing the scripts, and the hook entries in `settings.json` would be small one-liners (`type: command, command: ~/.gbrain/hooks/<name>.sh`).

This would close the documented-vs-enforced gap that makes "the brain compounds automatically" a *contract* rather than a *promise*.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Fresh install per `INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS.md`: clone, `bun install`, `bun link`, `gbrain init`.
2. Add gbrain MCP: `claude mcp add gbrain -- gbrain serve`.
3. Read `CLAUDE.md` (or whatever AGENTS.md is in scope) — describes always-on signal detection, brain-first lookup, quality conventions.
4. Open a Claude Code session in the project.
5. Send a message that mentions a person ("I talked to Pedro yesterday about the deal").
6. Observe: nothing automatically fires. No signal capture, no brain page update, no entity extraction. The agent might or might not handle it correctly depending on whether it remembers the convention.
7. Use `WebSearch` to research a topic the brain already has a page for.
8. Observe: agent calls WebSearch directly without first running `gbrain search`. The brain-first convention is documented but not enforced.

**Environment**

- gbrain version: `0.20.4`
- OS: macOS 26.3 (arm64) — applies to any platform where Claude Code runs
- Bun version: `1.3.13`
- Database: not engine-specific. The gap is in the install/setup layer, independent of PGLite/Postgres/Supabase.

**`gbrain doctor --json` output**

Not directly relevant — this is about the install/integration layer, not the brain database. doctor passes; the missing piece is hook wiring on the Claude Code side. (Doctor output available on request if helpful.)

---

**Workaround in use**

Built 7 hooks manually:

| Hook | Event | Convention enforced |
|---|---|---|
| `gbrain-signal-capture.sh` | UserPromptSubmit | Capture every user message + nudge for processing |
| `gbrain-brain-priors.sh` | UserPromptSubmit | Run `gbrain search` against message; inject high-relevance hits |
| `gbrain-claim-audit.sh` | UserPromptSubmit | Detects capability/effort questions; reminds Claude to verify against source |
| `gbrain-brain-first.sh` | PreToolUse on WebSearch/WebFetch/Agent | Run `gbrain search` against tool query; inject brain hits |
| `gbrain-minion-routing.sh` | PreToolUse on Agent | Read `~/.gbrain/preferences.json#minion_mode`; inject mode info |
| `gbrain-sync-after-edit.sh` | PostToolUse on Edit/Write | Auto-sync brain markdown repo |
| `gbrain-quality-lint.sh` | PostToolUse on `mcp__gbrain__put_page` | Lint for citations, cross-links, slop, placeholder dates |

Plus a shared `~/.claude/hooks/lib/gbrain-project-check.sh` that all hooks source for project-membership detection.

Each hook fires deterministically; the hook output (text injected into Claude's context) is the convention reminder. Compliance is then up to Claude, but the trigger is mechanical.

**Why this is fragile as a workaround**

- Every gbrain user has to rebuild this from scratch
- Hook scripts may drift from gbrain's evolving conventions (e.g. v0.21 might add a new always-on skill)
- No standard place for the community to share hook designs

**Proposed fix**

`gbrain setup-claude-code` (could run automatically as part of `gbrain init` or be a separate phase in `setup` skill):

1. Detects Claude Code installation (`~/.claude/settings.json` exists).
2. Writes a set of standard hook scripts to `~/.gbrain/hooks/` (e.g., `signal-capture.sh`, `brain-first.sh`, etc.) that delegate to `gbrain` CLI subcommands (`gbrain capture-signal`, `gbrain search`, etc.).
3. Adds hook entries to `~/.claude/settings.json` referencing those scripts. Backs up existing settings first.
4. Provides `gbrain setup-claude-code --uninstall` to remove cleanly.
5. (Optional) gbrain ships a `gbrain capture-signal` and `gbrain quality-lint` CLI subcommand surface that the hook scripts wrap, so users can also use them outside hooks.

The integration mirrors how `gbrain autopilot --install` already writes a launchd plist for ongoing maintenance. This would do the same for Claude Code's hook surface.

**Happy to send a PR** if this approach sounds right. Want to confirm:
- Hook script location (`~/.gbrain/hooks/` vs bundled in `~/gbrain/hooks/templates/`)
- Whether to wrap as new `gbrain` CLI subcommands (`gbrain capture-signal`, etc.) or keep the hook scripts as direct `gbrain search` / `gbrain put` callers
- Behavior on `gbrain upgrade` — should existing user-edited hook scripts be left alone or updated?
title: [Bug] Always-on conventions don't fire on Claude Code — install missing hook layer

I've used it to index the articles, blogs, social media, papers, etc.. but some of them have screenshots or images in them (architectural diagrams, code flow diagrams, algorithms, equations, etc..). Some of the emails I index do as well. 


Import pictures into brain and have some analysis done as to what's in the image, or how the article/link/email that the image(s) were embedded/attached to also correlate.

body:
### Observed

Running `gbrain dream --dry-run --json [--dir DIR]` prints a human-readable preamble to stdout before the JSON:

```
$ gbrain dream --dry-run --json --dir ~/brain | head -3
[dry-run] Would embed 0 chunks across 1953 pages
{
  "schema_version": "1",
```

The JSON starts at byte 49 (offset varies with page/chunk counts). Anything downstream that pipes stdout into `jq`, a JSON parser, or `JSON.parse()` gets a parse error on the first non-whitespace char.

Reproduces on release 0.18.2. Likely originates in `src/core/cycle.ts` embed phase (or `src/commands/embed.ts`) — a `console.log` guarded by `opts.dryRun` that didn't get the `--json` suppression treatment.

### Expected

With `--json`, stdout should be a single JSON document (or JSONL stream). All progress / human-readable output should go to stderr. This matches the documented contract in `docs/progress-events.md`:

> Progress always writes to **stderr**. Stdout stays clean for data output (`--json` payloads, final summaries, JSON action events from `extract`).

### Minimal reproducer

```bash
# Any brain with chunks that would otherwise embed.
gbrain dream --dry-run --json --dir ~/brain | python3 -c 'import sys, json; json.load(sys.stdin)'
# → json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

Compare `--dry-run` without `--json` — the preamble is fine there, that's a legitimate user-facing message.

### Suggested fix

In the embed phase (`src/core/cycle.ts:runPhaseEmbed` or wherever the `[dry-run] Would embed ...` line is emitted), gate the `console.log` on `!opts.json`, matching how the rest of the cycle phases handle it. Alternatively route the message to stderr via `process.stderr.write(...)`.

Search pattern to find the site:

```bash
rg --type ts 'Would embed' src/
```

### Context

Low severity — downstream consumers can defensively slice from the first `{` to the last `}` before parsing. Filed so future agents that pipe `gbrain dream --json` don't hit the same defensiveness requirement.
title: gbrain dream --dry-run --json prints [dry-run] preamble on stdout before JSON CycleReport

## Bug Report

### Summary
After upgrading from v0.18.2 to v0.19.0, the PGLite WASM crash (originally reported in #223) went from intermittent to **consistently reproducible** on macOS 25.3 arm64 with Bun 1.3.12. Every gbrain command that touches PGLite now fails immediately.

### Regression
- **v0.18.2:** WASM crash was intermittent — some commands (e.g. `gbrain embed --all`) initialized successfully and ran to completion
- **v0.19.0:** WASM crash is 100% consistent — no PGLite commands work at all

### Error
```
PGLite failed to initialize its WASM runtime.
  This is most commonly the macOS 26.3 WASM bug: https://github.com/garrytan/gbrain/issues/223
  Run `gbrain doctor` for a full diagnosis.
  Original error: Aborted(). Build with -sASSERTIONS for more info.
```

### Steps to Reproduce
1. macOS 25.3 (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) + Bun 1.3.12
2. Upgrade gbrain from v0.18.2 → v0.19.0 via `git pull && bun install`
3. Run any gbrain command: `gbrain embed --all`, `gbrain config show`, `gbrain stats`
4. All fail with WASM crash

### Environment
- OS: macOS Darwin 25.3.0 arm64
- Bun: 1.3.12
- gbrain: v0.19.0 (commit 78ba0b5)
- PGLite: 0.4.3

### Related
Refs #223

### Filed by
Peter Abilla (papiofficial) via OpenClaw agent
title: v0.19 regression: WASM crash now consistent on macOS 25.3 / Bun 1.3.12 (was intermittent in v0.18)
