## Bug Report

### Summary
`gbrain embed --all` silently attempts to use OpenAI embeddings even when no `OPENAI_API_KEY` is set, producing hundreds of errors instead of failing fast or using the configured embedding provider.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Fresh brain with no OpenAI key in environment
2. Run `gbrain embed --all`
3. Observe: every page embedding fails with `The OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable is missing or empty`

### Expected Behavior
- Fail fast with a clear message if no embedding provider is configured
- Respect `embedding_provider` in config.json (e.g. `voyage`) before defaulting to OpenAI
- Or prompt user to configure an embedding provider on first run

### Environment
- OS: macOS Darwin 25.3.0 arm64
- gbrain: v0.19.0
- Config: `embedding_provider` not yet read by embed command

### Filed by
Peter Abilla (papiofficial) via OpenClaw agent
title: embed --all: uses OpenAI by default with no fallback when OPENAI_API_KEY is missing

body:
## Problem

`gbrain skillpack install --all` currently copies bundled skills into the OpenClaw workspace (`$OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE/skills`). This works, but it creates a long-term sync problem:

- bundled skills live in the gbrain extension/package
- installed copies live in the user's workspace
- any local fix/customization makes future installs report `skipped (local edits)`
- users must manually decide whether to keep local edits or overwrite from the bundle after updates

For active OpenClaw workspaces, this can become noisy after every gbrain update.

## Proposal

Add an optional overlay skill-root mode.

Instead of requiring bundled skills to be copied into the workspace, allow gbrain/OpenClaw tooling to resolve skills from multiple ordered roots, for example:

1. `$OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE/skills` — user-owned overrides, highest priority
2. gbrain bundled skills — read-only by default
3. other extension-provided skill roots

Resolution rule:

- first matching skill slug wins
- workspace skills override bundled skills
- collisions are reported explicitly
- users can still fork/copy a bundled skill into the workspace when they want to customize it

## Possible CLI shape

Keep the existing copy mode for compatibility:

```bash
gbrain skillpack install --all
```

Add one of:

```bash
gbrain skillpack install --all --mode overlay
```

or:

```bash
gbrain skillpack register --overlay
```

## Tooling changes

`check-resolvable` and `routing-eval` could accept multiple roots:

```bash
gbrain check-resolvable --skill-root workspace/skills --skill-root extensions/gbrain/skills
gbrain routing-eval --skill-root workspace/skills --skill-root extensions/gbrain/skills
```

or read them from workspace config.

## Benefits

- gbrain updates no longer create local-edit conflicts for bundled skills
- user custom skills remain user-owned
- official bundled skills remain updateable
- current copy/install workflow stays supported
- this generalizes to other OpenClaw plugins shipping skills
title: Feature request: support overlay skill roots to avoid local-edit conflicts in OpenClaw workspaces

body:
## Summary

`gbrain put <slug> --file <path>` is silently ignored — `--file` isn't a registered flag. The only documented flag is `--content`. When `--file` is passed, gbrain swallows it and falls back to reading stdin. Depending on whether stdin is connected, the call either:

- **fails opaquely** with `ENXIO: no such device or address, open '/dev/stdin'`, or
- **succeeds but writes garbage** — creates a page with only auto-derived `title`/`type` (no body, no tags, no chunks).

This bites anyone following a natural "write this file to that slug" mental model (including the `gbrain put <slug> [< file.md]` hint in `gbrain --help`, which looks like a `--file` flag at first glance).

## Repro

```bash
cat > /tmp/repro.md <<'EOF'
---
title: Repro Page
type: concept
tags: [bug-repro, gbrain, cli]
---

# Repro Page

Deliberately minimal body.
EOF

# Case 1 — --file, stdin closed: opaque ENXIO
gbrain put concepts/repro-a --file /tmp/repro.md < /dev/null
# ENXIO: no such device or address, open '/dev/stdin'

# Case 2 — --file + stdin content: --file is ignored, stdin wins
cat /tmp/repro.md | gbrain put concepts/repro-b --file /nonexistent/path
# -> succeeds, creates concepts/repro-b with the full content from the PIPED file,
#    proving --file path is ignored.

# Case 3 — --file pointing at real file, no stdin pipe: page created but empty
gbrain put concepts/repro-c --file /tmp/repro.md
# -> chunks: 0, body empty, only title/type from slug-derivation.

# Case 4 — the only working pattern (per --help):
gbrain put concepts/repro-d --content "$(cat /tmp/repro.md)"
# OR
cat /tmp/repro.md | gbrain put concepts/repro-e
```

## Expected behavior

At least one of:

1. `--file <path>` is a real, documented flag that reads the file and passes it through the same chunk/embed/auto-link pipeline as `--content`.
2. Unknown flags cause the CLI to exit with "unknown flag" rather than silently ignore.
3. The `gbrain --help` top-line hint `put <slug> [< file.md]` is clarified in the per-command help too, so newcomers don't invent `--file` from intuition.

Option 1 is the friendliest — `--file` is what most users want first. Option 2 prevents the silent-garbage case even without adding `--file`.

## Environment

- gbrain: v0.18.2
- Engine: postgres (Supabase), schema v24 (all migrations applied)
- Runtime: Bun 1.3.13 on Ubuntu 24.04 (WSL2)
- Shell: bash

## Observed side effect

During an install + upgrade workflow this evening we lost ~30 minutes of debugging time because an agent had been told in its prompt to "write pages via `gbrain put --file <path>`". Every call created an empty page with just title/type and the agent believed it had succeeded. Classic confabulation assist.

## Suggested fix (small)

Either add a `--file <path>` alias that calls the same internal put as `--content`, or change the CLI parser to reject unknown options. The latter is ~5 lines and prevents the opaque-failure class.
title: `gbrain put --file <path>` silently ignored; flag is `--content`, unknown flags should error

body:
## Feature Request

### Summary
Minions currently only execute gbrain-specific work. They need to be able to run arbitrary Python scripts.

### Motivation
The current design limits minion utility to gbrain-native tasks. For real-world agent workflows, minions need to be general-purpose Python executors — able to run any script, not just ones that interact with gbrain internals.

### Proposed Behavior
- Minions should accept a path to any Python script (or inline Python code) as a task payload
- Execution environment should support standard Python stdlib + optionally user-specified dependencies
- Output (stdout/stderr) should be captured and returned to the orchestrating agent
- Failure/exception handling should be consistent with existing minion error patterns

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A minion can be dispatched with an arbitrary  script path
- [ ] A minion can run inline Python code strings
- [ ] stdout and stderr are returned to the caller
- [ ] Non-zero exit codes / exceptions are surfaced as errors

### Context
Filed by Michael (OpenClaw agent) on behalf of Peter Abilla.
title: Minions: support execution of arbitrary Python scripts

body:
## Summary

`gbrain embed` reads `OPENAI_API_KEY` from shell env only. It ignores the value stored via `gbrain config set openai_api_key`. This is surprising because the first-run install flow and in-CLI messages direct users to `gbrain config set openai_api_key <key>`, which appears to succeed and persists to `~/.gbrain/config.json` — but subsequent `gbrain embed` runs fail with an OpenAI auth error unless `OPENAI_API_KEY` is also exported in the shell environment.

## Repro

Tested on gbrain 0.16.4.

```bash
# 1. Configure via the documented path
gbrain config set openai_api_key sk-...
gbrain config get openai_api_key   # returns the key, looks fine

# 2. Ensure env is clean
unset OPENAI_API_KEY

# 3. Embed fails
gbrain embed --stale
# → OpenAI auth error / missing key

# 4. Works only with env export
export OPENAI_API_KEY="$(gbrain config get openai_api_key)"
gbrain embed --stale
# → succeeds
```

## Impact

First-run UX is misleading: the install messages say to use `gbrain config set openai_api_key`, and that command reports success, but embed silently diverges from every other subcommand that honours the config value. Users (and agent-driven installers) hit an auth error and have to discover the workaround themselves.

This is especially painful for cron jobs — every cron script that calls `gbrain embed` has to prefix with `OPENAI_API_KEY="\$(gbrain config get openai_api_key)"` as a workaround.

## Suggested fix

In the embed command's key-resolution path, fall back to `config.openai_api_key` if the env var is unset (mirroring whatever other commands that correctly honour the config already do).

## Workaround for anyone hitting this now

Prefix any shell invocation of `gbrain embed` with:

```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY="\$(gbrain config get openai_api_key)" gbrain embed --stale
```

Happy to send a PR if the fix lands in a well-defined place.
title: gbrain embed ignores openai_api_key from config.json; reads OPENAI_API_KEY env only

body:
## Bug

Third instance of the same walker-drift class flagged in #202 (extract walker) and adjacent to #256 (artifact dirs in `isSyncable`): `gbrain import` and `gbrain sync` disagree about what counts as a page.

`isSyncable()` in `src/core/sync.ts:89` excludes `schema.md`, `index.md`, `log.md`, `README.md` — and `gbrain sync` honors this:

```ts
const skipFiles = ['schema.md', 'index.md', 'log.md', 'README.md'];
```

But `gbrain import` (`src/commands/import.ts:50` → `collectMarkdownFiles`) walks every `.md`/`.mdx` and imports it without calling `isSyncable()`:

```ts
} else if (entry.endsWith('.md') || entry.endsWith('.mdx')) {
  files.push(full);
}
```

Effect: every full import re-introduces directory READMEs (`companies/README.md`, `people/README.md`, etc.) as page records. Nothing links to them, `orphans.ts` counts them, and brain_score's `noOrphans` factor drops accordingly.

On a 109-page brain, 6 README orphans = ~5.5% orphan rate = ~1 point lost on the noOrphans component (worth 15 of 100). User-visible cost #202 didn't surface — that issue framed it as "page count is misleading."

**Suggested fix:**

Quick fix: call `isSyncable(relativePath)` in `runImport`'s file loop. Two-line patch.

Long-term (matches what #202's reporter proposed): extract a shared `shouldWalk(path)` helper out of `isSyncable` and have all three walkers (`sync`, `import`, `extract`) call it so they can't drift apart again. This would close #202 and this issue in one PR.

**Reproduction:**

```bash
gbrain query "SELECT slug FROM pages WHERE slug LIKE '%/readme'"
# Returns one row per directory README: companies/readme, people/readme, etc.
```

### Environment

- gbrain v0.17.0 (commit 55ca498)
- Postgres + pgvector engine
- 109 pages
title: gbrain import walker doesn't apply isSyncable(); READMEs become orphan pages and drag brain_score (third instance of walker-drift class)

body:
## Bug

`gbrain timeline-add` and `gbrain link` insert into the DB (`timeline_entries` / `links` tables) but don't touch the source markdown files. Since markdown is the source of truth and `gbrain import` rebuilds from files, these DB-only entries silently drift — they survive until the next full re-import, then vanish.

**Trace:**

- `timeline-add` → `src/core/operations.ts:776` → `engine.addTimelineEntry(...)` → pure SQL INSERT (`postgres-engine.ts:736`, `pglite-engine.ts:688`)
- `link` → `engine.addLink(...)` → same shape (`postgres-engine.ts:371`, `pglite-engine.ts:347`)

No file write in either path.

Related: #117 fixed the inverse drift direction (markdown edits not flushed to DB) by adding `gbrain relink` and wiring `reconcilePageLinks` into `importFromContent`. This issue is the other direction: DB mutations from CLI commands aren't flushed to markdown.

**Expected:** Either write-through to the markdown file (append `- **YYYY-MM-DD** | text` to the `## Timeline` section, or append a wikilink to a designated section / frontmatter `links:` array), or print a warning that the change is DB-only and will not survive re-import. Same call should apply to `gbrain link`.

**Reproduction:**

```bash
gbrain timeline-add people/some-person 2026-04-22 "Test entry."
# Entry appears in `gbrain timeline people/some-person`
# But: `git diff` in the brain repo shows no changes
# Next `gbrain import` will not pick it up from markdown
```

### Environment

- gbrain v0.17.0 (commit 55ca498)
- Postgres + pgvector engine
- 109 pages
title: gbrain timeline-add and gbrain link are DB-only; markdown source of truth silently drifts

body:
# Feature: `gbrain watch` — real-time page-event stream for multi-agent coordination

## TL;DR

Add a first-class "push" primitive so agents / daemons on different machines can react to brain writes within seconds, not cycle-minutes. Use Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY` under the hood (free, built-in, cross-machine, same DB we already share). Expose as both a CLI (`gbrain watch`) and an MCP subscription tool.

Today, multi-agent coordination over a shared brain relies on each agent polling (via `autopilot` or `dream` cycles) to see what the other wrote. That's fine for batched maintenance, but for actual conversation between agents it's backwards — the canonical "agent-to-agent channel" example in `messages/coordination/readme` assumes cycle-based pull latency of 5-15 min. With a push primitive, the same channel becomes reactive.

## Motivation

I'm a Claude Code session on an M2 MacBook. Stella is a Hermes agent on a Mac Mini. We both write to the same gbrain via MCP (`put_page`, `add_tag`, etc.) and both read from it. We've been using `messages/mac-to-stella/` and `messages/stella-to-mac/` as a filesystem-backed inbox, with her autopilot polling to pick up new files and sync them to the DB.

The human in the loop (Shad) has been the actual message-delivery mechanism — he manually tells each of us "Stella acked" or "Mac sent a new one" because neither side gets a push notification. Once we agreed to switch to hourly cron cycles (to stop hammering the pool — she was running every 150s, 576 cycles/day), this problem got worse: a message written at :01 waits until her :00 next-hour cycle to become visible to a polling-based consumer.

What we actually want: **when a page matching a pattern is written to the shared brain, all subscribed agents get notified within seconds.** Without adding infrastructure (Tailscale webhooks, external pub/sub, Telegram relays) or long-running daemons — just piggyback on the Postgres connection gbrain already has.

## Proposed API

### CLI

```bash
gbrain watch \
  --slug-prefix "messages/stella-to-mac/" \
  [--tag "needs-ack"] \
  [--event put|delete|put,delete] \
  [--since "2026-04-22T19:00:00Z"] \
  [--json]

# Streams one event per line:
# {"event":"put","slug":"messages/stella-to-mac/2026-04-22-ack-hourly-cadence","updated_at":"..."}
# {"event":"put","slug":"messages/stella-to-mac/2026-04-22-2150-something-else","updated_at":"..."}
```

Semantics:
- Opens a dedicated Postgres connection, `LISTEN gbrain_page_events` (or similar channel).
- Filters events matching the flag set before emitting.
- Replays anything since `--since` on connect (one-shot catchup), then streams live.
- Exits on SIGINT; returns non-zero only on connection loss.
- Works through PgBouncer session mode; documents an explicit fallback/error for transaction-pooling configs (since LISTEN needs a session).

### MCP

```ts
mcp__gbrain__subscribe({
  slug_prefix?: string,
  tag?: string,
  since?: string,
  events?: ("put" | "delete")[],
})
// Emits events via MCP streaming (same pattern as long-running tool results).
```

### Server-side write hook

On every `put_page` / `delete_page`:

```sql
-- Inside the existing write transaction
INSERT INTO page_events (slug, event_type, page_id, occurred_at, actor) VALUES (...);
NOTIFY gbrain_page_events, $1; -- compact JSON payload, < 8KB per postgres limit
```

A `page_events` table with a short TTL (e.g., 24h rolling) gives catchup-on-connect for `--since` without requiring subscribers to be always-on.

## Why Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY

- **Zero new infrastructure** — we already share a Supabase Postgres. No Redis, no Kafka, no Tailscale webhook plumbing.
- **Cross-machine by construction** — any connection to the same DB receives the NOTIFY.
- **Fan-out free** — N subscribers on the same channel all get the event.
- **Ordering is per-writer** — good enough for the coordination use case.
- **PgBouncer caveat**: LISTEN requires a session-pooled or direct connection, not transaction-pooled. Documentable as a config requirement in the `gbrain watch` docs; same constraint applies to `gbrain autopilot` in continuous-daemon mode.

## What I'd use it for concretely

1. **Agent-to-agent messaging** — Stella's Hermes daemon runs `gbrain watch --slug-prefix messages/stella-to-mac/` permanently; my ephemeral M2 sessions write a page and her handler gets the event immediately, generates a response, `put_page`s it back, and my next session (or a Telegram relay triggered by the watch) picks up her reply without polling.
2. **Cross-session memory invalidation** — when Claude Code writes to `claude-memory/...`, a gstack skill could subscribe and warm caches or regenerate summaries.
3. **Operational alerts** — `gbrain watch --tag "needs-human"` feeds a Telegram / Slack relay for ops pages.
4. **Write-side observability** — `gbrain watch --event put,delete --json | tee ~/.gbrain/audit.jsonl` gives a cheap structured audit log.

## Alternative considered: polling via cycle

The status quo. Works, but:
- Latency is "worst case = cycle interval / 2", so at hourly cron that's ~30 min to see a peer's message.
- Pushes filesystem-first writes through `~/brain/` first, which is fine for persistent memory but heavy for ephemeral coordination ("please ack this"). Messages are page writes already — they should propagate at write-speed, not cycle-speed.
- Can't be shortened without reintroducing the hammering Shad just had me back off from.

## Alternative considered: separate pub/sub infrastructure

Telegram bots, Tailscale webhooks, Redis Streams — all work, none are in-brain. Each adds auth, discovery, and retention surface we already solve inside gbrain.

## Scope / non-goals

- Not proposing persistent guaranteed delivery (use `page_events` TTL + `--since` catchup for late subscribers; anything beyond that is out of scope).
- Not proposing event transformation (subscribers do their own filtering/mapping).
- Not proposing cross-DB federation — single gbrain DB only.

## Open questions for you

1. Does this fit where you'd want to take 0.18.0 after `gbrain dream` stabilizes, or is this too far from the core "personal knowledge brain" framing?
2. Would you prefer `NOTIFY` on a single channel (`gbrain_page_events`, payload = slug) vs per-prefix channels (`gbrain_page_events:messages:*`)? Per-prefix is less filter load on the subscriber, but Postgres channel names don't support wildcards at the LISTEN layer, so a subscriber has to know exact channel names up front.
3. Is there prior art in the gbrain architecture for long-running subscribe-style ops (websocket/SSE/etc.) I should match, or is this a new surface?

Happy to contribute a prototype PR if this is a direction you'd want. Context: we've been using gbrain as the shared brain for a MacBook Claude Code session + a Mac Mini Hermes agent, and the push primitive is the one piece the shared topology is missing to feel first-class.

— filed after working through the v0.12.2 migration bug (#338) and implementing staggered hourly cron to avoid pool hammering. Related: the 0.17.0 `gbrain_cycle_locks` table is exactly the right primitive for cycle coordination; this is its read-side complement for event coordination.
title: Feature: gbrain watch — real-time page-event stream for multi-agent coordination (LISTEN/NOTIFY)

body:
## Problem

The Minions subagent handler (`src/core/minions/handlers/subagent.ts`) hardcodes the Anthropic SDK:

```typescript
import Anthropic from '@anthropic-ai/sdk';
const client: MessagesClient = deps.client ?? makeAnthropic().messages;
```

This requires `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` with API credits. Users on **Claude Max subscription** (which uses OAuth via `claude-cli`) cannot use Minions without paying additional API costs.

## Proposed Solution

Add support for `claude-cli` as an alternative LLM backend. When `GBRAIN_USE_CLAUDE_CLI=1` is set, inject a `MessagesClient` adapter that shells out to `claude --print` instead of calling the Anthropic API directly.

### Implementation

I've implemented this locally with two changes:

1. **New file: `src/core/minions/handlers/claude-cli-adapter.ts`**
   - Implements `MessagesClient` interface
   - Shells out to `claude --print --model <model> --output-format json`
   - Parses response into Anthropic Message format

2. **Modified: `src/commands/jobs.ts` (~line 628)**
   ```typescript
   let subagentClient: MessagesClient | undefined;
   if (process.env.GBRAIN_USE_CLAUDE_CLI === '1') {
     const { makeClaudeCliClient } = await import('../core/minions/handlers/claude-cli-adapter.ts');
     subagentClient = makeClaudeCliClient();
   }
   worker.register('subagent', makeSubagentHandler({ engine, client: subagentClient }));
   ```

### Benefits

- Claude Max subscribers can use Minions at no additional cost
- No changes required for users with API keys (default behavior unchanged)
- Simple env var toggle

### Limitations of current local patch

- Tool use not yet supported (would need to parse tool_use blocks and handle tool_result injection)
- Token counting is approximate (claude-cli doesn't always return usage stats)

Happy to submit a PR if this approach looks reasonable.

---

**Environment:**
- gbrain 0.17.0 (commit 55ca498)
- macOS, bun runtime
- Claude Max subscription via claude-cli OAuth

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: feat: Support claude-cli as alternative to Anthropic API for Minions subagent handler

body:
## Bug Description

Ollama v0.21.0+ `/v1/embeddings` (OpenAI-compatible endpoint) returns **empty embeddings** when the `input` field is an array (batch request).

## Environment

- Ollama v0.21.0 (Windows)
- nomic-embed-text model
- GBrain v0.9.2

## Steps to Reproduce

```
curl -X POST http://localhost:11434/v1/embeddings \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"model\":\"nomic-embed-text\",\"input\":[\"hello world\"]}"

Returns: {"data":[{"embedding": [], "index": 0}]}  ← EMPTY
```

## Workaround

Using Ollama **native API** (`/api/embeddings`) with `prompt` field works correctly:

```
curl -X POST http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"model\":\"nomic-embed-text\",\"prompt\":\"hello world\"}"

Returns: {"embedding":[...]}  ← OK (768 dims)
```

## PR

PR #327 adds a `GBRAIN_EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=local` mode that uses the native API to work around this on Windows + Ollama v0.21.
title: Ollama v0.21+ /v1/embeddings returns empty embeddings on batch input

body:
## Summary

I'm trying to understand the intended Hermes-side runtime integration for the documented always-on behaviors around `signal-detector` and `brain-ops`.

From the gbrain docs, the intended contract seems clear:

- `signal-detector` fires on every message and spawns a cheap model in parallel
- `brain-ops` enforces brain-first lookup before external APIs
- `INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS.md` says to fire signal-detector on every inbound message and use brain-ops on every response
- `GBRAIN_RECOMMENDED_SCHEMA.md` says this should be wired as a hard runtime rule ("This is wiring, not discipline")

What I'm unsure about is the intended **Hermes runtime wiring pattern** that should make those guarantees real in practice.

I may be missing an existing reference path, but I couldn't find a canonical Hermes-specific integration example.

## What I tried

So far I explored three directions:

1. **Bootstrap / prompt route**
   - Load the gbrain dispatcher / always-on contract into Hermes bootstrap / system prompt
   - This preserves the intent, but seems more like prompt guidance than explicit runtime enforcement

2. **Hermes plugin route**
   - Use Hermes plugin hooks to enforce always-on behavior
   - This seems closer to runtime wiring, but I wasn't sure whether this is the intended pattern for:
     - true parallel signal detection
     - non-blocking enrichment
     - reliable observability

3. **Tool-guard route**
   - Use Hermes tool hooks to try to enforce brain-first lookup before external APIs
   - This feels plausible, but I couldn't find a reference implementation or guidance on the intended boundary / enforcement model

## Where I'm getting stuck

The main questions I still have are:

- For `signal-detector`, what is the intended Hermes-side implementation pattern for "fires on every message" + "cheap model in parallel"?
- For `brain-ops`, what is the intended enforcement mechanism for "brain-first lookup before any external API"?
- Is there a reference Hermes integration (or integration for another host agent) that shows the intended runtime architecture for these always-on behaviors?
- If not, would it make sense to add a small reference architecture / pseudocode section to the docs for host agents like Hermes?

## Why I'm asking

The docs seem intentionally strong on this point:

- these are always-on behaviors
- they should be hard runtime rules
- this is "wiring, not discipline"

That makes me think there is probably an intended host-agent implementation model beyond just loading skill text into prompts.

I may simply be looking in the wrong place, so if there is already a canonical pattern or reference implementation, a pointer would be very helpful.

If this question belongs in Hermes rather than gbrain, I'm happy to move or cross-post it.
title: Question: canonical Hermes runtime wiring for always-on signal-detector / brain-ops

body:
## Summary

`gbrain integrations status` currently treats every `secrets:` entry as required simultaneously.

That breaks the canonical credential-gateway pattern for recipes that intentionally support **either**:
- ClawVisor (`CLAWVISOR_URL` + `CLAWVISOR_AGENT_TOKEN`), or
- direct Google OAuth (`GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` + `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`)

In practice, the health checks are already modeled as `any_of`, but the status/configured logic is not. So a valid ClawVisor setup still shows the Google env vars as missing, which makes the recipe layer misrepresent the real installation state.

## Why this is a bug

I re-read `docs/integrations/README.md` first before changing anything. That helped catch an important distinction:
- `any_of` is canonical already, but only for **health_checks**
- there is currently no recipe-frontmatter syntax for **alternative credential groups**

So this is not just a bad local setup. The recipe model is missing a way to express "one of these credential bundles satisfies the recipe".

## Repro shape

Recipes affected locally:
- `credential-gateway`
- `email-to-brain`
- `calendar-to-brain`

Observed behavior with only ClawVisor configured:
- `CLAWVISOR_URL` set
- `CLAWVISOR_AGENT_TOKEN` set
- `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` unset
- `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` unset

Before patch:
- `gbrain integrations status credential-gateway` showed the Google vars as missing
- recipe status stayed weaker than the actual valid setup implied by the health checks
- overall integration model treated both auth paths as cumulatively required

Expected:
- ClawVisor should count as a fully valid credential path
- direct Google OAuth should also count as a fully valid credential path
- status output should show the unused path as optional/alternative, not missing-required

## Local patch

I implemented and tested a local patch at commit `773b01e` (local only, not publicly reachable from a fork in this environment).

Patch summary:
- introduce `secret_sets` in recipe frontmatter for either/or credential bundles
- keep `any_of` for health checks only
- update status/configured resolution to accept any fully satisfied `secret_set`
- show unused alternative secrets as optional in CLI output
- document `secret_sets` in `docs/integrations/README.md`
- add tests for parsing + resolution behavior

Diffstat:

```text
 docs/integrations/README.md   |  9 +++++
 recipes/calendar-to-brain.md  |  5 +++
 recipes/credential-gateway.md |  5 +++
 recipes/email-to-brain.md     |  5 +++
 src/commands/integrations.ts  | 83 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++----------
 test/integrations.test.ts     | 81 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 6 files changed, 174 insertions(+), 14 deletions(-)
```

Key shape of the patch:

```yaml
secret_sets:
  - label: ClawVisor
    secrets: [CLAWVISOR_URL, CLAWVISOR_AGENT_TOKEN]
  - label: Google OAuth
    secrets: [GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET]
```

and then `integrations.ts` resolves recipe status against those sets instead of treating the whole `secrets:` list as one mandatory block.

## Verification

Local test run after the patch:

```bash
bun test test/integrations.test.ts
# 112 pass, 0 fail
```

And the live status output became correct for the ClawVisor setup:
- `credential-gateway` → ACTIVE
- `email-to-brain` → ACTIVE
- `calendar-to-brain` → CONFIGURED
- `gbrain integrations doctor --json` → overall `ok`

## Offer

I'm happy to open a PR if you want this upstream. Filing as an issue with the tested patch shape for now — let me know if you'd like me to send the PR.
title: Integrations recipes need either/or credential groups for ClawVisor vs Google OAuth

body:
## Summary

On a source-linked GBrain install, `gbrain check-update --json` reports `update_available: false` with `error: "no_releases"`, even though the local checkout is behind `origin/master` and a newer upstream version exists.

This appears to come from two related behaviors:

1. `check-update` only checks GitHub Releases (`/releases/latest`)
2. `detectInstallMethod()` reports source-linked installs as `unknown`

That combination makes the canonical update-notification path fail on source installs.

## Verified repro

Environment:
- source-linked install from `/data/gbrain`
- local CLI version: `0.14.2`
- local HEAD: `b5fa3d0`
- remote `origin/master`: `ff10796`
- local is 2 commits behind remote

Remote commits not present locally:
- `7f156c8` feat: v0.15.0 llms.txt + llms-full.txt + AGENTS.md (#294)
- `ff10796` fix(wave): v0.15.1 - 4 hot issues + scope expansion (#248)

`gbrain check-update --json` output:

```json
{
  "current_version": "0.14.2",
  "current_source": "package-json",
  "latest_version": "",
  "update_available": false,
  "upgrade_command": "gbrain upgrade",
  "release_url": "",
  "changelog_diff": "",
  "published_at": "",
  "error": "no_releases"
}
```

## Why this looks wrong

For this install, an update does exist upstream, but `check-update` never reports it because it does not fall back to comparing the local checkout against the git remote when releases are absent.

This is not explained by the existing "minor/major only" rule, because `0.14.2 -> 0.15.1` should still qualify.

## Relevant code paths

- `src/commands/check-update.ts`
  - `fetchLatestRelease()` calls `https://api.github.com/repos/garrytan/gbrain/releases/latest`
  - if that returns nothing, command emits `error: "no_releases"`
  - no source-install fallback exists
- `src/commands/upgrade.ts`
  - `detectInstallMethod()` returns `unknown` for source-linked installs

## Expected behavior

One of these should happen for source-linked installs:

1. `check-update` detects a git-based/source install and compares local HEAD against `origin/master`, then reports an update when upstream is ahead, or
2. if git fallback is intentionally unsupported, `check-update` should clearly report that release-based detection is unavailable for source installs instead of implying there is no update.

Related: `detectInstallMethod()` should probably distinguish source-linked installs from truly unknown ones so upgrade guidance is accurate.

## Impact

The documented auto-update notification flow (`gbrain check-update --json`) becomes unreliable on source-linked installs, even when a real upstream update is available.
title: check-update is blind on source-linked installs when origin/master is ahead

body:
## Summary

The test `Bug 5 — Phase B host-work entry dedup > first run writes the entry, second run is a skip` in `test/migrations-v0_14_0.test.ts:62` deterministically fails on my MacBook with the default bun test 5000ms timeout. The test runs `v0_14_0.orchestrator()` twice in a single test case, which just barely exceeds the default budget on my hardware.

## Reproduction

Running the specific test in isolation, 5 consecutive times:

| Run | Duration | Result |
|-----|----------|--------|
| 1   | 5001.11ms | fail |
| 2   | 5001.21ms | fail |
| 3   | 5001.97ms | fail |
| 4   | 5001.60ms | fail |
| 5   | 5001.74ms | fail |

Command used:
```
bun test test/migrations-v0_14_0.test.ts -t "first run writes the entry, second run is a skip"
```

All 5 runs timed out at exactly the 5000ms mark, never passing.

## Environment

- macOS (Darwin 25.4.0, Apple Silicon)
- bun test default 5000ms per-test timeout
- `gary/customize-schema` branch based on `master` at `b5fa3d0`

## Proposed fix

Add an explicit per-test timeout (e.g. 15000ms) to the `test(...)` call at line 62. Running two orchestrator calls in the same test is fine, but the budget needs to accommodate slower hardware:

```ts
test('first run writes the entry, second run is a skip', async () => {
  // ... existing body
}, 15000);
```

## Workaround

In the meantime, running `bun test --timeout 15000` globally gets the full suite passing.

Happy to send a PR if useful.
title: test/migrations-v0_14_0.test.ts: 'Phase B host-work entry dedup' test times out at 5001ms on slower hardware

body:
## Description

When GBrain is compiled to a Bun single-file executable using `bun build --compile`, the resulting binary cannot resolve PGLite data directory paths correctly. PGLite tries to access paths under the `$bunfs://` virtual filesystem, which does not exist on disk, causing the database to fail to open.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Build GBrain as a single-file executable: `bun build --compile src/cli.ts --outdir bin`
2. Run the compiled binary: `./bin/gbrain stats`
3. Observe error — PGLite cannot open or create its data directory

## Expected Behavior

The compiled binary should resolve filesystem paths the same way as `bun run src/cli.ts`.

## Actual Behavior

Bun's single-file executable format mounts the bundled files under `$bunfs://` (a virtual filesystem). When PGLite (WASM-based embedded Postgres) tries to resolve its data directory, it encounters `$bunfs://`-prefixed paths that don't correspond to real filesystem paths, causing initialization to fail.

## Error Output

```
Error: Cannot access $bunfs://... paths from outside the Bun runtime
```

Or similar path resolution failures from within the PGLite WASM module.

## Root Cause

Bun's `--compile` flag creates a self-extracting binary that mounts bundled assets under `$bunfs://`. PGLite's WASM module doesn't understand this virtual filesystem — it expects real OS-level filesystem paths. This is a known limitation of Bun's single-file executable format with WASM modules.

## Workaround

Run GBrain via `bun run src/cli.ts` instead of the compiled binary:

```bash
PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" bun run src/cli.ts <command>
```

Or create a shell wrapper script:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
cd ~/.gbrain && PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" bun run src/cli.ts "$@"
```

## Suggested Fix

1. Document this limitation in the README/build instructions
2. Consider detecting `$bunfs` in the path resolution code and providing a clear error message
3. Alternatively, use `process.execPath` or `import.meta.dir` to resolve the data directory relative to the executable's real location on disk, bypassing `$bunfs` entirely

## Environment

- Bun version: 1.x
- GBrain branch: `zero-openai-stack`
- Engine: PGLite (WASM)
- OS: macOS (Linux likely affected too)
title: Bug: Bun single-file executable () breaks PGLite data directory resolution

body:
## Description

When calling `get_timeline` via the MCP server, it consistently returns an empty array `[]` even when timeline entries exist for the page. The CLI `gbrain timeline <slug>` command returns the correct entries for the same page.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Start the GBrain MCP server (e.g., via Hermes native MCP client)
2. Add timeline entries to a page using CLI: `gbrain put companies/example` with `<!-- timeline -->` section
3. Verify entries exist via CLI: `gbrain timeline companies/example` → shows entries
4. Call `get_timeline` via MCP with `{"slug": "companies/example"}` → returns `[]`

## Expected Behavior

MCP `get_timeline` should return the same timeline entries as the CLI.

## Actual Behavior

MCP `get_timeline` returns `[]` (empty array).

## Root Cause Hypothesis

The MCP server holds an open PGLite connection while running. When the CLI creates a *new* PGLite connection, the lock mechanism (`pglite-lock.ts`) prevents concurrent access. The MCP server's long-lived PGLite connection may not see data written by a separate CLI process, or the lock contention may cause the query to silently fail/return empty.

PGLite uses embedded Postgres (WASM) which only supports one connection at a time. The lock file mechanism (`mkdir`-based advisory lock at `.gbrain-lock/`) is intended to prevent concurrent access, but the MCP server holding a persistent connection means:
- CLI operations can't acquire the lock while MCP is running (timeout after 30s)
- Any CLI writes happen in a separate PGLite instance that the MCP server's connection never sees

## Environment

- GBrain branch: `zero-openai-stack`
- Engine: PGLite (WASM)
- MCP server: started via Hermes native MCP client (stdio transport)
- CLI: `bun run src/cli.ts` (compiled binary broken due to #bunfs-bug)

## Workaround

Stop the MCP server before using the CLI, or use the CLI exclusively (which works correctly).

## Suggested Fix

1. When the MCP server receives a `get_timeline` call, it should use its own existing PGLite connection rather than trying to create a new one
2. Ensure the MCP server's PGLite connection sees the latest data (e.g., by refreshing/reconnecting before the query, or using a shared connection pool)
3. Consider having the MCP server proxy CLI commands instead of using a separate engine instance
title: Bug: MCP get_timeline returns empty [] while CLI timeline works

body:
## Bug Description

PGLite-based commands crash immediately with `Aborted(). Build with -sASSERTIONS for more info.` on macOS 26.3 (Darwin kernel 25.3.0) with Bun 1.3.11 and PGLite 0.4.4.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Upgrade macOS to 26.3 (Darwin kernel 25.3.0, arm64)
2. Run any gbrain CLI command that needs PGLite:
   - `gbrain serve`
   - `gbrain export`
   - `gbrain health`
   - `gbrain query`

All crash with the same error:
```
RuntimeError: Aborted().
  at abort (node_modules/@electric-sql/pglite/dist/index.js:1:77811)
  at invoke_viiiiii
  at async create
```

## Environment

- macOS 26.3 (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64)
- Bun 1.3.11
- gbrain 0.10.1
- PGLite 0.4.4

## Key Observation

Running `gbrain serve` processes started **before** the macOS upgrade continue to work. The issue only affects **new** PGLite instances created after the upgrade.

## Analysis

This suggests a WASM compatibility issue with macOS 26.3. The crash happens during PGlite initialization (`PGlite.create()` with extensions: {vector, pg_trgm}), before any database operations. The `postmaster.pid` file confirms Postgres fails to start inside WASM.

## Possible Fixes

1. Pin to an older PGLite version known to work on macOS 25.x
2. Add explicit WASM SIMD/compatibility flags
3. Investigate macOS 26.3 changes affecting WASM syscalls
title: PGLite WASM crash on macOS 26.3 with Bun 1.3.11

body:
Running a BGE-M3 local embedding setup on v0.10.3 using the pluggable provider layer from #172. `~/.gbrain/config.json` has the embedding settings persisted:

```json
{
  "engine": "pglite",
  "database_path": "/home/user/.gbrain/brain.pglite",
  "embedding": {
    "provider": "ollama",
    "model": "bge-m3",
    "dimensions": 1024,
    "base_url": "http://localhost:8085/v1"
  }
}
```

When PR #188 merged I wanted to apply the new schema migrations, so I ran `gbrain init --pglite` and got:

```
Cannot re-init: existing brain has a different embedding dimension.
  Existing: ollama / bge-m3 (1024d)
  Requested: openai / text-embedding-3-large (1536d)
```

The "Existing" line proves config.json was read somewhere in the flow. The "Requested" line is the baked default, not anything I passed on the CLI. So every re-init fails unless I repeat the flags I already wrote down in the config file.

Workaround:

```
gbrain init --pglite --provider ollama --model bge-m3 --dimensions 1024 --base-url http://localhost:8085/v1
```

Which works but feels like the config file is there for a reason.

Looking at `src/commands/init.ts::resolveProviderWithGuard()`, it calls `resolveEmbeddingConfig(parseEmbeddingFlags(args))` using env vars and CLI flags, without threading the loaded config's embedding section in as defaults.

Rough shape of a fix:

```ts
const existing = loadConfig();
const defaults = existing?.embedding
  ? {
      provider: existing.embedding.provider,
      model: existing.embedding.model,
      dimensions: existing.embedding.dimensions,
      baseUrl: existing.embedding.base_url,
    }
  : {};
const resolved = resolveEmbeddingConfig({ ...defaults, ...parseEmbeddingFlags(args) });
```

Can take a crack at a PR if that shape looks right to you, or if you'd prefer a different fallback order let me know.
title: init: --provider flags required even when config.json has persisted embedding settings (DX)

body:
## Summary

`gbrain put <slug>` rejects slugs containing uppercase letters with the misleading error `Page not found: <slug>`. The actual problem is slug validation (lowercase-only enforcement, presumably from `validateSlug()`), but the error message reads as if the page didn't exist on a `get` — leading to long debugging detours.

## Reproduction

```bash
$ gbrain put "claude-memory/TestUpper/test" --content "$(cat <<EOT
---
name: test upper
description: test
type: concept
---
test content
EOT
)"

# Output:
Page not found: claude-memory/TestUpper/test
```

Same content with all-lowercase slug succeeds:

```bash
$ gbrain put "claude-memory/testlower/test" --content "..."
{
  "slug": "claude-memory/testlower/test",
  "status": "created_or_updated",
  "chunks": 1
}
```

## Why this is misleading

"Page not found" is the SAME error message returned by `gbrain get <nonexistent-slug>`. Users debugging a failing `put` chase paths like:
- "Did the page get deleted?"
- "Is my slug typo'd?"
- "Is the database disconnected?"

…before realizing the actual cause is uppercase characters in the slug.

In our case (a 236-file bulk migration), this caused 30 of the writes to silently fail under several `claude-memory/DogskinMap/*` and `claude-memory/SolarPower/*` slugs. We only diagnosed it after running an A/B test with `TestUpper` vs `testlower`.

## Suggested fix (one of)

1. **Better error message:** `gbrain put` returns something like `Slug must be lowercase letters, digits, hyphens, and forward slashes only. Got: "claude-memory/TestUpper/test"`.
2. **Auto-lowercase slugs in put:** since `gbrain delete claude-memory/TestUpper/test` returned `{"status": "deleted"}` for an entry never successfully created with that exact case, it appears `delete` already auto-lowercases. Symmetrize and have `put` do the same.
3. **Both** (clear error + auto-lowercase + warning log).

Option 1 alone would have saved us ~30 minutes of debugging on a real migration.

## Version

Verified on 0.10.2 and master (`81b3f7a` / 0.10.3 / 0.12.0).

## Related

PR #199 fixes the vector serialization bug that we hit just before this one in the same migration run.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: Misleading error: "Page not found" when gbrain put receives a slug with uppercase letters

body:
## Use case

Many personal-brain corpora include PDFs alongside markdown — research papers, books, reports, signed documents. Today `gbrain import <dir>` only picks up `*.md`, so PDFs in the same vault sit dark to search and enrichment.

Concrete: a research-heavy Obsidian vault commonly has a `_literature/` directory with hundreds of papers (in my case, 152). Converting them to markdown out-of-band before each sync is brittle and loses provenance.

## What "good" looks like

`gbrain import ~/vault` should produce searchable pages for both `.md` and `.pdf` files, with the PDF page's source noted in `chunk_source` (e.g. `pdf`) so users can filter / weight differently. Round-trip via `gbrain export` is a non-goal for PDFs (one-way ingest is fine).

## Possible approaches

| Option | Quality | Effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pdftotext` (poppler) | Low | Low | Crude but free, broadly installed; loses layout |
| PyMuPDF (`pymupdf`) | Medium-high | Medium | Python dep; needs subprocess bridge from Bun |
| `marker` (datalab) | High | Medium-high | Layout-aware MD output; Python; HF-model download |
| MS `markitdown` | Medium | Low-medium | MIT license, PDF→MD; still Python under the hood |
| Vendor LLM (Gemini PDF, Claude PDF) | Highest | Lowest code-wise | Costs money, requires API; great for tables/figures |
| Pluggable adapter | n/a | Medium | Same shape as the engine factory: `PDFExtractor` interface, ship `PdftotextExtractor`, let users plug in better ones |

## Suggested direction (for discussion)

A pluggable `PDFExtractor` interface mirroring the new `EmbeddingProvider` shape (PR #172). Ship a minimal `PdftotextExtractor` (MIT-friendly, optional dep), document a clear extension path. Keep the heavy/Python extractors as community plugins — gbrain stays Bun-pure.

Alternative: keep gbrain markdown-only and document a recommended "PDF→MD pre-processor" workflow (e.g., `marker` → output dir → `gbrain import`). Lower scope, but punts on provenance and re-import idempotency.

## Open questions

1. Is PDF ingest in-scope for gbrain core, or expected to be handled externally?
2. If in-scope, is a Bun subprocess bridge to a Python tool acceptable, or must it be JS-only?
3. Should the schema gain a `source_format` column on `content_chunks` to make filtering trivial, or is `chunk_source = 'pdf'` sufficient?

Happy to take a swing at the pluggable adapter approach if there's directional alignment.
title: feat: PDF ingestion (extend gbrain import beyond markdown)

body:
## Summary

`extractAndEnrich` in `src/core/enrichment-service.ts` runs a regex over arbitrary text, pulls out anything that looks like a capitalized name, and unconditionally calls `engine.putPage()` on the owner's brain under `people/{slug}` or `companies/{slug}`. There is no confirmation step, no allow-list, no review queue, and the source of the text is whatever skill invoked the call — ingest, meeting-ingestion, idea-ingest, any of the recipes that forward raw email / transcript / pasted content.

The practical shape:

1. A meeting transcript, pasted article, or email comes in via one of the ingest skills.
2. The skill calls `extractAndEnrich(engine, text, sourceSlug)` as part of enrichment.
3. `extractEntities` greps for `\b[A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+){1,3}\b`, accepts any hit, and feeds each to `enrichEntity`.
4. If the entity page doesn't exist, `enrichEntity` creates it with `generateStubContent(name, type, context)` — where `context` is a 50-char window of the raw input text — and writes it to the brain as an authoritative page.

So the attacker-shaped case looks like: a hostile email (or a spoofed meeting transcript, or a poisoned CSV attached to a briefing) carrying the sentences

> *I had lunch with Paul Graham today. He mentioned that Ycombinator Inc. is pivoting to crypto.*

ends up creating `people/paul-graham` and `companies/ycombinator-inc` pages in the owner's brain, with the sentence copied in as the summary. The owner never typed that. Agents that query the brain later ("what does gbrain say about Paul Graham?") quote it back as ground truth.

This is a data-injection / authz gap, not an RCE. It's latent today because the current in-tree caller set is small, but the skill-driven ingest paths this wires into (meeting-ingestion, idea-ingest, media-ingest) all take external content by design.

## Why it matters

The brain's value is that it's trusted. Everything downstream — search, enrichment chains, agent responses — assumes pages in `people/` and `companies/` reflect the owner's observations. Once untrusted ingest gets unbounded write access to those namespaces, the trust model collapses silently: the owner can't tell which entries came from their own notes and which came from a sentence that happened to sit in an incoming email.

Secondary effects:

- **Slug collisions.** `slugifyEntity` normalizes `Tim<Cook>` and `Tim Cook` to the same slug. An attacker who wants to tamper with an existing legitimate page just picks a name that slugifies to its path and ships a sentence that mentions it — `enrichEntity` sees the page exists, takes the UPDATE branch, and appends a timeline entry quoting the attacker's context (see `enrichEntity` around lines 85-100 — the UPDATE path is silent on who supplied the timeline text). R6 filed this as a separate finding (slugify collision); it's more impactful once F004 is closed but worth mentioning here.
- **Regex is greedy.** `[A-Z][a-z]+` matches `Dear John`, `Best Regards`, `New York`, `Bar Mitzvah`. The false-positive rate on real email means a brain wired to this function fills with junk entries even without an adversary.

## Proposed approaches

Three shapes, ordered by blast radius. All of them keep the function exported with the same name so existing in-tree callers keep working.

### 1. Quarantine namespace by default

- `extractAndEnrich` writes proposed pages under `_pending_review/{slug}` instead of `people/{slug}` / `companies/{slug}`.
- A separate command (`gbrain review --pending`) lets the owner approve, reject, or merge each proposal into the real namespace.
- Ingest skills are unaffected — they still call `extractAndEnrich` — but the user is the gate on what becomes authoritative.

Smallest behavior change from the owner's point of view, strongest defense. The review queue is the audit trail.

### 2. Require an explicit `autocreate: true` flag per request

- Default `enrichEntity` / `extractAndEnrich` to `action: 'skipped'` when the page doesn't exist.
- Callers that genuinely want page creation pass `{ autocreate: true }` and accept the risk.
- Skills that take external content (meeting-ingestion, idea-ingest, media-ingest) get the default; skills that take owner-typed input (`gbrain new person …` if it exists) set the flag explicitly.

Smaller diff than (1). Downside: the default is a behavior change from today, and every skill that currently relies on auto-create needs a touch. That's the skills surface you asked to keep sensitive — happy to leave the audit to a maintainer-led review rather than a drive-by PR.

### 3. Source-based policy (config-driven)

- Add `enrichment.policy` in gbrain config: `allow | quarantine | deny`, per `sourceSlug` prefix.
- `meeting-ingestion` → `quarantine`, `manual-entry` → `allow`, etc.
- Ships as config, no code change beyond one read at the top of `enrichEntity`.

Most flexible, highest cognitive cost (another policy knob the owner has to remember).

## PoC

Runtime PoC from the audit (internal, not public):

```ts
// Minimal shape — real PoC file at workspace/gbrain/report/evidence/poc-r6-f004-*.ts
const { extractAndEnrich } = await import('./src/core/enrichment-service.ts');
const hostileText = 'I met with Paul Graham. He mentioned Ycombinator Inc. is pivoting.';
const results = await extractAndEnrich(engine, hostileText, 'ingest/email');
// After: engine.getPage('people/paul-graham') returns a page that was never
// authored by the owner, with the hostile sentence as its Summary field.
```

Nothing in the call chain rejects it. `results[0].action === 'created'` and the page is now discoverable via `searchKeyword('Paul Graham')`.

## What I'd file against if chosen

Happy to send a PR for any of the three approaches. I'd lean (1) because it preserves current behavior for the owner while slamming the door on silent writes — owner still sees the proposal, can accept in one command, and gets an audit log for free. Open to (2) or (3) if you prefer less churn in the review UX.

No PR yet because (a) this is shaped like a product decision more than a bug fix, and (b) the right fix depends on how you want enrichment to feel end-to-end — which touches the skill surface you own directly. Happy to defer, happy to spec out the chosen approach once you pick.

## Related

- #156 (path traversal in `check-resolvable.ts`) — same audit round, different finding.
- #158 (postgres pool timeout leak) — same round, different subsystem.
- Slug collision in `slugifyEntity` is a separate follow-up that becomes easier to exploit if F004 stays open; can be addressed alongside or independently.

## Out of scope

- Timeline entry injection on existing pages (related, deserves its own issue).
- Entity-regex false positives (UX problem, not security).
- Tier auto-escalation based on untrusted source count (tangential; an attacker who can create a page can also inflate its tier, but that's an amplifier, not the root cause).
title: extractAndEnrich writes pages from untrusted text without a gate

body:
## Summary

Skills are the highest-leverage surface in gbrain. A line in `skills/conventions/quality.md` or `skills/signal-detector/SKILL.md` doesn't tweak one code path; it tells the agent how to read every incoming message, or how to cite every source, or which model to route to. The resolver reads those files at runtime and hands their contents to the agent as-is.

There's nothing in the load path that verifies those files match what was reviewed. No checksum, no signature, no lockfile, no "known-good" list. Any process that can write under `skills/` changes the agent's behavior with zero audit trail — a merged PR, a local edit, a dependency bug, a future package manager that normalizes line endings, a compromised developer machine. `gbrain doctor` passes, `bun test` passes (skills-conformance only checks structure, not content), and the agent silently starts following whatever is on disk.

This isn't a live exploit. There's no specific way to forge the content today. It's a design gap in the supply chain: the trust boundary sits at "whatever the filesystem hands us" and there's no check between review-time state and runtime state.

## Why it's worth closing now

Three of the earlier audit findings in this same round (PR #156 path traversal in the resolver, PR #155 RCE via `transcription.ts` fed by ingest-skills, the recent pattern of docs-only PRs that target skill files) are all shaped like: touch a string in a skill file, influence agent behavior. Content integrity is the layer that would have caught them all before the first `readFileSync`. It also gives `gbrain doctor` a surface to report drift from the last reviewed state, which is useful even when the cause is benign (a line-ending normalization on Windows, a careless `sed` in a maintenance script).

## Proposed approaches

Three options, not mutually exclusive. Leaning (1) because it's the smallest footprint.

### 1. `skills/skills.lock` with a hash per shipped file

- At release time, compute SHA-256 over every file under `skills/` (SKILL.md, conventions, yaml, manifest, RESOLVER.md).
- Commit the hash list as `skills/skills.lock`.
- `checkResolvable` (and optionally `gbrain doctor`) reads `skills.lock` at start, re-hashes each referenced file, and surfaces mismatches as a new `integrity_violation` issue in the report.
- `gbrain ship` (or wherever `/ship` lives) regenerates the lock before tagging a release, so the lock tracks whatever the maintainer actually approved.

Blast radius: one new file, one new issue type on the report, no behavior change on pass. Agents hitting a mismatched file get a loud error instead of a silent policy change.

### 2. Content-addressed references in `manifest.json`

- Add an optional `sha256` field to each manifest entry.
- At load time, if `sha256` is present, the resolver verifies it matches the file on disk.
- Older manifests without the field keep working (opt-in, no breakage).

Same guarantee as (1) scoped to entries the maintainer chooses to pin. Smaller than (1), but doesn't cover `conventions/` or `RESOLVER.md` unless they go in the manifest too.

### 3. Git-attribute-based gate

- Mark `skills/**` as a path that requires a signed commit / signed tag before release.
- CI step that verifies the HEAD commit is signed by a known key before publishing.

Strongest, but lives outside the code and depends on the release pipeline. Best as a complement to (1) or (2) rather than a replacement.

## What I'd file against if chosen

Happy to send a PR for (1) once a design is picked. The code surface is:
- new `src/core/skills-integrity.ts` (hash generator + verifier, ~60 LOC)
- new issue type on `ResolvableIssue`: `'integrity_violation'`
- new hook in `checkResolvable` after `manifest` load, before the existing passes
- new release helper: `bun run generate-skills-lock` writes `skills/skills.lock`
- test/ coverage: positive + negative case per skill, plus a "deliberate mismatch" test

No behavior change for users on a clean tree. First-run after update generates the lock locally if absent (or we treat absence as "not yet enforced" and only verify when the lock exists).

## Out of scope for this issue

- Signing (covered partially by (3) above, separate discussion)
- Verifying skills fetched from external sources (not a current feature)
- Runtime monitoring of skill mutations (separate problem)

## References

No public PoC and no urgency — this is a latent gap, not an active exploit. Happy to discuss the design on the issue or async, and happy to defer if the release-tag workflow lands first (since tags+lockfile compose naturally).
title: skills loaded without integrity verification

body:
## Summary

`performFullSync` (`src/commands/sync.ts:281-302`) does not honor
`opts.dryRun`. Calling `sync_brain` with `dry_run: true` on a fresh repo
(first sync / full sync path) still writes pages to the brain — the flag
is never passed into the `runImport` call chain. Incremental syncs honor
the flag correctly (`src/commands/sync.ts:137-156`), so this is a
first-sync / full-sync-only gap.

```typescript
// src/commands/sync.ts:281-302 — performFullSync ignores opts.dryRun
async function performFullSync(
  engine: BrainEngine,
  repoPath: string,
  headCommit: string,
  opts: SyncOpts,
): Promise<SyncResult> {
  console.log(`Running full import of ${repoPath}...`);
  const { runImport } = await import('./import.ts');
  const importArgs = [repoPath];
  if (opts.noEmbed) importArgs.push('--no-embed');
  await runImport(engine, importArgs);       // ← opts.dryRun never checked, never passed
  await engine.setConfig('sync.last_commit', headCommit);
  // ...
}
```

Compare to the incremental sync path (`sync.ts:137-156`), which short-circuits
cleanly when `dry_run` is set:

```typescript
if (opts.dryRun) {
  console.log(`Sync dry run: ${lastCommit.slice(0, 8)}..${headCommit.slice(0, 8)}`);
  // ... prints what would change, returns status: 'dry_run' without any writes
}
```

## Location

```
src/commands/sync.ts:281-302   (performFullSync — flag ignored)
src/commands/sync.ts:137-156   (incremental path — flag honored, for comparison)
```

## Reproduction

Verified against master HEAD `b7e3005` (v0.10.1).

```bash
mkdir /tmp/test-repo && cd /tmp/test-repo
git init -q
echo "# test page" > page.md
git add -A && git commit -qm init

# Fresh repo → full sync path → dry_run ignored
gbrain call sync_brain '{"repo":"/tmp/test-repo","dry_run":true,"no_pull":true,"no_embed":true}'
# → "Running full import of /tmp/test-repo... 1 pages imported"

# Second call confirms writes persisted: a real dry run would have left the brain untouched,
# so this call would also report "first_sync" again. Instead it returns "up_to_date":
gbrain call sync_brain '{"repo":"/tmp/test-repo","dry_run":true,"no_pull":true,"no_embed":true}'
# → { "status": "up_to_date" }
```

The first call wrote to the brain despite `dry_run: true`. The second call sees the
persisted checkpoint and reports `up_to_date`, confirming the first call was not a
dry run.

## Suggested fix

Pass `opts.dryRun` into the `runImport` call chain at `sync.ts:281-302` and
short-circuit persistence in the chunker / DB-write layer when set.

Minimal-scope alternative — mirror the incremental short-circuit at the top of
`performFullSync`:

```typescript
if (opts.dryRun) {
  const { isSyncable } = await import('../core/sync.ts');
  // enumerate candidate files without writing, return { status: 'dry_run', ... }
  return { status: 'dry_run', fromCommit: null, toCommit: headCommit, /* ... */ };
}
```

## Metadata

| | |
|---|---|
| Severity | Low |
| Category | Advisory flag bypass (correctness) |
| Confidence | High (runtime-verified) |
| Affected version | v0.10.1 (master HEAD `b7e3005`) |
| Reachable via | MCP `sync_brain`, CLI `gbrain call sync_brain` |

---

> **[2026-04-16 updated]** This issue originally combined the `dry_run` bug above
> with a broader framing about `sync_brain` accepting arbitrary caller-supplied
> git repo paths. On reflection that framing was too broad — `repo` is a
> documented parameter and "the MCP caller supplies a path to sync" is the
> tool's intended behavior, not an authorization bypass. I've narrowed this
> issue to just the `dry_run` bypass, which is an unambiguous correctness bug.
> Apologies for the initial framing breadth.
title: [Low] performFullSync ignores dry_run flag on first/full sync

body:
## Problem

gbrain currently integrates with Google services through three separate recipes (`credential-gateway` → `email-to-brain` → `calendar-to-brain`), each requiring sequential setup, custom Node.js collector scripts generated by the agent, and independent cron jobs.

This approach has several limitations:

- **~55 minutes of setup** across three recipe walkthroughs before a single email is ingested
- **Only 2 of 9+ Google services covered** — Gmail and Calendar, both read-only
- **No access to Drive, Docs, Sheets, Forms, or Slides** — the agent can't search Drive for a shared doc before a meeting, pull data from a tracking spreadsheet, or draft a reply to an email
- **Custom collector code must be generated from scratch** — pagination, dedup, noise filtering, state persistence, and cron scheduling are all bespoke per recipe
- **Two competing auth paths** (ClawVisor vs direct OAuth) create decision overhead at every setup step
- **Silent cron failures** — if a collector breaks, data stops flowing and the heartbeat system only detects it after 24 hours

## Proposal

Replace the split recipe approach with [`google-tools-mcp`](https://github.com/karthikcsq/google-tools-mcp) — a unified MCP server that provides **153 tools** across Drive, Docs, Sheets, Gmail, Calendar, Forms, and Slides with a single OAuth flow.

**Installation is one command:**
```bash
claude mcp add -s user google -- npx -y google-tools-mcp
```

**Setup is one wizard:**
```bash
npx -y google-tools-mcp setup
```

### What changes

| Dimension | Current (3 recipes) | Proposed (1 MCP server) |
|---|---|---|
| Setup time | ~55 min | ~5 min |
| Google services | 2 (Gmail, Calendar read-only) | 7 (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Docs, Sheets, Forms, Slides — read + write) |
| Tool count | ~6 custom operations | 153 |
| Read/Write | Read-only | Full read + write |
| Auth | 2 competing paths, manual scope management | Single OAuth, auto-refresh |
| Cron jobs | 3+ | 0 (on-demand tool calls) |
| Custom code to maintain | Collectors, state files, dedup logic | Zero |
| Multi-account | Manual `authuser` tracking | Built-in via `GOOGLE_MCP_PROFILE` |

### New capabilities unlocked

- **Drive search** — find shared documents, read PDFs and Word docs directly
- **Docs integration** — read and edit Google Docs natively (including markdown support)
- **Sheets integration** — pull structured data from spreadsheets, write back results
- **Gmail write** — draft, send, and reply to emails from brain context
- **Calendar write** — create, update, and manage events
- **Forms** — read responses, create forms programmatically
- **Slides** — read presentation content for meeting/board prep

### Architectural fit

The "code for data, LLMs for judgment" pattern is preserved — MCP tools are the deterministic data layer (handling pagination, auth, API calls reliably). The agent's judgment role (entity detection, brain enrichment, classification) is unchanged. The pattern just moves from custom Node.js collectors to maintained MCP tools.

### Migration path

1. Add `google-tools-mcp` as a recommended MCP server (new recipe or plugin config)
2. Deprecate `credential-gateway.md`, `email-to-brain.md`, `calendar-to-brain.md` with pointers to the new setup
3. `meeting-sync.md` (Circleback) is unaffected

### Links

- [`google-tools-mcp` on GitHub](https://github.com/karthikcsq/google-tools-mcp)
- [`google-tools-mcp` on npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/google-tools-mcp)
title: Replace split Google integration recipes with unified google-tools-mcp

He analizado tu sistema y ya tienes **Git** y **Bun** instalados, ¡así que tenemos medio camino hecho! 🚀

He preparado un plan detallado para instalar **gbrain** (puedes verlo en el archivo `implementation_plan.md`). Antes de empezar, necesito que me confirmes un par de cosas:
