title: Preguntas para la instalación de gbrain

body:
GBrain has email-to-brain for ongoing collection, but nothing for historical import. People with 10+ years of Gmail can't get that history into their brain.

I'd like to contribute a recipe that takes Google Takeout .mbox files and produces native GBrain pages (people, companies, projects, ideas, concepts) with provenance back to message IDs.

The pipeline: parse .mbox to SQLite (deterministic, using mbox-reader + mailparser + bun:sqlite), then the agent extracts entities batch by batch, resolves identities, materializes two-layer markdown pages, and imports via gbrain import + embed.

This would ship as a recipe markdown + companion TypeScript scripts in `scripts/email-archive-to-brain/`. The scripts handle the deterministic parts (parsing, normalization, materialization). The agent handles the judgment parts (extraction, identity disambiguation). This follows the "code for data, LLMs for judgment" pattern but with pre-built scripts for the complex parsing that's too error-prone to generate from scratch each install.

Full design doc with architecture, page templates, error catalog, and test plan: https://gist.github.com/lloydarmbrust/04996a9dfe6c1a0fa0b6d95f06262d68

Would you accept a PR for this? Any concerns about the approach, particularly shipping companion scripts with a recipe?

labels.name: proprietary
labels.description: Niche / non-mainstream API or tool integration

title: Recipe proposal: email-archive-to-brain (historical email import from Google Takeout)

body:
## What a brain holds

gbrain is not a file store. It's structured thinking: connections between ideas, reasoning patterns, context accumulated over time that only makes sense because someone organized it a certain way. The links between pages carry meaning. The tags represent a personal taxonomy. The timeline records how understanding evolved. The graph of backlinks maps how one concept relates to another in the owner's mental model.

This is different from a folder of documents. A folder can be copied and read by anyone. A brain has implicit context that only the owner fully understands, knowledge embedded in the organization itself, and private thoughts written to think through problems, not to share.

When the owner dies or becomes unavailable, the brain dies with them. If the database is local, it disappears when the disk is formatted. If it's hosted, it stays tied to an account nobody else controls. Either way, there is no mechanism for someone else to inherit it, and no way for the owner to decide in advance what should survive and what should not.

A book can be inherited. A diary can be read. But a second brain sits in a gap: it contains both knowledge worth preserving and thoughts meant to stay private. Today gbrain treats all content the same. The owner cannot say "this page is for my heirs" or "this page dies with me."

## The problem

Access is binary. You have the token and see everything, or you don't have it and see nothing.

There is no owner in the schema. No way to designate heirs. No lifecycle policy. The `access_tokens` table has a `scopes` column (`TEXT[]`) but nothing in the codebase validates it. Every token grants access to all 30+ operations. The `--readonly` flag exists but applies server-wide, not per-token or per-page.

Content is stored in plain text. The page body, timeline, search chunks, and raw data are all readable by anyone with access to the database or its credentials. A `visibility` flag in the database would only be application-layer access control. It can be bypassed by anyone who reaches the storage directly: stolen laptop, compromised Supabase account, leaked backup. Changing a database field should not be enough to read private content. The protection needs to be cryptographic.

## Threat model

Without encryption, access control is a permission check that lives in the application layer and can be bypassed at the storage layer:

| Scenario | Today | With encryption |
|---|---|---|
| Stolen laptop | Database readable, full brain exposed | Ciphertext without the passphrase |
| Compromised Supabase | All tables readable via SQL | Content columns are ciphertext |
| Malicious heir flips visibility flags | Private pages become readable | Still encrypted with owner_key, flag change does nothing |
| Stolen admin token | Full MCP access | Can list slugs and titles but body is ciphertext |
| Leaked database backup | Complete brain dump in plain text | Dump of ciphertext + metadata |

## Proposal

### 1. Scope enforcement

The infrastructure already exists. `access_tokens` has `scopes TEXT[]`, operations have `mutating: boolean`, and the CLI (`src/commands/auth.ts`) can create, list, and revoke tokens. The missing piece is validation.

Three scope levels:
- `read`: get_page, search, list_pages, get_tags, traverse_graph, get_timeline
- `write`: put_page, delete_page, add_tag, add_link, file_upload
- `admin`: auth create/revoke, export, config

Enforcement in the MCP server dispatch, where tool calls are routed to operation handlers:

```typescript
function authorizeOp(token: AccessToken, op: Operation): boolean {
  if (!token.scopes || token.scopes.length === 0) return true;
  const requiredScope = op.mutating ? 'write' : 'read';
  return token.scopes.includes(requiredScope) || token.scopes.includes('admin');
}
```

Tokens without scopes keep working as today (full access). New tokens can be scoped: `gbrain auth create "dashboard" --scope read`.

The `mcp_request_log` table already has columns for `token_name`, `operation`, `latency_ms`, `status`, and `created_at` but nothing writes to it. Logging should be wired up at the same enforcement point. The `last_used_at` column in `access_tokens` should be updated on each request. Both are needed later for inactivity detection.

### 2. Page-level encryption at rest

Each page gets a visibility level in its frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: Deal Flow Notes
visibility: private
---
```

Three levels:
- `private`: body encrypted with the owner's key. Only the owner can decrypt. This is the default.
- `heir`: body encrypted with a shared key that designated heirs hold. Owner and heirs can decrypt.
- `public`: stored in plain text. Anyone with a read-scoped token can access.

**What gets encrypted:**
- `pages.compiled_truth` (the main markdown content)
- `pages.timeline` (the timeline text)
- `content_chunks.chunk_text` (the chunked segments used for vector search)
- `raw_data.data` (sidecar JSONB from external sources)

**What stays in plain text:**
- `pages.slug` (needed for lookups)
- `pages.title` (needed for listing pages; the owner accepts that titles are visible metadata)
- `pages.type` (needed for filtering)
- `pages.frontmatter` (needed for visibility filtering, type/tag metadata)

**Key management:**

The owner sets a passphrase at setup (`gbrain init` or `gbrain config set-passphrase`). The passphrase is never stored. The key is derived using PBKDF2-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations and a random salt, matching the existing pattern in `src/commands/publish.ts`. On `gbrain serve` startup, the owner enters the passphrase and the derived key is held in process memory for the session. A `gbrain unlock` command allows unlocking mid-session.

For heirs: when the owner runs `gbrain config add-heir`, a separate heir_key is generated. The owner provides a passphrase for the heir (communicated out of band). The heir_key is derived from that passphrase the same way. Pages marked `visibility: heir` are encrypted with the heir_key. The heir enters their passphrase when connecting.

Encryption uses AES-256-GCM with a random 96-bit IV per page write, same as `publish.ts` already does. The salt, IV, and ciphertext are stored in the column as a structured wrapper. The 16-byte GCM auth tag prevents tampering.

**On cryptographic choices:**

This proposal uses the primitives gbrain already has (AES-256-GCM, PBKDF2-SHA256 100k iterations). These are solid and widely audited, but they represent a baseline, not the ceiling. Stronger options exist:

- **Key derivation:** Argon2id is the current recommendation (winner of the Password Hashing Competition). It's memory-hard, which makes brute-force attacks significantly more expensive than PBKDF2. The tradeoff is that Argon2 has less universal runtime support, particularly in WASM environments like PGLite.
- **Searchable encryption:** Schemes like CipherSweet or order-preserving encryption allow querying encrypted data without decrypting it. They weaken the encryption guarantees but would remove the "no search without key" limitation.
- **Key escrow / threshold schemes:** Shamir's Secret Sharing could split the heir key across multiple heirs, requiring N-of-M to reconstruct. This prevents any single heir from accessing content alone.
- **Hardware-backed keys:** Integration with platform keystores (macOS Keychain, TPM) would protect the owner key from memory dumps.

Starting with PBKDF2 + AES-256-GCM gives gbrain a real encryption layer with zero new dependencies, using code that already exists in the codebase. The door stays open to upgrade the primitives later without changing the architecture.

**Impact on search:**

This is the real tradeoff. gbrain search works two ways today:

1. **Keyword search** uses a `search_vector` tsvector column built by a Postgres trigger from plaintext `compiled_truth` and `timeline`. If those columns contain ciphertext, the trigger produces a useless tsvector.

2. **Vector search** uses OpenAI embeddings of `chunk_text` stored in `content_chunks.embedding` with an HNSW index. Embeddings cannot be generated from ciphertext.

For encrypted pages, both search methods only work when the key is active in the session. The application layer decrypts the content, builds the tsvector, and generates embeddings at write time while the key is in memory. When the owner is not in session (key not loaded), encrypted pages are not searchable. A read-only token without the passphrase can search public pages only.

This is a real limitation. But if search worked without the key, the search index itself would be a plaintext copy of the content. The content would not be protected. Accepting this tradeoff is the correct choice.

For PGLite (single-connection, single-user), this works naturally: the owner is always the one running the process, so the key is always active during use. For Postgres with remote MCP, the key lives in the MCP server's memory for the duration of the `gbrain serve` session.

### 3. Owner, heir & legacy policy

The brain needs to know who owns it and who should receive it.

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS brain_config (
  key   TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  value JSONB NOT NULL
);
```

```bash
gbrain config set-owner --name "..." --email "..."
gbrain config add-heir --name "..." --email "..." --scope read
gbrain config set-legacy --trigger inactivity --days 90 --action transfer
```

When designating an heir, gbrain creates a read-scoped token for the heir and generates the heir_key from the heir passphrase. The heir receives:
- A read-scoped token (for MCP access)
- A passphrase (for decrypting `heir` pages, communicated by the owner out of band)

The owner can revoke an heir at any time (`gbrain config remove-heir`), which revokes their token and re-encrypts `heir` pages with a new key for remaining heirs.

The legacy policy is stored in `brain_config` and defines what happens when the dead man's switch fires.

### 4. Dead man's switch

The `mcp_request_log` table already tracks timestamps per request and `access_tokens.last_used_at` exists in the schema. These provide the data for inactivity detection.

Activation mechanisms (not mutually exclusive):
- **Inactivity timeout**: no owner interaction in X days (configurable, checked against `last_used_at` on the owner's tokens). A background check on `gbrain serve` startup or a cron evaluates the condition.
- **Periodic confirmation**: gbrain sends a notification (email via configured SMTP or webhook) asking the owner to confirm activity. No response after a grace period activates the plan.
- **Manual activation**: an heir requests access through a CLI command. A configurable grace period (default 30 days) gives the owner time to deny before activation.

Actions when triggered:
- `transfer`: heir tokens are activated. Heirs can connect and decrypt `heir` pages with their passphrase.
- `export`: automatic export of `heir` + `public` pages, encrypted with the heir's passphrase, delivered to the heir's configured email.
- `publish`: pages marked `public` are exported as plain text HTML using the existing publish flow in `src/commands/publish.ts`.
- `delete`: pages marked `private` are permanently deleted. The owner_key passphrase was never shared. This content was meant to die with the owner.
- `lock`: brain becomes permanently read-only. No further writes accepted. The brain becomes an archive.

## What already exists in the codebase

| Component | Location | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `scopes TEXT[]` column | `access_tokens` table | Exists, never validated |
| `mutating` flag on operations | `src/core/operations.ts` | Exists, used only for `--readonly` |
| `--readonly` server mode | `src/mcp/server.ts` | Working, server-wide only |
| AES-256-GCM encryption | `src/commands/publish.ts` | Working, publish-only |
| PBKDF2 key derivation (100k rounds) | `src/commands/publish.ts` | Working |
| Token create/list/revoke CLI | `src/commands/auth.ts` | Working |
| Token hash storage (SHA-256) | `src/commands/auth.ts` | Working |
| `mcp_request_log` table | `schema.sql` | Exists, nothing writes to it |
| `last_used_at` column | `access_tokens` table | Exists, never updated |
| Export to markdown | `src/commands/export.ts` | Working |
| Content stripping for publish | `src/commands/publish.ts` | Working |
| Hybrid search (keyword + vector + RRF) | `src/core/search/hybrid.ts` | Working, plaintext only |
title: Feature: Digital Legacy & Encrypted Access Control

body:
## Summary

After `bun install -g gbrain` (or when gbrain is later updated), the symlink at `~/.bun/bin/gbrain` points to `cli.ts` which lacks the executable bit. Direct execution fails with `Permission denied`, breaking any MCP client that invokes the symlink path directly.

## Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.13 (Debian-based, Proxmox LXC)
- bun: 1.3.12
- gbrain: 0.9.3
- MCP client: Hermes Agent (hermes_cli.main gateway)

## Reproduction

```bash
$ ls -la ~/.bun/bin/gbrain
lrwxrwxrwx ... /home/eric/.bun/bin/gbrain -> ../install/global/node_modules/gbrain/src/cli.ts

$ ls -la ~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/gbrain/src/cli.ts
-rw-rw-r-- ... src/cli.ts

$ /home/eric/.bun/bin/gbrain --help
bash: /home/eric/.bun/bin/gbrain: Permission denied
```

cli.ts has the correct shebang (`#!/usr/bin/env bun`), but mode 664 lacks the `x` bit.

## Impact

MCP clients configured per the README (`command: gbrain`, `args: ["serve"]`) fail to start the stdio server. Observed in Hermes Agent logs:

```
WARNING tools.mcp_tool: Failed to connect to MCP server 'gbrain' 
(command=/home/eric/.bun/bin/gbrain): [Errno 13] Permission denied
```

## Notable behavior

The permissions get **reset on every gbrain update**. My logs show gbrain MCP worked for several hours on 2026-04-13, then broke the moment cli.ts was last written (during an update at 18:41), after which every subsequent restart failed. So this is not purely a first-install issue — it recurs.

## Workaround

Invoking via `bun run` explicitly works:

```bash
bun run ~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/gbrain/src/cli.ts serve
```

A wrapper script that execs via bun is a functional workaround but defeats the point of the global bin entry.

## Possible fixes

1. **Packaging**: add a `postinstall` in package.json that runs `chmod +x src/cli.ts` after install
2. **bun-side**: if `bun link` is responsible, its linking logic should preserve or set +x on shebang-bearing bin targets. (Worth confirming whether this is bun's behavior or the package's responsibility.)
3. **Fallback**: ship a small shell wrapper as the bin target and invoke `bun run cli.ts` from there

Happy to contribute a PR if you have a preferred direction.
title: gbrain symlink not executable after install/update on Linux, MCP invocation fails with Permission denied

body: Can gbrain support multiple repos? Example: gbrain repo -AND- NL/markdown project repo support ## What problem does this solve? 1. Use case Several markdown-based project repos contain meaningful documents and artifacts that must remain verbatim. GBrain should provide one searchable brain across those repos plus a dedicated gbrain repo for agent-written knowledge pages. 2. Required behavior The project representation should: - reflect each external repo’s current state - stay updated as external repos change - cite source documents faithfully - preserve provenance to original artifacts - remain searchable/queryable through GBrain - preserve external markdown repos as read-only sources 3. Source and storage model The project repos should be indexed, not rewritten. PDFs, scans, signed docs, and media should live in GBrain storage and remain discoverable through linked markdown pages. 4. Current product shape GBrain currently appears optimized for one indexed brain repo: - `gbrain sync` is single-repo-centric - sync state is shared - slugs are repo-relative - the page model favors parsed brain pages over external markdown repos as first-class read-only sources ## What does the solution look like? Not sure, not a programmer. I asked my clanker: 1.0 Multi-source brain model: add `source_id` + `source_mode` (`mutable_brain_repo | readonly_repo | artifact_storage`) to every page/file. 2.0 Per-source sync state: replace global `sync.repo_path` / `sync.last_commit` with per-source anchors. 3.0 Namespaced identity: slug = `source_id:path`, so identical repo-relative paths never collide. 4.0 Read-only repo mode: `gbrain attach-repo /path --mode readonly` indexes markdown but forbids rewrite-back. 5.0 Mutable brain mode: `gbrain attach-repo /path --mode brain` preserves today’s editable brain-page workflow. 6.0 Source fidelity contract: readonly pages store raw markdown hash + original path; GBrain may parse, never normalize back. 7.0 Unified query layer: results label `brain page`, `source doc`, or `artifact wrapper`, with source + mutability. 8.0 Artifact wrappers: binaries in storage get lightweight markdown metadata pages; OCR/transcript optional. 9.0 Cross-linking: brain pages can reference readonly docs and storage artifacts without taking ownership. 10.0 CLI shape: `attach-repo`, `detach-repo`, `sources list`, `sync --source |--all`. 11.0 Permissions: per-source write policy lets agents edit brain repo, read project repos, and access only allowed storage prefixes. 12.0 Default stance: keep single-brain-repo mode as default; multi-source stays explicit advanced mode. ## Side Note I made this excalidraw visual to help me understand the intended workflows for when working with gbrain across devices. Possibly it would help your non-programmer audience get a grasp of it a little quicker.
title: multiple repo support syncing/tracking

body:
## TL;DR

Are there any plans to support a **Claude Code-native** operating mode for GBrain — where the brain runs entirely on top of Claude Code's built-in model access (skills, slash commands, sub-agents) instead of requiring users to bring their own `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`?

The model here is [`gstack`](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code) style projects: pure markdown skills + slash commands, zero API keys, zero billing setup, the user just installs Claude Code and it works.

## Why

Today's install flow (from README) requires:

```
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...          # required for vector search
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...   # optional
```

For people who already pay for Claude Code (Pro / Max / Team), bringing a second set of API keys is real friction:

- Two billing relationships instead of one
- Have to provision and rotate keys
- Onboarding stalls at "go get an OpenAI key" — the opposite of "~30 minutes to a fully working brain"
- Many Claude Code users don't have an OpenAI account at all

GBrain's own ethos docs (`docs/ethos/THIN_HARNESS_FAT_SKILLS.md`, `MARKDOWN_SKILLS_AS_RECIPES.md`) already argue that markdown skills *are* the product. A Claude Code-native mode would be the logical end state of that philosophy.

## What it might look like

- **Embeddings**: optional. Default to keyword/trigram search via PGLite (already supported as a fallback). Skip the vector path entirely unless the user opts in. Or: use a local embedding model bundled via WASM.
- **Query expansion / reranking / dream cycle**: done by Claude Code itself via skills + sub-agents, not by API calls from the CLI process.
- **Ingestion summarization, entity extraction, back-linking**: same — invoked as Claude Code skills, not via `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`.
- **`gbrain init` mode flag**: e.g. `gbrain init --mode claude-code` that picks the keyless defaults and wires the skills accordingly.

Net effect: a user with only Claude Code installed can clone the repo, run `gbrain init`, and have a working brain with no third-party keys.

## Questions

1. Is this on the roadmap, or has it been considered and rejected? If rejected, what was the blocker (search quality without embeddings? something else)?
2. Would a community PR exploring a `--mode claude-code` path be welcome?
3. Are there constraints from the contract-first architecture (`src/core/operations.ts`) that would make this awkward?

Happy to prototype if there's interest. Thanks for the great project.
title: Plans for a Claude Code-native mode (no API keys, like gstack)?

body:
---

GBrain exposes 37 MCP tools to connected agents. Most are read operations, but several can permanently modify or destroy data:

- `delete_page` — permanently removes a knowledge page
- `put_page` — overwrites existing page content with no confirmation
- `revert_version` — reverts a page, discarding the current version
- `sync_brain` — full resync can overwrite multiple pages at once

When an agent is connected via MCP, there's no enforcement layer between the agent deciding to call `delete_page` and the page being gone. The agent doesn't need to be malicious — a hallucination, a misunderstood instruction, or a loop is enough.

**Suggestion:** Consider adding support for [PolicyLayer/Intercept](https://github.com/policylayer/intercept), an open-source MCP proxy that enforces policies on tool calls before execution. It sits between the agent and the MCP server transparently.

Example policy for GBrain:

```yaml
version: "1"
default: allow

tools:
  delete_page:
    rules:
      - action: require_approval
        approval_timeout: 5m

  put_page:
    rules:
      - rate_limit: 10/minute

  revert_version:
    rules:
      - action: require_approval

  sync_brain:
    rules:
      - rate_limit: 1/hour

hide:
  - delete_page
```

This would:
- Hold `delete_page` and `revert_version` for human approval before executing
- Rate limit `put_page` to prevent runaway write loops
- Limit `sync_brain` to once per hour
- Optionally hide `delete_page` from the agent entirely

One line to set it up:

```
npx -y @policylayer/intercept init
```

Happy to contribute a starter policy file or docs section if useful.
title: Add policy enforcement for destructive MCP tools (delete_page, put_page, sync_brain)

body:
## Description

When running `gbrain init --pglite` or any other command that interacts with the database, the bundled CLI fails with:

```
ENOENT: no such file or directory, open '//root/pglite.data'
```

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Clone the repository
2. Run `bun install`
3. Build the CLI with `bun run build`
4. Run `./bin/gbrain init --pglite`

## Expected Behavior

The CLI should initialize a local PGLite database at `~/.gbrain/brain.pglite` (or the path specified in config).

## Actual Behavior

The CLI fails with:
```
Setting up local brain with PGLite (no server needed)...
ENOENT: no such file or directory, open '//root/pglite.data'
```

## Root Cause Analysis

After investigating, I found:

1. The `init.ts` file correctly sets the database path:
   ```typescript
   const dbPath = opts.customPath || join(homedir(), '.gbrain', 'brain.pglite');
   ```

2. However, the PGlite library in the bundled binary uses a hardcoded internal path `//root/pglite.data` (Bun's virtual filesystem path).

3. This appears to be a `bun build --compile` issue where the PGlite library's virtual filesystem path isn't being resolved correctly.

## Workaround

Using the unbundled version (`bun run src/cli.ts`) also fails, but with a different error:
```
relation "pages" does not exist
```
This suggests the database schema wasn't initialized due to the path issue.

## Environment

- GBrain version: 0.9.0
- Bun version: 1.3.11
- OS: macOS (ARM64)
- Node.js: N/A (using Bun)

## Additional Notes

The underlying PGlite library works correctly when used directly in scripts (not through the CLI). For example:

```typescript
import { PGlite } from '@electric-sql/pglite';

const db = new PGlite('./brain.pglite');
await db.exec('CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS pages (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, content TEXT);');
// Works fine
```

This confirms the issue is specifically with the bundled CLI, not the PGlite library itself.
title: [Bug] PGlite path issue in bundled CLI: ENOENT '//root/pglite.data'

body:
**What problem does this solve?**

There are several more methods to improve gbrain such as reranking, and for comparing what embeddings are suitable for gbrain. Currently we have no way to measure search quality — hybrid search (vector + keyword + RRF), 4-layer dedup, and multi-query expansion all have tunable parameters (RRF K=60, cosine dedup threshold=0.85, type ratio cap=0.6, etc.) but changes are evaluated by vibes only. We also can't compare embedding models (e.g. `text-embedding-3-large` vs `gemini-embedding-2-preview` vs `qwen3-embedding:8b`) or validate whether adding reranking actually improves results.

**What does the solution look like?**

An evaluation harness with:
- **Ground truth format/interface**: a simple schema (JSON/YAML) for users to define query → expected relevant slugs pairs against their own brain data. Not a bundled dataset — each brain is different.
- **Retrieval metrics**: nDCG@k, MRR, Precision@k, Recall@k computed against user-provided ground truth
- **A/B comparison mode**: run the same query set against two configurations (different embeddings, with/without reranking, different RRF K values) and output a diff table
- **CLI command**: `gbrain eval` that runs the benchmark suite and prints a summary
- New operation in `operations.ts` so it's available via both CLI and MCP

**Follow-up issues (out of scope here):**
- DSPy integration — use eval metrics as optimization objectives for automatic prompt tuning
- Reranking — cross-encoder reranking stage in the search pipeline

**Alternatives considered**
- Using an external eval framework (RAGAS, BEIR) — too heavyweight for a personal knowledge brain, and our data schema is custom
- Bundling a fixed eval dataset — doesn't make sense for an open-source personal brain where every user's data is different
- LLM-as-judge for relevance scoring — useful as a complement (and could bootstrap initial ground truth) but not a replacement for deterministic metrics
- Manual spot-checking — current approach, doesn't scale and can't catch regressions
title: Feature request: Add evaluation metric for comparing different approaches

body:
GBrain solves retrieval at scale. Compiled truth is the right pattern for it.

Compiled truth works beautifully at the document level. "Current best understanding of topic X." But there's a layer above that which is harder to compile: the subject itself.

10,000 files capture what someone has said, thought, built. They don't capture who that person is in a form a model can act on consistently. Garry Tan 2019 and Garry Tan 2026 live in the same brain, same files. Different person.

AGENTS.md tells a claw what to do. That's procedural. What's missing is declarative: who the claw is when it acts on your behalf, as a runtime specification the model interprets directly.

I've been working on a schema for exactly this: encoding brand identity and strategy as machine-readable constraint specifications. Not invented. Excavated in an Human-AI Dialogue. Think of it as compiled single source of truth, but for the person behind the documents instead of the documents themselves.

Happy to share the full spec and the thinking behind it if you're interested. Easiest way: DM here or on X (@Max_brandkernel).
title: Proposal: identity schema as runtime specification layer above AGENTS.md

body:
Hi, thanks for building gbrain.

Request: support Org-mode (`.org`) files as an additional source format for `import` and `sync`.

This seems like a strong fit for the project because there is a meaningful overlap between:
- people using Org-mode
- people maintaining large personal knowledge bases, research archives, and long-lived text repositories

gbrain’s model of "repo as source of truth, indexed retrieval layer on top" maps naturally to Org repositories as well as Markdown repositories. Even an import/sync-only first pass would already unlock a substantial set of existing knowledge bases.

A minimal scope could be:
- `gbrain import` accepts `.org` files
- `gbrain sync` tracks add/modify/delete for `.org`
- basic Org metadata maps into the existing page model, for example:
  - `#+title:` -> title
  - `#+filetags:` -> tags
  - body -> compiled truth/content
  - optionally a conventional heading like `Timeline` -> timeline

This would not need to imply full Org semantics or perfect round-tripping. Import support alone would already be useful.

Using an existing Org parser would likely be preferable to custom parsing logic.

If this direction fits the project, a PR from a fork can be opened immediately with an import/sync-only first pass.
title: Feature request: Org-mode (.org) ingestion and sync support
